topics.title: Digital Investigations and Evidence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Digital Investigations and Evidence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Digital Investigation** is a specialized, scientifically validated process of identifying, preserving, collecting, validating, analyzing, interpreting, documenting, and presenting digital evidence derived from digital sources for the purpose of facilitating or furthering the reconstruction of events found to be criminal, or helping to anticipate unauthorized actions shown to be disruptive to planned operations.

**Digital Evidence** is any probative information stored or transmitted in digital form that a party to a court case may use at trial. It encompasses all electronic data — from files, logs, and metadata to network packets, memory dumps, and cloud artifacts — that can establish that a crime has been committed or provide a link between a crime and its perpetrator.

According to the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** and the **Scientific Working Group on Digital Evidence (SWGDE)**, digital evidence must be:

- **Admissible** — conforming to legal rules of evidence
- **Authentic** — provably tied to the source
- **Reliable** — collected using validated forensic methods
- **Complete** — providing the full picture, not a fragment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Under the IT Act 2000 (India) and its 2008 amendment, Section 79 and the corresponding **Information Technology (Procedure and Safeguards for Interception, Monitoring and Decryption of Information) Rules, 2009** recognize digital records as legally admissible evidence, provided their authenticity is established under **Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872** (now Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a traditional crime scene — say, a bank robbery. Detectives arrive, rope off the area, photograph everything, dust for fingerprints, and tag every piece of physical evidence in sealed bags. They keep a meticulous log of who handled the bag, when, and where it went. They never touch anything with bare hands because contamination would destroy the case.

**Digital investigation is the cyber equivalent of this process.** The "crime scene" is a hard drive, a smartphone, a server, a cloud account, or even volatile memory. The "fingerprints" are file timestamps, MAC addresses, log entries, deleted fragments, and metadata. The investigator's job is to be a **digital Sherlock Holmes** — but with a strict chain of custody and a reproducibility requirement (another qualified examiner must be able to repeat the analysis and reach the same conclusion).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> - **Hard disk** = A diary written in invisible ink
> - **Deleted file** = A page torn out but still leaving a faint impression on the next page
> - **Metadata** = Footprints leading up to the diary
> - **Chain of custody** = The signature log of every person who ever touched the diary bag from crime scene to courtroom

### 1.3 Categories of Digital Evidence

> [!NOTE]
> The standard **IOCE/SWGDE** classification divides digital evidence into three primary types:

| Category | Description | Persistence | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volatile Evidence** | Exists only while a system is powered on | Lost on power-off | RAM contents, running processes, network connections, ARP cache |
| **Non-Volatile (Persistent) Evidence** | Stored on persistent media | Survives power-off | Files, logs, registry hives, email, databases |
| **Latent Evidence** | Not immediately visible; requires carving/recovery | Recoverable from slack, unallocated, or hidden areas | Deleted files, steganographic content, slack space, bad sectors |

### 1.4 Order of Volatility (RFC 3227 — The "Doom Principle")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A foundational rule in digital investigation: **collect the most volatile evidence first.** The **Order of Volatility (OoV)** is typically stated as:
>
> $$ \text{CPU/Register Cache} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Routing Table, ARP Cache, Process Table} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Kernel Statistics, Memory} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Temporary File Systems} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Disk} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Remote Logging, Physical Configuration} $$
>
> This descending time-to-live ensures that the most fragile artifacts are captured before they vanish.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Digital Investigation Process Model

The investigation lifecycle is most commonly modeled after the **DFRWS (Digital Forensic Research Workshop) framework**, refined by NIST SP 800-86 and the ISO/IEC 27037:2012 standard. For the KTU 2024 syllabus, the canonical six-phase model is as follows:

#### **Phase 1 — Identification**
- Recognize that an incident has occurred.
- Determine the **type** of incident (data breach, malware, insider threat, child exploitation, etc.).
- Identify the **scope** — which systems, users, and time windows are involved.
- **Output:** Initial incident report, scope definition, and authorization to proceed.

#### **Phase 2 — Preservation**
- Isolate the system from the network (do **not** power off — see section 2.3).
- Secure physical and logical access.
- Begin **chain-of-custody documentation** at this exact moment.
- **Output:** Sealed evidence containers, photographic record, signed chain-of-custody form.

#### **Phase 3 — Collection**
- Acquire forensic images using validated tools (e.g., **FTK Imager, EnCase, dd, Guymager**).
- Generate cryptographic hashes (**MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256**) for integrity verification.
- **Output:** Forensically sound bit-stream image + cryptographic hash manifest.

#### **Phase 4 — Examination**
- Reduce the acquired data to a manageable, relevant subset.
- Recover deleted files, parse logs, extract registry hives, decode artifacts.
- **Output:** Targeted data set ready for analysis.

#### **Phase 5 — Analysis**
- Reconstruct timelines (super-timelines from multiple sources).
- Correlate user activity, network behavior, and file system events.
- Draw **defensible inferences** based on validated forensic principles.
- **Output:** Investigative findings report with conclusions and confidence levels.

#### **Phase 6 — Presentation**
- Prepare findings in a form suitable for **legal, administrative, or organizational** audiences.
- Use visual timelines, call graphs, and reproducible procedure documentation.
- **Output:** Expert witness report, courtroom exhibits, or executive briefing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this ordering matters in KTU exams:** Examiners routinely test whether students can map a scenario (e.g., "a compromised server was found") to the correct phase, and whether they know which artifacts are relevant at each phase.

### 2.2 The Principle of Exchange (Locard's Principle in Cyberspace)

Dr. Edmond Locard's forensic exchange principle states: *"Every contact leaves a trace."* In digital investigations, this manifests as:

- **User activity traces** — recent documents, prefetch, jump lists, shellbags
- **Application traces** — LNK files, MRU lists, userassist keys
- **Network traces** — DNS cache, browser history, connection logs
- **File system traces** — MAC (Modified, Accessed, Created/Changed) times, $MFT entries, $LogFile
- **Peripheral traces** — USB device identifiers in the registry (`SYSTEM\MountedDevices`)

### 2.3 The "Pull the Plug" Debate

A long-standing forensic debate: when a live system is identified as a crime scene, should the investigator:

| Approach | Action | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold Boot (Pull the Plug)** | Power down, image disk later | Preserves disk; eliminates anti-forensics | Loses all volatile RAM, network state, encryption keys |
| **Live Acquisition** | Acquire RAM first, then disk | Captures volatile evidence | Modifies the system; non-repeatable; defense can challenge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** The modern best practice (per NIST SP 800-86) is **live acquisition of volatile data first, followed by bit-stream imaging of persistent storage.** A blanket "pull the plug" is generally discouraged for servers, but acceptable for desktops where volatile data is less critical.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Process & Formulas)

| Concept | Definition / Formula / Rule | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Bit-stream image** | Complete sector-by-sector copy of a storage device | Must use write-blocker |
| **Hash integrity** | $H_{\text{original}} = H_{\text{acquired}} = H_{\text{verified}}$ | MD5 + SHA-1 + SHA-256 commonly compared |
| **Write-blocker** | Hardware/software device preventing write to source media | Mandatory for evidence admissibility |
| **Order of Volatility (OoV)** | Volatile → Non-volatile → Off-system | RFC 3227 standard |
| **Locard's Principle** | Every contact leaves a trace | Digital equivalent: every interaction creates artifacts |
| **Chain of Custody (CoC)** | Documented sequence of evidence control | Must show continuous, unbroken possession |
| **Daubert Standard** | Test for admissibility of expert testimony (US) | India uses analogous Ss. 45/65B BSA |
| **Forensic Soundness** | Methods must not alter original evidence | Repeatable, defensible, documented |
| **Anti-forensics** | Techniques to defeat investigation (encryption, stego, wiping) | Must be detected & documented |
| **Sections 65B / 45 BSA** | Indian admissibility for electronic evidence | Mandatory certificate for digital docs |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles in this module are foundational to:

- **Cybersecurity Operations** — Incident response teams use the same six-phase model.
- **Legal Tech & e-Discovery** — Litigation support relies on defensible preservation/collection.
- **Data Breach Notification** — Regulatory compliance (DPDPA 2023 in India, GDPR in EU) requires forensic-grade investigation.
- **Insider Threat Programs** — HR/legal coordination uses digital evidence to substantiate policy violations.
- **Malware Analysis** — Live RAM acquisition captures in-memory only payloads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Investigation Walkthrough

### 3.1 Detailed Procedure: From Incident Detection to Court-Ready Evidence

Below is an exhaustive, end-to-end procedure that an investigator must follow. Each step is numbered, the logic explained, and the output artifact named.

#### **Step 1 — Receive and Log the Incident Request**

Upon receiving a reported incident, the investigator must immediately establish a **case file** (also called an "investigation folder" or "case jacket"). The folder contains:

1. A unique case identifier (e.g., `CASE-2024-11-17-001`).
2. Initial reporting officer's name, agency, contact details.
3. Date and time of the report (using a synchronized, trusted NTP source).
4. Brief narrative of the alleged offense.
5. Scope statement: which systems, accounts, and time windows are involved.

The investigator assigns a **case lead** and opens a **chain-of-custody log** — a single, append-only document that will track every transfer of evidence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** A case opened without a unique identifier and timestamped intake violates the "authentication" requirement under Section 65B of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam. Courts can — and do — throw out cases where documentation is sloppy at the very first step.

#### **Step 2 — Authorization and Scope Confirmation**

The investigator must obtain **written authorization** before touching any system. Two critical legal documents:

- **Search warrant** (for criminal matters) under BNSS / CrPC provisions.
- **Corporate authorization letter** (for internal investigations) signed by an officer with authority over the system, plus an **employee consent form** if the device is personally owned but employer-managed (BYOD).

#### **Step 3 — Pre-Acquisition Documentation**

Before any data is collected, the investigator must document the **scene as-is**:

- Photograph the workstation screen (if powered on), the cables, attached USB devices, and the surrounding area.
- Record the time displayed on the system clock and compare with a trusted reference clock.
- Note the running operating system, visible applications, and the network status.
- If the system is a laptop, record the AC adapter state and battery percentage.

This pre-state documentation is the **baseline** against which all later "system modification" claims by the defense will be measured.

#### **Step 4 — Order of Volatility Acquisition (Live Data)**

Following RFC 3227, the investigator captures the most volatile data first. A typical live-acquisition sequence:

1. **Network state** — `netstat -anob`, `arp -a`, `ipconfig /all` (Windows) or `ss -tulnp`, `ip neigh`, `ifconfig` (Linux).
2. **Process and service state** — `tasklist /svc` (Windows), `ps auxf` (Linux), loaded kernel modules.
3. **Logged-in users and open files** — `query session`, `net session`, `w` command.
4. **Memory contents** — full RAM dump using FTK Imager, Magnet RAM Capture, or LiME (Linux Memory Extractor).
5. **Clipboard and temporary data** — `pclip`, recent shell history, scratch files.

The output of each command is hashed and written to a write-blocked external medium, not the host under investigation.

#### **Step 5 — Bit-Stream Imaging of Persistent Storage**

After volatile data is captured, the investigator creates a **forensically sound image** of the persistent storage:

1. Connect a **hardware write-blocker** between the source drive and the imaging workstation.
2. Use an imaging tool — common choices are `dcfldd`, `ewfexport` (libewf), `FTK Imager`, or `Guymager`.
3. Generate **two independent hashes** during acquisition:
   - **MD5** (128-bit, legacy) for fast integrity comparison.
   - **SHA-1** or **SHA-256** (NIST-recommended) for cryptographic strength.
4. Record the hashes in a verification manifest and sign it with the investigator's PGP key.

The relationship between original and acquired media is:

$$
H_{\text{original}} = H_{\text{image}}
$$

where $H$ denotes the cryptographic hash function. Any single bit difference between the two devices will produce a completely different hash output, due to the **avalanche property** of cryptographic hash functions.

#### **Step 6 — Verification and Evidence Sealing**

The investigator re-hashes the image on the destination media and compares:

$$
\text{If } H_{\text{verified}} = H_{\text{original}} \Rightarrow \text{Evidence accepted}
$$

$$
\text{If } H_{\text{verified}} \neq H_{\text{original}} \Rightarrow \text{Imaging process must be repeated}
$$

After verification, the original media is sealed in an **anti-static evidence bag**, signed across the seal by the investigator and a witness, and stored in a **secure evidence locker** with controlled access. A photograph of the sealed bag becomes part of the case file.

#### **Step 7 — Examination and Analysis (On the Image, Not the Original)**

The investigator works exclusively on a **working copy** of the image, never the original. Analysis tools include:

- **Autopsy / The Sleuth Kit** — open-source file system analysis.
- **EnCase Forensic** — industry standard, courtroom-validated.
- **X-Ways Forensics** — advanced artifact recovery.
- **Volatility** — memory forensics framework.
- **Plaso / log2timeline** — super-timeline generation.

The analysis must be **documented step-by-step** so that another qualified examiner can reproduce the same findings — this is the **repeatability principle** underpinning the Daubert and 65B admissibility tests.

#### **Step 8 — Reporting and Courtroom Presentation**

The final report contains:

1. **Executive summary** — non-technical overview.
2. **Methodology** — exact tools, versions, and procedures used.
3. **Findings** — each conclusion tied to specific evidence.
4. **Limitations** — what the analysis could and could not establish.
5. **Chain of custody summary** — every transfer, signer, date, and reason.
6. **Glossary of terms** — for non-technical triers of fact.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** A report that uses absolute language ("the defendant did X") without qualifying confidence levels ("based on the recovered artifacts and timeline correlation, it is more likely than not that…") is a frequent ground for cross-examination in Indian and international courts. Always frame conclusions probabilistically when certainty is not absolute.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Hash Verification Calculation

Suppose a 500 GB hard disk is imaged. The MD5 of the source is recorded as:

$$
H_{\text{MD5, source}} = \texttt{d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e}
$$

This is the MD5 of an empty string — used here purely as a familiar 32-hex-character example. In practice, MD5 collisions are possible and SHA-256 is preferred for high-stakes cases.

For the **image**, the SHA-256 hash is computed as:

$$
H_{\text{SHA-256, image}} = \texttt{e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855}
$$

Verification step:

$$
H_{\text{SHA-256, image}} \;\stackrel{?}{=}\; H_{\text{SHA-256, source}}
$$

If the hex strings match character-for-character, the imaging process is verified. If even one character differs, the imaging is **rejected** and must be repeated — a one-bit difference in a 500 GB image will produce a radically different SHA-256 output due to the avalanche effect, which is the **defining security property** of cryptographic hash functions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Six-Phase Digital Investigation Process (Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase1: Identification] --> B[Phase2: Preservation]
    B --> C[Phase3: Collection]
    C --> D[Phase4: Examination]
    D --> E[Phase5: Analysis]
    E --> F[Phase6: Presentation]
    F --> G[Case Closed or Re-Opened]

    A1[Incident Report and Scope] -.-> A
    B1[Isolation, Sealing, CoC Start] -.-> B
    C1[Bit-Stream Image and Hashing] -.-> C
    D1[Parsing, Carving, Recovery] -.-> D
    E1[Timeline, Correlation, Inferences] -.-> E
    F1[Expert Report, Court Exhibits] -.-> F

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#9467bd,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#8c564b,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#7f7f7f,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Chain of Custody Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Scene: Evidence Sealed by Investigator A] --> S2[Transport: Signed Chain of Custody Form]
    S2 --> S3[Lab Intake: Verified by Custodian B]
    S3 --> S4[Storage: Locked Evidence Locker with Access Log]
    S4 --> S5[Examination: Released to Examiner C on Signed Receipt]
    S5 --> S6[Analysis: Working Copy on Write-Blocked Workstation]
    S6 --> S7[Return: Original Resealed and Returned to Locker]
    S7 --> S8[Court: Released to Counsel on Production Order]

    style S1 fill:#003049,color:#ffffff
    style S2 fill:#d62828,color:#ffffff
    style S3 fill:#f77f00,color:#ffffff
    style S4 fill:#fcbf49,color:#000000
    style S5 fill:#eae2b7,color:#000000
    style S6 fill:#2a9d8f,color:#ffffff
    style S7 fill:#264653,color:#ffffff
    style S8 fill:#6a040f,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Order of Volatility (Descending Time-to-Live)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1[CPU Registers and Cache] --> V2[Routing Table and ARP Cache]
    V2 --> V3[Process Table and Kernel Memory]
    V3 --> V4[RAM: Full Memory Dump]
    V4 --> V5[Temporary File Systems: tmp, temp]
    V5 --> V6[Disk: Bit-Stream Image]
    V6 --> V7[Remote Logging and Backups]

    style V1 fill:#ff0000,color:#ffffff
    style V2 fill:#ff6b00,color:#ffffff
    style V3 fill:#ffb700,color:#000000
    style V4 fill:#ffd60a,color:#000000
    style V5 fill:#80ed99,color:#000000
    style V6 fill:#0077b6,color:#ffffff
    style V7 fill:#03045e,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 Digital Evidence Classification Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DE[Digital Evidence Universe]
    DE --> VOL[Volatile Evidence]
    DE --> PERS[Persistent Evidence]
    DE --> LAT[Latent Evidence]

    VOL --> V1a[RAM Dump]
    VOL --> V2a[Network Connections]
    VOL --> V3a[Running Processes]
    VOL --> V4a[Crypto Keys in Memory]

    PERS --> P1a[File System Artifacts]
    PERS --> P2a[Operating System Logs]
    PERS --> P3a[Application Data]
    PERS --> P4a[Registry and Config Files]

    LAT --> L1a[Deleted Files via Carving]
    LAT --> L2a[Slack and Unallocated Space]
    LAT --> L3a[Steganography Detection]
    LAT --> L4a[Shadow Copies and Volume Shadow]

    style DE fill:#1d3557,color:#ffffff
    style VOL fill:#e63946,color:#ffffff
    style PERS fill:#2a9d8f,color:#ffffff
    style LAT fill:#f4a261,color:#000000
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT866 Module 1]**
*Define digital evidence. List the four characteristics that digital evidence must possess to be admissible in a court of law.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Digital evidence is any probative information of probative value that is stored or transmitted in digital form and can be used by a court to establish facts in issue. It includes files, logs, emails, network packets, memory dumps, and metadata.

**Four Characteristics (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Admissible** — must conform to legal rules of evidence (e.g., Section 65B BSA in India).
2. **Authentic** — must be provably tied to the source and not tampered with.
3. **Reliable** — must be collected using forensically sound, validated methods.
4. **Complete** — must present the full picture, not a fragment that could mislead.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award the 0.5 mark per characteristic **only** if the student names it AND gives a one-line explanation. A list without explanation scores zero on the explanation component.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT866 Module 1]**
*State the Order of Volatility as defined in RFC 3227 and explain why it is critical to follow this order during live system investigation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Order (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{CPU/Registers} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Routing Table, ARP Cache, Process Table} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Kernel Statistics, Memory} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Temp File Systems} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Disk} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Remote Logs, Off-system Data}
$$

**Criticality (1.5 marks):** The order reflects decreasing time-to-live of the data. Volatile evidence such as RAM contents, network connections, and running processes vanish the instant power is lost or even when certain operations are performed. By capturing from most-volatile to least-volatile, the investigator ensures that fragile evidence is preserved **before** it is overwritten by subsequent collection steps. Violating the order risks permanent loss of evidence that cannot be recovered from any later phase.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern for a 14-mark question typically splits into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Either Part (a) or Part (b) may contain a sub-choice of OR between two questions; for this bank, **Question A** and **Question B** are independent full-length alternates a student can choose between.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Process & Procedure Oriented*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT866 Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **six-phase digital investigation process model** as adopted by NIST and DFRWS. For each phase, list one primary output artifact. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the **principle of chain of custody**. What are the mandatory fields that a chain-of-custody form must contain? Why is the absence of a complete chain of custody considered fatal to a prosecution under Indian evidence law? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Six-Phase Model (7 marks = 1 per phase + 1 for diagram):**

| Phase | Purpose | Primary Output Artifact |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Identification | Recognize incident and define scope | Incident report and authorization memo |
| 2. Preservation | Isolate and protect the system | Sealed evidence bag + initial CoC entry |
| 3. Collection | Acquire data without modification | Bit-stream image + hash manifest |
| 4. Examination | Reduce data to relevant subset | Parsed artifact set (logs, registry, files) |
| 5. Analysis | Reconstruct events and draw inferences | Super-timeline + correlation report |
| 6. Presentation | Communicate findings to stakeholders | Expert witness report + courtroom exhibits |

*(1 mark reserved for the labeled diagram — students must draw the cyclic flow with arrows, not just a list.)*

**(b) Chain of Custody — Detailed (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Chain of custody is the **continuous, documented, unbroken record** of seizure, control, transfer, analysis, and disposition of evidence, from the moment of collection to its final presentation in court.

**Mandatory Fields of a CoC Form (3 marks — 0.5 each + 1 for completeness):**

1. **Case identifier and unique evidence item number** (e.g., `EV-2024-001-Disk-Suspect-A`).
2. **Description of the evidence** (type, make, model, serial number, capacity).
3. **Date, time, and location** of each transfer (with timezone).
4. **Identity of every person** who handled the item (full name, designation, signature).
5. **Reason for transfer** (e.g., "for forensic imaging", "for courtroom display").
6. **Condition of seals** at each transfer (intact / broken / resealed with reason).
7. **Storage location** between transfers (locker ID, temperature if relevant).

**Why incomplete CoC is fatal under Indian law (3 marks):**

Under **Section 65B of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023** (carried forward from the Indian Evidence Act, 1872), a certificate accompanying electronic evidence must state, among other things, the **manner of acquisition, the device used, and the conditions of safekeeping**. If the chain of custody is broken — e.g., a gap in the log, an unsigned transfer, or unexplained tampering — the opposing counsel can argue that the evidence may have been **substituted, contaminated, or altered** during the unaccounted period. Indian courts (e.g., *Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer*, 2014; *Arjun Panditrao Khotkar v. Kailash Kushanrao Gorantyal*, 2020) have repeatedly held that **mere production of electronic records is insufficient — a provable, complete chain of custody is mandatory**. The result: the evidence may be **excluded** as not proved, leading to acquittal or dismissal of the case on that count.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Concept & Application Oriented*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT866 Module 1]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **volatile, non-volatile, and latent digital evidence**. For each type, give two concrete examples and indicate the typical tool used to acquire it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Order of Volatility**? Why is a "**pull the plug**" approach considered a forensic anti-pattern for live servers? Describe the correct live-acquisition procedure with an example sequence of commands for a Windows system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Volatile / Non-Volatile / Latent — Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Volatile | Non-Volatile (Persistent) | Latent |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Definition** (1 mark) | Exists only while system is powered | Stored on persistent media; survives power-off | Hidden or deleted data recoverable through carving |
| **Persistence** | Lost on power-off or reboot | Survives power-off | Survives power-off; may survive deletion |
| **Example 1** (1 mark) | RAM contents — running process memory | NTFS file system entries | Slack space remnants of deleted files |
| **Example 2** (1 mark) | Active TCP connections in netstat output | Windows Event Logs (System, Application) | Steganographic content inside JPEG |
| **Acquisition Tool** (1 mark) | FTK Imager, Magnet RAM Capture, LiME | FTK Imager, EnCase, dcfldd | Autopsy, The Sleuth Kit, PhotoRec, steghide detection suite |

**(b) Order of Volatility & Live Acquisition (7 marks):**

**Order of Volatility — restated (1 mark):** As defined in RFC 3227 — collect from most volatile (CPU cache) to least volatile (off-system logs/backups).

**Why "pull the plug" is an anti-pattern (2 marks):**

- **Loses volatile evidence permanently** — RAM contents, network state, encryption keys held in memory, and the list of running processes are all destroyed instantly when power is cut.
- **Prevents attribution of live attackers** — in a network intrusion, the attacker's active sessions, open sockets, and C2 (command-and-control) connections vanish.
- **Modern guidance (NIST SP 800-86)** explicitly recommends live acquisition for servers, especially when encrypted volumes or anti-forensics are suspected.

**Correct Windows Live-Acquisition Sequence (4 marks — 1 per step + 1 for sequencing rationale):**

1. **Document the screen** — photograph, record system clock vs. trusted reference.
2. **Capture network state** — execute `netstat -anob > E:\evidence\netstat.txt` and `arp -a > E:\evidence\arp.txt`.
3. **Capture process and service state** — `tasklist /svc > E:\evidence\tasklist.txt`, `sc query > E:\evidence\services.txt`.
4. **Capture memory** — run **FTK Imager** or **Magnet RAM Capture** to dump physical memory to the external write-blocked drive.
5. **Then proceed to disk imaging** — power down only after volatile data is preserved, using a controlled shutdown (`shutdown /s`) rather than pulling the power cable, to allow file system journals to close cleanly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks Loss:**
>
> 1. **CoC Skipping (–2 to –3 marks):** Students frequently describe phases of investigation but forget to mention **chain of custody**, **hash verification**, or **write-blocker usage**. These are *not* optional steps in KTU valuation; their absence is treated as a procedural gap.
> 2. **Hash Confusion (–1 mark):** Writing "MD5 is the only hash used" loses marks. Examiners expect at least **two independent hashes** (MD5 + SHA-1, or SHA-256) for cross-verification.
> 3. **Volatile Evidence Omission (–1 to –2 marks):** Listing only disk evidence without mentioning RAM, network state, and process list is a common error. Always cover **all three** categories.
> 4. **No Indian Law Reference (–1 mark):** For PECCT866 (a KTU India course), citing **Section 65B BSA / Section 65B Indian Evidence Act** explicitly earns the legal-applicability mark. Citing only "Daubert" or "Frye" (US standards) without Indian reference is incomplete.
> 5. **Diagram Omission in 14-mark questions (–1 to –2 marks):** KTU's 14-mark questions almost always reserve at least 1 mark for a **neat, labeled diagram**. A flow chart of the six phases or the CoC chain is the most cost-effective way to recover that mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Digital evidence** is admissible only if it is **Admissible, Authentic, Reliable, and Complete** — the AARC mnemonic.
- The **six-phase process** is: **Identification → Preservation → Collection → Examination → Analysis → Presentation** — memorize in order.
- The **Order of Volatility** (RFC 3227) is non-negotiable: collect the most volatile data first.
- **Chain of custody** must be **continuous, signed, and unbroken** from scene to court.
- **Bit-stream images** are mandatory; logical file copies are **not** forensically sound.
- Always use a **write-blocker** when imaging source media.
- **Two independent hashes** (e.g., MD5 + SHA-256) are the KTU-expected verification standard.
- **"Pull the plug"** is an anti-pattern for live servers; **live acquisition of volatile data first**, then controlled shutdown, then disk imaging.
- **Section 65B BSA** (formerly Section 65B Indian Evidence Act) is the **mandatory certificate** for digital evidence in Indian courts.
- The **Daubert / Frye** admissibility tests in the US, and the **Arjun Panditrao (2020) precedent** in India, all converge on one principle: **the methodology must be repeatable, peer-reviewed, and documented.**
- For KTU exams: always **label diagrams**, **cite sections of the BSA/IT Act**, and **state the hash function used**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Crime Scene Investigation Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Digital Crime Scene Investigation Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Digital Crime Scene Investigation Process** is the systematic, methodical, and legally defensible set of procedures used to identify, preserve, collect, validate, analyze, interpret, document, and present digital evidence derived from electronic devices and digital storage media, in a manner that preserves the integrity of the evidence and the **chain of custody**, such that the evidence remains admissible in a court of law.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this process is treated as a **forensically sound workflow** that bridges the technical world of computing with the legal world of jurisprudence. It is fundamentally governed by the principle that the *original digital artifact must never be altered*, and every action taken on the evidence must be **repeatable, auditable, and documented**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Glossary Terms to Memorize:**
> - **Digital Evidence**: Any information of probative value stored or transmitted in digital form.
> - **Chain of Custody**: The chronological documentation showing the seizure, control, transfer, analysis, and disposition of physical/digital evidence.
> - **Forensically Sound**: Procedures that do not alter the original evidence and can be independently verified.
> - **Volatile Data**: Information that is lost when the system is powered off (RAM, running processes, network connections).
> - **Non-Volatile Data**: Persistent data stored on media (HDD, SSD, USB drives).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Vault" Intuition

Imagine a bank has just been robbed. The detectives arrive and:

1. They **photograph** the entire vault *before touching anything* (preservation).
2. They **seal** the doors so no one else enters (isolation).
3. They **dust** for fingerprints and bag each item in a labeled container (collection).
4. They **log** every person who touches each item, with timestamps (chain of custody).
5. They **analyze** the bagged items only in a controlled lab (analysis).
6. They **present** the evidence in court with a complete audit trail (presentation).

A **Digital Crime Scene** is the same scenario, but the "vault" is a **hard drive, server, mobile device, or network**, and the "fingerprints" are **deleted files, log entries, registry keys, timestamps, and metadata**. The "bank" is the organization's IT infrastructure. The investigator must treat every byte with the same reverence a detective treats a smoking gun.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick — "PIE-CAP"**: The 6 phases of Digital Crime Scene Investigation are:
> **P**reparation → **I**dentification → **E**vidence Collection → **C**hain of Custody → **A**nalysis → **P**resentation

## 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU 2024 Scheme

This topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO1** of PECCT866: *"Understand the fundamental principles and methodologies of digital forensics investigation."* Examiners frequently test:
- The **order of volatility** (what evidence to collect first).
- The **principles** that govern digital investigations.
- The **difference between live and post-mortem acquisition**.
- The **role of chain of custody** in admissibility.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Phases — Operational Breakdown

### Phase 1: Preparation (Pre-Incident Readiness)

This phase happens *before* any crime occurs. The forensic team ensures readiness through:

- **Toolkit Assembly**: Pre-staging hardware write-blockers, forensic workstations (e.g., EnCase, FTK Imager, Autopsy), and validated software hashes.
- **Training & Certification**: Investigators trained in ACE, CHFI, GCFE, or equivalent credentials.
- **Legal Authorization**: Drafting pre-approved search warrants, court orders, and corporate policies.
- **Documentation Templates**: Pre-printed forms for chain of custody, evidence labels, and field notes.
- **Communication Plan**: Establishing contact protocols with legal counsel, law enforcement, and IT operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight**: Many students lose marks by describing the investigation as starting *at* the crime scene. The correct answer is that the **investigation process begins long before the crime occurs** — during the **Preparation phase**.

### Phase 2: Identification (Scene Recognition & Assessment)

The investigator must:

- **Recognize** that a digital crime scene exists (e.g., a compromised server, a phishing email, a ransomware note).
- **Scope** the scene: identify all devices, networks, users, and time windows involved.
- **Prioritize** evidence by *Order of Volatility* (OOV).
- **Photograph** the scene (monitor screens showing open applications, error messages, network cables plugged in, etc.).

### Phase 3: Evidence Collection (Acquisition)

This is the most critical phase technically. Two fundamental modes exist:

#### (a) Live Acquisition
Performed on a *running* system to capture **volatile data**:
- CPU registers, cache
- RAM contents (memory dump)
- Running processes and open files
- Network connections and routing tables
- Logged-in users
- Encryption keys (often only present in RAM)

#### (b) Post-Mortem / Dead Acquisition
Performed on a *powered-off* system to capture **non-volatile data**:
- Hard disk images (using **write-blockers**)
- File system metadata
- Deleted files and slack space
- Registry hives (Windows) or configuration files (Linux)
- Logs, emails, browser history

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cardinal Rule of Acquisition**:
> For a **bit-stream image**, the working formula relating source and image size is:
> $$\text{Image Size} = \text{Source Size} + \text{Slack Space} + \text{Bad Sectors}$$
> For **compressed images**, the storage footprint is:
> $$\text{Compressed Size} \approx \text{Source Size} \times (1 - C)$$
> where $C$ is the compression ratio (typically $0.4 \le C \le 0.7$ for EWF/AD1 formats).

### Phase 4: Chain of Custody (Documentation & Integrity)

Every transfer of evidence is logged with:
- **Who** collected it
- **When** (date/time with timezone)
- **Where** (location)
- **Why** (purpose of transfer)
- **How** (method of transfer)
- **Hash values** (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256) before and after

The **integrity verification equation** is:
$$\text{Integrity Verified} \iff H_{\text{original}}(M) = H_{\text{current}}(M)$$
where $H$ is a cryptographic hash function applied to evidence artifact $M$.

### Phase 5: Analysis (Examination & Interpretation)

The investigator uses forensic tools to:
- Recover deleted files (`file carving`).
- Correlate timestamps across multiple devices.
- Decrypt encrypted volumes (where legal).
- Identify Indicators of Compromise (IOCs).
- Reconstruct user activities and timelines.

### Phase 6: Presentation (Reporting & Court Testimony)

- Generate a **Forensic Investigation Report** with executive summary, methodology, findings, and conclusions.
- Use **glossary of technical terms** for non-technical audience (judges, juries).
- Maintain **peer-review** trail of all analysis steps.
- Be prepared for **cross-examination**.

## 2.2 The Order of Volatility (OOV) — A Critical KTU Concept

| Priority | Data Type | Typical Lifespan | Acquisition Tool |
|----------|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 (Highest) | CPU Registers, Cache | Nanoseconds | Hardware probes |
| 2 | RAM / Memory | Seconds to minutes (until power-off) | `dd`, `memdump`, WinPmem, FTK Imager |
| 3 | Network State (ARP cache, routing table) | Seconds to minutes | `netstat`, `arp -a`, `ip route` |
| 4 | Running Processes | Seconds to minutes | `ps`, Task Manager, `tasklist` |
| 5 | Logged-in Users / Open Files | Hours | `who`, `w`, `net session` |
| 6 | Temporary File Systems | Hours to days | Direct copy |
| 7 | Disk Files | Days to years | Forensic image with write-blocker |
| 8 | Remote Logging / Backups | Years | Server-side retrieval |
| 9 (Lowest) | Physical Configuration / Network Topology | Years | Documentation review |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "CRNLD-DPRT"**:
> **C**PU → **R**AM → **N**etwork → **L**ogged users → **D**isk → **P**rints → **R**emote logs → **T**opology
> (Collect from top to bottom, because the top items die first.)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Purpose / Engineering Utility |
|---------|----------------|-------------------------------|
| **Hash Match (Integrity)** | $H_{\text{original}}(M) = H_{\text{current}}(M)$ | Proves evidence has not been tampered with |
| **Image Size (Bit-stream)** | $\text{ImgSize} = \text{SrcSize} + \text{Slack} + \text{BadSectors}$ | Estimates storage needed for forensic image |
| **Compressed Image Size** | $\text{Compressed} \approx \text{SrcSize} \cdot (1 - C)$ | Storage planning in forensic lab |
| **Hash Collision Probability (birthday)** | $P \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / 2^{b+1}}$ | Justifies why SHA-256 ($b = 256$) is preferred over MD5 |
| **Order of Volatility Score** | Higher volatility = higher priority | Determines acquisition sequence |
| **Working Copy Rule** | Analysis performed on copy, original preserved | Maintains admissibility of evidence |
| **5-Point Rule for Acquisition** | Identify, Document, Preserve, Analyze, Present | Standard for KTU exam answer framing |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Digital Crime Scene Investigation Process is not limited to criminal prosecution. It is extensively used in:

- **Incident Response (IR)**: When a company suffers a data breach, IR teams follow this exact process.
- **Insider Threat Investigation**: Tracing employee misconduct on corporate systems.
- **Intellectual Property Theft**: Proving exfiltration of trade secrets.
- **Cyber Insurance Claims**: Documenting damages for insurance recovery.
- **Regulatory Compliance**: GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS breach notification requires forensic-grade documentation.
- **Disaster Recovery Validation**: Ensuring backup integrity using hash-based verification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standard Reference**: The process is codified in **ISO/IEC 27037:2012** (Guidelines for identification, collection, acquisition and preservation of digital evidence) — KTU examiners often cite this standard in model answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Procedure: A Live Digital Crime Scene Walkthrough

The following is the complete, KTU-2024-aligned procedure for handling a hypothetical digital crime scene involving a **suspected Windows server breach** in a corporate data center.

---

### **Step 1: Receive Notification and Verify Legal Authority**

The forensic investigator receives a call from the CISO. Before leaving for the scene, the investigator must:

- Confirm a **written legal authorization** (search warrant, court order, or senior management approval) is in place.
- Review the **scope** of the investigation (which systems, which time period, which employees).
- Sign in to the **case management system** and open a new case file with a unique **Case ID** (e.g., `CASE-2024-1142`).
- Verify all tools in the forensic kit are **functioning and hash-verified**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Logic**: Without legal authority, any evidence collected may be deemed *fruit of the poisonous tree* and inadmissible.

---

### **Step 2: Arrive at the Scene and Conduct Initial Survey**

Upon arrival, the investigator:

- **Photographs** the entire server room from multiple angles (wide shots first, then close-ups).
- **Notes** the state of all monitors, including any open windows, error messages, or terminal sessions.
- **Records** the physical cable connections (network, power, USB) — these can be crucial.
- **Identifies** all personnel present and notes their positions.
- **Establishes** a perimeter to prevent unauthorized access.

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Logic**: Photography *before* touching anything preserves the *as-was* state of volatile screen data, which would be lost the moment any system is rebooted or interacted with.

---

### **Step 3: Apply the Order of Volatility (OOV) Decision Tree**

The investigator decides what to acquire first based on the OOV priority:

**Decision Tree:**

```
Is the system currently running and connected to network?
├── YES → Perform LIVE acquisition first (RAM, processes, network)
│         ├── Volatile → RAM dump
│         ├── Semi-volatile → Network connections, logged-in users
│         └── Log → Open files, clipboard
│
└── NO  → Proceed to post-mortem acquisition
          ├── Power down using forensic-safe method (pull plug, not shutdown)
          ├── Image the disk using write-blocker
          └── Create hash of image
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Logic**: The *running* state of the system is the most forensically valuable because it contains keys, decrypted data, and active network sessions that vanish upon power-off.

---

### **Step 4: Perform Live Acquisition of Volatile Data**

For a **Windows Server 2019** system, the investigator uses command-line tools and writes output to an **external USB drive that has been wiped and hash-verified**.

**Step 4a — Capture RAM:**

The investigator runs a memory acquisition tool. The estimated memory dump time is:

$$T_{\text{acquire}} = \frac{S_{\text{RAM}}}{\text{Throughput}}$$

where $S_{\text{RAM}}$ is the RAM size and Throughput is the write speed of the destination media.

For example, for a server with $S_{\text{RAM}} = 64 \text{ GB}$ and $\text{Throughput} = 500 \text{ MB/s}$:

$$T_{\text{acquire}} = \frac{64 \times 1024 \text{ MB}}{500 \text{ MB/s}} = \frac{65536}{500} = 131.07 \text{ seconds} \approx 2.18 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4b — Capture Running Processes and Network State:**

The investigator runs equivalent of `netstat -ano`, `tasklist /svc`, and `arp -a`. Output is redirected to external USB:

$$\text{Command: } > \texttt{netstat -ano > E:\volatile\netstat.txt}$$

**Step 4c — Capture Logged-in Users and Open Files:**

The investigator records:
- `query session` (Windows) → logged-in users
- `openfiles /query /v` → open files
- `wevtutil epl Security E:\volatile\security.evtx` → Security event log

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Logic**: All commands are executed with output redirected to external media so that the *internal disk is not modified* — preserving forensic soundness.

---

### **Step 5: Secure the System and Power Down (Forensic-Safe Method)**

Once volatile data is captured, the investigator:

- **Does NOT** use the operating system's shutdown command (it executes many write operations).
- **Pulls the power plug directly** (for desktops) or uses the **"hard" power button** (for laptops).
- **Labels** all cables with colored tags before disconnection.
- **Photographs** the powered-off state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Logic**: A normal shutdown triggers `ntoskrnl.exe` to write shutdown events, clear page files, and update last-access timestamps — all of which destroy potential evidence.

---

### **Step 6: Post-Mortem Disk Acquisition Using a Write-Blocker**

The investigator:

- Connects the suspect disk to a **forensic workstation** via a **hardware write-blocker** (e.g., Tableau T35es).
- The write-blocker ensures that the host OS can **read** from the suspect disk but **cannot write** to it.
- Uses a forensic imager (FTK Imager, `dd`, or Guymager) to create a **bit-stream image**.

The **acquisition verification** uses dual hashing:

$$H_{\text{MD5}}(\text{Image}) = H_{\text{MD5}}(\text{Source})$$

$$H_{\text{SHA-256}}(\text{Image}) = H_{\text{SHA-256}}(\text{Source})$$

If both match, the image is **forensically identical** to the original.

**Step 6a — Image Size Calculation (Derivation):**

For a 2 TB ($= 2 \times 10^{12}$ bytes) source disk with **$0.5\%$ bad sectors** and the file system using **standard 4 KB clusters**:

$$\text{Slack Space} = N_{\text{files}} \times \text{AvgSlackPerFile}$$

Assuming average slack per file = $\frac{\text{ClusterSize}}{2} = \frac{4096}{2} = 2048 \text{ bytes}$, and $N_{\text{files}} = 500{,}000$:

$$\text{Slack} = 500{,}000 \times 2048 = 1.024 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes} \approx 1 \text{ GB}$$

$$\text{Bad Sectors} = 2 \times 10^{12} \times 0.005 = 1 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes} = 10 \text{ GB}$$

$$\text{Total Image Size} = 2 \text{ TB} + 1 \text{ GB} + 10 \text{ GB} \approx 2.010 \text{ TB}$$

**Step 6b — Acquisition Time:**

For a USB 3.0 write-blocker at $\sim 100 \text{ MB/s}$ sustained:

$$T_{\text{img}} = \frac{2.010 \times 10^{12} \text{ bytes}}{100 \times 10^6 \text{ B/s}} = 20{,}100 \text{ seconds} \approx 5.58 \text{ hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Logic**: Bit-stream imaging captures *every bit* including unallocated space, slack space, and bad sectors — which is why it is larger than the logical file size.

---

### **Step 7: Chain of Custody Documentation**

For every evidence item, the investigator fills a **Chain of Custody Form** containing:

| Field | Example Value |
|-------|---------------|
| **Case ID** | CASE-2024-1142 |
| **Evidence ID** | EVD-001 |
| **Description** | Seagate 2 TB HDD, S/N: ZFL12345 |
| **Collected By** | Investigator A. Sharma (Badge #4521) |
| **Date/Time Collected** | 2024-11-15, 14:32 IST |
| **Collected From** | Server Rack 3, Bay 7, DataCenter-BLR |
| **MD5 Hash** | `d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e` |
| **SHA-256 Hash** | `e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb924...` |
| **Transfer From → To** | A. Sharma → Evidence Locker #12 |
| **Purpose of Transfer** | Long-term storage |
| **Signature** | [Signed] |

**Chain of Custody Continuity Equation:**

For evidence to be admissible, the chain must be **unbroken**:

$$C_{\text{intact}} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \left( T_{i,\text{doc}} = T_{i,\text{actual}} \land P_{i,\text{auth}} \right)$$

where $T_{i,\text{doc}}$ is the documented transfer time, $T_{i,\text{actual}}$ is the actual time, and $P_{i,\text{auth}}$ is authorized personnel for transfer $i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Logic**: A single undocumented gap in the chain can render the entire evidence set inadmissible in court.

---

### **Step 8: Analysis Phase (Working Copy Only)**

The investigator **never** analyzes the original image. Instead:

- Creates **two working copies** of the image.
- Verifies both copies match the original via hash comparison.
- Performs all analysis (file carving, registry parsing, timeline generation) on the working copies.
- Stores the original in a **sealed, access-controlled evidence locker**.

**Timeline Reconstruction Equation:**

For events $E_1, E_2, \dots, E_n$ with timestamps $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n$ from multiple sources (Windows, Linux, application logs), the **unified timeline** is:

$$\text{SuperTimeline} = \text{Sort}_{\text{chronological}} \left( \bigcup_{k=1}^{K} \text{Timeline}_k \right)$$

where each $\text{Timeline}_k$ is normalized to **UTC** before merging.

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Logic**: Different time zones across servers, workstations, and cloud logs must be normalized to a single time standard (UTC) before correlation.

---

### **Step 9: Reporting and Presentation**

The final forensic report contains:

1. **Executive Summary** (1 page, non-technical)
2. **Case Background and Scope**
3. **Tools and Methodology Used**
4. **Findings** (with supporting screenshots and hash values)
5. **Timeline of Events**
6. **Conclusions and Opinions**
7. **Appendices** (raw logs, command outputs, hash lists)

The report is **peer-reviewed** by a senior investigator before submission.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Investigation Phase × Action Type

| Phase | Pre-Incident | On-Scene | Lab-Based |
|-------|--------------|----------|-----------|
| **Preparation** | Train team, stage tools, draft warrants | — | — |
| **Identification** | — | Photograph, scope, prioritize | — |
| **Collection** | — | Live + dead acquisition | Image verification |
| **Chain of Custody** | Pre-print forms | Fill forms on scene | Log every analysis access |
| **Analysis** | — | — | File carving, timeline, decryption |
| **Presentation** | — | — | Report, court testimony |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — The Complete Digital Crime Scene Investigation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Notification and Legal Authorization] --> B[Preparation Phase Toolkit Assembly Training Legal Warrants]
    B --> C[Arrival at Scene Initial Photography Cable Mapping Perimeter Setup]
    C --> D{Is System Running and Networked?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Live Acquisition Phase]
    D -- No --> F[Post-Mortem Acquisition Phase]
    
    E --> E1[Capture RAM and Memory Dump]
    E1 --> E2[Capture Network Connections ARP Routing Tables]
    E2 --> E3[Capture Running Processes and Logged-in Users]
    E3 --> E4[Capture Open Files and Clipboard]
    E4 --> G[Forensic-Safe Power-Down Pull Plug Directly]
    
    F --> F1[Connect Disk via Hardware Write-Blocker]
    F1 --> F2[Create Bit-Stream Image with dd or FTK Imager]
    F2 --> F3[Generate MD5 and SHA-256 Hashes]
    F3 --> H[Hash Verification Step]
    
    G --> H
    H --> I[Chain of Custody Documentation and Evidence Labeling]
    I --> J[Sealed Evidence Storage in Access-Controlled Locker]
    J --> K[Create Working Copies for Analysis]
    K --> L[File Carving and Deleted File Recovery]
    L --> M[Registry Parsing and Log Correlation]
    M --> N[Timeline Reconstruction across Sources]
    N --> O[Indicator of Compromise Identification]
    O --> P[Generate Forensic Report]
    P --> Q[Peer Review and Quality Check]
    Q --> R[Court Presentation and Testimony]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Order of Volatility Priority Stack

```mermaid

        P1B[CPU Cache L1 L2 L3]
    end

    end

        P3C[Kernel Statistics]
    end
    
    subgraph P4["Priority 4 - Hours"]
        P4A[Running Processes]
        P4B[Logged-in Users]
        P4C[Open File Handles]
    end

        P5A[Hard Disk Files]
        P5B[USB Storage]

    end
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Chain of Custody Lifecycle

```mermaid
    A2 -->|Transfer Logged| A3[Transport Vehicle]

    A5 -->|Analysis on Working Copy| A6[Forensic Workstation]

    A4 -->|Court Order| A7[Court Exhibit]
```
