
## 4.4 Architecture Diagram — Live vs. Post-Mortem Acquisition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LIVE["LIVE ACQUISITION MODE - System ON"]
        L1[Running Windows or Linux Host]
        L2[RAM Memory]
        L3[Network State]
        L4[Processes and Users]
        L1 --> L2
        L1 --> L3
        L1 --> L4
        L2 --> LEXT1[External USB - Hash Verified]
        L3 --> LEXT1
        L4 --> LEXT1
    end
    
    subgraph DEAD["POST-MORTEM ACQUISITION MODE - System OFF"]
        D1[Hard Disk or SSD]
        D2[Hardware Write-Blocker]
        D3[Forensic Workstation]
        D1 --> D2
        D2 --> D3
        D3 --> D4[Bit-Stream Image File]
        D4 --> D5[MD5 SHA-256 Hash Log]
    end
    
    LIVE --> POWEROFF[Forensic Safe Power-Down]
    POWEROFF --> DEAD
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "Digital Crime Scene Investigation Process" and list any four phases of the process.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The Digital Crime Scene Investigation Process is a systematic, forensically sound methodology used to identify, preserve, collect, analyze, and present digital evidence in a legally admissible manner. It ensures the integrity of evidence through proper documentation and chain of custody.
>
> The four phases are:
> 1. **Preparation** – Pre-incident readiness and toolkit staging.
> 2. **Identification** – Recognizing the digital scene and scoping evidence.
> 3. **Evidence Collection** – Live and post-mortem acquisition.
> 4. **Chain of Custody** – Documentation of every transfer of evidence.
>
> *(Alternative valid phases: Analysis, Presentation)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is the "Order of Volatility" in digital forensics? Arrange the following data types in descending order of volatility: Hard Disk Files, CPU Registers, RAM, Routing Tables, Logged-in Users, Backups.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The Order of Volatility (OOV) is the priority sequence in which digital evidence should be collected, based on how quickly the data disappears. Highly volatile data must be acquired first before it is lost.
>
> Descending order of volatility:
> 1. **CPU Registers** (nanoseconds)
> 2. **RAM** (seconds to minutes)
> 3. **Routing Tables** (seconds to minutes)
> 4. **Logged-in Users** (hours)
> 5. **Hard Disk Files** (days to years)
> 6. **Backups** (years)
>
> **Mnemonic**: "CRNLD-DPRT" — CPU, RAM, Network, Logged users, Disk, Prints, Remote logs, Topology.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Students often list the phases but do not explain *why* the order matters. Always justify with **Order of Volatility** and **chain of custody** reasoning.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Many students confuse *live acquisition* with *post-mortem acquisition* in terms of tools used. Remember: **write-blockers are used ONLY in post-mortem**.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Students forget to mention the **hash verification step** after imaging. This is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Chain of custody is often described in words but not as a **form with specific fields** (Case ID, Hash, Signature). Examiners expect tabular mention.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the six phases of the Digital Crime Scene Investigation Process in detail. For each phase, state the primary objective and list at least one key activity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the difference between **Live Acquisition** and **Post-Mortem Acquisition**. State two tools used for each and explain why a **hardware write-blocker** is mandatory in post-mortem imaging.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

> [!NOTE]
> The six phases are:
>
> **1. Preparation (Pre-Incident Readiness)**
> *Objective*: Ensure team, tools, and legal authority are ready before any incident occurs.
> *Key Activity*: Pre-staging forensic toolkit, securing search warrants, training investigators.
>
> **2. Identification (Scene Recognition)**
> *Objective*: Determine the scope of the digital crime scene.
> *Key Activity*: Photographing monitor screens, mapping network connections, listing all devices.
>
> **3. Evidence Collection (Acquisition)**
> *Objective*: Capture volatile and non-volatile evidence without altering it.
> *Key Activity*: RAM dump using FTK Imager; disk imaging using `dd` with write-blocker.
>
> **4. Chain of Custody (Documentation)**
> *Objective*: Maintain unbroken documentation of evidence handling.
> *Key Activity*: Filling evidence forms, generating MD5/SHA-256 hashes, signed transfer logs.
>
> **5. Analysis (Examination & Interpretation)**
> *Objective*: Extract probative information from acquired evidence.
> *Key Activity*: File carving, registry parsing, timeline correlation.
>
> **6. Presentation (Reporting & Testimony)**
> *Objective*: Communicate findings clearly to legal stakeholders.
> *Key Activity*: Writing the forensic report with executive summary and methodology, testifying in court.

#### Model Solution for (b):

> [!NOTE]
> **Live Acquisition** is performed on a *running* system to capture volatile data such as RAM, network connections, and running processes. Tools used: **FTK Imager (Memory module), WinPmem, `memdump`**.
>
> **Post-Mortem Acquisition** is performed on a *powered-off* system to capture non-volatile storage. Tools used: **FTK Imager (Disk module), `dd`, Guymager, EnCase**.
>
> **Block Diagram**:
>
> ```
> +----------------------+      +------------------------+
> | LIVE ACQUISITION     |      | POST-MORTEM ACQUISITION|
> |----------------------|      |------------------------|
> | Running System       |      | Powered-Off System     |
> |     |                |      |     |                  |
> |     v                |      |     v                  |
> | Capture RAM          |      | Disk --> Write-Blocker |
> | Capture Network      |      |     |                  |
> | Capture Processes    |      |     v                  |
> |     |                |      | Forensic Workstation   |
> |     v                |      |     |                  |
> | External USB         |      |     v                  |
> +----------------------+      | Bit-Stream Image       |
>                               | MD5 + SHA-256          |
>                               +------------------------+
> ```
>
> **Why write-blocker is mandatory in post-mortem imaging**:
> A hardware write-blocker is a device that sits between the suspect disk and the forensic workstation. It **electrically prevents any write command** from reaching the suspect disk. This guarantees that:
> 1. The original evidence is **not modified** during the imaging process.
> 2. The **forensic soundness** of the image is maintained.
> 3. The evidence remains **admissible in court**.
> 4. The hash of the source disk remains **identical** before and after acquisition.
>
> Without a write-blocker, even an accidental OS-level write (e.g., timestamp update) could destroy metadata and render evidence inadmissible.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **Chain of Custody**. List any six fields of a Chain of Custody form. Explain why a broken chain of custody can result in evidence being rejected by a court of law.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A forensic investigator is imaging a 500 GB hard disk using a USB 2.0 write-blocker that has a sustained write throughput of 30 MB/s. Estimate the **time required for the bit-stream image** to be created. Also calculate the **expected compressed image size** if the compression ratio is 0.6. Show all steps.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

> [!NOTE]
> **Chain of Custody** is the chronological, documented, and unbroken record of the seizure, custody, control, transfer, analysis, and disposition of evidence, from the moment it is collected to the moment it is presented in court.
>
> **Six fields of a Chain of Custody form**:
> 1. **Case ID** – Unique identifier of the case.
> 2. **Evidence ID** – Unique identifier of the specific evidence item.
> 3. **Date and Time of Collection** – With timezone.
> 4. **Collected By** – Name, badge number, and signature of the investigator.
> 5. **Hash Values** – MD5 and SHA-256 of the evidence.
> 6. **Transfer Log** – Each subsequent transfer with from/to, date/time, and signature.
>
> **Why a broken chain leads to rejection**:
> The chain of custody establishes **who, what, when, where, and how** the evidence was handled. If there is a gap — e.g., a period where the evidence was not in anyone's documented custody — the defense can argue that the evidence may have been **tampered with, switched, or contaminated**. Under the *best evidence rule* and the *reliability requirement* of the Indian Evidence Act (1872, Sections 65B and 45) and global equivalents, courts require the prosecution to prove the evidence is **authentic and untampered**. A broken chain breaks this proof, and the evidence is ruled **inadmissible**.

#### Model Solution for (b):

> [!NOTE]
> **Given**:
> - Source disk size: $S = 500 \text{ GB} = 500 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 512{,}000 \text{ MB}$
> - Sustained throughput: $R = 30 \text{ MB/s}$
> - Compression ratio: $C = 0.6$
>
> **Step 1: Calculate Uncompressed Image Size**
>
> $$\text{ImgSize}_{\text{uncomp}} = S + \text{Slack} + \text{BadSectors}$$
>
> For simplicity (assuming negligible slack and no bad sectors as per problem statement):
>
> $$\text{ImgSize}_{\text{uncomp}} \approx S = 512{,}000 \text{ MB}$$
>
> **Step 2: Calculate Time Required**
>
> $$T_{\text{image}} = \frac{\text{ImgSize}_{\text{uncomp}}}{R} = \frac{512{,}000 \text{ MB}}{30 \text{ MB/s}}$$
>
> $$T_{\text{image}} = 17{,}066.67 \text{ seconds}$$
>
> Converting to hours:
>
> $$T_{\text{image}} = \frac{17{,}066.67}{3600} \approx 4.74 \text{ hours}$$
>
> **Step 3: Calculate Compressed Image Size**
>
> $$\text{ImgSize}_{\text{comp}} = \text{ImgSize}_{\text{uncomp}} \times (1 - C)$$
>
> $$\text{ImgSize}_{\text{comp}} = 512{,}000 \times (1 - 0.6) = 512{,}000 \times 0.4 = 204{,}800 \text{ MB}$$
>
> Converting to GB:
>
> $$\text{ImgSize}_{\text{comp}} = \frac{204{,}800}{1024} = 200 \text{ GB}$$
>
> **Final Answer**:
> - Time for uncompressed bit-stream image ≈ **4.74 hours**
> - Compressed image size ≈ **200 GB**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Digital Crime Scene Investigation Process**
>
> - [x] The process is **systematic, methodical, and legally defensible** — never ad-hoc.
> - [x] Six phases: **P-I-E-C-A-P** (Preparation, Identification, Evidence Collection, Chain of Custody, Analysis, Presentation).
> - [x] **Preparation is pre-incident** — many students wrongly start the process at the scene.
> - [x] **Order of Volatility (OOV)**: CPU > RAM > Network > Users > Disk > Remote logs.
> - [x] **Live acquisition** = running system, captures volatile data, uses tools like WinPmem/FTK.
> - [x] **Post-mortem acquisition** = powered-off, uses **hardware write-blocker**, captures non-volatile data.
> - [x] **Write-blocker is mandatory** in post-mortem to prevent any modification of source disk.
> - [x] **Hash verification** (MD5 + SHA-256) is performed **before and after** every transfer.
> - [x] **Chain of Custody** must be **unbroken, signed, and timestamped** for every evidence item.
> - [x] **Bit-stream image** captures *every bit* including slack, unallocated, and bad sectors.
> - [x] **Analysis is done on working copies**, never on the original.
> - [x] **Forensic-safe power-down** = pull the plug, *not* OS shutdown.
> - [x] **Report must have**: Executive Summary, Methodology, Findings, Timeline, Conclusion.
> - [x] **Standard Reference**: ISO/IEC 27037:2012.
> - [x] **Legal Framework**: Indian Evidence Act Sections 65B (electronic evidence) and 45 (expert opinion).
> - [x] **Key Formula — Image Time**: $T = \frac{S_{\text{source}}}{R_{\text{throughput}}}$
> - [x] **Key Formula — Compressed Size**: $\text{Size}_{\text{comp}} = S_{\text{source}} \times (1 - C)$
> - [x] **Key Formula — Integrity**: $H_{\text{original}}(M) = H_{\text{current}}(M)$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the context of **Digital Forensics**, **Data Analysis** is formally defined as the systematic, methodological examination, evaluation, and interpretation of acquired digital evidence using validated forensic tools, statistical techniques, and logical reasoning to reconstruct events, identify perpetrators, and produce legally admissible findings. It represents the **fourth phase** of the standard Digital Forensic Investigation Process (following Identification, Preservation, and Acquisition) and the critical pre-reporting stage where raw bytes are transformed into meaningful intelligence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Data Analysis is the process of examining, correlating, and interpreting the collected digital evidence to extract meaningful information about the incident, identify patterns, reconstruct timelines, and derive conclusions that can be presented in a court of law.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **crime scene investigator (CSI)** walking into a chaotic room after a burglary. The room is full of dust, fingerprints, overturned furniture, and a knocked-over coffee cup. The investigator does not "see" the crime immediately — instead, they perform a **systematic analysis**:
- They dust for fingerprints (**artifact extraction**)
- They check the broken glass pattern (**file analysis**)
- They look at the security camera timestamps (**timeline analysis**)
- They cross-reference fingerprints with a criminal database (**correlation**)
- They interview witnesses to validate findings (**validation**)

**Data Analysis in Digital Forensics is the digital equivalent of this CSI process.** The "crime scene" is a hard drive, RAM dump, or network capture filled with millions of files, logs, and hidden data. The investigator (forensic analyst) uses specialized tools to sift through the noise, find the signal, and reconstruct "what happened, when, how, and by whom."

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Forensic Metrics Used in Data Analysis:**
> - **Hash Value (Digital Fingerprint):** A unique fixed-size alphanumeric string (e.g., MD5 = **128 bits / 32 hex characters**, SHA-1 = **160 bits / 40 hex characters**, SHA-256 = **256 bits / 64 hex characters**).
> - **Timestamps:** Recorded in **UTC (Coordinated Universal Time)** to maintain forensic integrity across time zones.
> - **Slack Space:** The unused space between the end of a file's logical content and the end of its last disk cluster (typically **0 to 4095 bytes** for NTFS).
> - **File Signature (Magic Number):** A constant numerical or text value used to identify file types (e.g., `FF D8 FF` for JPEG, `50 4B 03 04` for ZIP/DOCX).

### 1.4 Categories of Data Analysis in Forensics

| Category | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **File Analysis** | Examining file content, metadata, structure | PDF metadata, EXIF data in images |
| **Log Analysis** | Parsing system, application, and security logs | Windows Event Logs, `/var/log/auth.log` |
| **Registry Analysis** (Windows) | Inspecting Windows Registry hives for user activity | `NTUSER.DAT`, `SAM` hive |
| **Timeline Analysis** | Chronological reconstruction of events | Super-timeline with `log2timeline` |
| **Keyword/Pattern Analysis** | Searching for specific strings or regex patterns | Email addresses, credit card numbers |
| **Data Carving** | Recovering files based on signatures without filesystem metadata | Recovering JPEGs from unallocated space |
| **Network Analysis** | Examining packet captures and connection logs | Wireshark PCAP analysis |
| **Memory Analysis** | Analyzing RAM dumps for volatile artifacts | Volatility framework output |
| **Email Analysis** | Parsing mailbox files for communications | PST, OST, MBOX analysis |
| **Browser Forensics** | History, cookies, cache, downloads extraction | Chrome, Firefox, Edge artifacts |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Logical Phases of Forensic Data Analysis

The data analysis phase is not a single monolithic step; it is a structured, iterative process. Below is the detailed breakdown that examiners follow:

1. **Data Reduction (Triage)**
   - The forensic image may be terabytes in size. The analyst must filter irrelevant data to focus on case-relevant artifacts.
   - **How:** Apply keyword filters, hash filters (against NSRL — National Software Reference Library), and date-range filters.
   - **Why:** Reduces analysis time from weeks to days and minimizes cognitive overload.

2. **Data Extraction (Artifact Isolation)**
   - Identify and extract specific forensic artifacts relevant to the investigation (e.g., Registry keys, browser history databases, prefetch files, shellbags, LNK files).
   - **How:** Use tools like Autopsy, FTK, EnCase, X-Ways, or command-line utilities.
   - **Why:** These artifacts are the "ground truth" of user and system behavior.

3. **Data Correlation (Cross-Referencing)**
   - Link extracted artifacts to form a coherent narrative. For example, a Registry `UserAssist` entry showing `cmd.exe` execution at 02:30 AM can be correlated with a PowerShell script found in the user's Downloads folder, which can be further correlated with an outbound network connection in the firewall log.
   - **How:** Timeline alignment, event sequencing, entity relationship mapping.
   - **Why:** A single artifact is weak evidence; multiple correlated artifacts form a legally robust chain.

4. **Data Interpretation (Meaning Derivation)**
   - Translate technical findings into human-understandable conclusions. For instance, the Registry key `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist\{GUID}\Count\{ROT13}\HRZR_EHACNGU:P:\Qbphzragf\...` decoded from ROT13 becomes `C:\Users\...` and indicates a program was executed.
   - **How:** Apply domain knowledge, decode obfuscated values, interpret timestamps considering timezone and clock skew.

5. **Validation & Reporting (Truth Verification)**
   - Verify findings through alternative tools or methods to eliminate tool-specific artifacts or false positives.
   - **How:** Cross-verify with at least two independent tools; check hash integrity; peer review.
   - **Why:** Court admissibility requires verifiable, repeatable results.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** The data analysis phase operates under the **ACPO (Association of Chief Police Officers) Principle 3** — *"An audit trail or other record of all processes applied to digital evidence should be created and preserved. An independent third party should be able to examine those processes and achieve the same result."*

### 2.2 Key Analytical Frameworks

**A. The Locard's Exchange Principle (Adapted to Digital)**
- Originally a physical forensic principle: *"Every contact leaves a trace."*
- In digital forensics: Every user action leaves digital traces across the OS, applications, and network. The analyst's job is to find these traces.
- **Manifestation:** A user opening a file modifies: `$MFT` (Master File Table), `$LogFile`, `$UsnJrnl`, Prefetch, Registry (`UserAssist`, `RecentDocs`), Shellbags, and LNK files.

**B. The Daubert Standard (Legal Admissibility)**
- For evidence to be admissible, the methodology must be:
  - Tested and peer-reviewed
  - Known or potential error rate known
  - Standards exist and were followed
  - Generally accepted in the relevant community

**C. Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)**
- Data must be collected in order of volatility:
  1. CPU registers, cache (nanoseconds)
  2. Routing table, ARP cache, process table (seconds)
  3. Kernel statistics, memory (minutes)
  4. Temporary file systems (hours)
  5. Disk files (days)
  6. Remote logs, backups (years)

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the key mathematical and logical relationships used in forensic data analysis. All formulas are exam-critical under the KTU 2024 scheme.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hash Probability of Collision (Birthday Paradox)** | $P \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / (2 \cdot H)}$ | $n$ = number of hashes, $H$ = hash space size |
| 2 | **MD5 Hash Length** | $L_{MD5} = 128$ | **bits** = 32 hex chars |
| 3 | **SHA-1 Hash Length** | $L_{SHA1} = 160$ | **bits** = 40 hex chars |
| 4 | **SHA-256 Hash Length** | $L_{SHA256} = 256$ | **bits** = 64 hex chars |
| 5 | **Slack Space (per file)** | $S_{slack} = C - (F \bmod C)$ | $C$ = cluster size, $F$ = file size |
| 6 | **Cluster Size (Default NTFS)** | $C_{NTFS} = 4096$ | **bytes** (4 KB) |
| 7 | **File Slack (RAM + Disk Slack)** | $S_{file} = S_{RAM} + S_{disk}$ | $S_{RAM} = C - (F \bmod C)$, $S_{disk} = (F \div C + 1) \cdot C - F$ |
| 8 | **Data Reduction Ratio** | $R_{reduction} = \dfrac{V_{relevant}}{V_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of useful data |
| 9 | **Timestamp Conversion (Unix Epoch)** | $T_{UTC} = T_{epoch} + 11644473600$ | Seconds offset between Windows FILETIME (1601) and Unix (1970) |
| 10 | **Mean Time Between Artifacts** | $\overline{\Delta t} = \dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} (t_{i+1} - t_i)$ | $N$ = number of events |
| 11 | **Evidence Confidence Score** | $C_{score} = w_1 A_1 + w_2 A_2 + \dots + w_n A_n$ | $w_i$ = weight, $A_i$ = artifact corroboration (0-1) |
| 12 | **Storage Capacity Estimation** | $V = S \times C \times H \times 512$ | Bytes (where $S$ = sectors, $C$ = cylinders, $H$ = heads) |
| 13 | **Entropy of Data (Randomness)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Bits/symbol; **8** = max for bytes (encrypted/compressed) |
| 14 | **Anomaly Detection Threshold** | $\vert x_i - \mu \vert > k \cdot \sigma$ | $k$ typically = **2** or **3** (standard deviations) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pitfall:** Students often confuse **File Slack** and **Slack Space**. Remember: **Slack Space = Sector Slack + File Slack (RAM Slack + Disk Slack)**. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Incident Response (IR):** SOC analysts use data analysis techniques to identify Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) and determine the scope of a breach.
- **Law Enforcement:** Criminal investigations rely on timeline analysis to establish alibis or place suspects at digital "scenes."
- **Corporate E-Discovery:** Legal teams analyze email and document repositories to find relevant evidence for litigation.
- **Malware Analysis:** Reverse engineers correlate Registry changes, file drops, and network connections produced by malicious code.
- **Insider Threat Detection:** Behavioral analysis of user activity logs to detect data exfiltration or policy violations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Slack Space Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A file of size $F = 5300$ bytes is stored on an NTFS volume with cluster size $C = 4096$ bytes. Calculate the **RAM Slack** and **Disk Slack** for this file.

**Step 1 — Determine the number of full clusters used by the file's data.**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{full\_clusters} &= \lfloor F / C \rfloor \\[4pt]
&= \lfloor 5300 \, / \, 4096 \rfloor \\[4pt]
&= \lfloor 1.2939 \dots \rfloor \\[4pt]
&= 1 \text{ full cluster}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Logic:* The file occupies 1 complete cluster (4096 bytes) and spills over into a second cluster for the remaining 1204 bytes.

**Step 2 — Calculate the RAM Slack (bytes between end of file logical content and end of its last cluster).**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{RAM} &= C - (F \bmod C) \\[4pt]
&= 4096 - (5300 \bmod 4096) \\[4pt]
&= 4096 - 1204 \\[4pt]
&= 2892 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Logic:* Within the second (partial) cluster, only 1204 bytes are used by the file. The remaining 2892 bytes are "wasted" space where the OS writes RAM contents — a classic anti-forensics hiding place for adversaries.

**Step 3 — Calculate the Disk Slack (unused sectors between the file's last byte and the end of the last sector).**

For simplicity (assuming sector size = 512 bytes and the file ends mid-sector within the partial cluster):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Bytes used in partial cluster} &= 1204 \\[4pt]
\text{Bytes remaining in last sector} &= 512 - (1204 \bmod 512) = 512 - 180 = 332 \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
\text{Number of full unused sectors after last byte} &= \lfloor (2892 - 332) \, / \, 512 \rfloor = \lfloor 5 \, \rfloor = 5 \\[4pt]
S_{disk} &= 332 + (5 \times 512) = 332 + 2560 = 2892 \text{ bytes (in this simple case, equals RAM slack)}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Logic:* Disk slack = (sector slack) + (unused sectors). On older FAT systems, this could contain data from previously deleted files.

**Step 4 — Total Slack Space.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= S_{RAM} + S_{disk} = 2892 + 2892 = 5784 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Final Answer:* A 5300-byte file actually consumes $4096 + 4096 = 8192$ bytes on disk, leaving 2892 bytes of slack space (which can hide residual RAM/disk data).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Windows FILETIME to Human-Readable Timestamp

**Problem Statement:** A Windows Registry `LastWriteTime` value reads `0x01D6E0B5C9F2A3B0` (hexadecimal). Convert this to a human-readable date-time.

**Step 1 — Recognize the format.**

Windows FILETIME is a **64-bit integer** representing the number of **100-nanosecond intervals** since **January 1, 1601 (00:00:00 UTC)**.

$$T_{filetime} = 0x01D6E0B5C9F2A3B0 = 132,646,872,345,678,900 \text{ (in 100-ns intervals)}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to seconds by dividing by 10,000,000 (since $1 \text{ s} = 10^7 \times 100$ ns).**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{seconds} &= T_{filetime} \, / \, 10{,}000{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 132{,}646{,}872{,}345{,}678{,}900 \, / \, 10{,}000{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 13{,}264{,}687{,}234.56789 \text{ seconds since 1601-01-01}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the Windows-to-Unix Epoch offset (11644473600 seconds).**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{epoch} &= T_{seconds} - 11{,}644{,}473{,}600 \\[4pt]
&= 13{,}264{,}687{,}234.56789 - 11{,}644{,}473{,}600 \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}620{,}213{,}634.56789 \text{ seconds since 1970-01-01}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to UTC date-time.**

Using standard Unix conversion: $1{,}620{,}213{,}634$ seconds since epoch corresponds to:

$$\boxed{T_{UTC} = \text{May 15, 2021, 14:00:34 UTC}}$$

> *Logic:* This 3-step conversion is mandatory for any KTU question involving Windows timestamps. Examiners may give the raw hex value and expect the candidate to perform all 3 conversions.

---

### 3.3 Forensic Data Analysis — Analytical Decision Matrix

The following matrix is a high-yield reference for the KTU 2024 examination. It maps **artifacts** to the **type of evidence** they provide and the **analysis technique** applied.

| Artifact | OS | Type of Evidence | Analysis Technique | Tool Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `NTUSER.DAT` Registry Hive | Windows | User preferences, recently used programs, typed URLs | Registry parsing, key enumeration | Registry Explorer, RegRipper |
| `SAM` Hive | Windows | User account info, password hashes, login times | Hive extraction & parsing | `samdump2`, pypykatz |
| `$MFT` ($MFT) | Windows (NTFS) | File names, timestamps, sizes, paths | MFT entry parsing, `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` & `$FILE_NAME` attribute analysis | `analyzeMFT`, MFTExplorer |
| `$UsnJrnl` | Windows (NTFS) | File creation, modification, rename, deletion at volume level | USN Record parsing | `usnjrnl` parser, Plaso |
| Prefetch Files (`*.pf`) | Windows | Program execution evidence (first/last run, run count) | Prefetch parser | `PECmd`, Windows Prefetch Parser |
| LNK Files (`*.lnk`) | Windows | Recently accessed files including from network shares | LNK metadata extraction | `LECmd`, LECmd |
| Event Logs (`.evtx`) | Windows | System, Application, Security events | EVTX parsing, Event ID correlation | `evtx_dump`, `wevtx`, Hayabusa |
| Shellbags | Windows | Folder access history (even deleted folders) | Shellbag registry key parsing | `SBECmd`, ShellBags Explorer |
| `~/.bash_history` | Linux | Commands executed by the user | Line-by-line review, timestamp extraction | `cat`, `last`, `utmp` |
| `/var/log/auth.log` | Linux | Authentication attempts, sudo usage | Log parsing, failed/successful login correlation | `auth.log` viewer, `grep` |
| `/var/log/wtmp` | Linux | Login/logout records | `last` and `lastb` commands | `utmpdump` |
| `/var/log/syslog` | Linux | General system messages, service events | Pattern matching, log timeline | `journalctl`, `syslog-ng` |
| `~/.viminfo` | Linux | Vim editor history (search strings, commands) | File analysis | `cat`, `strings` |
| Cron Jobs (`/etc/crontab`, `/var/spool/cron/`) | Linux | Scheduled task evidence | Crontab parsing | `crontab -l`, `anacron` |
| Browser SQLite DBs (`places.sqlite`, `History`) | Cross-platform | Browsing history, downloads, bookmarks | SQL queries on `moz_*`, `chrome_*` tables | `sqlite3`, `Hindsight` |
| Email Files (`.pst`, `.ost`, `.mbox`) | Cross-platform | Communication evidence | Email header parsing, attachment extraction | `pffexport`, Aid4Mail, Paraben E3 |
| Memory Dump (`.raw`, `.mem`, `.dmp`) | Cross-platform | Volatile state, processes, network connections, malware | Memory forensics framework | **Volatility 2/3**, MemProcFS |
| Disk Image (`.dd`, `.E01`, `.raw`) | Cross-platform | Entire filesystem snapshot | Mounting, carving, indexing | **Autopsy**, FTK Imager, X-Ways |
| Page File (`pagefile.sys`) | Windows | Swapped-out memory contents | Strings extraction, keyword search | `strings`, Bulk Extractor |
| Hibernation File (`hiberfil.sys`) | Windows | Compressed memory at hibernation | Decompression & memory analysis | `volatility -f hiberfil.sys` |

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Constructing a Forensic Timeline (Methodology)

A timeline is the **single most powerful analytical artifact** in digital forensics. Below is the KTU-grade methodology.

**Step 1 — Extract timestamps from all relevant artifacts.**
Parse $MFT, $LogFile, $UsnJrnl, Event Logs, Registry hives, browser history, email headers, and prefetch files. Each parser outputs a CSV/timeline file.

**Step 2 — Normalize all timestamps to UTC.**
Convert Windows FILETIME and Unix epoch values to a common UTC format (`YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS`).

**Step 3 — Merge and sort all events chronologically.**
Use a tool like **Plaso (`log2timeline`)** to create a single super-timeline. Example command:

```bash
log2timeline.py --storage-file timeline.plaso /cases/evidence.E01
psort.py -w timeline.csv timeline.plaso
```

**Step 4 — Apply filters to the timeline.**
- **Date range filter:** Focus on the incident window.
- **Keyword filter:** Highlight events mentioning user names, IP addresses, filenames of interest.
- **Event type filter:** Isolate `EXECUTION` (prefetch, run keys) from `FILE_MODIFICATION` (MFT) events.

**Step 5 — Visualize and correlate.**
Plot the filtered timeline using tools like **Timesketch** or a spreadsheet. Identify clusters of activity, gaps, and anomalous events (e.g., logins at 3 AM, mass file deletions, unusual outbound connections).

**Step 6 — Document and validate.**
Save the filtered timeline as evidence. Independently re-run the analysis using a second tool to verify the findings (ACPO Principle).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**
*Define data analysis in the context of digital forensics. List any four categories of data analysis with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Data analysis in digital forensics is the systematic examination and interpretation of acquired digital evidence using forensic tools and techniques to identify, correlate, and reconstruct events of investigative relevance. It is the phase where raw data is transformed into actionable intelligence.

**Four Categories with Examples (2 Marks — ½ mark each):**

| # | Category | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **File Analysis** | Analyzing PDF metadata to identify the author and creation software |
| 2 | **Log Analysis** | Parsing Windows Security Event Log (`.evtx`) for failed logon events (Event ID 4625) |
| 3 | **Registry Analysis** | Examining `NTUSER.DAT` for `UserAssist` keys to determine recently executed programs |
| 4 | **Timeline Analysis** | Constructing a super-timeline using `log2timeline/Plaso` to reconstruct user activity chronologically |

*Other acceptable categories:* Memory Analysis, Network Analysis, Email Analysis, Browser Forensics, Data Carving, Keyword Analysis.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**
*What is slack space? Differentiate between RAM slack and disk slack with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Slack Space Definition (1 Mark):**
Slack space is the unused area in a disk cluster between the end of a file's logical content and the end of the cluster allocated to that file. On NTFS, the default cluster size is **4096 bytes (4 KB)**.

**RAM Slack (1 Mark):**
RAM slack is the space between the **end of the file's logical data** and the **end of the last sector** within the cluster occupied by the file. The OS may fill this with whatever data was present in RAM at write time (e.g., fragments of previously processed data, memory contents).

**Disk Slack (1 Mark):**
Disk slack is the space consisting of **entire unused sectors** that exist between the file's last sector and the end of the last cluster. It may contain residual data from previously deleted files that occupied those sectors.

**Example:**
A file of size **5000 bytes** on a volume with 4096-byte clusters and 512-byte sectors:
- Occupies 2 clusters (8192 bytes total).
- **RAM Slack** = $4096 - (5000 \bmod 4096) = 4096 - 904 = 3192$ bytes.
- **Disk Slack** = (remaining portion in last sector) + (full unused sectors after).

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam — Module 1, 14 Marks]**
*(a) Explain the different phases of forensic data analysis in detail with a block diagram. (7 Marks)*
*(b) Describe the Windows Registry analysis process. What are the key hives and their forensic significance? (7 Marks)*

### Model Answer — Q3(A)(a): Phases of Forensic Data Analysis (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Forensic data analysis is the **fourth phase** of the digital investigation process (per the NIST SP 800-86 framework), following identification, preservation, and acquisition. It transforms the acquired bitstream into meaningful, court-admissible intelligence.

**The Five Phases (5 Marks — 1 mark each):**

**1. Data Reduction (Triage):**
- The acquired image is often enormous (multi-terabyte). Reduction filters out irrelevant data using:
  - **Hash filtering:** Compare file hashes against the **NSRL (National Software Reference Library)** to eliminate known OS/application files.
  - **File-type filtering:** Focus on user-generated content (documents, images, emails) and exclude system files.
  - **Date-range filtering:** Limit analysis to the incident window.
  - **Keyword/Entity filtering:** Pre-define target names, IP addresses, or financial data to search for.

**2. Data Extraction (Artifact Isolation):**
- Extract specific forensic artifacts using specialized tools:
  - **Windows:** `NTUSER.DAT`, `SAM`, `SECURITY`, `SOFTWARE`, `SYSTEM` hives; `$MFT`; `Event Logs (EVTX)`; Prefetch files; LNK files; Jump Lists; Shellbags; Recycle Bin; Volume Shadow Copies.
  - **Linux:** `bash_history`, `auth.log`, `wtmp`, `syslog`, `cron`, `viminfo`, `~/.ssh/`, process accounting files.
  - **Email:** `.pst`, `.ost`, `.mbox`; SMTP headers; attachments.
  - **Memory:** Processes, network connections, loaded DLLs, registry keys in memory.

**3. Data Correlation (Cross-Referencing):**
- Link extracted artifacts into a coherent event chain. Example correlation chain:
  - **Registry `UserAssist`** (program execution) → **Prefetch file** (run count/duration) → **`$MFT` entry** (file creation timestamp) → **Event Log 4688** (process creation with command line) → **Firewall log** (outbound connection) → **Memory dump** (malicious process tree).
- This multi-artifact correlation is what makes digital evidence **legally robust** — a single artifact is weak, but corroboration is strong.

**4. Data Interpretation (Meaning Derivation):**
- Apply domain knowledge to translate raw technical data into investigative conclusions. Examples:
  - Decoding ROT13-obfuscated Registry `UserAssist` values.
  - Interpreting `LastWriteTime` in the context of time zones and daylight saving.
  - Distinguishing legitimate user activity from malware behavior (e.g., `powershell.exe -enc` is suspicious; `notepad.exe` opening a text file is benign).
  - Identifying anti-forensics: timestamp stomping, log clearing, secure deletion attempts.

**5. Validation & Reporting:**
- Cross-verify findings using **at least two independent tools** (e.g., Autopsy + X-Ways).
- Apply the **Daubert Standard** test for admissibility.
- Document every step in a chain-of-custody record.
- Generate a final forensic report with executive summary, methodology, findings, and supporting evidence references.

**Block Diagram (1 Mark):**

```
[Acquired Image] → [Reduction] → [Extraction] → [Correlation] → [Interpretation] → [Validation] → [Report]
                         ↑__________________________ Feedback Loop (Refinement) __________________________|
```

**Conclusion (Optional, for completeness):**
The five phases are not strictly linear; analysts iterate between correlation and interpretation as new findings emerge.

---

### Model Answer — Q3(A)(b): Windows Registry Analysis (7 Marks)

**Introduction to Windows Registry (1 Mark):**
The Windows Registry is a **hierarchical database** that stores low-level settings for the OS and applications. It is one of the richest sources of forensic evidence in Windows. The Registry is physically stored in files called **hives**, located in `C:\Windows\System32\config\` and user profiles.

**Key Hives and Their Forensic Significance (4 Marks — 1 per hive):**

| Hive File | Path | Forensic Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **SAM** | `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\SAM` | Stores **user account information**, including usernames, user RIDs, password hashes (LM/NTLM), and account policies. Critical for **credential recovery** and identifying local accounts. |
| **SYSTEM** | `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\SYSTEM` | Stores **system-wide configuration**: hardware, drivers, services, network configuration, USB device history (`USBSTOR` key), and **computer name**. Essential for identifying connected USB devices — a common requirement in data exfiltration cases. |
| **SOFTWARE** | `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\SOFTWARE` | Stores **installed application settings**, Windows version, network shares, and installed services. Useful for identifying installed software, malware persistence (services key), and user preferences. |
| **SECURITY** | `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\SECURITY` | Stores **security policies** and audit configurations. Less frequently used in routine cases but valuable for understanding the security posture. |
| **NTUSER.DAT** | `C:\Users\<username>\NTUSER.DAT` | The most forensic-rich hive. Stores **per-user settings**: `UserAssist` (program execution), `Run/RunOnce` (persistence), `TypedURLs` (URLs typed in browsers), `RecentDocs` (recently opened files), `TypedPaths` (paths typed in Explorer), `ComDlg32\LastVisitedPidlMRU` (recently accessed files via Open dialog). |
| **UsrClass.dat** | `C:\Users\<username>\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Windows\UsrClass.dat` | Stores **user-specific class registrations** and **Shellbags** — which reveal folder access history, even for folders on network shares or that have been deleted. |

**Registry Analysis Process (2 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Acquisition:** Use FTK Imager, Registry Explorer, or `reg save` (on a live system) to export hive files. Calculate and verify SHA-256 hashes before and after.

**Step 2 — Loading & Parsing:** Load hives into a parser such as **Registry Explorer (Eric Zimmerman)**, **RegRipper**, or **Autopsy**. Use plugins/RECmd to enumerate specific keys.

**Step 3 — Key Artifacts to Extract:**

- **UserAssist:** `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist\{GUID}\Count` — Decoded ROT13 shows executed programs with run count and timestamps.
- **Run/RunOnce Keys:** `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` — Reveals **persistence mechanisms** (malware auto-start locations).
- **TypedURLs:** `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\TypedURLs` — URLs typed into Internet Explorer.
- **RecentDocs:** `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\RecentDocs` — Recently opened files.
- **USBSTOR:** `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR` — USB device connection history with serial numbers and timestamps.
- **Shellbags:** Located in `UsrClass.dat` — Folder navigation history.
- **WordWheelQuery / TypedPaths:** `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\TypedPaths` — Paths typed into Explorer address bar.
- **BAM (Background Activity Moderator):** `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\bam\State\UserSettings\<SID>` — Tracks executable paths that have been recently active (Windows 10+).

**Step 4 — Timeline Construction:** Each Registry key has a **LastWriteTime** timestamp. By parsing key LastWriteTimes, an analyst can construct a detailed user activity timeline. Registry timestamps are in **Windows FILETIME (100-nanosecond intervals since 1601-01-01)** and must be converted to UTC for human reading.

**Step 5 — Documentation:** Record all findings, tool versions, hive hashes, and interpretations in the case log.

**Conclusion (Optional):**
The Windows Registry is often called the **"heart of Windows forensics"** because it contains unparalleled evidence of user activity, system configuration, and persistence mechanisms. Every Registry hive must be parsed exhaustively in any serious investigation.

---

### Model Answer — Q3(B): Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

*(a) Discuss in detail the various categories of forensic data analysis. For each category, mention the data sources, the analytical technique, and at least one tool used. (7 Marks)*

*(b) Explain the concept of timeline analysis. With a suitable example, describe the steps involved in constructing a forensic super-timeline using Plaso/log2timeline. (7 Marks)*

### Model Answer — Q3(B)(a): Categories of Forensic Data Analysis (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Forensic data analysis is a multi-pronged discipline. Modern investigations use **10+ categories** of analysis, each targeting a specific class of evidence. An investigator must apply all relevant categories to ensure thoroughness.

**Categories (6 Marks — pick any 6, 1 mark each):**

**1. File System Analysis**
- **Data Sources:** `$MFT`, `$LogFile`, `$UsnJrnl`, directory entries, file metadata, $STANDARD_INFORMATION, $FILE_NAME attributes, alternate data streams (ADS).
- **Technique:** MFT entry parsing, timeline of file creation/modification/access (MAC times), identification of deleted files from unallocated MFT entries, ADS detection.
- **Tools:** `analyzeMFT`, MFTExplorer, Autopsy, FTK, Sleuth Kit (`fls`, `icat`).

**2. Registry Analysis (Windows)**
- **Data Sources:** `SAM`, `SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`, `SECURITY`, `NTUSER.DAT`, `UsrClass.dat`.
- **Technique:** Hive parsing, key enumeration, LastWriteTime extraction, ROT13 decoding, persistence mechanism identification.
- **Tools:** Registry Explorer, RegRipper, `RECmd`, Autopsy Registry plugin.

**3. Log Analysis**
- **Data Sources:** Windows Event Logs (`.evtx`), Linux `syslog`, `auth.log`, `wtmp`, application logs, firewall logs, web server logs, IDS/IPS logs.
- **Technique:** Pattern matching, Event ID correlation, anomaly detection, timeline alignment, failed/successful login correlation.
- **Tools:** `evtx_dump`, Hayabusa, `journalctl`, Splunk, ELK Stack, Kibana.

**4. Memory (RAM) Analysis**
- **Data Sources:** Memory dump files (`.raw`, `.mem`, `.lime`, `.dmp`), `hiberfil.sys`, `pagefile.sys`, crash dumps.
- **Technique:** Process enumeration, network connection analysis, loaded DLL inspection, kernel module listing, registry key reconstruction, malware detection.
- **Tools:** **Volatility 2/3**, MemProcFS, Rekall, WinDbg (for crash dumps).

**5. Network Analysis**
- **Data Sources:** PCAP files, NetFlow records, firewall logs, IDS alerts, DNS query logs, proxy logs.
- **Technique:** Packet inspection, protocol decoding, IOC extraction (IPs, domains, hashes), communication pattern analysis.
- **Tools:** **Wireshark**, NetworkMiner, TShark, tcpdump, Zeek (Bro).

**6. Email Analysis**
- **Data Sources:** `.pst`, `.ost`, `.mbox`, `.eml`, webmail caches, SMTP headers.
- **Technique:** Header parsing (trace the path), attachment extraction, phishing URL extraction, communication graph construction.
- **Tools:** Aid4Mail, Paraben E3, `pffexport`, Thunderbird, MailXaminer.

**7. Browser Forensics**
- **Data Sources:** Browser profile directories containing `History`, `places.sqlite`, `Cookies`, `Login Data`, `Cache`, `Downloads`, extensions data.
- **Technique:** SQL queries on history/cookies tables, cache file carving, extension artifact analysis, downloads reconstruction.
- **Tools:** **Hindsight**, `sqlite3`, BrowsingHistoryView, ChromeCacheView, BrowserHistoryView.

**8. Data Carving & Unallocated Space Analysis**
- **Data Sources:** Unallocated clusters, file slack, memory dumps, swap/page files, bad clusters.
- **Technique:** File signature-based carving (header/footer matching), entropy analysis to detect encrypted/compressed data, string extraction.
- **Tools:** **PhotoRec**, **Scalpel**, **Foremost**, Bulk Extractor, `binwalk`.

**9. Malware Analysis (Triage Level)**
- **Data Sources:** Suspicious executables, scheduled tasks, services, autorun keys, dropped files.
- **Technique:** Static analysis (strings, imports, sections), dynamic analysis (sandbox execution), behavioral indicators (file/registry/network changes).
- **Tools:** **PEiD**, DIE (Detect It Easy), `strings`, **Cuckoo Sandbox**, ANY.RUN, VirusTotal, YARA rules.

**10. Cloud & Mobile Forensics (Emerging)**
- **Data Sources:** Cloud storage APIs (OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox), mobile device backups (iTunes, ADB), app databases.
- **Technique:** API-based acquisition, database parsing, app-specific artifact extraction.
- **Tools:** UFED, Cellebrite, MSAB, Magnet AXIOM, Cloud-aware Autopsy modules.

**Conclusion (Optional):**
A complete investigation uses **multiple categories in parallel**, with results cross-correlated. No single category is sufficient.

---

### Model Answer — Q3(B)(b): Timeline Analysis & Super-Timeline Construction (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Timeline analysis is the process of chronologically ordering digital events derived from file system metadata, logs, Registry, browser history, and other artifacts. The goal is to reconstruct **"what happened, in what order, and at what time"** on the suspect system. A **super-timeline** consolidates events from multiple sources into a single, unified, time-ordered dataset.

**Why Timeline Analysis is Critical (1 Mark):**
- Provides the **narrative spine** of the investigation.
- Reveals **cause-effect relationships** that are invisible in isolated artifacts.
- Identifies **gaps** (potential log tampering) and **clusters** (rapid sequential events suggesting automated activity or attack stages).
- Standard practice in **incident response** and **criminal forensics**.

**Key Timestamps Used in Windows Forensics (1 Mark):**
- **$STANDARD_INFORMATION** ($SI) attribute: Modified by user actions (can be tampered with).
- **$FILE_NAME** ($FN) attribute: Updated by the OS at creation/rename; **cannot be directly tampered with** — provides reliable timestamps.
- **$LogFile**: NTFS journal; records metadata changes.
- **$UsnJrnl**: Change journal; records file-level changes.
- **Registry LastWriteTime**: Per-key timestamps.
- **Event Log `TimeCreated`**: When the event was generated by the system.

**Step-by-Step Construction of a Super-Timeline using Plaso (3 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Create a Plaso storage file from the forensic image:**
```bash
log2timeline.py --storage-file case01.plaso /cases/evidence.E01
```
*This single command invokes all Plaso parsers (MFT, EVTX, Registry, browser history, $UsnJrnl, prefetch, etc.) and stores the consolidated events in a single SQLite-backed file.*

**Step 2 — Export the timeline to CSV for analysis:**
```bash
psort.py -w timeline.csv case01.plaso
```
*The output CSV has columns: `datetime, timestamp_desc, source, source_short, data_type, message`.*

**Step 3 — Apply filters using `psort`:**
- **Date range:**
  ```bash
  psort.py -o l2tcsv -w filtered.csv "date > '2024-01-15' AND date < '2024-01-20'" case01.plaso
  ```
- **Keyword search (e.g., for "powershell"):**
  ```bash
  psort.py -o l2tcsv -w powershell_events.csv "message contains 'powershell'" case01.plaso
  ```

**Step 4 — Visualize using Timesketch (web-based) or analyze in spreadsheet/Timeline Explorer.**

**Illustrative Example Timeline (1 Mark):**

A truncated super-timeline showing a ransomware attack:

| Date-Time (UTC) | Source | Event |
|---|---|---|
| 2024-01-18 02:14:22 | EVTX (Security 4624) | Successful logon for user `admin` from IP 10.0.0.99 |
| 2024-01-18 02:15:03 | EVTX (Security 4688) | Process created: `powershell.exe -enc <base64>` |
| 2024-01-18 02:15:11 | $MFT | File created: `C:\Users\admin\Desktop\decryptor.exe` |
| 2024-01-18 02:15:30 | Prefetch | `decryptor.exe` first execution recorded |
| 2024-01-18 02:16:00 | $UsnJrnl | Mass file rename events (`.docx → .docx.locked`) |
| 2024-01-18 02:16:45 | Firewall Log | Outbound HTTPS connection to IP 198.51.100.42 |
| 2024-01-18 02:17:00 | EVTX (System 7045) | Service installed: `WinRM` |

*This timeline conclusively shows the kill chain: intrusion → execution → payload delivery → encryption → exfiltration → persistence.*

**Conclusion (Optional):**
Super-timeline analysis is the **gold standard** of forensic data analysis. Plaso is the de-facto open-source tool for this purpose, supporting 200+ parsers across Windows, Linux, and macOS artifacts.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the phases of analysis with the overall investigation process.** The phases of *data analysis* (Reduction → Extraction → Correlation → Interpretation → Validation) are **distinct** from the overall digital forensic process (Identification → Preservation → Acquisition → Analysis → Reporting). Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for mixing these up.
>
> 2. **Stating tool names without explaining the technique.** Writing "Autopsy" alone is worth **0 marks**. You must explain **what** the tool does and **why** it is relevant to the analysis. Example: *"Autopsy's Registry parser extracts `UserAssist` keys from `NTUSER.DAT` to identify recently executed programs — the parser decodes the ROT13 obfuscation automatically and outputs the program path with run count and timestamps."* This gets **full marks**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the conversion step for Windows timestamps.** If a question provides a Windows FILETIME value, you **must** show all 3 steps: hex → decimal → divide by 10,000,000 → subtract 11,644,473,600 → convert to date. Skipping the epoch offset is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
>
> 4. **Not mentioning the ACPO/NIST/Daubert principles.** Examiners reward students who cite the underlying **forensic standards** (ACPO Principle 3, NIST SP 800-86, Daubert Standard). Always include a sentence on **standards & validation** to gain the extra **+1 mark** for "professional approach."
>
> 5. **Writing "Slack Space" and "File Slack" interchangeably.** They are **different**. File Slack = RAM Slack + Disk Slack. Total slack is computed as `Cluster Size - (File Size mod Cluster Size)` PLUS the remaining sectors. A 1-mark follow-up question is commonly asked on this distinction.
>
> 6. **Omitting Linux artifacts.** Since this is a **Windows AND Linux Forensics** course, questions on Linux artifacts (`bash_history`, `/var/log/auth.log`, `wtmp`, `cron`, `viminfo`) are **frequently asked**. Neglecting Linux is a strategic mistake.
>
> 7. **No diagram in 7-mark questions.** A 7-mark answer **must** include a **labeled diagram, table, or timeline**. Text-only answers are capped. The diagrams in SECTION_4 of these notes are **directly usable** in your exam.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 — Data Analysis: One-Page Rapid Revision**

✅ **Definition:** Data Analysis = systematic examination, correlation, and interpretation of acquired digital evidence to produce actionable, legally admissible intelligence.

✅ **Phases (in order):** Reduction (Triage) → Extraction → Correlation → Interpretation → Validation & Reporting.

✅ **Standards to cite:** **NIST SP 800-86** (Guide to Integrating Forensic Techniques), **ACPO Principles** (5 principles, esp. Principle 3 on audit trail), **Daubert Standard** (admissibility), **RFC 3227** (Order of Volatility).

✅ **Key Windows Artifacts to memorize:**
- Registry Hives: `SAM`, `SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`, `SECURITY`, `NTUSER.DAT`, `UsrClass.dat`.
- Filesystem: `$MFT`, `$LogFile`, `$UsnJrnl`, `$STANDARD_INFORMATION`, `$FILE_NAME`, ADS.
- Execution: Prefetch (`.pf`), LNK files, Jump Lists, UserAssist, BAM, AmCache, ShimCache.
- Logs: `Security.evtx` (4624/4625/4688/7045), `System.evtx`, `Application.evtx`.
- Browser: `History`, `places.sqlite`, `Cookies`, `Login Data`, `Cache`.
- Volatile/State: `$MFT`, Registry, Event Logs, Memory, Pagefile, Hiberfil.

✅ **Key Linux Artifacts to memorize:**
- User Activity: `~/.bash_history`, `~/.zsh_history`, `~/.viminfo`, `~/.python_history`.
- Logs: `/var/log/auth.log`, `/var/log/secure`, `/var/log/syslog`, `/var/log/wtmp`, `/var/log/btmp`, `/var/log/lastlog`, `/var/log/kern.log`, `/var/log/apache2/`.
- Persistence: `/etc/crontab`, `/var/spool/cron/`, `~/.bashrc`, `/etc/rc.local`, systemd timers.
- Auth: `wtmp` (use `last`), `btmp` (use `lastb`), `utmpdump`.
- System: `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow`, `/etc/sudoers`, `/etc/ssh/sshd_config`.

✅ **Key Formulas to memorize:**
- $S_{RAM} = C - (F \bmod C)$
- $T_{UTC} = (T_{filetime} / 10^7) - 11{,}644{,}473{,}600$
- Hash lengths: MD5 = **128 bits**, SHA-1 = **160 bits**, SHA-256 = **256 bits**
- Cluster size (NTFS default) = **4096 bytes**
- Epoch offset (Windows → Unix) = **11,644,473,600 seconds**

✅ **Key Tools to memorize:**
- **Autopsy + Sleuth Kit** — GUI + CLI, comprehensive
- **Volatility 2/3** — Memory analysis
- **Registry Explorer (Eric Zimmerman)** — Windows Registry
- **Plaso / log2timeline / psort** — Super-timeline
- **Hindsight** — Browser forensics
- **Wireshark / TShark** — Network analysis
- **PhotoRec / Scalpel / Foremost** — Data carving
- **Hayabusa** — Fast EVTX triage
- **Volatility `imageinfo`, `pslist`, `netscan`, `cmdscan`, `filescan`** — Common memory plugins

✅ **Analytical Concepts:**
- **Locard's Exchange Principle** (digital traces)
- **Order of Volatility** (RFC 3227)
- **Corroboration** (multiple artifacts > single artifact)
- **Cross-validation** (use 2+ tools)
- **Chain of Custody** (maintain at all times)
- **Hash integrity** (verify at every step)
- **Anti-forensics to detect:** timestomp, log wiping, secure deletion, encryption, steganography, alternate data streams.

✅ **Examination Mantra:** *Read the question, identify the artifact/topic, state the definition, list the techniques, name the tools, draw a diagram/table, and conclude with the relevant standard (NIST/ACPO/Daubert).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Toolkits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Toolkits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Forensic Toolkit** (also called a *Digital Forensics Workbench* or *Investigation Toolkit*) is an integrated collection of hardware devices, software applications, and standardized procedures used by a digital forensic examiner to acquire, preserve, analyze, and report on digital evidence in a forensically sound, repeatable, and legally admissible manner.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a toolkit is classified as:

- **Hardware Toolkit** — Physical write-blockers, drive duplicators, forensic bridges, evidence bags, Faraday bags, and image-storage media.
- **Software Toolkit** — Suites such as **Autopsy/The Sleuth Kit (TSK)**, **FTK (Forensic Toolkit)**, **EnCase Forensic**, **X-Ways Forensics**, **CAINE**, **SANS SIFT**, and **Volatility**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The term *toolkit* in this module refers to a **bundled, validated suite** — not a single tool. The examiner must understand *what* is in the kit, *why* each component is used, and *how* tool selection affects the admissibility of evidence under IT Act 2000 (India) / Section 65B Certificate requirements.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **surgeon's operation theatre**. The surgeon does not use one single magical device — instead, the operating room contains a *kit* of specialized instruments: scalpels, forceps, sutures, monitors, sterilization equipment. Each instrument has a specific purpose, and the combination produces a *legally and medically defensible* outcome.

A digital forensics toolkit works identically:

- The **write-blocker** is like the **sterilization tray** — it ensures the "patient" (the evidence drive) is not contaminated.
- The **imaging tool (e.g., `dd`, FTK Imager)** is like the **X-ray** — it produces an exact, bit-by-bit replica.
- The **analysis tool (Autopsy, EnCase)** is like the **scalpel** — it dissects the image without altering the original.
- The **hash verifier (MD5/SHA-1/SHA-256)** is like the **signature on the report** — it proves authenticity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Definition:** A *toolkit* is the **examiner's complete workstation** — hardware plus software plus documented procedures — needed to take a digital device from "seized piece of evidence" to "courtroom-ready exhibit."

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Constants

| Metric | Standard Value | Source / Authority |
|---|---|---|
| **Hash Function (Primary)** | **SHA-256** (256-bit) | NIST FIPS 180-4 |
| **Hash Function (Legacy)** | **MD5** (128-bit) | RFC 1321 — deprecated for court use |
| **Forensic Image Format** | **E01 (EnCase)** / **DD (raw)** / **AFF4** | Open Standard / Guidance Software |
| **Write-Blocker Interface** | USB 3.0, SATA, IDE, NVMe | NIST CFTT |
| **Bit-Stream Copy** | Every bit, including slack space | NIST SP 800-86 |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Decomposition of a Forensic Toolkit

A complete toolkit operates in **five logical layers**. Each layer is mandatory under KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1.

**Layer 1 — Evidence Acquisition (Hardware)**
- **Write-blockers** prevent the host OS from writing to the suspect media. Two classes exist:
  - *Hardware write-blocker* (Tableau, WiebeTech) — sits between drive and examiner machine.
  - *Software write-blocker* (e.g., `rdflck` on Linux) — kernel-level block.
- **Drive duplicators** produce 1-to-N forensic copies.

**Layer 2 — Imaging (Software)**
- Performs **bit-stream imaging** — copies every sector, including host-protected area (HPA), device configuration overlay (DCO), bad sectors, and unallocated space.
- Common tools: `dd` (Linux), `dcfldd`, `FTK Imager`, `Guymager`, `EnCase`.

**Layer 3 — Verification & Integrity**
- **Hash matching** confirms the copy is identical to the source. This is the *cornerstone* of forensic defensibility.
- Standard: **MD5 + SHA-1 + SHA-256** triple-hash for cross-verification.

**Layer 4 — Analysis**
- **File system analysis** (NTFS, FAT, ext4, APFS, HFS+).
- **Keyword search, registry analysis, log parsing, timeline generation, deleted-file recovery, carving.**

**Layer 5 — Reporting**
- Generates **chain-of-custody** documentation, hash logs, and examiner notes.
- Court-admissible PDFs / HTML reports.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Standard | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Hash Verification (MD5)** | $H = \text{MD5}(M)$ where $M$ is image bytes | Confirms bit-stream integrity |
| **Hash Verification (SHA-256)** | $H = \text{SHA-256}(M)$ | NIST-approved cryptographic integrity |
| **Match Condition** | $H_{\text{source}} = H_{\text{copy}}$ | Acquired image is admissible |
| **Hash Mismatch Indicator** | $\Delta = H_{\text{source}} \oplus H_{\text{copy}} \neq 0$ | Tamper detected — discard copy |
| **Imaging Throughput** | $T = \dfrac{S}{R}$ | $S$ = size (bytes), $R$ = read rate (bytes/sec) |
| **Acquisition Time Estimate** | $t_{\text{acq}} = \dfrac{D_{\text{total}}}{D_{\text{sec}} \times 512}$ | $D_{\text{total}}$ = data, $D_{\text{sec}}$ = sectors/sec |
| **Bit-Stream Coverage** | $\text{coverage} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{unreadable sectors}}{N_{\text{total sectors}}}$ | Must approach **1.0** (100%) |
| **Tool Validation (Repeatability)** | $\text{repeatability} = \dfrac{\text{matched results}}{N_{\text{test runs}}}$ | NIST CFTT criteria |
| **Write-Block Efficiency** | $P_{\text{wb}} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{write events logged}}{N_{\text{IO operations}}}$ | Must equal **1.0** (zero writes) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The equation $H_{\text{source}} = H_{\text{copy}}$ is the **single most-tested formula** in Windows & Linux Forensics papers. Examiners who cannot state the hash-match condition lose marks immediately.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Law Enforcement (Kerala Police Cyber Cell):** Uses commercial toolkits (FTK, EnCase) for criminal cases.
- **Incident Response (CERT-In):** Uses open-source (SANS SIFT, Volatility) for malware memory analysis.
- **Corporate / e-Discovery:** Uses toolkits to satisfy **IT Act 2000 §65B** certificate requirements.
- **Academic / Research:** Autopsy + The Sleuth Kit (TSK) on Kali Linux — most relevant for KTU lab exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hash-Based Image Verification

The forensic principle of "bit-stream copy" reduces mathematically to a **deterministic hash function** $H: \{0,1\}^{*} \rightarrow \{0,1\}^{n}$ that maps an arbitrarily long byte string to a fixed-length digest.

**Step 1 — Define the source image**
Let $S \in \{0,1\}^{N}$ represent the original suspect disk as a sequence of $N$ bytes.

**Step 2 — Acquire the forensic copy**
The examiner runs an imaging tool that reads $S$ sequentially and writes the result to a destination file $C$.

$$
C = \text{Imager}(S) = \big(S[0], S[1], \ldots, S[N-1]\big)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the source hash**
$$
H_S = \text{SHA-256}(S)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the copy hash**
$$
H_C = \text{SHA-256}(C)
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the admissibility condition**

$$
\text{Admissible} \iff H_S = H_C
$$

If even **one bit** differs, the image is rejected.

**Step 6 — Express as XOR mismatch**

$$
\Delta = H_S \oplus H_C
$$

$$
\text{Admissible} \iff \Delta = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Probability of accidental collision (SHA-256)**

By the **birthday bound**, the probability of a random collision is:

$$
P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-k^{2}/(2 \cdot 2^{256})}
$$

For $k = 2^{128}$ images (more than atoms in the observable universe), the probability remains negligibly small — hence SHA-256 is *forensically acceptable*.

### 3.2 Derivation: Acquisition Time Estimate

Given a 1 TB (1,000,204,886,016 bytes) disk imaged at 100 MB/s:

**Step 1 — Convert disk size to MB**

$$
S = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}204{,}886{,}016}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 953{,}674 \ \text{MiB}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply throughput formula**

$$
t = \dfrac{S}{R} = \dfrac{953{,}674}{100} = 9{,}536.74 \ \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hours**

$$
t_{\text{hrs}} = \dfrac{9{,}536.74}{3{,}600} \approx 2.65 \ \text{hours}
$$

This is the *minimum* realistic estimate — actual time grows with bad-sector retries and hashing overhead.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Toolkit Classification

| Toolkit Class | Examples | Cost | Best For | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Commercial Suite** | EnCase, FTK, X-Ways | High (₹ lakhs) | Court cases, law enforcement | Definitions only |
| **Open-Source Suite** | Autopsy, TSK, SIFT, CAINE | Free | Lab work, KTU practicals | **Highest** |
| **Hardware Toolkit** | Tableau write-blocker, DeepSpar | Medium-High | Field acquisition | Diagrams |
| **Command-line** | `dd`, `dcfldd`, `ewfexport` | Free | Linux forensic imaging | **Highest** |
| **Memory Forensics** | Volatility, Rekall | Free | RAM dump analysis | High |
| **Mobile Forensics** | Cellebrite UFED, MSAB XRY | High | Phone extraction | Medium |
| **Network Forensics** | Wireshark, NetworkMiner | Free | Packet analysis | Medium |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Five-Layer Forensic Toolkit Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Seized Digital Device] --> B[Layer 1: Hardware Write-Blocker]
    B --> C[Layer 2: Bit-Stream Imaging]
    C --> D[Layer 3: Hash Verification]
    D --> E{Admissible?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Layer 4: Forensic Analysis]
    E -- No --> G[REJECT - Reimage]
    F --> H[Layer 5: Reporting and Chain of Custody]
    H --> I[Court Admissible Evidence]
    G --> C
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Software Toolkit Sub-Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SW[Software Toolkit] --> S1[Disk Imagers]
    SW --> S2[File System Analyzers]
    SW --> S3[Memory Forensics Tools]
    SW --> S4[Network Forensics Tools]
    SW --> S5[Mobile Forensics Tools]
    SW --> S6[Report Generators]
    S1 --> S1A[dd and dcfldd]
    S1 --> S1B[FTK Imager]
    S1 --> S1C[Guymager]
    S2 --> S2A[Autopsy and TSK]
    S2 --> S2B[EnCase]
    S2 --> S2C[X-Ways]
    S3 --> S3A[Volatility]
    S3 --> S3B[Rekall]
    S4 --> S4A[Wireshark]
    S4 --> S4B[NetworkMiner]
    S5 --> S5A[Cellebrite UFED]
    S5 --> S5B[MSAB XRY]
    S6 --> S6A[Autopsy Report]
    S6 --> S6B[FTK Report]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Acquisition Workflow on Linux (KTU Lab Context)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Identify device using lsblk] --> P2[Step 2: Connect via hardware write-blocker]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Compute source hash using sha256sum]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Acquire image using dcfldd]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Compute copy hash]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Compare hashes]
    P6 --> P7{Equal?}
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Step 7: Mount image read-only and analyze]
    P7 -- No --> P9[Step 8: REJECT and Reimage]
    P8 --> P10[Step 9: Generate report using Autopsy]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Tool Selection Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the evidence type?} -->|Disk Drive| Q2[Use dd or FTK Imager]
    Q1 -->|Memory RAM| Q3[Use Volatility]
    Q1 -->|Mobile Phone| Q4[Use Cellebrite or XRY]
    Q1 -->|Network Traffic| Q5[Use Wireshark]
    Q2 --> Q6{Open-source or commercial?}
    Q6 -->|Open-source| Q7[Use dcfldd on Linux]
    Q6 -->|Commercial| Q8[Use FTK Imager on Windows]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*Define a forensic toolkit. List any four components of a digital forensic toolkit.*

**Model Answer:**

A **forensic toolkit** is an integrated collection of hardware devices, software applications, and standardized procedures used by a forensic examiner to acquire, preserve, analyze, and report on digital evidence in a legally admissible manner.

Four components:

1. **Hardware write-blocker** (e.g., Tableau T8u2) — prevents accidental modification of evidence.
2. **Bit-stream imaging software** (e.g., `dcfldd`, FTK Imager) — creates exact copy.
3. **Hash verification tool** (e.g., `sha256sum`, MD5summer) — ensures integrity.
4. **Analysis platform** (e.g., Autopsy, EnCase) — performs file-system, registry, and timeline analysis.

*(Examiner tip: Students who write only "software" lose 1 mark — toolkits include hardware AND procedures.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]**
*What is the role of a hardware write-blocker in a forensic toolkit? Why is it preferred over a software write-blocker?*

**Model Answer:**

A **hardware write-blocker** is a physical device placed between the suspect drive and the examiner's workstation. It intercepts and blocks all *write* signals at the electrical/ATA-protocol level, allowing only *read* operations.

**Why preferred over software write-blockers:**

1. **OS-independent** — works even before the host OS boots (no kernel bypass risk).
2. **Tamper-evident** — visually verifiable; cannot be disabled by malware.
3. **Court-defensible** — NIST CFTT-validated devices provide stronger evidentiary weight.
4. **No host-side writes** — zero possibility of timestamps, journal flushes, or driver writes contaminating the source.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]*

**(a)** *Explain in detail the five-layer architecture of a forensic toolkit with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of hashing in layer 3. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Compare commercial and open-source forensic toolkits. Give two examples of each and justify which is more suitable for a university laboratory in the KTU 2024 Scheme context. (7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **five-layer architecture** of a forensic toolkit is:

**Layer 1 — Evidence Acquisition (Hardware):**
The suspect device is connected to the examiner workstation through a **hardware write-blocker** (e.g., Tableau T9). The write-blocker ensures that no data is written back to the original evidence medium. This satisfies the **chain-of-custody** requirement under IT Act 2000 §65B.

**Layer 2 — Imaging:**
A **bit-stream image** is created using tools such as `dcfldd` (Linux) or FTK Imager (Windows). The image copies every sector — including:
- Allocated files
- Unallocated space (slack space)
- Host Protected Area (HPA)
- Device Configuration Overlay (DCO)
- Bad sectors (with retry)

**Layer 3 — Hash Verification (Integrity Layer):**

Hashing is the **mathematical backbone** of forensic integrity. The process is:

- The source device bytes $S$ are hashed: $H_S = \text{SHA-256}(S)$
- The copied image bytes $C$ are hashed: $H_C = \text{SHA-256}(C)$
- The admissibility condition is applied: $H_S = H_C$
- If equal, the image is accepted; if not, it is rejected and re-imaged.

Hashing detects even a **single-bit alteration** and provides **non-repudiation** in court.

**Layer 4 — Analysis:**
Tools such as **Autopsy, EnCase, FTK, X-Ways** examine:
- File system metadata (NTFS $MFT, ext4 inodes)
- Registry hives (Windows)
- Deleted file recovery
- Keyword search and timeline generation

**Layer 5 — Reporting:**
A forensically sound report is generated with:
- Examiner's notes
- Hash logs
- Screenshot evidence
- Chain-of-custody form

*(Refer to Section 4 Mermaid Diagram 4.1 for the workflow.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Feature | Commercial Toolkits | Open-Source Toolkits |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | ₹2–15 lakhs per license | Free (GPL / Apache) |
| **Support** | Vendor-backed (24/7) | Community forums |
| **Court Acceptance** | Widely accepted globally | Accepted when properly validated |
| **Examples** | EnCase, FTK, X-Ways | Autopsy, TSK, SIFT, Volatility |
| **Validation** | Vendor-certified | NIST-tested (TSK, Autopsy) |
| **Updates** | Periodic, paid | Frequent, free |

**Examples — Commercial:**
1. **EnCase Forensic** — industry gold standard, used by FBI, CBI.
2. **AccessData FTK** — strong email and database analysis.

**Examples — Open-Source:**
1. **Autopsy + The Sleuth Kit** — GUI + CLI, runs on Kali Linux.
2. **SANS SIFT Workstation** — pre-loaded with 100+ forensic tools.

**Justification for KTU University Lab:**
**Open-source toolkits (Autopsy + SIFT)** are more suitable for KTU 2024 Scheme laboratories because:
- Zero licensing cost for student access.
- Compatible with standard Linux lab machines.
- Mandated by **NEP 2020** skill-based learning (students learn the underlying commands, not just click a GUI).
- Industry-relevant — SIFT/Autopsy are used by CERT-In and many Indian SOCs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]*

**(a)** *Describe the process of forensic image acquisition on a Linux system. List the commands used and explain why `dcfldd` is preferred over `dd`. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Discuss the NIST criteria for tool validation. Why is tool validation critical in digital forensics? (7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Linux Forensic Imaging Process:**

**Step 1 — Identify the device:**
Use `lsblk` or `fdisk -l` to find the suspect device (e.g., `/dev/sdb`).

**Step 2 — Connect via write-blocker:**
Plug the suspect drive into the Linux machine through a hardware write-blocker (e.g., Tableau T8u2).

**Step 3 — Compute source hash:**
Issue:
`sudo sha256sum /dev/sdb > source.sha256`

**Step 4 — Acquire the image:**
Issue:
`sudo dcfldd if=/dev/sdb of=evidence.dd bs=4M hash=sha256 hashlog=copy.sha256`

**Step 5 — Verify the hashes:**
Compare `source.sha256` and `copy.sha256`. If they match, the image is admissible.

**Why `dcfldd` is preferred over `dd`:**

| Feature | `dd` | `dcfldd` |
|---|---|---|
| On-the-fly hashing | No | Yes (MD5/SHA-1/SHA-256) |
| Progress bar | No | Yes |
| Split output | No | Yes (`split` option) |
| Logging | No | Yes (verbose log) |
| Bad-sector handling | Stops | Retries + logs |

`dcfldd` was developed by the **DoD Computer Forensics Laboratory (DCFL)** specifically for forensic acquisitions — hence the name.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**NIST Tool Validation Criteria** (from **NIST CFTT — Computer Forensic Tool Testing** project):

1. **Repeatability** — the tool must produce the same result when run multiple times on identical input.
2. **Reliability** — the tool must not corrupt or alter the source.
3. **Documentation** — the tool's operation must be fully documented.
4. **Accuracy** — output (e.g., carved files, hashes) must be verifiable against ground truth.
5. **Robustness** — the tool must handle corrupted, encrypted, or unusual inputs gracefully.
6. **Transparency** — open algorithms and source code (preferred for open-source).

**Why tool validation is critical:**

- **Legal admissibility** — unvalidated tools can be challenged in court (e.g., *Daubert* standard in US, *Section 65B* in India).
- **Reproducibility** — another examiner using the same validated tool should obtain the same result.
- **Defensibility** — protects the examiner from accusations of evidence fabrication.
- **Chain of integrity** — links the toolkit, the procedure, and the result.
- **Professional standard** — validates the examiner's credentials under ISO/IEC 27037 guidelines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never write "toolkit = software only"** — examiners specifically look for the *hardware + software + procedure* triplet. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Do not confuse MD5 with SHA-256 in admissibility questions** — MD5 is *deprecated* for forensic use; mention SHA-256 for full marks.
> 3. **Always mention chain-of-custody** when discussing toolkit layers — it is the examiner's favourite test-point.
> 4. **For `dcfldd` questions, write the actual command** with `if=`, `of=`, and `hash=` options. Generic answers lose 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Avoid the word "best" toolkit** — KTU examiners deduct marks for non-objective claims. Use "most suitable" instead.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, "Overview of Toolkits":**

- A **forensic toolkit** = **hardware + software + procedure** (not just software).
- **Hardware components** include write-blockers, drive duplicators, Faraday bags.
- **Software components** include imagers, analyzers, hashers, reporters.
- The **five layers** are: (1) Acquisition → (2) Imaging → (3) Hash Verification → (4) Analysis → (5) Reporting.
- The **hash-match condition** is $H_S = H_C$ — required for admissibility.
- **SHA-256** is the current forensic standard; **MD5** is legacy/deprecated.
- **Hardware write-blockers** are preferred over software write-blockers (OS-independent, tamper-evident).
- **dcfldd** is preferred over `dd` (on-the-fly hashing, progress bar, split output).
- **NIST CFTT** provides the validation criteria: repeatability, reliability, accuracy, robustness.
- **Open-source toolkits** (Autopsy, SIFT, TSK, Volatility) are the KTU 2024 lab standard.
- **Commercial toolkits** (EnCase, FTK, X-Ways) dominate courtroom use.
- **Volatility** is the tool for memory (RAM) forensics.
- **Bit-stream image** captures *every* bit — including slack space, HPA, DCO, and bad sectors.
- **E01** (EnCase format) and **DD/raw** are the two most common forensic image formats.
- **IT Act 2000 §65B** certificate is mandatory in Indian courts — the toolkit must generate it.
- **Chain of custody** is the *unbroken* documentation trail — toolkits must support it.
- **SANS SIFT** is a pre-built Ubuntu-based forensic workstation (legacy; now available as SIFT-CLI).
- **CAINE** (Computer Aided INvestigative Environment) is an Italian-origin open-source live distro.
- **DEFT** (Digital Evidence & Forensic Toolkit) is another open-source live Linux distro.
- **Tool validation** is required for the **Daubert standard** of expert testimony.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computer Foundations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Computer Foundations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Computer Foundations" in a Forensics Context?

In the context of **PECCT866 – Windows and Linux Forensics**, *Computer Foundations* refers to the **fundamental hardware architecture, data representation schemes, and low-level software structures** that govern how a computer stores, processes, and retrieves digital evidence.
