   The firmware performs hardware diagnostics on RAM, storage controllers, video adapters, and input devices. Errors are reported via beep codes or on-screen messages. For UEFI systems, POST logs are often retained in NVRAM.

3. **Boot Device Selection (1 mark)**
   Firmware enumerates boot devices based on user-configured boot order. For legacy BIOS, it checks the MBR magic bytes (`0x55AA`) at sector 0. For UEFI, it scans the EFI System Partition (FAT32) for registered `.efi` files in NVRAM.

4. **Windows Boot Manager — `bootmgfw.efi` (1 mark)**
   The Windows Boot Manager is the UEFI bootloader located at `\EFI\Microsoft\Boot\bootmgfw.efi`. It reads the **Boot Configuration Data (BCD)** store, presents a boot menu if multiple operating systems are detected, and passes control to the Windows Loader.

5. **Windows Loader — `winload.exe` (1 mark)**
   Located at `C:\Windows\System32\winload.exe`, this component loads the Windows Kernel (`ntoskrnl.exe`), the Hardware Abstraction Layer (`hal.dll`), and the critical registry hive `SYSTEM`.

6. **Kernel Initialization (1 mark)**
   `ntoskrnl.exe` initializes memory management, the process scheduler, and the driver framework. It mounts the registry and spawns the **Session Manager Subsystem** (`smss.exe`).

7. **User-Mode Initialization and Login (1 mark)**
   `smss.exe` starts `wininit.exe`, which in turn starts the Service Control Manager (`services.exe`) and the Local Security Authority (`lsass.exe`). The logon UI (`logonui.exe`) is presented, completing the boot.

**Diagrammatic Representation (if asked, present the flow as in Section 4.1).**

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Describe the forensic significance of each stage of the boot process. What artifacts should an investigator collect?**

**Model Solution:**

Every stage of the boot process creates forensic artifacts that can be analyzed:

1. **Firmware Stage (1.5 marks)**
   - **Artifacts:** SPI flash dump of UEFI/BIOS, NVRAM variables (obtained via `chipsec` or vendor tools).
   - **Significance:** Detection of firmware rootkits, hardware implants, and persistent malware that survives OS reinstalls.

2. **MBR / GPT Stage (1.5 marks)**
   - **Artifacts:** First 512 bytes of disk (`dd if=/dev/sda bs=512 count=1`), GPT header at LBA 1.
   - **Significance:** MBR/GPT modifications indicate bootkit installation or partition table tampering.

3. **Bootloader Stage (1.5 marks)**
   - **Artifacts:** `bootmgfw.efi` (Windows) or `grubx64.efi` (Linux), BCD store, `grub.cfg`.
   - **Significance:** Modified bootloaders indicate pre-OS compromise. Hash these files against known-good baselines.

4. **Kernel Stage (1.5 marks)**
   - **Artifacts:** `ntoskrnl.exe`, `vmlinuz`, `initrd.img`, registry hive `SYSTEM`.
   - **Significance:** A tampered kernel or initrd contains persistent rootkits. The `SYSTEM` hive contains the `BootTime` value used for timeline reconstruction.

5. **Service / Init Stage (1 mark)**
   - **Artifacts:** `services.exe` configuration, systemd unit files in `/etc/systemd/system/`.
   - **Significance:** Persistence mechanisms are often registered as services or systemd units during boot.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Boot Process Forensics):**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> - Confusing **MBR** (first 512 bytes of disk) with **boot sector of a partition** (first sector of each partition, e.g., NTFS VBR).
> - Forgetting to mention that **BCD** is the modern replacement for legacy `boot.ini`.
> - Not stating **where** each artifact physically resides on disk.
> - Mixing up `smss.exe` (Session Manager) and `services.exe` (Service Control Manager).
> - Failing to mention that firmware artifacts require **hardware-level acquisition tools** (e.g., `chipsec`, Intel TxE), not standard forensic tools.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the Linux Booting Process using GRUB2 as the bootloader, including the role of initramfs and systemd.**

**Model Solution:**

The Linux booting process with GRUB2 proceeds as follows:

1. **Firmware and POST (1 mark)**
   Identical to Windows: UEFI/BIOS executes POST and locates a bootable device. For legacy BIOS, GRUB Stage 1 (446 bytes) resides in the MBR.

2. **GRUB2 Stages (2 marks)**
   - **Stage 1** (MBR, 446 bytes): Loads Stage 1.5.
   - **Stage 1.5** (located in the MBR gap, sectors 2–32): Contains filesystem drivers to read `/boot/grub/`.
   - **Stage 2** (`/boot/grub/`): Reads `grub.cfg`, displays menu, loads kernel and initrd.
   - **UEFI variant:** `grubx64.efi` is loaded directly from the EFI System Partition, bypassing stages 1/1.5.

3. **Kernel and initramfs Loading (2 marks)**
   GRUB loads `/boot/vmlinuz-<version>` and `/boot/initrd.img-<version>` into memory. The **initramfs** (Initial RAM Filesystem) is a compressed CPIO archive that contains essential kernel modules (especially storage drivers) needed to mount the real root filesystem.

4. **Kernel Handoff (1 mark)**
   The kernel decompresses itself, mounts initramfs as a temporary root, loads necessary drivers, then performs a `switch_root` (or `pivot_root`) syscall to switch to the actual root filesystem on disk.

5. **systemd — PID 1 (1 mark)**
   The kernel executes `/lib/systemd/systemd` (or `/sbin/init`) as the first user-space process. systemd reads `/etc/systemd/system/default.target` (typically a symlink to `graphical.target` or `multi-user.target`) and activates all dependent services, mount points, sockets, and timers in parallel.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Compare the boot processes of Windows and Linux. Provide a forensic comparison table.**

**Model Solution:**

| Comparison Aspect | Windows | Linux |
|---|---|---|
| **Default Bootloader** | Windows Boot Manager (`bootmgfw.efi`) | GRUB2 (also supports LILO, systemd-boot) |
| **Config File** | BCD store (binary, edited with `bcdedit`) | `grub.cfg` (text-based, human-readable) |
| **Kernel Filename** | `ntoskrnl.exe` | `vmlinuz-<version>` |
| **Initial RAM Disk** | `winpe.wim` (WinRE) or `boot.sdi` | `initrd.img-<version>` or `initramfs-<version>` |
| **Init System (PID 1)** | `smss.exe` → `wininit.exe` | `systemd` (modern) or `init` (SysV) |
| **Boot Logs** | Event Log (Event IDs 6005, 6008, 6009, 6013) | `dmesg`, `/var/log/boot.log`, `journalctl -b` |
| **Forensic Tool** | `bcdedit`, registry hive `SYSTEM` | `grub-mkconfig`, `/boot/` directory hashing |
| **Secure Boot** | Yes (default enabled in Win 8+) | Yes (with `shim` bootloader) |
| **Recovery Mode** | WinRE (`winre.wim`) | Single-user mode, rescue target |
| **Forensic Strength** | Centralized BCD & registry | Distributed config files, easier to spot edits |

**Key Forensic Insight (1 mark):**
Windows boots via a centralized, opaque binary store (BCD), making tampering harder to detect by manual inspection but easier to identify via baseline hash comparison. Linux boots via transparent text configuration files (`grub.cfg`, systemd units), making manual edits trivially visible but also easily exploited if file permissions are lax.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **boot process has three phases**: Pre-Boot, Bootloader, Kernel Init.
- ✅ **Legacy BIOS** boots from MBR (512 bytes); **UEFI** boots from ESP (FAT32 partition).
- ✅ Windows bootloader = `bootmgfw.efi`; config = **BCD** store.
- ✅ Linux bootloader = `grubx64.efi` (UEFI) or GRUB Stage 1/1.5/2 (Legacy); config = `/boot/grub/grub.cfg`.
- ✅ Windows kernel = `ntoskrnl.exe`; first user-space = `smss.exe` → `wininit.exe`.
- ✅ Linux kernel = `vmlinuz`; first user-space = `systemd` (always PID 1).
- ✅ The **chain of trust** must be verified at each stage for forensic integrity.
- ✅ **MBR magic bytes** = `0x55AA` at offset 510–511.
- ✅ **ESP GUID** = `C12A7328-F81F-11D2-BA4B-00A0C93EC93B`.
- ✅ Common boot forensic artifacts: MBR dump, ESP contents, `BCD`, `grub.cfg`, `ntoskrnl.exe`, `vmlinuz`, `initrd.img`.
- ✅ **Always hash and baseline bootloader files** before investigating.
- ✅ **Never boot a suspect system** before imaging the disk — bootkits can self-heal.
- ✅ Event IDs `6005` (boot complete) and `6008` (unexpected shutdown) are key Windows artifacts.
- ✅ Use `systemd-analyze blame` in Linux to identify slow boot components (persistence indicator).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hard Disk Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hard Disk Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hard Disk Technology** refers to the electromechanical data storage architecture that uses rapidly rotating rigid platters coated with magnetic material to store and retrieve digital information through magnetically-sensitive read/write heads. In the context of **Digital Forensics**, hard disk technology forms the foundational substrate upon which operating systems, user artifacts, and evidentiary data reside — making the understanding of its physical and logical geometry an absolute prerequisite for acquisition, analysis, and reporting under KTU 2024 Scheme outcomes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Relevance:** Every byte recovered during an investigation — deleted files, slack space, swap artifacts, partition metadata — originates from the physical and logical structures of the hard disk. Without understanding platter geometry, sector addressing, and encoding schemes, a forensic examiner cannot validate evidence integrity.

### 1.2 Key Engineering Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants are universally referenced in KTU Board examinations and forensic literature:

- **Spindle Speed (Server Class):** 15,000 RPM**
- **Spindle Speed (Enterprise):** 10,000 RPM**
- **Spindle Speed (Consumer):** 7,200 RPM**
- **Standard Sector Size:** 512 bytes (legacy) and **4,096 bytes (Advanced Format / 4Kn)**
- **Track Density:** measured in **Tracks Per Inch (TPI)**
- **Areal Density:** measured in **Gbits per square inch (Gb/in²)**
- **Head Flying Height:** **5 to 10 nanometers** (modern drives)

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Massive Revolving Library**

Think of a hard disk as a colossal multi-story circular library with the following mapping:

- **Platters** = The circular floors of the library (one stacked above another)
- **Tracks** = The concentric circular shelves on each floor
- **Sectors** = The individual book slots (each slot holds exactly 512 or 4,096 bytes)
- **Read/Write Heads** = The librarian's arms that extend and read/write books
- **Actuator Arm** = The robotic mechanism that moves the arms to the correct shelf
- **Spindle Motor** = The rotating base that spins the entire library

When a forensic examiner requests file `report.pdf` from track 2,500, sector 14, the "library" must rotate the right "shelf" under the "arm" — this entire orchestrated motion is what hard disk technology manages at microsecond precision.

### 1.4 Forensic Perspective: Why This Topic Matters

From a **Windows and Linux Forensics** standpoint, the examiner must understand that:

- **Deleted data** survives in sectors until overwritten — the platter does not "erase" magnetically upon deletion.
- **Slack space** (RAM slack + file slack) arises directly from fixed sector geometry.
- **Host Protected Area (HPA)** and **Device Configuration Overlay (DCO)** are hidden disk regions — only detectable when geometry is understood.
- **Bad sector reallocation** (G-List / P-List) shifts data between physical and logical addressing.
- **Wear-leveling** in SSDs is *not* present in HDDs, but **sector re-mapping** in HDDs creates forensic artifacts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Disk Geometry — The Foundational Layer

A hard disk is organized as a hierarchy of concentric structures:

| Layer | Definition | Forensic Significance |
|-------|------------|------------------------|
| **Platter** | Circular rigid disk coated with magnetic material | Multiple platters stacked on a single spindle |
| **Track** | A complete concentric circle on a platter surface | Data path tracked by Read/Write head |
| **Cylinder** | A vertical column of tracks sharing the same radius across all platters | Minimizes head movement during multi-platter reads |
| **Sector** | The smallest addressable unit; an arc of a track | Default 512 bytes; **4,096 bytes** in 4Kn (Advanced Format) |
| **Cluster / Block** | A logical grouping of sectors managed by the file system | NTFS default = **4,096 bytes (8 sectors)** |
| **Zone** | A group of tracks with identical sector counts (ZBR) | Outer zones have more sectors → higher transfer rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **CHS (Cylinder-Head-Sector) Addressing** is the legacy 24-bit addressing scheme limited to 8.4 GB. Modern drives use **LBA (Logical Block Addressing)** — a flat 48-bit linear address space supporting up to 144 PB.

### 2.2 Logical Structure of a Hard Disk

A forensic image is interpreted as a layered structure:

- **Level 0 (Physical):** Platters, heads, sectors, tracks
- **Level 1 (Logical Addressing):** LBA 0, LBA 1, LBA 2, …, LBA (N-1)
- **Level 2 (Partitioning):** MBR / GPT layout
- **Level 3 (File System):** NTFS, FAT32, ext4, XFS, APFS
- **Level 4 (Data Layer):** Files, directories, metadata, slack space, unallocated space

### 2.3 The Six Core Engineering Parameters

These parameters govern every performance and forensic calculation:

1. **Spindle Speed (ω)** — rotations per minute
2. **Average Seek Time (Tseek)** — actuator arm motion to target track
3. **Rotational Latency (Tlat)** — wait time for correct sector
4. **Transfer Rate (R)** — bytes per second read/written
5. **Capacity (C)** — total bytes addressable
6. **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** — reliability metric

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY FORMULAS — Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $C = Cyl \times Heads \times SPT \times BPS$ | Total Capacity from CHS geometry | Bytes |
| 2 | $T_{rot} = \dfrac{60}{RPM}$ | Time for one full platter rotation | seconds |
| 3 | $T_{lat} = \dfrac{T_{rot}}{2} = \dfrac{30}{RPM}$ | Average rotational latency (half rotation) | seconds |
| 4 | $T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{lat} + T_{xfer}$ | Total average access time | seconds |
| 5 | $R_{transfer} = \dfrac{SPT \times BPS}{T_{rot}}$ | Sequential transfer rate (inner to outer) | bytes/sec |
| 6 | $R_{MB/s} = \dfrac{SPT \times BPS \times RPM}{60 \times 2^{20}}$ | Transfer rate in MB/s | MB/s |
| 7 | $Areal\ Density = TPI \times BPI$ | Bits per square inch (Gb/in²) | Gb/in² |
| 8 | $LBA = ((Cyl \times HPC) + Head) \times SPT + (Sector - 1)$ | CHS → LBA conversion | dimensionless |
| 9 | $MTBF = \dfrac{Total\ Operating\ Hours}{Number\ of\ Failures}$ | Reliability metric | hours |
| 10 | $AFR = 1 - e^{\dfrac{-8760}{MTBF}}$ | Annualized Failure Rate | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-Symbol Substitution Rule:** Throughout this document, the vertical pipe symbol is rendered as `\vert` to prevent Markdown table corruption. For example, $\vert x \vert$ denotes absolute value.

### 2.5 Engineering & Forensic Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Disk Imaging (dd, FTK Imager, EnCase)** | Capacity verification via LBA count |
| **Bad Sector Analysis** | Detection via SMART logs and reallocated sector count |
| **File Carving** | Sector-level reconstruction relies on LBA arithmetic |
| **Performance Benchmarking** | Taccess used in SLA design for forensic workstations |
| **Evidence Authentication** | Bit-level verification across the entire LBA range |

### 2.6 Platter Recording Technologies

- **Constant Angular Velocity (CAV):** Platter rotates at fixed RPM. Outer tracks have unused capacity (older drives).
- **Zoned Bit Recording (ZBR) / Zone Constant Angular Velocity (ZCAV):** Outer zones contain **more sectors per track** than inner zones, optimizing capacity. Modern drives use **15 to 30 zones**.
- **Multiple Zone Recording (MZR):** Refinement of ZBR with tighter zone gradation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Impact of ZBR:** Sectors on outer tracks deliver higher data throughput. This is why forensic imaging at the "outer" regions of a disk appears faster than at the "inner" regions.

### 2.7 Data Encoding Schemes

| Encoding | Encoding Density | Bit Cell Width | Description |
|----------|------------------|----------------|-------------|
| **FM (Frequency Modulation)** | 0.5 | 2T | One clock bit per data bit |
| **MFM (Modified FM)** | 1.0 | 1T | Eliminated clock between adjacent 1s |
| **MMFM / M²FM** | 1.5 | — | Improved MFM variant |
| **RLL 2,7** | 1.5 | — | Run-Length Limited — maps data to bit patterns with constrained run lengths |
| **RLL 1,7** | 1.33 | — | Higher density variant |
| **PRML (Partial Response Max Likelihood)** | ~1.5+ | — | Signal processing-based detection |
| **EPRML** | ~1.7+ | — | Extended PRML |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** RLL (d,k) notation — `d` = minimum zeros between transitions; `k` = maximum zeros between transitions. RLL 2,7 means at least 2 and at most 7 zeros between magnetic flux reversals.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Disk Capacity from CHS Geometry

**Statement:** The total capacity of a hard disk is the product of the number of cylinders, the number of heads (recording surfaces), the number of sectors per track, and the number of bytes per sector.

**Given:**
- $Cyl$ = number of cylinders
- $Heads$ = number of read/write heads (equals number of recording surfaces)
- $SPT$ = sectors per track
- $BPS$ = bytes per sector

**Step 1 — Begin with the smallest addressable unit:**

$$
\text{1 Sector} = BPS \text{ bytes}
$$

This is by definition, the smallest unit of data the controller reads or writes atomically.

**Step 2 — Calculate the data capacity of one full track:**

$$
\text{1 Track} = SPT \times BPS \text{ bytes}
$$

Since each track is composed of a fixed number of sectors in classic CAV recording.

**Step 3 — Calculate the data capacity of one cylinder:**

$$
\text{1 Cylinder} = Heads \times SPT \times BPS \text{ bytes}
$$

A cylinder is the vertical column of all tracks at the same radius across all platters, all accessed simultaneously without head movement.

**Step 4 — Multiply by total cylinder count to obtain full capacity:**

$$
C = Cyl \times Heads \times SPT \times BPS
$$

**Step 5 — Express in higher units:**

$$
C_{MB} = \dfrac{C}{2^{20}}, \quad C_{GB} = \dfrac{C}{2^{30}}, \quad C_{TB} = \dfrac{C}{2^{40}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Note:** KTU expects binary multipliers (1 MB = $2^{20}$ bytes) unless otherwise specified. Decimal multipliers (1 MB = $10^6$) may apply in marketing contexts.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Average Rotational Latency

**Statement:** The average rotational latency is the time for the platter to rotate through half of one full revolution — the statistical mean of all possible arrival times of the target sector.

**Given:** Spindle speed in RPM

**Step 1 — Calculate the time for one complete revolution:**

$$
T_{rot} = \dfrac{60 \text{ seconds}}{RPM}
$$

60 seconds divided by rotations per minute yields seconds per rotation.

**Step 2 — Recognize that on average the target sector is half a revolution away:**

$$
T_{lat} = \dfrac{T_{rot}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
T_{lat} = \dfrac{60}{2 \times RPM} = \dfrac{30}{RPM}
$$

**Step 4 — Example for a 7,200 RPM drive:**

$$
T_{lat} = \dfrac{30}{7200} = 4.1667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \approx 4.17 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Example for a 15,000 RPM drive (enterprise):**

$$
T_{lat} = \dfrac{30}{15000} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2.0 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Doubling the RPM only halves the rotational latency. To halve the **total access time**, one must also reduce seek time — which is why high-RPM drives pair with low-mass actuator arms.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Sequential Transfer Rate

**Statement:** The sequential transfer rate is the number of bytes that can be read per second when reading consecutive sectors along a single track.

**Step 1 — Bytes read in one full rotation of a track:**

$$
B_{track} = SPT \times BPS
$$

**Step 2 — Time for one full rotation:**

$$
T_{rot} = \dfrac{60}{RPM}
$$

**Step 3 — Transfer rate (bytes per second):**

$$
R = \dfrac{SPT \times BPS}{T_{rot}} = \dfrac{SPT \times BPS \times RPM}{60}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to megabytes per second:**

$$
R_{MB/s} = \dfrac{SPT \times BPS \times RPM}{60 \times 2^{20}}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Example:**
- $SPT = 600$, $BPS = 512$ bytes, $RPM = 7200$

$$
R = \dfrac{600 \times 512 \times 7200}{60 \times 2^{20}} = \dfrac{2.213 \times 10^{9}}{6.291 \times 10^{7}} \approx 35.2 \text{ MB/s}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: CHS to LBA Conversion

**Statement:** The Legacy BIOS addressing scheme (CHS) can be converted to a flat LBA number using a linear transformation.

**Given:** Cylinder $C$, Head $H$, Sector $S$ (1-indexed)

**Step 1 — Define the head-per-cylinder count:**

$$
HPC = \text{Number of heads per cylinder}
$$

**Step 2 — Map the head position to a flat offset:**

$$
\text{Head Offset} = (C \times HPC) + H
$$

This linearizes the cylindrical and head indices.

**Step 3 — Map the sector position (1-indexed sectors):**

$$
LBA = ((C \times HPC) + H) \times SPT + (S - 1)
$$

The $(S - 1)$ term compensates for the 1-indexed sector numbering.

**Step 4 — Reverse conversion (LBA → CHS):**

$$
S_{new} = (LBA \mod SPT) + 1
$$

$$
H_{new} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{LBA}{SPT} \right\rfloor \mod HPC
$$

$$
C_{new} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{LBA}{SPT \times HPC} \right\rfloor
$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) and AFR

**Statement:** MTBF is a statistical reliability metric. The Annualized Failure Rate (AFR) is derived assuming a constant hazard rate.

**Step 1 — Definition of MTBF:**

$$
MTBF = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Hours Across All Units}}{\text{Number of Failures}}
$$

**Step 2 — Reliability function (exponential decay assumption):**

$$
R(t) = e^{\dfrac{-t}{MTBF}}
$$

**Step 3 — Annualized hours:**

$$
t_{year} = 365 \times 24 = 8760 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Probability of failure in one year:**

$$
AFR = 1 - R(8760) = 1 - e^{\dfrac{-8760}{MTBF}}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Example:** $MTBF = 1{,}000{,}000$ hours

$$
AFR = 1 - e^{\dfrac{-8760}{1{,}000{,}000}} \approx 0.00873 \approx 0.87\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Implication:** Drives with high AFR leave behind more defective sectors. Forensic examiners routinely encounter G-List reallocated sectors that contain fragments of original data.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | 5,400 RPM | 7,200 RPM | 10,000 RPM | 15,000 RPM |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|------------|------------|
| **Trot (ms)** | 11.11 | 8.33 | 6.00 | 4.00 |
| **Tlat (ms)** | 5.56 | 4.17 | 3.00 | 2.00 |
| **Typical Use** | Laptop / Cold storage | Desktop / NAS | Enterprise server | High-end server |
| **Power Draw (W)** | 1.5 – 2.5 | 4 – 8 | 6 – 9 | 8 – 12 |
| **Noise (dBA)** | 24 – 28 | 28 – 35 | 32 – 38 | 36 – 42 |
| **Forensic Risk** | High wear, low throughput | Balanced | Best evidence stability | Highest thermal risk |

---

### 3.7 Inter-Partition Cylinder Alignment

A critical forensic concept: a partition must start and end on **cylinder boundaries** (under MBR) to preserve CHS addressing integrity. Misalignment by even one sector causes:

- **Read/Write Amplification:** The OS may issue 4 KB I/O but the disk may perform an 8 KB operation.
- **Partition Recovery Errors:** Tools like `testdisk` rely on cylinder-aligned boundaries.
- **Performance Penalties of 30% to 200%** in misaligned partitions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Solution:** 4Kn (Advanced Format 4K native) drives and modern OSes (Windows Vista+, Linux 2.6+) use 1 MB partition alignment instead of cylinder alignment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define rotational latency. Compute the average rotational latency for a 10,000 RPM hard disk.*

**Model Answer:**

Rotational latency is the time delay between the moment the read/write head is positioned over the correct track and the moment the target sector rotates under the head. Since the target sector may be anywhere in the rotational cycle, the average is taken as half of one full rotation.

**Computation:**

$$
T_{rot} = \dfrac{60}{RPM} = \dfrac{60}{10000} = 0.006 \text{ s} = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{lat} = \dfrac{T_{rot}}{2} = \dfrac{6}{2} = 3 \text{ ms}
$$

**Answer:** The average rotational latency is **3 milliseconds**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is Zoned Bit Recording (ZBR)? Why is it used in modern hard disks?*

**Model Answer:**

**ZBR (Zoned Bit Recording)** — also known as **ZCAV (Zone Constant Angular Velocity)** — is a disk recording technique in which the platter is divided into multiple concentric zones, with outer zones containing more sectors per track than inner zones.

**Why ZBR is used:**

- Outer tracks have a longer circumference, so they can physically hold more sectors.
- CAV (Constant Angular Velocity) wastes this capacity by assigning a fixed sector count to all tracks.
- ZBR maximizes the total capacity of the drive.
- Outer zones also deliver a higher sequential transfer rate.

**Forensic Implication:** Data throughput during forensic imaging is non-uniform — outer regions image faster than inner regions.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Module 1 Choice 1]**

**(a)** A hard disk has the following geometry: 16,383 cylinders, 16 heads, 63 sectors per track, and 512 bytes per sector. Calculate:
   - (i) Total disk capacity in GB
   - (ii) Average rotational latency if the spindle spins at 7,200 RPM
   - (iii) Sequential transfer rate at the inner track in MB/s

**(b)** Explain the difference between CHS and LBA addressing schemes. Convert CHS = (Cylinder 100, Head 5, Sector 20) to LBA assuming 16 heads and 63 sectors per track.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Compute total capacity:**

$$
C = Cyl \times Heads \times SPT \times BPS
$$

$$
C = 16383 \times 16 \times 63 \times 512
$$

$$
C = 16383 \times 16 \times 32256 = 16383 \times 516096
$$

$$
C = 8{,}455{,}200{,}768 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to GB (binary):**

$$
C_{GB} = \dfrac{8{,}455{,}200{,}768}{2^{30}} = \dfrac{8.455 \times 10^{9}}{1.0737 \times 10^{9}} \approx 7.876 \text{ GB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the historical **8.4 GB CHS limit** of the legacy 24-bit addressing scheme.

**Step 3 — Average rotational latency at 7,200 RPM:**

$$
T_{lat} = \dfrac{30}{RPM} = \dfrac{30}{7200} = 4.1667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \approx 4.17 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Sequential transfer rate (inner track):**

Inner tracks in this CAV scheme have 63 sectors per track.

$$
R = \dfrac{SPT \times BPS \times RPM}{60 \times 2^{20}}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{63 \times 512 \times 7200}{60 \times 1{,}048{,}576}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{232{,}243{,}200}{62{,}914{,}560} \approx 3.69 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) **7.876 GB**
- (ii) **4.17 ms**
- (iii) **3.69 MB/s**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Differences between CHS and LBA:**

| Aspect | CHS (Cylinder-Head-Sector) | LBA (Logical Block Addressing) |
|--------|----------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Address Type** | 3D (C, H, S) | 1D (linear integer) |
| **Bit Width** | 24 bits (10/8/6 split) | 48 bits (modern) |
| **Capacity Limit** | 8.4 GB | 144 PB (48-bit) |
| **Physical Dependence** | Tied to geometry | Abstracted from geometry |
| **Modern Use** | Legacy BIOS, MBR | All modern drives |

**LBA Conversion:**

Given: C = 100, H = 5, S = 20, HPC = 16, SPT = 63

$$
LBA = ((C \times HPC) + H) \times SPT + (S - 1)
$$

$$
LBA = ((100 \times 16) + 5) \times 63 + (20 - 1)
$$

$$
LBA = (1600 + 5) \times 63 + 19
$$

$$
LBA = 1605 \times 63 + 19
$$

$$
LBA = 101{,}115 + 19 = 101{,}134
$$

**Final Answer:** **LBA = 101,134**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 1 Choice 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the major components of a hard disk drive and describe the read/write operation in sequence.

**(b)** A forensic examiner is imaging a 4 TB hard disk using `dd` at a transfer rate of 180 MB/s. If the imaging tool also computes SHA-256 at 450 MB/s and the bottleneck is the slower operation, calculate:
   - (i) Total time to image the entire disk
   - (ii) The maximum acceptable hash rate to keep the imaging tool fully utilized
   - (iii) Discuss the role of write-blockers in the imaging process

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Major Components of a Hard Disk Drive:**

1. **Platters** — Rigid circular disks coated with magnetic material
2. **Spindle Motor** — Rotates platters at constant RPM (5,400 / 7,200 / 10,000 / 15,000)
3. **Read/Write Heads** — Convert magnetic flux to electrical signals (and vice versa)
4. **Actuator Arm** — Positions the heads radially across the platter
5. **Voice Coil Motor** — Drives the actuator arm with closed-loop servo control
6. **Controller PCB** — Houses the disk controller, cache (DRAM), and firmware (Flash ROM)
7. **External Interface** — SATA, SAS, NVMe (newer), or legacy IDE/PATA
8. **Air Filter** — Maintains a clean internal environment

**Read Operation Sequence:**

$$
\text{Step 1: Host issues READ (LBA, count)}
$$
$$
\text{Step 2: Controller translates LBA → CHS}
$$
$$
\text{Step 3: Servo system positions head over target track (SEEK)}
$$
$$
\text{Step 4: Wait for rotational latency (LATENCY)}
$$
$$
\text{Step 5: Head detects magnetic flux transitions (READ)}
$$
$$
\text{Step 6: PRML equalizer reconstructs digital signal}
$$
$$
\text{Step 7: ECC validates data integrity}
$$
$$
\text{Step 8: Data transferred to controller cache → host}
$$

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- Disk size = 4 TB
- Imaging rate = 180 MB/s
- Hash rate = 450 MB/s

**Step 1 — Convert disk size to MB:**

$$
\text{Size}_{MB} = 4 \times 10^{6} \text{ MB} = 4{,}000{,}000 \text{ MB}
$$

Using binary convention: $4 \text{ TB} = 4 \times 2^{40} = 4{,}398{,}046{,}511{,}104$ bytes ≈ 4,194,304 MB

> [!NOTE]
> KTU typically uses SI convention (1 TB = $10^6$ MB) unless otherwise stated. We will use **4,000,000 MB** for clarity.

**Step 2 — Identify the bottleneck:**

Since hash rate (450 MB/s) > imaging rate (180 MB/s), the imaging tool is the bottleneck. The system throughput = **180 MB/s**.

**Step 3 — Total imaging time:**

$$
t = \dfrac{\text{Size}}{\text{Rate}} = \dfrac{4{,}000{,}000 \text{ MB}}{180 \text{ MB/s}} \approx 22{,}222.22 \text{ s}
$$

$$
t \approx \dfrac{22{,}222.22}{3600} \approx 6.17 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum acceptable hash rate:**

For the imaging tool to remain the bottleneck, the hash rate must be **≥ 180 MB/s**.

**Final Answers:**
- (i) **≈ 6.17 hours (22,222 seconds)**
- (ii) **Hash rate must be ≥ 180 MB/s**
- (iii) **Role of write-blockers:** A write-blocker is a hardware or software device that prevents any write commands from reaching the suspect drive. It ensures **evidence integrity** and **admissibility in court** by guaranteeing that the original disk is not modified during acquisition. Hardware write-blockers (e.g., Tableau, WiebeTech) intercept SATA/IDE/NVMe commands at the protocol level and filter out all write operations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Binary vs Decimal Confusion:** Students frequently lose 2 marks by using $10^9$ instead of $2^{30}$ for GB conversion. Read the question carefully.
> 2. **CHS → LBA Off-by-One:** The $(S - 1)$ term is critical. Sectors are **1-indexed** in CHS but **0-indexed** in LBA. Forgetting this loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Rotational Latency Half-Rotation:** Some students forget to divide by 2. The question asks for the *average* — that means half a revolution.
> 4. **Write-Blocker Misconception:** A write-blocker is NOT optional. Skipping its mention in forensic imaging answers results in a -2 mark penalty.
> 5. **Spindle Speed Units:** Mixing RPM with Hz or rad/s without conversion loses marks.
> 6. **MFM vs RLL Confusion:** MFM is 1.0 density; RLL 2,7 is 1.5 density. Students often claim RLL is "twice" MFM — this is incorrect.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU 2024 ESE**

- [ ] **Hard Disk** = electromechanical magnetic storage device with rotating platters and movable read/write heads.
- [ ] **Platters** are stacked on a **spindle**, rotated at fixed RPM.
- [ ] **Tracks** are concentric circles; **sectors** are arcs of tracks (default 512 bytes; 4,096 bytes in 4Kn).
- [ ] **Cylinder** = vertical column of tracks across all platters at the same radius.
- [ ] **CHS (Cylinder-Head-Sector)** = 3D 24-bit legacy addressing, limited to 8.4 GB.
- [ ] **LBA (Logical Block Addressing)** = 1D 48-bit modern addressing, supports up to 144 PB.
- [ ] **Capacity Formula:** $C = Cyl \times Heads \times SPT \times BPS$.
- [ ] **Rotational Latency:** $T_{lat} = \dfrac{30}{RPM}$ seconds.
- [ ] **Transfer Rate:** $R = \dfrac{SPT \times BPS \times RPM}{60 \times 2^{20}}$ in MB/s.
- [ ] **ZBR (Zoned Bit Recording):** Outer zones have more sectors per track → higher capacity and throughput.
- [ ] **MFM** encoding density = 1.0; **RLL 2,7** = 1.5.
- [ ] **MTBF** is statistical reliability; **AFR** is annualized failure rate.
- [ ] **Host Protected Area (HPA)** and **Device Configuration Overlay (DCO)** are hidden regions critical in forensics.
- [ ] **Write-blockers** are mandatory for forensic acquisitions to preserve evidence integrity.
- [ ] **Slack space** arises from fixed sector geometry; **unallocated space** is the forensic gold mine.
- [ ] **Bad sector remapping** (G-List for grown defects, P-List for primary defects) shifts logical addresses.
- [ ] **4Kn Advanced Format** drives require OS-level 1 MB partition alignment to avoid read/write amplification.
- [ ] **CHS to LBA formula:** $LBA = ((C \times HPC) + H) \times SPT + (S - 1)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Study Tip:** Hard Disk Technology forms the **physical foundation** of every subsequent module in this course — File Systems, Data Carving, Registry Forensics (Windows), and EXT/XFS/BTRFS analysis (Linux) all build upon the sector-, track-, and cylinder-level concepts covered here. Master this module first; the rest of the course depends on it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hard Disk Data Acquisition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hard Disk Data Acquisition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Hard Disk Data Acquisition** is the *forensically sound* process of creating a **bit-stream (sector-by-sector) image** of a physical storage medium (HDD, SSD, USB, optical media) in a manner that preserves the **original evidence's integrity, authenticity, and admissibility** in a court of law. It is the **first and most critical step** of any digital forensic investigation, governed by the principles of **repeatability, verifiability, and non-alteration** of the source media.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the term is also referred to as **"Forensic Imaging"** or **"Bit-Stream Imaging"**. The output is an *image file* (e.g., `.E01`, `.dd`, `.AFF`) that is a **mathematically exact replica** of every bit — including **deleted files, slack space, unallocated clusters, swap space, and hidden partitions** — of the source disk.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Think of it like a crime-scene fingerprint lifting process.** When police dust a glass for fingerprints, they do not *take the glass itself* as evidence — they create a perfect, verifiable copy on a lifting film. A Hard Disk Data Acquisition is the digital equivalent: we are **not seizing the original hard disk forever**; we are creating a **forensically perfect "lifting" of the disk** so investigators can examine the copy while the original remains preserved and untouched.

**A simpler real-world analogy:**

| Everyday Analogy | Forensic Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Photocopying an old, fragile manuscript page-by-page | Bit-stream imaging a disk sector-by-sector |
| Stamping a notarized seal on the copy to prove it matches the original | Generating **MD5 / SHA-1 / SHA-256** hash of source and image |
| Keeping the original manuscript in a sealed museum vault | Storing the original drive in a **faraday bag / evidence locker** |
| Letting multiple researchers study the photocopy | Letting investigators work on the **forensic image**, not the original |

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are standardized in the KTU 2024 Scheme and across globally accepted forensic frameworks (NIST SP 800-86, ISO/IEC 27037):

- **Sector size (legacy HDD):** `512 bytes`
- **Sector size (Advanced Format / modern HDD & SSD):** `4096 bytes` (4 KiB), often emulated as **8 × 512-byte logical sectors**
- **Default forensic hash algorithms:** `MD5` (128-bit) and `SHA-1` (160-bit); modern KTU/NIST preference: `SHA-256` (256-bit)
- **Minimum bit-rate requirement for write-blocked acquisition:** USB 2.0 baseline of `480 Mbps` (Hi-Speed USB)
- **Recommended write-blocker interface:** SATA-III at `6 Gbps` for modern drives

> [!IMPORTANT] **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):** A student must be able to *distinguish* between the four cardinal types of data acquisition — **Live, Dead, Logical, and Physical** — and explain *when* each is legally and technically appropriate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Data Acquisition

Forensic examiners classify acquisition strategies along **two orthogonal axes**:

1. **System State Axis** → *Live Acquisition* vs *Dead Acquisition*
2. **Data Granularity Axis** → *Logical Acquisition* vs *Physical Acquisition*

This gives us a clean **2 × 2 taxonomy** that the KTU 2024 module expects you to memorize:

| Acquisition Type | System State | Data Captured | Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dead – Physical** | Powered OFF | Entire disk (all sectors incl. deleted, slack, swap) | **Gold standard**; most legally defensible |
| **Live – Physical** | Powered ON | RAM + entire disk sectors | Encrypted disks where decryption keys live in RAM |
| **Dead – Logical** | Powered OFF | Only active file-system objects (files, folders) | Quick triage; non-critical investigations |
| **Live – Logical** | Powered ON | Active files + volatile data (network, processes) | Incident response on running servers |

---

### 2.2 The Five-Step Forensic Acquisition Workflow

The end-to-end acquisition is structured into five sequential, auditable steps. Each step is mandatory under KTU 2024 / ISO 27037 / NIST SP 800-86 guidelines.

- **Step 1 — Evidence Reception & Documentation:** Photograph the device, record make/model/serial/S.M.A.R.T. status, fill the *Chain of Custody* form.
- **Step 2 — Write-Blocking:** Insert a hardware or software write-blocker between the source disk and the forensic workstation. This guarantees the source can never receive a write command, preserving the **original evidence**.
- **Step 3 — Hashing the Source:** Compute the cryptographic hash of the source disk *before* imaging.
- **Step 4 — Bit-Stream Imaging:** Use a forensic imager to read every sector sequentially and write it to the destination file/storage.
- **Step 5 — Verification Hashing:** Compute the hash of the *resulting image* and compare it with the source hash. **A match proves forensic soundness.**

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] The following table contains every formula, conversion, and relationship a KTU 2024 student must internalize for the *Hard Disk Data Acquisition* topic. **No vertical pipes inside cells — LaTeX `\vert` is used where required.**

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Description | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sector size (legacy)** | $S_{\text{legacy}} = 512 \ \text{B}$ | Default sector for pre-2010 HDDs | Bytes |
| **Sector size (modern)** | $S_{\text{AF}} = 4096 \ \text{B} = 2^{12} \ \text{B}$ | Advanced Format (AF) sector | Bytes |
| **Logical-to-Physical AF mapping** | $N_{\text{logical}} = 8$ | One 4 KiB AF sector = 8 logical 512 B sectors | dimensionless |
| **Total disk capacity** | $C = N_{\text{sectors}} \times S_{\text{sector}}$ | Capacity from raw sector count | Bytes |
| **Acquisition time (lower bound)** | $T_{\text{acq}} \geq \dfrac{C}{R_{\text{read}}}$ | Theoretical minimum time given read bit-rate | seconds |
| **Hash output length (MD5)** | $L_{\text{MD5}} = 128 \ \text{bits}$ | Fixed digest size | bits |
| **Hash output length (SHA-1)** | $L_{\text{SHA1}} = 160 \ \text{bits}$ | Fixed digest size | bits |
| **Hash output length (SHA-256)** | $L_{\text{SHA256}} = 256 \ \text{bits}$ | Fixed digest size | bits |
| **Hash collision probability (birthday bound)** | $P_{\text{coll}} \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / (2 \cdot 2^{L})}$ | Probability of collision after $n$ hashes | dimensionless |
| **Forensic soundness condition** | $H_{\text{source}} \equiv H_{\text{image}}$ | Hash equality = bit-identical copy | Boolean |
| **Slack space (RAM slack)** | $S_{\text{RAMslack}} = S_{\text{sector}} - (L_{\text{file}} \bmod S_{\text{sector}})$ | Unused bytes at end of last sector | Bytes |
| **Slack space (file slack)** | $S_{\text{fileslack}} = S_{\text{cluster}} - (L_{\text{file}} \bmod S_{\text{cluster}})$ | Unused bytes in last allocated cluster | Bytes |
| **Order of Volatility (CoF)** | $\text{RAM} > \text{Swap} > \text{Disk} > \text{Offline}$ | RFC 3227 ordering | rank |
| **Write-block guarantee** | $W_{\text{cmd}} = 0$ for all $t \in [t_0, t_{\text{end}}]$ | No write commands reach source media | count |

---

### 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Real Forensic Engineering

- The **acquisition-time inequality** $T_{\text{acq}} \geq C / R_{\text{read}}$ is the single most-frequently-asked derivation in KTU ESE questions. It tells the examiner *how long the imaging process will take at minimum*, which is critical for **case-budget estimation** and **chain-of-custody timestamping**.
- The **forensic soundness condition** $H_{\text{source}} \equiv H_{\text{image}}$ is a **legal admissibility test**. If the hashes match, the image is **bit-identical** to the source and is therefore a legally valid substitute for the original in court.
- The **slack-space formulas** explain *why physical (not logical) acquisition is essential* — slack and unallocated space often contain deleted fragments of incriminating evidence that a logical copy **completely misses**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Acquisition Time

We derive the theoretical lower bound on the time taken to image an entire hard disk given a fixed read bit-rate. This is a **direct application of the data-rate definition** and appears in nearly every KTU ESE paper.

> **Given:** A hard disk of capacity $C$ bytes, a forensic workstation with a read interface providing a sustained bit-rate of $R_{\text{read}}$ bytes per second.
>
> **To find:** The minimum time $T_{\text{acq}}$ required to image every sector.

**Step 1 — Start from the definition of data rate.**

The data rate is the amount of data transferred per unit time. By definition,

$$
R_{\text{read}} \;=\; \frac{\text{Data transferred}}{\text{Time elapsed}}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for time.**

Solving the above equation for time, we obtain

$$
T_{\text{acq}} \;=\; \frac{\text{Data transferred}}{R_{\text{read}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the data transferred.**

To image the **entire** disk, the amount of data transferred must equal the full capacity $C$. Substituting gives

$$
T_{\text{acq}} \;=\; \frac{C}{R_{\text{read}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the inequality for the real (achievable) bound.**

In practice, a real interface never sustains the theoretical peak rate due to protocol overhead, seek time, and controller latency. The achievable bound is therefore a *greater-than-or-equal* relationship:

$$
T_{\text{acq}} \;\geq\; \frac{C}{R_{\text{read}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example (KTU-style).**

Consider a $2 \ \text{TB}$ hard disk acquired over a **USB 3.0** forensic bridge whose sustained read rate is $R_{\text{read}} = 100 \ \text{MB/s}$.

First, convert the capacity:

$$
C \;=\; 2 \ \text{TB} \;=\; 2 \times 10^{12} \ \text{bytes}
$$

Then, substitute:

$$
T_{\text{acq}} \;\geq\; \frac{2 \times 10^{12} \ \text{bytes}}{100 \times 10^{6} \ \text{bytes/s}} \;=\; 2.0 \times 10^{4} \ \text{s}
$$

Convert to hours:

$$
T_{\text{acq}} \;\geq\; \frac{2.0 \times 10^{4}}{3600} \ \text{h} \;\approx\; 5.56 \ \text{hours}
$$

**Result:** Imaging a 2 TB drive over a 100 MB/s bridge will take **at least 5.56 hours**. This is a *minimum* — real-world acquisitions often take 30–50% longer due to bad-sector retries and protocol overhead.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Hash Collision Probability (Birthday-Bound Argument)

> **Given:** A hash function with output length $L$ bits, producing $2^{L}$ possible digests.
> **To find:** The probability $P_{\text{coll}}$ of a collision after computing $n$ independent hashes.

**Step 1 — Probability of *no* collision for a single new hash.**

Each new hash has a probability of $\frac{1}{2^{L}}$ of colliding with one specific previous digest, and therefore a probability of

$$
P_{\text{no-coll, one}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{1}{2^{L}}
$$

of *not* colliding with that one previous digest.

**Step 2 — Extend to $n$ total hashes (so $n-1$ previous digests).**

Assuming independence, the probability of *no* collision across all previous digests is

$$
P_{\text{no-coll, all}} \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{1}{2^{L}}\right)^{n-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the exponential approximation.**

For large $L$, we use $\lim_{x \to 0} (1 - x) \approx e^{-x}$, giving

$$
P_{\text{no-coll, all}} \;\approx\; e^{-(n-1)/2^{L}} \;\approx\; e^{-n/2^{L}}
$$

**Step 4 — Probability of *at least one* collision.**

$$
P_{\text{coll}} \;=\; 1 - P_{\text{no-coll, all}} \;\approx\; 1 - e^{-n^{2}/(2 \cdot 2^{L})}
$$

(using the tighter birthday-paradox form $n(n-1)/2 \approx n^{2}/2$).

**Step 5 — Numerical interpretation for MD5 ($L = 128$).**

The 50%-collision threshold occurs at $n \approx 2^{64}$, which is computationally infeasible with classical computers — explaining why MD5 is still useful for *integrity verification* (against accidental corruption) even though it is broken for *collision attacks*.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Tool Comparison for KTU 2024

| Tool | Platform | Image Format | Hash Support | Write-Blocker Required? | Open Source? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `dd` (GNU) | Linux | `.dd` / `.raw` | Optional (external) | **Yes** (software) | **Yes** |
| `dcfldd` | Linux | `.dd` | MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256 inline | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| `dc3dd` | Linux | `.dd` | MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256 inline | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **FTK Imager** | Windows | `.E01`, `.dd`, `.AFF` | MD5, SHA-1 | Recommended | Free (proprietary) |
| **EnCase** | Windows | `.E01`, `.Ex01` | MD5, SHA-1 | Recommended | Commercial |
| **Guymager** | Linux | `.dd`, `.E01`, `.AFF` | MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256 | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **X-Ways Forensics** | Windows | `.dd`, `.E01` | MD5, SHA-1 | Recommended | Commercial |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: End-to-End Hard Disk Acquisition Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Evidence Reception and Labelling] --> B[Photograph Device and Record Serial]
    B --> C[Fill Chain of Custody Form]
    C --> D[Connect Write Blocker]
    D --> E[Hash Source Disk H1]
    E --> F[Select Forensic Imager Tool]
    F --> G[Acquire Bit Stream Image]
    G --> H[Hash Resulting Image H2]
    H --> I{Is H1 equal to H2}
    I -- Yes --> J[Image is Forensically Sound]
    I -- No  --> K[Discard Image and Reimage]
    J --> L[Store Source in Evidence Locker]
    L --> M[Mount Image Read Only for Analysis]
    M --> N[Begin Logical and Keyword Analysis]
    N --> O[Generate Forensic Report]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Acquisition Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Disk to be Acquired] --> B{Is the Disk Encrypted and Powered On}
    B -- Yes --> C[Live Physical Acquisition with RAM Dump]
    B -- No  --> D{Is Time Critical}
    D -- Yes --> E[Dead Logical Acquisition]
    D -- No  --> F[Dead Physical Acquisition - Gold Standard]
    C --> G[Verify with Hash]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Generate Forensic Image]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Chain of Custody Across the Imaging Process

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Off as Officer on Scene
    participant Lab as Forensic Lab Examiner
    participant WB as Hardware Write Blocker
    participant Img as Forensic Imager
    participant Store as Evidence Vault
    Off->>Lab: Hand over sealed drive with CoC form
    Lab->>Lab: Verify seal, photograph device
    Lab->>WB: Connect source drive via write blocker
    WB-->>Lab: Read-only access granted
    Lab->>Img: Initiate bit stream imaging
    Img->>Img: Compute source hash H1
    Img->>Img: Stream every sector to image file
    Img->>Img: Compute image hash H2
    Img-->>Lab: Report H1 equals H2 status
    Lab->>Store: Return source drive for vault storage
    Lab->>Lab: Begin case analysis on the image
```

> [!NOTE] The diagrams above are **block-level functional architecture flows** that model the *logical interactions* between actors and devices during the acquisition. They intentionally abstract away the physical SATA/USB cable geometry to focus on the workflow mandated by KTU 2024 Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Forensically Sound" image. List the two cryptographic hash algorithms most commonly used in KTU-accepted forensic imaging.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *forensically sound* image is a **bit-by-bit replica** of a source storage device that **(i) does not alter the original evidence in any way, (ii) can be mathematically proven identical to the source via cryptographic hashing, and (iii) is created using a write-blocker to guarantee no writes ever reach the source media**. The two hash algorithms most commonly used are:

1. **MD5** (128-bit digest) — for legacy and quick verification.
2. **SHA-1** (160-bit digest) — for stronger integrity assurance.

> [!NOTE] Modern KTU papers also accept **SHA-256** as a valid third answer for a bonus mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Logical Acquisition and Physical Acquisition of a hard disk. State one scenario where each is preferred.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Logical Acquisition | Physical Acquisition |
|---|---|---|
| **What is captured** | Only active files, folders, and file-system metadata | Every sector on the disk including deleted, slack, swap, unallocated |
| **Speed** | Fast (often minutes) | Slow (hours for TB-class drives) |
| **Tools** | FTK Imager, EnCase (logical mode) | `dd`, `dcfldd`, FTK Imager, EnCase |
| **Forensic defensibility** | Weaker — misses hidden/deleted data | **Strongest — gold standard** |
| **Preferred scenario** | Quick triage in a corporate incident-response on a live mail server | Court-bound criminal investigation needing hidden/deleted evidence |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

(a) **Explain the five-step forensic acquisition workflow in detail, with emphasis on the role of write-blockers and hash verification. (7 marks)**

(b) **A forensic examiner needs to image a 4 TB hard disk over a USB 3.0 forensic bridge with a sustained read rate of 150 MB/s. Compute the minimum time required and discuss two real-world factors that may extend this time beyond the theoretical bound. (7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q3(A)(a) — 7 marks

The KTU 2024 Module 1 acquisition workflow comprises the following **five mandatory steps**:

1. **Evidence Reception and Documentation (1 mark):** The examiner records the make, model, serial number, capacity, and visible damage of the device. The Chain of Custody (CoC) form is opened, and the device is photographed in its sealed condition.

2. **Write-Blocking (2 marks):** A *hardware* or *software* write-blocker is installed between the source disk and the forensic workstation. The write-blocker intercepts every SCSI/ATA command and **rejects all write commands**, guaranteeing that the source media receives **zero modifications** during acquisition. Common hardware blockers include Tableau and WiebeTech; common software blockers include `USBGuard` on Linux.

3. **Source Hash Computation (1 mark):** A cryptographic hash (MD5, SHA-1, or SHA-256) of the source disk is computed *before* imaging. This is the **baseline integrity value** that the image must reproduce.

4. **Bit-Stream Imaging (2 marks):** A forensic imager (`dd`, `dcfldd`, FTK Imager, or EnCase) reads every sector sequentially — including the Master Boot Record, partition table, file system structures, file data, slack space, and unallocated clusters — and writes them to a destination file in formats such as `.dd`, `.E01`, or `.AFF`.

5. **Verification Hashing (1 mark):** The hash of the resulting image is computed and compared to the source hash. **If both hashes are identical, the image is forensically sound and legally admissible.** A mismatch means the image is discarded and the process is repeated.

---

### Model Solution for Q3(A)(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Convert the disk capacity to bytes.**

$$
C \;=\; 4 \ \text{TB} \;=\; 4 \times 10^{12} \ \text{bytes}
$$

(Using the decimal definition $1 \ \text{TB} = 10^{12} \ \text{B}$ accepted by HDD vendors.)

**Step 2 — Identify the read bit-rate.**

$$
R_{\text{read}} \;=\; 150 \ \text{MB/s} \;=\; 150 \times 10^{6} \ \text{bytes/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the lower-bound formula.**

$$
T_{\text{acq}} \;\geq\; \frac{C}{R_{\text{read}}} \;=\; \frac{4 \times 10^{12}}{150 \times 10^{6}} \ \text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the numerical value.**

$$
T_{\text{acq}} \;\geq\; 2.667 \times 10^{4} \ \text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to hours.**

$$
T_{\text{acq}} \;\geq\; \frac{2.667 \times 10^{4}}{3600} \ \text{h} \;\approx\; 7.41 \ \text{hours}
$$

**Final Answer:** The theoretical minimum acquisition time is **≈ 7 hours 25 minutes** (3 marks for the calculation).

**Real-world factors that extend this time (2 marks each, 4 marks total):**

1. **Bad-sector retries (1 mark):** A real consumer HDD often has 1–10 remapped or pending bad sectors. The drive's firmware retries each bad sector multiple times (often 10–30 s per sector) before reporting a read error, dramatically slowing acquisition.

2. **Protocol overhead and seek time (1 mark):** A spinning-platter HDD has an average seek time of 8–12 ms. Sequential bit-stream imaging produces a constant stream of seeks across the entire disk surface, which is slower than contiguous file reads.

3. **USB protocol overhead (bonus):** USB 3.0 has ≈ 8–10% protocol overhead for packet headers, acknowledgments, and flow control, reducing effective throughput below the nominal 5 Gbps.

4. **Hashing overhead (bonus):** Inline MD5/SHA-256 hashing competes with the imaging thread for CPU cycles. On a slow forensic workstation, this can add 5–15% to the wall-clock time.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read This Carefully:**
> - **Do not** confuse the decimal ($1 \ \text{TB} = 10^{12} \ \text{B}$) and binary ($1 \ \text{TiB} = 2^{40} \ \text{B}$) definitions of terabyte. Most KTU answer keys accept the **decimal** definition because hard-disk vendors use it. Mixing the two will cost you 1 full mark.
> - **Always** state the formula $T_{\text{acq}} \geq C / R_{\text{read}}$ *before* plugging in numbers. The KTU 2024 valuation scheme explicitly awards 1 mark for writing the correct governing equation.
> - **Always** convert your final answer into a *practical* unit (hours, minutes). Leaving the answer in seconds is considered incomplete and may lose 0.5–1 mark.

---

**Q3(B). [Internal Choice Alternative — 14 Marks]**

(a) **With the help of a block diagram, explain the architecture of a hardware write-blocker. Why is it preferred over a software write-blocker in court-admissible cases? (7 marks)**

(b) **A forensic examiner acquires a 1 TB disk and the MD5 hash of the source is `d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e`. After imaging, the MD5 hash of the resulting `.E01` file is `d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e`. The SHA-1 hash of the source is `da39a3ee5e6b4b0d3255bfef95601890afd80709` and of the image is `da39a3ee5e6b4b0d3255bfef95601890afd80709`. What can the examiner conclude about forensic soundness? Justify your answer with reference to the forensic soundness condition. (7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q3(B)(a) — 7 marks

**Architecture of a Hardware Write-Blocker (Block Diagram in Words, 4 marks):**

A hardware write-blocker is a small physical device placed *in-line* between the source disk (Source) and the forensic workstation (Host). Its internal architecture consists of:

- **Input Port (Source side):** Receives the source disk's SATA/IDE/USB/NVMe interface.
- **Output Port (Host side):** Presents a virtual read-only disk to the forensic workstation.
- **Embedded Bridge Controller (FPGA/ASIC):** The brain of the device. It decodes every ATA/SCSI/NVMe command from the host and applies a *write-command filter* that drops or rejects any WRITE, TRIM, SECURE ERASE, or format command.
- **Read-Pass-Through Buffer:** Allows READ commands to flow to the source unchanged, returning the data to the host.
- **Status LED / Display:** Visually indicates whether the device is operating in read-only (BLOCKED) or read-write (UNBLOCKED) mode.

**Why Hardware > Software for Court Cases (3 marks):**

1. **Independent of the host OS:** Hardware blockers operate at the firmware/protocol layer, so they cannot be subverted by rootkits, kernel-level malware, or OS bugs. Software blockers run inside the very OS that may be compromised.
2. **Tamper-evidence:** Hardware blockers are physically sealed and emit an auditable log of every blocked command, which the defense cannot dispute.
3. **Court precedent:** Multiple high-court rulings in India and abroad have explicitly preferred hardware write-blockers for chain-of-custody integrity. The KTU 2024 syllabus reflects this by mandating hardware blockers in all "court-bound" case studies.

---

### Model Solution for Q3(B)(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — State the forensic soundness condition.**

$$
H_{\text{source}} \equiv H_{\text{image}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare the MD5 values.**

$$
H_{\text{MD5, source}} \;=\; \text{d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e}
$$
$$
H_{\text{MD5, image}} \;=\; \text{d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e}
$$

The MD5 hashes are **identical** ✓ (2 marks).

**Step 3 — Compare the SHA-1 values.**

$$
H_{\text{SHA1, source}} \;=\; \text{da39a3ee5e6b4b0d3255bfef95601890afd80709}
$$
$$
H_{\text{SHA1, image}} \;=\; \text{da39a3ee5e6b4b0d3255bfef95601890afd80709}
$$

The SHA-1 hashes are **identical** ✓ (2 marks).

**Step 4 — Conclude.**

Because **both** independent cryptographic hashes match across the source and the image, the forensic soundness condition is satisfied. The image is therefore **bit-identical** to the source and is **legally admissible** as a true copy of the original evidence (3 marks).

**Bonus observation (1 extra mark):** The two given MD5/SHA-1 digests are the famous digests of the **empty string** — meaning both the source and the image are *empty* (zero-byte) files. This is a typical KTU trick question. The hash equality still holds, and the image is still forensically sound, but the examiner should report that the *content* is empty.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> - Writing *"the hashes are similar"* or *"the hashes look the same"* instead of *bit-identical* will cost 1 full mark. The forensic vocabulary **must** use the word **"bit-identical"** or **"mathematically equivalent"**.
> - Forgetting to mention *which* hash algorithm you are comparing (MD5 vs SHA-1) is a 0.5-mark deduction.
> - Failing to invoke the *forensic soundness condition* explicitly in the answer will cost 1 mark — the KTU 2024 marking scheme always rewards naming the governing principle.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1**

- **Forensically Sound Image = bit-by-bit replica + verifiable hash + write-blocked source.**
- **Four acquisition types:** Live/Dead × Physical/Logical. **Dead-Physical is the gold standard** for court cases.
- **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227):** `CPU/Registers > RAM > Network State > Running Processes > Disk > Off-line Storage`.
- **Write-Blocker purpose:** Intercept and **reject all write/format/TRIM commands** while passing read commands to the source.
- **Hash algorithms accepted by KTU 2024:** MD5 (128-bit), SHA-1 (160-bit), SHA-256 (256-bit). At least **two** are recommended for cross-verification.
- **Forensic soundness condition:** $H_{\text{source}} \equiv H_{\text{image}}$.
- **Minimum acquisition time:** $T_{\text{acq}} \geq \dfrac{C}{R_{\text{read}}}$.
- **Common forensic image formats:** `.dd` (raw bit-stream), `.E01` (EnCase, compressed + metadata), `.AFF` (Advanced Forensic Format, open).
- **Common forensic tools (must memorize):** `dd`, `dcfldd`, `dc3dd`, **FTK Imager**, **EnCase**, **Guymager**, **X-Ways Forensics**.
- **Slack space formulas:** RAM slack uses **sector size**; file slack uses **cluster size**. Both contain recoverable deleted data — a major reason to prefer **physical** over logical acquisition.
- **Chain of Custody (CoC):** A legally binding log documenting *who* handled the evidence, *when*, *where*, and *what they did to it*. **Every** transfer must be signed and timestamped.
- **Documentary evidence to retain:** Photos of the device in its received state, original packaging, CoC form, hash values, tool name and version, write-blocker make/model, operator signature.
- **NIST 800-86 / ISO 27037 compliance:** The KTU 2024 syllabus is aligned with these global standards. Memorize the four principles: **non-interference, accountability, reproducibility, and verifiability.**

> [!WARNING] **Final KTU 2024 Board Tip:** In Part-B answers, **always** start with a 2–3 sentence *definition* of every key term you use. Examiners explicitly award 1 mark for a *crisp, syllabus-aligned definition* at the start of long answers. Then proceed to the derivation, block diagram, and conclusion in that exact order.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reading Source Data and Writing Output Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reading Source Data and Writing Output Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of digital forensics, **Source Data** refers to the raw, unaltered digital evidence acquired from a target system — including disk drives, volatile memory, network captures, registry hives, and log files. **Output Data** refers to the structured, court-admissible forensic artifacts generated after analysis: forensic images, extracted evidence files, hash manifests, timelines, and examiner reports.

**Reading Source Data** is the controlled, forensically sound acquisition of these artifacts using validated tools while preserving bit-for-bit integrity. **Writing Output Data** is the deterministic generation of documented, reproducible forensic deliverables that satisfy evidentiary standards (Daubert / Section 65B Indian Evidence Act).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-1 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly lists "Reading Source Data and Writing Output Data" as the *foundation* of every forensic workflow. Without verifiable read/write operations, the entire chain of custody collapses.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crime Scene Photographer"

Imagine a forensic crime-scene investigator arriving at a house:

| Crime Scene (Analogy) | Digital Forensics (Mapping) |
|---|---|
| Photographing the room without moving anything | **Reading source data** using bit-stream imaging |
| Camera timestamp + serial number on every photo | **Hashing (MD5/SHA-256)** to seal integrity |
| Writing a sealed evidence bag label | **Writing output data** as forensic report |
| Not allowing anyone to re-enter the scene | **Write-blocking** the source media |
| Court-accepted photo album | **Standard forensic image format** (E01, AFF, DD) |

The investigator does not "edit" the scene — they **read** it, **seal** it, and later **write** their findings into a separate, signed document. The digital forensic examiner follows the exact same discipline.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Forensic image integrity hash algorithms**: **MD5 (128-bit)**, **SHA-1 (160-bit)**, **SHA-256 (256-bit)**
- **Standard sector size**: **512 bytes** (legacy) / **4096 bytes (4Kn)** (modern Advanced Format drives)
- **Forensic image file size**: 1:1 mirror of source (e.g., a 500 GB drive produces a 500 GB `.dd` file)
- **Recommended minimum hashing**: **SHA-256** (MD5/SHA-1 considered collision-weakened for legal challenges)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Bit-Stream Image:** A *bit-for-bit* copy of every sector on a storage device, including slack space, unallocated clusters, and partition metadata. It is the digital equivalent of photographing an entire crime scene at the pixel level.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Forensic Read/Write Operations

**Pillar 1 — Source Data Acquisition (Reading)**

The reading process must satisfy three properties:

1. **Completeness** — every bit is captured (no sector skipped)
2. **Accuracy** — bits are unchanged (verified by hash)
3. **Non-interference** — source media is never modified (write-blocked)

**Pillar 2 — Integrity Preservation (Hashing)**

Every read operation produces a cryptographic digest. Any future verification that re-computes the hash and gets a different value proves tampering.

**Pillar 3 — Output Generation (Writing)**

Output must be:
- **Reproducible** (another examiner can repeat the analysis)
- **Auditable** (timestamps, tool versions, operator names logged)
- **Admissible** (chain-of-custody maintained)

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — Operational Logic

- **Why bit-stream and not file-copy?** File-copy ignores deleted files, slack space, $MFT, $LogFile, pagefile.sys — the very artifacts forensic examiners hunt.
- **Why hash the source AND the image?** So the defense attorney cannot argue the image was fabricated or corrupted in transit.
- **Why a write-blocker?** Windows / Linux auto-mount drives on insertion; this writes `RECYCLER`, `.Spotlight-V100`, `/var/log/lastlog`, etc., polluting the evidence.
- **Why standardized output formats?** Proprietary formats may not be readable in 10 years; `.dd` and `.E01` are open or widely documented.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Acquisition time estimate | $T_{acq} = \dfrac{S_{drive}}{R_{read}}$ | $S$ in bytes, $R$ in B/s |
| Storage required for image | $S_{img} \geq 1.0 \times S_{drive} + H_{overhead}$ | Add **~10%** for E01 compression overhead |
| Hash verification | $H_{src} \stackrel{?}{=} H_{img}$ | Match ⇒ integrity preserved |
| MD5 digest length | $\vert H_{MD5} \vert = 128 \text{ bits} = 32 \text{ hex chars}$ | Deprecated for new investigations |
| SHA-1 digest length | $\vert H_{SHA1} \vert = 160 \text{ bits} = 40 \text{ hex chars}$ | Deprecated since **SHAttered (2017)** |
| SHA-256 digest length | $\vert H_{SHA256} \vert = 256 \text{ bits} = 64 \text{ hex chars}$ | **Current KTU-recommended standard** |
| Compression ratio (E01/EnCase) | $r_c = \dfrac{S_{compressed}}{S_{raw}}$ | Typically **0.30 – 0.60** for OS drives |
| Sectors per GB (512n) | $n_{sec} = \dfrac{1024^3}{512} = 2{,}097{,}152$ | 4Kn uses $\dfrac{1024^3}{4096}$ |
| Block transfer size (dd) | $bs \in \{512, 4096, 64 \times 1024\}$ | Trade-off: speed vs. error granularity |
| Chain-of-custody fields | $\{C, T, O, H, S, D\}$ | Case, Time, Operator, Hash, Storage, Device |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Incident Response (IR):** SOC analysts image compromised hosts before re-imaging, preserving attacker TTPs.
- **E-Discovery:** Litigation support teams read mail archives (PST/OST) and write Redacted PDFs as output.
- **Malware Analysis:** Researchers dump RAM (`/proc/kcore`, `winpmem`) and write YARA-scanned output reports.
- **Law Enforcement (Kerala Police Cyber Cell):** Use of `dd`, `dcfldd`, and `ewfacquire` to image devices seized under IT Act §66–§74.
- **Cloud Forensics:** Reading from cloud snapshots and writing to immutable WORM storage for compliance.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Shortcut:** Always hash *before* hashing again. Compute $H_1$ on the source, copy, compute $H_2$ on the copy. Never trust a tool that does not show both hashes side-by-side.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why SHA-256 Is the Modern KTU Standard

We compare collision resistance across the three hash families accepted in forensic practice.

A **collision** occurs when two distinct inputs $M_1 \neq M_2$ produce the same digest:
$$H(M_1) = H(M_2) \quad \text{where} \quad M_1 \neq M_2$$

The theoretical brute-force collision cost for an $n$-bit hash is the **birthday-bound** complexity:
$$C_{collision} \approx 2^{n/2}$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MD5:} \quad n &= 128 \text{ bits} \\
C_{collision}^{MD5} &\approx 2^{128/2} = 2^{64} \approx 1.84 \times 10^{19} \text{ operations} \\
&\text{(Broken in 2004; collision in seconds on modern GPUs.)} \\[6pt]
\text{SHA-1:} \quad n &= 160 \text{ bits} \\
C_{collision}^{SHA1} &\approx 2^{80} \approx 1.20 \times 10^{24} \text{ operations} \\
&\text{(SHAttered, 2017 — chosen-prefix collision demonstrated.)} \\[6pt]
\text{SHA-256:} \quad n &= 256 \text{ bits} \\
C_{collision}^{SHA256} &\approx 2^{128} \approx 3.40 \times 10^{38} \text{ operations} \\
&\text{(No practical collision attack as of 2024.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Only SHA-256's $2^{128}$ collision barrier is computationally infeasible. Hence KTU examiners are trained to **always** record a SHA-256 alongside any legacy MD5.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Forensic Image Formats

| Format | Extension | Open? | Compression | Splitting | Metadata |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Raw / DD | `.dd`, `.img`, `.raw` | ✅ Open | ❌ None | Manual | None — external sidecar |
| EnCase (Expert Witness) | `.E01`, `.Ex01` | ❌ Proprietary (documented) | ✅ zlib / deflate | ✅ (`.E01`–`.E99`) | Embedded case info + hashes |
| Advanced Forensic Format | `.aff`, `.aff4` | ✅ Open (AFF4 v1) | ✅ LZMA / zlib | ✅ | Embedded metadata |
| SMART (ASR) | `.s01` | ❌ Proprietary | ✅ | ✅ | Embedded |
| FTK (AD1) | `.ad1` | ❌ Proprietary | ✅ | ✅ | Embedded |
| X-Ways | `.ctr` | ❌ Proprietary | ✅ | ✅ | Embedded |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Reading Source Data with `dd` and Verifying Integrity

This is the canonical Linux forensic-acquisition sequence.

**Step 1 — Identify the source device (read-only mode):**
```bash
sudo fdisk -l /dev/sdb
```
This lists partitions, sector size, and total size.

**Step 2 — Hash the source device (pre-acquisition seal):**
```bash
sudo md5sum    /dev/sdb > /evidence/case042_src_md5.txt
sudo sha256sum /dev/sdb > /evidence/case042_src_sha256.txt
```
*Logic:* The source is hashed *before* imaging so that if the device is later challenged, the original state is mathematically documented.

**Step 3 — Acquire the bit-stream image:**
```bash
sudo dd if=/dev/sdb of=/evidence/case042_image.dd \
        bs=4M conv=noerror,sync status=progress
```
*Logic:* `if` = input file (source), `of` = output file (image), `bs=4M` = 4 MiB block transfer (fast + low syscall overhead), `conv=noerror,sync` = continue on read errors and pad bad sectors with null bytes (preserves byte offset).

**Step 4 — Hash the resulting image (post-acquisition verification):**
```bash
sudo md5sum    /evidence/case042_image.dd > /evidence/case042_img_md5.txt
sudo sha256sum /evidence/case042_image.dd > /evidence/case042_img_sha256.txt
```

**Step 5 — Compare hashes (integrity assertion):**
$$H_{src} \stackrel{?}{=} H_{img}$$
If equal ⇒ bit-for-bit copy confirmed.

**Step 6 — Convert image to a compressed, splittable archive format (writing output):**
```bash
sudo ewfexport -t /evidence/case042_image \
               -f encase6 \
               -c best \
               -S 4G \
               /evidence/case042_image.dd
```
*Logic:* `ewfexport` wraps the raw `.dd` into the **EnCase `.E01`** format with **deflate** compression and **4 GiB segment splitting** (FAT32 / DVD compatibility).

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Reading Source Data on Windows (FTK Imager Workflow)

1. Launch **FTK Imager** → *File* → *Create Disk Image*.
2. Select **Physical Drive** source.
3. Choose destination = `E01` (EnCase format) with case number `case042`.
4. Set fragment size = **4 GB**, compression = **Best (deflate)**.
5. Click **Start** — produces `.E01`, `.E02`, `.E03` … plus `.txt` log.
6. **Image Verify** option automatically re-reads and re-hashes the image and asserts equality.

### 3.5 Step-by-Step: Writing a Court-Admissible Output Report

The output data must answer six forensic questions (the **5W1H + integrity** matrix):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Who} &= \text{Examiner (name, certification, badge)} \\
\text{What} &= \text{Device, OS, filesystem, image hash} \\
\text{When} &= \text{Timestamp (UTC + IST), from NTP-synced clock} \\
\text{Where} &= \text{Physical location + storage vault ID} \\
\text{Why} &= \text{Case reference + authorizing order} \\
\text{How} &= \text{Tool, version, command-line} \\
\text{Integrity} &= \text{MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256 of source AND image}
\end{aligned}
$$

A valid output report therefore contains — at minimum — the seven-tuple:
$$R = \{W_{who}, W_{what}, W_{when}, W_{where}, W_{why}, W_{how}, H\}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Forensic Read/Write Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crime Scene: Target Device] --> B[Write Blocker Hardware]
    B --> C[Linux Workstation: examiner]
    C --> D[Step 1: fdisk -l to identify]
    D --> E[Step 2: md5sum + sha256sum of source]
    E --> F[Step 3: dd if=dev of=image.dd]
    F --> G[Step 4: md5sum + sha256sum of image]
    G --> H{Step 5: H_src = H_img?}
    H -- No --> I[ABORT: Re-acquire]
    H -- Yes --> J[Step 6: ewfexport to E01]
    J --> K[Step 7: Mount read-only: xmount or ewfmount]
    K --> L[Step 8: Run TSK Autopsy / Plaso / log2timeline]
    L --> M[Step 9: Write Output Report]
    M --> N[Step 10: Sign + Hash + Archive in Vault]
    N --> O[Court-Admissible Evidence]
```

### 4.2 Read-Operation Tool Stack (Layered Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Layer1[Physical Layer]
        src1[Source Disk /dev/sdb]
        src2[Memory Dump winpmem.lime]
        src3[Network Capture pcap]
    end
    subgraph Layer2[Acquisition Layer]
        tool1[dd / dcfldd]
        tool2[ewfacquire / ewfinfo]
        tool3[FTK Imager]
    end
    subgraph Layer3[Integrity Layer]
        h1[md5sum]
        h2[sha256sum]
        h3[xhash from SANS SIFT]
    end
    subgraph Layer4[Format Layer]
        f1[Raw .dd]
        f2[EnCase .E01]
        f3[AFF4 .aff4]
    end
    subgraph Layer5[Analysis Layer]
        a1[The Sleuth Kit fls icat mmls]
        a2[Autopsy GUI]
        a3[Volatility for RAM]
    end
    subgraph Layer6[Output Layer]
        o1[HTML Report]
        o2[CSV Timeline]
        o3[XML STIX for SIEM]
        o4[Court-ready PDF with hashes]
    end
    Layer1 --> Layer2 --> Layer3 --> Layer4 --> Layer5 --> Layer6
```

### 4.3 Write-Operation: Output Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Forensic Findings] --> q1{Need a timeline?}
    q1 -- Yes --> tl[log2timeline → plaso → CSV]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Need to carve files?}
    q2 -- Yes --> cv[foremost / photorec / scalpel]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Need malware scan?}
    q3 -- Yes --> yara[YARA + ClamAV → JSON]
    q3 -- No --> q4{Need human narrative?}
    q4 -- Yes --> rpt[Examiner's Narrative Report PDF]
    tl --> final[Hash + Sign + Chain-of-Custody]
    cv --> final
    yara --> final
    rpt --> final
    final --> vault[Vault / Evidence Locker]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** The *Layer 3 → Layer 4* arrow in Mermaid shows that **integrity verification happens before format conversion**, never after. This sequence is a common KTU viva question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define "bit-stream image" in digital forensics. Why is it preferred over a simple file-copy?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **bit-stream image** is a sector-by-sector, bit-for-bit copy of an entire storage device (1 mark).
- Unlike file-copy, it captures **deleted files, slack space, unallocated clusters, swap/pagefile, and metadata** (1 mark).
- It preserves the original **evidentiary state** required for admissibility under Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Model]**
*List any three forensic image formats with one distinguishing property each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **`.dd` (Raw):** Open format, uncompressed, requires external sidecar for metadata (1 mark).
- **`.E01` (EnCase):** Proprietary, embedded hashes + case info, supports compression and 4 GB segment splitting (1 mark).
- **`.aff4`:** Modern open format, supports parallelism, designed for very large (>1 TB) acquisitions (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE rules, each Part-B question has an **internal choice**. Below, both alternatives are provided with full model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the complete procedure for acquiring a forensic image of a suspect's hard disk on a Linux workstation, including integrity verification. *(7 marks)*
