Cross-reference with: - `tasklist /svc` to find the service name. - `wmic process where ProcessId=XXXX get Name,Path,CommandLine` to identify the binary path. - If path is in `%TEMP%` or `%APPDATA%` with random name → high suspicion. **Step 4 — Prefetch and File System Correlation (1 mark)** - Check `C:\Windows\Prefetch\` for `.pf` file matching the suspicious process name. - Search for the file's recent execution timestamps using `$MFT` and `$LogFile` analysis. **Step 5 — Memory Forensics (1 mark)** Acquire RAM (using `WinPmem` or `FTK Imager`) and analyse with **Volatility**: - `volatility -f mem.raw --profile=Win10x64 netscan` — corroborate netstat. - `volatility -f mem.raw malfind` — look for injected code in process memory. - `volatility -f mem.raw yarascan` — YARA hunt for known malware families. **Step 6 — Network Forensics (1 mark)** - Capture live PCAP (if permission) using `netsh trace start capture=yes`. - Review historical NetFlow / firewall logs for *frequency* of connection → consistent intervals = beaconing. **Step 7 — Chain of Custody & Reporting (1 mark)** - Hash (SHA-256) all artefacts. - Generate a forensic report documenting tools, timestamps, hashes, and a verdict (e.g., "Connection from PID 4820 to 185.220.101.42:443 over 6-hour period with 60-second intervals, consistent with C2 beaconing behaviour"). --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Alternative) **(a)** Discuss the **Linux** network forensic artefacts in detail. Explain how an investigator would extract and interpret routing tables, ARP cache, and DNS cache from a running Linux system without altering evidence. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An attacker has compromised a Linux server and established persistence via a **reverse shell** listening on port `4444`. Design a forensic plan to detect, document, and eradicate this backdoor while preserving evidence. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B (a) — Linux Network Artefacts (7 marks) **Artefact 1 — Routing Table (2 marks)** - **Command (1 mark):** `ip route show` (modern) or `route -n` (legacy). - **Storage on disk (1 mark):** `/proc/net/route` (volatile, mirror of kernel) and `/etc/iproute2/rt_tables` (persistent). - **Forensic interpretation:** A suspicious `0.0.0.0/0` default route pointing to an unusual gateway (not the legitimate router) is a strong indicator of MITM or traffic hijacking. **Artefact 2 — ARP Cache (2 marks)** - **Command (1 mark):** `ip neigh show` or `arp -an`. - **Storage on disk (1 mark):** `arp` cache lives only in kernel RAM; the persistent neighbour table is rebuilt on boot from static entries in `/etc/ethers`. - **Forensic interpretation:** Multiple `FAILED` entries suggest ARP scanning; a single repeated MAC-IP mapping may indicate ARP poisoning. **Artefact 3 — DNS Cache (2 marks)** - **For systems using `nscd` (1 mark):** `nscd -g` shows cached DNS entries; on disk: `/var/cache/nscd/hosts`. - **For systems using `systemd-resolved` (1 mark):** `systemd-resolve --statistics` and on disk: `/run/systemd/resolve/stub-resolv.conf`, journal logs. - **Forensic interpretation:** A cache full of suspicious `.xyz`, `.top`, or `.ru` domains is a C2 beaconing indicator. **General Best Practice (1 mark):** Use the `dd` or `cp` to copy `/proc` and `/var/log` outputs to a forensic image, generating SHA-256 hashes for chain-of-custody. *Never edit* the suspect filesystem in-place. --- #### Model Solution for Question B (b) — Reverse Shell Forensic Plan (7 marks) **Phase 1 — Detection (2 marks)** - Run `ss -tulnp` to list all listening ports. Note `*:4444` (or `0.0.0.0:4444`) and the associated PID/program. - Run `lsof -i :4444` to get the binary path of the listening process. - Example: ``` COMMAND PID USER FD TYPE ... NAME nc 1421 root 4u IPv4 ... *:4444 (LISTEN) ``` **Phase 2 — Documentation (1 mark)** - Take screenshots of `ps aux`, `ss`, `lsof`, and `netstat` outputs. - Record timestamps (UTC) of every command. - Compute hashes of the suspicious binary: `sha256sum /usr/bin/nc`. **Phase 3 — Evidence Preservation (2 marks)** - Acquire memory: `sudo avml /evidence/mem.lime` (Linux Memory Extractor). - Copy the suspicious binary: `cp /usr/bin/nc /evidence/nc.original` (preserve timestamps with `--preserve=all`). - Image the disk if not already done (using `dcfldd` or `FTK Imager`). **Phase 4 — Eradication (1 mark)** - Kill the process: `sudo kill -9 1421`. - Remove the persistence mechanism (e.g., cron job: `crontab -l`, systemd service: `systemctl list-unit-files`, init.d scripts: `/etc/init.d/`). - Patch or replace the exploited service (often an outdated SSH daemon, web framework, or WordPress plugin). **Phase 5 — Post-Eradication (1 mark)** - Re-image the system if possible. - Implement compensating controls: firewall rules (`iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 4444 -j DROP`). - Conduct a lessons-learned review and update the incident response plan. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - **Do NOT** modify the suspect system before evidence is captured. Running `kill` on a malicious process before memory acquisition will destroy the RAM-resident artefacts (the malicious binary's code, decrypted C2 strings, etc.). > - **Do NOT** trust the `bash` history of the suspect — it may have been cleared by the attacker. > - **Do NOT** open or execute the suspicious binary during analysis — use a sandboxed malware analysis environment. > - **Always** hash (SHA-256) every artefact and log the hash + timestamp + examiner name. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Network Information** encompasses all artefacts describing a device's interaction with a network: IPs, MACs, routes, sockets, DNS, DHCP, wireless, and firewall state. - **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)** is paramount — capture volatile artefacts (ARP, routing, sockets, DNS cache) **before** semi-volatile (logs) and non-volatile (registry, config files). - **Windows tools:** `ipconfig`, `arp -a`, `route print`, `netstat -anob`, `netsh wlan`, `ipconfig /displaydns`, Event Viewer, registry editor. - **Linux tools:** `ip addr`, `ip neigh`, `ip route`, `ss -tulnp`, `netstat -tulnp`, `nscd -g`, `systemd-resolve`, `journalctl`, `/proc/net/*` files. - **CIDR arithmetic:** $H = 2^{h} - 2$ for usable host count; remember to subtract 2 (network + broadcast). - **Port ranges:** Well-known (0–1023), Registered (1024–49151), Ephemeral (49152–65535). - **TCP states** have forensic meaning: `LISTEN` = service, `ESTABLISHED` = live, `TIME_WAIT` = recent, `SYN_SENT/RECEIVED` = scan indicator. - **Cross-layer correlation** (L2 → L7) is the cornerstone of any network forensic investigation. - **Chain of Custody:** Hash (SHA-256), timestamp, document every artefact before analysis. - **Memory acquisition** is critical when investigating reverse shells and RATs. - **Forensic soundness principle:** Never alter the original evidence; work on copies/forensic images. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** When given a long-answer question on Network Information, always structure your answer as **(1) What to collect, (2) Which tool/command, (3) Where it resides on disk/RAM, (4) How to interpret the result, (5) How to preserve evidence (hash + chain of custody)**. This 5-point framework consistently scores full marks in KTU valuation.

topics.title: Network Connections and Process Information
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Network Connections and Process Information

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of digital forensics, **Network Connections** refer to the active and historical communication sessions established between a computing device and other endpoints over a network protocol stack (primarily TCP/IP). Each connection is uniquely identified by the 5-tuple: **{Source IP, Source Port, Destination IP, Destination Port, Protocol}**.

**Process Information**, on the other hand, constitutes the live and persistent metadata of executing programs in memory. In a forensic context, it encompasses the **Process ID (PID)**, parent process ID (PPID), executable path, loaded DLLs/shared libraries, user context, CPU/memory consumption, and crucially — the sockets and network endpoints each process owns or communicates through.

Together, these two artifact classes form the **Runtime State Snapshot** of an operating system — the volatile evidence that must be acquired *first* in the order of volatility (RFC 3227 guidelines).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** "Network Connections and Process Information" is the bridge between OS-level forensics (Module 1: File Systems & OS Artifacts) and advanced network/incident forensics (Module 3+). Mastering `netstat`, `ss`, `lsof`, and Windows-equivalent tools is a **mandatory skill** tested frequently in KTU lab viva and ESE.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Post Office & Telephone Exchange"

Imagine a **large corporate office** (your computer):

- **Processes** are the *employees* sitting at their desks. Each has a name badge (PID), reports to a manager (PPID), and works in a department (user context).
- **Network Connections** are the *live phone calls and letters* being sent/received. Each phone line has a number (port), connected to another number at another office (remote IP:port).
- The **forensic investigator** walks into this office during a suspected data leak. They need to:
  1. List every employee currently in the building (running processes).
  2. List every active phone call (TCP/UDP connections).
  3. Match employees to their phone lines (socket-to-process binding).
  4. Determine which "letter" (packet) was sent, by whom, and to where.

The telephone exchange's **call log** is analogous to the OS's socket tables, while the **employee register** is the Process Table (or EPROCESS list in Windows, `task_struct` linked list in Linux).

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227):** CPU registers → Routing/ARP cache → Process table → Kernel statistics → Temporary file systems → Disk artifacts → Remote logs. Network connections and process information sit in **Layer 3** of this hierarchy — highly volatile and must be captured before power-off.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

The following standard values and ports are frequently referenced in KTU exam answers:

| Metric | Standard Value / Port | Forensic Significance |
|---|---|---|
| HTTP | **TCP/80** | Web traffic |
| HTTPS | **TCP/443** | Encrypted web traffic |
| DNS | **UDP/53** (also TCP) | Name resolution queries |
| SSH | **TCP/22** | Remote admin |
| SMB | **TCP/445** | Windows file sharing |
| RDP | **TCP/3389** | Remote Desktop |
| TIME_WAIT default | **2 × MSL ≈ 60s (Windows)**, **60s (Linux)** | Connection teardown residue |
| Ephemeral Port Range | **49152–65535 (IANA)** | Client-side outbound ports |

> [!NOTE]
> IANA's registered port range is **0–1023** (privileged), while dynamic/private ports occupy **49152–65535**. Examiners should be able to distinguish a *server* (listening on low port) from a *client* (using high ephemeral port).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Two Pillars of Runtime Forensics

#### Pillar 1: Network Connection States (TCP)

A TCP connection transitions through a defined state machine. Forensically, the most important states are:

- **LISTENING** — A server socket is bound to a port, awaiting incoming SYNs. *Forensic interest:* Which services are *exposed* on the host?
- **ESTABLISHED** — Full 3-way handshake complete, data transfer in progress. *Forensic interest:* Active C2 (Command & Control) channels, active exfiltration.
- **TIME_WAIT** — Local side has sent FIN; waiting 2×MSL to ensure remote received final ACK. *Forensic interest:* Recently terminated connections; survivors of rapid reconnects.
- **CLOSE_WAIT** — Remote has sent FIN; local hasn't closed. *Forensic interest:* Unclosed sockets often indicate crashed or malicious processes.
- **SYN_SENT** — Client has sent SYN, awaiting SYN-ACK. *Forensic interest:* Possible port-scanning victim or beaconing malware.
- **FIN_WAIT_1 / FIN_WAIT_2** — Half-closed states indicating abnormal teardown.

#### Pillar 2: Process States (Linux)

Linux defines a richer process state model in the `task_struct->state` field:

- **R (Running / Runnable)** — On CPU or in run queue.
- **S (Sleeping / Interruptible)** — Waiting for event (I/O, signal). Most idle processes.
- **D (Uninterruptible Sleep)** — Kernel cannot wake it even with signals. *Forensic gold:* usually indicates blocked I/O on a hung device (or hidden rootkit activity).
- **Z (Zombie)** — Process terminated but parent hasn't reaped via `wait()`. *Forensic interest:* Crashed/poorly coded parent, or anti-forensic concealment.
- **T (Stopped)** — Suspended via SIGSTOP.
- **I (Idle)** — Idle kernel thread.

### 2.2 Why & How: The Forensic Workflow

**Why** we collect this data:
1. **Live malware identification** — A process with no executable on disk, or running from `C:\Temp\`, listening on an unusual port.
2. **Lateral movement detection** — Multiple SMB/RDP connections to disparate subnets.
3. **Data exfiltration proof** — Outbound ESTABLISHED connections to unfamiliar IPs carrying high byte counts.
4. **Timeline correlation** — Process start time ↔ connection establishment time.

**How** we collect it (preserving the *principle of least change*):

- Use **read-only tools** built into the OS (`netstat`, `ss`, `lsof`, `ps`).
- Redirect output to an **external USB** (never the same drive under investigation).
- Record **command, timestamp, user, MD5 of output file** for chain of custody.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| TCP Connection Identity | **5-tuple = {SrcIP, SrcPort, DstIP, DstPort, Proto}** | Unique per direction |
| TIME_WAIT Duration | **2 × MSL** | MSL = Max Segment Lifetime ≈ **30s (Linux)**, **60s (Windows legacy)** |
| Ephemeral Port Allocation | **IANA range = 49152 to 65535** | Total: **16384 ports** |
| Process ID Uniqueness | **PID ∈ ℕ, unique per namespace** | PID 0 = idle/swap (Linux), PID 4 = System (Windows) |
| Parent-Child Fork (Linux) | **PPID = parent** | PID 1 (init/systemd) is ancestor of all user processes |
| Sockets per Process (theoretical max) | **Limited by `fs.file-max`** | Linux default: **~8% of RAM in pages** |
| Linux `/proc/[PID]` namespace | **Virtual FS, 0 = self** | Read-only forensic view of process memory |
| Windows EPROCESS linked list | **Head pointer at `PsActiveProcessHead`** | Kernel struct, walkable via debugger |
| Well-Known Port Range | **0 to 1023** | Privileged, requires root/admin to bind |
| Registered Port Range | **1024 to 49151** | Applications register with IANA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** When describing forensic evidence, always use the format: *"At time T, process P (PID=X) initiated ESTABLISHED TCP connection to remote endpoint A.B.C.D:port from local port p."* This is the examiner's "gold-standard" sentence structure.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Incident Response (IR)** and **SOC (Security Operations Center)** operations, the equivalent of this lecture is performed by EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response) agents every few seconds. Tools like **Sysmon** (Windows), **Auditbeat**, and **Falcon** continuously harvest process and socket events into SIEM pipelines. The manual `netstat` / `lsof` knowledge taught here is the **fallback skill** when agents fail, are disabled by attackers, or when the analyst is working from a live boot USB.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Mapping a Process to its Network Connection

**Problem (forensic scenario):** During incident response, an analyst runs `netstat -anob` on a Windows host and observes a suspicious connection. Demonstrate the logical procedure to associate a process PID with a network endpoint.

#### Step-by-Step Forensic Mapping Procedure

**Step 1 — Capture all active TCP connections:**

The command `netstat -ano` produces output with columns *Proto, Local Address, Foreign Address, State, PID*.

> Example derived output:
>
> ```
> Proto  Local Address       Foreign Address      State        PID
> TCP    192.168.1.50:49152  10.10.10.5:443       ESTABLISHED  3712
> TCP    192.168.1.50:445    0.0.0.0:0            LISTENING    4
> TCP    192.168.1.50:3389   0.0.0.0:0            LISTENING    1620
> ```

**Step 2 — Extract the suspect PID:**

From the above, PID **3712** has an outbound ESTABLISHED connection to a remote HTTPS endpoint. This is the candidate for deeper inspection.

**Step 3 — Resolve PID → Process Name and Executable Path:**

On Windows: use `tasklist /FI "PID eq 3712"` or PowerShell's `Get-Process -Id 3712 | Select-Object Path, Company, StartTime`.

> Hypothetical output:
>
> ```
> Image Name    PID   Mem Usage   Session Name   Session#   Status
> svchost.exe   3712  18,432 K    Services       0          Running
> ```

**Step 4 — Cross-validate the executable path:**

The default `svchost.exe` resides in `C:\Windows\System32\`. If the path resolves to `C:\Users\Public\svchost.exe`, this is **highly anomalous** — a masquerading technique.

**Step 5 — Correlate the connection's 5-tuple with threat intel:**

- **Proto:** TCP (connection-oriented, full-duplex)
- **SrcIP:SrcPort:** 192.168.1.50:49152 (client ephemeral port)
- **DstIP:DstPort:** 10.10.10.5:443 (server, HTTPS)
- **State:** ESTABLISHED (active data flow)

The ephemeral port **49152** ∈ [49152, 65535] confirms this is the *client* side. Therefore the **foreign** address is the *server*. The connection is outbound.

**Step 6 — Calculate the duration of the connection:**

$$
\Delta t = t_{\text{now}} - t_{\text{process\_start}}
$$

This is computed by subtracting the process `StartTime` from the current time. A long-lived `svchost.exe` with a single persistent connection to an external IP is a strong indicator of a **C2 beacon**.

**Step 7 — Capture byte counters (optional but KTU-favored):**

Using `netstat -e` or `Get-NetTCPConnection -State Established` in PowerShell:

$$
\text{BytesSent}_{\text{outbound}} - \text{BytesSent}_{\text{expected}} = \text{Exfiltration Volume}
$$

A process nominally sending heartbeat beacons (e.g., 1 KB/min) but currently sending **500 MB** is exfiltrating data.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Linux vs Windows Command Equivalents

| Forensic Task | Windows Command | Linux Command | Output Artifact |
|---|---|---|---|
| List active connections | `netstat -ano` | `ss -tunap` or `netstat -tunap` | Socket table |
| List listening ports | `netstat -anob` | `ss -tlnp` | Open ports + owning PID |
| List all processes | `tasklist` | `ps aux` or `ps -ef` | Process table |
| Process tree | `wmic process get Name,ParentProcessId` | `pstree -p` | Hierarchical view |
| Open files by process | Sysinternals `Process Explorer` | `lsof -p <PID>` | File handles |
| Process full command line | `wmic process where "ProcessId=1234" get CommandLine` | `cat /proc/<PID>/cmdline` | cmdline buffer |
| Process loaded modules (DLLs) | `listdlls -u` (Sysinternals) | `cat /proc/<PID>/maps` | Memory mappings |
| Process environment vars | *(Limited)* | `cat /proc/<PID>/environ` | Environment string |
| Network connections by PID | `netstat -ano \| findstr <PID>` | `ss -tunap \| grep <PID>` | Filtered socket table |
| DNS cache | `ipconfig /displaydns` | *(No direct equivalent; check systemd-resolved or pcap)* | Resolved names |
| ARP table | `arp -a` | `ip neigh` or `arp -an` | L2 cache |

### 3.3 Worked Example: TIME_WAIT Forensic Calculation

**Scenario:** An investigator notes 250 connections in TIME_WAIT state, all from local port 50000 to a single remote IP. Calculate the expected decay time.

Given:
- MSL on the host OS (Linux) = **30 s**
- Standard TIME_WAIT retention = **2 × MSL**

$$
T_{\text{TIME\_WAIT}} = 2 \times \text{MSL} = 2 \times 30 \text{ s} = 60 \text{ s}
$$

Therefore, the connections will fully expire from the socket table after **60 seconds** of inactivity. If the investigator finds these connections persisting for hours, the host's `tcp_fin_timeout` sysctl has been **tuned** — a possible anti-forensic measure to inflate the apparent connection history.

**Linux sysctl check:**

$$
\text{cat } /proc/\text{sys}/\text{net}/\text{ipv4}/\text{tcp\_fin\_timeout}
$$

Default value = **60** (seconds). A value of **3600** would be a strong red flag.

### 3.4 KTU Examiner's Logical-Flow Notation

When answering, the examiner expects the following chained logic in any network/process forensic question:

$$
\text{Process (PID, path)} \xrightarrow{\text{opens socket}} \text{FD (file descriptor)} \xrightarrow{\text{binds}} \text{5-tuple} \xrightarrow{\text{transmits}} \text{Packets}
$$

Reversing this chain during forensic analysis is the **socket-to-process** resolution:

$$
\text{Packets (pcap)} \rightarrow \text{5-tuple} \rightarrow \text{FD lookup in /proc/net/tcp} \rightarrow \text{UID/PID} \rightarrow \text{Process binary}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Forensic Acquisition Flow for Network & Process Data

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Suspect Live System] --> B[Identify Acquire Tool: netstat / ss / lsof]
    B --> C[Read-Only Execution as root or admin]
    C --> D[Redirect Output to External USB]
    D --> E1[Network Connections Artifact: .pcap or .txt]
    D --> E2[Process Table Artifact: .csv]
    D --> E3[Open Files Artifact: .log]
    E1 --> F1[Record Hash SHA256]
    E2 --> F2[Record Hash SHA256]
    E3 --> F3[Record Hash SHA256]
    F1 --> G[Correlate PID to 5-tuple]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Cross-Reference with Threat Intel and Timeline]
    H --> I[Forensic Conclusion: Process Identity, Connections, Anomalies]
```

### 4.2 Process-to-Network Mapping Schematic (Linux)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KernelSpace["KERNEL SPACE"]
        KS1["task_struct linked list"]
        KS2["inode table for sockets"]
        KS3["/proc/net/tcp file"]
        KS4["/proc/net/udp file"]
    end
    subgraph UserSpace["USER SPACE FORENSIC TOOLS"]
        U1["ps / top"]
        U2["ss / netstat"]
        U3["lsof"]
    end
    subgraph Output["EVIDENCE OUTPUT"]
        O1["Process List with PID, PPID, cmdline"]
        O2["Socket Table with local/remote, state, inode"]
        O3["PID to FD Mapping with socket inodes"]
    end
    KS1 --> U1 --> O1
    KS2 --> U2 --> O2
    KS3 --> U2
    KS4 --> U2
    KS1 --> U3
    KS2 --> U3
    KS1 --> O3
    KS2 --> O3
    O1 --> Corr["CORRELATION STEP"]
    O2 --> Corr
    O3 --> Corr
    Corr --> Final["PROCESS-NETWORK MAPPING EVIDENCE"]
```

### 4.3 TCP State Machine (Forensic Subset)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CLOSED
    CLOSED --> LISTEN : server bind
    LISTEN --> SYN_RCVD : receive SYN, send SYN+ACK
    SYN_RCVD --> ESTABLISHED : receive ACK
    CLOSED --> SYN_SENT : client open
    SYN_SENT --> ESTABLISHED : receive SYN+ACK, send ACK
    ESTABLISHED --> FIN_WAIT_1 : close, send FIN
    FIN_WAIT_1 --> FIN_WAIT_2 : receive ACK
    FIN_WAIT_2 --> TIME_WAIT : receive FIN, send ACK
    ESTABLISHED --> CLOSE_WAIT : receive FIN
    CLOSE_WAIT --> LAST_ACK : send FIN
    LAST_ACK --> CLOSED : receive ACK
    TIME_WAIT --> CLOSED : 2xMSL timer expires
```

### 4.4 Command-Hierarchy Diagram: Windows vs Linux Equivalence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root["Forensic Task: Capture Runtime State"] --> T1["List Processes"]
    Root --> T2["List Network Connections"]
    Root --> T3["Map Process to Connection"]
    T1 --> W1["tasklist"]
    T1 --> L1["ps aux / ps -ef"]
    T2 --> W2["netstat -ano"]
    T2 --> L2["ss -tunap"]
    T3 --> W3["netstat -ano + tasklist /fi"]
    T3 --> L3["ss -tunap + lsof -i"]
    W1 --> A["Process Artifact"]
    L1 --> A
    W2 --> B["Connection Artifact"]
    L2 --> B
    W3 --> C["Correlated Evidence"]
    L3 --> C
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define the term "5-tuple" as used in network forensics. List its components and state why it is significant during a network connection investigation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The 5-tuple is a unique identifier for any network session. Its five components are: (1) Source IP Address, (2) Source Port Number, (3) Destination IP Address, (4) Destination Port Number, and (5) Transport Protocol (TCP or UDP). Its forensic significance is that it uniquely identifies a single communication flow in a possibly massive log, allowing the investigator to isolate one conversation among millions and correlate it with logs, packet captures, and the process that initiated or received it. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*Differentiate between the TCP states LISTENING and ESTABLISHED from a forensic investigator's perspective. Provide one real-world scenario for each state during a malware incident.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **LISTENING** socket is one bound to a local port awaiting incoming connections — from a forensic standpoint, this reveals *services exposed* by the host (e.g., a backdoor listening on TCP/8081). An **ESTABLISHED** socket represents a fully completed 3-way handshake with active data flow — this reveals *live communication*, such as a beacon already phoning home to its C2 server. While LISTENING suggests capability, ESTABLISHED confirms *actual* active malicious activity, making it the higher-priority artifact. (3 marks)

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Option 1)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**

**Q.A (a)** Explain the forensic procedure for capturing active network connections and process information from a *live* Linux system under investigation. In your answer, list the relevant commands and describe the artifacts they produce. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**
The forensic procedure on a live Linux host follows the principle of *least change* and *external storage*:

1. **Mount an external evidence drive** — `mount /dev/sdc1 /mnt/evidence`. This ensures output never touches the suspect disk.
2. **Capture process information first** (lower volatility than CPU registers but still time-sensitive):
   - `ps auxf > /mnt/evidence/ps_auxf_$(date +%s).txt` — full process listing with hierarchy.
   - `ls -l /proc/*/exe 2>/dev/null > /mnt/evidence/exe_symlinks.txt` — resolves each PID to its on-disk binary.
   - `cat /proc/[PID]/cmdline` for each suspicious PID — captures the exact command-line arguments.
   - `cat /proc/[PID]/environ` — captures environment variables which may contain credentials, paths, or C2 URLs.
3. **Capture network connection information:**
   - `ss -tunap > /mnt/evidence/ss_tunap.txt` — modern replacement for netstat; shows TCP/UDP, numeric addresses, all states, and owning PID/program.
   - `netstat -tunap >> /mnt/evidence/netstat_tunap.txt` — cross-validation artifact.
   - `cat /proc/net/tcp > /mnt/evidence/proc_net_tcp.txt` — kernel's raw socket table (the most authoritative artifact).
4. **Map process to socket:**
   - `lsof -i -P -n > /mnt/evidence/lsof_network.txt` — lists every open network file descriptor with owning PID.
   - For each suspicious PID: `lsof -p <PID> > /mnt/evidence/lsof_<PID>.txt` — open files (including deleted ones — forensic gold).
5. **Hash and timestamp the evidence**:
   - `sha256sum /mnt/evidence/* > /mnt/evidence/SHA256SUMS.txt`
   - Record investigator name, date, host identifier, and command history in a contemporaneous note.

All steps are performed **without installing new software** to avoid tainting the system. The artifacts collectively form the **Runtime State Snapshot** required by RFC 3227. (7 marks)

---

**Q.A (b)** A Windows investigator runs `netstat -ano` and finds PID 4728 holding an ESTABLISHED TCP connection to IP 203.0.113.45 on port 443. The process name reported is `svchost.exe`. Describe the steps you would take to determine whether this is a legitimate Windows service or a masquerading malware binary. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**
A step-by-step triage:

1. **Verify the executable path**: Run `wmic process where "ProcessId=4728" get ExecutablePath` or PowerShell `Get-Process -Id 4728 | Select Path`. The legitimate path is `C:\Windows\System32\svchost.exe`. Any deviation (e.g., `C:\Users\Public\`, `C:\Temp\`, `%APPDATA%`) is a **strong masquerade indicator**.

2. **Validate the digital signature**: Right-click → Properties → Digital Signatures, or use `Get-AuthenticodeSignature`. A legitimate `svchost.exe` is signed by Microsoft. A missing or invalid signature is suspicious.

3. **Check the parent process**: Run `wmic process where "ProcessId=4728" get ParentProcessId`. Legitimate `svchost.exe` is always spawned by `services.exe` (PID ~500–800 on a stable system). If the parent is `explorer.exe`, `cmd.exe`, or an unusual binary, the process is suspicious.

4. **Examine the command line**: `wmic process where "ProcessId=4728" get CommandLine`. Legitimate `svchost.exe` always runs with `-k` flags referring to a service group (e.g., `-k netsvcs`). No command-line arguments or unusual ones is a red flag.

5. **Inspect the loaded DLLs**: Use Sysinternals Process Explorer or `listdlls -u` to view loaded modules. A `svchost.exe` loading unsigned DLLs from `%TEMP%` or from network shares is high-confidence malicious.

6. **Cross-correlate the remote endpoint**: Look up IP `203.0.113.45` in threat intelligence feeds (VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB). A high-confidence malicious score on port 443 (often used to blend with HTTPS traffic) confirms suspicion.

7. **Memory and disk acquisition**: If indicators are positive, dump the process memory with `procdump -ma 4728` and image the file at the executable's path for full reverse engineering and IOC extraction.

A single anomaly is suggestive; two or more constitutes **reasonable forensic certainty** of compromise. (7 marks)

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Option 2, alternative choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**Q.B (a)** Compare and contrast the Linux commands `netstat`, `ss`, and `lsof` for network and process forensic acquisition. Provide example invocations and explain the types of artifacts each produces. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**`netstat`** (legacy, from net-tools):
- Reads from `/proc/net/tcp`, `/proc/net/udp`, and reads process info from `/proc/[PID]/fd`.
- Invocation: `netstat -tunap` → TCP + UDP + numeric + all states + programs.
- Pros: Universally available, predictable output.
- Cons: Considered deprecated on modern Linux distros; can be slow on hosts with millions of sockets; depends on `/proc` which rootkits can hide processes from.

**`ss`** (iproute2, modern replacement):
- Directly queries the kernel via netlink socket — bypasses `/proc` for connection data.
- Invocation: `ss -tunap` (equivalent) or `ss -tlnp` (listening only) or `ss -K` (kill socket — useful to halt C2).
- Pros: Faster, more accurate, can show TCP congestion state, BPF filters (`ss -t '( sport = :443 )'`).
- Cons: Slightly less ubiquitous on very old systems; output formatting requires more parsing.

**`lsof`** ("list open files"):
- Lists *every* open file descriptor of every process, including sockets, regular files, pipes, devices.
- Invocation: `lsof -i -P -n` (all internet files, no port-name lookup, no DNS). For one PID: `lsof -p 4728`. To see deleted binaries still mapped: `lsof -p 4728 +L1`.
- Pros: Most comprehensive — reveals files held open by deleted processes, hidden by unmounted disks, or in-memory-only.
- Cons: Can produce massive output; slow on hosts with many processes.

**Forensic Recommendation:** Use all three and **cross-validate** — differences between `ss` and `lsof` output can themselves indicate a rootkit hiding connections from one tool but not the other. (7 marks)

---

**Q.B (b)** Explain the TCP TIME_WAIT state. Why is it relevant during forensic analysis? A forensic investigator discovers 1000 connections in TIME_WAIT on a Linux host where the default `tcp_fin_timeout` sysctl has been changed to 3600 seconds. What does this suggest, and what command would you use to confirm the sysctl value? *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**
**TIME_WAIT definition**: When a TCP connection is gracefully closed, the *active closer* (the side that sent the first FIN) enters TIME_WAIT for 2 × MSL (Maximum Segment Lifetime). This delay ensures that any stray final segments from the network are discarded before the 4-tuple is reused, preventing old data from corrupting a new connection on the same port pair.

**Forensic relevance**:
1. TIME_WAIT sockets represent **recently terminated connections** — they prove a connection *did* exist, even if it is no longer active.
2. An unusually **large number** of TIME_WAITs to a single remote IP suggests rapid connect/disconnect activity (port scanning, beaconing, or burst exfiltration).
3. Abnormal **duration** of TIME_WAIT (longer than the OS default) may indicate **anti-forensic tuning** by an attacker trying to preserve evidence of their connections, or to delay socket reuse for evasion.

**Interpretation of the 1000 connections + 3600-second sysctl**:
- Default `tcp_fin_timeout` on Linux is **60 seconds**.
- A value of **3600 seconds (1 hour)** is **highly anomalous** on a default installation.
- This strongly suggests an attacker (or malware) with root privileges has modified the kernel parameter to inflate the socket table's forensic footprint, potentially to:
  - Obscure a recently terminated C2 channel that they do not want reaped.
  - Force the legitimate investigator to see a confusingly large set of stale connections.
  - Potentially trigger a **socket exhaustion denial-of-service** as a side effect.

**Command to confirm**:

```bash
sysctl net.ipv4.tcp_fin_timeout
cat /proc/sys/net/ipv4/tcp_fin_timeout
```

Either command returns the live value. To verify it persists across reboots, inspect `/etc/sysctl.conf` and files in `/etc/sysctl.d/`. The presence of the modified value in these files confirms it was **deliberately set**, not a transient runtime tweak. (7 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "listening port" with "established connection"** — These are different states. A port can be *listening* (waiting) without any client. Don't equate them.
> 2. **Forgetting the chain-of-custody angle** — Just running `netstat` is not enough; you must mention output redirection to external media, hashing (SHA-256), and timestamping for full KTU marks.
> 3. **Mixing up Windows and Linux command syntax** — `netstat -ano` (Windows) ≠ `netstat -tunap` (Linux). Examiners specifically check for cross-platform command knowledge.
> 4. **Omitting the PID** — A network connection without a process owner is half-evidence. Always resolve to the PID and then to the binary path.
> 5. **Ignoring ephemeral port ranges** — A student who says "the source port is 50000, therefore it must be a server" loses marks. 50000 is ephemeral → *client*.
> 6. **Forgetting TIME_WAIT** — Examiners love this state. Mention 2 × MSL explicitly.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **5-tuple** uniquely identifies every network flow: {SrcIP, SrcPort, DstIP, DstPort, Protocol}.
- **TCP states** of forensic value: LISTENING, ESTABLISHED, TIME_WAIT, CLOSE_WAIT, SYN_SENT.
- **Linux process states**: R, S, D (uninterruptible — forensic gold), Z (zombie), T, I.
- **Tool chain**:
  - Windows: `netstat -ano`, `tasklist`, `wmic process`, PowerShell `Get-NetTCPConnection`.
  - Linux: `ss -tunap`, `ps aux`, `lsof -i`, `/proc/[PID]/` virtual filesystem.
- **Order of volatility** places process tables and socket tables in **Layer 3** — capture before powering off.
- **TIME_WAIT** = 2 × MSL. Linux MSL ≈ 30s → TIME_WAIT ≈ 60s default. Windows ≈ 60s MSL → 120s.
- **Ephemeral ports**: 49152–65535 (IANA). Indicates the *client* side.
- **Privileged ports**: 0–1023. Indicates the *server* side.
- **Digital signature + parent process + command line** is the triumvirate that distinguishes legitimate `svchost.exe` from a masquerade.
- **Cross-validate** with multiple tools; differences between tools can themselves be indicators of compromise.
- **Always** redirect output to external media and hash (SHA-256) every artifact.
- **Remember**: A network connection without a process owner is incomplete evidence; a process without network context is a snapshot without a story.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process-to-Port Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process-to-Port Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Process-to-Port Mapping** is the forensic technique of establishing a definitive correlation between a running **process** (identified by its Process ID/PID and executable image) and the **network socket** (identified by the IP address, transport-layer protocol, and port number) it has bound, opened, or is actively communicating through. In the context of Windows and Linux Forensics, it is the foundational investigative step that transforms an opaque network endpoint into a named, attributable software entity — the *who* behind the *what* on the wire.

Formally, every active communication channel in a host can be described as a **5-tuple**:

$$\text{Connection} = \{ \text{Protocol},\ \text{Local\_IP},\ \text{Local\_Port},\ \text{Remote\_IP},\ \text{Remote\_Port} \}$$

A Process-to-Port Map resolves the **6th element** of this tuple: the owning `PID` and the parent process tree.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 2 objective explicitly requires students to demonstrate the ability to *enumerate live processes, identify listening ports, and bind the process owner to the socket* using native OS utilities. This is a direct CO (Course Outcome) mapping toward **CO2 — "Perform live-system forensic acquisition and correlation in Windows and Linux environments."**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Switchboard" Model

Imagine a large international hotel (your **Operating System**). Every guest who checks in (every **incoming TCP/UDP connection**) is assigned a unique **room number** (the **port number** between 0–65535). Behind the front desk sits a **switchboard operator** (the **kernel's socket subsystem**). When a guest makes a call or receives one, the switchboard must instantly know **which room is associated with which guest's name on the registration card**.

- The **guest's name** = the **Process name** (e.g., `svchost.exe`, `sshd`)
- The **room number** = the **Port number** (e.g., `443`, `22`)
- The **registration card** = the **socket descriptor** in the kernel

Now imagine a security investigator (forensic analyst) walks in and asks: *"Who is in room 1337 right now, and what did they sign in with?"* The switchboard pulls up the ledger — this is **Process-to-Port Mapping**. Without it, an attacker hiding inside the hotel looks like just another anonymous guest. With it, the investigator sees the masquerade immediately.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in forensics:** A port by itself is meaningless evidence. `0.0.0.0:4444` could be Metasploit, a benign admin tool, or a backup agent. Only by binding it to a `PID` and an executable path does it become **attributable, admissible, and actionable** intelligence.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Process** | An instance of a program in execution, with its own virtual address space, identified by a **PID**. |
| **Port (TCP/UDP)** | A 16-bit logical endpoint (range **0 – 65535**) used to multiplex network traffic to the correct application. |
| **Socket** | The OS-level endpoint = `(IP, Port, Protocol)` pair, plus state. |
| **Listening Port** | A port bound by a process waiting for incoming connections (state = `LISTEN`). |
| **Established Port** | A port on the active side of a completed TCP handshake (state = `ESTABLISHED`). |
| **PID** | Process Identifier — a non-negative integer uniquely assigned by the kernel. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Port Binding

A complete Process-to-Port mapping is produced through a deterministic four-stage pipeline. Every forensic tool (Windows or Linux) ultimately traverses this same logical chain.

**Step 1 — Socket Creation**
A user-mode process invokes a syscall (Windows: `Winsock2` / `WSASocket`; Linux: `socket()`) requesting a new socket descriptor. At this moment, the kernel allocates an internal `file`/`socket` structure but **no port is yet bound**.

**Step 2 — Binding to a Local Endpoint**
The process calls `bind()` (Windows) or `bind()` (Linux) specifying the local IP and port. If the port is privileged (<1024 on Linux) and the process lacks `CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE`, the syscall fails — a frequent forensic indicator of misconfigured malware.

**Step 3 — Listen / Connect**
- A *server* process calls `listen()`, transitioning the socket to the **LISTEN** state.
- A *client* process calls `connect()`, transitioning to **SYN_SENT → ESTABLISHED**.

**Step 4 — Forensic Enumeration**
The investigator (or the forensic tool) queries kernel-maintained tables — in Windows, the **TDI / Winsock Catalog** and the **EProcess** linked lists; in Linux, the `/proc/net/tcp`, `/proc/net/udp`, and `/proc/<pid>/fd` pseudo-filesystems — and **joins** the socket metadata to the process metadata via the PID.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Table — Port Number Categories

> [!NOTE]
> The **IANA** (Internet Assigned Numbers Authority) classifies the 16-bit port space into three regulatory bands. Memorize the boundaries and the well-known examples below — they are recurring KTU questions.

| Port Range | Category | Allocation Authority | Forensic Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0 – 1023** | **Well-Known Ports** | IANA (strict) | Reserved for system / root services. Anything user-land listening here is anomalous. |
| **1024 – 49151** | **Registered Ports** | IANA (loose) | Vendor-assigned for client/server apps. Common malware landing zone. |
| **49152 – 65535** | **Dynamic / Private / Ephemeral Ports** | OS-chosen at runtime | Used as the **source port** of outbound client connections. High churn — captures require timestamping. |

### Critical Well-Known Ports to Memorize

| Port | Protocol | Service | Forensic Red Flag |
|---|---|---|---|
| **20/21** | TCP | FTP | Cleartext credentials — frequent exfiltration channel. |
| **22** | TCP | SSH | Brute-force target; root shells on compromised boxes. |
| **23** | TCP | Telnet | Legacy cleartext; should not exist on modern systems. |
| **25** | TCP | SMTP | Open relays abused for spam / phishing. |
| **53** | TCP/UDP | DNS | **DNS tunneling** exfiltration over TXT/NULL records. |
| **80 / 443** | TCP | HTTP / HTTPS | C2 (Command & Control) beacons often masquerade here. |
| **135 / 139 / 445** | TCP | SMB / RPC | EternalBlue, WannaCry, lateral movement. |
| **3389** | TCP | RDP | Hands-on-keyboard attacker access. |
| **4444** | TCP | Metasploit default | **Iconic Metasploit handler port.** |
| **5900** | TCP | VNC | Unattended remote desktops — frequent backdoor. |
| **8080 / 8443** | TCP | HTTP alt-proxy | Webshells / reverse-proxy C2. |

## 2.3 The Cross-Platform Tool Matrix

| Task | Windows Command | Linux Command | Forensic Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| List listening ports with PID | `netstat -ano -p tcp` | `ss -tulpn` | Port → PID |
| Process-to-port (full) | `netstat -anb` (admin) | `lsof -i` | Port → PID + executable path |
| Detailed socket state | `Get-NetTCPConnection` (PowerShell) | `ss -tan state established` | 5-tuple + state |
| Process tree | `tasklist /v` or `Get-Process` | `ps auxf` or `pstree -p` | Parent/child lineage |
| Open file handles of a PID | `handle.exe -p <PID>` (Sysinternals) | `ls -l /proc/<PID>/fd` | Sockets appear as file descriptors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Trap:** Students often confuse **`netstat -an`** (anonymous — no PID, fast) with **`netstat -ano`** (with PID, slightly slower). Only the `-o` flag produces the **O**wner PID required for the mapping. Examiners *will* deduct marks if the PID column is missing.

## 2.4 The Forensic Utility of Process-to-Port Mapping

1. **C2 Detection** — A `powershell.exe` process bound to `0.0.0.0:4444` with an `ESTABLISHED` connection to an external IP is the textbook signature of a reverse shell.
2. **Lateral Movement Tracing** — A `svchost.exe -k netsvcs` instance holding an outbound `:445` connection to a peer host indicates SMB-based pivoting.
3. **Living-off-the-Land (LOLBins)** — Detecting `rundll32.exe`, `mshta.exe`, or `bitsadmin.exe` holding non-standard ports flags fileless malware.
4. **Timestamp Correlation** — A new listening port appearing in the netstat snapshot, whose creation time predates a known user login, indicates a **persistence implant**.
5. **Memory Forensics Hand-off** — The PIDs identified here become the inputs to Volatility's `netscan` and `pslist` plugins for cross-validation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Example

## 3.1 Decoding a `netstat -ano` Output Line

Consider the following forensic excerpt obtained on a suspect Windows host:

```
Proto  Local Address          Foreign Address        State           PID
TCP    0.0.0.0:135            0.0.0.0:0              LISTENING       1124
TCP    0.0.0.0:445            0.0.0.0:0              LISTENING       4
TCP    192.168.1.50:49158     203.0.113.77:4444      ESTABLISHED     3188
TCP    192.168.1.50:49201     198.51.100.22:443      ESTABLISHED     4024
UDP    0.0.0.0:5353          *:*                                     1064
```

### Step 1 — Tokenize the Columns
Each row is parsed into the **5-tuple** plus PID. The parsing rule is unambiguous:

- **Proto:** Transport-layer protocol (`TCP` or `UDP`).
- **Local Address:** `IP:Port` of the *host's* endpoint. `0.0.0.0` means "listening on all interfaces."
- **Foreign Address:** `IP:Port` of the *remote* peer. `0.0.0.0:0` (or `*:*` for UDP) means no peer is bound yet — characteristic of `LISTENING` sockets.
- **State:** TCP FSM state (LISTENING, ESTABLISHED, TIME_WAIT, etc.). UDP has no state.
- **PID:** Owner process identifier.

### Step 2 — Resolve the Foreign Address:Port

The forensic critical line is row 3. Let us unpack it:

$$
\text{Local} = (192.168.1.50,\ 49158) \quad ; \quad \text{Remote} = (203.0.113.77,\ 4444)
$$

The local port 49158 falls in the **Dynamic / Ephemeral** band — perfectly normal for a client-side outbound connection. The remote port **4444** is the iconic **Metasploit Meterpreter default handler port**. This pairing is *highly suspicious*.

### Step 3 — Correlate PID to Executable

Run the auxiliary command:

```
tasklist /FI "PID eq 3188"
```

Suppose the output reveals:

```
Image Name                     PID      Mem Usage
=====================================================
powershell.exe                 3188     84,032 K
```

### Step 4 — Build the Forensic Inference

$$
\text{Inference} = \begin{cases} \text{Process:} & \texttt{powershell.exe (PID 3188)} \\ \text{Local Endpoint:} & 192.168.1.50 : 49158 \\ \text{Remote Endpoint:} & 203.0.113.77 : 4444 \\ \text{Protocol:} & \text{TCP, ESTABLISHED} \\ \text{Likely Behavior:} & \text{Reverse shell / Meterpreter C2 channel} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 5 — Cross-Validate with Parent Process

A legitimate `powershell.exe` is spawned by `explorer.exe` (interactive) or `svchost.exe` (some services). If `wmic process where (ProcessId=3188) get ParentProcessId,Name` returns a parent PID that resolves to `cmd.exe` spawned by `WINWORD.EXE` (Microsoft Word), the inference is **confirmed** as a malicious macro-driven reverse shell.

## 3.2 The Analytical Decision Matrix

When triaging a process-port pair, an investigator scores it across four forensic dimensions:

| Indicator | Benign Score | Malicious Score | Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Process Name vs Port** | `nginx` on `:443` | `powershell.exe` on `:4444` | High |
| **Parent-Child Lineage** | `svchost.exe → spoolsv.exe` | `WINWORD.EXE → cmd.exe → powershell.exe` | Very High |
| **Remote IP Reputation** | Known corporate CDN | Newly registered, low-reputation AS | Medium |
| **Connection State & Duration** | Short, HTTP-like bursts | Long-lived ESTABLISHED on odd ports | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Rule:** No single indicator is decisive. KTU examiners expect a **cumulative-evidence** answer — present all four dimensions, then state the inferred verdict.

## 3.3 Linux Variant — Reading `/proc/net/tcp`

Linux exposes the same information as a kernel pseudo-file. The example below shows a listener:

```
sl  local_address rem_address   st tx_queue rx_queue tr tm->when retrnsmt   uid  timeout inode
0:  00000000:0050 00000000:0000 0A 00000000:00000000 0        0 0 0 0 0 0  0   12345
```

Decoding the fields:

$$
\text{local\_address} = \underbrace{00000000}_{\text{0.0.0.0 (all interfaces)}} : \underbrace{0050}_{\text{hex of port 80}}
$$

$$
\text{rem\_address} = 00000000:0000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{No remote peer (LISTEN state)}
$$

$$
\text{st} = 0A_{\text{hex}} = 10_{\text{decimal}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{TCP state LISTEN (per Linux include/net/tcp_states.h)}
$$

The **`inode`** field (`12345`) is the critical join key. The investigator then executes:

```
ls -l /proc/*/fd/* 2>/dev/null | grep "socket:\[12345\]"
```

The first `*` that matches yields the **PID** that owns the socket — completing the Process-to-Port Map.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Reminder:** Port numbers in `/proc/net/tcp` and `/proc/net/udp` are printed in **hexadecimal** (lowercase, network byte order). The port value `0050` is **not** `50` decimal — although coincidentally equal here, the examiner will test you on a port like `0x1F90` (which is `8080` decimal) or `0xC000` (`49152` decimal).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Process-to-Port Mapping Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Suspect Host Booted] --> B[Process P Initiated by Kernel or User]
    B --> C[Process P requests socket from kernel]
    C --> D{Bind to Local Port?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Socket enters LISTEN state]
    D -- No --> F[Connect to remote IP and Port]
    E --> G[Remote peer connects]
    G --> H[Socket enters ESTABLISHED state]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Kernel updates socket table in memory]
    I --> J[Forensic tool queries socket table]
    J --> K[netstat or ss or lsof executes]
    K --> L[Joins socket inode to PID]
    L --> M[Resolves PID to executable image and path]
    M --> N[Forensic analyst evaluates Process to Port tuple]
    N --> O{Indicator of Compromise?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Flag for incident response]
    O -- No --> Q[Mark as benign baseline]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Cross-Platform Tool Decision Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start[Investigator arrives at live system] --> OS{Operating System?}
    OS -- Windows --> W1[Open elevated cmd or PowerShell]
    W1 --> W2[Run netstat minus ano]
    W2 --> W3[Extract PID column]
    W3 --> W4[Run tasklist slash FI for each PID]
    W4 --> W5[Build process to port map]
    OS -- Linux --> L1[Open terminal as root]
    L1 --> L2[Run ss minus tulpn]
    L2 --> L3[Extract PID and exe column]
    L3 --> L4[Run ps minus p for each PID]
    L4 --> L5[Build process to port map]
    W5 --> Merge[Unified forensic record]
    L5 --> Merge
    Merge --> Output[Report with timestamp and chain of custody]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Forensic Correlation Engine (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        A1[Live netstat snapshot]
        A2[Live tasklist snapshot]
        A3[/proc net tcp snapshot]
        A4[/proc pid fd snapshot]
    end
    subgraph CorrelationLayer[Correlation Layer]
        B1[Socket table parser]
        B2[Process table parser]
        B3[Inode to PID joiner]
        B4[Parent child reconstructor]
    end
    subgraph AnalysisLayer[Analysis Layer]
        C1[Port reputation engine]
        C2[Process reputation engine]
        C3[Lineage anomaly detector]
        C4[Timeline correlator]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        D1[Process to Port tuple table]
        D2[Anomaly report]
        D3[Chain of custody log]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A4 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B2 --> B4
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> C3
    C1 --> C2 --> C4
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    C4 --> D2
    C1 --> D3
    C2 --> D3
    C3 --> D3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram above mirrors the *Volatility `netscan` + `pslist` + `pstree` cross-validation* pipeline used in memory forensics, illustrating that the live-system Process-to-Port Map and the post-mortem memory map must be reconciled for evidentiary integrity.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Process-to-Port Mapping. Why is it considered a foundational step in live-system forensics?**

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

Process-to-Port Mapping is the technique of correlating a running process (identified by its PID and executable) with the network socket — specifically the local IP, transport protocol, and port number — that the process is bound to or communicating through. It is foundational in live-system forensics because:

1. **Attribution:** A port by itself is anonymous; the PID binds the socket to a named executable, transforming a network artifact into an attributable entity.
2. **Incident Triage:** Investigators can rapidly identify C2 channels, backdoors, and reverse shells (e.g., `powershell.exe` on `:4444`).
3. **Evidence Collection:** The output forms a snapshot admissible in chain-of-custody, later cross-validated with memory forensics (Volatility `netscan`).
4. **Anomaly Detection:** Unusual process-port pairings (e.g., `svchost.exe` on a non-standard port) are first indicators of compromise (IOCs).

*(Examiner allocates 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four justifications.)*

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Well-Known, Registered, and Dynamic/Private ports with examples.**

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Port Range | Category | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **0 – 1023** | Well-Known Ports (IANA strict, root-only binding on Linux) | 22 (SSH), 80 (HTTP), 443 (HTTPS) |
| **1024 – 49151** | Registered Ports (vendor applications) | 3306 (MySQL), 8080 (HTTP-alt) |
| **49152 – 65535** | Dynamic / Private / Ephemeral (OS-assigned for outbound) | Source port of a Chrome HTTPS connection |

*(1 mark for the range, 1 mark for correct example per row, 1 mark for distinguishing the binding privilege difference.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module 2 carries one 14-mark question with internal choice. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Full-model solutions are required.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Explain in detail the procedure to perform Process-to-Port Mapping on a live Windows system. List the commands and interpret a sample output containing at least three entries — one legitimate, one suspicious, and one anomalous UDP entry.** *(7 marks)*

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Adapted]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Elevation and Baseline Acquisition**
Open an elevated (Administrator) Command Prompt or PowerShell. Elevation is mandatory because `netstat -b` requires it to resolve the executable image behind each socket.

**Step 2 — Execute the Mapping Command**
The most informative single command is:

```
netstat -ano -b
```

Flags explained:

- `-a` : display all connections and listening ports
- `-n` : numerical addresses (no DNS resolution — preserves forensic speed and accuracy)
- `-o` : **owning PID** (the critical join key)
- `-b` : executable name (requires elevation; may take seconds per entry)

**Step 3 — Sample Output Interpretation**

```
Proto  Local Address         Foreign Address      State         PID    Executable
TCP    0.0.0.0:80            0.0.0.0:0            LISTENING     4      System
TCP    192.168.1.50:49222    198.51.100.5:443     ESTABLISHED   4012   chrome.exe
TCP    192.168.1.50:49887    203.0.113.77:4444    ESTABLISHED   3188   powershell.exe
UDP    192.168.1.50:5353     *:*                  NONE          1064   svchost.exe
```

**Entry 1 — Legitimate (Row 1):**
- Process: `System` (PID 4)
- Port: `80/TCP LISTENING` on all interfaces
- Inference: The Windows HTTP listener, characteristic of a Web server role or the Print Spooler service. Expected on a server-class host.

**Entry 2 — Legitimate (Row 2):**
- Process: `chrome.exe` (PID 4012)
- Port: `49222` (ephemeral local) → `443` (HTTPS remote)
- Inference: Standard outbound HTTPS browsing. The local port in the dynamic range and the remote on a registered port match the textbook web-browsing pattern.

**Entry 3 — Suspicious (Row 3):**
- Process: `powershell.exe` (PID 3188)
- Port: `49887` (ephemeral) → `4444` (Metasploit default handler) at `203.0.113.77`
- Inference: **Highly suspicious reverse shell / Meterpreter C2 channel.** The port `4444` is iconic, the protocol is HTTPS-like but on a non-standard port, and `powershell.exe` is a known LOLBin. The examiner expects the student to name this as a C2 indicator.

**Entry 4 — Anomalous UDP (Row 4):**
- Process: `svchost.exe` (PID 1064) hosting the `fdrespub` service
- Port: `5353/UDP` (mDNS / LLMNR)
- Inference: Legitimate, but if observed on a *server* in a corporate DMZ, the broadcast-like mDNS service represents an information-disclosure vulnerability. Anomalous in *context* (host role), not in *content*.

**Step 4 — Cross-Reference and Document**
Record the timestamp, the full output, and the resolved `tasklist` for each PID. Hash the log file (SHA-256) for chain-of-custody. Export as a forensic artifact.

*(Marks: 2 for procedure, 3 for sample output construction, 2 for forensic inference — total 7.)*

---

#### **(b) Discuss the limitations of live-system Process-to-Port Mapping and how memory forensics complements it. Use appropriate tools and techniques.** *(7 marks)*

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Adapted]`

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations of Live Mapping:**

1. **Volatility of Evidence** — A live system is a moving target. A malicious process may terminate between the `netstat` capture and the `tasklist` lookup, producing an orphan PID.
2. **Rootkit Concealment** — A kernel-level rootkit can unhook the system call used by `netstat` and present a sanitized socket table, hiding the malicious connection.
3. **Insufficient Context** — `netstat` does not reveal the *contents* of a connection, only its endpoints. Encryption (HTTPS) prevents payload inspection.
4. **Permission Gaps** — On locked-down systems, the investigator may not have admin rights to run `netstat -b`.
5. **No Historical State** — Live tools see only *current* sockets; past connections are not retained.

**Memory Forensics Complement (Volatility):**

The investigator acquires a memory image (e.g., using `winpmem`, `FTK Imager`, or `Magnet RAM Capture`) and runs:

- `volatility -f mem.raw --profile=Win10x64 netscan` — enumerates all sockets from kernel pool tags, **including those hidden from `netstat`** by rootkits that do not patch the `netstat` path.
- `volatility -f mem.raw --profile=Win10x64 pslist` and `pstree` — reconstructs the process list from `EProcess` structures, bypassing the `PsActiveProcessHead` hook that some rootkits use.
- `volatility -f mem.raw --profile=Win10x64 malfind` — detects injected code inside legitimate processes (the hidden half of a C2 channel).

**Cross-Validation Method:**

1. Capture live `netstat -ano` output → record PIDs and endpoints.
2. Acquire memory image → run `netscan`.
3. **Diff the two outputs.** Any socket present in `netscan` but absent from `netstat` (or vice versa) is an indicator of rootkit tampering.
4. For each PID appearing in both, verify the executable path matches in `pslist` and on disk. Mismatches indicate PID/process spoofing.

This dual approach satisfies the **forensic principles of redundancy and corroboration**, strengthening the evidentiary value of the final Process-to-Port Map.

*(Marks: 3 for limitations, 2 for Volatility techniques, 2 for the cross-validation method — total 7.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) Demonstrate Process-to-Port Mapping on a live Linux system using `ss`, `lsof`, and the `/proc` filesystem. Provide a step-by-step procedure and interpret a sample output.** *(7 marks)*

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Adapted]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Acquire Live Socket State with `ss`**
The modern replacement for `netstat`:

```
sudo ss -tulpn
```

Flags:
- `-t` : TCP sockets
- `-u` : UDP sockets
- `-l` : listening sockets
- `-p` : show process (requires root)
- `-n` : numerical (no DNS)

**Sample Output:**

```
Netid  State      Recv-Q  Send-Q   Local Address:Port    Peer Address:Port
tcp    LISTEN     0       128      0.0.0.0:22            0.0.0.0:*        users:(("sshd",pid=892,fd=3))
tcp    LISTEN     0       128      127.0.0.1:3306        0.0.0.0:*        users:(("mysqld",pid=1156,fd=10))
tcp    ESTAB      0       0        192.168.1.20:51234    203.0.113.55:1337 users:(("bash",pid=4201,fd=3))
udp    UNCONN     0       0        0.0.0.0:53            0.0.0.0:*        users:(("named",pid=1102,fd=7))
```

**Interpretation:**

| Row | Process | Port | State | Inference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `sshd` (PID 892) | 22/TCP | LISTEN | Legitimate OpenSSH server. |
| 2 | `mysqld` (PID 1156) | 3306/TCP loopback | LISTEN | Database server bound to localhost only — good security posture. |
| 3 | `bash` (PID 4201) | ephemeral → `203.0.113.55:1337` | ESTABLISHED | **Highly suspicious** — a `bash` process holding a long-lived connection to a remote port `1337` ("leet") is characteristic of a reverse shell, likely spawned by an exploit in a web app. |
| 4 | `named` (PID 1102) | 53/UDP | UNCONN | Legitimate BIND DNS server. |

**Step 2 — Cross-Verify with `lsof`**

```
sudo lsof -i -P -n
```

This produces a per-process view of all open network files, useful for redundancy.

**Step 3 — Correlate via `/proc` Inode**

The `/proc/net/tcp` and `/proc/net/udp` files contain an `inode` column. To find the PID:

```
sudo ls -l /proc/*/fd 2>/dev/null | grep "socket:\[<INODE>\]"
```

**Step 4 — Build the Forensic Record**
Combine `ss -tulpn`, `lsof -i`, and the `/proc` join into a single tabular forensic artifact, hashed and timestamped.

*(Marks: 2 for procedure, 3 for output interpretation, 2 for the /proc join — total 7.)*

---

#### **(b) An investigator discovers the following output on a Linux server. Identify the anomalies, attribute them to processes, and recommend containment steps.** *(7 marks)*

**Given Output:**

```
Proto  Recv-Q  Send-Q  Local Address         Foreign Address       State       PID/Program
tcp    0       0      0.0.0.0:8080          0.0.0.0:*            LISTEN      2210/java
tcp    0       0      0.0.0.0:4444          0.0.0.0:*            LISTEN      3344/perl
tcp    0       0      192.168.1.20:44321    185.220.101.45:9050  ESTAB       3344/perl
tcp    0       0      192.168.1.20:22       198.51.100.7:51422   ESTAB       1140/sshd
```

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Model Solution:**

**Anomaly 1 — `perl` on port `4444` (LISTEN):**
A `perl` interpreter listening on a non-standard port is a classic signature of a **Perl-based reverse shell handler** (e.g., a CPAN script). `perl` is not a server binary; the only legitimate reason it would be `LISTEN` is as a forensic implant or a custom backdoor.

**Anomaly 2 — `185.220.101.45:9050` (Tor exit node):**
Port `9050` is the default **Tor SOCKS proxy** port. The IP `185.220.101.45` is a known **Tor exit node** range. The `ESTABLISHED` connection from PID 3344 (`perl`) to this endpoint is a strong indicator of **Tor-based C2 exfiltration**.

**Anomaly 3 — Process Reuse:**
The same `perl` PID (3344) holds *both* the listening port and the outbound connection — a textbook reverse-shell design: a listener on `:4444` and an outbound beacon to the C2 over Tor.

**Attribution Table:**

| Indicator | Process | Port | Endpoint | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Listening backdoor | `perl` (PID 3344) | 4444/TCP | 0.0.0.0 | Reverse shell listener |
| C2 channel | `perl` (PID 3344) | ephemeral → 9050 | 185.220.101.45 (Tor) | Tor-based exfiltration |
| Benign baseline | `java` (PID 2210) | 8080/TCP | 0.0.0.0 | Likely Tomcat — needs verification |
| Benign baseline | `sshd` (PID 1140) | 22/TCP ESTAB | 198.51.100.7 | Admin session — verify against allow-list |

**Recommended Containment Steps:**

1. **Isolate** — Disconnect the host from the network (cable pull or VLAN quarantine) to prevent further exfiltration.
2. **Acquire Volatile Evidence** — Immediately capture memory (`avml` or `LiME`), then disk, *before* rebooting.
3. **Kill the Malicious Process** — `sudo kill -9 3344` and verify the port is freed with `ss -tulpn | grep 4444`.
4. **Identify the Dropper** — `ls -l /proc/3344/exe` reveals the on-disk path of the `perl` script. Hash it (SHA-256) and search the file system for other instances.
5. **Check Persistence** — Inspect `crontab`, `/etc/systemd/system/`, `~/.bashrc`, and `/etc/rc.local` for any reference to the malicious script.
6. **Block at the Perimeter** — Add the Tor exit-node IP and the `:4444`/`:9050` destination ports to the firewall deny-list.
7. **Rotate Credentials** — All credentials used on or accessible from the host must be considered compromised.
8. **Document Chain of Custody** — Every command and its output must be hashed and logged.

*(Marks: 3 for anomaly identification with reasoning, 2 for attribution table, 2 for containment steps — total 7.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `-o` flag in `netstat`** — yields no PID; the entire mapping collapses. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing LISTEN and ESTABLISHED** — a `LISTEN` is local-only; an `ESTABLISHED` is the active channel. Mixing them up breaks the C2 inference. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Reporting process name without parent lineage** — examiners want *parent → child* because the parent (e.g., `WINWORD.EXE`) is often more indicative of compromise than the child (`powershell.exe`). (–2 marks)
> 4. **Quoting a port in decimal when `/proc/net/tcp` prints hex** (e.g., reporting `0x50` as `80` is fine, but misreading `0xC000` as `49152` is forgivable; *failing to state the base* is not). (–1 mark)
> 5. **Omitting chain-of-custody hashing** in the procedure — the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned COs require evidentiary integrity. (–1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Process-to-Port Mapping = binding a `PID` + executable to a `(Local_IP, Local_Port, Protocol)` socket.
- **The 5-tuple:** `{Protocol, Local_IP, Local_Port, Remote_IP, Remote_Port}` is the network identity; the **6th element** (PID) is what forensic mapping adds.
- **Port Bands (memorize):** **0–1023** Well-Known, **1024–49151** Registered, **49152–65535** Dynamic/Private.
- **Iconic Forensic Ports:** `:4444` (Metasploit), `:3389` (RDP), `:445` (SMB), `:5900` (VNC), `:9050` (Tor), `:8080`/`8443` (alt-web C2).
- **Windows Primary Command:** `netstat -ano -b` (admin) — the `-o` flag is the *O*wner PID.
- **Windows PowerShell:** `Get-NetTCPConnection | Select-Object LocalAddress, LocalPort, OwningProcess, State`.
- **Linux Modern Command:** `ss -tulpn` (preferred over deprecated `netstat`).
- **Linux Legacy / Detail:** `lsof -i -P -n` and the `/proc/net/tcp` + `/proc/<pid>/fd` inode join.
- **Remember:** Port numbers in `/proc/net/tcp` are in **hexadecimal**.
- **Suspicious Pairings:** `powershell.exe` / `cmd.exe` / `bash` / `perl` on `:4444`, `:1337`, `:8080` (when not a known web service).
- **Suspicious Lineage:** `WINWORD.EXE → cmd.exe → powershell.exe` (macro attack); `apache2 → bash` (web-shell).
- **Rootkit Defense:** Always corroborate live `netstat`/`ss` output with memory forensics (Volatility `netscan`, `pslist`).
- **Chain of Custody:** Hash (SHA-256) every captured output, timestamp it, and store write-protected.
- **Urgency Principle:** Live evidence is volatile — capture the Process-to-Port Map *first*, then deep-dive.
- **CO Mapping (KTU 2024):** This topic directly serves **CO2** (Live-system acquisition) and **CO4** (Incident response correlation).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Process Memory** refers to the virtual address space allocated by the operating system (OS) to each running process, encompassing code, data, stack, heap, and shared library segments. In the context of **digital forensics**, process memory is the volatile RAM content associated with a process at the time of acquisition, which contains critical artifacts such as encryption keys, decrypted plaintext, network credentials, running threads, loaded modules, registry hives, open file handles, and remnants of malicious code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT866 - M2):** Process Memory is the volatile, per-process virtual address space managed by the OS kernel. In Windows, it is represented by the `_EPROCESS` and `_KPROCESS` kernel structures; in Linux, by the `task_struct` and `mm_struct`. Memory forensics is the discipline of acquiring and analyzing this volatile space using tools such as **Volatility**, **FTK Imager**, and **LiME (Linux Memory Extractor)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Mailroom of an Office Building"**
> Imagine a large corporate office building (the **Physical RAM**) with hundreds of employees (processes) working simultaneously. Each employee has their own **P.O. Box** (virtual address space) labeled with a unique number. The **mailroom clerk** (the **Memory Management Unit / MMU**) translates every P.O. Box number into a physical shelf in the mailroom using a translation ledger (the **page table**). When a forensic investigator arrives, they cannot simply read the mailroom shelves directly because the translation ledger is needed. By capturing the mailroom clerk's ledger and the current state of all mailboxes at a frozen moment in time, the investigator can reconstruct what every employee was doing — even if the employee has since left the building.

### 1.3 Key Metrics and Boundaries

| Parameter | Windows (x64) | Linux (x64) |
|---|---|---|
| **Default Virtual Address Space (User Mode)** | **128 TB** (`0x00000000 00000000` – `0x00007FFF FFFFFFFF`) | **128 TB** (`0x00000000 00000000` – `0x00007FFFFFFFFFFF`) |
| **Kernel Virtual Address Space** | **128 TB** | **128 TB** |
| **Page Size** | **4 KB** (standard), **2 MB** (Large), **1 GB** (Huge) | **4 KB** (standard), **2 MB** (Huge), **1 GB** (Gigantic) |
| **Max Physical RAM (typical server)** | **6 TB** (Win Server 2022) | **64 TB** (kernel dependent) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The management of process memory is governed by four interdependent mechanisms:

- **Virtual Address Translation:** Every memory access issued by a process uses a **virtual address**. The CPU's **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** consults the **page table** maintained by the kernel to resolve the virtual address into a **physical address**.
- **Paging:** Memory is divided into fixed-size **pages** (typically **4 KB**). This eliminates external fragmentation and enables demand paging.
- **Demand Paging & Swapping:** Pages not actively in use are evicted to the **page file** (`pagefile.sys` in Windows, `swap partition` in Linux) and reloaded on demand.
- **Memory Protection & Isolation:** Each process operates in its own virtual address space, enforced by the MMU using **ring levels** (Windows) or **CPL (Current Privilege Level)** (Linux). User-mode processes cannot directly read kernel memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Process Memory Forensics**
> When malware executes, it leaves traces in process memory that are NOT present on disk. This includes: unpacked payloads, in-memory injected DLLs, network connections and sockets, registry keys opened in RAM, decrypted configuration strings, and C2 (Command & Control) communication buffers. Capturing process memory before the system powers down is the **only** way to recover these volatile artifacts.

### 2.2 Anatomy of a Process Memory Layout

Every process has a logical layout comprising five canonical regions:

| Region | Purpose | Growth Direction | Forensic Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Text Segment (.text)** | Executable machine code (CPU instructions) | Fixed | Contains injected shellcode; check for RWX anomalies |
| **Data Segment (.data / .bss)** | Initialized and uninitialized global/static variables | Fixed | May contain C2 URLs, decryption keys |
| **Heap** | Dynamic allocations (`malloc`, `new`) | Grows **upward** (high addresses) | Buffer overflow targets; malicious payload staging |
| **Stack** | Function frames, return addresses, local variables | Grows **downward** (low addresses) | ROP chain artifacts; saved credentials |
| **Shared Libraries / Mapped Files** | DLLs (Windows) / .so files (Linux) | Variable | DLL hijacking, hollowing artifacts |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all high-yield formulas and parameters required for KTU ESE questions on Process Memory.

| Concept | Formula / Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Virtual-to-Physical Translation | `PA = (PFN × PageSize) + offset` | `PA` = Physical Address, `PFN` = Page Frame Number, `offset` = intra-page displacement |
| Number of Pages per Process | `N = ceil(VAS / PageSize)` | `VAS` = Virtual Address Space size, `PageSize` = 4096 B (standard) |
| Page Table Size (1-level, theoretical) | `PTS = 2^n × EntrySize` | `n` = virtual page number bits, `EntrySize` = 8 B (x64 PTE) |
| Working Set | `WS = Σ (pages in physical RAM for a process)` | Excludes swapped-out pages; visible via `GetProcessMemoryInfo` / `/proc/[pid]/status` |
| Resident Set Size (RSS) | `RSS = WS × PageSize` | In bytes; visible in `ps` output column `RSS` |
| Virtual Size (VSZ) | `VSZ = VAS_total × PageSize` | Total virtual memory mapped (Linux `ps`) |
| Compression Ratio (Memory Dump) | `CR = CompressedSize / UncompressedSize` | Typical: 0.30 – 0.60 for raw `.raw` dumps |
| Acquisition Time (Volatility) | `T_a ≈ DumpSize / DiskWriteSpeed` | E.g., 8 GB / 500 MB/s ≈ 16 s + parsing time |
| Hash Integrity | `H = SHA256(DumpFile)` | Mandated by ACPO principle for evidence integrity |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Virtual Address to Physical Address Resolution (x64 4-Level Paging)

The **x86_64** architecture uses a **4-level page table hierarchy**: PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT → Physical Frame.

**Given:**
- Virtual Address (linear): `0x00007FF6 1234 5678`
- Page Size: $4096 \text{ bytes} = 2^{12}$
- PTE Entry Size: $8 \text{ bytes}$
- PML4 Base (CR3): `0x00000000 1AB8 0000`

**Step 1 — Decompose the Virtual Address into 4-level Indices + Offset**

The 48-bit canonical virtual address is split into five fields (4 indices + 1 offset):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VA} &= \underbrace{b_{47..39}}_{\text{PML4 Index}} \;\; \underbrace{b_{38..30}}_{\text{PDPT Index}} \;\; \underbrace{b_{29..21}}_{\text{PD Index}} \;\; \underbrace{b_{20..12}}_{\text{PT Index}} \;\; \underbrace{b_{11..0}}_{\text{Page Offset}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Extract Each Index for `0x00007FF6 1234 5678`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PML4 Index} &= (0x7FF6 \; 1234 \; 5678) \gg 39 \; \& \; 0x1FF = 0 \times 0 = 0 \\
\text{PDPT Index}  &= (0x7FF6 \; 1234 \; 5678) \gg 30 \; \& \; 0x1FF = 0 \times 1FF = 511 \\
\text{PD Index}    &= (0x7FF6 \; 1234 \; 5678) \gg 21 \; \& \; 0x1FF = 0 \times 0AB = 171 \\
\text{PT Index}    &= (0x7FF6 \; 1234 \; 5678) \gg 12 \; \& \; 0x1FF = 0 \times 123 = 291 \\
\text{Offset}      &= 0x7FF6 \; 1234 \; 5678 \; \& \; 0xFFF = 0x678
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Walk the Page Table (PML4E Lookup)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PML4 Base (CR3)} &= \text{0x00000000 1AB8 0000} \\
\text{PML4E Address}   &= \text{PML4 Base} + (\text{PML4 Index} \times 8) \\
\text{PML4E Address}   &= \text{0x1AB80000} + (0 \times 8) = \text{0x1AB80000}
\end{aligned}
$$

The PML4E is read from physical memory; assume its value is `0x00000000 2B40 0067` (with bit 0 = Present, bits 51:M = PDPT base).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PDPT Base} &= 0x00000000 \; 2B40 \; 0000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Walk to PDPT**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PDPTE Address} &= \text{PDPT Base} + (511 \times 8) = 0x2B40 \; 0000 + 0xFF8 = 0x2B40 \; 0FF8
\end{aligned}
$$

Assume PDPTE value = `0x00000000 3C20 0067`. PD Base = `0x3C20 0000`.

**Step 5 — Walk to PD**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PDE Address} &= \text{PD Base} + (171 \times 8) = 0x3C20 \; 0000 + 0x558 = 0x3C20 \; 0558
\end{aligned}
$$

Assume PDE value = `0x00000000 4D80 0067`. PT Base = `0x4D80 0000`.

**Step 6 — Walk to PT (Final Page Table)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PTE Address} &= \text{PT Base} + (291 \times 8) = 0x4D80 \; 0000 + 0x918 = 0x4D80 \; 0918
\end{aligned}
$$

Assume PTE value = `0x00000000 5E90 0025` (Present=1, Read/Write=0, etc.). Physical Frame Base = `0x5E90 0000`.

**Step 7 — Compute the Final Physical Address**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical Address} &= (\text{Physical Frame Base}) + \text{Offset} \\
\text{Physical Address} &= 0x00000000 \; 5E90 \; 0000 + 0x678 \\
\text{Physical Address} &= 0x00000000 \; 5E90 \; 0678
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Insight:** Memory forensics tools like **Volatility** read the `CR3` register value from the `_KPROCESS` (Windows) or `task_struct.mm.pgd` (Linux) structure to seed the page table walk for each process. This is how virtual addresses are translated back during offline analysis.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Memory Dump Size Estimation

**Given:** System with 16 GB physical RAM, OS overhead = 1.5 GB, process overhead = 8%, acquisition compression = 40%.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Raw Dump Size}        &= 16 \; \text{GB} \quad (\text{full physical RAM}) \\
\text{Usable Process Space} &= 16 - 1.5 = 14.5 \; \text{GB} \\
\text{Compressed Dump Size} &= 16 \times 0.40 = 6.4 \; \text{GB} \\
