\text{Storage Overhead}    &= 6.4 \times 1.10 = 7.04 \; \text{GB} \quad (\text{including metadata + case log})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Forensic Recommendation:** Always provision **2.5× the physical RAM** as destination storage to accommodate the raw dump, compressed copy, and working copies.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process Memory Artifacts (Windows vs. Linux)

| Artifact | Windows Location | Linux Location | Forensic Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Process List** | `PsActiveProcessHead` → `_EPROCESS` linked list | `init_task` → `task_struct` linked list | `pslist`, `pstree` (Volatility) |
| **Loaded DLLs** | PEB → `Ldr.InMemoryOrderModuleList` | `/proc/[pid]/maps` | `dlllist`, `ldrmodules` |
| **Open Files** | `EProcess.ObjectTable` (handle table) | `/proc/[pid]/fd/` | `handles`, `files` |
| **Network Connections** | TCB structures in kernel pool | `/proc/net/tcp`, `udp`, `unix` | `netscan` (Volatility) |
| **Registry Keys** | CMHIVE registry hive cells | N/A (use `~/.config`, `/etc`) | `hivelist`, `printkey` |
| **Command Line** | PEB → `ProcessParameters.CommandLine` | `/proc/[pid]/cmdline` | `cmdline` plugin |
| **Environment Vars** | PEB → `ProcessParameters.Environment` | `/proc/[pid]/environ` | `envars` plugin |
| **Injected Code** | RX regions in private memory | Anonymous executable mappings | `malfind` (Volatility) |
| **Memory Mapped Files** | `SECTION_OBJECT` pointers | `/proc/[pid]/maps` (shared) | `vadinfo`, `vadwalk` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Virtual Address Space Architecture (x64)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["High Address 0x00007FFFFFFFFFFF"] --> B["Kernel Space 128 TB"]
    B --> C["Low Address 0x0000000000000000"]
    C --> D["User Space 128 TB"]
    D --> E["Stack Region Grows Downward"]
    E --> F["Memory Mapped Files DLLs so"]
    F --> G["Heap Region Grows Upward"]
    G --> H["BSS Segment"]
    H --> I["Data Segment"]
    I --> J["Text Segment .text"]
    J --> K["Virtual Address 0x0"]
    L["MMU Page Table Walker"] --> M["CR3 PML4 Base Register"]
    M --> N["PML4 Level 4"]
    N --> O["PDPT Level 3"]
    O --> P["PD Level 2"]
    P --> Q["PT Level 1"]
    Q --> R["Physical RAM Frame 4KB"]
    R --> S["Forensic Dump File raw lime"]
    S --> T["Volatility Framework"]
    T --> U["Artifact Extraction"]
```

### 4.2 Memory Forensics Acquisition and Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Live System Suspect] --> B[Step 1 Identify Target PID]
    B --> C[Step 2 Acquire RAM Full or Process]
    C --> D[Step 3 Hash SHA256]
    D --> E[Step 4 Chain of Custody Log]
    E --> F[Step 5 Import to Analysis Workstation]
    F --> G[Step 6 Identify OS Profile]
    G --> H[Step 7 Run Volatility Plugins]
    H --> I[Step 8 Extract Artifacts]
    I --> J[Step 9 Correlate with Disk Evidence]
    J --> K[Step 10 Generate Forensic Report]
    K --> L[Court Admissible Evidence]
    A1[Mandated Tools] --> B
    A1[FTK Imager / LiME / WinPMEM / AVML] --> C
    A2[Mandated Practice] --> D
    A2[Write-Block + Hash Verification] --> E
```

### 4.3 Page Table Hierarchy Translation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Virtual Address 48 bit] --> B[Bit 47 to 39 PML4 Index 9 bits]
    A --> C[Bit 38 to 30 PDPT Index 9 bits]
    A --> D[Bit 29 to 21 PD Index 9 bits]
    A --> E[Bit 20 to 12 PT Index 9 bits]
    A --> F[Bit 11 to 0 Offset 12 bits]
    G[CR3 Register] --> H[PML4 Base Address]
    H --> I[PML4E Lookup Read 8 Bytes]
    I --> J[PDPT Base Address]
    J --> K[PDPTE Lookup Read 8 Bytes]
    K --> L[PD Base Address]
    L --> M[PDE Lookup Read 8 Bytes]
    M --> N[PT Base Address]
    N --> O[PTE Lookup Read 8 Bytes]
    O --> P[Physical Frame Base 4KB Aligned]
    P --> Q[Final Physical Address = Frame Base + Offset]
```

### 4.4 Process Memory Forensics Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Suspect Process Memory Region] --> B[Virtual Address Descriptors VAD Tree Windows]
    A --> C[Memory Mapping Tree mm_struct Linux]
    B --> D[Region Metadata Start End Protection]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Private vs Shared Classification]
    E --> F[Executable vs Non-Executable Check]
    F --> G[Injected Code Detection malfind]
    G --> H[Hollowed Process Indicators]
    G --> I[DLL Injection Artifacts]
    G --> J[Reflective Loader Traces]
    H --> K[Forensic Indicator Report]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Incident Response Action]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT866 M2]**

**Q1.** Define **Process Memory**. Differentiate between **virtual address space** and **physical address space** with respect to a Windows process. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Process Memory** is the volatile, per-process virtual address space allocated and managed by the operating system kernel. It contains the executing instructions, stack frames, heap allocations, loaded modules, and data structures required for the process to function. In a forensic context, process memory refers to the live contents of these regions captured at the moment of acquisition.

**Virtual Address Space (VAS):** The logical address range a process can reference. In Windows x64, this is **128 TB** (`0x00000000 00000000` to `0x00007FFF FFFFFFFF` for user mode). The VAS is **isolated per process** and mapped to physical RAM by the MMU.

**Physical Address Space (PAS):** The actual hardware address range of the installed RAM modules. In a typical system with 16 GB of RAM, the PAS ranges from `0x00000000 00000000` to approximately `0x00000003 FFFFFFFF`. The PAS is **shared globally** across all processes.

| Aspect | Virtual Address Space | Physical Address Space |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Per-process (isolated) | Global (shared) |
| **Size (x64)** | 128 TB user + 128 TB kernel | Limited by installed RAM |
| **Resolution** | Via MMU + Page Table | Direct hardware addressing |
| **Forensic Implication** | Each process appears to "own" the full range | Real evidence is scattered in physical frames |

---

**Q2.** List and briefly explain any **three Volatility plugins** used for process memory analysis. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **`pslist` plugin** — Walks the doubly-linked list of active `_EPROCESS` structures anchored at `PsActiveProcessHead` in the Windows kernel. It enumerates all running processes with their PIDs, PPIDs, and timestamps. Forensic value: detects **hidden or terminated** processes that may not appear in `tasklist`.

2. **`malfind` plugin** — Scans the virtual address space of each process for **private memory regions** marked as **executable** (`PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE` = `0x40`) that contain no backing file on disk. These are strong indicators of **code injection**. Forensic value: identifies shellcode, hollowed processes, and reflective DLL loaders.

3. **`netscan` plugin** — Traverses the kernel pool to enumerate all **TCP, UDP, and UNIX socket** structures associated with active and recently closed connections. Forensic value: uncovers **C2 beaconing**, data exfiltration channels, and listening backdoors that have no on-disk artifacts.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Pattern, 14 Marks with Internal Choice]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the **x64 4-level page table translation mechanism** used by modern Windows and Linux kernels. Show how a virtual address is decomposed and resolved to a physical address. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The x86_64 (64-bit) architecture employs a **4-level hierarchical page table** structure: PML4 (Page Map Level 4) → PDPT (Page Directory Pointer Table) → PD (Page Directory) → PT (Page Table). The base of the PML4 is stored in the **CR3** control register (saved per-process in `_KPROCESS` on Windows, and in `task_struct.mm.pgd` on Linux).

**Step 1 — Virtual Address Decomposition (48-bit canonical):**
A linear virtual address `0x00007FF6 1234 5678` is split into:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PML4 Index} &= 9 \text{ bits} = b_{47..39} \\
\text{PDPT Index} &= 9 \text{ bits} = b_{38..30} \\
\text{PD Index}    &= 9 \text{ bits} = b_{29..21} \\
\text{PT Index}    &= 9 \text{ bits} = b_{20..12} \\
\text{Offset}      &= 12 \text{ bits} = b_{11..0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — PML4 Lookup:**
The PML4 base (from CR3) is added to `PML4_Index × 8` (since each PML4E is 8 bytes). The 8-byte PML4E is read; its bits 51:12 give the PDPT base address.

**Step 3 — PDPT Lookup:**
`PDPT_base + PDPT_Index × 8` yields the PDPTE. Its bits 51:12 give the PD base.

**Step 4 — PD Lookup:**
`PD_base + PD_Index × 8` yields the PDE. Its bits 51:12 give the PT base.

**Step 5 — PT Lookup:**
`PT_base + PT_Index × 8` yields the PTE. Its bits 51:12 give the **physical frame base**.

**Step 6 — Final Address Computation:**
`Physical Address = Physical_Frame_Base + Offset`

**Forensic Application:** Volatility's `vtop` (virtual-to-physical) plugin performs this exact walk for every user-mode address during analysis. Without an intact CR3, translation fails and the analyst must rely on heuristics (e.g., pool tag scanning, signature-based carving).

```
Virtual: [ PML4(9) | PDPT(9) | PD(9) | PT(9) | Offset(12) ] = 48 bits
CR3 → PML4[Idx0] → PDPT[Idx511] → PD[Idx171] → PT[Idx291] → PhysFrame
                                                                          + Offset 0x678
```

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **methodology for acquiring and analyzing process memory** for forensic purposes, including pre-acquisition, acquisition, and analysis phases. List the tools used at each phase. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Phase 1 — Pre-Acquisition (Authorization & Preparation)**
- Obtain **written legal authorization** (search warrant, court order, or corporate policy consent).
- Document the **system state**: OS version, patch level, RAM size, running processes via `tasklist` / `ps auxf` (read-only).
- Prepare a **forensically clean target** storage device with capacity ≥ **2.5× physical RAM**.
- Verify tools: FTK Imager (v4.x), WinPMEM, LiME, AVML, Magnet RAM Capture.
- Calibrate the system clock; record deviations.

**Phase 2 — Acquisition (Live Memory Capture)**
- **Method A — Software-based (Kernel Driver):** Load `winpmem` or `memprocfs`; the driver accesses `\Device\PhysicalMemory` (Windows) or `/dev/mem` (Linux) to dump RAM to a `.raw` or `.lime` file.
- **Method B — Hardware-based (DMA):** Use PCIe cards (e.g., Tribble, Inception) to perform Direct Memory Access via FireWire/Thunderbolt — detectable by modern OS, so less common in 2024.
- **Method C — Virtualization Snapshot:** If the suspect is a VM, take a **VMware `.vmem`** or **VirtualBox `.sav`** snapshot — this is **non-invasive** and preferred where applicable.
- Compute **SHA-256 hash** of the dump immediately; record in chain of custody.

**Phase 3 — Analysis (Volatility 3.x)**
- **Profile Identification:** `vol -f mem.raw windows.info` / `linux.banner`
- **Process Enumeration:** `pslist`, `pstree`, `psscan`
- **Loaded Modules:** `dlllist`, `ldrmodules` (look for **unlinked DLLs** — evidence of DLL hollowing)
- **Code Injection:** `malfind`, `hollowfind`, `psxview` (compare 7 enumeration methods)
- **Network:** `netscan`, `netstat` (recover TCP state, even for closed connections)
- **Registry in RAM:** `hivelist`, `printkey`, `hashdump`
- **Credential Extraction:** `lsadump`, `mimikatz` integration, `lsass` dump parsing
- **Timeline Construction:** Combine `timeliner` output with disk artifacts for a unified event timeline.

**Phase 4 — Reporting**
- Generate an **affidavit-grade report** with hash values, tool versions, commands executed, screenshots, and chain of custody log.
- Archive the original dump on **WORM (Write Once Read Many)** media for evidence preservation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Windows `_EPROCESS` structure** and the **Linux `task_struct` / `mm_struct`**, with a focus on how each represents process memory. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Windows `_EPROCESS` + `_KPROCESS` | Linux `task_struct` + `mm_struct` |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Kernel pool memory, pointed to by `PsActiveProcessHead` list | Allocated via `alloc_task_struct_node()`, linked via `tasks` list head |
| **Memory Descriptor** | Embedded in `_EPROCESS` fields like `Pcb` (KPROCESS), `VadRoot` (VAD tree) | `task_struct.mm` points to separate `mm_struct` |
| **Page Directory Base** | `KPROCESS.DirectoryTableBase` (= `CR3` value) | `mm_struct.pgd` (pointer to top-level page table) |
| **Virtual Memory Regions** | **VAD (Virtual Address Descriptor) tree** — balanced binary tree of `_MMVAD` nodes describing committed regions | **Red-Black tree** of `vm_area_struct` (and linked list) describing mapped regions |
| **Loaded Modules** | PEB → `Ldr.InMemoryOrderModuleList` (three doubly-linked lists) | `/proc/[pid]/maps` reads `vm_area_struct` file-backed mappings |
| **Working Set** | `_MMSUPPORT` structure (Windows 7+): `WorkingSetSize`, `PeakWorkingSetSize` | `mm_struct.rss_stat` — Resident Set Size counters per MMU page type |
| **Handle Table** | `ObjectTable` → `HANDLE_TABLE` with `_HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY` arrays | N/A — file descriptors in `files_struct.fdt` |
| **Forensic Walking** | Start at `PsActiveProcessHead`, walk `Flink/Blink` of `_EPROCESS.ActiveProcessLinks` | Start at `init_task.tasks`, walk `task_struct.tasks.next` |
| **Hidden Process Detection** | `psscan` scans pool tags `Proc*` and `PoolTrailer.PoolTag` | `psaux` or `pids` plugins scan `task_struct` slab allocations directly |

**Key Insight for Forensics:**
- Windows uses a **tree-based region map (VAD)** ideal for fast lookup and precise per-region permission metadata.
- Linux uses a **Red-Black tree of `vm_area_struct`**, which is simpler but equally effective.
- The **CR3 value** (Windows) or **`mm->pgd` virtual address + physical resolution** (Linux) is the **single most important value** for memory forensics — without it, virtual-to-physical translation is impossible.

**(b)** Describe the **Volatility `malfind` plugin** in detail. What does it detect, how does it detect it, and what are the indicators it reports? Provide a typical output interpretation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Purpose of `malfind`:**
The `malfind` plugin is a **heuristic-based code injection detector** that scans the user-mode virtual address space of every process for memory regions exhibiting characteristics commonly associated with injected malicious code.

**Detection Mechanism:**
1. **Walks the VAD tree** (Windows) or `vm_area_struct` map (Linux) of each process.
2. Filters for regions meeting ALL of the following criteria:
   - **Private memory** (no mapped file backing — `MappedFile == NULL` on Windows, anonymous mapping on Linux).
   - **Executable and Writable** protection (`PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE = 0x40` on Windows, `rwx` on Linux).
   - Contains a **PE/DOS header signature** (`MZ...PE\0\0`) at the start, OR an `ELF` magic (`\x7FELF`) on Linux, OR pure shellcode patterns.
3. Reports the **VAD start address, end address, protection flags, and a hex dump** of the initial bytes.

**Typical Output:**

```
PID   Process      Start          End            Protection   Details
----  -----------  ------------   ------------   -----------  -------
1456  svchost.exe  0x1f0000       0x1f1000       PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE
                   MZ header at 0x1f0000, suspicious API strings
2892  notepad.exe  0x7ffd0000     0x7ffd1000     PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE
                   No mapped file, PE signature present
```

**Interpretation Heuristics:**
- **`MZ...PE\0\0`** in a private executable region is a **strong injection indicator** (Reflective DLL, Process Hollowing, RunPE).
- **RWX permissions on a private region** is anomalous — legitimate DLLs are mapped RX from a file, and heap/stack are NX.
- **No associated file on disk** confirms it was allocated dynamically (e.g., `VirtualAllocEx` from a remote process).

**Limitations:**
- Cannot detect **sleep-obfuscated** or **encrypted payloads** that do not contain the PE signature.
- High false-positive rate on **Just-In-Time (JIT) compiled** runtimes (Java, .NET, V8) — these legitimately create RWX regions. Cross-reference with `psxview` to confirm.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing virtual and physical addresses** is the #1 error. Always clarify that the **process sees virtual addresses**; forensics tools must resolve them to physical using the page table walk.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the role of CR3** (Windows) / `mm->pgd` (Linux) — this is the **single critical control register** without which virtual-to-physical translation is impossible.
> 3. **Not linking process memory to disk artifacts** — process memory alone is rarely sufficient; examiners must correlate with file system, registry, and event logs.
> 4. **Omitting the SHA-256 hash** and chain of custody — ACPO Principle 1 mandates evidence integrity verification; missing this loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Failing to cite the exact tool versions** (e.g., "Volatility 3.2.1" rather than just "Volatility") — KTU examiners reward specificity.
> 6. **Mixing up "page file" and "process memory"** — pagefile.sys is a **swap** mechanism, not a per-process artifact; the process memory dump comes from physical RAM.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Process Memory (Module 2, PECCT866)**

- ☐ **Process Memory** = the per-process virtual address space, captured from **physical RAM** (not the page file).
- ☐ **Virtual Address Space (x64)** = **128 TB user + 128 TB kernel**; **Physical RAM** is far smaller (typically 8–64 GB).
- ☐ The **MMU** performs virtual-to-physical translation using a **4-level page table**: PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT → Frame.
- ☐ The base of the page table is held in **CR3 (Windows)** or **`mm->pgd` (Linux)**; this is the **forensic anchor** for translation.
- ☐ Standard **page size = 4 KB**; each PTE = 8 bytes; canonical virtual addresses = **48 bits** in current x64.
- ☐ Five canonical process regions: **Text, Data, BSS, Heap (grows up), Stack (grows down)**, plus **shared libraries**.
- ☐ **Volatility** is the de-facto framework; key plugins: `pslist`, `pstree`, `psscan`, `psxview`, `malfind`, `netscan`, `dlllist`, `cmdline`, `files`, `handles`, `envars`, `hivelist`, `printkey`, `hashdump`.
- ☐ **Acquisition tools**: **FTK Imager** (Windows), **WinPMEM / Magnet RAM Capture** (Windows), **LiME** (Linux), **AVML** (cross-platform), **MemProcFS** (cross-platform).
- ☐ **Best practice**: use **2.5× RAM** as destination storage; hash with **SHA-256**; document chain of custody; prefer **`.vmem` snapshots** for VMs (non-invasive).
- ☐ **Process memory is volatile** — must be acquired **before** system power-off; order of volatility: **CPU registers > RAM > disk > logs > backups**.
- ☐ **Code injection indicators**: private + executable + writable memory with PE/ELF signature but no mapped file → investigate with `malfind`.
- ☐ **Hidden process detection** uses multiple enumeration sources (`psxview` cross-references 7 methods including `pslist`, `psscan`, `thrdproc`, `pspcid`).
- ☐ Always correlate process memory artifacts with **disk evidence** (prefetch, shimcache, amcache, registry) for a **complete forensic picture**.
- ☐ **ACPO Principle**: never work on the original dump; always use a forensic copy; preserve the original bit-for-bit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Status
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Network Status

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Network Status** in the context of digital forensics refers to the comprehensive real-time snapshot and historical record of all active, passive, and dormant network communication states of a computing system. It encompasses **active TCP/UDP connections**, **listening sockets**, **bound ports**, **routing tables**, **interface statistics**, **ARP cache entries**, and **DNS resolver configurations**, all of which are critical artifacts in live forensics, incident response, and post-mortem analysis.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Windows and Linux Forensics*, "Network Status" is treated as a sub-topic under the broader module of **Live System Forensics / Volatile Evidence Acquisition**. The principle is governed by the **Order of Volatility (O.V.)** defined in RFC 3227 — *"Guidelines for Evidence Collection and Archiving"* — where network connections, routing tables, and process-to-port mappings are classified as **highly volatile** and must be acquired before the system is powered down.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large corporate office building** with hundreds of rooms:

- The **rooms** are like **IP addresses and ports** on a computer.
- **People talking in rooms** are like **active network connections** (data flowing).
- **People standing at doors waiting for visitors** are like **listening ports** (services awaiting incoming traffic).
- The **building's internal phone directory** is the **ARP cache** (mapping IP ↔ MAC).
- The **hallway directions posted on walls** are the **routing table** (how to reach other networks).
- The **security guard's logbook** is the **firewall / iptables** (who is allowed in).

When a forensic investigator arrives at a "crime scene" (a suspect system), they must **photograph the phone directory, copy the hallway signs, and note who is talking to whom** *before* the building is shut down. That is **Network Status acquisition**.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **TCP States**: 11 states defined by RFC 793 — *LISTEN, SYN-SENT, SYN-RECEIVED, ESTABLISHED, FIN-WAIT-1, FIN-WAIT-2, CLOSE-WAIT, CLOSING, LAST-ACK, TIME-WAIT, CLOSED*.
- **Well-Known Port Range**: **0 – 1023** (privileged, requires root/admin).
- **Registered Port Range**: **1024 – 49151**.
- **Dynamic/Private Port Range**: **49152 – 65535** (used by client-side ephemeral ports).
- **Default TTL Values**: **64** (Linux), **128** (Windows), **255** (Network Devices).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: Examiners frequently ask about the *Order of Volatility* and *why network status must be acquired before disk imaging*. Always cite **RFC 3227** in your answers for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Network Status

The forensic analysis of network status is decomposed into **five logical pillars**, each representing a distinct layer of evidence:

### Pillar 1 — Active Connections & Sockets
- Represents **currently established or in-progress TCP/UDP sessions**.
- On **Windows**: enumerated via `netstat -ano`, `Get-NetTCPConnection` (PowerShell), or `netsh`.
- On **Linux**: enumerated via `netstat -tulnp`, `ss -tulnp`, or parsing `/proc/net/tcp` and `/proc/net/udp`.

### Pillar 2 — Listening Ports (Bound Services)
- Identifies **services waiting for inbound connections** — the system's "attack surface."
- A *legitimate* web server should have **port 80/443** open; a *suspicious* service on **port 4444 (Metasploit default)** is a red flag.

### Pillar 3 — Routing Tables
- The system's **network "map"** showing how packets reach remote destinations.
- Modifications here can indicate **MITM attacks, covert channels, or persistence mechanisms** (e.g., malicious default gateway).

### Pillar 4 — ARP Cache & Neighbor Table
- Maps **Layer-3 IP addresses ↔ Layer-2 MAC addresses**.
- Duplicate or spoofed MAC entries indicate **ARP poisoning / L2 attacks**.

### Pillar 5 — DNS Resolver Cache & Hosts File
- Contains **recently resolved domain names** — invaluable for identifying **C2 (Command & Control) callbacks**, malware beaconing domains, or recently visited malicious sites.

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters (The "Why" Behind the "How")

| Forensic Question | Network Artifact That Answers It | Forensic Tool |
|---|---|---|
| Is the system talking to a C2 server right now? | Active TCP/UDP connections to external IPs | `netstat -ano`, `ss -tp` |
| What services are exposed to the network? | Listening ports (LISTEN state) | `netstat -an`, `ss -tlnp` |
| Has the attacker altered network paths? | Routing table entries | `route print`, `ip route` |
| Is there an active L2 spoofing attack? | ARP cache anomalies | `arp -a`, `ip neigh` |
| What domains did the user/malware contact? | DNS resolver cache | `ipconfig /displaydns`, `journalctl -u systemd-resolved` |
| Which process owns a suspicious connection? | Process-to-port mapping (PID column) | `netstat -anob`, `ss -tp` |

## 2.3 The Process-to-Port Correlation Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept**: A raw `netstat` output showing `ESTABLISHED 192.168.1.5:49152 → 45.33.32.156:443` is **insufficient evidence**. The forensic value is in answering *"WHICH process owns this connection?"* — this requires the **PID-to-Process mapping** cross-referenced with `tasklist /v` (Windows) or `ps auxf` (Linux).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Value | Forensic Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TCP Header Length** | $H_{TCP} = \left( \text{Data Offset field} \right) \times 4$ | Bytes (20–60) | Determining hidden payload offsets |
| **Port Number Range** | $P \in [0, 65535]$ | Unsigned 16-bit | Classifying service vs. ephemeral |
| **Checksum (TCP/UDP)** | $C = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} W_i + C_{prev}$ (one's complement) | 16-bit | Integrity verification of captured packets |
| **Time-Wait Duration** | $T_{TW} = 2 \times MSL$ where $MSL = 60s$ | 120 seconds (Linux default) | Interpreting stale connection entries |
| **Ephemeral Port Range (Linux)** | $P_{eph} \in [32768, 60999]$ (default) | Port numbers | Distinguishing client connections |
| **Ephemeral Port Range (Windows)** | $P_{eph} \in [49152, 65535]$ | Port numbers | Distinguishing client connections |
| **5-Tuple of a Connection** | $\{SrcIP, SrcPort, DstIP, DstPort, Protocol\}$ | Tuple | Uniquely identifying a network flow |
| **Sequence Number Wrap** | $SEQ_{wrap} = 2^{32} - 1$ | 4,294,967,295 | Detecting anomalous retransmissions |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Incident Response (IR)**: SOC analysts use network status snapshots within the **first 60 seconds** of an alert to identify patient zero and lateral movement.
- **Malware Triage**: Reverse engineers correlate `netstat` output with **process trees** to detect *process injection* (e.g., `svchost.exe` connecting to an external IP on a high port).
- **Legal Evidence**: Network status logs are **admissible under Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872** (now under **Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023**) when accompanied by a proper **Section 63 certificate (now Section 57 BSA)**.
- **Threat Hunting**: Detecting **beaconing behavior** by analyzing periodic reconnections in TIME_WAIT / ESTABLISHED cycles.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Ephemeral Port Selection Algorithm (Linux)

In modern Linux kernels, ephemeral port selection follows a **modified sequential allocation** with randomization for security. Let us derive the expected exhaustion point.

Let the ephemeral range be:
$$
R_{eph} = [P_{min}, P_{max}]
$$

The **total available ephemeral ports** are:
$$
N_{eph} = P_{max} - P_{min} + 1
$$

For a typical Linux system (default `ip_local_port_range`):
$$
N_{eph} = 60999 - 32768 + 1 = 28232 \text{ ports}
$$

The **rate of port consumption** $R_c$ is governed by:
$$
R_c = \frac{N_{conn}}{T}
$$

where $N_{conn}$ is the number of new outbound connections per unit time $T$.

The **time to exhaustion** $T_{exh}$ is therefore:
$$
T_{exh} = \frac{N_{eph}}{R_c} = \frac{P_{max} - P_{min} + 1}{N_{conn}/T}
$$

**Numerical Example**: If a malware opens **1,000 new connections per second**:
$$
T_{exh} = \frac{28232}{1000} = 28.232 \text{ seconds}
$$

This is why **port exhaustion attacks** (SYN floods) can cripple a system in under a minute — a critical insight for forensic analysis of DoS incidents.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of TIME_WAIT Duration

RFC 793 mandates that a connection remains in **TIME_WAIT** for **2 × MSL** (Maximum Segment Lifetime) to ensure all stray segments from the old connection expire before the same 4-tuple is reused.

$$
T_{TW} = 2 \times MSL
$$

With the standard $MSL = 60$ seconds:
$$
T_{TW} = 2 \times 60 = 120 \text{ seconds}
$$

This explains why a forensic `netstat` snapshot may show **hundreds of TIME_WAIT entries** for a recently used service — they are not malicious, but a high count of TIME_WAIT entries for a single destination can indicate **aggressive polling** (a malware beacon).

### 3.1.3 The 5-Tuple Uniqueness Proof

A network connection is uniquely identified by the **5-tuple**:
$$
\Phi = \{IP_{src}, P_{src}, IP_{dst}, P_{dst}, Proto\}
$$

**Proof of uniqueness**: TCP/IP stacks enforce that no two active connections may share the same 5-tuple. If two connections had identical 5-tuples, the receiver could not demultiplex incoming segments correctly. Thus, in any snapshot of network status, each 5-tuple corresponds to **at most one** active flow.

This is why forensic analysts use the 5-tuple as the **primary key** in their network evidence logs.

### 3.1.4 Analytical Matrix — TCP State Transitions in Forensics

| State | Entry Condition | Exit Condition | Forensic Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LISTEN** | Socket bound, awaiting SYN | Receives SYN → SYN-RECEIVED | Identifies exposed services |
| **SYN-SENT** | Client sent SYN, awaiting SYN-ACK | Receives SYN-ACK → ESTABLISHED | Outbound connection initiation |
| **SYN-RECEIVED** | Server received SYN, sent SYN-ACK | Receives ACK → ESTABLISHED | Half-open server state |
| **ESTABLISHED** | 3-way handshake complete | FIN or RST sent/received | Active data transfer — primary C2 indicator |
| **FIN-WAIT-1** | Local sent FIN, awaiting ACK | Receives ACK → FIN-WAIT-2 | Normal termination start |
| **FIN-WAIT-2** | Local ACK'd, awaiting remote FIN | Receives FIN → TIME-WAIT | Half-closed |
| **CLOSE-WAIT** | Remote sent FIN, local ACK'd | Application closes → LAST-ACK | Remote-initiated close pending |
| **CLOSING** | Simultaneous close | Receives ACK → TIME-WAIT | Rare; indicates unusual teardown |
| **LAST-ACK** | Local sent FIN after CLOSE-WAIT | Receives ACK → CLOSED | Final close |
| **TIME-WAIT** | Both sides closed | 2×MSL timeout → CLOSED | Prevents old-segment interference |
| **CLOSED** | No connection | N/A | Not visible in netstat |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Network Status Acquisition Workflow (Live Forensics)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Incident Detected] --> nodeB[Isolate System from Network]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Acquire Volatile Network State]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Active Connections]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Listening Ports]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Routing Table]
    nodeC --> nodeG[ARP Cache]
    nodeC --> nodeH[DNS Cache]
    nodeD --> nodeI[Correlate PIDs with Processes]
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Compare Against Baseline]
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeK[Generate Forensic Report]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[Preserve Evidence Chain of Custody]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Proceed to Disk Imaging]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Network Status Tools

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UserSpace[USER SPACE]
        toolA[netstat]
        toolB[ss]
        toolC[ip command]
        toolD[PowerShell NetTCPConnection]
        toolE[lsof]
    end
    subgraph KernelSpace[KERNEL SPACE]
        proc1[proc/net/tcp]
        proc2[proc/net/udp]
        proc3[proc/net/route]
        proc4[proc/net/arp]
        proc5[socket inode table]
    end
    subgraph Hardware[NETWORK HARDWARE]
        nicA[NIC Interface eth0]
        nicB[NIC Interface wlan0]
    end
    toolA --> proc1
    toolA --> proc2
    toolA --> proc3
    toolB --> proc1
    toolB --> proc2
    toolB --> proc5
    toolC --> proc3
    toolC --> proc4
    toolD --> proc5
    toolE --> proc5
    proc1 --> nicA
    proc2 --> nicA
    proc3 --> nicA
    proc4 --> nicA
    proc5 --> nicA
    proc5 --> nicB
```

## 4.3 TCP State Transition Diagram (Forensic View)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LISTEN
    LISTEN --> SYNRECEIVED : Receive SYN
    LISTEN --> SYNSENT : Send SYN
    SYNSENT --> ESTABLISHED : Receive SYN+ACK
    SYNRECEIVED --> ESTABLISHED : Receive ACK
    ESTABLISHED --> FINWAIT1 : Send FIN
    ESTABLISHED --> CLOSEWAIT : Receive FIN
    FINWAIT1 --> FINWAIT2 : Receive ACK
    FINWAIT2 --> TIMEWAIT : Receive FIN
    CLOSEWAIT --> LASTACK : Send FIN
    LASTACK --> CLOSED : Receive ACK
    TIMEWAIT --> CLOSED : 2xMSL timeout
    CLOSED --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Forensic Decision Tree — "Is This Connection Malicious?"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[netstat Output Entry] --> q1{Is state LISTEN?}
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{Port is expected?}
    q1 -->|No| q3{Is state ESTABLISHED?}
    q2 -->|No| alert1[FLAGGED - Unexpected Service]
    q2 -->|Yes| ok1[Normal]
    q3 -->|Yes| q4{Destination IP is internal?}
    q3 -->|No| ok2[Likely Normal]
    q4 -->|Yes| ok3[Normal LAN Traffic]
    q4 -->|No| q5{Is process signed and known?}
    q5 -->|No| alert2[FLAGGED - Unsigned Process to External IP]
    q5 -->|Yes| q6{Is port suspicious?}
    q6 -->|Yes 4444 8080 8443| alert3[FLAGGED - Common Malware Port]
    q6 -->|No| ok4[Likely Normal Application Traffic]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define the term "Order of Volatility" as per RFC 3227. Why must network status be acquired before disk imaging during a live forensic investigation?*

**Model Answer** (3 marks):
The **Order of Volatility (O.V.)** defined in **RFC 3227** prescribes the sequence in which digital evidence must be collected based on how quickly it degrades. The hierarchy (from most to least volatile) is:
1. CPU registers, cache
2. Routing table, ARP cache, process table, kernel statistics, **network connections**
3. Temporary file systems
4. Disk files
5. Remote logging and monitoring data
6. Physical configuration, network topology

**Network status must be acquired before disk imaging** because TCP connections, listening sockets, ARP cache, and routing tables are **lost the moment the system powers down or is disconnected from the network**. Once lost, this volatile intelligence — which reveals active C2 channels, attacker persistence, and lateral movement — **cannot be recovered from the disk image**. (3 marks)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*List and briefly explain any three TCP states that a forensic investigator would consider "high-interest" during live network status analysis.*

**Model Answer** (3 marks):
1. **ESTABLISHED** — Indicates an active, fully-formed connection with data exchange. Forensic interest: a suspicious process (e.g., `svchost.exe`, `powershell.exe`) holding an ESTABLISHED connection to an external IP is a strong indicator of a **C2 channel**. (1 mark)
2. **LISTEN** — Represents a service awaiting inbound connections. Forensic interest: an unexpected LISTEN socket (e.g., on port 4444 — Metasploit default) reveals an **attacker's backdoor** or rogue service. (1 mark)
3. **SYN-SENT** — Indicates the local host has initiated a connection but not received a SYN-ACK. Forensic interest: a **large number of SYN-SENT entries** to a single IP indicates a **SYN flood attack** in progress. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Question Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the forensic significance of analyzing network status during incident response. Discuss the Windows `netstat -ano` command in detail, including its output columns and how PID correlation is performed. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the TCP 3-way handshake. Describe how a forensic investigator can use TIME_WAIT state analysis to detect potential beaconing malware. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Forensic Significance of Network Status & `netstat -ano`** (7 marks)

The **forensic significance of network status analysis** lies in its ability to reveal the **"who, what, when, and to whom"** of a compromised system's communications — information that exists *only* in volatile memory and is destroyed upon system shutdown.

**Windows `netstat -ano` command**:
- `-a` → Displays **all** connections and listening ports.
- `-n` → Shows addresses and port numbers in **numerical form** (no DNS resolution — critical for speed and accuracy in forensics).
- `-o` → Displays the **owning Process ID (PID)** for each connection.

**Output Columns**:
| Column | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Proto | Protocol (TCP/UDP) |
| Local Address | Local IP:Port |
| Foreign Address | Remote IP:Port |
| State | LISTEN, ESTABLISHED, TIME_WAIT, etc. |
| PID | Owning Process Identifier |

**PID Correlation Process**:
1. Run `netstat -ano > netstat_evidence.txt` and **hash** the file (MD5 + SHA256).
2. Identify suspicious ESTABLISHED connections to external IPs.
3. Note the **PID** of each suspicious connection.
4. Run `tasklist /v /fi "PID eq <PID>"` to identify the process name and full path.
5. Cross-reference with `wmic process where "ProcessId=<PID>" get Name,ExecutablePath,CommandLine`.
6. A mismatch (e.g., `PID 4588` claims to be `svchost.exe` but command line shows a PowerShell download cradle) confirms compromise.

(7 marks — full breakdown: 2 marks significance, 2 marks command explanation, 2 marks PID correlation, 1 mark chain-of-custody)

**(b) TCP 3-Way Handshake & TIME_WAIT Beaconing Detection** (7 marks)

**TCP 3-Way Handshake**:

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Server
    Client->>Server: SYN (seq=x)
    Server->>Client: SYN+ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1)
    Client->>Server: ACK (seq=x+1, ack=y+1)
    Note over Client,Server: ESTABLISHED
```

**TIME_WAIT Beaconing Detection**:
Malware that maintains **persistent C2 channels** typically uses either:
- **Long-lived keep-alive connections** (single ESTABLISHED for hours), OR
- **Short-lived periodic connections** (connect → exfiltrate → close → wait → repeat).

The second pattern generates a **rhythmic burst of TIME_WAIT entries** with the same foreign IP and a consistent interval (e.g., every 60 seconds). The forensic detection methodology:
1. Capture `netstat` output at **multiple time intervals** (e.g., every 10 seconds for 10 minutes).
2. Extract all connections where the state is **TIME_WAIT** and the foreign IP is external.
3. Compute the **time delta** between successive connections to the same IP.
4. A **low standard deviation** in the delta (e.g., $60s \pm 2s$) is a **strong indicator of automated beaconing**.
5. Confirm by correlating PIDs across the time samples.

(7 marks — full breakdown: 3 marks handshake, 2 marks beaconing concept, 2 marks detection methodology)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Paper]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the `netstat` and `ss` commands in Linux for forensic network status analysis. Provide a sample output interpretation for a suspicious connection. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the role of ARP cache and routing table analysis in detecting Layer-2 attacks and MITM incidents. How would you preserve these artifacts forensically? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) `netstat` vs `ss` for Linux Forensics** (7 marks)

| Feature | `netstat` | `ss` |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Legacy BSD tool (deprecated in many distros) | Modern iproute2 replacement |
| **Data Source** | Reads `/proc/net/tcp` (slow, parses text) | Directly queries kernel via **netlink sockets** |
| **Speed** | Slow on systems with thousands of connections | **10–100× faster** |
| **Filtering** | Limited | Powerful filters: `ss state established`, `ss dport = :4444` |
| **Output Detail** | Basic | Extended (congestion window, RTT, cgroup info) |
| **PID Correlation** | `-p` flag | `-p` flag (requires root) |
| **Forensic Preference** | Useful when `ss` is unavailable | **Preferred in modern KTU labs** |

**Sample Suspicious Output Interpretation**:
```
tcp   ESTAB   0   0   192.168.1.105:49152   45.33.32.156:443   users:(("python3",pid=8821,fd=4))
```
- **Local endpoint**: `192.168.1.105:49152` (ephemeral — confirms client-side).
- **Foreign endpoint**: `45.33.32.156:443` (Linode cloud IP — common malware hosting).
- **Protocol/State**: TCP, ESTABLISHED.
- **Process**: `python3`, PID **8821** — **highly suspicious** (Python is rarely a legitimate long-lived client).
- **Forensic action**: Immediately dump process memory with `gcore 8821`, check `/proc/8821/exe`, and isolate.

(7 marks)

**(b) ARP Cache & Routing Table Forensics** (7 marks)

**ARP Cache Analysis for MITM Detection**:
A normal ARP cache shows **one MAC address per IP**. In an **ARP poisoning attack**, the attacker sends forged ARP replies mapping their MAC address to the **default gateway's IP**. Forensic indicators:
- **Duplicate MAC entries** for different IPs.
- A **gateway IP** mapped to a MAC address outside the known vendor OUI range.
- **Multiple ARP entries** with the same MAC but different IPs.

**Routing Table Analysis**:
Routing table manipulation is a **stealthy persistence mechanism**. Forensic indicators:
- A **default route** pointing to an unusual gateway IP.
- **Static routes** to internal subnets via external IPs.
- **Metric manipulation** (lower metrics to attacker-controlled routes).

**Preservation Methodology**:
1. **Windows**: `arp -a > arp_evidence.txt`, `route print > route_evidence.txt`.
2. **Linux**: `ip neigh show > neigh_evidence.txt`, `ip route show > route_evidence.txt`.
3. **Acquire the raw kernel structures**: copy `/proc/net/arp` and `/proc/net/route`.
4. **Hash every output** with `sha256sum` for chain of custody.
5. **Triage cross-reference**: compare against the organization's known network baseline.

(7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in KTU Valuation**:
> 1. **Omitting the PID-to-Process correlation step** — merely listing `netstat` output without explaining how the PID is mapped to a process loses **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing the ephemeral port ranges** of Windows (`49152–65535`) and Linux (`32768–60999`) — examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting RFC 3227** — any question on "why network status is volatile" without citing RFC 3227 loses at least **1 mark**.
> 4. **Using `nslookup` in a forensic context** — `nslookup` is a **live DNS query** and is **NOT forensically sound**; always use `ipconfig /displaydns` (Windows) or inspect `/var/log/resolved` (Linux) for the **historical** DNS cache.
> 5. **Failing to hash the evidence** — in KTU 2024 Scheme, every acquired artifact must have its **MD5 and SHA256** recorded. No hash = **chain of custody broken** = mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist**:
> - **Network status** is **highly volatile** evidence — acquire **first**, per **RFC 3227**.
> - The **5 pillars** are: Active Connections, Listening Ports, Routing Table, ARP Cache, DNS Cache.
> - **Windows tools**: `netstat -ano`, `netstat -anob`, `Get-NetTcpConnection`, `netsh`, `arp -a`, `route print`, `ipconfig /displaydns`.
> - **Linux tools**: `netstat -tulnp`, `ss -tulnp`, `ip a`, `ip route`, `ip neigh`, `cat /proc/net/tcp`, `arp -a`.
> - **TCP has 11 states**; the **ESTABLISHED** and **LISTEN** states are the most forensically significant.
> - **Ephemeral port range**: Windows `49152–65535`; Linux `32768–60999` (default).
> - **TIME_WAIT = 2 × MSL = 120 seconds** (standard); rhythmic TIME_WAIT bursts indicate **beaconing malware**.
> - **PID correlation** is **mandatory** — always map PIDs to process names and full executable paths.
> - **Hash every output file** (MD5 + SHA256) to maintain **chain of custody**.
> - **Beaconing detection** relies on **low standard deviation** in the time delta between repeated connections to the same external IP.
> - **ARP poisoning** indicators: duplicate MACs, unknown vendor OUIs on gateway IP.
> - **Routing table tampering** indicators: unknown default gateway, suspicious static routes, manipulated metrics.
> - **Forensically sound DNS analysis** uses the **local resolver cache**, **NOT live DNS queries** (`nslookup` is forbidden in forensic workflows).
> - **TLS/SSL inspection**: an ESTABLISHED connection to port **443** does not mean the data is encrypted beyond reach — server certificates are visible in memory dumps of the owning process.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nonvolatile Information
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Nonvolatile Information

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nonvolatile Information** in the context of digital forensics refers to any data, metadata, or residual artifacts that are **persistently stored** on a secondary storage medium (e.g., HDD, SSD, USB, optical media) and **survive a system power-off, reboot, or hard reset** without requiring continuous electrical refreshment.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (PECCT866), nonvolatile information forms the **backbone of post-mortem forensic investigation** because it allows examiners to reconstruct user activity, file transactions, application usage, and system events long after the live (volatile) state has been destroyed. The scientific principle is rooted in the **WORM (Write Once Read Many)** property of magnetic/optical storage and the **floating-gate transistor** mechanism in NAND flash memory.

### Classification of Digital Evidence by Persistence

| Evidence Class | Persistence | Power Required | Forensic Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volatile** | Lost on shutdown | Yes (RAM, CPU cache) | **High** (order of volatility) |
| **Nonvolatile** | Survives shutdown | No (HDD, SSD, ROM) | **Primary** (post-mortem) |
| **Network/Persistent** | Stored remotely | No (Cloud, logs) | Secondary (jurisdictional) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 specifically targets the **acquisition and interpretation** of nonvolatile artifacts — distinguishing between *allocated*, *unallocated*, *slack*, and *hidden* sectors of storage media.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locked Filing Cabinet" Model

Imagine a **post office** with two rooms:

- **The Front Desk (Volatile / RAM):** Holds the envelopes currently being processed. The instant the postmaster flicks the lights off (`power loss`), the unsorted envelopes fall to the floor and the desk clears. You lose the *current transaction context*.
- **The Basement Vault (Nonvolatile / HDD/SSD):** Every letter ever received is filed, dated, cross-referenced, and stored — sometimes in plain view, sometimes misfiled, sometimes partially destroyed but still recoverable as fragments. The lights can be off for years, and the letters remain.

**Nonvolatile information is the "Basement Vault" of digital evidence.** Even when a criminal wipes a file, traces remain in:

- The **slack space** (the air gap between the last letter in an envelope and the envelope's edge).
- The **metadata** (the index card on the front of each filing cabinet drawer).
- The **swap file** (the desk where the postmaster scribbled drafts before filing).
- The **unallocated clusters** (the recycling bin of the vault).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Sector size (legacy CHS):** **512 bytes** (industry baseline since the 1980s; advanced format drives use 4096-byte sectors called "4Kn").
- **Cluster (block) size:** Ranges from **512 bytes (1 sector)** to **64 KB (128 sectors)**, configurable during filesystem formatting.
- **Page size (NAND flash):** Typically **4 KB, 8 KB, or 16 KB** for read/program operations.
- **Erase block (NAND flash):** Typically **128 KB to 4 MB**; fundamental to wear-leveling and TRIM behavior.
- **Standard cluster sizes for NTFS:** Default **4 KB** for volumes up to 16 TB.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Forensic Metric:** The **Cluster Size** is the atomic unit of disk allocation. A file occupying 1 byte still consumes an *entire* cluster on disk — the remaining bytes become **file slack**.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Nonvolatile Information

Nonvolatile data on a Windows or Linux forensic image is stratified into **four logical zones**, each requiring distinct acquisition tools (FTK Imager, dd, dc3dd, The Sleuth Kit, Autopsy).

### Zone 1 — Allocated Space (Active Data)

The region where the filesystem has *committed* cluster runs to active files. Each cluster holds exactly one file's data (no intermixing). Recovery is straightforward via directory entry traversal.

### Zone 2 — Unallocated Space (Free Space / Slack Pool)

Clusters not currently bound to any file in the filesystem's metadata. They contain **deleted data remnants**. The OS reuses these clusters when new files are written. Forensic tools carve signatures (e.g., magic bytes `FF D8 FF` for JPEG) to recover orphaned artifacts.

### Zone 3 — Slack Space (The Forensic Goldmine)

The unintentional gap between the *logical end-of-file* and the *physical end-of-cluster*. Two sub-types:

- **RAM Slack (Sector Slack):** The bytes from the end of the file's last sector up to the end of that 512-byte sector. Often filled with a copy of RAM buffers (hence the name) — historically a rich evidence source for passwords, encryption keys, and memory fragments.
- **File Slack:** The bytes from the end of the file up to the end of the last allocated cluster.

### Zone 4 — Hidden / Obscured Areas

Regions deliberately or incidentally concealed from the OS view:

- **Host Protected Area (HPA)** — set via ATA commands; invisible to BIOS.
- **Device Configuration Overlay (DCO)** — manufacturer-controlled region.
- **Alternate Data Streams (ADS)** — NTFS-specific secondary file streams.
- **Bad Cluster / Bad Sector Lists** — marked unusable but data may persist.
- **Volume Shadow Copies (VSS)** — Windows restore points (whole-volume snapshots).
- **Extended Partitions / Linux LVM / RAID metadata**.

## 2.2 File System Theory — The Allocation Engine

### FAT32 Layout

```
| Reserved Sectors | FAT Region #1 | FAT Region #2 | Data Region (Clusters 2..N) |
```

- **Boot Sector (BPB):** Contains parameters `BytsPerSec`, `SecPerClus`, `TotSec32`, `FATSz32`, `RootClus`.
- **FAT entries:** 32-bit per cluster; `0x00000000` = free, `0x0FFFFFF8`-`0x0FFFFFFF` = end-of-chain.

### NTFS Layout (More Complex)

```
| $MFT (Master File Table) | $MFTMirr | $LogFile | $Volume | $Bitmap | $Boot | $BadClus | $Secure | $UpCase | $Extend | Data Clusters |
```

- **Everything in NTFS is a file** — even the volume metadata ($MFT, $LogFile).
- **$MFT** contains **MFT Entries (1 KB each by default)**, each describing a file's attributes.
- **Alternate Data Streams:** Allowed via `$DATA` attribute with non-default name, e.g., `report.docx:hidden`.

### ext4 Layout (Linux)

```
| Superblock (at offset 1024) | Group Descriptors | Block Bitmap | inode Bitmap | inode Table | Data Blocks |
```

- **Inodes** (index nodes) store file metadata: mode, UID, GID, size, timestamps, block pointers (12 direct, 1 indirect, 1 double-indirect, 1 triple-indirect).
- **Journaling (ext3/ext4):** The journal is itself a nonvolatile forensic artifact preserving uncommitted transaction intent.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations govern *every* cluster/sector offset calculation in forensic disk analysis. Master these.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bytes per Cluster (BPC)** | $BPC = \text{BytsPerSec} \times \text{SecPerClus}$ | $BPC$ in bytes |
| 2 | **Total Data Sectors** | $\text{DataSec} = \text{TotSec} - (\text{ResvdSec} + (\text{FATSz} \times \text{NumFATs}))$ | Sectors |
| 3 | **Total Data Clusters** | $\text{CountOfClusters} = \dfrac{\text{DataSec}}{\text{SecPerClus}}$ | Integer (clusters) |
| 4 | **Cluster Offset (Bytes)** | $\text{ClusOff} = \text{ResvdSec} \times \text{BytsPerSec} + (\text{ClusterNo} - 2) \times BPC$ | Bytes from start of disk |
| 5 | **RAM Slack (Bytes)** | $S_{\text{ram}} = \text{BytsPerSec} - (\text{FileSize} \bmod \text{BytsPerSec})$ | $\bmod$ is modulo |
| 6 | **File Slack (Bytes)** | $S_{\text{file}} = BPC - (\text{FileSize} \bmod BPC)$ | Bytes |
| 7 | **MFT Entry Address (Bytes)** | $\text{MFTAddr} = \text{StartCluster}_{MFT} \times BPC + \text{PartitionStart}$ | Bytes from disk start |
| 8 | **Time Conversion (FAT)** | $\text{Time} = ((\text{Hour} \times 2048) + (\text{Min} \times 32) + \text{Sec}/2) \times 65536 + ((\text{Year}-1980) \times 512) + ((\text{Month} \times 32) + \text{Day})$ | Encoded 32-bit |
| 9 | **Sectors per Track (CHS)** | $\text{SPT} = \text{TotSec32} / (\text{Heads} \times \text{Cyls})$ | Legacy geometry |
| 10 | **HPA Detection** | $\text{TrueCapacity} - \text{AccessibleCapacity} = \text{HPA Size}$ | Sectors |

> [!WARNING]
> In tables, **never** use the literal pipe `|` for math (e.g., $|x|$) — it will break markdown rendering. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

## 2.4 Real-World Forensic Utility

- **Criminal Forensics:** Recovering deleted exculpatory documents, contraband images in unallocated space.
- **Incident Response:** Identifying malware persistence in NTFS ADS or in $MFT-resident alternate data streams.
- **Intellectual Property Theft:** Proving file origination via $MFT timestamps even after deletion.
- **Anti-Forensics Detection:** Identifying deliberate wiping via toolmark analysis (e.g., DoD 5220.22-M overwrites still leave magnetic residue on HDDs).
- **Compliance Auditing:** Verifying retention of regulated financial records across VSS snapshots.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Slack Space Calculation

### Problem Statement

> A FAT32 volume has `BytsPerSec = 512` and `SecPerClus = 8`. A file named `case.docx` has a logical size of `14,237 bytes`. Compute (a) Bytes per Cluster, (b) RAM Slack, (c) File Slack, (d) Total Slack, and (e) the byte offset of the file's first cluster, given `ResvdSec = 32`, `NumFATs = 2`, `FATSz = 2048` sectors, and the file's starting cluster number is `Cluster 5`.

### Step (a) — Bytes per Cluster

$$
BPC = \text{BytsPerSec} \times \text{SecPerClus}
$$

Substituting the known values:

$$
BPC = 512 \;\text{bytes} \times 8
$$

$$
\boxed{BPC = 4096 \;\text{bytes (4 KB)}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** A cluster is the *product* of the sector size (atomic disk unit) and the sectors-per-cluster count (filesystem allocation unit). On modern volumes, this typically yields 4 KB.

### Step (b) — RAM Slack (Sector Slack)

The file's last occupied sector holds bytes from position $(14237 \bmod 512)$ to position $511$ as slack.

$$
S_{\text{ram}} = \text{BytsPerSec} - (\text{FileSize} \bmod \text{BytsPerSec})
$$

Compute the modulo:

$$
14237 \bmod 512
$$

We perform long division:

$$
14237 = 512 \times 27 + r
$$

$$
512 \times 27 = 13824
$$

$$
r = 14237 - 13824 = 413
$$

So $14237 \bmod 512 = 413$. Substituting:

$$
S_{\text{ram}} = 512 - 413
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{\text{ram}} = 99 \;\text{bytes}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The file ends at byte offset 413 *within* its final sector. Bytes 414 through 511 (99 bytes) are RAM slack — historically filled with residual memory from the OS write buffer.

### Step (c) — File Slack

The file's last cluster holds bytes from position $(14237 \bmod 4096)$ to position $4095$ as slack.

$$
S_{\text{file}} = BPC - (\text{FileSize} \bmod BPC)
$$

Compute the modulo:

$$
14237 \bmod 4096
$$

Perform long division:

$$
14237 = 4096 \times 3 + r
$$

$$
4096 \times 3 = 12288
$$

$$
r = 14237 - 12288 = 1949
$$

So $14237 \bmod 4096 = 1949$. Substituting:

$$
S_{\text{file}} = 4096 - 1949
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{\text{file}} = 2147 \;\text{bytes}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Of the 2147 bytes of file slack, the first 99 are *also* counted as RAM slack. The remaining 2048 bytes are pure file slack beyond the last sector boundary.

### Step (d) — Total Slack

$$
S_{\text{total}} = S_{\text{ram}} + (S_{\text{file}} - S_{\text{ram}})
$$

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 99 + (2147 - 99)
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{\text{total}} = 2147 \;\text{bytes}}
$$

Alternatively, more directly:

$$
S_{\text{total}} = BPC - (\text{FileSize} \bmod BPC) = 4096 - 1949 = 2147
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Total slack = bytes wasted in the *last allocated cluster* beyond the file's logical end. This is the region where deleted-but-not-overwritten data can survive.

### Step (e) — Byte Offset of First Cluster

$$
\text{ClusOff} = \text{ResvdSec} \times \text{BytsPerSec} + (\text{ClusterNo} - 2) \times BPC
$$

Substituting `ClusterNo = 5`:

$$
\text{ClusOff} = 32 \times 512 + (5 - 2) \times 4096
$$

$$
\text{ClusOff} = 16384 + 3 \times 4096
$$

$$
\text{ClusOff} = 16384 + 12288
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{ClusOff} = 28672 \;\text{bytes}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Cluster numbering begins at cluster **2** (clusters 0 and 1 are reserved/reserved-as-end-markers in FAT). To reach cluster 5, we must skip the reserved region (`ResvdSec × BytsPerSec`) plus 3 cluster-widths of data region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the partition itself starts at sector 2048 of the disk (a common GPT-aligned value), the *disk-absolute* offset would be `(2048 × 512) + 28672 = 1,076,480` bytes from the physical disk's LBA 0.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing File System Nonvolatile Artifacts

| Artifact | FAT32 | NTFS | ext4 | Forensic Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| File metadata | 32-byte directory entry | 1 KB MFT entry | 128/256-byte inode | NTFS & ext4 highest |
| Deletion marker | First char of name = `0xE5` | MFT flag bit set, cluster reusable | Inode link count → 0 | FAT32 most recoverable |
| Timestamp count | 2 (write, access) | 4 (create, modify, access, MFT modify) | 4 (atime, mtime, ctime, dtime) | NTFS/ext4 richer |
| File slack | Yes (worst case large) | Yes (cluster-aligned) | Yes (block-aligned) | Equal |
| ADS support | No | **Yes** | xattr (similar) | NTFS only |
| Built-in journaling | No | Yes ($LogFile) | Yes (jbd2) | Anti-forensic challenge |
| Recycle Bin artifact | `$RECYCLE.BIN` (per-volume) | Same | None (per-shell) | Win unique |
| Volume Shadow | No (post-Vista: yes, VSS) | **Yes** | LVM snapshots | NTFS richest |

## 3.3 Walkthrough — Decoding a FAT32 Directory Entry Timestamp

Suppose a FAT32 entry contains the encoded timestamp value `0x4A77B5C0` (4 bytes, little-endian → bytes `C0 B5 77 4A`).

**Step 1 — Date Field (lower 16 bits):** `0xB577`

$$
\text{Year} = (0xB577 \gg 9) + 1980 = (0x5A) + 1980 = 90 + 1980 = 2070
$$

$$
\text{Month} = (0xB577 \gg 5) \;\&\; 0x0F = 0x0B = 11 \;(\text{November})
$$

$$
\text{Day} = 0xB577 \;\&\; 0x1F = 0x17 = 23
$$

**Step 2 — Time Field (upper 16 bits):** `0x4A7A` (after byte swap consideration)

$$
\text{Hour} = 0x4A7A \gg 11 = 0x09 = 9
$$

$$
\text{Minute} = (0x4A7A \gg 5) \;\&\; 0x3F = 0x3D = 61
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Minute value 61 is **impossible** in FAT32 (valid range 0–59). A value of 63 indicates a **time stomping** event — an anti-forensic technique where the timestamp was deliberately crafted to appear normal but contains logical impossibilities.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Physical Disk Layout — High-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MBR[MBR - Master Boot Record - Sector 0 - 512 bytes]
    EB[Extended Partitions - Optional]
    P1[Partition 1 - NTFS or ext4 - File System Region]
    P2[Partition 2 - Optional - Swap or Data]
    P3[Partition 3 - Optional - HPA Hidden Region]
    P4[Partition 4 - Optional - Recovery Partition]

    MBR --> EB
    MBR --> P1
    MBR --> P2
    MBR --> P3
    MBR --> P4

    subgraph P1_Detail[Partition 1 - Nonvolatile Information Zones]
        direction TB
        VBR1[Volume Boot Record - BPB Parameters]
        RSV[Reserved Region - FS Metadata]
        FAT_A[File Allocation Table - Cluster Map]
        FAT_B[File Allocation Table - Backup Copy]
        ROOT[Root Directory - FAT32 only - NTFS uses MFT]
        DATA[Data Region - Clusters 2 to N]
        ALLOC[Allocated Clusters - Active File Data]
        UNALL[Unallocated Clusters - Deleted Data Remnants]
        SLACK[Slack Space - Hidden Forensic Artifacts]
        ALTDS[NTFS Alternate Data Streams - Hidden File Streams]
        META[$MFT - NTFS Master File Table - File Metadata]
    end

    P1 --> P1_Detail
    DATA --> ALLOC
    DATA --> UNALL
    DATA --> SLACK
    DATA --> ALTDS
    P1_Detail --> META
```

## 4.2 Slack Space Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[File Logical Size - 14,237 bytes] --> B[Allocate Cluster 4096 bytes]

    subgraph CLUSTER[Single 4096 byte Cluster]
        direction TB
        S1[Sector 1 - 512 bytes - File Data]
        S2[Sector 2 - 512 bytes - File Data]
        S3[Sector 3 - 512 bytes - File Data]
        S4[Sector 4 - 512 bytes - File Data]
        S5[Sector 5 - 512 bytes - File Data]
        S6[Sector 6 - 512 bytes - File Data]
        S7[Sector 7 - 512 bytes - File Data - ends at byte 413]
        S8_RAM[RAM Slack - 99 bytes - bytes 414 to 511 - filled with OS buffer residue]
        S8_FILE[File Slack - 2048 bytes - bytes 512 to 2559 - contains pre-allocation residue]
    end

    B --> CLUSTER
    S7 --> S8_RAM
    S8_RAM --> S8_FILE
```

## 4.3 NTFS $MFT Entry Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ENTRY[MFT Entry - 1024 bytes - Fixed]

    ENTRY --> HDR[FILE Record Header - 48 bytes - Magic 0x46494C45 FILE]
    ENTRY --> UP[$UPDATE SEQUENCE - Array for crash consistency]
    ENTRY --> ATTR[Attribute Block - Variable - Resident or Non-Resident]

    HDR --> SIG[Signature FILE]
    HDR --> FLG[Flags - In Use, Directory, Modified]
    HDR --> USED[Used Size of Entry]
    HDR --> ALLOC_SZ[Allocated Size of Entry]

    ATTR --> A0[Attribute 0x10 - STANDARD_INFORMATION - 4 timestamps]
    ATTR --> A30[Attribute 0x30 - FILE_NAME - Long and Short names]
    ATTR --> A80[Attribute 0x80 - DATA - Default stream]
    ATTR --> A81[Attribute 0x81 - DATA - Alternate Data Stream - Hidden]
    ATTR --> A90[Attribute 0x90 - INDEX_ROOT - For directories]
    ATTR --> AA0[Attribute 0xA0 - INDEX_ALLOCATION - B-tree for large dirs]
    ATTR --> AB0[Attribute 0xB0 - BITMAP - Cluster allocation map]
```

## 4.4 Linux ext4 Inode Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INODE[Inode - 128 or 256 bytes - ext4]

    INODE --> MODE[i_mode - File type and permissions]
    INODE --> UID[i_uid - Owner User ID]
    INODE --> SIZE[i_size - File Size in Bytes]
    INODE --> ATIME[i_atime - Last Access - nanosecond precision]
    INODE --> CTIME[i_ctime - Inode Change - metadata modify]
    INODE --> MTIME[i_mtime - Data Modification time]
    INODE --> DTIME[i_dtime - Deletion time - forensic gold]
    INODE --> BLKS[i_blocks - 512 byte sectors allocated]
    INODE --> FLAGS[i_flags - Immutable, Append-only, Compressed]

    INODE --> D0[Block 0 - Direct pointer]
    INODE --> D1[Block 1 - Direct pointer]
    INODE --> D2[Block 2 - Direct pointer]
    INODE --> D3[Block 3 - Direct pointer]
    INODE --> D4[Block 4 - Direct pointer]
    INODE --> D5[Block 5 - Direct pointer]
    INODE --> D6[Block 6 - Direct pointer]
    INODE --> D7[Block 7 - Direct pointer]
    INODE --> D8[Block 8 - Direct pointer]
    INODE --> D9[Block 9 - Direct pointer]
    INODE --> D10[Block 10 - Direct pointer]
    INODE --> D11[Block 11 - Direct pointer]
    INODE --> I1[Block 12 - Indirect pointer - addresses 4 KB block of block numbers]
    INODE --> I2[Block 13 - Double Indirect - addresses block of indirect blocks]
    INODE --> I3[Block 14 - Triple Indirect - addresses block of double indirect blocks]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Seized Storage Media] --> WRITE[Write Block Hardware - Prevents Modification]
    WRITE --> HASH1[Compute Original Hash - SHA-256 or MD5]

    IMG --> HASH2[Verify Image Hash - Chain of Custody]

    PARSE --> Z1[Zone 1 - Allocated Data - Direct File Recovery]

    PARSE --> Z4[Zone 4 - Hidden Areas - ADS detection - HPA analysis]

    Z1 --> RPT[Forensic Report - Timeline Analysis - MAC Time Aggregation]
    Z2 --> RPT
    Z3 --> RPT
    Z4 --> RPT
    RPT --> COURT[Court Admissible Evidence]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define nonvolatile information. List four categories of nonvolatile data found on a Windows forensic image.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Nonvolatile information is data that is persistently stored on secondary storage media and **retains its state without continuous electrical power**. Unlike volatile data in RAM/CPU registers, it survives system shutdown, reboot, or power loss, making it the **primary source of post-mortem forensic evidence**.
>
> **Four Categories (1 mark — ¼ each):**
> 1. **Allocated space** — clusters bound to active files.
> 2. **Unallocated space** — clusters free for reuse but may contain deleted data.
> 3. **Slack space** (RAM slack + file slack) — gap between logical EOF and end-of-cluster.
> 4. **Hidden areas** — Host Protected Area, Alternate Data Streams, bad clusters, Volume Shadow Copies, DCO regions.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the difference between RAM slack and file slack? Why is slack space forensically significant?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **RAM Slack (1½ marks):** Bytes from the *end of the file's last sector* up to the *end of that 512-byte sector*. Historically, the OS write buffer dumped its contents here — forensic examiners have recovered **passwords, encryption keys, and process memory fragments** from this region.
>
> **File Slack (1½ marks):** Bytes from the *end of the file* up to the *end of the last allocated cluster*. This region may contain fragments of **previously deleted files** that previously occupied those same clusters.
>
> **Forensic Significance:** Slack space is not visible to the file owner or application, yet contains residual data — making it a classic location for data hiding and a critical acquisition target during forensic imaging.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) norms, every Part-B question carries an **internal choice**. **Answer either Question A or Question B in full.**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the layered architecture of a hard disk with respect to forensic acquisition. Discuss the role of **MBR, VBR, FAT, and Data Region** in a FAT32 file system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A FAT32 volume has the following BIOS Parameter Block (BPB) values:

- `BytsPerSec = 512`
- `SecPerClus = 4`
- `ResvdSec = 32`
- `NumFATs = 2`
- `FATSz = 4096` sectors
- `TotSec32 = 2,097,152` (≈ 1 GB volume)

A file `forensic_log.txt` occupies 3,289 bytes starting at **Cluster 4**. Compute:

1. Total data sectors
2. Total data clusters
3. Bytes per cluster
4. RAM slack
5. File slack
6. Byte offset of the file's first cluster from the start of the partition

**(7 marks)**

### Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Layered Architecture (7 marks)

**Layer 1 — Master Boot Record (MBR) — 1 mark**

The MBR resides at **LBA 0 (sector 0)** of the disk. It is 512 bytes and contains:

- 446 bytes of bootstrap code.
- 64-byte partition table (four 16-byte entries).
- 2-byte magic signature `0x55AA`.

Forensic value: tells the examiner **how many partitions exist, their types, sizes, and starting sectors**. A damaged MBR can hide partitions — examiners use tools like `testdisk` to recover.

**Layer 2 — Volume Boot Record (VBR) — 2 marks**

Each partition begins with a VBR (also called the Boot Sector). For FAT32, the VBR contains the **BIOS Parameter Block (BPB)** — a critical structure holding:

- `BytsPerSec`, `SecPerClus`, `ResvdSec`, `NumFATs`, `FATSz32`, `RootClus`, `TotSec32`, etc.

These parameters are **the forensic DNA** of the volume — without them, no file recovery is possible. The VBR is forensic-image **sector 0** of the partition.

**Layer 3 — FAT Region — 2 marks**

The File Allocation Table is a **doubly redundant array** of 32-bit cluster pointers. Each cluster in the data region has a corresponding FAT entry:

- `0x00000000` → cluster is free.
- `0x00000002..0x0FFFFFEF` → points to next cluster in chain.
- `0x0FFFFFF8..0x0FFFFFFF` → end-of-file marker.
- `0x00000001` → reserved.

Forensic value: **deletion in FAT32** is achieved by *zeroing* the directory entry's first character to `0xE5` and deallocating the FAT chain. The data clusters become *unallocated* but their contents persist.

**Layer 4 — Data Region — 2 marks**

The largest region, containing **cluster 2 through cluster N**. Divided into:

- Allocated clusters (active file data).
- Unallocated clusters (free for reuse).
- Slack spaces within allocated clusters.

This is where **99% of user evidence lives** — documents, images, executables, registry hives, email stores, browser caches, etc.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Total Data Sectors (1 mark):**

$$
\text{DataSec} = \text{TotSec32} - (\text{ResvdSec} + \text{NumFATs} \times \text{FATSz})
$$

$$
\text{DataSec} = 2097152 - (32 + 2 \times 4096)
$$

$$
\text{DataSec} = 2097152 - (32 + 8192) = 2097152 - 8224
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{DataSec} = 2,088,928 \;\text{sectors}}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Data Clusters (1 mark):**

$$
\text{CountOfClusters} = \dfrac{\text{DataSec}}{\text{SecPerClus}} = \dfrac{2088928}{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CountOfClusters} = 522,232 \;\text{clusters}}
$$

**Step 3 — Bytes per Cluster (1 mark):**

$$
BPC = \text{BytsPerSec} \times \text{SecPerClus} = 512 \times 4
$$

$$
\boxed{BPC = 2048 \;\text{bytes}}
$$

**Step 4 — RAM Slack (1 mark):**

$$
S_{\text{ram}} = 512 - (3289 \bmod 512)
$$

Compute $3289 \bmod 512$:

$$
512 \times 6 = 3072, \quad 3289 - 3072 = 217
$$

So $3289 \bmod 512 = 217$:

$$
S_{\text{ram}} = 512 - 217
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{\text{ram}} = 295 \;\text{bytes}}
$$

**Step 5 — File Slack (1 mark):**

$$
S_{\text{file}} = BPC - (3289 \bmod BPC)
$$

Compute $3289 \bmod 2048$:

$$
3289 - 2048 = 1241
$$

So $3289 \bmod 2048 = 1241$:

$$
S_{\text{file}} = 2048 - 1241
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{\text{file}} = 807 \;\text{bytes}}
$$

**Step 6 — Byte Offset of First Cluster (2 marks):**

$$
\text{ClusOff} = \text{ResvdSec} \times \text{BytsPerSec} + (\text{ClusterNo} - 2) \times BPC
$$

$$
\text{ClusOff} = 32 \times 512 + (4 - 2) \times 2048
$$

$$
\text{ClusOff} = 16384 + 2 \times 2048
$$

$$
\text{ClusOff} = 16384 + 4096
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{ClusOff} = 20,480 \;\text{bytes from partition start}}
$$

**Final Verification:** RAM slack (295 B) is a *subset* of file slack (807 B), and $807 - 295 = 512$ — exactly one sector's worth of pure file slack beyond the sector boundary. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Discuss the structure of the **NTFS Master File Table ($MFT)**. With a neat diagram, explain at least **six attributes** of an MFT entry and their forensic significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the forensic importance of the following nonvolatile artifacts and the tools used to extract them: **(7 marks, 1 each + 1 for tools)**

1. Host Protected Area (HPA)
2. Alternate Data Streams (ADS)
3. Volume Shadow Copies (VSS)
4. Swap File (`pagefile.sys` / `swap partition`)
5. Unallocated clusters with file signatures
6. Linux ext4 journal (`jbd2`)
7. `$MFT` resident files (small files stored inside the MFT entry itself)

### Question B — Model Solution Outline

**Part (a) — NTFS $MFT Structure (7 marks):**

- **$MFT overview (1 mark):** Located at cluster specified by BPB; default first 12.5% of volume reserved.
- **Entry structure (2 marks):** 1024 bytes; FILE signature; $STANDARD_INFORMATION, $FILE_NAME, $DATA, $INDEX_ROOT, $BITMAP.
- **Six attributes (3 marks):** $STANDARD_INFORMATION (timestamps), $FILE_NAME (long/short name + parent dir reference), $DATA (default + ADS), $ATTRIBUTE_LIST (for entries > 1024 B), $OBJECT_ID (GUID), $REPARSE_POINT (symlinks).
- **Resident vs Non-Resident (1 mark):** If data fits in 1024 B, stored *inside* the MFT entry (resident) — useful for small malware analysis.

**Part (b) — Artifact Forensic Importance (7 marks, 1 each):**

1. **HPA (1 mark):** Set via ATA command `SET MAX ADDRESS`; bypasses BIOS — can hide entire OS partitions. **Tools:** `hdparm -N`, `ATATool`, EnCase.
2. **ADS (1 mark):** Hidden secondary streams under NTFS — `type secret.txt > report.docx:hidden`. Invisible in Explorer. **Tools:** `dir /R`, `streams.exe` (Sysinternals), FTK.
3. **VSS (1 mark):** Snapshot copies preserving prior states of files. **Tools:** `vssadmin list shadows`, FTK Imager, libvshadow (Sleuth Kit).
4. **Swap (1 mark):** Holds memory pages swapped to disk — credentials, decryption keys, process memory. **Tools:** `strings`, Volatility (with pagefile), `page_brute`.
5. **File Carving (1 mark):** Recovering files by scanning unallocated space for magic bytes (`PK\x03\x04`, `\xFF\xD8\xFF`). **Tools:** `foremost`, `scalpel`, `photorec`.
6. **ext4 journal (1 mark):** Pre-commit transaction log; reveals intent of incomplete operations (e.g., partial file writes). **Tools:** `jls`, `jcat` (Sleuth Kit), `debugfs -c`.
7. **$MFT resident files (1 mark):** Files < 700 bytes often stored *inside* MFT entry — **malware authors favor this** because antivirus doesn't scan $MFT directly. **Tools:** `analyzeMFT`, MFT parsers in Autopsy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Conflating *unallocated* and *slack* space.** Unallocated = clusters free for reuse. Slack = bytes wasted *within* an allocated cluster. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply `SecPerClus`** when computing bytes per cluster — students often report `BytsPerSec` directly. **Always** apply $BPC = \text{BytsPerSec} \times \text{SecPerClus}$.
> 3. **Cluster numbering.** FAT cluster numbering **begins at 2** (not 0 or 1). Forgetting the `−2` offset loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Modulo confusion in slack.** RAM slack uses `mod BytsPerSec`; file slack uses `mod BPC`. Mixing these yields wrong answers.
> 5. **ADS omission.** NTFS examiners *must* mention ADS — it is a favorite **14-mark question** in KTU because it bridges file system theory and incident response.
> 6. **HPA misconceptions.** HPA is *not* erased data — it is a *deliberately hidden region* of the disk. Explain with the ATA `SET MAX ADDRESS` command.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This Before the Exam:**

- ⚡ **Nonvolatile = Survives Power Off.** HDD, SSD, optical, USB, ROM.
- ⚡ **4 Forensic Zones:** Allocated, Unallocated, Slack, Hidden.
- ⚡ **Cluster = atomic allocation unit.** $BPC = \text{BytsPerSec} \times \text{SecPerClus}$.
- ⚡ **RAM Slack** = `BytsPerSec − (FileSize mod BytsPerSec)`. Contains OS buffer residue.
- ⚡ **File Slack** = `BPC − (FileSize mod BPC)`. May contain deleted file residue.
- ⚡ **FAT32 cluster numbering starts at 2.** Offset = `ResvdSec × BytsPerSec + (ClusterNo − 2) × BPC`.
- ⚡ **NTFS** uses 1 KB MFT entries; supports ADS; rich 4-timestamp model.
- ⚡ **ADS** — the canonical hidden data vector on Windows. Always check `dir /R`.
- ⚡ **HPA/DCO** — physically hidden disk regions; use `hdparm`, not the OS, to detect.
- ⚡ **VSS** — forensic goldmine for prior file states. Decoded via `libvshadow`.
- ⚡ **ext4** uses inodes (128/256 B) with `i_dtime` as the *deletion timestamp* — invaluable.
- ⚡ **Swap/pagefile** — even on modern systems, holds passwords, fragments, decrypted buffers.
- ⚡ **File carving** signatures: `PK\x03\x04` (ZIP/DOCX), `\xFF\xD8\xFF` (JPEG), `\x89PNG` (PNG), `%PDF` (PDF), `MZ` (EXE).
- ⚡ **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227):** Registers → Cache → RAM → Disk → Remote logs.
- ⚡ **Chain of custody:** Hash original → Image → Hash image → Parse → Report → Court.
- ⚡ **Write-blocking** is non-negotiable before any forensic acquisition.
- ⚡ **Time stomping** detection: invalid minutes/seconds (e.g., minute = 63) in FAT32 timestamps.
- ⚡ **Resident $MFT files** = hidden malware vectors; always audit MFT-resident data.
- ⚡ **Journaling (NTFS $LogFile, ext4 jbd2)** = preservation of *intent* even when transaction incomplete.
- ⚡ **Default NTFS cluster = 4 KB** for volumes ≤ 16 TB. FAT32 default = 4 KB–32 KB depending on volume size.
- ⚡ **HPA detection** = `TrueCapacity − AccessibleCapacity` in sectors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In the ESE, *always* show the **formula first**, then substitute, then solve. Examiners award **method marks** even when arithmetic errors occur. A correctly-stated $BPC$ equation with a wrong multiplication still earns 1 of 2 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Live Response Methodologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Live Response Methodologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Live Response** is a *volatile data acquisition* and *triage methodology* in digital forensics wherein an investigator interacts directly with a powered-on, running computer system to collect ephemeral, time-sensitive digital evidence before powering down the machine. It is governed by the **Order of Volatility (OoV)** prescribed in **IETF RFC 3227 — Guidelines for Evidence Collection and Archiving**.

In the context of **PECCT866 — Windows and Linux Forensics (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, live response methodologies specifically deal with:

1. **Volatile State Capture:** CPU registers, RAM contents, network connections, running processes, logged-in users, and open file handles.
2. **Non-Volatile Triage:** Quick examination of registries, event logs, scheduled tasks, and file system metadata without performing a full bit-stream image.
3. **Evidence Integrity:** Hashing, timestamping, and chain-of-custody preservation while the system is still running.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must clearly distinguish between *Live Response*, *Dead Box Forensics* (post-mortem), and *Remote Forensics*. The Module 2 paper frequently asks a 3-mark question on the **Order of Volatility**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hospital emergency room:

- **Dead Box Forensics** is like a **coroner** examining a body in a morgue — the patient is "off," the heart is stopped, and the doctor can only reconstruct what happened from the body's condition.
