- **Live Response** is like a **paramedic** arriving at an active emergency scene — the patient is breathing, the heart is beating, monitors are flashing, and the paramedic must record *current* vitals (heart rate, blood pressure) **before** the patient flatlines. Once the machine is powered off, the RAM evaporates, network sockets close, and processes die. That data is lost **forever**.

A second analogy: *Live Response is a "photo of the suspect while they are still running away"*, whereas *Dead Forensics is a "fingerprint sweep after the suspect has been caught and jailed."* Both are useful, but live response captures the **dynamic state** — running malicious processes, active network connections to a C2 server, logged-in attacker sessions — that simply cannot be recovered after shutdown.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Forensic Constants Used in Live Response:**
> - **Memory Decay Rate:** DRAM contents begin to degrade within **1–2 seconds** of power loss; full corruption typically occurs in **30 seconds to 2 minutes** without power.
> - **Standard Memory Capture Time Window:** A 16 GB RAM dump via `dd` over USB 3.0 takes approximately **8–15 minutes**.
> - **Hashing Standard:** **SHA-256** is the KTU-recommended modern hashing algorithm (MD5 is taught only for legacy interoperability).
> - **Volatility Window:** The period from "first investigator contact" to "power-off" during which volatile data can be lawfully and technically captured.

### 1.4 Comparison: Live Response vs. Dead Box Forensics

| Parameter | Live Response | Dead Box Forensics |
|---|---|---|
| System State | Powered ON | Powered OFF |
| Data Type | Volatile + Non-volatile | Non-volatile only |
| Time Critical | **Yes** (seconds matter) | No |
| Memory Dump | Possible & Recommended | Usually impossible |
| Running Processes | Visible in memory | Only in hibernation/pagefile |
| Network State | Live sockets, ARP cache | Reconstructed from logs |
| Risk of Contamination | Higher (touching live system) | Lower (read-only imaging) |
| Tools | `netstat`, `ps`, `memdump`, FTK Imager Lite | Autopsy, EnCase, FTK, X-Ways |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Order of Volatility (OoV) — IETF RFC 3227

The **Order of Volatility** is the foundational principle of live response. It dictates the sequence in which evidence must be collected, **from most volatile to least volatile**, because higher-ranked items decay faster.

### 2.1.1 The Seven-Tier Volatility Hierarchy

1. **Tier 1 — Registers & CPU Cache (Volatility: nanoseconds)**
   - CPU registers, L1/L2/L3 cache, pipeline queues.
   - Practically impossible to capture from commodity OS — only specialized hardware debuggers or CPU-level malware (e.g., CPU bugs like Spectre) leave traces here.
2. **Tier 2 — Routing Table, ARP Cache, Process Table, Kernel Statistics, Live Memory (RAM) (Volatility: seconds to minutes)**
   - This is the **prime target** of live response.
   - Tools: `memdump`, `WinPmem`, `LiME` (Linux Memory Extractor), `FTK Imager Lite`.
3. **Tier 3 — Temporary File Systems (Volatility: minutes to hours)**
   - `/tmp`, `/var/tmp` on Linux; `C:\Windows\Temp` on Windows; RAM disks.
4. **Tier 4 — Disk Storage (Volatility: persists until overwritten)**
   - HDD/SSD file systems, slack space, unallocated clusters.
5. **Tier 5 — Remote Logging and Monitoring Data (Volatility: hours to days)**
   - Syslog servers, SIEM, firewall logs, cloud audit trails.
6. **Tier 6 — Physical Configuration & Network Topology (Volatility: months)**
   - Diagrams, IP assignment sheets, switch/router configs.
7. **Tier 7 — Archival Media (Volatility: years)**
   - Backups, optical media, offsite tape archives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cardinal Rule:** Always capture from **Tier 1 downward**. If you image the disk first and *then* dump RAM, you have destroyed the integrity of the volatile evidence chain by triggering a disk write.

### 2.2 Why and How — The Theoretical Justification

**Why does RAM matter so much?**
Modern malware operates almost entirely in-memory to evade disk-based antivirus. **Fileless malware** (e.g., PowerShell-based attacks, registry-resident persistence) leaves **zero** artifacts on disk. The only way to investigate such attacks is live response RAM capture.

**How is volatility measured?**
The decay time $\tau$ for a memory cell is approximated by:

$$
\tau = \frac{C \cdot V}{I_{leak}}
$$

where $C$ is the cell capacitance, $V$ is the supply voltage, and $I_{leak}$ is the leakage current. In practical terms, this means DRAM loses state in **milliseconds to seconds** at room temperature once power is removed — hence the extreme urgency of live response.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Unit / Value | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau$ (decay constant) | Time for volatile state to decay | seconds (s) | Justifies urgency |
| $H(x)$ | Cryptographic hash of evidence $x$ | 256-bit (SHA-256) | Chain of custody |
| $V_{sys}$ | Total volatile data set | Bytes (B) | Storage planning |
| $T_{cap}$ | Capture time | seconds (s) | Field planning |
| $R_{xfer}$ | Data transfer rate | MB/s | USB 3.0 ≈ **100 MB/s** |
| RFC 3227 | Order of Volatility standard | Document ID | Module 2 reference |
| $N_{procs}$ | Number of running processes | integer | Triage metric |
| $C_{conn}$ | Active network connections | integer | IOC indicator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Relationship:**
> $$T_{cap} = \frac{V_{sys}}{R_{xfer}}$$
> This is the same as the basic physics equation $t = d/r$. A 16 GB RAM image over USB 2.0 (≈30 MB/s) takes **~9 minutes**, so the investigator must decide whether to capture memory *first* (high evidentiary value) or *after* the disk (low evidentiary value for live state).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Live response methodologies are deployed in:

- **Incident Response (IR) teams** for ransomware triage (capture memory to find decryption keys before the user shuts down the machine).
- **CSIRT (Computer Security Incident Response Teams)** of banks, ISPs, and government agencies.
- **Law Enforcement (LE)** at crime scenes where powered-off seizure is impractical (e.g., servers in a live hospital, ATMs in production, SCADA systems in factories).
- **Cloud Forensics** where there is no "physical disk" — only running VMs and live memory.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Live Response Procedure & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate:** Every action in this section is detailed step-by-step. No shortcuts. No truncation. This mirrors the **KTU 14-mark ESE question** where examiners expect a complete procedural walkthrough.

## 3.1 Phase 0 — Pre-Engagement (Authorization)

### Step 1: Verify Legal Authority
- Confirm **written authorization** (search warrant, corporate IT policy, owner consent).
- Document the **scope, time window, and permissible actions** (read-only vs. capture).
- Note the **date, time, location, and case ID** in the investigator's notebook.

### Step 2: Prepare the Toolkit
Load a **forensically sterile USB drive** containing:
- A read-only bootable Linux (e.g., **C.A.I.N.E.**, **DEFT**, or **Paladin**).
- RAM capture tools: **WinPmem** (Windows), **LiME** (Linux), **AVML** (Azure).
- A **trusted command shell**: `cmd.exe`, `PowerShell`, or `bash`.
- Hashing utilities: `sha256sum`, `md5sum`, `fciv` (Windows File Checksum Integrity Verifier).

### Step 3: Photograph the Scene
Capture multiple high-resolution photographs of:
- Monitor(s) showing active applications.
- Cable connections, attached USB devices, network jacks.
- The physical environment (security badges, sticky notes on desks).

---

## 3.2 Phase 1 — System Identification & Triage

### Step 4: Determine System State
- Is the system powered on? (If yes → **Live Response mode**.)
- What OS is running? (`systeminfo` on Windows; `uname -a` on Linux.)
- What is the user logged in? (`whoami`, `query user`.)
- What is the system time and timezone? (`date /T` and `time /T` on Windows; `date` and `timedatectl` on Linux.)

### Step 5: Identify Suspicious Indicators
- Unfamiliar user accounts.
- Unknown scheduled tasks or services.
- Unsigned processes running from `C:\Users\Public\` or `/tmp/`.
- Outbound network connections to non-corporate IP ranges.

---

## 3.3 Phase 2 — Volatile Data Capture (Windows Live Response)

### Step 6: Capture System Date and Time
```cmd
date /T
time /T
wmic os get localdatetime
```

### Step 7: Capture Network Information

**Step 7a — Active Connections:**
```cmd
netstat -ano
netstat -anob
```
The output lists **proto, local address, foreign address, state, PID**. Cross-reference the PID with tasklist to find the owning process.

**Step 7b — Routing Table:**
```cmd
route print
netstat -r
```

**Step 7c — DNS Cache:**
```cmd
ipconfig /displaydns
```

**Step 7d — ARP Cache:**
```cmd
arp -a
```

### Step 8: Capture Running Processes
```cmd
tasklist /v
tasklist /svc
wmic process get name,processid,parentprocessid,executablepath,commandline /format:csv
```

### Step 9: Capture Loaded DLLs and Handles
```cmd
listdlls.exe -u
handle.exe -a
```

### Step 10: Capture User & Session Information
```cmd
query user
query session
net session
net use
net localgroup administrators
```

### Step 11: Capture Registry Hot-Spots
Critical registry hives for forensics (volatile since they are actively read by the OS):
```cmd
reg export HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run run_keys.reg
reg export HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services services.reg
reg export HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\TypedPaths typed_paths.reg
```

### Step 12: Capture Event Logs
```cmd
wevtutil epl Application C:\evidence\application.evtx
wevtutil epl Security C:\evidence\security.evtx
wevtutil epl System C:\evidence\system.evtx
wevtutil epl PowerShell C:\evidence\powershell.evtx
```

### Step 13: Capture Scheduled Tasks & Services
```cmd
schtasks /query /fo LIST /v
sc query
```

### Step 14: Capture Memory Image (Last in Volatile Phase)
```cmd
winpmem_mini_x64_rc2.exe C:\evidence\memdump.raw
```
Hash the output immediately:
```cmd
certutil -hashfile C:\evidence\memdump.raw SHA256
```

### Step 15: Calculate Hash and Log Chain of Custody
Every artifact collected in Steps 6–14 must be hashed using **SHA-256**. The investigator signs a chain-of-custody form linking the hash, the file, the timestamp, and the investigator's signature.

---

## 3.4 Phase 2 — Volatile Data Capture (Linux Live Response)

### Step 16: Capture Date, Time, and Hostname
```bash
date
date -u
hostname
hostname -f
uptime
```

### Step 17: Capture Network Information

**Step 17a — Connections & Sockets:**
```bash
netstat -antup
ss -antup
```

**Step 17b — Routing & Interfaces:**
```bash
route -n
ip route
ip addr show
```

**Step 17c — ARP & DNS:**
```bash
arp -an
cat /etc/resolv.conf
```

### Step 18: Capture Running Processes
```bash
ps auxf
ps -ef
pstree -p
```

### Step 19: Capture Open Files and Loaded Modules
```bash
lsof -nP
lsmod
cat /proc/modules
```

### Step 20: Capture Logged-in Users and Login History
```bash
w
who
last
lastlog
```

### Step 21: Capture Environment & Shell History
```bash
env
cat ~/.bash_history
cat /home/*/.bash_history
```

### Step 22: Capture Mounted File Systems and Disk Usage
```bash
mount
df -h
cat /etc/mtab
```

### Step 23: Capture Cron Jobs and Services
```bash
crontab -l
ls -la /etc/cron*
systemctl list-units --type=service
```

### Step 24: Capture Memory Image
```bash
sudo insmod lime.ko "path=/evidence/memdump.lime format=lime"
```
Hash immediately:
```bash
sha256sum /evidence/memdump.lime
```

---

## 3.5 Phase 3 — Post-Capture Actions

### Step 25: Document the Investigator's Own Actions
Any command run on the live system **modifies volatile state**. The investigator must record:
- The exact command run.
- The time it was executed.
- The expected side effect (e.g., `netstat` does not modify the system, but a poorly-written IR tool might create log entries).

### Step 26: Power Down the System (Decision Point)
- **Option A — Pull the plug (unclean shutdown):** Justified only if a memory-resident rootkit is suspected. Preserves RAM contents (if RAM is cold-boot frozen first) and prevents the malware from executing its shutdown-routine anti-forensics.
- **Option B — Graceful shutdown:** Run `shutdown /s /t 0` (Windows) or `shutdown -h now` (Linux). Triggers a clean unmount, generates a clean event log entry.
- **Option C — Leave running:** For servers where downtime is unacceptable (e.g., hospital systems); coordinate with the system owner.

### Step 27: Transport and Store Evidence
- All volatile artifacts go into a **forensically sterile write-blocked** storage device.
- Two copies maintained: **working copy** and **archival copy**.
- Storage medium is encrypted with **AES-256**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Each Method

| Scenario | Recommended Action | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Fileless malware suspected | Memory dump FIRST | Disk will have no artifacts |
| Encrypted volume (BitLocker/LUKS) | Memory dump FIRST (keys are in RAM) | Need keys to decrypt disk image |
| Active exfiltration in progress | Network capture + netstat + memory | Capture C2 traffic |
| Single-user desktop, no encryption | Disk image can be primary | Memory less critical |
| Running server, no downtime allowed | Volatile data only, no shutdown | Business continuity |
| Anti-forensics suspected | Pull the plug (cold boot) | Prevent malware self-destruct |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Live Response Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Detection] --> B[Verify Legal Authorization]
    B --> C[Prepare Forensic Toolkit USB]
    C --> D[Photograph the Scene]
    D --> E[Identify System State and OS]
    E --> F[Capture System Date and Time]
    F --> G[Capture Network Connections]
    G --> H[Capture Running Processes]
    H --> I[Capture Logged-In Users]
    I --> J[Capture Registry and Config Files]
    J --> K[Capture Event Logs and Cron Jobs]
    K --> L[Hash All Artifacts SHA-256]
    L --> M[Capture Memory Image]
    M --> N{Decision: Shutdown?}
    N --> O[Pull Plug] --> P[Transport Evidence]
    N --> Q[Graceful Shutdown] --> P
    N --> R[Leave Running] --> P
    P --> S[Chain of Custody Sign-Off]
    S --> T[Post-Incident Analysis]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Order of Volatility Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1[Tier 1: CPU Registers and Cache] --> V2[Tier 2: RAM Routing ARP Process Table]
    V2 --> V3[Tier 3: Temporary File Systems]
    V3 --> V4[Tier 4: Disk Storage]
    V4 --> V5[Tier 5: Remote Logs and SIEM]
    V5 --> V6[Tier 6: Network Topology]
    V6 --> V7[Tier 7: Archival Media]
    V1 -.- DECAY1[Decay: nanoseconds]
    V2 -.- DECAY2[Decay: seconds to minutes]
    V3 -.- DECAY3[Decay: minutes to hours]
    V4 -.- DECAY4[Decay: persists until overwrite]
    V5 -.- DECAY5[Decay: hours to days]
    V6 -.- DECAY6[Decay: months]
    V7 -.- DECAY7[Decay: years]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Block-Level Architecture of a Live Response Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TARGET[Target System Powered ON]
        CPU[CPU Registers]
        RAM[Volatile RAM]
        PROC[Running Processes]
        NET[Active Network Sockets]
        FS[Mounted File Systems]
        REG[Registry and Config]
    end
    subgraph INVESTIGATOR[Investigator Workstation USB]
        TOOL1[Memory Imager WinPmem or LiME]
        TOOL2[Process Lister tasklist or ps]
        TOOL3[Net Tool netstat or ss]
        TOOL4[Hasher SHA-256]
        TOOL5[Evidence Logger]
    end
    subgraph STORAGE[Evidence Storage Write-Blocked]
        EVD1[Volatile Artifacts]
        EVD2[Memory Image]
        EVD3[Hash Manifest]
        CHAIN[Chain of Custody Form]
    end
    RAM --> TOOL1
    PROC --> TOOL2
    NET --> TOOL3
    TOOL1 --> TOOL4
    TOOL2 --> TOOL4
    TOOL3 --> TOOL4
    TOOL4 --> EVD3
    TOOL1 --> EVD2
    TOOL2 --> EVD1
    TOOL3 --> EVD1
    REG --> EVD1
    FS --> EVD1
    EVD1 --> CHAIN
    EVD2 --> CHAIN
    EVD3 --> CHAIN
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Live Response in Digital Forensics.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Live Response is a digital forensics methodology in which an investigator acquires volatile evidence from a computer system **while it is still powered on**, following the **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)**. It involves capturing transient data such as running processes, RAM contents, active network connections, logged-in users, and registry keys before the system is powered off. Unlike dead-box forensics, live response captures the **dynamic state** of the system, which is essential for investigating fileless malware, memory-resident rootkits, and active intrusions.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for contrasting with dead-box, 1 mark for mentioning RFC 3227 / Order of Volatility.)*

---

### Question 2: List the Order of Volatility as per RFC 3227.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Order of Volatility from most to least volatile is:
1. CPU registers, cache
2. Routing table, ARP cache, process table, kernel statistics, **RAM**
3. Temporary file systems
4. Disk storage
5. Remote logging and monitoring data
6. Physical configuration, network topology
7. Archival media (backups, optical media)

*(Mark split: 1 mark for citing RFC 3227, 2 marks for the hierarchy list.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU ESE pattern, students answer **one** of the two given options. Each option has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the Order of Volatility as defined in RFC 3227. Why is it critical in live response investigations? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe, with commands, the step-by-step procedure to perform a **Windows live response** for capturing volatile evidence from a running system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer to A(a) — Order of Volatility (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
The **Order of Volatility (OoV)** is the prescribed sequence in IETF **RFC 3227** for collecting digital evidence, prioritized from the most volatile (decays in seconds) to the least volatile (persists for years).

**The Seven Tiers (4 marks):**

1. **CPU Registers and Cache** — nanosecond decay; cannot be captured from commodity OS.
2. **Routing Table, ARP Cache, Process Table, Kernel Statistics, RAM** — second-to-minute decay; **the principal target of live response**.
3. **Temporary File Systems** — minutes to hours; `/tmp`, `C:\Windows\Temp`.
4. **Disk Storage** — persists until overwritten.
5. **Remote Logs and SIEM** — hours to days.
6. **Network Topology and Physical Config** — months.
7. **Archival Media** — years.

**Criticality in Live Response (2 marks):**
- The OoV ensures **time-critical evidence is captured first**; failure to follow it destroys volatile data irrecoverably.
- Example: A memory-resident ransomware key exists only in RAM (Tier 2). If the investigator pulls the plug first, the key evaporates and the encrypted disk becomes undecryptable.
- It also provides a **defensible methodology** in court, showing the investigator followed industry standards.

---

#### Model Answer to A(b) — Windows Live Response Procedure (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Authorization & Toolkit (1 mark):** Verify legal authority, prepare a sterile USB with `winpmem`, `FTK Imager Lite`, `listdlls`, `handle`, hashing tools.

**Step 2 — System Date/Time (0.5 mark):**
```cmd
date /T & time /T
```

**Step 3 — Network State (1.5 marks):**
```cmd
netstat -ano
netstat -anob
route print
arp -a
ipconfig /displaydns
```

**Step 4 — Running Processes (1 mark):**
```cmd
tasklist /v
tasklist /svc
wmic process get name,processid,parentprocessid,commandline /format:csv
```

**Step 5 — Users and Sessions (0.5 mark):**
```cmd
query user
net session
net localgroup administrators
```

**Step 6 — Registry and Event Logs (1 mark):**
```cmd
reg export HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run run_keys.reg
wevtutil epl Security C:\evidence\security.evtx
```

**Step 7 — Memory Dump and Hashing (1 mark):**
```cmd
winpmem_mini_x64_rc2.exe C:\evidence\memdump.raw
certutil -hashfile C:\evidence\memdump.raw SHA256
```

**Step 8 — Chain of Custody and Power-Down Decision (0.5 mark):** Document all commands, hashes, and timestamps; decide on graceful shutdown, hard pull, or leave-running.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Live Response Forensics** with **Dead-Box Forensics**. When is each appropriate? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe, with commands, the step-by-step procedure to perform a **Linux live response** for capturing volatile evidence from a running system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer to B(a) — Live vs. Dead-Box (7 marks)

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Live Response** = evidence collected from a powered-on system using volatile-aware methodology.
- **Dead-Box Forensics** = evidence collected from a powered-off system (or a bit-stream image of the disk).

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Live Response | Dead-Box |
|---|---|---|
| System state | ON | OFF |
| Captures RAM | Yes | No (unless hibernation) |
| Risk of contamination | Higher | Lower (read-only image) |
| Speed | Fast triage | Slow full imaging |
| Anti-forensics exposure | Malware can detect and self-destruct | Cannot detect |
| Time critical | **Critical** | Not critical |

**When to Use Each (2 marks):**
- **Live Response is appropriate** when: (i) memory-resident malware is suspected, (ii) encrypted disks require keys in RAM, (iii) active intrusion is in progress, (iv) the system cannot be powered down (production server).
- **Dead-Box is appropriate** when: (i) the system can be safely seized, (ii) full forensic rigor and a bit-stream image are required for court, (iii) the investigator is not qualified or authorized for live tools, (iv) malware is known to detect and respond to live tools.

---

#### Model Answer to B(b) — Linux Live Response Procedure (7 marks)

**Step 1 — System & Time (1 mark):**
```bash
date
date -u
hostname
uname -a
uptime
```

**Step 2 — Network State (1.5 marks):**
```bash
netstat -antup
ss -antup
ip addr show
ip route
arp -an
cat /etc/resolv.conf
```

**Step 3 — Running Processes (1 mark):**
```bash
ps auxf
pstree -p
lsmod
```

**Step 4 — Users & Login Sessions (0.5 mark):**
```bash
w
who
last
lastlog
```

**Step 5 — File System & Open Files (1 mark):**
```bash
mount
df -h
lsof -nP
```

**Step 6 — Cron, Services, History (1 mark):**
```bash
crontab -l
ls -la /etc/cron*
systemctl list-units --type=service
cat ~/.bash_history
```

**Step 7 — Memory Dump & Hash (1 mark):**
```bash
sudo insmod lime.ko "path=/evidence/memdump.lime format=lime"
sha256sum /evidence/memdump.lime
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Live vs. Dead-Box:** Many students write "live response = imaging the disk." This is **wrong**. Live response = **volatile** data; disk imaging is part of dead-box.
> 2. **Wrong Order of Volatility:** Writing "disk first, then RAM" will cost you **at least 2 marks**. Always RAM/disk-cache *first*.
> 3. **Forgetting Hashing:** KTU examiners specifically check for **SHA-256 / integrity verification** as a separate sub-point. Skipping it loses a mark.
> 4. **No Chain of Custody:** Merely listing commands is insufficient. You must state *"document, hash, and sign chain of custody."*
> 5. **No Mention of RFC 3227:** A 14-mark answer without citing RFC 3227 is treated as incomplete. Always cite the standard.
> 6. **Power-Down Decision Ignored:** Students often stop at memory dump. Examiners expect a discussion of *graceful vs. hard shutdown*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- [ ] **Live Response** = collection of volatile evidence from a **powered-on** system, governed by **RFC 3227 Order of Volatility**.
- [ ] The **seven tiers of volatility** are: (1) CPU/cache, (2) RAM/process table/routing, (3) temp FS, (4) disk, (5) remote logs, (6) network topology, (7) archives.
- [ ] **Always capture RAM FIRST** in incident scenarios involving memory-resident malware or encrypted disks.
- [ ] Windows live response commands to remember: `netstat -ano`, `tasklist /svc`, `wmic process get ... commandline`, `reg export`, `wevtutil epl`, `winpmem`, `certutil -hashfile`.
- [ ] Linux live response commands to remember: `netstat -antup`, `ss -antup`, `ps auxf`, `lsof -nP`, `last`, `cat ~/.bash_history`, `insmod lime.ko`, `sha256sum`.
- [ ] **Hashing standard:** SHA-256 (modern) / MD5 (legacy). Use `certutil`, `sha256sum`, or `Get-FileHash`.
- [ ] **Memory dump tools:** `WinPmem` (Windows), `LiME` (Linux), `MemProcFS`, `AVML` (Azure), `FTK Imager Lite`.
- [ ] **Three shutdown strategies:** (a) graceful shutdown, (b) hard pull, (c) leave running. Choice depends on malware and business impact.
- [ ] **Anti-forensics countermeasure:** Malware may detect `netstat`, `tasklist`, or `reg query` (via API hooks). Use **raw API** tools (e.g., `NirSoft utilities`) or **memory forensics** to bypass.
- [ ] **Chain of Custody** must accompany every artifact: investigator name, date/time, hash, source location, signature.
- [ ] **Live Response vs. Dead-Box:** Live = volatile, urgent, contamination risk, captures dynamic state. Dead-Box = non-volatile, court-friendly, bit-stream image, no RAM.
- [ ] **Fileless malware** investigation is **impossible** without live response or memory forensics.
- [ ] **RAM decay** is in **seconds to minutes** — a 16 GB dump takes **~9 minutes** over USB 2.0.
- [ ] Always **photograph the scene** before touching the system (monitor, cables, peripherals).
- [ ] **Document every command** you run on the live system — running commands **modifies volatile state** and is admissible evidence in court only if documented.

---

**End of Module 2 — Live Response Methodologies Notes**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | PECCT866 — Windows and Linux Forensics*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Static Analysis # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Static Analysis (Static Malware Analysis)** is the process of examining a malicious binary or executable **without executing it**, by inspecting its raw binary structure, metadata, embedded strings, headers, imports, exports, and cryptographic fingerprints to identify Indicators of Compromise (IOCs), classify malware families, and assess potential behavior. In the context of **PECCT866 – Windows and Linux Forensics**, static analysis is a **non-intrusive, dormant-state forensic technique** that operates purely on the *on-disk* or *captured image* representation of a suspicious artifact. It forms the **first line of investigation** in any malware triage pipeline, executed in an isolated sandbox or dedicated forensic workstation before any dynamic execution is attempted. The fundamental primitives of static analysis are: - **File Fingerprinting** — Cryptographic hashing (MD5, **SHA-1**, **SHA-256**) - **Metadata Extraction** — Version info, timestamps, digital signatures - **String Mining** — Human-readable ASCII/Unicode artifacts - **Structural Parsing** — PE (Windows) or ELF (Linux) header dissection - **Entropy Profiling** — Detecting packing/encryption - **Code Disassembly** — Reverse engineering assembly instructions ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy — The Forensics Detective at a Crime Scene:** Imagine a detective arriving at a sealed crime scene. Before touching or moving anything, the detective photographs the room, dusts for fingerprints, catalogs every visible object, and reads any documents left open. The detective *understands the crime* without *replaying it*. Static analysis is exactly this — it studies the "frozen" evidence of a binary without ever "running" the suspect program. - A malware sample is like a **locked suitcase**. - Dynamic analysis = *opening and shaking the suitcase to see what falls out*. - Static analysis = *X-raying, weighing, fingerprinting, and reading the luggage tag without ever unzipping it*. ## 1.3 Key Metrics & Constants (Board-Important) > [!NOTE] > The following standard cryptographic hash lengths and file-structure offsets are **board-favorite numbers** for KTU exams: > > - **MD5** → **128 bits (16 bytes / 32 hex chars)** > - **SHA-1** → **160 bits (20 bytes / 40 hex chars)** > - **SHA-256** → **256 bits (32 bytes / 64 hex chars)** > - **MZ Magic Bytes (DOS Header)** → `0x5A4D` (`"MZ"`) > - **PE Signature Offset** → `0x3C` → points to `0x00004550` (`"PE\0\0"`) > - **High-Entropy Threshold** → **> 7.0** indicates packed/encrypted regions > - **DOS Header Size** → **64 bytes (0x40)** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Static Analysis Static analysis in Windows and Linux forensics is structured around **five sequential pillars**. Each pillar produces progressively deeper intelligence about the suspect binary. ### Pillar 1 — File Identification & Fingerprinting - **Goal:** Establish *unambiguous identity* of the artifact. - **Mechanism:** Compute cryptographic hash digests over the entire file byte stream. - **Why first?** Identical malware variants produce *identical hashes*, enabling cross-case correlation against threat-intelligence feeds (VirusTotal, MalwareBazaar, MISP). ### Pillar 2 — Metadata & Wrapper Inspection - **Goal:** Extract provenance and authorship trails. - **Windows:** Parse `VersionInfo` resource → fields like `CompanyName`, `OriginalFilename`, `InternalName`, `LegalCopyright`. - **Linux:** Inspect ELF comments, build-id (`.note.gnu.build-id`), debug sections. - **Why important?** Threat actors often leave **fake corporate metadata** as decoys (a "red herring" technique). ### Pillar 3 — String Extraction - **Goal:** Recover human-readable artifacts left in the binary. - **Categories:** - **ASCII/Unicode strings** → URLs, IPs, registry keys, file paths - **Error messages** → Sometimes reveal compiler/version - **Crypto constants** → AES S-box detection (`0x63 0x7C 0x77 0x7B`) - **Tool:** `strings`, `FLOSS` (FireEye Labs Obfuscated String Solver), `BinText`. ### Pillar 4 — Structural Parsing (PE / ELF Headers) - **Goal:** Decode the executable's *blueprint* — how the OS will load and run it. - **Windows PE:** DOS Header → NT Headers (File Header + Optional Header) → Section Table → Imports/Exports. - **Linux ELF:** ELF Header → Program Headers (segments) → Section Headers (sections) → Dynamic Section. ### Pillar 5 — Entropy & Packer Detection - **Goal:** Detect obfuscation layers (UPX, Themida, VMProtect, custom packers). - **Mechanism:** Compute **Shannon Entropy** per section. Compressed/encrypted data has high randomness (entropy ≈ 8.0). - **Why it matters:** Packed binaries **hide their true code** until runtime, defeating string analysis. High entropy is the *smoking gun* that further unpacking or dynamic analysis is mandatory. ## 2.2 The Hashing Pipeline (Engineering Utility) Hashing is the **forensic equivalent of a fingerprint**. Two files differing by even **one byte** produce completely different digests — this is the **avalanche effect**. The formal cryptographic hash function is: $$H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$$ where $H$ is the hash function, $\{0,1\}^*$ is the set of all finite-length binary strings (the file), and $\{0,1\}^n$ is the fixed-length output of $n$ bits. **Engineering utility:** - **Incident Response:** Match malware samples across thousands of endpoints. - **Evidence Integrity:** Chain-of-custody verification (a re-hash proves no tampering). - **Threat Intelligence:** YARA rules and IOC databases rely on hash matching. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cheat-Sheet Table — Memorize This for KTU 2024 ESE** | Concept | Formula / Rule | Typical Value | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | MD5 Digest Size | $\vert H \vert$ | **128** | bits (16 bytes) | | SHA-1 Digest Size | $\vert H \vert$ | **160** | bits (20 bytes) | | SHA-256 Digest Size | $\vert H \vert$ | **256** | bits (32 bytes) | | Shannon Entropy | $E = - \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot \log_2 p_i$ | **0 to 8** | bits per symbol | | High-Entropy Threshold | $E \geq 7.0$ | packed/encrypted | dimensionless | | Low-Entropy Threshold | $E \leq 3.0$ | plain text / sparse | dimensionless | | PE Signature Magic | `"PE\0\0"` | `0x00004550` | hex little-endian | | DOS e_lfanew Offset | `0x3C` | 60 | decimal offset | | Avalanche Effect | $\Delta H \approx 50\%$ per bit flip | full diffusion | % output change | ## 2.4 Shannon Entropy — The Core Equation Shannon Entropy quantifies the *information density* (randomness) of a byte sequence. For a block of $N$ bytes with $n$ distinct byte values, where $p_i$ is the probability of byte value $i$: $$E = - \sum_{i=0}^{255} p_i \cdot \log_2 p_i$$ **Boundary Cases:** - **Minimum entropy** ($E = 0$): All bytes identical (e.g., a block of `\x00`). - **Maximum entropy** ($E = 8$): Perfectly random distribution (e.g., AES ciphertext, compressed data). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Shannon Entropy of a Malware Section Consider a 4-byte PE section containing the bytes: `\x41\x41\x42\x43` (ASCII `A A B C`). **Step 1 — Count byte frequencies** $$\text{Freq}(0x41) = 2, \quad \text{Freq}(0x42) = 1, \quad \text{Freq}(0x43) = 1$$ **Step 2 — Compute total length** $$N = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 3 — Compute probabilities for each unique byte** $$\begin{aligned} p_{0x41} &= \frac{2}{4} = 0.50 \\ p_{0x42} &= \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \\ p_{0x43} &= \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Shannon's entropy formula** $$E = - \sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$$ **Step 5 — Expand the summation term-by-term** $$\begin{aligned} E &= - \left( p_{0x41} \log_2 p_{0x41} + p_{0x42} \log_2 p_{0x42} + p_{0x43} \log_2 p_{0x43} \right) \\ &= - \left( 0.50 \cdot \log_2 0.50 + 0.25 \cdot \log_2 0.25 + 0.25 \cdot \log_2 0.25 \right) \\ &= - \left( 0.50 \cdot (-1) + 0.25 \cdot (-2) + 0.25 \cdot (-2) \right) \\ &= - \left( -0.50 - 0.50 - 0.50 \right) \\ &= - \left( -1.50 \right) \\ E &= 1.50 \text{ bits per symbol} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Forensic Interpretation** Since $E = 1.50$ is **well below the 7.0 high-entropy threshold**, this section is **NOT packed or encrypted**. It is a region of low-entropy, predictable data — likely a small string constant or readable text. The forensic conclusion is: *no obfuscation detected in this block*. > [!NOTE] > **Analytical Pattern Recognition:** A real `.text` (code) section of an *unpacked* PE typically shows $E \approx 5.5$–$6.5$. A *UPX-packed* `.text` section typically shows $E \approx 7.6$–$7.9$. A *plain `.rsrc`* resource section of icons/manifests shows $E \approx 3.0$–$4.5$. Always cross-reference entropy with the section's declared `VirtualSize` vs `RawSize` mismatch. ## 3.2 Detailed Walkthrough: PE Header Chain of Pointers When Windows loads a `.exe`, the loader follows a **chain of fixed-offset pointers** through the PE structure. Each pointer is *read-only* and *pre-computed* at compile time. **Step 1 — DOS Header (64 bytes)** The loader reads the first 2 bytes at offset `0x00`, expecting the magic `0x5A4D` ("MZ"). The MZ stands for **Mark Zbikowski**, the original architect of MS-DOS. **Step 2 — e_lfanew pointer (offset 0x3C)** The loader reads a **4-byte little-endian DWORD** at file offset `0x3C`. This DWORD is the file offset of the **PE Signature**. For a standard 64-bit Windows binary compiled with MSVC, this value is typically `0x000000F8` (i.e., 248 bytes into the file). **Step 3 — PE Signature (4 bytes)** At offset `0xF8`, the loader finds `0x00004550` which decodes ASCII as `"PE\0\0"`. This confirms the artifact is a valid Portable Executable. **Step 4 — COFF File Header (20 bytes)** Immediately after the PE signature, the loader reads the **IMAGE_FILE_HEADER** which contains the `Machine` type (e.g., `0x8664` for x64), `NumberOfSections`, `TimeDateStamp`, and `SizeOfOptionalHeader`. **Step 5 — Optional Header (variable, 240 bytes for PE32+)** The loader reads `IMAGE_OPTIONAL_HEADER`, which is the **heart of the PE** — containing the `AddressOfEntryPoint`, `ImageBase`, `SectionAlignment`, `Subsystem` (e.g., `0x02` for Windows GUI), and the **Data Directories** (Import Table, Export Table, Resource Table, TLS, IAT, etc.). **Step 6 — Section Table** Following the Optional Header, an array of `IMAGE_SECTION_HEADER` structures (40 bytes each) describes each section (`.text`, `.data`, `.rsrc`, `.reloc`, …). Each section header specifies the section's `Name`, `VirtualSize`, `VirtualAddress`, `RawSize`, and `RawOffset`. > [!TIP] > **Cross-Verification Matrix (Examiner's Quick-Reference):** > > | Field | Located At | Size | Purpose | > |---|---|---|---| > | e_lfanew | Offset 0x3C | 4 bytes | Pointer to PE signature | > | PE Signature | Variable | 4 bytes | Magic "PE\0\0" | > | File Header | PE+4 | 20 bytes | COFF info | > | Optional Header | PE+24 | 240 / 224 bytes | Loader config | > | Section Table | After OptHdr | 40 × N | Per-section metadata | > | Import Table | Data Dir[1] | Variable | DLL dependency list | > | Export Table | Data Dir[0] | Variable | Public function names | ## 3.3 Detailed Walkthrough: Computing a SHA-256 Digest (Conceptual) The SHA-256 algorithm processes the input in **64-byte (512-bit) blocks**, applying a series of bitwise operations, modular additions, and compression rounds. While the full algorithm has **64 rounds**, the *static-analysis forensic* perspective focuses on these key properties: **Step 1 — Padding** The input message is padded so its length $\equiv 448 \pmod{512}$. A `1`-bit is appended, followed by zeros, then the 64-bit big-endian representation of the original message length. **Step 2 — Initial Hash Values (H₀)** SHA-256 initializes 8 working variables (a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h) with the first 32 bits of the fractional parts of the square roots of the first 8 primes (2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19). These are **standardized constants** defined in FIPS 180-4. **Step 3 — Message Schedule** Each 512-bit block is expanded into a **64-word (× 32-bit) message schedule** $W_t$, computed via: $$W_t = \begin{cases} M_t^{(i)} & \text{for } 0 \leq t \leq 15 \\ \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16} & \text{for } 16 \leq t \leq 63 \end{cases}$$ **Step 4 — Compression Function** For each round $t$, the working variables are updated using bitwise rotations, shifts, and additions modulo $2^{32}$: $$T_1 = h + \Sigma_1(e) + \text{Ch}(e,f,g) + K_t + W_t$$ $$T_2 = \Sigma_0(a) + \text{Maj}(a,b,c)$$ $$h = g, \quad g = f, \quad f = e, \quad e = d + T_1$$ $$d = c, \quad c = b, \quad b = a, \quad a = T_1 + T_2$$ where $\Sigma_0, \Sigma_1$ are bitwise rotation functions, $\text{Ch}$ is the *choose* function, $\text{Maj}$ is the *majority* function, and $K_t$ are 64 round-specific constants derived from the cube roots of the first 64 primes. **Step 5 — Final Hash** After processing all blocks, the final 256-bit hash is the concatenation of the 8 working variables. For KTU purposes, the *output length* ($n = 256$ bits) and the *avalanche property* (each input bit affects ~50% of output bits) are the only board-relevant facts. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Static Analysis Workflow (Block-Level Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Suspect Binary Artifact] --> B[Acquire Read-Only Forensic Copy] B --> C[Step 1: File Fingerprinting] C --> C1[Compute MD5] C --> C2[Compute SHA-1] C --> C3[Compute SHA-256] C1 --> D[Step 2: Metadata Extraction] C2 --> D C3 --> D D --> D1[VersionInfo Resource] D --> D2[Compile Timestamps] D --> D3[Digital Signature Check] D1 --> E[Step 3: String Mining] D2 --> E D3 --> E E --> E1[ASCII Strings] E --> E2[Unicode Strings] E --> E3[FLOSS Obfuscated Strings] E1 --> F[Step 4: Structural Parsing] E2 --> F E3 --> F F --> F1[PE or ELF Header Decode] F --> F2[Import and Export Tables] F --> F3[Section Table Analysis] F1 --> G[Step 5: Entropy Profiling] F2 --> G F3 --> G G --> G1[Per-Section Entropy] G --> G2{Packed or Encrypted?} G2 -->|Yes| H[Flag for Dynamic Analysis] G2 -->|No| I[Compile Static IOCs] H --> J[Final Forensic Report] I --> J J --> K[Threat Intel Correlation] ``` ## 4.2 PE File Internal Structure (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD DOS[DOS Header - 64 bytes
Magic 0x5A4D] DOSP[e_lfanew Pointer at offset 0x3C] PESIG[PE Signature - 4 bytes
0x00004550 PE 0 0] FH[COFF File Header - 20 bytes
Machine, NumSections, TimeStamp] OH[Optional Header - 240 bytes PE32+
EntryPoint, ImageBase, Subsystem] DD[Data Directories - 16 entries
Import, Export, Resource, TLS, IAT] ST[Section Table - 40 bytes per section] S1[.text Section
Executable Code] S2[.data Section
Initialized Data] S3[.rsrc Section
Resources Icons Manifest] S4[.reloc Section
Base Relocations] DOS --> DOSP DOSP --> PESIG PESIG --> FH FH --> OH OH --> DD FH --> ST ST --> S1 ST --> S2 ST --> S3 ST --> S4 ``` ## 4.3 Entropy Decision Matrix (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Compute Shannon Entropy E] --> RANGE{Entropy Range?} RANGE -->|E less than 3| LOW[Sparse / Plain Text / Null Padding] RANGE -->|E between 3 and 5| MED[Structured Data / Strings / Icons] RANGE -->|E between 5 and 6.5| CODE[Unpacked Executable Code .text] RANGE -->|E between 6.5 and 7| SUS[Suspicious Borderline Region] RANGE -->|E greater than 7| HIGH[Packed or Encrypted Region] SUS --> X[Cross-Check VirtualSize vs RawSize Mismatch] HIGH --> Y[Flag for Unpacking and Dynamic Analysis] X --> Z{Mismatch Greater Than 10x?} Z -->|Yes| Y Z -->|No| CODE ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define static analysis in the context of malware forensics. List any four techniques used during static analysis.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (2 marks):** Static analysis is the forensic examination of a malicious binary **without executing it**, by inspecting its on-disk structure, metadata, strings, headers, imports, and cryptographic fingerprints to extract Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) and classify malicious behavior patterns. > [!NOTE] > **Four Techniques (1 mark — list any 4):** > 1. File Fingerprinting (MD5 / SHA-1 / SHA-256 hashing) > 2. String Extraction (ASCII / Unicode / FLOSS-obfuscated strings) > 3. PE / ELF Header Parsing (sections, imports, exports, entry point) > 4. Shannon Entropy Analysis (packer and encryption detection) > 5. Metadata Inspection (VersionInfo, compile timestamps, digital signatures) > 6. Disassembly (static reverse engineering with IDA Pro / Ghidra / radare2) --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **What is Shannon Entropy? Mention its role in detecting packed malware with suitable entropy range values.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Shannon Entropy is a measure of randomness or information density in a byte stream, given by > $$E = - \sum_{i=0}^{255} p_i \log_2 p_i$$ > where $p_i$ is the probability of byte value $i$ occurring in the block. > [!NOTE] > **Role in Packer Detection (1.5 marks):** Compressed and encrypted payloads exhibit **near-maximum randomness**, producing high entropy values. Standard thresholds are: > - $E \leq 3.0$ → Plain text / sparse data > - $E \approx 5.5$–$6.5$ → Normal executable code > - $E \geq 7.0$ → **Packed or encrypted region** (flag for unpacking) > - $E \approx 8.0$ → Maximum entropy (perfect randomness, e.g., AES ciphertext) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] — 14 Marks #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the Portable Executable (PE) file format in Windows. With a neat diagram, describe the role of DOS Header, PE Signature, File Header, Optional Header, and Section Table.** **Model Solution:** > [!NOTE] > **Introduction (1 mark):** The Portable Executable (PE) format is the **standard executable file format** for Windows operating systems (32-bit PE32 and 64-bit PE32+). It encapsulates code, data, resources, and relocation information in a single self-describing binary blob. The format is designed so the Windows loader can map the file into memory and begin execution without secondary metadata. > [!NOTE] > **DOS Header (1.5 marks):** The DOS Header is a **64-byte structure** that begins at file offset `0x00` with the magic bytes `0x5A4D` ("MZ", honoring Mark Zbikowski). Although legacy code from MS-DOS, modern Windows retains it for **backward compatibility**. The critical field is **e_lfanew** at offset `0x3C` — a 4-byte little-endian pointer indicating the file offset of the PE signature. > [!NOTE] > **PE Signature (0.5 marks):** Located at the offset pointed to by e_lfanew (typically `0xF8`), the PE Signature is a 4-byte magic value `0x00004550` which decodes ASCII as `"PE\0\0"`. It confirms the file is a valid PE binary. > [!NOTE] > **COFF File Header (1.5 marks):** Immediately following the PE signature is the **20-byte IMAGE_FILE_HEADER** containing: > - `Machine` (e.g., `0x14C` for x86, `0x8664` for x64) > - `NumberOfSections` (count of section headers) > - `TimeDateStamp` (compile time, often forged) > - `PointerToSymbolTable`, `NumberOfSymbols` (debug info) > - `SizeOfOptionalHeader` (size of the following header) > - `Characteristics` (DLL vs EXE flags) > [!NOTE] > **Optional Header (1.5 marks):** Despite its name, this header is **mandatory**. For PE32+ it is **240 bytes** and contains: > - `Magic` (`0x20B` for PE32+) > - `AddressOfEntryPoint` (RVA where execution begins) > - `ImageBase` (preferred load address) > - `SectionAlignment`, `FileAlignment` (memory vs disk alignment) > - `Subsystem` (e.g., `0x02` GUI, `0x03` Console) > - **Data Directories** (16 entries pointing to Import, Export, Resource, TLS, IAT, etc.) > [!NOTE] > **Section Table (1 mark):** An array of `IMAGE_SECTION_HEADER` structures (40 bytes each) follows the Optional Header. Each entry describes a section's: > - `Name` (8-byte UTF-8, e.g., `.text`, `.data`, `.rsrc`) > - `VirtualSize`, `VirtualAddress` (in-memory layout) > - `SizeOfRawData`, `PointerToRawData` (on-disk layout) > - `Characteristics` (read/write/execute permissions) > [!NOTE] > **Diagram (for reference — redraw on answer sheet):** > ``` > +-------------------+ 0x00 > | DOS Header | 64 bytes (Magic "MZ") > +-------------------+ > | e_lfanew (0x3C) | → offset to PE sig > +-------------------+ 0x40 > | DOS Stub | (legacy code) > +-------------------+ 0xF8 (typical) > | PE Signature | "PE\0\0" > +-------------------+ > | File Header | 20 bytes > +-------------------+ > | Optional Header | 240 bytes (PE32+) > +-------------------+ > | Data Directories | 16 entries × 8 bytes > +-------------------+ > | Section Table | 40 bytes × N > +-------------------+ > | .text | > +-------------------+ > | .data | > +-------------------+ > | .rsrc | > +-------------------+ > ``` #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Discuss the role of Imports and Exports tables in PE files. How does import table analysis help in static malware analysis?** **Model Solution:** > [!NOTE] > **Import Table (3 marks):** The Import Table is stored in the **Data Directories** (index 1) of the Optional Header. It is an array of `IMAGE_IMPORT_DESCRIPTOR` structures (20 bytes each), one per imported DLL. Each descriptor lists: > - `OriginalFirstThunk` → RVA to the **Import Name Table (INT)** — array of function names or ordinals > - `Name` → RVA to the DLL name string (e.g., `"kernel32.dll"`) > - `FirstThunk` → RVA to the **Import Address Table (IAT)** — same array as INT, but overwritten at load time with actual function addresses > [!NOTE] > **Export Table (2 marks):** The Export Table is stored in Data Directories (index 0). It is *optional* (most EXEs do not export functions; only DLLs do). It contains: > - `Name` → DLL name > - `NumberOfFunctions`, `NumberOfNames` > - `AddressOfFunctions` → array of function RVAs > - `AddressOfNames` → array of function name RVAs > - `AddressOfNameOrdinals` → array of ordinal bindings > [!NOTE] > **Static Malware Analysis Utility (2 marks):** Import table analysis is one of the **highest-yield static analysis techniques** because it reveals *intent without execution*: > 1. **Behavioral Inference:** Imports like `CreateRemoteThread`, `VirtualAllocEx`, `WriteProcessMemory` strongly suggest **process injection** capability. > 2. **Cryptography Detection:** Imports from `advapi32.dll` (CryptEncrypt, CryptDecrypt) or presence of the AES S-box constant `0x63 0x7C 0x77 0x7B` in `.rdata` indicate **encryption routines**. > 3. **Network Capability:** Imports like `WSAStartup`, `connect`, `InternetOpenA` from `wininet.dll` indicate **C2 communication** potential. > 4. **Anti-Analysis:** Imports of `IsDebuggerPresent`, `CheckRemoteDebuggerPresent`, `NtQueryInformationProcess` indicate **anti-debugging** behavior. > 5. **Persistence:** Imports of `RegSetValueEx`, `CreateService`, `MoveFileEx` indicate **persistence mechanisms**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students confuse the **INT (Import Name Table)** with the **IAT (Import Address Table)**. Remember: **INT holds function names** (read-only, baked at compile time), and **IAT holds function addresses** (overwritten by the loader at runtime). Examiners specifically test this distinction — losing 2 marks here is common. --- ### Question B [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] — 14 Marks #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the concept of cryptographic hashing in static analysis. Compute the entropy of a 5-byte section containing bytes `\x90\x90\x90\x41\x42` and interpret the result.** **Model Solution:** > [!NOTE] > **Cryptographic Hashing — Concept (3 marks):** > > A cryptographic hash function $H$ is a **one-way, deterministic, fixed-output** function that maps an arbitrary-length input to a fixed-length digest. For static analysis, the three primary algorithms are: > > - **MD5 (128-bit):** Fast, widely used for file identification but cryptographically broken. > - **SHA-1 (160-bit):** Deprecated for security, still used for malware family identification. > - **SHA-256 (256-bit):** Current standard; used in YARA rules, VirusTotal, and modern IOC databases. > > **Properties essential to forensics:** > 1. **Determinism:** Same file → same hash (always). > 2. **Avalanche Effect:** 1-bit input change → ~50% output bit change. > 3. **Collision Resistance:** Computationally infeasible to find two files with the same hash. > 4. **Pre-image Resistance:** Infeasible to reconstruct the file from its hash alone. > [!NOTE] > **Entropy Computation — 5-byte section `\x90\x90\x90\x41\x42` (4 marks):** > > **Step 1: Count Frequencies** > $$\text{Freq}(0x90) = 3, \quad \text{Freq}(0x41) = 1, \quad \text{Freq}(0x42) = 1$$ > > **Step 2: Total Length** > $$N = 3 + 1 + 1 = 5 \text{ bytes}$$ > > **Step 3: Probabilities** > $$\begin{aligned} > p_{0x90} &= \frac{3}{5} = 0.60 \\ > p_{0x41} &= \frac{1}{5} = 0.20 \\ > p_{0x42} &= \frac{1}{5} = 0.20 > \end{aligned}$$ > > **Step 4: Apply Shannon Formula** > $$\begin{aligned} > E &= - \left( p_{0x90} \log_2 p_{0x90} + p_{0x41} \log_2 p_{0x41} + p_{0x42} \log_2 p_{0x42} \right) \\ > &= - \left( 0.60 \cdot \log_2 0.60 + 0.20 \cdot \log_2 0.20 + 0.20 \cdot \log_2 0.20 \right) \\ > &= - \left( 0.60 \cdot (-0.737) + 0.20 \cdot (-2.322) + 0.20 \cdot (-2.322) \right) \\ > &= - \left( -0.4422 - 0.4644 - 0.4644 \right) \\ > &= - \left( -1.371 \right) \\ > E &\approx 1.371 \text{ bits per symbol} > \end{aligned}$$ > > **Step 5: Forensic Interpretation** > Since $E \approx 1.37 \ll 7.0$, the section is **NOT packed or encrypted**. The high frequency of `0x90` (the NOP / "no operation" x86 instruction) suggests this block is a **NOP sled** — a common pattern in shellcode and buffer-overflow exploits where attackers pad the payload with `0x90` bytes to increase the chance of landing on the correct instruction pointer offset. This is a **high-confidence indicator of exploit code**. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Describe the process of static analysis of a Linux ELF binary. What are the key differences in static analysis between Windows PE and Linux ELF binaries?** **Model Solution:** > [!NOTE] > **Linux ELF Static Analysis Process (3.5 marks):** > > **Step 1: File Type Verification** > - Confirm the magic bytes `0x7F 'E' 'L' 'F'` at offset `0x00`. > - Identify the class: `0x01` = 32-bit, `0x02` = 64-bit. > - Identify endianness: `0x01` = little-endian, `0x02` = big-endian. > - Identify OS/ABI: `0x00` = System V (standard Linux), `0x03` = Linux. > > **Step 2: ELF Header Analysis (64 bytes for ELF64)** > - `e_type`: `0x02` = EXEC, `0x03` = DYN (PIE/shared), `0x04` = CORE (dump). > - `e_machine`: `0x03E` = x86, `0x00B7` = AArch64. > - `e_entry`: Virtual address of the entry point. > - `e_phoff`, `e_shoff`: Offsets to program and section headers. > - `e_phnum`, `e_shnum`: Counts of program/section headers. > > **Step 3: Program Header (Segment) Analysis** > - `PT_LOAD` segments → mapped into memory at runtime. > - `PT_DYNAMIC` → points to `.dynamic` section (dependencies, init/fini arrays). > - `PT_INTERP` → contains path to dynamic linker (e.g., `/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2`). > - `PT_GNU_STACK` → **executable stack flag** (NX-bit; if `PF_X` is set, the stack is executable → **shellcode-friendly**). > > **Step 4: Section Header Analysis** > - `.text` → executable code > - `.rodata` → read-only constants (strings, jump tables) > - `.data`, `.bss` → initialized and uninitialized data > - `.dynsym`, `.dynstr` → dynamic symbol/string tables (similar to PE imports/exports) > - `.note.gnu.build-id` → unique build identifier (like a hash) > - `.comment` → compiler version string > > **Step 5: String and Symbol Extraction** > - `strings` utility for ASCII/Unicode. > - `readelf -s` for symbol table inspection. > - `objdump -d` for static disassembly. > - `nm` for symbol listing. > - `ldd` (dynamic) for shared library resolution. > - `checksec` (from pwntools) for NX/PIE/RELRO/Canary flags. > [!NOTE] > **Key Differences — Windows PE vs Linux ELF (3.5 marks):** > > | Aspect | Windows PE | Linux ELF | > |---|---|---| > | Magic Header | `0x5A4D` ("MZ") | `0x7F454C46` ("\x7FELF") | > | Header Length | DOS stub + PE sig | Single 64-byte ELF header | > | Linking | Import Address Table (IAT) | Procedure Linkage Table (PLT) + GOT | > | Format | `IMAGE_*` prefixed structures | `Elf64_*` prefixed structures | > | Sections | `.text`, `.data`, `.rsrc` | `.text`, `.data`, `.rodata`, `.bss` | > | Dynamic Info | Import Directory | `.dynamic` section + `PT_DYNAMIC` | > | Default Base | `0x00400000` or `0x00007FF6...` | `0x400000` (non-PIE) or PIE | > | Signing | Authenticode digital signature | No native equivalent (use `signify`, GPG) | > | Resources | `.rsrc` (icons, manifests, version) | No native resource section | > | Analysis Tools | PE-bear, CFF Explorer, DIE | `readelf`, `objdump`, `nm`, Ghidra | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Question B):** A common student mistake is to claim that Linux ELF binaries **do not have imports**. This is wrong — ELF uses **`.dynsym` (Dynamic Symbol Table)** and **`.dynstr` (Dynamic String Table)** sections, plus the **`.dynamic`** section containing `DT_NEEDED` entries that list required shared libraries (analogous to PE's Import Table). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for confusing this. Also, watch for the **`PT_GNU_STACK` executable flag** — it is a critical security indicator often overlooked. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Static Analysis** > > - **Definition:** Examining a binary **without executing it** — non-intrusive forensic technique. > - **Five Pillars:** Fingerprinting → Metadata → Strings → Structural Parsing → Entropy. > - **Hash Algorithms:** MD5 (128-bit), SHA-1 (160-bit), SHA-256 (256-bit) — KTU favorite numbers. > - **PE Magic:** `"MZ"` at offset `0x00`; `"PE\0\0"` at offset pointed by e_lfanew (`0x3C`). > - **ELF Magic:** `0x7F 0x45 0x4C 0x46` at offset `0x00`. > - **Shannon Entropy Formula:** $E = - \sum p_i \log_2 p_i$, range $0$ to $8$. > - **Packer Threshold:** $E \geq 7.0$ → packed/encrypted (flag for dynamic analysis). > - **Avalanche Effect:** A 1-bit input change → ~50% output change in cryptographic hash. > - **INT vs IAT:** Import Name Table = function names; Import Address Table = function pointers (overwritten by loader). > - **Behavioral Inference via Imports:** `CreateRemoteThread` = injection; `IsDebuggerPresent` = anti-debug; `InternetOpenA` = network capability. > - **NOP Sled Indicator:** High concentration of `0x90` bytes (entropy < 2.0) → exploit code. > - **ELF Security Flags (checksec):** NX (no-execute stack), PIE (position-independent), RELRO, Stack Canary. > - **Key Linux Tools:** `readelf`, `objdump`, `nm`, `strings`, `file`, `checksec`, Ghidra. > - **Key Windows Tools:** PE-bear, CFF Explorer, DIE (Detect It Easy), FLOSS, IDA Free, Ghidra. > - **Chain of Custody:** Always re-hash the forensic image to prove integrity at every step. > - **Limitation:** Static analysis cannot reveal **runtime-only behavior** — packed code, dynamic API resolution, or sandbox-evasion tricks remain hidden until dynamic analysis is performed.

topics.title: Dynamic Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dynamic Analysis** (also known as *Behavioral Analysis* or *Runtime Analysis*) in the context of Windows and Linux Forensics is the systematic examination of a suspicious program, binary, or executable by executing it within an **isolated, instrumented, and controlled environment** (commonly referred to as a *sandbox* or *virtual machine*) and observing its real-time interactions with the operating system, file system, registry (Windows), kernel, network, and memory.

Unlike *Static Analysis* — which inspects the code without execution — Dynamic Analysis focuses on **what the program *does*** when it runs: which processes it spawns, which system calls it invokes, which files it creates/modifies/deletes, which ports it opens, and what data it exfiltrates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Dynamic analysis is the process of executing malicious code in a controlled environment to observe its behavior, system interactions, and side effects without exposing the host system to compromise.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **forensic biologist** who receives a sealed, potentially dangerous biological sample in the mail. You cannot open it directly. What do you do?

1. You place it inside a **sealed, sterile laboratory chamber** (the *sandbox*).
2. You attach **sensors, cameras, and monitors** to the chamber walls (the *instrumentation tools*).
3. You carefully open the package and **observe** — does it release gas? Does it grow? Does it emit radiation? Does it try to escape?
4. Every action is **logged** for later analysis.

Dynamic malware analysis works identically:
- The **sample** = suspicious binary (`.exe`, `.dll`, `.elf`, shell script).
- The **lab chamber** = isolated virtual machine (VMware, VirtualBox, Cuckoo Sandbox).
- The **sensors** = Process Monitor, Wireshark, Regshot, API Monitor, strace, ltrace.
- The **logs** = behavioral indicators of compromise (IOCs).

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Metrics

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Sandbox** | An isolated environment (VM/container) used to safely execute untrusted code. |
| **IOC (Indicator of Compromise)** | Observable artifact (file hash, IP, registry key) confirming malicious activity. |
| **API Call Trace** | A sequential log of system API invocations made by the program. |
| **Process Hollowing** | A stealth technique where a legitimate process is replaced by malicious code. |
| **Persistence Mechanism** | Method by which malware survives reboot (registry run keys, cron jobs, systemd services). |
| **C2 (Command & Control)** | Remote server the malware communicates with for instructions. |
| **MITRE ATT&CK** | Industry-standard framework cataloguing adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs). |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard industry execution time for a sandbox detonation ranges from **60 seconds to 5 minutes**, with **120 seconds** being the most commonly accepted baseline for capturing meaningful behavioral data without false positives.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Phases of Dynamic Analysis (Operational Breakdown)

Dynamic Analysis is performed in **five sequential phases**. Each phase builds upon the data harvested in the previous one.

### Phase 1 — Environment Preparation
- Deploy a **clean snapshot** of Windows (e.g., Windows 10/11) or Linux (Ubuntu, REMnux).
- Install **monitoring tools** BEFORE detonation.
- Take a **baseline snapshot** (disk + memory) for delta comparison.
- Disable host network access — route all traffic through an **isolated virtual network**.

### Phase 2 — Instrumentation Setup
- Attach toolchains:
  - **Windows:** Process Monitor (ProcMon), Process Explorer, Wireshark, Regshot, API Monitor, Sysmon.
  - **Linux:** `strace`, `ltrace`, `tcpdump`, `inotifywait`, `auditd`, `Volatility` for memory.
- Configure logging verbosity to **maximum detail**.
- Start packet capture on the virtual NIC.

### Phase 3 — Sample Detonation (Execution)
- Transfer the sample into the sandbox using a **shared folder** or **drag-and-drop** (never via email/USB for chain-of-custody integrity).
- Execute the binary with appropriate arguments (sometimes requires command-line flags to trigger malicious logic).
- Allow the program to "sleep" past anti-VM checks if necessary.

### Phase 4 — Behavior Observation & Logging
- Monitor in real-time:
  - **Process tree** (parent → child relationships).
  - **File system mutations** (creates, writes, deletes).
  - **Registry mutations** (Windows only — `HKLM`, `HKCU`).
  - **Network connections** (DNS queries, HTTP requests, raw sockets).
  - **API call sequences** (`CreateFile`, `RegSetValueEx`, `InternetOpenUrlA`).

### Phase 5 — IOC Extraction & Reporting
- Diff pre- and post-execution snapshots (Regshot, `find` with timestamps).
- Extract hashes (MD5, SHA-256) of dropped files.
- Map observed behaviors to **MITRE ATT&CK techniques**.
- Generate a forensic report with timeline of events.

## 2.2 Why Dynamic Analysis Matters (The "Why")

| Static Limitation | Dynamic Solution |
|---|---|
| Packed/obfuscated binaries hide code | Execution unpacks code in memory |
| Anti-disassembly tricks | Runtime reveals true logic |
| Encrypted C2 strings | Decrypted at runtime |
| Conditional malicious code | Triggered by specific environment |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| File Hash (Integrity) | $H = \text{Hash}(\text{file contents})$ | MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256 |
| Network Latency Observation | $t_{\text{beacon}} = t_{n+1} - t_n$ | seconds (C2 beacon interval) |
| Entropy of Packed Section | $E(s) = - \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \log_{2} p_i$ | bits/byte; $E > 7.0$ indicates packing |
| CPU Usage Spike Detection | $\Delta \text{CPU} = \text{CPU}_{\text{peak}} - \text{CPU}_{\text{baseline}}$ | percent |
| YARA Rule Match Score | $S = \dfrac{\vert \text{matched strings} \vert}{\vert \text{total strings} \vert} \times 100$ | percent |
| Process Injection Ratio | $R_{\text{inj}} = \dfrac{\text{anomalous RWX regions}}{\text{total memory regions}}$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **entropy equation** $E(s)$ is critical: legitimate code typically has $E \approx 5.5$ to $6.5$, while encrypted/packed payloads exhibit $E > 7.2$. This is a high-yield KTU formula.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Dynamic Analysis is the **backbone of incident response** and **threat intelligence** at enterprises. Security Operations Centers (SOCs) use automated sandboxing (e.g., **Cuckoo Sandbox, Joe Sandbox, ANY.RUN, Hybrid Analysis**) to triage thousands of suspicious files daily. The IOCs harvested during dynamic analysis are pushed into SIEM systems (Splunk, QRadar) and EDR platforms (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne) to detect future attacks across the enterprise.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Entropy Calculation for Packed Code

The **Shannon Entropy** formula is used to detect whether a code section is packed or encrypted. Below is the complete step-by-step derivation.

### Step 1 — Define the Discrete Probability Space

Consider a byte sequence $s = (b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_N)$ of length $N$, where each byte $b_i \in \{0, 1, \ldots, 255\}$.

The probability of byte value $v$ appearing in the sequence is:

$$
p_v = \frac{\text{count}(v)}{N}
$$

### Step 2 — Construct the Information Content Function

Claude Shannon defined the **information content** (or surprise) of an event with probability $p$ as:

$$
I(p) = \log_{2}\left(\frac{1}{p}\right) = -\log_{2}(p)
$$

This is the number of bits required to encode the event. Rare events carry more information than common ones.

### Step 3 — Derive the Expected (Average) Entropy

The expected value of information across all possible byte values is the **entropy**:

$$
E(s) = \sum_{v=0}^{255} p_v \cdot I(p_v)
$$

Substituting the expression from Step 2:

$$
E(s) = \sum_{v=0}^{255} p_v \cdot (-\log_{2} p_v)
$$

This simplifies to the canonical Shannon entropy formula:

$$
E(s) = - \sum_{v=0}^{255} p_v \log_{2} p_v
$$

### Step 4 — Apply to a Practical Forensic Example

Suppose we analyze a 1024-byte section of a Windows executable (`mimikatz.exe`) and observe the byte frequency distribution as follows:

| Byte Value Class | Frequency | Probability |
|---|---|---|
| 0x00 | 8 | 0.0078 |
| 0x4D (M) | 16 | 0.0156 |
| 0x5A (Z) | 14 | 0.0137 |
| 0x90 (NOP) | 200 | 0.1953 |
| 0xCC (INT3) | 50 | 0.0488 |
| ... (other) | 736 | 0.7188 |

The entropy is computed as:

$$
E(s) = - \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i \log_{2} p_i
$$

Plugging in numerical values:

$$
E(s) = - \bigl[ 0.0078 \log_{2}(0.0078) + 0.0156 \log_{2}(0.0156) + \ldots + 0.7188 \log_{2}(0.7188) \bigr]
$$

### Step 5 — Interpret the Result

$$
E(s) \approx 6.42 \text{ bits/byte}
$$

Since $E(s) < 7.0$, this region is **NOT packed**. If, however, the analysis yields:

$$
E(s) = 7.92 \text{ bits/byte}
$$

then the region is **strongly indicative of packing or encryption**, warranting deeper dynamic analysis to unpack the binary at runtime.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Dynamic vs. Static vs. Hybrid Analysis

| Parameter | Static Analysis | Dynamic Analysis | Hybrid Analysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| Code Execution | **Not required** | **Required** | Required (selectively) |
| Anti-VM Evasion | Cannot bypass | Can bypass via patching | Bypasses via emulation |
| Speed | Fast (seconds) | Slow (minutes per sample) | Medium |
| Stealth Code Coverage | Low | High | Very High |
| Sample Integrity | Preserved | May be destroyed/modified | Preserved |
| Tool Examples | IDA Pro, Ghidra, PEiD | Cuckoo, ProcMon, Wireshark | Hybrid sandbox + Ghidra |
| False Negative Risk | High | Medium | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: End-to-End Dynamic Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acquire Suspicious Sample] --> B[Hash Sample MD5 SHA256]
    B --> C[Deploy Clean VM Snapshot]
    C --> D[Install Monitoring Tools]
    D --> E[Take Baseline Snapshot]
    E --> F[Start Network Capture]
    F --> G[Transfer Sample to Sandbox]
    G --> H[Execute Sample Detonation]
    H --> I{Anti-VM Check Detected}
    I -->|Yes| J[Patch Environment or Use HIDE]
    I -->|No| K[Observe Process Behavior]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Log Process Tree]
    K --> M[Log File System Changes]
    K --> N[Log Registry Changes]
    K --> O[Log Network Traffic]
    L --> P[Diff Pre Post Snapshots]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Extract IOCs Hashes IPs]
    Q --> R[Map to MITRE ATT&CK]
    R --> S[Generate Forensic Report]
    S --> T[Restore VM Snapshot]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Windows Process Monitoring Architecture (ProcMon)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOST["HOST OS - SANDBOX"]
        PROC[Suspicious Process]
        PROC --> FS[File System Activity]
        PROC --> REG[Registry Activity]
        PROC --> NET[Network Activity]
        PROC --> MEM[Memory Allocations]
    end
    subgraph PROCMON["PROCMON KERNEL DRIVER"]
        F1[File System Filter]
        F2[Registry Filter]
        F3[Network Filter]
        F4[Process Filter]
    end
    FS --> F1
    REG --> F2
    NET --> F3
    PROC --> F4
    F1 --> LOG[Central Event Log]
    F2 --> LOG
    F3 --> LOG
    F4 --> LOG
    LOG --> UI[ProcMon GUI Display]
    LOG --> EXP[Export to CSV PML]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Linux Dynamic Analysis Tool Stack (strace + auditd)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SMPL[Linux ELF Binary Sample] --> EXEC[Execute in Isolated Container]
    EXEC --> STRACE[strace System Call Trace]
    EXEC --> LTRACE[ltrace Library Call Trace]
    EXEC --> AUDITD[auditd Kernel Audit Daemon]
    EXEC --> INOTIFY[inotifywait File Watcher]
    EXEC --> TCPDUMP[tcpdump Packet Capture]
    STRACE --> AGG[Aggregator Script]
    LTRACE --> AGG
    AUDITD --> AGG
    INOTIFY --> AGG
    TCPDUMP --> AGG
    AGG --> DB[(SQLite IOC Database)]
    DB --> RPT[Forensic Report Generator]
    RPT --> OUT[PDF HTML Output]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: MITRE ATT&CK Technique Mapping Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OBS[Observed Behavior] --> TA1[TA0002 Execution]
    OBS --> TA2[TA0003 Persistence]
    OBS --> TA3[TA0005 Defense Evasion]
    OBS --> TA4[TA0011 Command and Control]
    TA1 --> T1[T1059 Command Interpreter]
    TA2 --> T2[T1547 Boot Autostart]
    TA3 --> T3[T1027 Obfuscated Files]
    TA4 --> T4[T1071 Application Layer Protocol]
    T1 --> MAT[MITRE ATT&CK Navigator]
    T2 --> MAT
    T3 --> MAT
    T4 --> MAT
    MAT --> THREAT[Threat Intelligence Feed]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Dynamic Analysis. List any four tools used for Dynamic Analysis in Windows Forensics.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Dynamic Analysis** is a behavioral malware examination technique that involves executing a suspicious binary inside an isolated, instrumented environment (such as a sandbox or virtual machine) to observe its runtime interactions with the operating system, file system, registry, memory, and network — without exposing the host to compromise.

**Four Tools for Windows Dynamic Analysis:**

1. **Process Monitor (ProcMon)** — Captures real-time file system, registry, and process/thread activity using kernel-level filter drivers.
2. **Wireshark** — Captures and analyzes live network packets generated by the sample.
3. **Regshot** — Takes before-and-after registry snapshots to diff modifications.
4. **API Monitor** — Hooks and logs Windows API calls made by the running process.

> [!NOTE]
> **1 mark** for definition, **2 marks** for tools (0.5 each).

---

### Question 2: What is an Indicator of Compromise (IOC)? Give two examples.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

An **Indicator of Compromise (IOC)** is a forensic artifact or observable evidence — such as a file hash, IP address, domain, registry key, or process behavior — that indicates a system has been breached or is currently infected with malicious software.

**Two Examples:**

1. **File Hash:** SHA-256 of a dropped malicious DLL: `a4f5c3d9...b8e2f1`.
2. **Registry Persistence Key:** `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\Updater` pointing to a suspicious executable.

**Other examples:** C2 IP address, mutex name, abnormal DNS query, unusual outbound traffic on port 443.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the five phases of Dynamic Malware Analysis with a neat workflow diagram.**

**Model Solution:**

The five phases of Dynamic Malware Analysis are:

**Phase 1 — Environment Preparation:**
A clean virtual machine (VMware/VirtualBox) running Windows 10 or Linux Ubuntu is deployed. All monitoring tools are installed. A **baseline snapshot** is captured to enable rollback and delta-comparison.

**Phase 2 — Instrumentation Setup:**
Toolchains are configured:
- Windows: ProcMon, Process Explorer, Wireshark, Regshot, API Monitor, Sysmon.
- Linux: `strace`, `ltrace`, `tcpdump`, `inotifywait`, `auditd`.
Network is isolated — all traffic routed through an internal virtual NIC.

**Phase 3 — Sample Detonation:**
The suspicious binary is transferred (via shared folder) into the sandbox and executed. If the binary has anti-VM checks, patches are applied or sandbox-evasion bypass tools are used (e.g., `pafish` patches).

**Phase 4 — Behavior Observation:**
Real-time monitoring captures:
- Process tree (parent → child process relationships via `wmic` or `ps`).
- File system mutations (creates, writes, deletes — timestamped).
- Registry changes (Windows).
- Network connections (DNS, HTTP, raw sockets via Wireshark).
- API call sequences (via API Monitor or `strace`).

**Phase 5 — IOC Extraction & Reporting:**
Pre- and post-execution states are diffed (Regshot, `find -newer`). Hashes of dropped files (MD5, SHA-256) are computed. Behaviors are mapped to **MITRE ATT&CK** techniques. A forensic report is generated.

**Diagram:** Refer to the workflow in Section 4.1.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention the **baseline snapshot** step. Examiners specifically check for this — it carries **1 mark** as it enables delta-comparison (a core forensic principle).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain how Process Monitor (ProcMon) works at the kernel level to capture file system, registry, and process events.**

**Model Solution:**

**ProcMon Architecture:**

ProcMon operates using a **kernel-mode filter driver** that intercepts I/O Request Packets (IRPs) before they reach the underlying drivers. It works as follows:

1. **Driver Loading:** When ProcMon starts, it loads a kernel driver (`PROCMON24.SYS`) that registers filter callbacks with the Windows I/O Manager.
2. **Hook Installation:** The driver attaches to:
   - **File System Filter** — intercepts `IRP_MJ_CREATE`, `IRP_MJ_WRITE`, `IRP_MJ_DELETE` calls.
   - **Registry Filter** — registers `CmRegisterCallback` to intercept registry operations.
   - **Process/Thread Filter** — hooks `PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine` for process creation/termination events.
   - **Network Filter** — uses `WFP` (Windows Filtering Platform) callouts.
3. **Event Capture:** Each intercepted event is timestamped (with 100-nanosecond precision using `QueryPerformanceCounter`) and forwarded to user-mode.
4. **Buffering:** Events are buffered in a shared memory ring buffer to minimize context-switch overhead.
5. **User-Mode Display:** The GUI subscribes to the buffer and displays events in real-time, with filtering by process, operation, or path.
6. **Export:** Events can be exported as CSV or PML (ProcMon Log) for offline analysis.

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 (ProcMon Kernel Architecture).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often describe ProcMon as a "user-space tool." The correct answer must emphasize the **kernel-mode filter driver** — this is the technical core examiners test.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the role of Sandboxing in Dynamic Analysis. Compare manual vs. automated sandboxing.**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Sandboxing:**
A sandbox is an **isolated execution environment** (typically a virtual machine or container) that allows malicious code to run safely without compromising the host system or network. It provides:
- **Containment** — prevents malware from spreading to production systems.
- **Reversibility** — VM snapshots allow rollback to a clean state after each analysis.
- **Instrumentation** — hooks and sensors capture behavioral data.
- **Network Isolation** — prevents real C2 communication and data exfiltration.

**Comparison: Manual vs. Automated Sandboxing**

| Parameter | Manual Sandbox | Automated Sandbox |
|---|---|---|
| **Setup Time** | 30–60 minutes per VM | Minutes per sample (pre-built) |
| **Tooling** | Manual configuration of ProcMon, Wireshark, etc. | Pre-configured instrumentation |
| **Sample Throughput** | 5–10 samples/day | 1000+ samples/day |
| **Anti-VM Bypass** | Human creativity (patching, timing) | Heuristic-based evasion detection |
| **Cost** | Lower initial cost, high labor | Higher license cost (Joe Sandbox, etc.) |
| **Examples** | Custom VirtualBox + REMnux | **Cuckoo Sandbox, Joe Sandbox, ANY.RUN, Hybrid Analysis** |
| **Best For** | Deep-dive APT analysis | High-volume triage |

**Cuckoo Sandbox Architecture (Brief):**
- **Host:** Runs the Cuckoo daemon and manages VMs.
- **Guest:** Isolated Windows/Linux VM with `cuckoo_agent.py`.
- **Analysis:** Submit sample → Cuckoo executes in guest → returns behavioral report (JSON/PDF).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain how Wireshark and `tcpdump` are used to capture and analyze network behavior of a malware sample during dynamic analysis.**

**Model Solution:**

**Wireshark (Windows/Linux GUI):**
Wireshark is a graphical packet analyzer used during dynamic analysis to capture and inspect live network traffic generated by a malware sample.

**Usage Steps:**
1. **Select Interface:** Choose the virtual NIC of the sandbox VM.
2. **Start Capture:** Begin packet capture BEFORE detonation to ensure no traffic is missed.
3. **Apply Display Filters:** Use filters like `http.request`, `dns.qry.name`, `tcp.port == 443`, `ip.addr == 172.16.x.x`.
4. **Analyze Protocols:** Inspect HTTP requests (URLs, User-Agents), DNS queries (C2 domains), TLS handshakes (SNI field reveals C2 domains even in HTTPS).
5. **Export Objects:** Extract downloaded files via `File → Export Objects → HTTP`.

**`tcpdump` (Linux CLI):**
`tcpdump` is the command-line equivalent used primarily in Linux sandboxes.

**Usage Examples:**

```bash
# Capture all traffic on interface eth0 to file
tcpdump -i eth0 -w capture.pcap

# Capture only DNS queries
tcpdump -i eth0 port 53 -w dns_capture.pcap

# Read captured file with verbose output
tcpdump -r capture.pcap -nn -vv
```

**Forensic Indicators Extracted from Network Capture:**
