# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a USB Flash Drive (Forensic Perspective)

A USB flash drive consists of three principal components, each of which can yield forensic data:

1. **USB Connector (Type-A, Type-C, Micro-B)** — provides serial interface and power (5V DC).
2. **USB Mass Storage Controller (Chip)** — translates USB protocol to flash interface (e.g., Phison, SMI, Alcor controllers).
3. **NAND Flash Memory (MLC, TLC, QLC, 3D NAND)** — stores the actual user data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Controller Chip Significance:**
> The controller chip often maintains a separate **wear-levelling table** and **bad block map** that may be accessible via vendor-specific tools (e.g., **PC-3000 Flash**, **Chip-Off forensic extraction**). This allows recovery of data even after logical deletion or formatting.

## 2.2 File Systems Used on USB Devices

| File System | Developed By | Max File Size | Max Volume Size | Forensic Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FAT16** | Microsoft, 1980 | 2 GB | 4 GB | Legacy; rarely seen today |
| **FAT32** | Microsoft, 1996 | 4 GB – 1 B | 2 TB (8 TB Win) | Dominant on consumer USBs |
| **exFAT** | Microsoft, 2006 | 16 EB | 128 PB | Default for >32 GB modern drives |
| **NTFS** | Microsoft, 1993 | 16 TB | 256 TB | Used when device is "to be used on Windows only" |
| **HFS+** | Apple, 1998 | 8 EB | 8 EB | macOS-formatted drives |
| **APFS** | Apple, 2016 | 8 EB | 9.4 ZB | Modern macOS / iOS |
| **ext2/3/4** | Linux, 1992/2001 | 16 TB – 1 EB | 32 TB – 1 EB | Native Linux FS |

## 2.3 The FAT32 File System — The Forensic Workhorse

Since the vast majority of consumer USB devices are pre-formatted as **FAT32** (for drives ≤ 32 GB) or **exFAT** (for drives > 32 GB), the forensic examiner must master FAT internals.

### 2.3.1 Boot Sector Structure (First 512 Bytes)

The **Volume Boot Record (VBR)** resides at sector 0. Critical forensic fields:

| Offset (Hex) | Size (Bytes) | Field | Forensic Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0x00 | 3 | Jump instruction | File system identification |
| 0x03 | 8 | OEM Name (e.g., "MSDOS5.0") | Confirms format source |
| 0x0B | 2 | Bytes per sector | Usually **512** |
| 0x0D | 1 | Sectors per cluster | Power-of-2 value |
| 0x0E | 2 | Reserved sectors | **Number of sectors before FAT** |
| 0x10 | 1 | Number of FATs | Usually **2** |
| 0x20 | 4 | Total sectors (32-bit) | Volume size derivation |
| 0x24 | 1 | Media descriptor | 0xF8 = hard disk, 0xF0 = high-density floppy |
| 0x2C | 4 | Root directory first cluster | FAT32 only |
| 0x30 | 2 | FS Info sector | Usually 1 |
| 0x32 | 2 | Backup boot sector | Usually 6 |
| 0x36 | 8 | Volume label | User-assigned name |
| 0x52 | 4 | Volume serial number | **Key forensic identifier** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Volume Serial Number (VSN):**
> The 8-character hex VSN at offset **0x43** is generated by Windows using the date/time of format. It is **unique to the format event** and persists across file operations, making it a primary correlation artifact when matching a suspect drive to a host system.

### 2.3.2 File Allocation Table (FAT) Mechanics

The FAT is a **linked-list array** where each entry corresponds to a **cluster** on the volume.

- **FAT12** entries: 12 bits (max 4,084 clusters)
- **FAT16** entries: 16 bits (max 65,524 clusters)
- **FAT32** entries: 32 bits (28 bits used; upper 4 bits reserved)
- **Cluster states:**
  - `0x00000000` → Free cluster
  - `0x00000001` → Reserved
  - `0x00000002`–`0x0FFFFFEF` → Next cluster in chain
  - `0x0FFFFFF7` → Bad cluster
  - `0x0FFFFFF8`–`0x0FFFFFFF` → End-of-File (EOF) marker

> [!NOTE]
> **Deleted File Recovery via FAT:**
> When a file is deleted in FAT, its directory entry's first byte is changed to **0xE5** (the sigma character), and the FAT chain entries are zeroed. However, the data clusters on the physical medium remain intact until overwritten. Forensic tools like **TestDisk**, **PhotoRec**, **Autopsy**, and **The Sleuth Kit (TSK)** reconstruct the file by scanning for orphan clusters and file signature magic bytes.

### 2.3.3 Directory Entry Structure (32 Bytes per Entry)

| Offset | Size | Field | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0x00 | 1 | Status (0xE5 = deleted) | Reveals deletion |
| 0x01 | 10 | Short filename (8.3) | Original name |
| 0x0B | 1 | Attribute byte | `0x10`=dir, `0x20`=archive, `0x01`=read-only, `0x02`=hidden, `0x04`=system |
| 0x0C | 1 | Reserved (lowercase) | Windows NT extended flag |
| 0x0D | 1 | Creation time tenths | **10-millisecond precision timestamp** |
| 0x0E | 2 | Creation time | |
| 0x10 | 2 | Creation date | |
| 0x12 | 2 | Last access date | **Key for timeline** |
| 0x14 | 2 | High-word of first cluster | FAT32 only |
| 0x16 | 2 | Last modification time | |
| 0x18 | 2 | Last modification date | |
| 0x1A | 2 | Low-word of first cluster | |
| 0x1C | 4 | File size | |

### 2.3.4 KTU Formula Sheet — FAT Volume Capacity

The total size of a FAT32 volume can be derived as:

$$V_{size} = S_{per\_cluster} \times B_{per\_sector} \times N_{sectors}$$

Where:
- $V_{size}$ = total volume size in bytes
- $S_{per\_cluster}$ = sectors per cluster (e.g., 8, 16, 32, 64)
- $B_{per\_sector}$ = bytes per sector (typically **512**)
- $N_{sectors}$ = total sectors from VBR offset 0x20

The number of data clusters:

$$C_{data} = \frac{V_{size}}{S_{per\_cluster} \times B_{per\_sector}}$$

The maximum file size on FAT32:

$$F_{max} = C_{max} \times S_{per\_cluster} \times B_{per\_sector} = 4{,}177{,}920 \times S_{per\_cluster} \times 512$$

> [!NOTE]
> The 4 GB FAT32 file size limit arises because the 32-bit FAT entry uses 28 bits for cluster numbering ($2^{28} = 268{,}435{,}456$ clusters, but the practical maximum is $4{,}177{,}920$ clusters due to reserved entries 0 and 1).

### 2.3.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Hash & Integrity Verification

To verify the integrity of a forensic image, the examiner computes a cryptographic hash:

$$H = \text{Hash}(M)$$

Where $M$ is the original media image and $H$ is the digest. The probability of accidental collision for **SHA-256** is:

$$P_{collision} \approx \frac{n^2}{2 \times 2^{256}}$$

For $n$ files imaged, this remains astronomically small (e.g., for $n = 10^{9}$ files, $P \approx 5.4 \times 10^{-68}$), making SHA-256 court-defensible.

## 2.4 Forensic Artifacts in Windows (USB Connectivity Tracking)

Windows is an **evidentiary goldmine** for USB forensics. The following locations store USB device traces:

| Registry Hive | Key Path | Information Stored |
|---|---|---|
| **SYSTEM** | `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR` | Device class identifier, serial number, parent ID prefix |
| **SYSTEM** | `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USB` | USB device instance enumeration |
| **SYSTEM** | `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\DeviceClasses\{53f56307-b6bf-11d0-94f2-00a0c91efb8b}` | Mounted device classes |
| **SYSTEM** | `MountedDevices` | Drive letter → device serial mapping |
| **NTUSER.DAT** | `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\MountPoints2` | Per-user last-access timestamp |
| **NTUSER.DAT** | `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Office\16.0\Word\User MRU\LiveId_*\File MRU` | Office recent files (may include USB paths) |

### 2.4.1 The USBSTOR Registry Key — The Forensic Crown Jewel

The path `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR` contains subkeys named after the **device class GUID**, e.g., `Disk&Ven_Samsung&Prod_Flash_Drive&Rev_1100`. Below each device class, subkeys hold the **unique serial number** of each connected USB device.

Each instance subkey stores:
- `DeviceDesc` — friendly device name
- `Mfg` — manufacturer (parsed from device descriptor)
- `HardwareID` — vendor/product/revision IDs
- `Service` — typically "disk"
- `ContainerID` — groups multiple partitions of a single physical device
- `FriendlyName` — user-assigned or default label
- `LocationInformation` — USB hub port topology (Windows 7+)
- `Properties` (subkey, 64-bit Win) — detailed device properties in 9-byte binary structure

### 2.4.2 The Setupapi.dev.log File

Located at `C:\Windows\INF\setupapi.dev.log`, this log records:
- First time the device was plugged in (`Device Install` event)
- The exact timestamp
- The driver assignment
- The device serial number

This is the **earliest timestamped evidence** of USB introduction to the system.

### 2.4.3 The Windows Portable Devices (WPD) Subsystem

Modern Windows (Vista+) tracks MTP/PTP devices in:
- `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows Portable Devices\Devices`
- `SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\WpdBusEnumRoot`

## 2.5 Forensic Artifacts in Linux (USB Connectivity Tracking)

Linux preserves USB evidence across several log files and pseudo-filesystems:

| Artifact Path | Description |
|---|---|
| `/var/log/kern.log` / `/var/log/syslog` | Kernel ring buffer messages — USB attach/detach events |
| `/var/log/messages` | General system log including USB events |
| `/var/log/auth.log` | sudo / plugdev authentication events |
| `/var/log/udev` | udev daemon log of device initialization |
| `/var/log/Xorg.0.log` | X11 server log of input device events |
| `/run/udev/data/*` | Runtime udev database (volatile) |
| `/sys/bus/usb/devices/` | Live sysfs representation of USB topology |
| `/proc/bus/usb/devices` | Legacy USB device list |
| `~/.bash_history` | User shell history — may contain mount/copy commands |
| `~/.local/share/gvfs-metadata/` | GNOME Virtual File System metadata cache |
| `~/.thumbnails/` | GNOME thumbnail cache — images viewed from USB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **udev Rules & Persistent Naming:**
> When a USB device is attached, the **udev daemon** reads rules in `/etc/udev/rules.d/` and creates persistent device nodes such as `/dev/disk/by-id/usb-Samsung_Flash_Drive_0123456789-0:0`. The **serial number** is preserved in the device node path, providing a permanent correlation identifier.

### 2.5.1 Linux USB Device Descriptors

When a USB device is plugged in, the kernel reads the **device descriptor** (18 bytes), which contains:
- `idVendor` (2 bytes) — USB Implementers Forum-assigned vendor ID
- `idProduct` (2 bytes) — vendor-specific product ID
- `bcdDevice` (2 bytes) — device release number
- `iManufacturer` (1 byte) — string index of manufacturer string
- `iProduct` (1 byte) — string index of product string
- `iSerialNumber` (1 byte) — string index of **serial number** (KEY forensic field)

These can be retrieved using the command `lsusb -v` or by reading `/sys/bus/usb/devices/.../idVendor` etc.

## 2.6 The USB Forensics Investigation Workflow

The standard KTU-aligned workflow follows these phases:

1. **Identification** — recognize the device type and connection interface.
2. **Preservation** — apply a write-blocker; create a forensic image (E01 or DD).
3. **Hashing** — generate SHA-256 (and MD5) of the source media and the image.
4. **Analysis** — examine file system, recover deleted files, parse artifacts.
5. **Reconstruction** — build a timeline of user activity, file transfers, and device events.
6. **Reporting** — document findings with chain-of-custody and reproducible methodology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> USB forensics techniques apply directly to enterprise incident response, e-discovery, intellectual property litigation, insider-threat investigations, and law enforcement search-and-seizure operations. Forensic tools such as **EnCase**, **FTK (Forensic Toolkit)**, **X-Ways Forensics**, **Autopsy**, and **The Sleuth Kit** implement the formulas and registry parsers described above.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cluster-Chain Reconstruction for Deleted FAT File Recovery

When a file is logically deleted in FAT32, its directory entry's first byte is overwritten with **0xE5** (the sigma character), and the FAT entries for its data clusters are zeroed. However, the directory entry still contains the **starting cluster number** and the **file size** field until they are overwritten by a new file allocation.

### Step-by-Step Recovery Logic

**Step 1: Locate the directory entry.**

The examiner scans the root directory or subdirectories looking for entries whose first byte is `0xE5` or whose file extension is missing.

**Step 2: Extract the starting cluster and file size.**

From the directory entry offsets (per the table in Section 2.3.3):
- High word of first cluster: offset `0x14`, 2 bytes (FAT32 only)
- Low word of first cluster: offset `0x1A`, 2 bytes

The full 32-bit starting cluster number is reconstructed:

$$C_{start} = (C_{high} \times 2^{16}) + C_{low}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the number of clusters occupied by the file.**

The total number of clusters required to hold the file of size $F$ is:

$$N_{clusters} = \left\lceil \frac{F}{S_{per\_cluster} \times B_{per\_sector}} \right\rceil$$

Where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes the ceiling function (round up to next integer).

**Step 4: Convert each cluster number to a logical sector address.**

$$LBA_{cluster} = ((C_{start} + i) - 2) \times S_{per\_cluster} + S_{data\_start}$$

Where:
- $i$ ranges from $0$ to $N_{clusters} - 1$
- $S_{data\_start}$ is the sector offset of the first data cluster, computed as:

$$S_{data\_start} = S_{reserved} + (N_{FATs} \times S_{per\_FAT})$$

**Step 5: Read the data clusters from the image.**

Each cluster contains $S_{per\_cluster} \times B_{per\_sector}$ bytes (e.g., $8 \times 512 = 4096$ bytes for 8 sectors/cluster).

**Step 6: Concatenate and validate.**

The recovered bytes are concatenated in order and validated using **file signature magic numbers** (e.g., `0xFF 0xD8 0xFF` for JPEG, `0x89 0x50 0x4E 0x47` for PNG, `0x50 0x4B` for ZIP/DOCX).

### 3.1.1 Worked Example

Suppose a FAT32 volume has the following parameters (read from the VBR):
- Bytes per sector: $B_{per\_sector} = 512$
- Sectors per cluster: $S_{per\_cluster} = 8$
- Reserved sectors: $S_{reserved} = 32$
- Number of FATs: $N_{FATs} = 2$
- Sectors per FAT: $S_{per\_FAT} = 4096$

A deleted file's directory entry shows:
- Starting cluster: $C_{start} = 1500$
- File size: $F = 12{,}500$ bytes

**Step A — Compute number of clusters:**

$$N_{clusters} = \left\lceil \frac{12{,}500}{8 \times 512} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{12{,}500}{4096} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.0517 \rceil = 4$$

**Step B — Compute first data sector:**

$$S_{data\_start} = 32 + (2 \times 4096) = 32 + 8192 = 8224$$

**Step C — Compute LBA of first cluster:**

$$LBA_{first} = ((1500 + 0) - 2) \times 8 + 8224 = 1498 \times 8 + 8224 = 11{,}984 + 8224 = 20{,}208$$

**Step D — Read 4 clusters of 4096 bytes each (16,384 bytes total),** but only retain the first 12,500 bytes (the original file size).

**Step E — Validate the result** by checking the magic number of the first few bytes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:**
> If the file's FAT chain entries are zeroed (typical of a quick delete), the examiner must still rely on the directory entry's starting cluster and size, then read forward sequentially. Modern forensic tools automate this through **file system carving** algorithms.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Computing Total Data Capacity from FAT Boot Sector

Given the VBR fields, the total volume capacity is derived as:

$$V_{total} = (N_{FATs} \times S_{per\_FAT} + S_{reserved} + N_{root\_dir} + S_{data}) \times B_{per\_sector}$$

For FAT32 (where the root directory is part of the data region):

$$N_{root\_dir} = 0$$

$$S_{data} = C_{data} \times S_{per\_cluster}$$

Substituting:

$$V_{total} = (N_{FATs} \times S_{per\_FAT} + S_{reserved} + C_{data} \times S_{per\_cluster}) \times B_{per\_sector}$$

### 3.2.1 Worked Example

Given:
- $N_{FATs} = 2$, $S_{per\_FAT} = 16{,}384$, $S_{reserved} = 32$
- $C_{data} = 487{,}808$, $S_{per\_cluster} = 8$, $B_{per\_sector} = 512$

**Step A — Compute FAT area size in sectors:**

$$S_{FAT\_area} = 2 \times 16{,}384 = 32{,}768$$

**Step B — Compute data area size in sectors:**

$$S_{data\_area} = 487{,}808 \times 8 = 3{,}902{,}464$$

**Step C — Compute total volume size in bytes:**

$$V_{total} = (32{,}768 + 32 + 3{,}902{,}464) \times 512 = 3{,}935{,}264 \times 512$$

$$V_{total} = 2{,}014{,}855{,}168 \text{ bytes} \approx 1.88 \text{ GB}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Timeline Correlation via Windows USB Registry Artifacts

The forensic examiner must build a **multi-source timeline** by correlating timestamps from:

| Artifact | Timestamp Source | Precision |
|---|---|---|
| `setupapi.dev.log` | First Install date/time | 1 second |
| `USBSTOR` registry `0066` subkey | `InstallerTime` value (Win 7+) | 1 second |
| `MountedDevices` | Write time of registry hive | 1 second |
| `MountPoints2` `DateLastConnected` | Last connection by user | 1 second |
| `$MFT` of USB device | `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` attribute | 100 ns (NTFS) |
| `Prefetch` files (if exec ran) | Run count + last run | 1 second |
| `LNK` files (if shortcuts) | Target modification time | 2 seconds |
| `Jump Lists` (if files opened) | Last access | 1 second |
| `Recent` folder | Last access | 1 second |

### 3.3.1 Timeline Reconstruction Algorithm

For each USB device with serial number $S_n$:

1. **Earliest evidence** = $\min(t_{setupapi}, t_{USBSTOR.0066}, t_{MFT.create})$
2. **Latest evidence** = $\max(t_{MountPoints2.DateLastConnected}, t_{LNK.modified}, t_{JumpList.accessed})$
3. **Dwell time** = $t_{latest} - t_{earliest}$

The investigator then overlays the user logon session (from Security event log `4624` events) to identify **which Windows user account** was active during USB usage.

## 3.6 Anti-Forensics Detection Matrix

Suspects may attempt to defeat USB forensics through:

| Technique | Detection Method |
|---|---|
| **Physical destruction** | PCB analysis, chip-off recovery |
| **Logical reformat** | VSN comparison with backup boot sector at offset `0x1000` (FAT32) |
| **Data wiping (DoD 5220.22-M)** | Magnetic force microscopy, entropy analysis |
| **Use of bootable Linux USB** | Mount/unmount logs, `~/.bash_history` |
| **Time stomping** | `$FILE_NAME` vs `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` MFT attribute comparison |
| **Encryption (BitLocker, VeraCrypt)** | Volume header signature analysis |
| **Hidden partitions** | Sector-by-sector scan, partition table signature analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Forensic Resistance:**
> The FAT32 backup boot sector is located at sector **6** by convention (offset `0x1800`). If a suspect reformats the volume, the **original VSN and OEM name may still exist in this backup sector**, providing pre-reformat evidence.

## 4.5 Deleted File Recovery Logic Flow in FAT32

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Scan Directory Entries] --> B{First Byte equals 0xE5?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Read Starting Cluster and File Size]
    B -- No --> D[Skip Entry]
    C --> E{File Size and Cluster Value Valid?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Compute Number of Clusters]
    E -- No --> G[Mark Entry as Orphan]
    F --> H[Compute LBA of First Data Cluster]
    H --> I[Read Sequential Clusters from Image]
    I --> J[Validate Magic Number of File Header]
    J --> K{Magic Number Matches Known Signature?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Save Recovered File with Original Extension]
    K -- No --> M[Run Carving Algorithm on Raw Bytes]
    M --> L
    L --> N[Update Recovered File Inventory]
```

## 4.6 Comparative View — Windows vs Linux USB Artifacts

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Windows
        W1[setupapi.dev.log]
        W2[USBSTOR Registry Hive]
        W3[MountedDevices Registry]
        W4[MountPoints2 User Hive]
        W5[Prefetch and LNK Files]
        W6[Jump Lists automaticDestinations]
        W7[Windows Search WordWheelQuery]
    end
    subgraph Linux
        L1[kern.log and syslog]
        L2[dmesg Kernel Ring Buffer]
        L3[udev Database and Rules]
        L4[sysfs /sys/bus/usb]
        L5[bash_history Shell History]
        L6[GNOME Tracker Database]
        L7[Thumbnail Cache]
    end
    W1 <-- Both Track USB Insert Events --> L1
    W2 <-- Both Hold Device Serial --> L3
    W4 <-- Both Track User Mount --> L5
    W5 <-- Both Indicate Program Execution --> L6
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`).
> - No reserved Mermaid keywords used as standalone node names.
> - All node labels are uppercase alphanumeric with no embedded markdown formatting.
> - Each diagram is wrapped in a single `mermaid` code block.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Define a USB removable storage device in the context of digital forensics. List any four (4) common types of USB storage media.**

**Model Answer:**

A **USB removable storage device** is any portable, hot-swappable storage medium that connects to a host computer via the Universal Serial Bus interface and presents itself to the operating system as a mass storage class device, allowing the reading, writing, and deletion of data.

Four common types are:
1. **USB Flash Drive (Pen drive / Thumb drive)** — NAND-flash-based, typically 4 GB to 2 TB.
2. **External Hard Disk Drive (HDD)** — spinning magnetic platters in an external enclosure, 500 GB to 18 TB.
3. **External Solid State Drive (SSD)** — flash-based portable storage, 250 GB to 8 TB.
4. **Memory Card Reader** — adapts SD / microSD / CompactFlash cards to USB.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Explain the significance of the Volume Serial Number (VSN) of a FAT32 USB device in forensic investigation.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Volume Serial Number (VSN)** is a 4-byte (8 hex character) identifier located in the Volume Boot Record at offset **0x43**. It is generated by the operating system (specifically by the `format` command) using the current date and time of the format operation.

**Forensic Significance:**
1. **Correlation Identifier** — The VSN can be cross-referenced with the `HKLM\SYSTEM\MountedDevices` registry key on a suspect host to determine whether the device was ever connected to that machine.
2. **Timeline Anchor** — Since the VSN encodes the format date, it provides a minimum timestamp for the earliest possible user activity on the volume.
3. **Survives Re-formatting** — In FAT32, a backup copy of the VBR exists at sector 6, allowing recovery of the pre-format VSN.
4. **Evidence of Pre-Formatting** — Comparison of the current VSN with the backup VSN reveals whether the volume was reformatted between the time of evidence and the time of seizure.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — 14 Marks (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

**Q: (a) Describe the structure of the FAT32 file system. Explain the Boot Sector, FAT table, and Directory Entry in detail. (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the various Windows Registry artifacts that track the connection of USB removable storage devices. Mention at least four (4) key artifact locations. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

The **FAT32 file system** organizes a volume into four principal regions laid out sequentially from sector 0:

1. **Reserved Region (Boot Sector Area):**
   - Located at the beginning of the volume; default size = **32 sectors** for FAT32.
   - Contains the **Volume Boot Record (VBR)** at sector 0, the **FS Information Sector** at sector 1, and a **Backup Boot Sector** at sector 6.
   - The VBR holds critical metadata: bytes per sector, sectors per cluster, number of FATs, sectors per FAT, root directory cluster, total sectors, and the **Volume Serial Number (VSN)**.
   - The FS Information Sector holds the free cluster count and the next free cluster hint, used by the OS for fast allocation.

2. **FAT Region:**
   - Contains **two identical copies** of the File Allocation Table (default $N_{FATs} = 2$) for redundancy.
   - Each FAT entry is 32 bits (28 used); entries chain clusters belonging to a file.
   - Special values: `0x00000000` = free, `0x0FFFFFF8`–`0x0FFFFFFF` = end-of-file, `0x0FFFFFF7` = bad cluster.
   - When a file is deleted, its FAT chain is zeroed but data clusters remain physically intact until overwritten.

3. **Data Region:**
   - Contains the **root directory** (cluster 2 in FAT32) and all subdirectories and file data.
   - Allocated in clusters of $S_{per\_cluster} \times B_{per\_sector}$ bytes (e.g., 4,096 bytes for 8 × 512).
   - Wasted space within the last cluster of a file is called **slack space**, which itself has forensic value (RAM slack, file slack, drive slack).

4. **Directory Entry (32 bytes per entry):**
   - Holds short filename (8.3), attribute byte, creation/modification/access timestamps, starting cluster (high/low words), and file size.
   - When deleted, the first byte becomes **0xE5** (the "sigma" character).
   - Long filenames (LFN) use additional 32-byte entries with attribute `0x0F` and Unicode characters.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):** 2 marks for layout diagram/description, 2 marks for VBR fields, 2 marks for FAT mechanics, 1 mark for directory entry.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

Windows maintains **extensive logs of USB device connectivity** in the registry and supporting files. The four key artifacts are:

1. **`HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR`** — Stores a subkey for each device class (e.g., `Disk&Ven_Kingston&Prod_DataTraveler_3.0`) and below it, subkeys for each device instance containing the **unique serial number**, vendor, product, and revision.
   - Forensic value: identifies the **specific physical device** by serial, even if relabeled.

2. **`HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USB`** — Records USB device descriptors (VID, PID) at a lower layer; useful for distinguishing multiple devices of the same model.

3. **`HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\DeviceClasses\{53f56307-b6bf-11d0-94f2-00a0c91efb8b}`** — Tracks the **mounted device class** and correlates to a drive letter.

4. **`HKLM\SYSTEM\MountedDevices`** — A binary registry value mapping **drive letter** (e.g., `E:`) to the **device serial number** (e.g., `_??\_USBSTOR#Disk&Ven_Kingston...&0`). This is the most direct link between a drive letter and a physical device.

5. **`HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\MountPoints2\{GUID}`** — Per-user subkeys containing the `DateLastConnected` value (Windows 7+), which is a **FILETIME timestamp** of the last time that user mounted the device.

6. **`C:\Windows\INF\setupapi.dev.log`** — A plain-text log file showing the **first installation** of each USB device, with millisecond-accurate timestamp and device serial number.

7. **USBSTOR Property subkey** (64-bit Windows 7+) — Contains the `0064`, `0065`, `0066`, `0067` subkeys holding user-assigned labels, device descriptions, and **first/last connection times** in binary format.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):** 1 mark per key artifact (≈6), 1 mark for the role of the registry in correlating user, device, and time.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

**Q: (a) Explain the forensic process for imaging a USB removable storage device. What are the role and types of write-blockers in this process? (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss Linux-based artifacts that record USB device connections. List the major log files, sysfs paths, and shell history files useful for USB forensics. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**The Imaging Process (5 steps):**

1. **Identification and Documentation:**
   - Record the device's make, model, serial number, capacity, and physical condition.
   - Photograph the device and its connector to preserve the original state.

2. **Application of a Write-Blocker:**
   - Connect the USB device to the forensic workstation **only through a write-blocker**.
   - The write-blocker electronically or logically prevents any write command from reaching the device, ensuring the integrity of the original evidence.
   - **Types of write-blockers:**
     - **Hardware write-blockers** (e.g., Tableau T8-R2, WiebeTech Forensic UltraDock) — physical devices that filter USB/SCSI/ATA commands at the protocol level. Most reliable.
     - **Software write-blockers** (e.g., USBLock, RDR Toolkit, Tableau's software equivalent) — OS-level drivers that intercept write calls. Less reliable; may be bypassed.
     - **Registry-based blocking** (e.g., Windows `WriteProtect` key) — modifies the system registry; forensic value limited.

3. **Hashing the Source Device:**
   - Compute **MD5** and **SHA-256** digests of the entire device surface. The hash acts as a **digital fingerprint** proving the image matches the original.

4. **Forensic Imaging:**
   - Use a forensic imaging tool such as **FTK Imager**, **dd** (with `conv=noerror,sync`), **dcfldd**, **Guymager**, or **EnCase**.
   - Output formats: **DD (raw)**, **E01 (EnCase)**, **AFF**, **Ex01**.
   - The image is verified by re-hashing and comparing to the source hash.

5. **Documentation and Chain of Custody:**
   - Record the date, time, examiner, tool version, hash values, and image path in the case notes.
   - Store the original device in an evidence locker under seal.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):** 1 mark each for the 5 steps, 2 marks for the write-blocker types and roles.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

Linux preserves USB evidence in multiple layers, and the forensic examiner must examine all of them.

**1. Kernel Log Files:**

- `/var/log/kern.log` and `/var/log/syslog` — contain kernel messages generated by the USB subsystem when a device is attached or detached. Example message:
  ```
  usb 1-2: new high-speed USB device number 5 using ehci-pci
  usb-storage 1-2:1.0: USB Mass Storage device detected
  sd 6:0:0:0: [sdb] Attached SCSI removable disk
  ```
- `/var/log/messages` — older distributions store general system messages here.
- `dmesg` (volatile ring buffer) and `journalctl -k` (systemd-based) — the most recently generated USB events.

**2. sysfs Hierarchy:**

- `/sys/bus/usb/devices/` — directory tree representing the live USB topology.
- Each device has subdirectories `1-1`, `1-2`, etc., with files including:
  - `idVendor`, `idProduct`, `bcdDevice`
  - `manufacturer`, `product`, `serial`
  - `speed`, `maxchild`
- `/proc/bus/usb/devices` — legacy text-file equivalent.

**3. udev Database:**

- `/run/udev/data/c{devnum}:{busnum}` — runtime database of device metadata.
- `/etc/udev/rules.d/*.rules` — persistent rules files; may reveal user-modified naming and symlinks.
- `/dev/disk/by-id/usb-*` — symlinks that embed the device serial number in the path.

**4. Shell History:**

- `~/.bash_history`, `~/.zsh_history`, `~/.python_history` — may contain commands such as `mount /dev/sdb1 /mnt/usb`, `cp secret.pdf /media/usb/`, or `dd if=evidence.img of=/dev/sdb`.

**5. Desktop Environment Artifacts:**

- `~/.local/share/gvfs-metadata/` — GVFS metadata for GNOME.
- `~/.local/share/tracker/` — Tracker index database (GNOME search).
- `~/.cache/thumbnails/` — generated thumbnails of images and videos viewed from the USB device.
- `~/.local/share/recently-used.xbel` — GTK recently-used list.

**6. Authentication Logs:**

- `/var/log/auth.log` (Debian/Ubuntu) or `/var/log/secure` (RHEL) — records `sudo`, `polkit`, and `plugdev` group authentication events when a non-root user mounts a USB device.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):** 1 mark per major layer (kernel, sysfs, udev, history, desktop, auth), 1 mark for integration / correlation insight.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing USBSTOR and USB registry keys** — They are different. `Enum\USB` holds the device descriptor; `Enum\USBSTOR` holds the mass-storage class subkeys with serial numbers. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the backup boot sector** — Students often describe the VBR at sector 0 only. Always mention the **backup at sector 6** for FAT32.
>
> 3. **Mixing up cluster states in FAT** — The 0x0FFFFFF7 (bad cluster), 0x0FFFFFF8–0x0FFFFFFF (EOF), and 0x00000000 (free) are commonly confused. Memorize these hex values.
>
> 4. **Omitting file slack and RAM slack** in FAT analysis questions — Examiners award marks for awareness of **unallocated space forensics**.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning SHA-256 vs MD5** — MD5 is deprecated for forensic hashing due to collision attacks. **SHA-256 is the KTU-preferred modern answer**.
>
> 6. **Ignoring Linux artifacts** — Many students write only Windows answers. KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly includes Linux forensics. Always give parallel Linux examples.
>
> 7. **Not mentioning the order of volatility** — When asked about preservation, state the **RFC 3227 order of volatility**: CPU registers → RAM → disk → remote logs → archival media.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
>
> - USB removable storage includes pen drives, external HDDs/SSDs, memory card readers, and USB-attached mobile devices in MSC mode.
> - The **FAT32 file system** dominates USB media ≤ 32 GB; **exFAT** dominates drives > 32 GB.
> - The **VBR** is at sector 0; the **backup VBR** is at sector 6 (FAT32).
> - The **Volume Serial Number (VSN)** at VBR offset **0x43** is the prime correlation identifier.
> - FAT32 maximum file size = **4 GB minus 1 byte** due to 28-bit cluster numbering.
> - Deleted file recovery in FAT uses the **0xE5** signature in the directory entry's first byte.
> - File slack = unused bytes in the last cluster of a file; can contain residual RAM or process data.
> - Windows registry locations for USB: `USBSTOR`, `USB`, `MountedDevices`, `MountPoints2`, `DeviceClasses`.
> - `setupapi.dev.log` gives the **earliest** timestamp of first USB insertion.
> - `MountPoints2` contains **per-user** `DateLastConnected` values.
> - Linux USB artifacts: `dmesg`, `/var/log/kern.log`, `syslog`, `auth.log`, `~/.bash_history`, `~/.cache/thumbnails/`, sysfs `/sys/bus/usb/devices/`, udev `/run/udev/data/`.
> - `lsusb -v` retrieves the device descriptor: `idVendor`, `idProduct`, `iSerialNumber`.
> - **Write-blockers** are mandatory during imaging; hardware types are preferred over software.
> - **SHA-256** is the modern forensic hash; MD5 is acceptable for legacy compatibility but deprecated.
> - Imaging formats: **E01** (EnCase, with metadata + compression), **DD** (raw bit-stream), **AFF** (open standard).
> - The KTU investigation workflow: **Identification → Preservation → Imaging → Hashing → Analysis → Timeline → Reporting**.
> - Anti-forensics techniques: reformatting, secure wiping, encryption, time stomping, physical destruction — all have counter-detection methods.
> - The order of volatility per **RFC 3227** must guide the preservation sequence.
> - The **udev** persistent device-node naming on Linux (`/dev/disk/by-id/usb-Vendor_Model_Serial`) is the Linux equivalent of Windows MountedDevices.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mounted Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mounted Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Windows and Linux Forensics**, a **Mounted Device** refers to any physical or virtual storage volume (USB drives, external hard disks, optical media, network shares, ISO images, or internal partitions) that has been logically attached to the operating system's file system hierarchy, making its contents accessible to users and applications. Forensic artifacts of mounted devices are recorded by the operating system in **persistent configuration files and registry hives**, which are critical sources of **digital evidence** for reconstructing user activity, peripheral device usage, and data exfiltration events.

In **Windows**, the primary artifact repository is the Registry hive at:
`HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\MountedDevices`

In **Linux**, the corresponding artifacts reside in:
- `/etc/mtab` (legacy mount table)
- `/proc/mounts` (kernel-space live mount information)
- `/etc/fstab` (static filesystem table)
- `~/.bash_history` and shell logs (evidence of mount commands)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Mounted Devices is a high-yield topic in Module 3 because it provides a **direct, non-volatile, and tamper-evident** trail of all storage devices ever connected to a system. Examiners frequently test the ability of students to map Registry keys to physical USB devices and to interpret volume serial numbers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **secure corporate building with a visitor logbook** at the reception desk:
- Every time an external person (USB drive, external HDD) enters the building and is "plugged in" (mounted), the security guard writes down their **name, ID number, the time they arrived, and which room they visited**.
- The **Mounted Devices registry key** in Windows is exactly this visitor logbook — it records the **Volume Serial Number, Device Serial Number, the drive letter assigned, and the timestamp** of when the device was first mounted.
- The **Linux `/proc/mounts` file** is the live equivalent of the security camera feed, showing who is currently inside the building.
- The **`/etc/fstab`** is the building's permanent directory of "allowed regulars" — devices expected to be there at every boot.

Just as a forensic investigator can scan a visitor log to know **who visited a suspect, when, and how many times**, a digital forensic examiner uses Mounted Device artifacts to establish **device attribution, timeline reconstruction, and data movement** during an investigation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in court:** A suspect may deny connecting a USB drive to their laptop, but the `MountedDevices` registry key — written by the kernel itself — does not lie. It is considered **system-generated evidence** and carries significant weight in judicial proceedings.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Volume Serial Number (VSN)** | A unique identifier assigned by Windows to a volume during formatting (e.g., `A4E5-6B12`). Stored in the volume's boot sector. |
| **Device Serial Number (DSN)** | A hardware-level identifier reported by the USB device's firmware (e.g., `1234567890ABCDEF`). |
| **Device Instance ID** | A Windows-generated hierarchical identifier using enumerator paths (e.g., `USBSTOR\DISK&VEN_KINGSTON&PROD_DATATRAVELER&REV_PMAP\...`). |
| **Mount Point** | The directory or drive letter at which a filesystem is logically attached. |
| **Registry Hive** | A binary file (e.g., `SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`) that stores Registry data on disk. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Windows: The `MountedDevices` Registry Key

The `MountedDevices` key is located at:
`HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\MountedDevices`

It contains two types of value entries:
- **`\DosDevices\<DriveLetter>:`** — A symbolic link to a mounted volume, storing the volume's binary signature or GUID.
- **`\??\Volume{<GUID>}`** — A unique volume object name used by the Windows Object Manager.

### 2.1.1 Binary Signature Values
Each entry stores a **binary structure** containing:
1. **MBR Disk Signature** (4 bytes) — for legacy MBR-formatted volumes.
2. **GPT Partition GUID** (16 bytes) — for modern GPT-formatted volumes.
3. **Volume Serial Number (8 bytes)** — for FAT/NTFS volumes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A device may be recorded under **two drive letters** if the user reassigned letters, or under **no drive letter** if mounted to an NTFS folder (a "volume mount point"). Investigators must correlate both `\DosDevices` and `\??\Volume` entries.

### 2.1.2 Companion Registry Artifacts (Forensic Triangle)

| Registry Key | Path | Forensic Value |
|---|---|---|
| **USBSTOR** | `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR` | Lists all USB storage devices ever connected, with serial number, vendor, and product strings. |
| **USB** | `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USB` | Lists all USB devices (not just storage). |
| **DeviceClasses** | `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\DeviceClasses` | Maps device interface GUIDs to device instances. |
| **SetupAPI Log** | `C:\Windows\INF\SetupAPI.dev.log` | Plain-text log of device installation events. |

## 2.2 Linux: Mounted Device Artifacts

### 2.2.1 `/etc/mtab`
- A **legacy** file that historically mirrored the kernel's mount table.
- On modern systems, it is often a **symlink to `/proc/mounts`**.
- Format: `<device> <mount_point> <filesystem_type> <options> <dump> <pass>`

### 2.2.2 `/proc/mounts`
- A **virtual filesystem (procfs)** file generated in real-time by the kernel.
- Contains entries for all currently mounted filesystems, including pseudo-filesystems like `proc`, `sysfs`, `tmpfs`.
- **Forensic limitation:** A reboot or unmount removes the entry. Investigators must image the live filesystem or recover memory.

### 2.2.3 `/etc/fstab`
- A **static configuration file** edited by the system administrator.
- Lists devices and mount points that should be mounted **automatically at boot**.
- Forensic value: Identifies **persistent storage intent** and external devices the user configured for automatic mounting.

### 2.2.4 `/var/log/messages` and `dmesg`
- The kernel ring buffer (`dmesg`) and system logs record **hot-plug events** for USB devices.
- Example: `usb 1-1: new high-speed USB device number 4 using ehci_hcd`

## 2.3 Forensic Significance & Engineering Utility

- **Attribution:** Links a specific USB device (by serial number) to a specific Windows machine.
- **Timeline Reconstruction:** Registry `Last Write Time` of the `USBSTOR` key subkey indicates when the device was **first connected**.
- **Anti-Forensics Detection:** A user may wipe the USBSTOR key, but a forensic examiner can still recover entries from `SYSTEM` hive transaction logs and from **Volume Shadow Copies**.
- **Cross-Platform Forensics:** In a corporate environment, an external HDD may be mounted on both Windows and Linux machines; the same VSN appears in both artifacts.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| Concept | Windows Location | Linux Location | Forensic Query |
|---|---|---|---|
| Live Mount Table | `HKLM\SYSTEM\MountedDevices` | `/proc/mounts` | List currently mounted volumes |
| Persistent Mount Table | `HKLM\SYSTEM\MountedDevices` | `/etc/fstab` | Identify auto-mounted devices |
| USB Device History | `HKLM\SYSTEM\...\Enum\USBSTOR` | `/var/log/dmesg` | List all USB devices ever connected |
| Volume Identifier | Volume Serial Number (boot sector) | UUID (`blkid` output) | Match a volume across systems |
| Mount Timestamp | `Last Write Time` of Registry key | `mtime` of `/etc/mtab` | Establish timeline |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Mounted device forensics is used in **insider threat investigations** (data exfiltration via USB), **child exploitation cases** (suspect's external drive), and **corporate IP theft** (stolen trade secrets moved to removable media). The **NIST SP 800-86** guide explicitly recommends MountedDevices as a Tier-1 artifact in any Windows forensic examination.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Forensic Methodology & Analytical Matrix

Since "Mounted Devices" is a procedural and analytical topic (not a closed-form numerical problem), Section 3 is dedicated to the **exhaustive step-by-step forensic methodology** and the **analytical matrix** used to correlate Windows and Linux artifacts. Every transition is explicitly written for KTU valuation.

## 3.1 Windows Forensics: Mounted Device Examination Procedure

### Step 1: Acquire the Registry Hive
The `MountedDevices` key resides in the **`SYSTEM` hive**, located at:
`C:\Windows\System32\config\SYSTEM`

Use a forensically sound tool (e.g., FTK Imager, `reg save HKLM\SYSTEM SYSTEM.hive`) to extract the hive. Verify the acquisition hash (MD5/SHA-256) matches the source.

### Step 2: Parse the `MountedDevices` Key
Mount the `SYSTEM` hive in a forensic registry viewer (e.g., Registry Explorer, RegRipper). Navigate to:
`SYSTEM\MountedDevices`

For each value, classify:
- **Type 1:** `\DosDevices\X:` (where X is a drive letter) → MBR/NTFS volume entry.
- **Type 2:** `\??\Volume{GUID}` → Volume object manager entry.

### Step 3: Decode the Binary Signature

The binary data within each value follows the `_FILE_SYSTEM_RECOGNITION_INFORMATION` structure.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Binary Payload} &= \begin{cases}
\text{MBR Signature (4 bytes)} \;\Vert\; \text{Reserved (4 bytes)} \;\Vert\; \text{Partition Offset} & \text{if MBR} \\
\text{GPT Partition GUID (16 bytes)} \;\Vert\; \text{Partition Type GUID (16 bytes)} \;\Vert\; \text{Unique Partition GUID (16 bytes)} & \text{if GPT} \\
\text{VSN (8 bytes)} \;\Vert\; \text{Reserved (4 bytes)} & \text{if FAT/NTFS}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- If the first 4 bytes correspond to a valid MBR signature (non-zero, not `0x55 0xAA` at offset 510), the entry represents an MBR disk.
- If the first 16 bytes form a valid GPT-style GUID (mixed-endian format), the entry represents a GPT partition.
- Otherwise, treat the entry as a Volume Serial Number (FAT/NTFS).

### Step 4: Correlate with `USBSTOR` Subkeys
For each mounted USB device:
1. Note the **Volume Serial Number** (8 bytes) decoded from Step 3.
2. Navigate to `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR`.
3. Match the device's hardware serial number under:
   `USBSTOR\Disk&Ven_<Vendor>&Prod_<Product>&Rev_<Revision>\<SerialNumber>`

### Step 5: Extract Timeline Data
For the matched `USBSTOR` subkey:
- Right-click → Export → Note the **`LastWriteTime`** (file system timestamp of the key).
- This timestamp indicates the **last time the device was plugged in or its driver was updated**.

### Step 6: Cross-Reference SetupAPI Log
Open `C:\Windows\INF\SetupAPI.dev.log` and search for the device's VID/PID (Vendor ID / Product ID). The log provides:
- `Device Install` event timestamp.
- `Device Start` event timestamp.
- Driver installation details.

### Step 7: Document and Report
Produce a forensic report including:
- Hash values of the acquired hive.
- Screenshots of each Registry key.
- A timeline table (Date/Time | Device Serial | Action).

## 3.2 Linux Forensics: Mounted Device Examination Procedure

### Step 1: Image the Live Filesystem
Use `dd` or `dcfldd` to acquire the disk. If the system is live, immediately capture:
```bash
cat /proc/mounts > /evidence/proc_mounts_snapshot.txt
cat /etc/mtab > /evidence/mtab_snapshot.txt
blkid > /evidence/blkid_snapshot.txt
dmesg > /evidence/dmesg_snapshot.txt
```

### Step 2: Parse `/proc/mounts`
Each line in `/proc/mounts` has 6 fields separated by spaces:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Line}_i &= \text{Device}_i \;\Vert\; \text{MountPoint}_i \;\Vert\; \text{FSType}_i \;\Vert\; \text{Options}_i \;\Vert\; \text{Dump}_i \;\Vert\; \text{Pass}_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- A device beginning with `/dev/sdX` → block device.
- A device beginning with `//server/share` → CIFS/SMB network mount.
- A device beginning with `tmpfs`, `proc`, `sysfs` → pseudo-filesystem (excluded from forensic interest).

### Step 3: Decode UUIDs with `blkid`
The `blkid` command displays block device attributes:
```bash
/dev/sdb1: UUID="A4E5-6B12" TYPE="vfat" LABEL="USBDRIVE"
```
The `UUID` field is the Linux equivalent of Windows' VSN and is used to correlate a physical device across systems.

### Step 4: Parse `/etc/fstab`
Lines in `/etc/fstab` use the format:
`UUID=<uuid>  <mount_point>  <fstype>  <options>  <dump>  <pass>`

The investigator identifies **non-default mount points** (e.g., `/mnt/usb1`, `/media/external`) as evidence of persistent external storage usage.

### Step 5: Extract USB Hot-Plug Events from `dmesg`
Filter the kernel ring buffer for USB events:
```bash
dmesg | grep -i "usb" > /evidence/usb_events.txt
```
Look for lines such as:
```
[12345.678] usb 1-1: new high-speed USB device number 4 using ehci_hcd
[12346.012] usb-storage 1-1:1.0: USB Mass Storage device detected
```

The numeric prefix is a **timestamp in seconds since boot**, which can be cross-referenced with the system uptime to derive a wall-clock time.

### Step 6: Correlate Shell History
Check `~/.bash_history` of the suspect user for commands like:
```bash
mount /dev/sdb1 /mnt/usb
umount /mnt/usb
dd if=/dev/sda of=/dev/sdb1 bs=4M
```
The presence of such commands provides **user-intent evidence** of device interaction.

### Step 7: Document and Report
Produce a forensic report mirroring the Windows structure, with explicit correlation tables between `/proc/mounts`, `blkid` UUIDs, and `dmesg` events.

## 3.3 Analytical Correlation Matrix

| Evidence Layer | Windows Artifact | Linux Artifact | Cross-Platform Linkage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Live Mount | `MountedDevices` key | `/proc/mounts` | Drive letter ↔ Mount point |
| Persistent Mount | `MountedDevices` (after reboot) | `/etc/fstab` | Volume Signature ↔ UUID |
| Device History | `USBSTOR` subkeys | `dmesg` USB events | Serial number ↔ Kernel device node |
| User Activity | `UserAssist` (shellbags) | `~/.bash_history` | Program execution ↔ Mount command |
| File Activity | `$MFT` (NTFS Master File Table) | `ext4` inode table | File creation time ↔ Inode mtime |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Windows Mounted Device Forensic Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acquire SYSTEM Registry Hive] --> B[Parse MountedDevices Key]
    B --> C{Classify Entry Type}
    C -->|DosDevices X:| D[MBR or NTFS Volume]
    C -->|?? Volume GUID| E[GPT Partition]
    C -->|No Drive Letter| F[Volume Mount Point]
    D --> G[Decode Binary Signature]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Extract Volume Serial Number]
    H --> I[Correlate with USBSTOR Subkey]
    I --> J[Match Hardware Serial Number]
    J --> K[Extract LastWriteTime]
    K --> L[Cross-Reference SetupAPI Log]
    L --> M[Build Forensic Timeline]
    M --> N[Generate Examiner Report]
```

## 4.2 Linux Mounted Device Forensic Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Acquire Live Filesystem Image] --> Q[Capture proc mounts Snapshot]
    Q --> R[Capture blkid Output]
    R --> S[Capture dmesg USB Events]
    S --> T[Parse proc mounts Entries]
    T --> U{Filter Pseudo-FS}
    U -->|Exclude| V[Skip proc sysfs tmpfs]
    U -->|Include| W[Identify Block Device Mounts]
    W --> X[Match UUID with blkid]
    X --> Y[Cross-Reference etc fstab]
    Y --> Z[Check bash history for mount Commands]
    Z --> AA[Correlate Timeline with dmesg Timestamps]
    AA --> AB[Generate Examiner Report]
```

## 4.3 Cross-Platform Artifact Correlation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WindowsMachine
        WHIVE[SYSTEM Hive]
        WLOG[SetupAPI Log]
        WMF[MFT Entries]
    end
    subgraph LinuxMachine
        LPROC[proc mounts]
        LFSTAB[etc fstab]
        LDMESG[dmesg USB Events]
    end
    subgraph PhysicalDevice
        USB[USB Drive]
    end
    WHIVE -->|Volume Serial Number| USB
    LPROC -->|Mount Point Device Node| USB
    LDMESG -->|Hot Plug Event| USB
    USB -->|Hardware Serial| WLOG
    USB -->|UUID Label| LFSTAB
    WMF -->|File MAC Times| WLOG
```

## 4.4 Registry Key Hierarchy (Windows)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE --> SYSTEM
    SYSTEM --> CurrentControlSet
    CurrentControlSet --> Enum
    Enum --> USBSTOR
    USBSTOR --> DiskVendorProductRev
    DiskVendorProductRev --> SerialNumberInstance
    SerialNumberInstance --> Properties
    SerialNumberInstance --> LogConf
    SerialNumberInstance --> DeviceParameters
    SYSTEM --> MountedDevices
    MountedDevices --> DosDevicesC
    MountedDevices --> DosDevicesD
    MountedDevices --> VolumeGUID1
    MountedDevices --> VolumeGUID2
```

## 4.5 Linux Mount Stack Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    KERNEL[Linux Kernel VFS Layer] --> PROCFS[procfs]
    PROCFS --> PROCMOUNTS[proc mounts]
    KERNEL --> MOUNTCMD[mount umount syscalls]
    MOUNTCMD --> MTABFILE[etc mtab]
    USERSPACE[User Space] --> FSTAB[etc fstab]
    USERSPACE --> BASHHIST[bash history]
    BASHHIST -->|mount command| MOUNTCMD
    FSTAB -->|on boot| MOUNTCMD
    KERNEL --> DMESG[dmesg Ring Buffer]
    DMESG -->|USB hot plug| USBDEV[USB Block Device]
    USBDEV -->|device node| PROCMOUNTS
```
