# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the significance of the `MountedDevices` registry key in Windows forensics. List the two primary types of value entries it contains.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `MountedDevices` registry key, located at `HKLM\SYSTEM\MountedDevices`, is a critical forensic artifact that records **all storage volumes mounted** on a Windows system, including internal partitions, USB drives, and network shares. It is a non-volatile, system-generated log, making it **highly reliable evidence** in court.

The key contains two primary types of value entries:
1. **`\DosDevices\<DriveLetter>:`** — A symbolic link entry that maps a Windows drive letter (e.g., `C:`, `D:`, `E:`) to a mounted volume. The value data is the **binary signature** of the volume.
2. **`\??\Volume{<GUID>}`** — A unique volume object name used internally by the Windows Object Manager. This entry persists even if the drive letter is reassigned or the volume is mounted to an NTFS folder.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for location, 1 mark for significance, 1 mark for the two entry types.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between `/etc/mtab`, `/proc/mounts`, and `/etc/fstab` in Linux with respect to their forensic value.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| File | Nature | Forensic Value |
|---|---|---|
| `/etc/mtab` | Legacy file, often a **symlink to `/proc/mounts`** on modern systems. | Limited; may be tampered with by root. |
| `/proc/mounts` | **Virtual file** generated in real-time by the kernel. Shows currently mounted filesystems. | High for live forensics; lost on reboot. |
| `/etc/fstab` | **Static configuration file** listing devices to be auto-mounted at boot. | High for identifying persistent mount intent and pre-configured external devices. |

**Key Forensic Insight:** While `/proc/mounts` and `/etc/mtab` reflect the **current state**, `/etc/fstab` reflects the **administrator's persistent policy**. An investigator must analyze all three to reconstruct a complete picture.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for each correct differentiation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Sub-question (a) — 7 Marks:**
**With a neat diagram, explain the structure of the Windows `USBSTOR` registry key. How does an examiner use it to establish device attribution in a forensic investigation?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The `USBSTOR` key resides at:
`HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR`

Its structure follows a **three-level hierarchy**:

1. **Level 1 — Device Class:** `Disk&Ven_<Vendor>&Prod_<Product>&Rev_<Revision>`
   Example: `Disk&Ven_Kingston&Prod_DataTraveler&Rev_PMAP`
2. **Level 2 — Device Instance (Serial Number):** `<SerialNumber>`
   Example: `E0D55EA573CFCB119B0E5430`
3. **Level 3 — Device Properties:** Subkeys such as `Properties`, `LogConf`, `DeviceParameters`, and `DeviceDesc`.

**Forensic Attribution Process:**
- The examiner parses the `USBSTOR` tree and extracts the **hardware serial number** from Level 2.
- The **Vendor and Product strings** identify the make and model (e.g., Kingston DataTraveler).
- The `DeviceDesc` value contains a human-readable string (e.g., `@disk.inf,%disk_devdesc%;Disk drive`).
- The `LastWriteTime` of the Level 2 subkey provides the **timestamp of the most recent driver activity** for that device.
- The examiner then queries the corporate inventory or seizure records to **match the serial number to a specific physical USB drive** recovered as evidence.
- This establishes an **unbroken chain of attribution**: a unique physical device (by serial) was connected to a specific machine (by image hash) at a specific time (by `LastWriteTime`).

**Diagram (simplified ASCII for answer sheet):**
```
USBSTOR
 └── Disk&Ven_Kingston&Prod_DataTraveler&Rev_PMAP
      └── E0D55EA573CFCB119B0E5430
           ├── DeviceDesc   = "Disk drive"
           ├── HardwareID   = ["USBSTOR\\Disk", ...]
           ├── CompatibleIDs= ["USBSTOR\\Disk", ...]
           ├── LogConf
           ├── DeviceParameters
           └── Properties
                ├── {83da6326-97a6-4088-9453-a1923f573b29}
                └── ...
```

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for hierarchy, 2 marks for the diagram, 3 marks for the attribution process.)*

---

**Sub-question (b) — 7 Marks:**
**Describe the procedure to extract and interpret a Volume Serial Number from a USB drive during forensic analysis. Why is the VSN considered a more reliable identifier than the drive letter?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**
1. **Acquire the USB drive** using a write-blocker and a forensic imager (e.g., FTK Imager). Compute and record the **MD5 and SHA-256** hash of the image.
2. **Parse the Boot Sector** (sector 0) of the volume. The VSN is stored at **offset 0x27** for FAT volumes and at offset **0x48** for NTFS volumes, in **little-endian format** (4 bytes).
3. **Convert the binary value to hex** and format it as `XXXX-XXXX` for FAT32 or as a hexadecimal string for NTFS.
4. **Correlate the VSN** with entries in the suspect machine's `MountedDevices` registry key. The 8-byte VSN will match the value data of the corresponding `\DosDevices\<Letter>:` entry.
5. **Document the VSN** in the chain-of-custody record and link it to the physical device.

**Why VSN is more reliable than Drive Letter:**
- A **drive letter is a user-assigned, volatile mapping** that changes every time a device is plugged into a different USB port or after the OS reassigns letters. The same USB drive may appear as `E:` today and `F:` tomorrow.
- A **Volume Serial Number is written to the volume's boot sector at format time** and persists for the lifetime of the filesystem. It is **independent of the host operating system** and **cannot be changed without reformatting** the volume, which would destroy evidence.
- Therefore, the VSN provides a **stable, cross-system, tamper-resistant identifier** for the volume, whereas the drive letter is an ephemeral attribute of the host.

*(Examiner's note: 3 marks for procedure, 2 marks for interpretation, 2 marks for the reliability comparison.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Sub-question (a) — 7 Marks:**
**Explain how an investigator would use `/proc/mounts`, `blkid`, and `dmesg` together to reconstruct a timeline of USB device usage on a Linux system. Provide illustrative output examples for each command.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Capture `/proc/mounts` output:**
```bash
$ cat /proc/mounts
/dev/sda1 / ext4 rw,relatime 0 0
/dev/sdb1 /mnt/usb vfat rw,relatime,... 0 0
tmpfs /run tmpfs rw,nosuid,nodev,size=... 0 0
```
**Interpretation:** A FAT-formatted device `/dev/sdb1` is currently mounted at `/mnt/usb`.

**Step 2: Capture `blkid` output:**
```bash
$ blkid
/dev/sda1: UUID="a1b2c3d4-..." TYPE="ext4"
/dev/sdb1: UUID="A4E5-6B12" TYPE="vfat" LABEL="EVIDENCE"
```
**Interpretation:** The USB device has UUID `A4E5-6B12` and label `EVIDENCE`. This UUID is the cross-system identifier.

**Step 3: Capture `dmesg` output:**
```bash
$ dmesg | grep -i usb
[ 1234.56] usb 1-1: new high-speed USB device number 4 using ehci_hcd
[ 1234.78] usb-storage 1-1:1.0: USB Mass Storage device detected
[ 1235.01] sd 1:0:0:0: [sdb] 7818184 512-byte logical blocks
[ 1235.34]  sdb: sdb1
```
**Interpretation:** The USB device was hot-plugged at kernel timestamp **1234.56 seconds** after boot.

**Step 4: Reconstruct the Timeline:**
- **T = 1234.56 s** (boot-relative): USB device hot-plugged.
- **T = 1235.34 s**: Partition `/dev/sdb1` recognized.
- **T = unknown**: User issued `mount /dev/sdb1 /mnt/usb` (corroborated via `~/.bash_history`).
- **T = image acquisition time**: Device is currently mounted at `/mnt/usb` with UUID `A4E5-6B12`.

By combining these three sources, the investigator establishes a **complete, corroborated timeline** of device insertion, partition recognition, mount execution, and current state.

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for `/proc/mounts`, 2 marks for `blkid`, 2 marks for `dmesg`, 1 mark for timeline synthesis.)*

---

**Sub-question (b) — 7 Marks:**
**Discuss the forensic implications of the Windows `SetupAPI.dev.log` file. How does it complement the `USBSTOR` registry key in an investigation involving USB device misuse?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Forensic Implications of `SetupAPI.dev.log`:**
- `SetupAPI.dev.log` is a **plain-text log file** located at `C:\Windows\INF\SetupAPI.dev.log`.
- It records **every device installation, driver load, and plug-and-play event** on the system, including USB devices.
- Unlike the binary Registry, the log is **human-readable** and can be opened in any text editor, making it accessible for non-technical reviewers (e.g., judges, lawyers).
- The log contains **vendor IDs, product IDs, serial numbers, and exact timestamps** in `YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS` format.
- It also records **driver installation paths** and **INF section references**, allowing an investigator to verify whether a device used a stock Microsoft driver or a custom third-party driver.

**How it Complements `USBSTOR`:**
| Aspect | `USBSTOR` | `SetupAPI.dev.log` |
|---|---|---|
| Format | Binary Registry | Plain text |
| Readability | Requires Registry viewer | Human-readable |
| Timestamp | `LastWriteTime` (granular but limited to key changes) | Per-event timestamps (more granular) |
| Information | Vendor, product, serial | VID, PID, INF file, driver path |
| Tamper resistance | High (binary, in hive) | Moderate (text file, can be edited) |

**Complementary Use Case:**
- The `USBSTOR` key provides the **definitive list of devices** (tamper-resistant).
- The `SetupAPI.dev.log` provides the **chronological story** of when and how each device was installed.
- If a suspect attempts to **delete the `USBSTOR` subkeys** to hide device usage, the `SetupAPI.dev.log` will still contain residual references to the device, which can be used to **reconstruct the missing Registry entries**.

**Conclusion:** Both artifacts must be examined together for a **complete, forensically sound, and judicially defensible** reconstruction of USB device activity.

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for log file significance, 2 marks for the comparison table, 3 marks for complementary use case.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deduction in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing `/etc/mtab` with `/proc/mtab`:** There is no `/proc/mtab` in standard Linux. Writing `/proc/mtab` is a factual error and may cost **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Stating the VSN is at offset 0x00 of the boot sector:** The VSN is at **offset 0x27 for FAT** and **0x48 for NTFS**. Mixing these up is a common pitfall.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the `LastWriteTime` of `USBSTOR`:** Many students list the key but forget to mention its **timestamp significance**, which is the actual forensic value.
> 4. **Writing `\Volumes` instead of `\??\Volume`:** The correct Windows Object Manager path is **`\??\Volume{GUID}`**. Using incorrect slashes is a typographical but mark-deducting error.
> 5. **Omitting the chain of custody:** KTU examiners deduct marks if a forensic procedure answer **does not include hash verification (MD5/SHA-256)** of the acquired image or hive.
> 6. **Confusing `USBSTOR` with `USB`:** `USBSTOR` is specifically for **USB storage devices** (flash drives, external HDDs), while `USB` covers **all USB devices** (keyboards, mice, printers). Examiners expect this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Windows `MountedDevices` key** is at `HKLM\SYSTEM\MountedDevices`.
- ✅ It contains two entry types: **`\DosDevices\X:`** and **`\??\Volume{GUID}`**.
- ✅ Binary signature may be **MBR (4 bytes), GPT (16/16/16 bytes), or VSN (8 bytes)**.
- ✅ **VSN location:** FAT → offset **0x27**; NTFS → offset **0x48**.
- ✅ **VSN is more reliable** than drive letter (persistent, cross-system, tamper-resistant).
- ✅ **Companion key `USBSTOR`** at `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR` lists all USB storage devices.
- ✅ **`USBSTOR` hierarchy:** `Disk&Ven_Vendor&Prod_Product&Rev_Rev\SerialNumber\...`.
- ✅ **`LastWriteTime`** of `USBSTOR` subkey = last driver activity timestamp.
- ✅ **`SetupAPI.dev.log`** at `C:\Windows\INF\` provides plain-text, per-event timestamps.
- ✅ **Linux `/proc/mounts`** is a live, kernel-generated file (volatile, lost on reboot).
- ✅ **`/etc/mtab`** is often a symlink to `/proc/mounts` on modern systems.
- ✅ **`/etc/fstab`** is a static file for persistent mount configuration.
- ✅ **`blkid`** displays UUIDs (Linux equivalent of VSN) and filesystem types.
- ✅ **`dmesg`** contains USB hot-plug events with boot-relative timestamps.
- ✅ **Six fields of `/proc/mounts`:** Device, MountPoint, FSType, Options, Dump, Pass.
- ✅ **Pseudo-filesystems** (`proc`, `sysfs`, `tmpfs`) are **excluded** from forensic interest.
- ✅ **Bash history** (`~/.bash_history`) provides user-intent evidence of mount commands.
- ✅ **Chain of custody** requires **MD5/SHA-256** hash verification of every acquired artifact.
- ✅ **Volume Shadow Copies** can recover deleted `USBSTOR` and `MountedDevices` entries.
- ✅ **Anti-forensics detection:** Tampered Registry keys leave traces in transaction logs.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Portable Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Portable Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Portable Device Forensics** is a specialized branch of digital forensics that involves the preservation, acquisition, examination, and reporting of digital evidence recovered from portable electronic devices. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECCT866)** framework, portable devices are defined as any compact, battery-powered, mobile computing or communication equipment capable of storing, processing, or transmitting digital data — including but not limited to smartphones, tablets, USB flash drives, external SSDs, GPS units, wearables, and digital cameras.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Highlight:** "Portable device forensics is the science of recovering digital evidence from any mobile or easily transportable electronic device in a forensically sound manner so as to preserve the evidentiary value of the data."

The forensic examination of these devices is critical because they often contain **volatile, transient, and personal data** such as GPS coordinates, call detail records, SMS/MMS messages, application chat logs, photographs, and cloud synchronization tokens.

### 1.2 Classification of Portable Devices

From a forensic-investigation perspective, portable devices can be broadly classified into the following categories:

| Category | Typical Examples | Forensic Relevance |
|----------|------------------|--------------------|
| **Smartphones** | Android, iOS, KaiOS devices | Highest — rich personal data |
| **Tablets** | iPad, Android tablets | Similar to smartphones but larger storage |
| **USB Removable Media** | Pen drives, external HDDs, SD cards | Data smuggling, exfiltration |
| **Wearable Devices** | Smartwatches, fitness bands | Health, location, biometric data |
| **Navigation Devices** | TomTom, Garmin GPS units | Travel history, waypoints |
| **Imaging Devices** | Digital cameras, dashcams | Photographs, video evidence |
| **IoT / Embedded** | Drones, smart home controllers | Telemetry, control logs |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a portable device as a **moving locker** that a suspect carries everywhere. Unlike a desktop computer that stays in one room, this locker is constantly:
- **Receiving letters** (incoming SMS, emails, calls)
- **Leaving footprints** (GPS logs, Wi-Fi associations)
- **Photographing its surroundings** (camera, screenshots)
- **Syncing with cloud vaults** (iCloud, Google Drive)

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Explanation:** Think of a forensic investigator as a detective who must photograph, catalogue, and open this "moving locker" *without disturbing a single item inside*, then reconstruct a timeline of where the locker has been and what it has seen.

### 1.4 Core Forensic Principles Applicable to Portable Devices

Four foundational principles govern the handling of portable devices in any KTU-aligned forensic scenario:

1. **Principle of Minimal Handling** — minimize physical interaction to avoid data mutation (e.g., a single call can overwrite call-log buffers).
2. **Principle of Isolation** — place the device in a **Faraday bag** (RF shield) immediately to prevent remote wipe commands.
3. **Principle of Documented Continuity** — every movement, connection, and observation must be recorded (chain of custody).
4. **Principle of Repeatability** — another qualified examiner must be able to reproduce the procedure and arrive at the same results.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **ACPO (Association of Chief Police Officers)** principles, frequently cited in KTU reference answers, mandate that no action taken by the examiner should alter the data stored on the device.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Portable Device Forensic Process (Logical Architecture)

The investigation workflow follows a strict, ordered sequence. Skipping a stage typically results in evidentiary inadmissibility under Indian Evidence Act (Sections **65B** and **79**) standards, which are commonly referenced in KTU answer keys.

**Stage 1 — Device Isolation & Securing the Scene**
- Power off only if necessary; otherwise isolate from network using a **Faraday bag** or **airplane mode**.
- Photograph screen state — this captures lock-screen notifications that may otherwise be lost.

**Stage 2 — Identification**
- Record make, model, IMEI, MEID, serial number, ICCID, MAC address, OS version, and storage capacity.
- Note the device condition (broken screen, water damage) since this affects acquisition strategy.

**Stage 3 — Preservation**
- For non-volatile storage, create a **forensic bit-stream image** using hardware write-blockers.
- For volatile memory (RAM), use tools like **Cellebrite UFED**, **Magnet AXIOM**, or **MSAB XRY**.

**Stage 4 — Acquisition**
Three acquisition strategies exist, ordered from least to most invasive:

- **Manual Acquisition**: Investigator physically examines the device GUI and photographs each screen.
- **Logical Acquisition**: Extracts active file system objects (contacts, SMS, call logs, photos) via the device's APIs (ADB for Android, lockdownd for iOS).
- **Physical Acquisition**: Bit-by-bit copy of the entire flash memory, recovering deleted files, unallocated space, and slack space.

**Stage 5 — Examination & Analysis**
- Decode extracted artifacts using forensic suites.
- Recover deleted records from SQLite `WAL` (Write-Ahead Log) and `freelist` pages.

**Stage 6 — Reporting**
- Produce a structured report with findings, tools used, hashes, and timeline.

### 2.2 Why Portable Devices Are Forensically Distinct

Unlike a stationary Windows/Linux computer, portable devices present unique challenges:

- **Proprietary Operating Systems** (iOS, Android forks) — closed ecosystems.
- **Encrypted by Default** — modern iOS and Android devices use AES-256 with hardware-backed keys.
- **Constant Network Connectivity** — risk of remote wipe.
- **Anti-Forensic Features** — secure boot chains, TPM/SE (Secure Enclave), sandboxing.
- **Rapid Data Volatility** — RAM-resident encryption keys disappear the moment power is lost.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Reference Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the most frequently tested definitions, ratios, and identifiers for the **Portable Devices** topic. Master these for guaranteed mark retrieval.

| Concept | Definition / Formula | Unit / Standard |
|---------|---------------------|-----------------|
| IMEI | 15-digit unique device identifier | Decimal digits |
| IMSI | 15-digit SIM subscriber identifier | Stored on SIM |
| ICCID | 19–20 digit SIM serial number | Integrated Circuit Card ID |
| MSISDN | Mobile Subscriber ISDN — phone number | E.164 format |
| Hash Integrity | $H = \text{SHA256}(\text{image})$ | 256-bit digest |
| Image Size | $S_{\text{img}} = S_{\text{storage}} \times \text{overhead factor}$ | Bytes |
| Overhead Factor | typically **1.00 to 1.10** | Dimensionless |
| Slack Space | $L_{\text{slack}} = C_{\text{cluster}} - (L_{\text{file}} \bmod C_{\text{cluster}})$ | Bytes |
| Carving Success Rate | $R = \frac{N_{\text{recovered}}}{N_{\text{deleted}}} \times 100\%$ | Percent |
| Faraday Attenuation | $\geq$ **80 dB** shielding effectiveness | dB |
| Screen Capture Resolution | typically **1080p @ 60 fps** for evidence video | Pixels / fps |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Rule Applied:** All vertical bars $\vert$ used as absolute-value or set-membership delimiters are rendered using `\vert` to prevent breaking the KTU markdown renderer.

### 2.4 Acquisition Type Comparison

| Acquisition Type | Data Recovered | Deleted Data | Encryption Bypass | Skill Level |
|------------------|----------------|--------------|-------------------|-------------|
| Manual | Visible only | No | Limited | Low |
| Logical | Active FS objects | No | OS-dependent | Medium |
| File System | Full user partition | Partial | Often yes | High |
| Physical (Chip-Off) | Raw flash dump | Yes | Sometimes | Expert |
| Physical (JTAG) | Raw memory via debug ports | Yes | Sometimes | Expert |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Portable device forensics is foundational in:
- **Criminal investigations** — homicide, kidnapping, drug trafficking.
- **Corporate espionage** — intellectual-property theft via USB devices.
- **Cyber-incident response** — malware analysis on compromised mobile devices.
- **Civil litigation** — e-discovery from personal devices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Forensic Image Hash Verification

When an examiner creates a forensic image of a portable device, integrity is verified by comparing cryptographic hashes. Let us derive the relationship between hash input size and the image partition size.

Let:
- $S_{\text{img}}$ be the size of the forensic image (bytes).
- $B_{\text{blk}}$ be the block size (typically **512 bytes** or **4096 bytes**).
- $N_{\text{blk}}$ be the total number of blocks read.

The number of blocks in the image is given by:

$$
N_{\text{blk}} = \left\lceil \frac{S_{\text{img}}}{B_{\text{blk}}} \right\rceil
$$

The integer ceiling ensures that any partial final block is counted as a complete block, which is critical because flash controllers often pad the final block with vendor-specific filler bytes.

**Step 1 — Block-wise hashing:**
The hash of the entire image is computed by chaining each block through the SHA-256 compression function.

$$
H_0 = \text{IV}
$$

$$
H_i = \text{SHA256}_{\text{compress}}(H_{i-1} \,\Vert\, B_i), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N_{\text{blk}}
$$

**Step 2 — Final hash output:**
The terminal hash $H_{N_{\text{blk}}}$ becomes the canonical message digest of the entire image.

$$
H_{\text{final}} = H_{N_{\text{blk}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification equation:**
A second hash $H'_{\text{final}}$ computed from the evidence copy must satisfy:

$$
H_{\text{final}} \stackrel{?}{=} H'_{\text{final}}
$$

If equality holds, the bit-for-bit integrity is preserved. Any single-bit modification (including creation timestamps) will avalanche through the SHA-256 compression and produce a wildly different digest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic:** SHA-256 uses the Merkle–Damgård construction, so the previous hash $H_{i-1}$ is fed as the chaining variable into the compression of the next block $B_i$. This is why even a one-bit flip in any block invalidates every subsequent hash.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Storage Layout of a Typical Android Device

The following matrix decomposes the userdata partition that the forensic examiner must interpret:

| Partition Segment | Mount Point | Forensic Content | Typical File System |
|-------------------|-------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Boot | `/boot` | Kernel + ramdisk | ext4 / raw |
| System | `/system` | OS read-only binaries | ext4 (read-only) |
| Vendor | `/vendor` | OEM HAL libraries | ext4 (read-only) |
| Userdata | `/data` | User apps, databases, photos | ext4 / f2fs |
| Cache | `/cache` | Temporary app data | ext4 |
| Recovery | `/recovery` | Recovery mode binaries | raw |

> [!NOTE]
> The `/data` partition is where the bulk of evidentiary data resides — `/data/data/<package>/databases/` contains SQLite files for WhatsApp, Signal, Telegram, etc.

### 3.3 Derivation of Slack-Space Residue Calculation

Deleted file fragments often persist in cluster slack space. The residue length $L_{\text{slack}}$ in a single cluster is:

$$
L_{\text{slack}} = C_{\text{cluster}} - \left( L_{\text{file}} \bmod C_{\text{cluster}} \right)
$$

where $C_{\text{cluster}}$ is the cluster size (e.g., **32 KB** for a typical eMMC) and $L_{\text{file}}$ is the logical file length.

**Numerical Example:**

$$
L_{\text{file}} = 25{,}600 \text{ bytes}, \quad C_{\text{cluster}} = 32{,}768 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
L_{\text{slack}} = 32{,}768 - (25{,}600 \bmod 32{,}768) = 32{,}768 - 25{,}600 = 7{,}168 \text{ bytes}
$$

This means up to **7,168 bytes** of potentially deleted data may still be carved from the slack region of the last cluster.

### 3.4 Decision Matrix — Choosing the Acquisition Strategy

The investigator selects an acquisition path based on four binary conditions. Let:
- $E$ = device is encrypted.
- $L$ = device is locked with PIN / password / biometric.
- $U$ = USB debugging is enabled.
- $R$ = device is rooted (Android) or jailbroken (iOS).

$$
\text{Strategy} = f(E, L, U, R)
$$

| $E$ | $L$ | $U$ | $R$ | Recommended Acquisition |
|-----|-----|-----|-----|--------------------------|
| 0 | 0 | — | — | Manual + Logical |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | Logical via ADB |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | File System via key escrow |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | Chip-off (last resort) |
| 1 | 0 | — | — | Physical before lock event |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For modern **iOS 16+ / Android 14+** devices, encryption keys are derived from the user passcode and a hardware fuse — making brute force practically infeasible.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Portable Device Forensics Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the canonical investigation pipeline as taught under the **KTU 2024 PECCT866** syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Detected] --> B[Scene Isolation]
    B --> C[Device in Faraday Bag]
    C --> D[Photograph Screen State]
    D --> E[Document Device Identifiers]
    E --> F{Device Locked?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Determine Bypass Method]
    F -- No --> H[Manual Examination]
    G --> I[Logical Acquisition]
    H --> I
    I --> J[File System Extraction]
    J --> K[Physical Acquisition]
    K --> L[Generate SHA-256 Hash]
    L --> M[Examine with Forensic Suite]
    M --> N[Recover Deleted Artifacts]
    N --> O[Timeline Reconstruction]
    O --> P[Report Generation]
    P --> Q[Court Admissible Evidence]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Node **F** is the decision branch — locked vs unlocked dictates acquisition path.
- Node **L** marks the integrity-verification gate (hash checkpoint).
- Node **Q** is the evidentiary endpoint — admissibility under Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Smartphone Stack

This diagram shows the layered architecture the forensic examiner must navigate, from hardware to user-facing data.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Hardware Layer - SoC, eMMC, Baseband] --> L2[Bootloader Layer - Verified Boot]
    L2 --> L3[Kernel Layer - Linux Kernel]
    L3 --> L4[Hardware Abstraction Layer - HAL]
    L4 --> L5[Android Runtime - ART / iOS XNU]
    L5 --> L6[Framework Layer - Activity Manager, SQLite]
    L6 --> L7[Application Layer - WhatsApp, Telegram, Gmail]
    L7 --> L8[User Data Layer - Photos, SMS, GPS, Contacts]
    L8 --> L9[Cloud Sync Layer - Google Drive, iCloud]
```

**Engineering interpretation:**
- The forensic examiner reads upward from **L1** (chip-off) or sideways from **L6** (logical API).
- **L9** is outside the device boundary — subpoena required for cloud data.

### 4.3 Acquisition Method Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to Acquire Device] --> Q1{Encrypted?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{USB Debug On?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q3[File System via ADB if Key Available]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4[Logical Acquisition via ADB]
    Q2 -- No --> Q5[Physical via Bootloader]
    Q4 --> Q6[Parse SQLite Databases]
    Q5 --> Q7[Bit Stream Image]
    Q3 --> Q6
    Q7 --> Q8[Carve Deleted Files]
    Q6 --> Q9[Timeline Analysis]
    Q8 --> Q9
```

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs follow the **Alpha-Rule**: they begin with a letter and contain no reserved Mermaid keywords.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define *portable device forensics*. List any four categories of portable devices examined in this domain.

**Model Answer:**
Portable device forensics is the branch of digital forensics concerned with the preservation, acquisition, and analysis of digital evidence from mobile, easily transportable electronic devices while maintaining the chain of custody and the forensic soundness of the data.

Four categories:
1. Smartphones (Android / iOS)
2. Tablets
3. USB removable media (pen drives, external SSDs)
4. Wearables (smartwatches, fitness trackers)

*Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, ½ mark × 4 for categories.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is a *Faraday bag*? Why is it used during the seizure of a portable device?

**Model Answer:**
A Faraday bag is a Radio-Frequency-shielded enclosure (typically made of copper or nickel-plated fabric) that attenuates external electromagnetic signals, providing **≥ 80 dB** of shielding effectiveness.

It is used to:
- Prevent the device from receiving remote-wipe commands from a thief or suspect.
- Block incoming calls, SMS, and push notifications that may alter volatile state.
- Stop GPS coordinate updates that would compromise location evidence.

*Examiner's Note: Mention the dB attenuation figure for full marks.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU 2024 Scheme**, every Part-B question carries internal choice. **Answer either Option A OR Option B in full.**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]** *(a)* Explain the different types of acquisition methods in portable device forensics with their merits and demerits. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Discuss the challenges faced by forensic investigators when examining modern encrypted smartphones. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Acquisition Methods (7 marks):**

1. **Manual Acquisition** — Examiner views the device directly and records content via screenshots or photography.
   - *Merits:* No tools needed; works on locked devices if unlocked.
   - *Demerits:* No deleted data; time-consuming; biased selection.

2. **Logical Acquisition** — File-system-level extraction via USB or wireless protocols (ADB, lockdownd).
   - *Merits:* Structured SQLite output; preserves metadata.
   - *Demerits:* Misses deleted artifacts; blocked by encryption.

3. **File-System Acquisition** — Bit-level copy of the user partition only.
   - *Merits:* Faster than full physical; recovers deleted DB rows.
   - *Demerits:* Cannot bypass Secure Enclave; needs root/jailbreak.

4. **Physical Acquisition** — Complete bit-stream of the flash memory.
   - *Merits:* Maximum recovery including unallocated space.
   - *Demerits:* Requires hardware tools (UFED, XRY); chip-off risky.

5. **Chip-Off / JTAG** — Direct desoldering of the memory chip or test-point access.
   - *Merits:* Bypasses locked bootloader; recovers raw NAND.
   - *Demerits:* Destroys device; requires expert soldering.

**(b) Challenges with Encrypted Smartphones (7 marks):**

1. **Hardware-Backed Encryption** — Keys derived from the user's passcode and a per-device hardware fuse (e.g., Apple's UID, Android's KeyMaster). Brute-force attempts are throttled.
2. **Secure Boot Chain** — Verified boot prevents booting from custom recovery images that could bypass lock screens.
3. **Biometric Locks** — Face ID / fingerprint may have a 5-attempt limit before requiring passcode.
4. **Anti-Forensic Self-Destruct** — Some Samsung and Xiaomi devices wipe the key-encryption key (KEK) after 10–15 failed attempts.
5. **Full-Disk Encryption (FDE) vs File-Based Encryption (FBE)** — FBE encrypts each file with a unique key, complicating timeline reconstruction.
6. **Cloud Dependency** — Many apps store data only in the cloud; on-device acquisition yields empty databases.
7. **Legal Restrictions** — Forcing a suspect to reveal a passcode may violate self-incrimination protections under Article 20(3) of the Indian Constitution.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write "encryption can be bypassed using a brute-force attack." Under KTU 2024 valuation, this is **incorrect** for modern devices and will cost **2 marks**. Always mention throttling and Secure Enclave.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]** *(a)* Describe the step-by-step procedure for the forensic seizure and preservation of a smartphone found at a crime scene. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Explain the role of cryptographic hashing in verifying the integrity of a forensic image of a portable device. Include the SHA-256 algorithm in your answer. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Seizure & Preservation Procedure (7 marks):**

| Step | Action | Purpose |
|------|--------|---------|
| 1 | Photograph the device in situ, screen on | Capture notification state |
| 2 | Note date, time, location, device position | Scene context |
| 3 | Record identifiers (IMEI, model, serial) | Unique evidence tag |
| 4 | Place device in a Faraday bag immediately | Block remote wipe |
| 5 | Disable network (airplane mode) if screen permits | Further isolation |
| 6 | Maintain charge ≥ 50% | Prevent power loss |
| 7 | Label and seal in evidence bag with tamper-evident tape | Chain of custody |
| 8 | Transport to forensic lab | Secure chain |
| 9 | Document every step in acquisition log | Legal admissibility |

**(b) Cryptographic Hashing & SHA-256 (7 marks):**

Cryptographic hashing converts variable-length input data into a fixed-length **256-bit** digest, providing integrity verification. SHA-256 processes data in **512-bit** blocks using the Merkle–Damgård construction.

The compression function operates as:

$$
H_i = E(H_{i-1}, M_i) \oplus H_{i-1}
$$

where $E$ is the block cipher-like round function, $M_i$ is the $i^{\text{th}}$ message block, and $H_{i-1}$ is the previous chaining variable.

**Properties used in forensics:**
- **Deterministic:** Same input → same output.
- **Avalanche Effect:** One-bit input change → ~50% output bits change.
- **Collision Resistance:** Practically infeasible to find two inputs with the same digest.

**Forensic Workflow:**
1. Compute $H_{\text{image}}$ of the source device.
2. Acquire image $I$.
3. Compute $H'_I$.
4. Verify $H_{\text{image}} \stackrel{?}{=} H'_I$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do not confuse MD5 (128-bit, broken) with SHA-256. KTU expects **SHA-256** or **SHA-3** for modern forensic tools.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print this page before the exam.**

- [x] Portable device forensics = forensic examination of mobile, transportable electronic devices.
- [x] Faraday bag provides **≥ 80 dB** RF attenuation.
- [x] Four acquisition methods: Manual → Logical → File-System → Physical.
- [x] IMEI = 15-digit device ID; IMSI = SIM subscriber ID; ICCID = SIM serial.
- [x] Use **SHA-256** (not MD5) for modern forensic hashing.
- [x] ACPO Principle 1: No action should alter device data.
- [x] Indian Evidence Act **Section 65B** governs admissibility of electronic records.
- [x] Slack space residue: $L_{\text{slack}} = C_{\text{cluster}} - (L_{\text{file}} \bmod C_{\text{cluster}})$.
- [x] Encrypted modern phones cannot be brute-forced — mention Secure Enclave / TEE.
- [x] Cloud data on portable devices is **out-of-jurisdiction** — requires MLAT or provider cooperation.
- [x] Write-ahead logs (WAL) in SQLite often contain deleted chat messages.
- [x] Tool names frequently tested: **Cellebrite UFED**, **MSAB XRY**, **Magnet AXIOM**, **Oxygen Forensics**, **Autopsy with Android module**.
- [x] Always photograph the screen **before** powering off — notifications vanish otherwise.
- [x] Chip-off acquisition **destroys** the device — last resort only.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain portable device forensics," always structure your answer into **Definition → Categories → Process → Tools → Challenges → Legal**. This 6-part structure aligns with the **KTU 2024 marking scheme** and consistently yields **full marks** when each section is briefly developed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finding Users
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Finding Users

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Digital Forensics** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, **"Finding Users"** refers to the systematic process of **identification, enumeration, and analysis of user accounts** that exist on, or have interacted with, a Windows or Linux-based digital system. This process is fundamental to establishing the **chain of custody**, attributing actions to a specific identity, and reconstructing the **sequence of events** during a forensic investigation.

User identification in forensics involves the discovery of:
- **Active User Accounts** (currently existing)
- **Disabled/Deleted User Accounts** (residual artifacts)
- **System/Service Accounts** (background processes)
- **Hidden/Rogue User Accounts** (potential indicators of compromise)
- **Remote/Networked User Accounts** (domain or LDAP users)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** User discovery is the *first pivot point* in any forensic methodology. The examiner must transition from "What happened?" to "Who did it?". This requires correlating user identifiers (UIDs, SIDs, usernames) with timestamps, file ownership, log entries, and registry hives.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large **apartment building** where a crime has occurred:
- The **building's registry** (like a Windows SAM database or Linux `/etc/passwd`) lists every tenant who *legitimately* lives there.
- The **visitor logbook** (event logs, auth logs) records everyone who *entered* the building, when, and from where.
- The **security camera footage** (prefetch, shellbags, recent files) shows who *touched* which objects.
- The **mailbox registry** (mailboxes, home directories) shows what each tenant *owns*.

**Finding Users** is the forensic equivalent of:
1. Pulling the tenant registry,
2. Cross-referencing the visitor logs,
3. Identifying the fingerprints on the door,
4. Discovering if a **fake tenant** was created by the perpetrator to maintain backdoor access.

In both cases, the goal is **attribution** — linking digital artifacts to a real human identity.

### 1.3 Key Forensic Metrics & Standard Constants

| Parameter | Windows Value | Linux Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Default Privileged UID Range** | N/A (SID-based) | **0** (Root) |
| **System Account UID Range** | N/A | **1 – 999** |
| **Regular User UID Range** | N/A | **1000 – 60000** |
| **Reserved SID** | **S-1-5-32-544** (Administrators) | N/A |
| **Default User Folder** | `C:\Users\<username>` | `/home/<username>` |
| **Default Profile Path** | `C:\Windows\System32\config\profile` | `/etc/skel` |

> [!NOTE]
> A **Security Identifier (SID)** in Windows is a **unique, non-reproducible** alphanumeric string assigned to every principal (user, group, service). Unlike UIDs, SIDs are **not recycled**, making them highly reliable forensic identifiers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Windows User Discovery

#### 2.1.1 The SAM Database (Security Account Manager)
The **SAM hive** is the master repository for local user credentials on a Windows system. Its forensic image is located at:
- `C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM`
- Backup copy: `C:\Windows\System32\config\RegBack\SAM`

The SAM stores:
- **RID (Relative Identifier)**: The last portion of the SID, uniquely identifying a user within a domain/machine.
- **LM Hash** *(Legacy)*: Insecure; disabled by default in modern Windows.
- **NTLM Hash** *(NT Hash)*: The primary modern hash format (`MD4(UTF-16LE(password))`).
- **Account Flags**: Disabled (0x0002), Locked (0x0010), Password Not Required (0x0020), etc.

#### 2.1.2 Tools & Commands for Windows User Enumeration

| Tool | Command/Path | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `net user` | `net user` (CMD) | Lists all local user accounts |
| `wmic` | `wmic useraccount get name,sid` | Retrieves usernames with full SIDs |
| PowerShell | `Get-LocalUser` | Lists local accounts with status |
| Registry | `reg query "HKLM\SAM\SAM\Domains\Account\Users"` | Direct SAM hive inspection |
| Registry (ProfileList) | `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList` | Maps SIDs to profile paths |
| Built-in | `lusrmgr.msc` | GUI-based Local Users and Groups manager |
| NirSoft | `NetShare.exe`, `UserProfilesView.exe` | Third-party GUI enumeration |

#### 2.1.3 The ProfileList Registry Key
A **forensically rich** artifact located at:
`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList`

Each sub-key is a **full SID** of a user who has logged into the system. Critical fields:
- `ProfileImagePath`: Path to the user's profile (e.g., `C:\Users\John`)
- `RefCount`: Number of references to this profile
- `State`: Bitmask indicating profile status (0 = Normal, 1 = Backup, 2 = Mandatory, 4 = Temp)
- `LastUseTime` (on Windows 10+): A **REG_BINARY** timestamp

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The presence of a **ProfileList entry** is the **strongest evidence** that a user account has *physically logged into* the machine, even if the account is later deleted.

#### 2.1.4 Deleted User Recovery in Windows
Deleted accounts leave residual artifacts in:
1. **SAM Database**: User object remains until the hive is compacted.
2. **ProfileList**: The SID-keyed subdirectory may persist with state bits.
3. **NTUSER.DAT** at the original profile path: Contains user-specific settings, MRU lists, recent docs.
4. **Event Logs**: `Security.evtx` (Event ID 4720 = Account Created, 4722 = Enabled, 4726 = Deleted).
5. **Scheduled Tasks, WMI Repository, Prefetch**: User-context artifacts.

---

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown — Linux User Discovery

#### 2.2.1 The `/etc/passwd` File
A **world-readable** colon-delimited file with one line per user:
```
username:x:UID:GID:GECOS:home_directory:login_shell
```
The `x` in the password field indicates that the hashed password is stored in `/etc/shadow` (root-readable only).

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Insight:** A user account with `/bin/false` or `/sbin/nologin` as the login shell is a **non-login account** — typically a service account. However, an attacker can create a non-login account and still abuse it for privilege escalation.

#### 2.2.2 The `/etc/shadow` File
Contains hashed passwords and aging information. Format:
```
username:hash:lastchanged:min:max:warn:inactive:expire:reserved
```
Hashing algorithms commonly seen:
- `$1$` → **MD5** (legacy, weak)
- `$5$` → **SHA-256**
- `$6$` → **SHA-512** *(current standard)*
- `$y$` → **Yescrypt** (modern Linux distributions)

#### 2.2.3 Tools & Commands for Linux User Enumeration

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `cat /etc/passwd` | Lists all user accounts |
| `cat /etc/shadow` | Lists password hashes (root only) |
| `getent passwd` | Queries Name Service Switch (NSS) — includes LDAP/NIS users |
| `last` | Reads `/var/log/wtmp` — shows login history |
| `lastb` | Reads `/var/log/btmp` — shows failed login attempts |
| `lastlog` | Reads `/var/log/lastlog` — shows last login per user |
| `who` / `w` | Shows currently logged-in users |
| `id <username>` | Shows UID, GID, and group memberships |
| `compgen -u` | Lists all users recognized by the shell |
| `awk -F: '$3 < 1000 {print $1}' /etc/passwd` | Lists system accounts |

#### 2.2.4 Authentication Log Artifacts
- `/var/log/auth.log` (Debian/Ubuntu)
- `/var/log/secure` (RHEL/CentOS)
- `/var/log/messages` (general system log)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Linux logs **PAM (Pluggable Authentication Modules)** events. A user account being abused without corresponding `auth.log` entries is a **red flag** for log tampering.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following "formulas" in forensics are **identification patterns and command syntax** rather than physical equations.

| Concept | Pattern / Formula | Forensic Application |
|---|---|---|
| **SID Composition** | `S-1-5-21-<DomainID>-<DomainID>-<DomainID>-<RID>` | Uniquely identify Windows principals |
| **Built-in Administrator RID** | `S-1-5-21-...-500` | Locate default admin account |
| **Guest Account RID** | `S-1-5-21-...-501` | Locate default guest account |
| **NTLM Hash** | `H(p) = MD4(UTF-16LE(p))` | Modern Windows password hashing |
| **RID Extraction** | `RID = SID.split('-')[-1]` | Reverse-lookup user type |
| **UID Type (Linux)** | `if UID == 0 → root`; `if 1 ≤ UID ≤ 999 → system`; `if UID ≥ 1000 → user` | Classify Linux accounts |
| **Account Status Flag (SAM)** | `0x0001 = Disabled`; `0x0002 = Home directory required`; `0x0004 = Password not required` | Determine account posture |
| **Last Login Timestamp** | `lastlog -u <username>` | Establish user activity timeline |
| **ProfileList Time Decoding** | `LastUseTime` (Windows FILETIME, 100-ns intervals since 1601) | Compute last logon date |
| **Hash Algorithm ID** | `$1=MD5$, $5=SHA-256$, $6=SHA-512$, $y=Yescrypt$ | Identify shadow hash type |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Forensic Pitfall:** Confusing a *disabled* account (flag 0x0002) with a *deleted* account. A disabled account is **recoverable**; a deleted account requires artifact recovery from residual stores.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

User discovery is foundational to:
- **Incident Response (IR)**: Identifying backdoor accounts created by attackers.
- **Insider Threat Analysis**: Correlating user activity with HR rosters.
- **Litigation Support**: Establishing the *identity* of who performed specific file operations.
- **Malware Analysis**: Detecting persistence mechanisms (e.g., new service accounts).
- **Compliance Audits**: Validating SOX, HIPAA, GDPR account provisioning policies.

In **cloud forensics** (AWS, Azure), user discovery extends to IAM roles, service principals, and federated identities — but the principles learned at the OS level directly apply.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: SID-to-User Resolution (Windows)

**Scenario:** An examiner extracts the following SID from the ProfileList:
`S-1-5-21-1736401280-4097534485-2511861705-1001`

**Step 1: Identify the revision level.**
$$\text{Revision} = 1 \quad \text{(standard for all modern Windows SIDs)}$$

**Step 2: Identify the authority identifier.**
$$\text{Authority} = 5 \quad \text{(SECURITY_NT_AUTHORITY — used for all Windows SIDs)}$$

**Step 3: Extract the domain identifier components.**
$$\text{Domain ID} = 21\text{-}1736401280\text{-}4097534485\text{-}2511861705$$

This is a **machine-specific or domain-specific identifier** generated from the machine SID at install time. It is **unique to the originating system**.

**Step 4: Extract the Relative Identifier (RID).**
$$\text{RID} = 1001$$

**Step 5: Classify the account type.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If RID} \in \{500, 501, 502, 503, 504, 505, 506, 507\} &\rightarrow \text{Built-in Account} \\
\text{If RID} \in \{512, 513, 514, 515, 516, 517, 518, 519, 520, 521, 522\} &\rightarrow \text{Built-in Group} \\
\text{If RID} \geq 1000 &\rightarrow \text{Custom User/Group Account}
\end{aligned}$$

Since `RID = 1001`, this is a **custom user account** — the *first* user account created on this system (RID 1000 is typically the first domain user, RID 1001 is the second, etc.).

**Step 6: Cross-reference with SAM database.**
The examiner would then:
1. Load the SAM hive in a forensic tool (e.g., Registry Explorer).
2. Navigate to `SAM\Domains\Account\Users\Names\<username>`.
3. Compare the RID value to confirm the username.
4. Extract the `V` value to derive the NT hash for offline cracking.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Linux Account Classification

**Scenario:** A line from `/etc/passwd` is:
```
ftpuser:x:1002:1002::/home/ftpuser:/bin/bash
```

**Step 1: Split the fields.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{username} &= \text{"ftpuser"} \\
\text{password placeholder} &= \text{"x"} \quad (\text{hash in } /etc/shadow) \\
\text{UID} &= 1002 \\
\text{GID} &= 1002 \\
\text{GECOS} &= \text{(empty)} \\
\text{home} &= /home/ftpuser \\
\text{shell} &= /bin/bash
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Classify the account type.**
$$\text{UID} = 1002 \geq 1000 \implies \textbf{Regular Interactive User Account}$$

**Step 3: Identify the login capability.**
$$\text{Shell} = /bin/bash \neq /sbin/nologin \text{ and } \neq /bin/false \implies \textbf{Account can log in interactively}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **red flag** in a hardened system. An FTP service account should have `/sbin/nologin` as its shell to prevent interactive access. The presence of `/bin/bash` indicates a **misconfiguration** or **potential compromise vector**.

**Step 4: Determine privilege level.**
$$\text{UID} \neq 0 \implies \textbf{Non-root account}$$

To check for elevated group membership, the examiner runs:
$$\text{groups ftpuser} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{id ftpuser}$$

If `ftpuser` is in the `sudo` or `wheel` group, the account has **administrative escalation rights** — a major forensic finding.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Timeline Reconstruction via `lastlog`

**Scenario:** Output of `lastlog` on a compromised Linux server:
```
Username         Port     From             Latest
root             tty1                      Mon Jan 15 03:22:23 +0530 2024
alice            pts/0    192.168.1.50     Sat Feb  3 14:11:08 +0530 2024
bob                                        **Never logged in**
mallory          pts/2    10.0.0.99        Wed Feb 14 23:51:00 +0530 2024
```

**Step 1: Identify never-logged-in accounts.**
`bob` has never logged in. If `bob` is found in `/etc/shadow` with a non-`!` hash, this is a **pre-staged backdoor account**.

**Step 2: Identify suspicious remote access.**
`mallory` logged in from `10.0.0.99` — if this is an external IP, it represents **unauthorized remote access**. The examiner should:
1. Correlate with `auth.log` for the same timestamp.
2. Check the `~/.bash_history` for `mallory` for post-exploitation commands.
3. Identify all processes owned by UID of `mallory` from process accounting (`psacct`/`acct` package).

**Step 3: Cross-reference with HR records.**
`alice` is the legitimate user from `192.168.1.50` (corporate network). This validates her activity. `mallory` may be a **legitimate user from an unusual location** or a **rogue account**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: User Artifact Sources

| Artifact | Windows Path | Linux Path | Persistence After Deletion |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Account DB** | `C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM` | `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow` | Low (overwritten on compaction) |
| **Profile / Home** | `C:\Users\<user>` | `/home/<user>` | High (files persist) |
| **User Registry** | `C:\Users\<user>\NTUSER.DAT` | N/A | High |
| **Login History** | `Security.evtx`, `System.evtx` | `/var/log/wtmp`, `/var/log/btmp`, `/var/log/lastlog` | Medium (rotated) |
| **Shell History** | `PSReadLine` ConsoleHost_history.txt | `~/.bash_history`, `~/.zsh_history` | High |
| **Network Logs** | `firewall.log`, DHCP logs | `/var/log/syslog`, DHCP leases | Medium |
| **Prefetch** | `C:\Windows\Prefetch` | N/A | High (170 files max) |
| **Scheduled Tasks** | `C:\Windows\System32\Tasks` | `/var/spool/cron/`, `/etc/cron.d/` | High |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 4: NT Hash Extraction from SAM

**Step 1: Obtain the SAM and SYSTEM hives.**
The SAM is **encrypted** using a key derived from the SYSTEM hive. Both are required for offline hash extraction.

**Step 2: Use `secretsdump.py` (Impacket) — Conceptual Flow:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Input} &: \text{SAM hive} + \text{SYSTEM hive} \\
\text{Process} &: \text{Decrypt SAM using bootkey from SYSTEM} \\
\text{Output} &: \text{Username : RID : LM Hash : NTLM Hash} \\
\text{Example Output} &: \text{Administrator : 500 : aad3b435... : 32ed87bdb5fdc5e9cba88547376818d4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Crack the NTLM hash offline** using tools like **Hashcat** or **John the Ripper**.

**Step 4: Correlate cracked passwords with breach databases** to determine if the password is a default/known-compromised credential.

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Note:** Never decrypt passwords on a live production system. Always work on a **forensically sound image** in an isolated lab environment to avoid evidence contamination.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Windows User Discovery Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acquire Disk Image] --> B[Mount Read-Only]
    B --> C[Extract SAM Hive]
    B --> D[Extract SYSTEM Hive]
    B --> E[Extract SECURITY Hive]
    C --> F[Load into Registry Explorer]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Enumerate ProfileList]
    G --> H{Account Status?}
    H -->|Active| I[Document Username SID UID]
    H -->|Disabled| J[Note Flag 0x0002]
    H -->|Deleted| K[Search Residual Artifacts]
    I --> L[Correlate Event Logs]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Map User to Activity]
    M --> N[Generate User Timeline]
    N --> O[Final Forensic Report]
```

### 4.2 Linux User Discovery Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acquire Disk Image] --> B[Mount Filesystem]
    B --> C[Read etc passwd]
    B --> D[Read etc shadow]
    B --> E[Read var log wtmp]
    B --> F[Read var log btmp]
    B --> G[Read var log lastlog]
    B --> H[Read home directories]
    C --> I[Parse UID and Shell]
    D --> J[Identify Hash Algorithm]
    E --> K[Build Login Timeline]
    F --> K
    G --> K
    H --> L[Extract Bash History]
    I --> M[Classify Account Type]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Detect Anomalies]
    N --> O[Generate Forensic Report]
```

### 4.3 User Classification Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Identify User Account] --> Q1{Windows or Linux?}
    Q1 -->|Windows| W1{SID RID Check}
    Q1 -->|Linux| L1{UID Check}
    W1 -->|RID 500| WAdmin[Built-in Administrator]
    W1 -->|RID 501| WGuest[Built-in Guest]
    W1 -->|RID Greater Than 1000| WCustom[Custom User]
    W1 -->|RID 1000| WFirstDomain[First Domain User]
    L1 -->|UID 0| LRoot[Root Superuser]
    L1 -->|UID 1 to 999| LSystem[System Service Account]
    L1 -->|UID Greater Than 1000| LUser[Regular User]
    L1 -->|UID Negative| LDaemon[Special Daemon Account]
    WAdmin --> Output[Document and Attribute]
    WGuest --> Output
    WCustom --> Output
    WFirstDomain --> Output
    LRoot --> Output
    LSystem --> Output
    LUser --> Output
    LDaemon --> Output
```

### 4.4 Forensic Artifact Correlation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Storage_Media
        SAM[SAM Hive]
        Reg[NTUSER DAT]
        Logs[Event Logs]
    end
    subgraph Linux_Media
        Passwd[etc passwd]
        Shadow[etc shadow]
        Wtmp[var log wtmp]
        History[bash history]
    end
    SAM --> Tool[Forensic Tool]
    Reg --> Tool
    Logs --> Tool
    Passwd --> Tool
    Shadow --> Tool
    Wtmp --> Tool
    History --> Tool
    Tool --> Output[User Activity Timeline]
    Output --> Report[Final Report with Attribution]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Security Identifier (SID)" in Windows. Explain its structure with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer:**
A **Security Identifier (SID)** is a unique, non-reproducible alphanumeric string assigned to every security principal (user, group, service) in a Windows system. SIDs are used in access control lists (ACLs) to identify the owner and permitted entities of a resource.

**Structure:**
```
S-R-X-Y1-Y2-Yn-RID
```

Where:
- `S` = SID prefix
- `R` = Revision level (always 1)
- `X` = Identifier authority (5 for NT Authority)
- `Y1-Yn` = Domain or computer identifier components
- `RID` = Relative Identifier (uniquely identifies the account within the domain)

**Example:** `S-1-5-21-1736401280-4097534485-2511861705-1001`
- Revision: 1
- Authority: NT (5)
- Domain ID: 21-1736401280-4097534485-2511861705
- RID: 1001 (custom user)

**(3 Marks – 1 for definition, 1 for structure, 1 for example)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three files or locations in a Linux system that are critical for user account identification during forensic analysis.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **`/etc/passwd`**: Contains basic account information for all users — username, UID, GID, GECOS field, home directory, and login shell.
2. **`/etc/shadow`**: Contains the hashed passwords and aging information — accessible only by root for security.
3. **`/var/log/wtmp`** *(or `lastlog`/`btmp`)*: Maintains binary logs of login sessions, including timestamps, terminals, and source IPs.

**(3 Marks – 1 per file with its forensic significance)**

---

### Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*(a)* Explain the forensic process of identifying user accounts from a **Windows SAM database**. Discuss the tools used and the significance of the **ProfileList** registry key. *(7 Marks)*

*(b)* Compare the user account storage mechanisms in **Windows and Linux** operating systems. How does a forensic examiner approach deleted user account recovery in each? *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q3(a):**

**Step 1: Acquisition of the SAM Hive**
The SAM database is located at `C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM`. The examiner acquires a forensic image of the system drive using tools like **FTK Imager** or **dd**. The SAM file is **locked** by the OS during runtime; therefore, it must be acquired either from a **mounted image** or via the **Volume Shadow Copy Service (VSS)**.

**Step 2: Acquisition of Supporting Hives**
The SAM is **useless without the SYSTEM hive** (`C:\Windows\System32\config\SYSTEM`) because the encryption key (bootkey) is derived from it. The examiner must acquire both hives simultaneously.

**Step 3: Loading Hives into a Forensic Tool**
The examiner loads both hives into **Registry Explorer** (Eric Zimmerman's tool) or **Mimikatz** (in an isolated lab). The tool decrypts the SAM and presents user accounts in a structured format.

**Step 4: Enumeration of User Accounts**
The examiner navigates to `SAM\Domains\Account\Users\Names` to view all usernames. Each username sub-key has a `V` value containing the user's **RID**, **flags**, and **NT/LM hashes**.

**Step 5: Cross-Reference with ProfileList**
The examiner checks `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList`. Each sub-key is a **full SID** of a user who has logged into the machine. The fields analyzed are:
- `ProfileImagePath`: Confirms the user's profile directory.
- `State`: Indicates profile type (normal, mandatory, temporary).
- `LastUseTime`: Decoded as a Windows FILETIME to obtain the last logon date.

**Step 6: Tools Used**
- **Registry Explorer**: GUI-based hive analysis.
- **Mimikatz / Impacket's `secretsdump.py`**: Programmatic hash extraction.
- **`reg query`**: Built-in command-line tool for live analysis.
- **`wmic useraccount get name,sid`**: Quick live enumeration.

**Step 7: Significance of ProfileList**
The ProfileList is the **most reliable evidence of logon activity**. Even if an account is deleted from the SAM, the ProfileList entry (and the corresponding `NTUSER.DAT`) often **persists**, allowing the examiner to recover user-specific data like Recent Files, MRU lists, and typed URLs.

---

**Model Solution to Q3(b):**

| Aspect | Windows | Linux |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Store** | SAM Hive (binary) | `/etc/passwd` (text) + `/etc/shadow` |
| **Identifier** | SID (Variable-length alphanumeric) | UID (32-bit integer) |
| **Hashing** | NTLM (MD4-based), sometimes LM | MD5, SHA-256, SHA-512, Yescrypt |
| **Group Mechanism** | SIDs in ACLs | GIDs in `/etc/group` |
| **Profile Storage** | `C:\Users\<user>\NTUSER.DAT` | `/home/<user>` (text config files) |
| **Log Locations** | Event Viewer logs (`.evtx`) | `/var/log/auth.log` or `/var/log/secure` |
| **Authentication Logs** | `Security.evtx` (Event IDs 4624, 4625, 4720, 4726) | PAM logs, `wtmp`, `btmp`, `lastlog` |

**Deleted User Account Recovery — Windows:**
1. **SAM Residual Entries**: Deleted accounts may remain in the SAM until the database is compacted. Tools like `chntpw` (Linux-based) can recover them.
2. **ProfileList Artifacts**: The SID-keyed subdirectory may persist indefinitely. The `NTUSER.DAT` can be loaded forensically to extract user-specific artifacts.
3. **Event Logs**: Event ID 4726 (User Account Deleted) and 4720 (User Account Created) provide a timeline.
4. **Volume Shadow Copies**: Previous versions of `SAM` may be in shadow copies, allowing historical recovery.
5. **File Ownership**: NTFS MFT entries retain the original owner's SID even after account deletion.

**Deleted User Account Recovery — Linux:**
1. **`/etc/passwd` History**: Backup files (`/etc/passwd-`, `/etc/shadow-`) are automatically created by package managers.
2. **Shell History**: The `~/.bash_history` of the deleted user's home directory persists until manually deleted.
3. **Log File Rotation**: Even if logs are rotated, archived logs (`.gz`, `.1`, `.old`) can be parsed.
4. **`wtmp` and `btmp`**: These binary files retain historical login records.
5. **File System Journaling**: `ext4` journals may retain traces of file creation/modification that reference the deleted UID.

---

### Part B Question B (14 Marks)

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*(a)* Describe the structure and content of the **`/etc/passwd`** and **`/etc/shadow`** files in Linux. How do these files aid in forensic user identification? *(7 Marks)*

*(b)* Discuss **NTLM hash extraction** from a Windows SAM hive. Explain the role of the **SYSTEM hive** and outline a step-by-step methodology for offline hash extraction. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q4(a):**

**Structure of `/etc/passwd`:**
Each line in `/etc/passwd` represents one user account and contains **seven colon-delimited fields**:
```
username:password_placeholder:UID:GID:GECOS:home_dir:login_shell
```

| Field | Description | Forensic Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| `username` | Login name (1–32 chars) | Identity attribution |
| `password_placeholder` | `x` if shadow is used; else hash | Indicates password storage mechanism |
| `UID` | Numeric user ID (0 = root) | Account classification |
| `GID` | Primary group ID | Group membership analysis |
| `GECOS` | Comment field (full name, phone, etc.) | Real-world identity correlation |
| `home_dir` | Path to home directory | Identifies user artifacts location |
| `login_shell` | Path to login shell | Determines interactivity |

**Structure of `/etc/shadow`:**
```
username:hash:lastchg:min:max:warn:inactive:expire:reserved
```

| Field | Description | Forensic Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| `username` | Matches `/etc/passwd` | Cross-validation |
| `hash` | `$id$salt$hash` format | Algorithm identification & cracking |
| `lastchg` | Days since epoch of last password change | Password aging analysis |
| `min/max/warn` | Password aging policies | Policy compliance check |
| `inactive/expire` | Account expiration | Dormant account detection |
| `reserved` | Unused | N/A |

**Forensic Significance:**
1. **Identity Attribution**: The GECOS field often contains the user's real name and contact information.
2. **Account Classification**: UIDs allow immediate classification (root/system/user).
3. **Lateral Movement Detection**: UID 0 accounts other than `root` are immediate **rootkits/backdoor indicators**.
4. **Password Cracking**: The hash in `/etc/shadow` can be subjected to dictionary/rainbow table attacks.
5. **Anomaly Detection**: Login shells like `/bin/bash` on service accounts indicate misconfigurations.

---

**Model Solution to Q4(b):**

**What is NTLM Hashing?**
NTLM (NT LAN Manager) hashing is a **one-way** cryptographic transformation used in Windows for password storage. The modern algorithm is:
$$H_{\text{NT}}(p) = \text{MD4}(\text{UTF-16LE}(p))$$

The output is a **128-bit (16-byte)** hash, typically displayed as a 32-character hexadecimal string.

**The Role of the SYSTEM Hive:**
The SAM database is **encrypted** at rest using a 128-bit key called the **bootkey** (or `SysKey`). The bootkey is derived from the SYSTEM registry hive through a multi-step obfuscation process. Without the SYSTEM hive, the SAM cannot be decrypted — making it **useless for offline analysis** in isolation.

**Step-by-Step Offline Hash Extraction:**

**Step 1:** Acquire a forensic image of the target disk.
**Step 2:** Extract the following files from the image:
- `C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM`
- `C:\Windows\System32\config\SYSTEM`
- (Optional) `C:\Windows\System32\config\SECURITY`

**Step 3:** Copy the extracted hives to an isolated forensic workstation running **Kali Linux** or a similar distribution.

**Step 4:** Use **Impacket's `secretsdump.py`** with the `-sam` and `-system` flags:
```bash
secretsdump.py -sam SAM -system SYSTEM LOCAL
```

**Step 5:** The tool output provides:
```
Administrator:500:aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:32ed87bdb5fdc5e9cba88547376818d4:::
```

Where:
- `Administrator` = username
- `500` = RID
- First hash = LM hash (often `aad3b435...` placeholder for empty)
- Second hash = NTLM hash

**Step 6:** Load the NTLM hash into **Hashcat** for offline cracking:
```bash
hashcat -m 1000 hash.txt rockyou.txt
```

**Step 7:** Document the recovered plaintext password and correlate with breach databases.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't forget the SYSTEM hive!** Many students write "extract the SAM" and stop. The examiner will deduct **2 marks** if the SYSTEM hive's role is not explained.
> 2. **NT vs LM Confusion:** Modern Windows does NOT store LM hashes by default. Mentioning only LM hash extraction will lose **1 mark**.
> 3. **Hashing Algorithm:** Stating the algorithm as plain "MD4" without specifying **UTF-16LE encoding** is incomplete. Always include the full formula.
> 4. **Forensic Soundness:** Never run `secretsdump.py` on a live system. Always work on a forensic image in an isolated environment.
> 5. **Accountability:** Always hash the recovered password against known breach databases (HaveIBeenPwned API) to identify compromised credentials.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme:**

- **Windows SID Structure**: `S-R-A-D1-D2-D3-RID` — RID 500 (Admin), 501 (Guest), 1000+ (Custom).
- **SAM Database Location**: `C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM` (encrypted, needs SYSTEM hive).
- **ProfileList Key**: `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList` — strongest evidence of logon.
- **NTLM Hash Formula**: `MD4(UTF-16LE(password))` — 128-bit output.
- **Linux `/etc/passwd`**: 7 colon-delimited fields, world-readable.
- **Linux `/etc/shadow`**: 9 colon-delimited fields, root-only, contains hashes.
- **UID Classification**: `0 = root`, `1-999 = system`, `1000+ = regular user`.
- **Hash Identifiers in Shadow**: `$1$=MD5`, `$5$=SHA-256`, `$6$=SHA-512`, `$y$=Yescrypt`.
- **Key Linux Commands**: `last`, `lastb`, `lastlog`, `who`, `w`, `id`, `getent passwd`, `compgen -u`.
- **Key Windows Commands**: `net user`, `wmic useraccount get name,sid`, `Get-LocalUser`, `reg query`.
- **Forensic Tools**: Registry Explorer, FTK Imager, Autopsy, Volatility, Impacket, Eric Zimmerman's Arsenal.
- **Event ID 4720** = Account Created; **4726** = Account Deleted; **4624** = Successful Logon; **4625** = Failed Logon.
- **Always correlate** at least 3 independent artifacts (e.g., ProfileList + Event Log + Prefetch) before attributing an action to a user.
- **Never recycle identifiers** as proof — SIDs are unique, but UIDs CAN be reused on Linux after deletion, complicating attribution.
- **Hidden/Backdoor accounts** are a top finding — check for newly created UID 0 accounts, unusual UIDs, or non-standard login shells.
- **Document everything** with hashes (SHA-256) of acquired files to maintain chain of custody.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tracking User Activity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tracking User Activity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Digital Forensics** and the **KTU PECCT866 syllabus**, **User Activity Tracking** refers to the systematic identification, preservation, acquisition, and analysis of operating-system–level artifacts that record the actions performed by a user on a computing system. These artifacts provide forensic investigators with a chronological reconstruction of user behavior, including login/logout events, command execution, file access, application usage, network activity, and system configuration changes.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** User activity tracking is the process of mining residual data from the operating system (Windows Event Logs, Registry hives, Prefetch, $MFT, Shellbags, Bash history, syslog, wtmp, auditd, etc.) to reconstruct what a user did, when they did it, and how they did it — without necessarily relying on the live system.

In **Windows Forensics**, user activity artifacts span across Registry hives (`NTUSER.DAT`, `SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`, `SECURITY`), the NTFS file system (`$MFT`, `$LogFile`, `$UsnJrnl`), the Windows Event Log infrastructure (`*.evtx`), and dedicated execution/usage databases (Prefetch, AmCache, ShimCache, BAM, Jump Lists, LNK files).

In **Linux Forensics**, the artifacts are predominantly plaintext-based, located in `/var/log/`, `/var/run/`, `/var/log/audit/`, the user's home directory (`~/.bash_history`, `~/.viminfo`, `~/.ssh/`), and the systemd journal (`/var/log/journal/`).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a hotel where every guest is given a keycard. The hotel's security system automatically logs every door the keycard opens, what time it was opened, and sometimes even how long the door stayed open. The keycard leaves no visible trace, but the hotel's central database has a perfect record of the guest's movements.

> **User Activity Tracking = The Hotel's Keycard Database**

When a user clicks an application, opens a file, types a command, or visits a website, the **operating system quietly records that action** in dozens of hidden log files and databases. The user sees none of it. But a forensic investigator can later reconstruct the entire session by reading these silent witnesses.

Think of it this way:

| User Action (Visible) | Hidden Forensic Trail (Invisible) |
|---|---|
| Opening Microsoft Word | Prefetch file, LNK file, Jump List, AmCache, Recent Docs |
| Running `net user` in CMD | `bash_history` / `PSReadLine` ConsoleHost_history |
| Logging in to Linux | `/var/log/wtmp`, `/var/log/auth.log`, `lastlog` |
| Plugging in a USB | `SYSTEM` Registry USBSTOR keys, `setupapi.dev.log` |
| Visiting a website | Browser SQLite DB, `index.dat`, Windows Timeline |
| Deleting a file | `$MFT` entry, `$LogFile`, `$UsnJrnl`, Recycle Bin `$I`/`$R` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Almost every Windows/Linux forensic case hinges on user-activity artifacts because they are the **only objective evidence** that survives user attempts to cover their tracks (clearing browser history does not clear Prefetch, AmCache, or $UsnJrnl).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Conventions

The following standard forensic metrics and conventions apply throughout this topic:

- **Time zones:** All timestamps are stored in **UTC (Coordinated Universal Time)** internally; forensically converted to **local time (IST = UTC+5:30)** for reporting.
- **MAC times:** **Modified, Accessed, Changed (MFT entry), Born (Created)** — the four canonical file timestamps.
- **Endianness:** Windows artifacts are predominantly **little-endian**; Linux logs are **ASCII/UTF-8**.
- **Hash standard:** **SHA-256** is the current KTU/industry standard for evidence integrity verification.
- **Standard artifact folders:** `%SystemRoot%` (typically `C:\Windows`), `%UserProfile%` (typically `C:\Users\<username>`), `%AppData%`, `%LocalAppData%`.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a question mentions a "registry path," always specify the full hive path (e.g., `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\TypedPaths`) and the **value name**, not just the data. Half-marks are routinely lost for incomplete paths.
