# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Windows User Activity Artifacts — Complete Inventory

### 2.1.1 Windows Event Logs (`.evtx`)

Windows records over **200+ event IDs** across three primary logs and dozens of specialized logs. The forensic value of event logs is the **Event ID** — a numeric code that uniquely identifies the type of occurrence.

> **Primary Event Log Locations:**
> - `%SystemRoot%\System32\winevt\Logs\Security.evtx`
> - `%SystemRoot%\System32\winevt\Logs\System.evtx`
> - `%SystemRoot%\System32\winevt\Logs\Application.evtx`

| Event ID | Log | Forensic Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **4624** | Security | Successful Logon |
| **4625** | Security | Failed Logon (brute-force detection) |
| **4634/4647** | Security | Logoff |
| **4648** | Security | Logon with explicit credentials (runas / lateral movement) |
| **4672** | Security | Special privileges assigned (Admin logon) |
| **4720** | Security | User account created |
| **4722** | Security | User account enabled |
| **4724** | Security | Password reset attempt |
| **4726** | Security | User account deleted |
| **4728/4732/4756** | Security | Member added to security-enabled group |
| **4738** | Security | User account changed |
| **1102** | Security | Audit log was cleared (anti-forensics indicator) |
| **4688** | Security | New process created (with command-line auditing) |
| **4697** | Security | Service installed |
| **7045** | System | Service installed (post-Vista) |
| **1001** | Application | Windows Error Reporting / crash dumps |
| **104** | SystemLog/Operational | Event log was cleared |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Forensics Indicator:** Event ID **1102** ("The audit log was now cleared") is the most critical red flag in a forensic investigation. A legitimate user **never** clears the Security event log.

### 2.1.2 Prefetch Files (Execution Evidence)

Prefetch is a Windows performance optimization feature that records metadata about processes that have been launched. Each executable generates a `.pf` file in `C:\Windows\Prefetch\`.

**What Prefetch records:**
- Executable name
- Run count (up to **1024** in Windows 10+; **114** in Windows 7)
- Last run timestamp (up to **8** in Windows 8/8.1; **64** in Windows 10)
- Files and directories accessed during the first **10 seconds** of execution

**Forensic Significance:** Confirms that a program **ran**, when it ran, and how many times — even if the program was deleted afterward.

### 2.1.3 Registry-Based User Activity Artifacts

The Windows Registry is the single richest source of user-activity evidence. Key forensic hives and their corresponding value names:

| Registry Path | Artifact Type | Data Recorded |
|---|---|---|
| `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\TypedPaths` | Typed URLs in Explorer | URLs typed into the Explorer address bar |
| `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\RunMRU` | Most Recently Used commands | Commands typed into the Run dialog (`Win+R`) |
| `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\RecentDocs` | Recently opened documents | Last 150 (Windows 10) recent file opens |
| `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\ComDlg32\LastVisitedPidlMRU` | Last visited folders in Open/Save dialogs | Folder paths used in common dialogs |
| `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Office\<version>\<app>\Place MRU` | MS Office Recent Places | Folders accessed in Office apps |
| `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Office\<version>\<app>\File MRU` | MS Office Recent Files | Files opened in Office apps |
| `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Applets\Wordpad\Recent File List` | WordPad recent files | Last files opened in WordPad |
| `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Search\RecentApps` | Windows Search recent apps | Apps launched via Windows Search |
| `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR` | USB device history | Vendor ID, Product ID, Serial Number of USB devices |
| `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USB` | USB device history (parent) | USB hubs and devices |
| `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\USBSTOR` | USB install history | First/last insertion times |
| `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\MountPoints2` | Mounted volumes / drive letters | Volumes mounted (e.g., USB drives) |
| `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\AppCompatCache` (Shimcache) | Application compatibility cache | Executables that may have been run (no timestamps in Win10+) |
| `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\ComDlg32\OpenSavePidlMRU` | Open/Save dialog recent paths | Recent file paths in common dialogs |

### 2.1.4 UserAssist — The ROT13-Encoded Treasure

**Location:** `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist`

**Encoding:** All value names are **ROT13-encoded** GUIDs. The two key GUIDs are:
- `{CEBFF5CD-ACE2-4F4F-9178-9926F41749EA}` — Executable file execution count
- `{F4E57C4B-2036-45F0-A9AB-443BCFE33D9F}` — Shortcut file execution count

**Data Recorded:** Application name, run count, last execution timestamp, focus time (Windows 7+), and total count.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students forget to mention that **ROT13 decoding** is mandatory to read UserAssist values. The `MicrosoftEdge.exe` seen in the raw registry is actually `Zvpebfnyhqtr.rkr` (ROT13). Examiners deduct marks for this omission.

### 2.1.5 LNK Files, Jump Lists, and Shellbags

**LNK Files:** Shortcut files automatically created in `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Recent\` when a user opens a file. Each `.lnk` contains:
- Target path (absolute and relative)
- MAC times of the target
- Drive serial number
- Network share information
- Machine ID (for remote files)

**Jump Lists (Automatic Destinations):** `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Recent\AutomaticDestinations\` — Custom `.automaticDestinations-ms` files per application.
**Custom Destinations:** `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Recent\CustomDestinations\` — Pinned/removed items.

**Shellbags:** `NTUSER.DAT` and `UsrClass.dat` store folder-view preferences — **proof a user navigated into a folder**, even if the folder was on a USB drive that is no longer connected.

### 2.1.6 NTFS File System Artifacts

| Artifact | Location | Forensic Value |
|---|---|---|
| `$MFT` | `%SystemRoot%\System32\$MFT` (or `\$Extend\$UsnJrnl:$J`) | Master File Table — every file/folder, with resident MAC times |
| `$LogFile` | `%SystemRoot%\System32\$LogFile` | NTFS journal — recovery of recent file system transactions |
| `$UsnJrnl` | `%SystemRoot%\System32\$Extend\$UsnJrnl` | Update Sequence Number Journal — every file change |
| `$I30` (Index Attributes) | Inside each directory | Deleted file names within a directory |
| `$Secure` | `%SystemRoot%\System32\$Secure` | Security descriptors — ownership history |
| **Recycle Bin** | `C:\$Recycle.Bin\<user-SID>\$Ixxxxx` + `$Rxxxxx` | `$I` = metadata; `$R` = file content |

### 2.1.7 Other Critical Windows Artifacts

| Artifact | Path | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **AmCache** | `C:\Windows\AppCompat\Programs\AmCache.hve` (Win8+) | Executable SHA1 hash, path, first/last execution time |
| **BAM (Background Activity Moderator)** | `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\bam\State\UserSettings\<SID>` | Executable full path + last execution time (Win10 1709+) |
| **Shimcache** | `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\AppCompatCache` | Last **1024** (or **96** in Win7) executables (no timestamps in Win10) |
| **Windows Search DB** | `C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Search\Data\Applications\Windows\Windows.edb` | ESE database of indexed files |
| **PowerShell Console History** | `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\PSReadLine\ConsoleHost_history.txt` | All PowerShell commands typed |
| **Scheduled Tasks** | `C:\Windows\System32\Tasks\` | Task XML files (creation/execution time) |
| **WMI Repository** | `C:\Windows\System32\wbem\Repository` | WQL query history (persistence detection) |
| **Volume Shadow Copies** | `System Volume Information` | Point-in-time snapshots of the entire volume |

## 2.2 Linux User Activity Artifacts — Complete Inventory

### 2.2.1 Linux Login & Authentication Logs

| File / Tool | Path / Command | Records |
|---|---|---|
| `/var/log/wtmp` | Binary | Successful logins, reboots, runlevel changes (`last` command) |
| `/var/run/utmp` | Binary | Currently logged-in users (`who`, `w` commands) |
| `/var/log/btmp` | Binary | Failed login attempts (`lastb` command) |
| `/var/log/lastlog` | Binary | Last successful login of every user (`lastlog` command) |
| `/var/log/auth.log` (Debian/Ubuntu) | Plaintext | Authentication events: sudo, SSH, PAM |
| `/var/log/secure` (RHEL/CentOS) | Plaintext | Authentication events: sudo, SSH, PAM |
| `/var/log/syslog` | Plaintext | General system messages |
| `/var/log/kern.log` | Plaintext | Kernel messages |
| `/var/log/cron` | Plaintext | Cron job execution |
| `/var/log/daemon.log` | Plaintext | Daemon (service) messages |
| `/var/log/Xorg.0.log` | Plaintext | X server log |
| `journalctl` (systemd) | Binary database at `/var/log/journal/` | Unified journal of all systemd-managed services |

### 2.2.2 Linux Shell & Command History

| Artifact | Path | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Bash history** | `~/.bash_history` | Plaintext list of commands typed |
| **Bash history (with timestamps)** | `~/.bash_history` (if `HISTTIMEFORMAT` set) | Commands with Unix epoch timestamps |
| **Zsh history** | `~/.zsh_history` | Zsh command history |
| **Fish history** | `~/.local/share/fish/fish_history` | Fish shell history |
| **Python REPL** | `~/.python_history` | Python interactive sessions |
| **MySQL history** | `~/.mysql_history` | MySQL commands |
| **PSReadLine equivalent** | No direct equivalent (uses bash_history) | — |
| **Vim info** | `~/.viminfo` | Vim editor history (search patterns, file edits) |
| **SSH** | `~/.ssh/known_hosts`, `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` | Remote hosts accessed & trusted keys |

### 2.2.3 Linux Process & Execution Tracking

| Tool/Mechanism | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Process Accounting (`acct` / `psacct`)** | Records every command run, by whom, when, and CPU time used |
| **`auditd` (Linux Audit Framework)** | Granular syscall/file/watch tracking; logs to `/var/log/audit/audit.log` |
| **`sysdig` / `falco`** | Kernel-level system call capture |
| **`inotifywait`** | Real-time file system watcher |
| **`lsof`** | Lists open files (per-process) at time of execution |
| **`/proc/<pid>/`** | Live process metadata (volatile — must be captured during incident) |

### 2.2.4 Linux Network Activity

| Artifact | Path/Tool | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `ss` / `netstat` | Live command | Active connections |
| `/var/log/ufw.log` | UFW firewall | Blocked/allowed connections |
| `/var/log/iptables.log` | iptables | Firewall events |
| `/var/log/nginx/` / `/var/log/apache2/` | Web server | HTTP access/error logs |
| `~/.ssh/known_hosts` | SSH | Remote servers contacted |
| `/var/log/dhcpd.log` | DHCP | IP assignments |
| ARP cache | `ip neigh` | Recently contacted MAC addresses |

### 2.2.5 Linux File Access Evidence

| Artifact | Description |
|---|---|
| **File MAC times** (`stat` command) | Standard inode timestamps |
| **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** | Who accessed/modified a file |
| **POSIX ACLs** (`getfacl` / `setfacl`) | Granular permissions trail |
| **`/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow`** | Account creation/modification |
| **`/etc/sudoers`, `/etc/sudoers.d/`** | Privilege escalation trail |
| **`/etc/crontab`, `/etc/cron.*`, `/var/spool/cron/`** | Scheduled task persistence |
| **`/etc/rc.local`, `/etc/init.d/`** | Boot persistence |
| **`~/.bashrc`, `~/.profile`, `~/.bash_logout`** | User-level persistence |
| **`ld.so.preload`** | Library injection persistence |
| **Systemd timer units** | Modern cron alternative |

## 2.4 Real-World Forensic Engineering Utility

In **Incident Response (IR)**, the artifacts above are used in the following high-stakes scenarios:

1. **Insider threat detection** — UserAssist + BAM + AmCache confirm execution of unauthorized tools (e.g., `mimikatz.exe`, `psexec.exe`).
2. **Data exfiltration investigation** — USBSTOR + Shellbags + LNK + $MFT reconstruct file movement to removable media.
3. **Brute-force attack detection** — Event 4625 spike analysis (multiple failed logons in a short time window).
4. **Privilege escalation trail** — Event 4672 + 4728/4732/4756 + auth.log `sudo` entries.
5. **Anti-forensics detection** — Event 1102, `$LogFile` gaps, deleted `$MFT` entries, `last` command showing wiped wtmp.
6. **Ransomware timeline** — Prefetch + $UsnJrnl + Shadow Copy deletions = full encryption timeline.
7. **Linux persistence hunt** — crontab, systemd timers, .bashrc injection, ld.so.preload.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to "list artifacts for tracking user activity," always structure your answer as: **(1) Windows artifacts (with paths), (2) Linux artifacts (with paths), (3) For each, what action it proves, (4) Any time-range limitations.**

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Methodology & Forensic Procedure

## 3.1 Standard Forensic Workflow for User Activity Reconstruction

The KTU 2024 syllabus (and industry best practice from NIST SP 800-86) prescribes a **four-phase methodology** for any user-activity investigation. The following derivation walks through each phase in detail.

### Step 1 — Identification (Scoping the Investigation)

The investigator must first determine:

$$
\text{Scope} = \{\text{Users}\} \times \{\text{Time Window}\} \times \{\text{Artifact Types}\}
$$

**Analytical Logic:**

1. Identify all user accounts present in the system:
   - **Windows:** Parse `SAM` hive → enumerate `RID` → map to usernames
   - **Linux:** Parse `/etc/passwd` → `UID \geq 1000` = human user

2. Define the time window of interest (date of incident ± buffer).

3. Select the artifact set based on incident type:
   - Execution: Prefetch, UserAssist, BAM, AmCache
   - File access: LNK, Jump Lists, RecentDocs, $MFT
   - Network: Browser DBs, TypedURLs, Linux auth.log
   - Login: Event 4624/4625, wtmp, lastlog

### Step 2 — Preservation (Bit-Stream Imaging)

$$
\text{Evidence File} = f(\text{Source Disk}) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \text{SHA-256}(\text{Source}) = \text{SHA-256}(\text{Image})
$$

**Standard Tools:** FTK Imager, `dd` (`dd if=/dev/sda of=image.dd bs=4M conv=noerror,sync`), Guymager, EnCase.

**Verification:** The hash equality above is the mathematical guarantee of forensic integrity. Any deviation invalidates the evidence.

### Step 3 — Acquisition (Logical Artifact Extraction)

For each artifact, the extraction procedure is:

**Windows Example — Prefetch:**

1. Mount the forensic image **read-only** (e.g., using FTK Imager or Arsenal Image Mounter).
2. Navigate to `C:\Windows\Prefetch\`.
3. Copy all `.pf` files to the working directory.
4. Parse using **PECmd** (Eric Zimmerman's Prefetch Explorer Command Line) or **Windows-Prefetch-Parser**.

**Linux Example — Bash History:**

1. Mount image read-only: `mount -o ro,loop image.dd /mnt/evidence`
2. List user homes: `ls /mnt/evidence/home/`
3. Extract: `cp /mnt/evidence/home/*/.bash_history /mnt/working/`
4. Verify with `sha256sum`.

### Step 4 — Analysis (Timeline Construction)

The **Super-Timeline** is constructed by:

$$
\text{SuperTimeline} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{N} \text{Artifact}_i \rightarrow \text{sort}(\text{timestamp}_{UTC})
$$

This produces a single chronological CSV of all activity, which is then analyzed for:
- Anomalies (e.g., logon at 03:00 AM)
- Correlations (e.g., USB insert + file copy of customer DB)
- Anti-forensics (e.g., Event 1102, deleted wtmp)

## 3.2 Worked Example — Reconstructing a Suspicious Program Execution

**Scenario:** A forensic examiner suspects that an attacker ran `mimikatz.exe` on a Windows 10 system at some point in the last 30 days.

**Step-by-step analytical chain:**

| # | Action | Tool | Expected Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Parse `Security.evtx` for Event 4688 | `evtx_dump`, `chainsaw`, `hayabusa` | `mimikatz.exe` process creation events with command line |
| 2 | Parse `Prefetch` for `MIMIKATZ` | `PECmd.exe -d C:\Windows\Prefetch` | `MIMIKATZ.EXE-XXXXXXXX.pf` with run count & last run times |
| 3 | Parse `AmCache.hve` for `mimikatz` | `AmcacheParser.exe` | SHA-1 hash, file path, first execution timestamp |
| 4 | Parse BAM registry for `mimikatz` | `RegistryExplorer` + `AppCompatCacheParser` | Full path + last execution timestamp (Win10 1709+) |
| 5 | Parse `NTUSER.DAT\...\UserAssist` (ROT13) | `UserAssistView`, `RegistryExplorer` | Encoded value name decoded → execution count |
| 6 | Check `Shimcache` for `mimikatz` | `AppCompatCacheParser.exe` | Confirms presence (not necessarily execution in Win10) |
| 7 | Check `$MFT` for `mimikatz.exe` | `MFTECmd.exe` | Even if deleted, the $MFT entry persists with timestamps |
| 8 | Check `$UsnJrnl` | `MFTECmd.exe -f --usn` | Every file system operation involving the file |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** Even if the attacker **deleted** `mimikatz.exe` and **cleared** the Prefetch (e.g., with `prefetch.exe /R`), the **AmCache, BAM, $MFT, and $UsnJrnl entries persist**. This is why a layered artifact approach is the forensic gold standard.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Linux Brute-Force SSH Attack

**Scenario:** A Linux server's `auth.log` shows numerous failed SSH attempts. Reconstruct the attack timeline.

**Step-by-step analytical chain:**

$$
\text{Failed Attempts} = \sum_{t=t_0}^{t_1} \mathbb{1}\left[\text{auth.log entry matches "Failed password"}\right]
$$

| # | Action | Command | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Count failed logins | `grep "Failed password" /var/log/auth.log \vert wc -l` | Total count |
| 2 | Extract source IPs | `grep "Failed password" /var/log/auth.log \vert awk '{print $(NF-3)}' \vert sort \vert uniq -c \vert sort -rn` | Top attacking IPs |
| 3 | Extract target usernames | `grep "Failed password" /var/log/auth.log \vert awk '{print $(NF-5)}' \vert sort \vert uniq -c \vert sort -rn` | Top targeted users |
| 4 | Check successful logins from same IP | `grep "Accepted password" /var/log/auth.log \vert grep "<suspicious_IP>"` | Confirms breach if any |
| 5 | Check `btmp` for failed logins | `lastb` (reads `/var/log/btmp`) | Chronological list of failed logins |
| 6 | Check `~/.bash_history` for post-breach activity | `cat /home/<compromised_user>/.bash_history` | Commands run by attacker after login |
| 7 | Check `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` | `cat /home/<user>/.ssh/authorized_keys` | Persistence backdoor |
| 8 | Check `auditd` for syscalls | `ausearch -m USER_LOGIN,USER_AUTH -ts today` | Comprehensive auth audit |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Artifact Strengths vs. Limitations

| Artifact | Strength | Limitation | Time Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Prefetch | Confirms execution | Disabled by default in Win10 1803+ (registry) | Last 1024 runs (Win10) |
| UserAssist | GUI exec + count | ROT13 must be decoded; overwritten | Limited by Registry hive size |
| BAM | Full path + last exec | Requires Win10 1709+ | Last 32 entries |
| AmCache | SHA-1 hash included | First-run only (last run not always recorded) | All entries since OS install |
| Shimcache | Survives deletion | Win10+ has **no timestamps** | Last 1024 (Win7=96) |
| LNK | MAC of target file | Not human-readable natively | Last accessed items only |
| Jump Lists | App-specific recents | Per-app custom format | App-dependent |
| $MFT | Every file ever existed | Timestamps are local time | Permanent (until secure wipe) |
| $UsnJrnl | Every change | Rolls over (~8 MB on default) | Last few weeks typical |
| Event Logs | Highly structured | 1102 = cleared; size limits | Configurable retention |
| Bash history | Exact commands | Easy to clear (`history -c`) | Configurable (`$HISTSIZE`) |
| wtmp | All logins | Binary; can be wiped | Permanent (until wiped) |
| auditd | Granular syscall | Resource-intensive | Configurable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often state that an artifact "stores all commands typed." This is **incorrect**. The default `HISTSIZE` in most Linux distros is **1000** lines, and once exceeded, the oldest is overwritten. Always state **retention limits** in your exam answers.

## 4.2 Linux User Activity Artifact Architecture

graph TD
    L[Linux Forensic Image] --> M[User Home Directories]

## 4.3 User Activity Forensic Investigation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1 Identification] --> P2[Phase 2 Preservation]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Acquisition]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4 Analysis]

    P1 --> P1A[Identify user accounts SAM etc passwd]
    P1 --> P1B[Define time window of incident]
    P1 --> P1C[Select artifact set based on incident type]

    P2 --> P2A[Create bit stream image dd FTK]
    P2 --> P2B[Verify SHA 256 hash]
    P2 --> P2C[Mount read only]

    P3 --> P3A[Extract Windows artifacts Prefetch Registry Event Logs]
    P3 --> P3B[Extract Linux artifacts wtmp auth log bash history]
    P3 --> P3C[Parse with specialized tools PECmd AmcacheParser etc]

    P4 --> P4A[Build super timeline CSV]

```

    E4688[Event 4688 Process Created] -->|Correlate with| P1[Prefetch]
    E4688 -->|Correlate with| P2[UserAssist]

    E4720[Event 4720 Account Created] -->|Correlate with| L1[/etc passwd entry]

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]
**Explain any three Windows Registry artifacts that can be used to track user activity on a Windows system. For each, state the exact registry path and the user action it records.**

**Model Answer:**

Three Windows Registry artifacts for user activity tracking are:

1. **UserAssist**
   - **Path:** `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist`
   - **Records:** GUI application execution count and last run timestamp. Value names are **ROT13-encoded** GUIDs, the most common being `{CEBFF5CD-ACE2-4F4F-9178-9926F41749EA}` for executables.
   - **Proves:** The user double-clicked an application from the GUI.

2. **RunMRU**
   - **Path:** `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\RunMRU`
   - **Records:** Commands typed into the **Run dialog** (`Win+R`). The values are stored in `MRUList` order, with `a`, `b`, `c` ... representing most-recent-first ordering.
   - **Proves:** The user explicitly launched a program by typing its path/command.

3. **TypedPaths**
   - **Path:** `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\TypedPaths`
   - **Records:** URLs and folder paths **typed** (not clicked) into the Windows Explorer address bar. Stored as `url1`, `url2`, ... in MRU order.
   - **Proves:** The user manually navigated via the address bar (often used to access hidden shares or USB drives).

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]
**List the four primary Linux log files used to track user authentication and login activity. State the exact file path and the type of information each records.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | File | Path | Information Recorded |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `wtmp` | `/var/log/wtmp` | Successful logins, logouts, reboots, system events (read with `last` command) |
| 2 | `btmp` | `/var/log/btmp` | Failed/bad login attempts (read with `lastb` command) |
| 3 | `lastlog` | `/var/log/lastlog` | Last successful login of every user on the system (read with `lastlog` command) |
| 4 | `auth.log` (Debian/Ubuntu) / `secure` (RHEL) | `/var/log/auth.log` or `/var/log/secure` | Authentication events: sudo, SSH, PAM, user switching, password changes |

All four are **binary** files except `auth.log`/`secure`, which are plaintext. The information is critical for detecting brute-force attacks (via `btmp`) and confirming breaches (via `wtmp`).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the Windows **Prefetch** mechanism as a forensic artifact. Cover its location, contents, the operating-system differences in retention, and the forensic evidence it provides. Explain how an investigator would parse it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **Shellbags** and **LNK files** as Windows user-activity artifacts. Compare their forensic value in reconstructing a user's folder-navigation history, especially for removable media.

---

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Prefetch (7 Marks)

**Definition:** Prefetch is a Windows performance optimization feature introduced in Windows XP. It monitors the first **10 seconds** of process execution and stores the metadata to a `.pf` file in `C:\Windows\Prefetch\`. The OS preloads referenced files into memory on subsequent runs to reduce startup time.

**Location & Naming Convention:**

$$
\text{File} = \text{`<EXECUTABLE>-<HASH>.pf`}
$$

For example, `NOTEPAD.EXE-` followed by an 8-character hexadecimal hash of the executable's path.

**Contents of a `.pf` file:**
- Executable name
- Run count (number of times launched)
- Last run timestamps (multiple in Win 8/10)
- File and directory references opened during the first 10 seconds of execution
- Volume and device information

**OS-Specific Retention Limits:**

| OS | Max Run Count | Max Last Run Timestamps |
|---|---|---|
| Windows XP/Vista | 114 | 1 |
| Windows 7 | 114 | 8 |
| Windows 8/8.1 | 1024 | 8 |
| Windows 10 | 1024 | **64** (Win10 1507+), **8** (older builds) |

**Forensic Value:** Confirms that an application was **executed**, even if it has been deleted. Provides run counts and timestamps, allowing timeline reconstruction.

**Parsing:** Tools used are:
- **PECmd.exe** (Eric Zimmerman) — command-line, exports to CSV
- **Windows-Prefetch-Parser** (Python-based)
- **FTK**, **X-Ways**, **EnCase** (commercial GUI tools)

**Example PECmd command:**

$$
\text{PECmd.exe -d C:\textbackslash Windows\textbackslash Prefetch --csv "C:\textbackslash Output"}
$$

This recursively processes all `.pf` files and outputs a CSV containing executable name, run count, and last run timestamps.

#### Part (b) — Shellbags vs. LNK Files (7 Marks)

**LNK Files (Shortcut Files):**
- **Location:** `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Recent\`
- **Format:** Binary, contains:
  - Target absolute and relative paths
  - **MAC times** of the target file
  - Drive serial number
  - **NetBIOS name** (for remote files)
  - **Machine ID** (GUID for remote machines)
- **Forensic Value:** Proves a user **opened** a specific file or shortcut. The MAC times of the **target** are extremely valuable as they show when the target file was last modified before being opened.
- **Limitations:** Only records the most recently accessed items; cleared when the user empties "Recent Items."

**Shellbags:**
- **Location:** 
  - `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\Shell\BagMRU` and `Bags`
  - `UsrClass.dat\Local Settings\Software\Microsoft\Windows\Shell\BagMRU`
- **Format:** Binary registry values; each "bag" represents a folder the user navigated into.
- **Recorded Information:** Folder path, **folder size**, **folder MAC times**, **view preferences** (Details, List, Tiles), **window position**.
- **Forensic Value:** Proves a user **navigated into** a folder — even if the folder was on a USB drive, network share, or has been deleted.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | LNK Files | Shellbags |
|---|---|---|
| Records | Specific file/shortcut opened | Folder navigated into |
| Location | AppData Recent folder | NTUSER.DAT and UsrClass.dat |
| Survives folder deletion | No (file must exist) | **Yes** (critical advantage) |
| Includes MAC times | Yes (of target) | Yes (of folder) |
| Network/USB evidence | Yes (with NetBIOS, MachineID) | Yes (path retained) |
| Volatility | Cleared on Recent Items wipe | Persists in registry until hive deletion |

**Investigative Conclusion:** When investigating folder navigation on **removable media (USB)**, Shellbags are **superior** because the registry value persists even after the USB is ejected. LNK files also exist for USB navigation, but Shellbags provide a more complete picture of folder traversal depth.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **BAM (Background Activity Moderator)** and **AmCache** artifacts in Windows 10. Explain how they are used in forensic investigations to confirm program execution even after the program and its Prefetch have been deleted.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain how Linux **Process Accounting (`acct`/`psacct`)** and **Audit Framework (`auditd`)** are used to track user activity. Compare the two mechanisms in terms of granularity, performance impact, and forensic utility.

---

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — BAM and AmCache (7 Marks)

**BAM (Background Activity Moderator):**
- **Location:** `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\bam\State\UserSettings\<SID>`
- **Available:** Windows 10 **1709** (Fall Creators Update) and later
- **Records:** Full path of every executable run in the foreground or background, along with the **last execution timestamp** in **Windows FILETIME (100-nanosecond intervals since 1601-01-01)**.
- **Limitations:** Only the **most recent** run is recorded per executable; BAM only retains the last **32** entries per user in older builds.
- **Forensic Power:** Crucial for **modern** Windows 10+ investigations because Prefetch is often disabled via Group Policy (`DisablePrefetch=1`) in enterprise environments.

**AmCache:**
- **Location:** `C:\Windows\AppCompat\Programs\AmCache.hve`
- **Format:** Registry hive (not `.evtx` — the file extension is misleading; it is a binary hive)
- **Records (per executable):**
  - Full file path
  - **SHA-1 hash** of the executable
  - **First execution timestamp** (when first seen)
  - **Last execution timestamp** (some versions)
  - File size, version, PE metadata

**Why BAM + AmCache Defeat Prefetch Deletion:**

$$
\text{Evidence Layers} = \{\text{Prefetch}\} \cup \{\text{BAM}\} \cup \{\text{AmCache}\} \cup \{\text{Shimcache}\} \cup \{\text{UserAssist}\}
$$

An attacker can:
1. Delete Prefetch files → BAM and AmCache still record execution
2. Delete `mimikatz.exe` → $MFT, $UsnJrnl, and AmCache still hold the SHA-1 and path
3. Clear UserAssist → AmCache's first-execution timestamp still exists
4. Disable Prefetch via GPO → BAM and AmCache remain intact

**Parsing Tools:** `AppCompatCacheParser.exe` (Eric Zimmerman) for both BAM (when in `SYSTEM` hive) and AmCache.

#### Part (b) — Process Accounting vs. auditd (7 Marks)

**Process Accounting (`acct` / `psacct`):**

The Process Accounting subsystem is enabled by:

$$
\text{State} = \{\text{accton on}, \text{accton off}\}
$$

With the binary `/var/account/pacct` as the default log file.

**Command to enable:**

$$
\text{sudo accton /var/account/pacct}
$$

**Records per command:**
- User who executed the command
- Command name
- CPU time consumed
- Start time / elapsed time
- TTY (terminal)

**Read with `lastcomm`:** `lastcomm <user>` shows that user's commands.

**Granularity:** **Coarse** — only records command name, not arguments.

**Performance Impact:** **Minimal** — captures only the high-level exec events.

**audit Framework (`auditd`):**

The Linux Audit Framework is configured via `/etc/audit/auditd.conf` and rule files in `/etc/audit/rules.d/`.

**Key audit rules for user activity tracking:**

$$
\text{-w /etc/passwd -p wa -k identity\_change}
$$

$$
\text{-w /etc/shadow -p wa -k identity\_change}
$$

$$
\text{-w /var/log/auth.log -p wa -k auth\_log\_change}
$$

$$
\text{-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -k exec\_syscall}
$$

**Records:** Full **syscall** detail — every `execve` with **complete command-line arguments**, every file open (`openat`), every privilege change, every network bind/connect.

**Read with `ausearch` and `aureport`:**

$$
\text{ausearch -m EXECVE -ts today} \rightarrow \text{all execve syscalls}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Process Accounting (`acct`) | Audit Framework (`auditd`) |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Command name only | Full syscall with arguments |
| Configuration | Single toggle | Per-rule file/directory/syscall filters |
| Performance Impact | **Low** | **High** (especially with execve rules) |
| Storage | Binary, small | Text, can grow rapidly |
| Log file | `/var/account/pacct` | `/var/log/audit/audit.log` |
| Forensics use | Quick triage | Deep investigation, compliance |
| Argument capture | **No** | **Yes** |

**Investigative Conclusion:** Use `acct` for **first-pass triage** (lightweight, always-on in many distros). Use `auditd` for **compliance-grade** logging (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, FedRAMP) where full command-line evidence is required.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting ROT13 decoding** in UserAssist → examiner deducts 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing `auth.log` (Debian) with `secure` (RHEL)** — they are **distribution-specific**, not interchangeable. Always state the OS family.
> 3. **Stating Prefetch is "always enabled"** — wrong. Windows 10 1803+ has it disabled by default in some configurations and admins commonly turn it off.
> 4. **Omitting time-conversion details** — timestamps are stored in **UTC FILETIME** (Windows) and **Unix epoch** (Linux). Examiners expect a note on conversion.
> 5. **Confusing AmCache and Shimcache** — AmCache = full SHA-1 + path; Shimcache (Win10+) = no timestamps.
> 6. **Missing Event ID 1102** as the "log cleared" red flag — this is the **highest-value** event ID for detecting anti-forensics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Windows Event Log critical IDs:** 4624 (success logon), 4625 (fail), 4648 (explicit creds), 4672 (admin), 1102 (**CLEARED — RED FLAG**), 4688 (process), 4720/4726 (account create/delete), 7045 (service).
- **Prefetch:** `C:\Windows\Prefetch\*.pf`; up to 1024 runs (Win10); 8 → 64 timestamps (Win8 → Win10).
- **UserAssist:** ROT13-encoded GUIDs in `NTUSER.DAT\...\UserAssist`.
- **BAM:** Win10 1709+; full path + last exec time in `HKLM\SYSTEM\...\bam\State\UserSettings\<SID>`.
- **AmCache:** `C:\Windows\AppCompat\Programs\AmCache.hve`; SHA-1, first exec, path.
- **Shimcache:** No timestamps in Win10+; useful only for confirming program **presence**, not execution.
- **LNK:** `%AppData%\Microsoft\Windows\Recent\*.lnk`; includes target MAC times + NetBIOS + MachineID.
- **Shellbags:** Folder-navigation proof, persists in `NTUSER.DAT` and `UsrClass.dat` even after deletion.
- **$MFT:** Permanent record of every file; resident timestamps survive deletion.
- **$UsnJrnl:** Every file system change; rolls over after ~8 MB.
- **Linux wtmp/btmp/lastlog:** All binary; `last`, `lastb`, `lastlog` commands; `/var/log/wtmp` is the master login log.
- **Linux auth.log vs. secure:** Debian/Ubuntu uses `auth.log`; RHEL/CentOS uses `secure`.
- **Bash history:** `~/.bash_history`; default `HISTSIZE=1000`; can be cleared with `history -c`.
- **auditd:** Granular syscall logging; `/var/log/audit/audit.log`; queried with `ausearch` and `aureport`.
- **Anti-forensics indicators:** Event 1102, deleted Prefetch, missing $MFT entries, suspicious ROT13, wiped wtmp, `ld.so.preload` modification.
- **Toolkit:** Eric Zimmerman's tools (PECmd, MFTECmd, AmcacheParser, RegistryExplorer, AppCompatCacheParser), FTK Imager, Autopsy, Volatility, `dd`, `grep`, `ausearch`, `last`, `lastb`, `lastlog`.
- **Golden Rule:** No single artifact is conclusive — always use **layered analysis** across Prefetch, BAM, AmCache, Shimcache, UserAssist, $MFT, and Event Logs to build a robust timeline.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linux Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Linux Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Linux** is a free, open-source, **monolithic, modular, multitasking, multi-user Unix-like operating system kernel** first released by Linus Torvalds on **17 September 1991**. The complete GNU/Linux operating system is a synergistic combination of the **Linux Kernel** (the core scheduler and hardware interface) and the **GNU Userland** (utilities, libraries, and shells from the GNU Project), distributed under the **GNU General Public License (GPLv2)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of *Windows and Linux Forensics*, Linux is positioned as a **forensically-viable platform** because of its native support for diverse file systems (ext2, ext3, ext4, XFS, Btrfs), transparent system logging, command-line precision, and the existence of dedicated forensic distributions such as **Kali Linux**, **CAINE**, **DEFT**, and **SANS SIFT**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized)**
> *Linux* is a Unix-like, open-source operating system kernel that, when packaged with GNU utilities, forms a complete OS. It is characterized by a hierarchical file system, strict user-based permission model, and a modular monolithic kernel architecture that is critical in digital forensic investigation and incident response.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a computer as a **large office building**:
- The **Hardware** (CPU, RAM, Disk) is the building's foundation, walls, plumbing, and electrical wiring.
- The **Kernel** is the **building's central operations manager** — it knows where every desk (process) is, schedules who uses the elevator (CPU) when, and ensures no two people (processes) fight over the same room (memory).
- The **Shell** is the **reception desk intercom system** — you speak commands through it, and the operations manager executes them.
- The **User Applications** (Firefox, LibreOffice) are the **tenants** of the building — they rent resources from the operations manager to do their work.
- The **File System** is the **filing cabinet system** of the building — every document has an address, and the cabinet has rules about who can read, modify, or destroy a file.

A **forensic investigator** is like a **building inspector who arrives after hours**: they need to know the building layout intimately, understand the rules of the operations manager, and be able to read the filing cabinets (logs, file metadata) without disturbing the evidence. Linux, with its transparent and verbose record-keeping, is uniquely suited to this task.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

| Constant / Metric | Standard Value (Linux Default) | Forensic Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Default UID for root** | **0** | Identifies the superuser account |
| **Reserved System UID range** | **1 – 999** | Identifies service/system accounts (daemons) |
| **Regular User UID range** | **1000 – 60000** | Identifies human user accounts |
| **Default shell** | **/bin/bash** (Bourne Again Shell) | Primary command interpreter |
| **Maximum filename length** | **255 bytes** | File system limit |
| **Maximum path length** | **4096 bytes** | Path resolution limit |
| **Default inode ratio** | **1 inode per 4096 bytes** | Affects max file count per partition |
| **POSIX permissions bits** | **9 bits (rwx × 3 classes)** | File access control model |
| **Linux epoch** | **00:00:00 UTC, 1 January 1970** | Unix time reference (time_t = 0) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Open Source Philosophy (The Four Freedoms of Free Software)**
> 1. **Freedom 0** – Run the program for any purpose.
> 2. **Freedom 1** – Study how the program works and modify it.
> 3. **Freedom 2** – Redistribute copies.
> 4. **Freedom 3** – Improve the program and release improvements to the public.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Linux Architecture — Layered Analytical Breakdown

The Linux OS is conventionally divided into **four logical layers**, each isolated by well-defined interfaces:

**Layer 1 — Hardware (Physical Layer)**
- CPU, RAM, Disk (HDD/SSD/NVMe), NIC, USB controllers.
- The kernel communicates with hardware via **device drivers** loaded as Loadable Kernel Modules (LKMs).

**Layer 2 — Linux Kernel (Core Layer)**
- The **only privileged software** that executes in **Ring 0** (x86 protection rings).
- Responsibilities: **Process scheduling, memory management, virtual file system (VFS), network stack, device drivers, inter-process communication (IPC)**.
- Linux uses a **monolithic architecture with modular design** — it is a single large kernel image, but functionality can be dynamically extended by loading/unloading modules (`insmod`, `rmmod`, `modprobe`).

**Layer 3 — Shell (Command Interpreter Layer)**
- The user-space program that reads commands and dispatches them to the kernel via **system calls** (e.g., `open`, `read`, `write`, `fork`, `exec`).
- Types: **Bourne Shell (sh)**, **Bourne Again Shell (bash)**, **C Shell (csh)**, **Korn Shell (ksh)**, **Z Shell (zsh)**.

**Layer 4 — User Applications (Userland Layer)**
- Compiled binaries and interpreted scripts residing in `/usr/bin`, `/usr/local/bin`, `/opt`, and `/bin`.
- For forensics: `dd`, `ddrescue`, `testdisk`, `photorec`, `sleuthkit`, `autopsy`, `volatility`, `foremost`, `scalpel`.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Layer

| Layer | Why It Exists | Forensic Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Hardware | Physical execution substrate | Source of raw magnetic/silicon artifacts |
| Kernel | Enforce security & share resources | Contains the **most authoritative evidence** (process tables, inodes, sockets) |
| Shell | Translate intent to syscall | **Command history** (`~/.bash_history`) is direct evidence of user actions |
| Applications | User productivity | Contain user-generated artifacts (browser history, document metadata) |

### 2.3 Linux Distributions (Distros) — The Forensic Ecosystem

A **distribution** is a coherent bundle consisting of the **Linux kernel + GNU userland + package manager + default applications + desktop environment**, curated and released by a community or vendor.

| Distribution | Base | Package Manager | Forensic Use Case | Default UID root |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Kali Linux** | Debian | `apt` / `dpkg` | Offensive + forensic toolkit pre-loaded | 0 |
| **Ubuntu** | Debian | `apt` / `dpkg` | General-purpose investigation workstation | 0 |
| **Fedora** | Red Hat | `dnf` / `rpm` | Server forensics (RHEL-compatible) | 0 |
| **CentOS Stream** | Red Hat | `dnf` / `rpm` | Enterprise server artifact analysis | 0 |
| **Arch Linux** | Independent | `pacman` | Custom build for advanced investigators | 0 |
| **CAINE** | Ubuntu | `apt` | Live forensic boot environment | 0 |
| **SANS SIFT** | Ubuntu | `apt` | Incident response & forensic suite | 0 |
| **DEFT** | Ubuntu | `apt` | Live forensic distro | 0 |
| **BlackArch** | Arch | `pacman` | Offensive + forensic tools extension | 0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Kernel Naming Convention**: Linux kernels follow the schema `A.B.C[-D]` where:
> - **A** = major version (e.g., **6**)
> - **B** = minor version (e.g., **6**)
> - **C** = patch level (e.g., **3**)
> - **D** = distribution / patch descriptor (e.g., `am`, `generic`)
>
> Example: `6.6.3-amd64` = Linux 6.6.3, AMD 64-bit build.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Permission & Identity Mathematics

The Linux permission model is **mathematically rigorous** and is one of the highest-yield topics in the KTU 2024 ESE paper for this course. The following table summarizes the analytical operations an examiner must master.

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Computational Form | Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_{octal}$ | Octal permission representation | $P_{octal} = U \cdot 64 + G \cdot 8 + O$ | $[0, 777]$ |
| $U, G, O$ | User / Group / Other class octal digit | $U = 4 \cdot r + 2 \cdot w + 1 \cdot x$ | $[0, 7]$ each |
| $r, w, x$ | Read, write, execute bits | Binary bits | $\{0, 1\}$ each |
| $S_{set}$ | Special bit mask | $S_{set} = 4 \cdot SUID + 2 \cdot SGID + 1 \cdot Sticky$ | $\{0, 1\}$ each |
| $UID_{class}$ | User class classification | $[0, 999] \rightarrow$ system; $[1000, 60000] \rightarrow$ user; $[60001, 65533] \rightarrow$ service | integer |
| $T_{unix}$ | Unix timestamp | $T_{unix} = \text{seconds since 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC}$ | integer, $32$ or $64$-bit |
| $M_{umask}$ | Default permission mask | $M_{default} = 666 \ \& \ \neg M_{umask}$ (files); $777 \ \& \ \neg M_{umask}$ (dirs) | octal |
| $I_{ratio}$ | Inode density | $I_{ratio} = \dfrac{S_{partition}}{4096}$ | inodes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note**: The `\&` symbol denotes the **bitwise AND** operation. The `\neg` symbol denotes the **bitwise NOT** operation. The vertical bar is replaced with `\vert` in tables to preserve markdown table integrity. When computing `umask`, remember that the kernel first applies the **maximum permission (666 for files, 777 for directories)**, then **subtracts the umask bits** using bitwise NOT + AND.

### 2.5 File System Hierarchy Standard (FHS) — Critical Directories

The FHS (currently **FHS 3.0**, maintained by the Linux Foundation) defines the directory structure. Forensic examiners must navigate this hierarchy with absolute fluency.

| Path | FHS Classification | Contents | Forensic Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| `/` | Root of the entire tree | All other directories | Mount point of the root partition |
| `/bin` | Essential user binaries | `ls`, `cp`, `mv`, `cat`, `chmod` | Must be available in single-user mode |
| `/sbin` | Essential system binaries | `fsck`, `mkfs`, `ifconfig`, `iptables` | System administration commands |
| `/etc` | Host-specific configuration | `passwd`, `shadow`, `fstab`, `hosts` | **CRITICAL — contains user accounts and network config** |
| `/home` | User home directories | `~/.bash_history`, user documents | **CRITICAL — primary evidence source for user activity** |
| `/root` | Root user home | `.bash_history` of root | Privileged user command history |
| `/var` | Variable data | `/var/log` (system logs), `/var/mail`, `/var/spool` | **CRITICAL — system logs (syslog, auth.log)** |
| `/var/log` | System logs | `auth.log`, `syslog`, `kern.log`, `wtmp`, `utmp` | **Highest forensic value for timeline reconstruction** |
| `/tmp` | Temporary files | Volatile artifacts, malware staging | Often cleared at boot — **examine before reboot** |
| `/proc` | Process information (virtual) | PID directories, kernel parameters | **Live system state, no on-disk persistence** |
| `/dev` | Device files | `/dev/sda`, `/dev/null`, `/dev/random` | Direct access to raw hardware |
| `/usr` | User system resources (read-only) | `/usr/bin`, `/usr/sbin`, `/usr/lib` | Application binaries and libraries |
| `/opt` | Optional add-on software | Third-party installations | Custom application evidence |
| `/boot` | Boot loader files | `vmlinuz`, `initrd.img`, `grub/` | Kernel image, **MANDATORY for kernel-level analysis** |
| `/mnt` & `/media` | Mount points | Removable media, network mounts | Temporary file system mounts |
| `/lost+found` | Orphaned inodes | Files detached after crash | **Recovered fragments after filesystem corruption** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `/proc` and `/sys` are special**: These are **virtual (pseudo) file systems** generated entirely in memory by the kernel. They contain no files on disk. `/proc/<PID>/` contains runtime metadata of every running process — making it an invaluable live-acquisition target during incident response.

### 2.6 User and Group Identity Model

Linux identifies every principal (user or service) by a numeric **User ID (UID)** and assigns a primary **Group ID (GID)**. The mapping is stored in two critical files:

| File | Format | Permission | Contains |
|---|---|---|---|
| `/etc/passwd` | Colon-separated, 7 fields | World-readable (`-rw-r--r--`) | Username, `x` placeholder, UID, GID, GECOS, home dir, shell |
| `/etc/shadow` | Colon-separated, 9 fields | **Root-only** (`-rw-------`) | Username, hashed password, last change, min/max age, warning, inactivity, expiration, reserved |
| `/etc/group` | Colon-separated, 4 fields | World-readable | Group name, `x`, GID, member list |
| `/etc/gshadow` | Colon-separated, 4 fields | **Root-only** | Group passwords (rarely used) |

**Format of `/etc/passwd` entry (7 colon-separated fields):**
```
username : password_placeholder : UID : GID : GECOS : home_directory : login_shell
```

Example line:
```
forensic:x:1001:1001:Forensic Investigator:/home/forensic:/bin/bash
```

### 2.7 Real-World Forensic Utility

| Domain | How Linux Concepts Are Applied |
|---|---|
| **Incident Response** | Mounting suspect disk images read-only with `mount -o ro,noexec,nodev` |
| **Timeline Analysis** | Parsing `/var/log/*` and `~/.bash_history` for the **Body File** (mactime) format used by The Sleuth Kit |
| **Memory Forensics** | Using `/proc/iomem`, `/proc/kcore` and `LiME` to dump RAM |
| **Network Forensics** | Reading `/proc/net/tcp`, `/proc/net/arp` for live connection state |
| **Malware Analysis** | Quarantining binaries in `/var/lib/quarantine/` and analyzing ELF headers in `/usr/bin` |
| **Data Recovery** | `extundelete`, `photorec`, `testdisk` for inode-level recovery from damaged partitions |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Octal Permission Conversion (Symbolic → Octal → Decimal)

A common KTU question asks: *"Given the symbolic permission string `-rwxr-x--x`, compute the octal representation and explain the meaning of each bit."*

**Step 1: Parse the symbolic string into the three classes.**

The 10-character permission string is parsed as:
- Position 1: file type (`-` = regular file, `d` = directory, `l` = symlink, `c` = char device, `b` = block device, `p` = pipe, `s` = socket)
- Positions 2–4: **User class** (owner)
- Positions 5–7: **Group class**
- Positions 8–10: **Other class** (world)

For `-rwxr-x--x`:
- User  = `rwx` → bits (1, 1, 1)
- Group = `r-x` → bits (1, 0, 1)
- Other = `--x` → bits (0, 0, 1)

**Step 2: Convert each class to its octal digit using the formula $U = 4r + 2w + 1x$.**

$$
U = 4(1) + 2(1) + 1(1) = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7
$$

$$
G = 4(1) + 2(0) + 1(1) = 4 + 0 + 1 = 5
$$

$$
O = 4(0) + 2(0) + 1(1) = 0 + 0 + 1 = 1
$$

**Step 3: Concatenate the three octal digits left-to-right (User, Group, Other).**

$$
P_{octal} = U \cdot 10^{2} + G \cdot 10^{1} + O \cdot 10^{0} = 7 \cdot 100 + 5 \cdot 10 + 1 = 751
$$

**Step 4: (Optional) Convert the full 9-bit binary vector to decimal for validation.**

$$
B = (111101001)_2 = 1 \cdot 2^{8} + 1 \cdot 2^{7} + 1 \cdot 2^{6} + 1 \cdot 2^{5} + 0 \cdot 2^{4} + 1 \cdot 2^{3} + 0 \cdot 2^{2} + 0 \cdot 2^{1} + 1 \cdot 2^{0}
$$

$$
B = 256 + 128 + 64 + 32 + 0 + 8 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 489_{10}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion Logic**: The octal form (`751`) is **NOT the decimal form (`489`)**. Octal `751` represents a 9-bit permission vector, while decimal `489` is the same vector evaluated in base 10. KTU examiners often give partial credit for stating both forms.

**Step 5: Interpret each special class bit (SUID, SGID, Sticky).**

If the user-class execute bit is replaced by `s` (and SUID is set), the numeric value becomes:
$$
P_{SUID} = (4 \cdot U + S_{set}) \cdot 64 + G \cdot 8 + O
$$
where $S_{set} = 4$ if SUID is on, $2$ if SGID is on, $1$ if sticky is on.

Example: `4755` = SUID set, owner `rwx`, group `r-x`, other `r-x`. Executable runs as the file owner.

### 3.2 Derivation B — Default Permission Calculation Using `umask`

**Step 1: Identify the maximum default permission set by the kernel.**

For files: $M_{max}^{file} = 666$ (`rw-rw-rw-`)
For directories: $M_{max}^{dir} = 777$ (`rwxrwxrwx`)

**Step 2: Convert the umask to a binary NOT-mask.**

Assume `umask = 022`. The binary form is:
$$
\text{umask} = 022 = (010 \ 010)_{2}
$$

$$
\neg \text{umask} = (101 \ 101)_{2} = 755_{8}
$$

**Step 3: Apply bitwise AND between the maximum mask and the negated umask.**

For files:
$$
M_{default}^{file} = 666 \ \& \ \neg(022) = 666 \ \& \ 755
$$

Convert to binary and align:
$$
666 = (110 \ 110 \ 110)_{2}, \quad 755 = (111 \ 101 \ 101)_{2}
$$

Bitwise AND:
$$
\begin{aligned}
110 \ \& \ 111 &= 110 \\
110 \ \& \ 101 &= 100 \\
110 \ \& \ 101 &= 100
\end{aligned}
$$

Result: $(110 \ 100 \ 100)_{2} = 644_{8} \rightarrow \texttt{rw-r--r--}$

**Step 4: Repeat for directories.**

$$
M_{default}^{dir} = 777 \ \& \ 755 = 755_{8} \rightarrow \texttt{rwxr-xr-x}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why files start at 666, not 777**: The kernel intentionally omits the execute bit from the maximum file permission for **security reasons** — newly created files should not be executable by default. The `umask` is the second layer of restriction applied by the user environment.

### 3.3 Derivation C — UID Classification and Forensic Implication

**Step 1: Map a given UID to its class.**

$$
\text{class}(UID) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{root} & \text{if } UID = 0 \\
\text{system} & \text{if } 1 \le UID \le 999 \\
\text{regular} & \text{if } 1000 \le UID \le 60000 \\
\text{service} & \text{if } 60001 \le UID \le 65533 \\
\text{nobody/anon} & \text{if } UID = 65534 \\
\text{unset} & \text{if } UID = 65535
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 2: Worked example.**

The UID `33` is encountered in `/etc/passwd`:
$$
1 \le 33 \le 999 \implies \text{class} = \text{system}
$$

The UID `1001` is encountered:
$$
1000 \le 1001 \le 60000 \implies \text{class} = \text{regular}
$$

**Step 3: Forensic interpretation.**

A file owned by UID `33` is almost certainly owned by the `www-data` service account (Apache/Nginx default). Activity under this UID indicates a **web-server process**, not a human user — a critical distinction when attributing intrusion actions.

### 3.4 Derivation D — Inode Density and File-Capacity Calculation

**Step 1: Compute the maximum number of inodes for a partition.**

$$
N_{inodes} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{S_{bytes}}{R_{bytes\_per\_inode}} \right\rfloor
$$

For a **10 GB** partition with the default 1-inode-per-4096-byte ratio:
$$
N_{inodes} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{10 \times 2^{30}}{4096} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{10737418240}{4096} \right\rfloor = 2621440
$$

**Step 2: Compute the storage overhead of the inode table.**

Each ext4 inode occupies **256 bytes** by default:
$$
S_{inode\_table} = N_{inodes} \times 256 = 2621440 \times 256 = 671088640 \text{ bytes} \approx 640 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 3: Forensic implication.**

If a partition shows 100% inode usage but only 50% data usage, the partition is **inode-exhausted** — typical of attacks that create millions of small files (e.g., `mkdir` bombs, fork bombs, or mass-log injection).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Boot Process Stage-by-Stage Timeline

The Linux boot sequence is a **deterministic, ordered pipeline**. Each stage produces forensic artifacts.

| Stage | Component | Action | Forensic Artifact | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **BIOS/UEFI** | Power-On Self-Test (POST), locate MBR/GPT | CMOS/NVRAM, boot order | Hardware fault → beep codes |
| 2 | **Bootloader (GRUB2)** | Load kernel + initrd to RAM | `/boot/grub/grub.cfg` (tamper evidence) | Kernel panic if misconfigured |
| 3 | **Kernel** | Decompress, initialize hardware, mount root FS as read-only | `dmesg` log, `/var/log/kern.log` | "Kernel panic — not syncing" |
| 4 | **init / systemd (PID 1)** | First user-space process; starts all services | `/var/log/syslog`, `journalctl` | Service start failure |
| 5 | **getty / display manager** | Spawn login prompts (TTY) | `/var/log/wtmp`, `/var/log/auth.log` | Login authentication failure |
| 6 | **User shell** | Read `~/.bashrc`, present prompt | `~/.bash_history` | History tampering (note timestamps) |

**Cumulative forensic timeline (Equation form):**

$$
T_{boot} = T_{BIOS} + T_{GRUB} + T_{kernel} + T_{init} + T_{getty} + T_{shell}
$$

Forensic investigators reconstruct $T_{boot}$ by **parsing `/var/log/dmesg` and `journalctl --list-boots`**, then cross-referencing with `wtmp` login records to establish the chain of custody from power-on to user interaction.

### 3.6 Comprehensive Formula Recap Table

| # | Formula | Domain | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U = 4r + 2w + x$ | File permissions | Convert rwx triplet to octal digit |
| 2 | $P_{octal} = 64U + 8G + O$ | File permissions | Concatenate three octal digits |
| 3 | $S = 4 \cdot SUID + 2 \cdot SGID + 1 \cdot Sticky$ | Special bits | Compute special-bit contribution |
| 4 | $M_{default} = M_{max} \ \& \ \neg(umask)$ | umask | Compute default permissions |
| 5 | $N_{inodes} = \lfloor S_{partition} / R_{ratio} \rfloor$ | File system | Maximum file count per partition |
| 6 | $S_{itable} = N_{inodes} \times 256$ | File system | Inode table storage overhead |
| 7 | $T_{unix} = \text{seconds since epoch}$ | Timestamps | Convert date to integer timestamp |
| 8 | $P_{PID_{root}} = 1$ (always) | Process tree | PID 1 is the init/systemd parent |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Layered Linux Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userA[User Applications]
    shellA[Shell bash zsh sh]
    syslibA[GNU C Library glibc]
    kernelA[Linux Kernel]
    hwA[Hardware CPU RAM Disk NIC]
    arrow1[syscalls ring transition]
    arrow2[device drivers LKMs]
    userA --> shellA
    shellA --> syslibA
    syslibA --> arrow1
    arrow1 --> kernelA
    kernelA --> arrow2
    arrow2 --> hwA
```

**Reading guide:** Every rectangle is a logical layer; arrows show the **direction of request** (top → bottom) and the **direction of result** (bottom → top). The `arrow1` edge represents the **ring-3 to ring-0 transition** triggered by a syscall instruction; the `arrow2` edge represents the kernel reaching hardware registers through a device driver.

### 4.2 Linux Boot Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pwrA[Power On POST]
    biosA[BIOS or UEFI Firmware]
    grubA[GRUB2 Bootloader Stage 1 1.5 2]
    kernA[Linux Kernel vmlinuz load]
    initrdA[initrd or initramfs mount temporary root]
    mountA[Mount real root filesystem as RW]
    initA[init or systemd PID 1 spawn]
    svcA[Start system services targets]
    gettyA[getty spawn on TTYs]
    loginA[Login prompt or Display Manager]
    pwrA --> biosA
    biosA --> grubA
    grubA --> kernA
    kernA --> initrdA
    initrdA --> mountA
    mountA --> initA
    initA --> svcA
    svcA --> gettyA
    gettyA --> loginA
```

### 4.3 File System Hierarchy Standard (FHS) Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootA[Root /]
    binA[bin essential user binaries]
    sbinA[sbin essential system binaries]
    etcA[etc host configuration]
    homeA[home user data]
    root2A[root root user home]
    varA[var variable data]
    logA[var log system logs]
    procA[proc virtual process info]
    sysA[sys virtual kernel info]
    tmpA[tmp temporary files]
    devA[dev device files]
    usrA[usr user system resources]
    optA[opt optional software]
    bootA[boot boot loader files]
    mntA[mnt media mount points]
    lostA[lost+found recovered fragments]
    rootA --> binA
    rootA --> sbinA
    rootA --> etcA
    rootA --> homeA
    rootA --> root2A
    rootA --> varA
    varA --> logA
    rootA --> procA
    rootA --> sysA
    rootA --> tmpA
    rootA --> devA
    rootA --> usrA
    rootA --> optA
    rootA --> bootA
    rootA --> mntA
    rootA --> lostA
```

### 4.4 Process Tree (PID / PPID Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pid1A[PID 1 systemd or init]
    pid100A[PID 100 sshd]
    pid250A[PID 250 bash]
    pid320A[PID 320 sshd child]
    pid421A[PID 421 bash user]
    pid500A[PID 500 grep command]
    pid1A --> pid100A
    pid100A --> pid250A
    pid250A --> pid320A
    pid320A --> pid421A
    pid421A --> pid500A
```

**Reading guide:** `PPID(250) = 100`, `PPID(320) = 250`, etc. Investigators traverse the tree using `pstree -p` and capture it with `ps auxf` for the volatile evidence file.

### 4.5 Permission Bit Anatomy (Octal Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bitR[r bit 4 read]
    bitW[w bit 2 write]
    bitX[x bit 1 execute]
    sumA[Octal digit 0 to 7]
    bitR --> sumA
    bitW --> sumA
    bitX --> sumA
```

For example: `rwx = 4+2+1 = 7`; `r-x = 4+0+1 = 5`; `--x = 0+0+1 = 1`.
