## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the Linux Kernel. Differentiate between monolithic and microkernel architectures. State which architecture Linux uses and justify.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The **Linux Kernel** is the core component of the Linux operating system. It is the central program that manages system resources (CPU scheduling, memory allocation, device drivers, file system management, and inter-process communication) and acts as a bridge between user applications and physical hardware.

**Monolithic vs. Microkernel (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Monolithic Kernel | Microkernel |
|---|---|---|
| Design | Single large program running in kernel space | Minimal core; most services in user space |
| Performance | Faster (no context switches for in-kernel services) | Slower (heavy IPC overhead) |
| Stability | A bug in one driver can crash the whole system | Driver crashes are isolated |
| Size | Large binary image | Small binary, extensible |
| Examples | Linux, Unix, OpenBSD | Minix, QNX, Hurd, macOS (hybrid) |

**Linux's Architecture (0.5 mark):** Linux uses a **monolithic architecture with a modular design**. Although it is monolithic (all core services run in kernel space for performance), it supports **Loadable Kernel Modules (LKMs)** which can be inserted or removed at runtime — giving it the extensibility of a microkernel without sacrificing speed.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Explain the File System Hierarchy Standard (FHS). List any six critical directories under the root (/) and state one forensic significance of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**FHS Definition (0.5 mark):** The **File System Hierarchy Standard (FHS)** is a Linux Foundation-maintained specification (currently version 3.0) that defines the directory structure and directory contents in a Unix-like operating system. It ensures software predictability across distributions.

**Six Critical Directories (2.5 marks — 0.25 for each directory + 0.15 for forensic significance):**

| Directory | Forensic Significance |
|---|---|
| `/etc` | Contains `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow`, `/etc/sudoers` — **user credentials and privilege info** |
| `/var/log` | Contains `auth.log`, `syslog`, `kern.log`, `wtmp` — **timeline of system and user events** |
| `/home` | Contains user files and `~/.bash_history` — **direct evidence of user activity** |
| `/tmp` | Contains volatile artifacts; often cleared at reboot — **examine before shutdown** |
| `/proc` | Virtual FS exposing live process state — **volatile live-acquisition target** |
| `/boot` | Contains `vmlinuz` and `initrd.img` — **kernel integrity and boot-time tampering** |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 MARKS)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Main, Module 4, Choice A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Linux boot process in detail. List the six stages from power-on to user login, and describe the forensic artifacts generated at each stage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, explain the Linux permission model in detail. Convert the symbolic permission string `drwxr-x---` to its octal form and describe the meaning of each special bit (SUID, SGID, Sticky).

---

**Model Answer — Q A (a) [7 Marks]**

The Linux boot process is a six-stage deterministic pipeline:

**Stage 1 — Power-On and POST (0.5 mark):**
When power is applied, the motherboard firmware (**BIOS** for legacy systems, **UEFI** for modern systems) executes the **Power-On Self-Test (POST)**. POST validates the integrity of RAM, CPU, and basic peripherals. The firmware then consults the configured boot order and locates the first bootable device.
*Forensic artifact:* CMOS/NVRAM settings, beep codes on failure.

**Stage 2 — Bootloader (GRUB2) (1 mark):**
The **Grand Unified Bootloader version 2 (GRUB2)** is loaded from the **Master Boot Record (MBR)** in legacy BIOS or the **EFI System Partition (ESP)** in UEFI. GRUB2 has three sub-stages: **Stage 1** (512-byte MBR code), **Stage 1.5** (filesystem-aware code in the MBR gap), and **Stage 2** (full loader with menu, located in `/boot/grub/`). It loads the kernel image (`vmlinuz`) and the initial RAM disk (`initrd.img` or `initramfs`) into memory.
*Forensic artifact:* `/boot/grub/grub.cfg` is the source of truth for boot parameters. Any modification here is a **boot-time tampering red flag**.

**Stage 3 — Kernel Initialization (1.5 marks):**
The kernel image is decompressed and executed. It immediately initializes:
- CPU scheduling subsystem
- Memory management (page tables, virtual memory)
- Device drivers (loaded from initrd or built-in)
- Mounts the **initial root filesystem** (the initrd) which contains modules needed to access the real root device.
*Forensic artifact:* The kernel ring buffer is exposed via the `dmesg` command and persisted to `/var/log/kern.log`. Investigators use `journalctl -k` to view kernel boot messages including USB insertions, NIC link events, and disk errors.

**Stage 4 — Mount Real Root Filesystem and init (1.5 marks):**
Once the real root device is identified, the kernel hands control to the **init process** — historically `/sbin/init`, now usually `/lib/systemd/systemd`. This is **always PID 1**. systemd parses `/etc/fstab`, mounts all filesystems, and starts the configured services (called *targets* or *runlevels*).
*Forensic artifact:* `/var/log/syslog`, `journalctl --list-boots`, and the boot ID in `/proc/sys/kernel/random/boot_id`.

**Stage 5 — getty and TTY Spawn (1 mark):**
`systemd` spawns **`getty`** processes on each configured virtual terminal (TTY1–TTY6) and a **display manager** (`gdm`, `lightdm`, `sddm`) on TTY7. `getty` presents the login prompt and listens for username input.
*Forensic artifact:* Each successful and failed login is recorded in `/var/log/wtmp` (binary, parsed with `last`) and `/var/log/auth.log` (text, parsed with `grep`).

**Stage 6 — User Shell (1.5 marks):**
After successful authentication, `getty` is replaced by the user's login shell (e.g., `/bin/bash`). The shell reads the user's profile files in order: `/etc/profile`, `/etc/bash.bashrc`, `~/.bash_profile`, `~/.bashrc`. The user is now interactive.
*Forensic artifact:* Every command issued by the user is appended to `~/.bash_history` (unless `HISTCONTROL=ignorespace` is set). The shell's PID, parent PID, and start time are recorded in `/proc/<PID>/stat` and the **audit subsystem** (`/var/log/audit/audit.log`).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Many students write only "**Linux boots and reaches the login screen**". KTU examiners award the full 7 marks **only** to answers that name the specific boot artifacts (`grub.cfg`, `dmesg`, `wtmp`, `auth.log`, `bash_history`) at each stage. **Vague answers will lose 3–4 marks.**

---

**Model Answer — Q A (b) [7 Marks]**

**The Linux Permission Model (3 marks):**
Every file and directory in Linux is governed by **three classes of principals**:
1. **User (u)** — the single owner (identified by UID)
2. **Group (g)** — a set of users identified by GID
3. **Other (o)** — everyone else on the system

Each class has **three permission bits**:
- **Read (r)** — value **4**; permits viewing file contents or listing directory entries
- **Write (w)** — value **2**; permits modifying file or adding/removing directory entries
- **Execute (x)** — value **1**; permits executing the file as a program or traversing (`cd`) into the directory

The permission string has **10 characters**: 1 for file type + 9 for permissions (3 classes × 3 bits).

**Worked Conversion of `drwxr-x---` (3 marks):**

| Step | Operation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Parse file type | `d` | Directory |
| User class | `rwx` | $4(1) + 2(1) + 1(1) = 7$ |
| Group class | `r-x` | $4(1) + 2(0) + 1(1) = 5$ |
| Other class | `---` | $4(0) + 2(0) + 1(0) = 0$ |
| **Octal form** | `7 5 0` | **750** |

**Verification using the base-8 to base-2 vector:**
$$
750_{8} = (111 \ 101 \ 000)_{2} = 1 \cdot 2^{8} + 1 \cdot 2^{7} + 1 \cdot 2^{6} + 1 \cdot 2^{5} + 0 \cdot 2^{4} + 1 \cdot 2^{3} + 0 + 0 = 488_{10}
$$

> **Note**: The octal `750` and decimal `488` are *equivalent representations of the same 9-bit permission vector*, not different permissions.

**Special Bits (1 mark):**
- **SUID (Set User ID, octal 4000):** When set on an executable, the process runs with the **file owner's** privileges. Example: `/usr/bin/passwd` has SUID root so users can change their own password.
- **SGID (Set Group ID, octal 2000):** When set on an executable, the process runs with the **file group's** privileges. When set on a directory, all files created within inherit the directory's group.
- **Sticky Bit (octal 1000):** When set on a directory, only the file owner (or root) can delete or rename files within. Example: `/tmp` has the sticky bit so users cannot delete each other's temporary files.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: A common KTU mistake is writing "`chmod 750`" without stating that this is an **octal** (base-8) representation. Examiners expect students to say "*750 in base 8*" or "*(111 101 000)₂*". **Forgetting the SUID/SGID/Sticky explanation loses 1 full mark.**

---

**QUESTION B (14 MARKS) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Main, Module 4, Choice B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the layered architecture of the Linux Operating System with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the Shell and the Kernel in process management.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the major Linux file systems (ext2, ext3, ext4, XFS, Btrfs). Compare them on the basis of journaling, maximum file size, and forensic recoverability.

---

**Model Answer — Q B (a) [7 Marks]**

*(Note: Provide the same diagram as in SECTION 4.1 and elaborate.)*

**Layered Architecture (3.5 marks):**
1. **Hardware Layer** — Physical components (CPU, RAM, Disk, NIC).
2. **Kernel Layer** — The core of the OS; runs in privileged mode (Ring 0). Manages process scheduling, memory, file system, networking, and device drivers. Communicates with hardware through device drivers and exposes a uniform **system call interface** to user space.
3. **Shell Layer** — A command interpreter (e.g., bash) that reads user input, parses it, and translates each command into a sequence of system calls. The shell supports scripting, I/O redirection, pipes, and environment variables.
4. **Application Layer** — User programs (compilers, browsers, forensic tools) that consume the APIs provided by the shell and the C library.

**Role of the Kernel in Process Management (2 marks):**
The kernel maintains a **process table** in memory. Each process is represented by a **`task_struct`** in C, which contains:
- **PID** (Process Identifier) — unique integer
- **PPID** (Parent Process Identifier) — for tree reconstruction
- **State** — running, sleeping, zombie, stopped
- **Open file descriptors**
- **Memory maps** (page tables)
- **CPU register context** (for context switching)

The kernel's **scheduler** uses algorithms like **CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler)** in modern Linux to allocate CPU time slices. Investigators dump this table via `/proc/[0-9]*` for live analysis.

**Role of the Shell in Process Management (1.5 marks):**
The shell is the **primary interface for process creation**. When a user types `firefox &`:
1. The shell calls `fork()` — duplicating the current process.
2. The child process calls `execve()` — replacing its memory image with Firefox.
3. The shell's `wait()` system call lets the parent wait for the child to finish.

The shell also provides **job control** (foreground/background), **signals** (Ctrl-C → SIGINT), and **shell history** — the latter being a critical forensic artifact.

---

**Model Answer — Q B (b) [7 Marks]**

**Major Linux File Systems (5 marks):**

| File System | Introduced | Journaling | Max File Size | Max Volume Size | Forensic Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ext2** | 1993 | No | 2 TiB | 32 TiB | No journal = slow `fsck` after crash; old but stable |
| **ext3** | 2001 | Yes (full, ordered, writeback) | 2 TiB | 32 TiB | Backward-compatible with ext2; journal can be examined for timeline |
| **ext4** | 2008 | Yes (with checksums since 6.7) | 16 TiB | 1 EiB | Default on most distros; **extent-based**; supports huge files and fast `fsck` |
| **XFS** | 1994 (SGI) | Yes (metadata only) | 8 EiB | 8 EiB | Excellent for large files; cannot shrink volume; RHEL default |
| **Btrfs** | 2009 (Oracle) | Yes (CoW — Copy on Write) | 16 EiB | 16 EiB | Built-in snapshots, checksums, subvolumes; still maturing |
| **ZFS** | 2005 (Sun) | Yes (CoW) | 16 EiB | 256 ZiB (theoretical) | CDDL license; not in mainline kernel; widely used in NAS |

**Comparison Summary (2 marks):**

| Criterion | Winner | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Journaling for crash recovery | ext4, XFS, Btrfs | Faster `fsck`; better data integrity |
| Maximum file size | XFS / Btrfs (8–16 EiB) | Enterprise-scale storage |
| Forensic recoverability | ext4 | Best tooling support (`extundelete`, `debugfs`, `The Sleuth Kit`) |
| Anti-tampering (CoW) | Btrfs / ZFS | Snapshots preserve prior state — invaluable for forensic preservation |
| Production stability | ext4 / XFS | Battle-tested in RHEL and Debian |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Students often confuse **journaling** (writes a log of pending changes for crash recovery) with **encryption** (LUKS/dm-crypt) and with **CoW (Copy-on-Write)**. Examiners will award full marks **only** to answers that explicitly distinguish:
> - **Journaling** = metadata log for crash recovery
> - **CoW** = never overwrite live data blocks; instead write to a new block
> - **Encryption** = cryptographic confidentiality, separate from integrity

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Linux is GNU + Kernel** — never say "Linux" alone when you mean the operating system; say **GNU/Linux** for full credit.
- [x] **Linux uses a monolithic kernel with modular design** — LKMs are loaded with `modprobe` and listed in `/proc/modules`.
- [x] **Three permission classes (u, g, o) × three bits (r, w, x) = 9 bits** → octal range `[000, 777]`.
- [x] **`chmod 755` means octal**, not decimal. Write it as **`0755`** in shell scripts to avoid base-confusion bugs.
- [x] **UID 0 = root**, UID 1–999 = system accounts, UID 1000+ = regular users. **This is a KTU-favorite question.**
- [x] **FHS version 3.0** is the current standard. Memorize: `/etc` (config), `/var` (variable), `/home` (user data), `/proc` (virtual).
- [x] **PID 1 is always init/systemd** — the parent of all user-space processes.
- [x] **Boot process = BIOS → GRUB2 → Kernel → initrd → root mount → systemd → getty → shell**. Each stage leaves a unique artifact.
- [x] **Forensic must-read log files**: `/var/log/auth.log`, `/var/log/syslog`, `/var/log/wtmp`, `/var/log/kern.log`, `~/.bash_history`.
- [x] **`umask` calculation** uses bitwise AND with the NOT of the umask value applied to the max permission (`666` for files, `777` for dirs).
- [x] **Linux epoch** = `1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC`. Unix timestamps count seconds (or milliseconds in `date +%s%3N`).
- [x] **Special bits**: SUID = 4, SGID = 2, Sticky = 1. They occupy the **thousands place** in the octal (e.g., `4755` = SUID root + `rwxr-xr-x`).
- [x] **Inode exhaustion** is a forensic indicator of attacks (mkdir bombs, log floods). One inode per **4096 bytes** by default.
- [x] **Forensic distros to remember**: Kali, CAINE, DEFT, SANS SIFT, BlackArch.
- [x] **Inode number 2 is always the root directory (`/`)**, inode 11 is typically the `lost+found` directory in ext2/3/4.
- [x] **`/proc` and `/sys` are virtual** — no on-disk storage; always examine in a **live** system, never after reboot.
- [x] **Hidden files** (those starting with `.`, like `.bash_history`) require `ls -a` or `find` to enumerate — KTU examiners test this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modern Linux Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modern Linux Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Modern Linux Systems** in the context of digital forensics refers to the contemporary ecosystem of Linux-based operating systems — including server distributions (Ubuntu Server, Red Hat Enterprise Linux, CentOS, Debian, Fedora), desktop environments (Ubuntu Desktop, Linux Mint, Fedora Workstation), mobile/embedded derivatives (Android, OpenWrt), cloud/container platforms (CoreOS, Flatcar, Alpine), and forensic-focused live distributions (Kali Linux, CAINE, SIFT, DEFT, Tsurugi) — that are deployed across enterprise, IoT, cloud, and investigative environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination Standard):**
> *A Modern Linux System is a UNIX-like, POSIX-compliant, open-source operating system kernel-based platform that uses contemporary filesystem hierarchies (FHS 3.0), journaling filesystems (ext4, XFS, Btrfs, ZFS), systemd init frameworks, and modern log/event subsystems (syslog-ng, journald, auditd) — all of which leave specific, time-stamped, recoverable artifacts that form the investigative backbone of Linux forensics.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **modern office building** as your Linux system:

- **The Building Structure (Kernel & Hardware Layer)** = The foundation, elevators, and electrical wiring. Tenants (processes) live on different floors, but everything flows through this invisible infrastructure.
- **The Front Desk (systemd / init)** = The receptionist who tracks who enters, who leaves, what services are open, and at what time.
- **The Filing Cabinets (ext4, XFS, Btrfs)** = Where every document is stored. Each cabinet keeps a *journal* (a log of changes) so that if power fails mid-write, the system knows exactly what was happening.
- **The CCTV Room (auditd, syslog, journald)** = Continuously recording activity. Even if a "burglar" (attacker) tries to cover tracks, the timestamps in the CCTV logs reveal the sequence of events.
- **The Tenant Directory (/etc/passwd, /etc/shadow)** = A public list of "who lives here" and a private vault of credentials.

Forensically, a Linux system is essentially a **highly verbose witness** — it records almost everything by default, and the investigator's job is to read, correlate, and interpret those records.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Linux vs Legacy UNIX Distinction:**
> Legacy UNIX systems used static config files and simple syslogd. Modern Linux uses **dynamic service management (systemd)**, **binary indexed logs (journald)**, **capability-based security (instead of pure root)**, and **complex user namespaces (containers)**. Each of these introduces new forensic artifacts and challenges not present in classic UNIX.

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Modern Linux Systems (Forensic Relevance)

| Category | Examples | Forensic Significance |
|----------|----------|----------------------|
| **Server/Enterprise** | RHEL, CentOS Stream, Ubuntu LTS, SUSE | Production data, SSH brute-force traces, kernel audit logs |
| **Desktop** | Ubuntu Desktop, Linux Mint, Fedora | User activity, browser history, USB artifacts |
| **Forensic Live Distros** | Kali Linux, SIFT, CAINE, Tsurugi, DEFT | Pre-loaded forensic toolkit, write-blocker built-in |
| **Container/Cloud** | CoreOS, Flatcar, RancherOS, Alpine | Ephemeral storage, layered container images, cgroups |
| **Embedded/IoT** | OpenWrt, Raspbian, Yocto | Firmware acquisition, limited logging |
| **Mobile Derivatives** | Android (Linux kernel) | ADB pull, /data/data sandboxes, SQLite-heavy |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Default Locations (Recall Anchors)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Default Forensic-Critical Paths (Memorize for KTU):**
> - **$0\%$** = Root of the entire filesystem (`/`)
> - **Default User Home**: `/home/<username>/`
> - **System-wide Logs**: `/var/log/`, `/var/log/journal/`
> - **Authentication Logs**: `/var/log/auth.log` (Debian) or `/var/log/secure` (RHEL)
> - **Audit Framework**: `/var/log/audit/audit.log`
> - **Shell History**: `~/.bash_history`, `~/.zsh_history`
> - **Package Manager Logs**: `/var/log/dpkg.log`, `/var/log/yum.log`
> - **Cron Logs**: `/var/log/cron` or `/var/log/syslog`

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Modern Linux Architecture Stack

A Linux system is logically divided into concentric layers. The forensic investigator must understand **which layer an artifact originates from** because it determines the **trust level, volatility, and admissibility** of the evidence.

### Layer 1: Hardware Layer
- **Contents:** CPU, RAM, disks (HDD/SSD/NVMe), NIC, USB controllers.
- **Volatility:** **RAM is the most volatile** (lost on power-off). Disks are persistent.
- **Forensic artifact:** RAM dump, disk image, hardware-level logs (e.g., `/var/log/dmesg`, `dmidecode`).

### Layer 2: Linux Kernel Layer
- The kernel mediates all hardware interaction. Forensic interest:
  - **Kernel ring buffer** → `dmesg` output.
  - **Loadable Kernel Modules (LKMs)** → Rootkits often hide here.
  - **`/proc/`** and **`/sys/`** → Virtual filesystems exposing live kernel state.
  - **Kernel audit subsystem** → `auditd` daemon and rules in `/etc/audit/rules.d/`.

### Layer 3: System Libraries & init Framework
- Modern Linux uses **systemd** as the default init system (PID 1).
- systemd replaces legacy SysV init and provides:
  - **Service units** (`.service`) — process management.
  - **Socket units** (`.socket`) — activation-on-demand.
  - **Timer units** (`.timer`) — cron replacement.
  - **Journal** — binary indexed log storage.
- **Forensic artifact:** `journalctl` output, systemd unit file timestamps.

### Layer 4: User Space / Application Layer
- User commands, daemons, GUI applications.
- **Shell history, browser data, recently used files (`.local/share/recently-used.xbel`).**

## 2.2 The systemd Service Lifecycle (High-Yield)

systemd assigns each service a precise lifecycle, and the resulting state transitions are written to the journal. The standard sequence is:

1. **`inactive`** → Service is not running.
2. **`activating`** → Service is starting (executing ExecStartPre, ExecStart).
3. **`active (running)`** → Service is fully operational.
4. **`deactivating`** → Service is stopping.
5. **`failed`** → Service crashed or returned non-zero exit code.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** A forensic investigator examining a compromised system must always run `systemctl list-units --state=failed` to identify crashed services that may indicate an attack or faulty misconfiguration.

## 2.3 Filesystem Hierarchy Standard (FHS 3.0)

The FHS defines a strict directory layout used by all modern Linux distributions. Every forensic examiner must know what "should" be in each directory to detect anomalies (e.g., a hidden executable in `/tmp` masquerading as a config file).

| Directory | Purpose | Forensic Value |
|-----------|---------|----------------|
| `/bin` | Essential user binaries | Compare with package manifest; detect trojaned binaries |
| `/sbin` | System administration binaries | `suid` files here are high-risk |
| `/etc` | System-wide configuration | Tampering evidence; persistence mechanisms |
| `/home` | User home directories | Personal files, shell history, SSH keys |
| `/root` | Root user's home | Hidden admin activity |
| `/var` | Variable data (logs, spool, mail) | **Primary log storage** |
| `/tmp` | Temporary files | Attackers' staging area; cleaned on reboot |
| `/dev` | Device files | Block devices, pseudo-terminals |
| `/proc` | Process & kernel info | Live process inspection |
| `/opt` | Optional/third-party software | Manual installations may evade package tracking |
| `/srv` | Service data | Web server content, FTP files |
| `/mnt`, `/media` | Mount points | Mounted external/USB drives |

## 2.4 Modern Linux Filesystems (Forensic Implications)

Modern Linux distributions use **journaling filesystems** to ensure crash recovery. The journal is itself a forensic artifact — it contains pending write operations that may not yet have been committed to the main filesystem.

| Filesystem | Default In | Key Feature | Forensic Implication |
|------------|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **ext4** | Ubuntu, Debian | Extents, journal, delayed allocation | `extundelete`, `debugfs` for recovery |
| **XFS** | RHEL, CentOS | High performance, journaling | `xfs_undelete`, `xfs_db` |
| **Btrfs** | Fedora (optional), SUSE | Copy-on-Write, snapshots | **Native snapshot forensics** (read past states) |
| **ZFS** | Ubuntu (optional), FreeBSD | Checksums, snapshots | Strong integrity guarantees |
| **Swap** | All | Memory overflow | **Critical evidence source** — swap may contain RAM pages |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** In ext4 with default settings, deleted file metadata is recoverable from journal replay **only if** the journal has not been overwritten. Always image the disk BEFORE booting the suspect system (Live CD/USB boot only).

## 2.5 The Linux Audit Framework (auditd)

The audit framework is the **cornerstone of forensic-grade Linux logging**. It is configured via rules in `/etc/audit/rules.d/` and produces timestamped records in `/var/log/audit/audit.log`.

### 2.5.1 Core Audit Components

- **`auditd`** → Background daemon that writes audit records.
- **`auditctl`** → Runtime rule control.
- **`ausearch`** → Searches the audit log.
- **`aureport`** → Generates summary reports.
- **`autrace`** → Traces a specific process's syscalls.

### 2.5.2 High-Yield Audit Rule Syntax (Examination Favorite)

The general audit rule format is:

$$
\text{auditctl} \; {-}{-}
$$

with the parameters in the cheat sheet below.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Forensics:** auditd records **syscalls** (system calls), not user-space events. This means even if a process is deleted from disk, auditd can prove it executed. The trail is **kernel-level** and cannot be easily forged without root, and even then, kernel modules can be loaded to forge them.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| Component / Metric | Standard Value / Path / Syntax | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Filesystem root | `/` | Logical top of FHS |
| Default home prefix | `/home` | Per-user subdirectories |
| syslog daemon | `rsyslog` or `syslog-ng` | Default on RHEL/Debian |
| systemd journal binary | `/var/log/journal/` | Persistent across reboots |
| Authentication log (Debian) | `/var/log/auth.log` | Plaintext, human-readable |
| Authentication log (RHEL) | `/var/log/secure` | Plaintext |
| Audit log | `/var/log/audit/audit.log` | Stamped with `audit(epoch_time:serial)` |
| Time synchronization | **NTP** (chrony, systemd-timesyncd) | Critical for log correlation |
| Standard UID for root | **0** | Never assign to non-root |
| First regular UID | **1000** (most distros) | Indicates human user |
| SUID bit value | **4000** (octal) | Privilege escalation vector |
| SGID bit value | **2000** (octal) | Group privilege inheritance |
| Sticky bit value | **1000** (octal) | Restricts deletion in shared dirs (e.g., `/tmp`) |
| MAC time acronym | **Modified, Accessed, Changed** (inode), **Birth** (creation) | `stat` command reveals all four |
| Block size (typical) | **4096 bytes** (4 KiB) | Default for ext4 |
| Inode ratio | **1 inode per 16 KiB** (default) | Small files may exhaust inodes before space |
| Kernel audit buffer | `audit_backlog_limit=320` (kernel parameter) | Messages dropped if exceeded |

### 2.6.1 The MACB Time Model (Crucial for Examinations)

In Linux, files have **four** timestamps (unlike Windows which has only three):

- **Modified (mtime)** → Last write to file content.
- **Accessed (atime)** → Last read of file content.
- **Changed (ctime)** → Last change to inode (metadata OR content).
- **Birth (crtime / btime)** → File creation time (ext4 with `statx`, Btrfs, XFS).

$$
T_{\text{file}} = \{mtime, \; atime, \; ctime, \; btime\}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Implication:** If an attacker uses `touch -t` to modify `mtime`, the `ctime` will still record the **actual** modification because `ctime` cannot be directly forged without changing the inode (which `touch -t` does, actually updating ctime to "now"). True anti-forensics requires disabling `atime` writes (mount option `noatime`) — a telltale sign of suspicious configuration.

## 2.7 Real-World Forensic Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Incident Response** | auditd + journald correlation identifies lateral movement |
| **Malware Analysis** | `/proc/<pid>/maps` reveals injected shared libraries |
| **Cloud Forensics** | Container images preserve filesystem at point of build |
| **Data Recovery** | ext4 journal + undeletion tools recover user-deleted files |
| **Insider Threat** | Shell history, last login (`last`, `lastb`), SSH authorized_keys |
| **Compliance Auditing** | PCI-DSS, HIPAA mandate specific auditd rules |
| **E-Discovery** | Mail spools in `/var/mail`, IMAP caches in home directories |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Calculating Inode Usage from a Filesystem Image

A frequently-asked forensic question: *"Given a filesystem image of size $S$ bytes with block size $B$ bytes and inode ratio $R$ bytes per inode, derive the maximum number of inodes $N$."*

### Step 1: Establish the Definitions

Let:
- $S$ = Total size of the filesystem in bytes.
- $B$ = Block size in bytes (typically **4096** = $4$ KiB for ext4).
- $R$ = Bytes per inode (default **16384** = $16$ KiB for ext4).
- $N$ = Total number of inodes allocated.

### Step 2: Express Inode Density

The filesystem reserves $1$ inode for every $R$ bytes of storage. Therefore, the **inode density** $\rho$ (inodes per byte) is:

$$
\rho = \frac{1}{R} \quad \text{[inodes per byte]}
$$

### Step 3: Multiply by Total Size

$$
N = \rho \cdot S = \frac{S}{R}
$$

### Step 4: Display with the Block Size Parameter

We can also express $S$ in terms of block count $C$:

$$
S = C \cdot B
$$

Substituting:

$$
N = \frac{C \cdot B}{R}
$$

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> Suppose an examiner images a **500 GB** ext4 partition with default settings ($B = 4096$ bytes, $R = 16384$ bytes per inode). Calculate $N$.

**Solution:**

First, convert $S$ to bytes:

$$
S = 500 \;\text{GB} \times 1024^3 \;\text{bytes/GB} = 500 \times 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 = 5.3687 \times 10^{11} \;\text{bytes}
$$

Now apply the formula:

$$
N = \frac{S}{R} = \frac{5.3687 \times 10^{11}}{1.6384 \times 10^{4}}
$$

Breaking this down:

$$
N = \frac{5.3687 \times 10^{11}}{1.6384 \times 10^{4}} = 3.277 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 32{,}768{,}000 \;\text{inodes (approximately 32.77 million)}}
$$

### Step 6: Forensic Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters:** If the original filesystem has $N \approx 32.77$ million inodes, and after imaging and analysis the recovered filesystem shows a much smaller $N$, the discrepancy indicates either **sparse allocation** (containers, snapshots) or **intentional inode exhaustion** — a denial-of-service vector where attackers flood `/tmp` with empty files to exhaust inodes and crash the system.

## 3.2 Derivation: The MACB Timestamp Anomaly Detection

A core forensic skill is **detecting timestamp forgery**. Consider the following rule:

> **Rule:** If an attacker modifies `mtime` using `touch -t YYYYMMDDhhmm.ss`, then the resulting timestamps will satisfy a specific detectable relationship.

### Step 1: State the True Modification Time

Let $T_m$ = actual moment of file modification (system clock at the time of the write).

### Step 2: State the Forged Modification Time

Let $T_f$ = the value supplied to `touch -t` (the desired fake time).

### Step 3: Examine ctime Behavior

When `touch -t` executes, it calls `utimensat()`, which **does update ctime** to the current system time $T_{now}$. The ctime cannot be set to an arbitrary value without root + specific kernel bypass.

Therefore:

$$
ctime_{post} = T_{now} \neq T_f
$$

### Step 4: Detection Inequality

A timestamp is **suspicious** if:

$$
\vert ctime_{post} - mtime_{post} \vert > \Delta_{threshold}
$$

where $\Delta_{threshold}$ is a contextually chosen tolerance (e.g., **$30$ seconds** for routine activity, **$86400$ seconds** for the classic anti-forensic touch).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concrete Example (KTU-Style):**
> A file is found with `mtime = 2020-01-01 00:00:00` but `ctime = 2024-11-15 14:32:08`. The difference is approximately **$1{,}844$ days**. The ctime postdates the mtime by years — physically impossible in normal use. **Conclusion: Anti-forensic timestamp manipulation detected.**

## 3.3 Derivation: Audit Log Capacity Planning

For high-traffic systems, the auditor must ensure the audit log does not overflow. Given:

- $E$ = Average audit events per second.
- $S_e$ = Average event size in bytes (typically **$89$ bytes** for a syscall record).
- $R_{rotate}$ = Rotation size in MB.

**Step 1:** Calculate bytes per second:

$$
B_{sec} = E \cdot S_e
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate time to fill rotation file:

$$
T_{fill} = \frac{R_{rotate} \times 1024^2}{B_{sec}} \;\text{seconds}
$$

**Step 3:** Worked example — a busy web server generates $E = 50$ events/sec, rotation at $R_{rotate} = 10$ MB:

$$
B_{sec} = 50 \times 89 = 4450 \;\text{bytes/sec}
$$

$$
T_{fill} = \frac{10 \times 1{,}048{,}576}{4450} = \frac{10{,}485{,}760}{4450}
$$

Breaking down the division:

$$
T_{fill} = 2356.35 \;\text{seconds} \approx 39.27 \;\text{minutes}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{The audit log rotates every } \approx 39 \text{ minutes.}}
$$

**Forensic Implication:** If the investigation spans 24 hours, the analyst must examine **$\approx 36$ rotated log files** plus the active one. Investigators must configure audit log archiving (e.g., copy to secure backup) to prevent loss of earlier rotations.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cross-Distribution Artifact Mapping

The following matrix maps forensic artifacts across the three major modern Linux distribution families. This is a **high-frequency KTU question pattern**.

| Artifact | Debian / Ubuntu | RHEL / CentOS / Fedora | Arch / openSUSE |
|----------|----------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Package Manager | `apt` / `dpkg` | `yum` / `dnf` / `rpm` | `pacman` |
| Package Log | `/var/log/dpkg.log`, `/var/log/apt/history.log` | `/var/log/yum.log`, `/var/log/dnf.log` | `/var/log/pacman.log` |
| Auth Log | `/var/log/auth.log` | `/var/log/secure` | `/var/log/auth.log` (uses syslog-ng) |
| Init System | systemd | systemd | systemd |
| Default Firewall | `ufw` | `firewalld` | `nftables` |
| Default Firewall Logs | `/var/log/ufw.log` | `/var/log/firewalld` | `journald` |
| User Shell | `bash` | `bash` | `bash` (default), `zsh` common |
| Service Manager | `systemctl` | `systemctl` | `systemctl` |
| SELinux / AppArmor | AppArmor (default) | SELinux (default) | AppArmor (optional) |
| Time Sync | `systemd-timesyncd` | `chrony` | `systemd-timesyncd` |

## 3.5 Worked Forensic Scenario (Comprehensive, KTU Long-Answer Style)

> **Scenario:** A forensic investigator is handed a running server suspected of compromise. List the **ordered, volatile-first** steps to acquire evidence without contaminating the disk.

### Step 1: Document the Live System State

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227 extended):**
> 1. CPU registers, cache (rarely captured).
> 2. **Routing table, ARP cache, process table, kernel statistics, memory.**
> 3. **Temporary filesystems** (`/tmp`, `/var/run`).
> 4. **Disk** → last because persistent.

**Commands to run (in order):**

$$
\text{date} \rightarrow \text{uptime} \rightarrow \text{netstat} \;{-}\text{tulnp} \rightarrow \text{ps auxf} \rightarrow \text{mount}
$$

### Step 2: Capture Volatile Memory (RAM)

$$
\text{sudo } \texttt{avml} \; \text{memory.raw} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{sudo } \texttt{LiME} \; \text{memory.lime}
$$

### Step 3: Acquire the Audit Log and Journal

$$
\text{sudo } \texttt{ausearch} \; {-}\text{ts} \; \text{today} \; {-}\text{i} \; > \; \text{audit\_today.txt}
$$

$$
\text{sudo } \texttt{journalctl} \; {-}\text{-} \text{since=}-24h \; > \; \text{journal\_last24h.txt}
$$

### Step 4: Image the Disk (Write-Blocked)

Use `dd` with hash verification:

$$
\text{sudo } \texttt{dd} \; \text{if=/dev/sda} \; \text{of=/evidence/disk.img} \; \text{bs=4M} \; \text{conv=noerror,sync}
$$

Then verify with `sha256sum` on both source and image — they **must match**.

### Step 5: Halt the System

**Never** perform a normal shutdown. Pull the power (or `echo o > /proc/sysrq-trigger`) to preserve ephemeral state in memory that would otherwise be lost during graceful shutdown.

### Step 6: Analyze Offline

Mount the image read-only and run forensic tools (Autopsy, Sleuth Kit, Volatility for Linux).

## 3.6 Summary of Forensic Acquisition Decisions

| Decision Point | Recommended Choice | Rationale |
|----------------|--------------------|-----------|
| Live vs dead acquisition? | **Live** if disk encryption may be active | Need decryption keys from RAM |
| Imaging tool? | `dd`, `dcfldd`, `ewfexport`, `guymager` | Hash-verified, forensically sound |
| Filesystem mount? | **Read-only** (`mount -o ro,noexec,nodev,noload`) | Prevents accidental writes; `noload` skips journal replay |
| Time source for logs? | NTP-synchronized before incident | Correlate logs with external sources |
| Use of `atime`? | Disable during analysis (`mount -o noatime`) | Prevents timestamp updates from contaminating evidence |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Modern Linux Forensic Architecture (Layered View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef hw fill:#1f2a44,stroke:#88a,color:#fff
    classDef kern fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#3498db,color:#fff
    classDef init fill:#34495e,stroke:#e74c3c,color:#fff
    classDef user fill:#3d566e,stroke:#f39c12,color:#fff
    classDef log fill:#1e3a1e,stroke:#2ecc71,color:#fff
    classDef foren fill:#4a1a1a,stroke:#e74c3c,color:#fff

    HW[Hardware Layer - CPU RAM Disk NIC USB]:::hw
    KERN[Linux Kernel - LKMs Audit Subsystem proc sys]:::kern
    INIT[Init Framework - systemd PID 1 Service Manager]:::init
    USER[User Space - Shells Daemons GUI Apps]:::user

    LOG1[Journald - var log journal]:::log
    LOG2[rsyslog syslog-ng - var log]:::log
    LOG3[auditd - var log audit]:::log
    LOG4[Application Logs - Apache Nginx MySQL]:::log

    FOREN[Forensic Investigator]:::foren
    ACQ[Acquire Image - dd ewfexport avml]:::foren
    ANALYZE[Analyze - SleuthKit Autopsy Volatility]:::foren
    REPORT[Correlate and Report]:::foren

    HW --> KERN
    KERN --> INIT
    INIT --> USER

    KERN -.generates.-> LOG3
    INIT -.manages.-> LOG1
    USER -.produces.-> LOG2
    USER -.produces.-> LOG4

    LOG1 --> FOREN
    LOG2 --> FOREN
    LOG3 --> FOREN
    LOG4 --> FOREN
    LOG3 --> ACQ
    LOG1 --> ACQ
    ACQ --> ANALYZE
    ANALYZE --> REPORT
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Service Lifecycle State Machine (systemd)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Inactive
    Inactive --> Activating : systemctl start
    Activating --> Active : ExecStart success
    Activating --> Failed : ExecStart non-zero exit
    Active --> Reloading : systemctl reload
    Reloading --> Active : reload complete
    Active --> Deactivating : systemctl stop
    Deactivating --> Inactive : ExecStop success
    Deactivating --> Failed : ExecStop failure
    Failed --> Inactive : systemctl reset-failed
    Active --> [*] : system shutdown
    Failed --> [*] : system shutdown
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Define the Filesystem Hierarchy Standard (FHS) in Linux. List any four top-level directories specified by FHS 3.0 and their forensic significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Filesystem Hierarchy Standard (FHS)** is a specification maintained by the Linux Foundation that defines the directory structure and contents of a Linux distribution to ensure consistency across distributions. It allows software to predict the location of installed files, system configuration, and user data. (1.5 marks)

**Four top-level directories with forensic significance:** (1.5 marks — 0.375 each)

| Directory | Forensic Significance |
|-----------|----------------------|
| `/etc` | Contains system-wide configuration. Tampering indicates persistence mechanism or unauthorized change. |
| `/var/log` | Houses log files (syslog, auth.log, secure). Primary source of event reconstruction. |
| `/home` | User-generated content: documents, shell history, browser caches, SSH keys. |
| `/tmp` | World-writable temporary space. Frequently used by attackers as a staging area; cleaned on reboot. |

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Explain the concept of journaling in modern Linux filesystems. Why is it important for digital forensics?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Journaling** is a technique used by modern filesystems (ext4, XFS, Btrfs) where metadata changes are first written to a reserved area called the **journal** (or log) before being committed to the main filesystem. In the event of a crash or power loss, the journal can be replayed to bring the filesystem to a consistent state. (1.5 marks)
>
> **Forensic importance:** (1.5 marks)
> 1. The journal itself contains **recently pending operations** that may include metadata of files later deleted or modified — recoverable evidence.
> 2. It provides a **time-ordered record** of filesystem modifications useful for timeline reconstruction.
> 3. Tools like `extundelete` and `debugfs` parse the journal to recover deleted files that are not yet overwritten.
> 4. The journal can also be a **target of anti-forensic attacks** — an attacker may attempt to overwrite or forge journal entries.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the Linux Audit Framework (`auditd`) in detail. Explain its architecture, key configuration files, and the syntax of a watch rule. Provide at least one example rule for monitoring changes to the `/etc/passwd` file.

**(b) [7 marks]** A forensic investigator is handed a running production server suspected of compromise. Outline the **Order of Volatility** principle and describe the **step-by-step procedure** the investigator should follow to acquire volatile and non-volatile evidence without contaminating the system.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**1. Overview of auditd (1 mark)**

The Linux Audit Framework is a kernel-level logging system that records security-relevant events at the syscall level. It is composed of:
- **Kernel audit subsystem** (compiled in or as a module).
- **`auditd` daemon** (user-space collector and writer).
- **Audit rules** (define what to watch).
- **Audit log file** (default: `/var/log/audit/audit.log`).

**2. Architecture Diagram (1 mark)** — see Section 4.5 of these notes.

**3. Key Configuration Files (2 marks)**

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `/etc/audit/auditd.conf` | Main daemon configuration (log file path, rotation, disk space actions) |
| `/etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules` | Persistent audit rules loaded at boot |
| `/etc/audit/plugins.d/` | Plugin configuration (e.g., remote logging) |

**4. Watch Rule Syntax (2 marks)**

The general syntax of an audit watch rule is:

$$
\text{auditctl} \; {-}w \; \text{<path>} \; {-}p \; \text{<permissions>} \; {-}k \; \text{<key>}
$$

where:
- `-w` → Specify the file or directory to watch.
- `-p` → Trigger permissions: `r` (read), `w` (write), `x` (execute), `a` (attribute change).
- `-k` → A user-defined key string to identify the rule in searches.

**5. Example Rule for /etc/passwd (1 mark)**

$$
\text{auditctl} \; {-}w \; \text{/etc/passwd} \; {-}p \; \text{wa} \; {-}k \; \text{passwd\_changes}
$$

To make it persistent, place the same line in `/etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules`.

**6. Useful audit tools (Bonus 0 marks but reinforces answer):** `ausearch -k passwd_changes`, `aureport -f -i`.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**1. Order of Volatility Principle (2 marks)**

Per **RFC 3227** and standard forensic practice, evidence must be acquired from the **most volatile to least volatile** source. The order is:

1. CPU registers, cache (rarely captured in Linux forensics).
2. **Routing table, ARP cache, process table, kernel statistics, memory.**
3. **Temporary filesystems** (`/tmp`, `/var/run`).
4. **Disk** (most persistent).

**2. Step-by-Step Procedure (5 marks)**

**Step 1: Document the System (0.5 marks)**
$$
\text{date} \; \vert \; \text{uptime} \; \vert \; \text{hostname} \; \vert \; \text{uname} \; {-}\text{a}
$$
Record exact command output for the chain of custody log.

**Step 2: Capture Network State (0.5 marks)**
$$
\text{netstat} \; {-}\text{tulnp} \; \vert \; \text{ss} \; {-}\text{tulnp} \; \vert \; \text{ip} \; \text{neighbor} \; \vert \; \text{arp} \; {-}\text{a}
$$
This identifies open connections, listening ports, and ARP neighbors (potential pivot targets).

**Step 3: Capture Process State (1 mark)**
$$
\text{ps} \; \text{auxf} \; \vert \; \text{pstree} \; {-}\text{p} \; \vert \; \text{lsof} \; \vert \; \text{ls} \; \text{/proc/}
$$
Process tree reveals parent-child relationships (often the smoking gun for malware parent processes).

**Step 4: Acquire RAM (1 mark)**
$$
\text{sudo } \texttt{avml} \; \text{memory.raw} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{sudo } \texttt{LiME}
$$
Acquire to an external USB drive. **Compute SHA-256 hash immediately.**

**Step 5: Collect Logs (1 mark)**
$$
\text{sudo } \texttt{journalctl} \; {-}\text{-} \; \text{since=}-24h \; > \; \text{evidence/journal.txt}
$$
$$
\text{sudo } \texttt{ausearch} \; {-}\text{ts} \; \text{today} \; {-}\text{i} \; > \; \text{evidence/audit.txt}
$$

**Step 6: Image the Disk (0.5 marks)**
$$
\text{sudo } \texttt{dd} \; \text{if=/dev/sda} \; \text{of=/evidence/disk.img} \; \text{bs=4M} \; \text{conv=noerror,sync}
$$
Verify with `sha256sum`.

**Step 7: Halt the System (0.5 marks)**
Use `echo o > /proc/sysrq-trigger` or pull the power. **Do NOT** perform a graceful `shutdown`/`reboot` because it may execute startup/shutdown scripts that overwrite evidence.

**Forensic output:** A complete evidence package (memory image, disk image, logs, command outputs) with documented hashes and chain of custody.

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Alternative Internal Choice)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast the **ext4, XFS, and Btrfs** filesystems in terms of their architectural features, performance characteristics, recovery support, and forensic tools used to analyze them.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **MACB timestamp model** in Linux. With a suitable example, demonstrate how an investigator can detect anti-forensic timestamp manipulation using the relationship between `mtime` and `ctime`.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (5 marks — 1.25 per row + tool row)**

| Feature | ext4 | XFS | Btrfs |
|---------|------|-----|-------|
| **Default Distro** | Ubuntu, Debian | RHEL, CentOS | Fedora (optional), SUSE |
| **Max Volume Size** | 1 EiB (theoretical) | 8 EiB | 16 EiB |
| **Journaling** | Yes (metadata only by default) | Yes (metadata) | Yes (CoW-based) |
| **Snapshots** | No native support | No native support | **Yes — native** |
| **Checksums** | No (added in metadata only since ext4 metadata_csum) | Yes (metadata) | Yes (data + metadata) |
| **Recovery Tools** | `extundelete`, `debugfs`, `e2fsck` | `xfs_undelete`, `xfs_repair`, `xfs_db` | `btrfs restore`, `btrfs scrub` |
| **Allocation** | Extents + delayed allocation | Extents + B+tree free space | Extents + CoW |
| **Performance Strength** | General purpose, low overhead | High concurrency, large files | Snapshots, integrity |

**Discussion (2 marks):**

- **ext4** is the most widely deployed and has the richest ecosystem of forensic recovery tools. Its primary weakness is the lack of native snapshots.
- **XFS** excels in high-throughput enterprise workloads (e.g., large databases). It is harder to recover deleted files because its free-space management is more aggressive.
- **Btrfs** is the most forensic-friendly of the three because of **native snapshots** — an investigator can mount a snapshot of the filesystem at a past point in time and recover overwritten content.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**1. The MACB Model (2 marks)**

Every file in Linux has **four** timestamps stored in the inode:

- **Modified (mtime)** — Last write to the file's data.
- **Accessed (atime)** — Last read of the file's data.
- **Changed (ctime)** — Last change to the inode (metadata or data).
- **Birth (btime / crtime)** — File creation time (available via `statx` syscall since Linux 4.11).

$$
T_{\text{file}} = \{mtime, \; atime, \; ctime, \; btime\}
$$

**2. The `stat` Command Output (1 mark)**

Running `stat <filename>` produces output like:

```
File: /home/user/report.txt
Size: 1024       	Blocks: 8          IO Block: 4096   regular file
Access: 2024-11-15 09:30:21.123456789 +0530
Modify: 2024-11-15 09:30:21.123456789 +0530
Change: 2024-11-15 09:30:21.123456789 +0530
 Birth: 2024-11-15 09:30:21.123456789 +0530
```

**3. Anti-Forensic Detection (3 marks)**

Suppose the investigator finds:
- `mtime = 2020-01-15 10:00:00`
- `ctime = 2024-11-15 14:32:08`

The relationship is:

$$
\Delta = ctime - mtime = 2024\text{-}11\text{-}15 \; 14:32:08 - 2020\text{-}01\text{-}15 \; 10:00:00
$$

Converting to days:

$$
\Delta \approx 4 \;\text{years} \;\text{and} \; 10 \;\text{months}
$$

> **Detection logic:** In normal operation, `ctime >= mtime` always (because ctime updates with any change). However, the **gap** between them should be **small** for routine files. A gap of **years** between mtime and ctime is **physically impossible** without timestamp manipulation, because:
> 1. If the file had truly been modified 4 years ago and untouched since, ctime would also be 4 years old.
> 2. The only way mtime is older than ctime by years is if **`touch -t` was used to backdate mtime**, and the system clock was 2024-11-15 at the time of the operation (which then became ctime).

**4. Mitigation by Attackers (1 mark)**

Skilled attackers disable `atime` updates (mount option `noatime`) and use `debugfs` (ext4) or direct inode manipulation to forge all four timestamps. Detection then relies on:
- Cross-correlating with **log timestamps** (`/var/log/auth.log`).
- **MAC time of parent directory** (touching a file updates the parent directory's mtime).
- **File content analysis** (embedded EXIF, internal timestamps, magic byte analysis).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing `ctime` with "creation time".** In Linux, `ctime` means **change time** (metadata), NOT creation time. Windows uses "Creation Time" differently. Examiners mark this distinction strictly.
> 2. **Forgetting the fourth timestamp.** Many students answer with only three (mtime, atime, ctime). The fourth — **btime / crtime / birth time** — is available in ext4 (with `statx`) and is a high-yield addition.
> 3. **Recommending `shutdown` to halt a suspect system.** A normal shutdown triggers systemd services, log rotation, and clearing of `/tmp` and `/var/run`. The correct procedure is **pull the power** or use `sysrq` trigger.
> 4. **Mounting the image read-write.** Always use `mount -o ro,noexec,nodev,noload` to prevent accidental modification.
> 5. **Skipping hash verification.** Every forensic image MUST be hashed (SHA-256 minimum) before and after acquisition. Examiners will deduct marks for omitting this.
> 6. **Ignoring the difference between Debian and RHEL log paths.** Writing `/var/log/auth.log` for a RHEL system (correct path: `/var/log/secure`) is a common cross-distribution error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print-Friendly):**

### Core Concepts
- [x] **Modern Linux** = UNIX-like, FHS-compliant, systemd-init, journaled FS, audit-framework capable.
- [x] **FHS 3.0** defines directory structure; `/etc`, `/var`, `/home`, `/tmp` are forensic hot zones.
- [x] **systemd** (PID 1) is the modern init system; service states: inactive → activating → active → deactivating → failed.
- [x] **Audit framework** = `auditd` (daemon) + `auditctl` (rule control) + `ausearch` / `aureport` (query).

### Filesystem Knowledge
- [x] **ext4** — Default in Ubuntu/Debian; uses extents and journaling.
- [x] **XFS** — Default in RHEL; high performance, B+tree free-space management.
- [x] **Btrfs** — Copy-on-Write with native snapshots; integrity via checksums.
- [x] **Journaling** stores pending operations in a log area before commit; recoverable forensic data.

### Timestamps (The 4-Tuple)
- [x] **mtime** — Last content modification.
- [x] **atime** — Last content access.
- [x] **ctime** — Last inode change (cannot be forged without root + inode manipulation).
- [x] **btime** — File creation time (statx, ext4/Btrfs/XFS).

### Critical Log Paths
- [x] `/var/log/auth.log` (Debian) or `/var/log/secure` (RHEL) — Authentication events.
- [x] `/var/log/audit/audit.log` — auditd records.
- [x] `/var/log/journal/` — systemd binary journal.
- [x] `/var/log/dpkg.log` or `/var/log/yum.log` — Package installation history.

### Forensic Procedures
- [x] **Order of Volatility:** Network → Process → Memory → Disk.
- [x] **Acquire RAM first** if disk encryption is suspected.
- [x] **Hash every artifact** (SHA-256 minimum).
- [x] **Mount evidence image read-only** with `ro,noexec,nodev,noload`.
- [x] **Never `shutdown` a suspect system** — pull power or use `sysrq`.

### Common User IDs and Bit Values
- [x] **Root UID = 0**; **First user UID = 1000** (most distros).
- [x] **SUID = 4000** (octal); **SGID = 2000**; **Sticky = 1000**.
- [x] Default block size = **4096 bytes**; default inode ratio = **16 KiB per inode**.

### Tools to Know (Toolbelt)
- [x] **Imaging:** `dd`, `dcfldd`, `ewfexport`, `guymager`.
- [x] **Memory:** `avml`, `LiME`.
- [x] **Filesystem analysis:** Sleuth Kit (`fls`, `icat`, `mmls`, `fsstat`), `Autopsy`, `The Sleuth Kit`.
- [x] **Log analysis:** `journalctl`, `ausearch`, `aureport`, `logwatch`.
- [x] **Recovery:** `extundelete`, `photorec`, `scalpel`, `testdisk`.

### Anti-Forensic Indicators to Watch
- [x] `noatime` mount option (suspicious in production).
- [x] Disabled auditd / cleared `/var/log`.
- [x] Shell history gaps or `unset HISTFILE`.
- [x] Unsigned SUID binaries in unusual paths.
- [x] Cron jobs with encoded (base64) commands.
- [x] Kernel modules loaded from `/tmp` or `/dev/shm`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Forensic Analysis of Linux Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Forensic Analysis of Linux Systems** is the systematic application of investigative methodologies, scientific procedures, and legally admissible techniques to identify, preserve, analyze, and present digital evidence extracted from Linux-based operating systems, file systems, and associated artifacts. It encompasses the examination of volatile and non-volatile data sources, including file system structures, log files, user activities, running processes, network connections, and kernel-level artifacts, while maintaining the chain of custody and ensuring forensic soundness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Linux forensics module is designed to impart hands-on competency in analyzing ext-family file systems, interpreting system logs (syslog, auth.log, kern.log), recovering deleted inodes, examining shell histories, and using command-line forensic utilities like `dd`, `The Sleuth Kit (TSK)`, `foremost`, `scalpel`, and `Volatility` on Linux platforms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — "The Linux Detective Inspecting a Crime Scene Apartment":**

Imagine a detective entering a rented apartment (the Linux system) after a suspect has left. The detective cannot just look at the surface — they must inspect:

- **The Filing Cabinet (File System)**: The apartment has a strict cataloging system. Every document (file) has a labeled drawer (inode) with metadata (owner, size, timestamps). Deleted documents may still leave empty folders, and the detective can reconstruct where they were.
- **The Guest Register (Log Files)**: Every entry and exit is logged. The doorbell camera (`auth.log`), the living-room motion sensor (`syslog`), and the kitchen heat detector (`kern.log`) all keep records.
- **The Voicemail of Commands (Shell History)**: The suspect's typed commands in the terminal are like a voicemail — they show intent and actions.
- **The Running Appliances (Live Processes)**: Lights and appliances currently on are like live processes. If you pull the plug (power off), they vanish (volatile evidence).
- **The Postal Service (Network Connections)**: Letters sent and received reveal communication with the outside world.

The Linux detective must work quickly, document everything, and never disturb the original scene (write-blockers and read-only mounts).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Linux is forensic-friendly because it is open-source, transparent, and highly scriptable. Almost every action generates a log, and most data is stored in plain text, making evidence recovery exceptionally rich compared to closed systems.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Critical Paths in Linux

The following critical paths are universally standardized across Linux distributions (per **FHS — Filesystem Hierarchy Standard**):

| Forensic Target | Path | Artifact Type |
|---|---|---|
| User Shell History | `~/.bash_history` | Bash commands entered |
| Authentication Log | `/var/log/auth.log` | Login attempts, sudo usage |
| System Log | `/var/log/syslog` | General system events |
| Kernel Log | `/var/log/kern.log` | Kernel messages |
| Boot Log | `/var/log/boot.log` | Boot sequence events |
| Cron Jobs | `/etc/crontab`, `/var/spool/cron/` | Scheduled tasks |
| User Accounts | `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow` | Account credentials |
| Last Logins | `/var/log/wtmp`, `/var/log/btmp` | Login records |
| Running Processes | `/proc/[PID]/` | Live process state |
| Network Connections | `/proc/net/tcp`, `/proc/net/udp` | Active sockets |
| Logical Volume Info | `/etc/fstab`, `/etc/mtab` | Mounted file systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Evidence Collection:** Always mount the suspect disk in **read-only** mode using `mount -o ro` or use a hardware **write-blocker** to preserve the original evidence integrity. The default hash algorithm used for evidence integrity is **MD5** or **SHA-256**, with **SHA-256** being the modern forensic standard.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linux File System Architecture (ext2/ext3/ext4)

The **extended file system (ext)** family is the default for most Linux distributions. Each version introduces forensic-relevant enhancements.

### 2.1.1 Core Structural Components

The ext-family file system is organized into the following logical blocks:

- **Boot Block (Block 0)**: Contains the boot loader code. Located at the very first sector.
- **Superblock (Block 1)**: The "master index" containing file system metadata — total inodes, total blocks, block size, free block count, mount count, last mount time, last write time.
- **Group Descriptors**: Describe block groups (each group contains a copy of the superblock, inode bitmap, block bitmap, inode table, and data blocks).
- **Inode Bitmap**: Tracks which inodes are allocated.
- **Block Bitmap**: Tracks which data blocks are allocated.
- **Inode Table**: Contains all inodes for that block group.
- **Data Blocks**: Store the actual file contents.

### 2.1.2 The Inode — Forensic Cornerstone

An **inode** (index node) is a data structure that stores information about a file **except its name and actual data**. Each inode is **128 bytes (ext2/ext3)** or **256 bytes (ext4)** in size.

**Forensic Significance of Inodes:**
- Every file is uniquely identified by an **inode number**.
- When a file is "deleted," the inode is marked free, but the data blocks remain untouched until overwritten.
- The `ls -i` command reveals inode numbers.
- The `stat` command reveals detailed inode metadata.

**Inode Structure Fields (Critical for Forensics):**

| Field | Size | Forensic Value |
|---|---|---|
| `i_mode` | 2 bytes | File type & permissions (0755, 0644) |
| `i_uid` / `i_gid` | 2 bytes each | Owner User ID / Group ID |
| `i_size` | 4 bytes (ext2/3) / 8 bytes (ext4) | File size in bytes |
| `i_atime` | 4 bytes | Last access time |
| `i_mtime` | 4 bytes | Last modification time |
| `i_ctime` | 4 bytes | Inode change time |
| `i_dtime` | 4 bytes | **Deletion time** (key for forensic recovery) |
| `i_links_count` | 2 bytes | Hard link count |
| `i_blocks` | 4 bytes | Number of 512-byte blocks allocated |
| `i_block[15]` | 60 bytes | **12 direct + 1 indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect** block pointers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MAC (Modified, Accessed, Changed) Times for Forensics:**
> - **mtime**: When file content was last modified.
> - **atime**: When file content was last read.
> - **ctime**: When inode metadata was last changed (cannot be manually set — increases forensic reliability).
> - **crtime** (ext4 only / birth time): When the file was created.
> - **dtime**: When the file was deleted (the forensic "smoking gun").

### 2.1.3 ext3 vs ext4 — Forensic Differences

| Feature | ext3 | ext4 |
|---|---|---|
| Journaling | Yes (metadata only by default) | Yes (metadata + data, configurable) |
| Max file size | 2 TiB | 16 TiB |
| Max file system size | 16 TiB | 1 EiB |
| Timestamps | s, ms, ns (3 levels) | ns precision by default |
| Inode size | 128 bytes default | 256 bytes default |
| Birth time (crtime) | Not stored | Stored in `i_crtime` |
| Extent-based allocation | No | Yes (replaces block lists) |

## 2.2 The Linux Boot Process — Forensic Trace

The Linux boot sequence leaves distinct forensic evidence at each stage:

1. **BIOS/UEFI** → Reads MBR/GPT, loads bootloader.
2. **Bootloader (GRUB/LILO/SYSLINUX)** → Loads kernel and initramfs.
3. **Kernel Initialization** → Mounts root file system, starts `init`/`systemd`.
4. **init/systemd (PID 1)** → Reads `/etc/inittab` or systemd units, starts services.
5. **Runlevel/Target Reached** → User login (TTY or GUI) initiated.

**Forensic Artifacts per Stage:**

| Stage | Artifact | Path/Location |
|---|---|---|
| Bootloader config | `grub.cfg`, `menu.lst` | `/boot/grub/` |
| Kernel messages | Ring buffer | `dmesg` command |
| Boot log | `/var/log/boot.log` | systemd-boot |
| Service start order | Systemd journal | `journalctl` |
| Init scripts | `/etc/init.d/`, `/etc/rc*.d/` | SysVinit style |
| Systemd units | `/etc/systemd/system/`, `/lib/systemd/system/` | systemd style |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Command | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Hash Computation (Forensic Integrity) | $\text{SHA-256}(E) = H$ | $E$ = evidence file, $H$ = hash digest |
| Evidence Block Acquisition | `dd if=/dev/sda of=image.dd bs=4M conv=noerror,sync` | Bit-stream copy with error tolerance |
| File Recovery (Inode-based) | `extundelete /dev/sdaX --restore-all` | Recovers deleted files from ext file systems |
| Slack Space Extraction | $\text{Slack} = B_{\text{alloc}} - F_{\text{size}}$ | $B_{\text{alloc}}$ = allocated blocks, $F_{\text{size}}$ = file size |
| File System Block Size | $\text{Block Size} = 1024 \times 2^n$ bytes (n=0,1,2,3) | Common values: 1024, 2048, 4096 |
| Timestamp Decoding (Unix Epoch) | $T_{\text{human}} = T_{\text{epoch}} + \text{UTC Offset}$ | Unix epoch = Jan 1, 1970 00:00:00 UTC |
| Unallocated Space Search | `grep -a -B 100 -A 100 "keyword" /dev/sdaX` | Searches raw disk for string patterns |
| Carving Deleted Files | `foremost -t all -i image.dd -o output/` | File carving by header/footer signatures |
| Mounting Read-Only | `mount -o ro,noload /dev/sdaX /mnt/evidence` | Prevents accidental modification |
| Memory Acquisition | `avml /dev/mem > mem.lime` or `LiME` | Linux Memory Extractor |
| Block Group Size | $\text{BG Size} = 8 \times \text{Block Size}$ | Default for ext2/3/4 (with 4096-byte blocks = 32 MiB) |
| Inode Ratio | $\text{Bytes per Inode} = \frac{\text{FS Size}}{\text{Inode Count}}$ | Typically 16384 bytes/inode (mkfs default) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Linux forensics techniques are applied in incident response (IR), insider threat detection, malware analysis, intellectual property theft investigations, regulatory compliance audits (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR), and law enforcement cybercrime units. The same inode-based recovery principles underpin modern eDiscovery tools used in litigation support.

## 2.4 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Forensic Step

- **Why mount read-only?** Because mounting with write permissions can update atime timestamps, change superblock mount counts, and write to journals — all of which destroy evidence.
- **Why use `dd` with `conv=noerror,sync`?** The `noerror` flag continues reading past disk errors; `sync` pads incomplete blocks with zeros to maintain byte-for-byte sector alignment.
- **Why hash the evidence?** Hashing provides mathematical proof that the analyzed copy is identical to the original. Any single bit change yields a completely different hash (avalanche effect).
- **Why examine `/proc`?** It is a virtual file system exposing live kernel data structures — process memory maps, open file descriptors, network sockets — but only exists while the system is running (volatile).
- **Why check shell history last?** Bash updates `.bash_history` only on session exit, so a live examination may show the running session's commands via `history` while the file on disk is stale.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Timestamp Correlation Across Artifacts

### Problem Setup
A forensic examiner finds a deleted file on an ext4 partition. The superblock indicates the file system was created on `2024-08-15 10:30:00 UTC`. The inode of the deleted file reports the following:

- `i_atime` = 1724659200
- `i_mtime` = 1724745600
- `i_ctime` = 1724745601
- `i_dtime` = 1724832000
- `i_crtime` = 1724144000

The shell history of the suspected user shows the command `rm secret.txt` was executed, and `/var/log/auth.log` shows a sudo session ending around the deletion time. **Derive the chronological narrative of the file's lifecycle.**

### Step-by-Step Decoding

**Step 1: Convert each Unix epoch timestamp to human-readable UTC.**

The conversion formula is:
$$T_{\text{human}} = \text{epoch2utc}(T_{\text{epoch}})$$

Using standard Unix epoch conversion (where `1724144000` corresponds to the baseline):

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{crtime}} &= \text{epoch2utc}(1724144000) \\
&= \textbf{2024-08-20 16:00:00 UTC}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Conversion logic:* Adding 1724144000 seconds to the Unix epoch (1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC) yields 2024-08-20 16:00:00 UTC. This is the **birth time** — when the inode was first allocated and the file created.

**Step 2: Decode the modification time (mtime).**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{mtime}} &= \text{epoch2utc}(1724745600) \\
&= \textbf{2024-08-27 16:00:00 UTC}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Conversion logic:* This represents the last time the file's *content* was modified. The investigator should compare this with the user's last login and the application logs to identify what change was made.

**Step 3: Decode the access time (atime).**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{atime}} &= \text{epoch2utc}(1724659200) \\
&= \textbf{2024-08-26 16:00:00 UTC}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The atime (1724659200) is **one day before** the mtime (1724745600). This means the file was *read* on Aug 26, then *modified* on Aug 27. In a corporate espionage scenario, this could indicate the file was opened for review the day before being altered.

**Step 4: Decode the change time (ctime).**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{ctime}} &= \text{epoch2utc}(1724745601) \\
&= \textbf{2024-08-27 16:00:01 UTC}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Conversion logic:* ctime is always ≥ mtime. The 1-second gap here is consistent with a `chmod` or `chown` operation performed immediately after content modification, OR with metadata updates that the file system performs atomically.

**Step 5: Decode the deletion time (dtime).**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{dtime}} &= \text{epoch2utc}(1724832000) \\
&= \textbf{2024-08-28 16:00:00 UTC}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The dtime marks when the inode was deallocated. The file was deleted ~24 hours after the last modification. The investigator correlates this with the sudo session end in `/var/log/auth.log`.

**Step 6: Cross-correlate with the system log timeline.**

| Timestamp (UTC) | Event Source | Event Description |
|---|---|---|
| 2024-08-20 16:00:00 | Inode crtime | `secret.txt` created (inode allocated) |
| 2024-08-26 16:00:00 | Inode atime | File opened for reading |
| 2024-08-27 16:00:00 | Inode mtime | File contents modified |
| 2024-08-27 16:00:01 | Inode ctime | Metadata changed (e.g., permissions) |
| 2024-08-28 16:00:00 | Inode dtime | File deleted via `rm` |
| ~2024-08-28 16:00:00 | `auth.log` | `sudo` session terminated for user `alice` |

**Step 7: Calculate the time delta between key events.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{\text{create→access}} &= 1724659200 - 1724144000 = 515{,}200 \text{ seconds} \\
&= 5.963 \text{ days} \approx \textbf{6 days}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The file existed untouched for 6 days after creation before its first read access.

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{\text{access→modify}} &= 1724745600 - 1724659200 = 86{,}400 \text{ seconds} \\
&= \textbf{exactly 1 day}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Conversion logic:* A precise 24-hour gap suggests an automated job (e.g., daily cron) modified the file, OR a deliberate human action with timestamp correlation to a daily task.

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{\text{modify→delete}} &= 1724832000 - 1724745600 = 86{,}400 \text{ seconds} \\
&= \textbf{exactly 1 day}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The exact 1-day pattern between access→modify and modify→delete is highly suspicious and may indicate a script. Real user behavior rarely produces such uniform intervals.

**Step 8: Forensic Conclusion.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Inference:**
> The exact 1-day intervals strongly suggest **automated activity** (a script or scheduled job) rather than human user behavior. The `secret.txt` file was created, reviewed 6 days later, then modified and deleted in a precise 24-hour cycle. The examiner should investigate the user's crontab entries for evidence of an automated deletion script.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Log File Forensic Indicators

| Log File | Suspicious Pattern | Forensic Indicator |
|---|---|---|
| `/var/log/auth.log` | Repeated `Failed password` entries from same IP | Brute force attack |
| `/var/log/auth.log` | `sudo` by user not in `/etc/sudoers` | Privilege escalation attempt |
| `/var/log/auth.log` | `Accepted publickey` from unfamiliar IP | Unauthorized SSH access |
| `/var/log/syslog` | Service restarts at unusual hours | Malware persistence |
| `/var/log/kern.log` | `segfault` from unusual binary | Exploit attempt |
| `/var/log/wtmp` | Gaps in login history | Tampering with logs (use `last -f /var/log/wtmp.1`) |
| `/var/log/btmp` | Many failed logins for root | Brute force on root account |
| `~/.bash_history` | `unset HISTFILE` followed by commands | Anti-forensics behavior |
| `~/.bash_history` | `wget` / `curl` from external URL | Possible malware download |
| `/etc/crontab` | Cron job running `nc` (netcat) | Reverse shell persistence |
| `/var/spool/cron/crontabs/` | Cron entries with `/dev/tcp/` | Bash reverse shell |
| `/etc/passwd` | UID 0 (root) account with normal username | Hidden root account (backdoor) |

## 3.3 Inode Recovery Mathematical Model

The probability of recovering a deleted file depends on the elapsed time and disk activity:

$$P_{\text{recovery}} = e^{-\lambda \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $P_{\text{recovery}}$ = probability of successful file recovery
- $\lambda$ = disk write activity rate (inodes overwritten per hour)
- $t$ = time elapsed since deletion (in hours)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A busy server (e.g., web server with $\lambda \approx 50$) will overwrite deleted inodes rapidly, making recovery nearly impossible within hours. A cold storage system ($\lambda \approx 0.01$) may preserve deleted inodes for months. This is why forensic examiners prioritize **immediate disk imaging** of compromised systems.

### Worked Example

A forensic examiner arrives at a compromised database server. The estimated write rate is $\lambda = 30$ inodes/hour. The attacker deleted incriminating log files approximately $\lambda = 30 \cdot 0.5 = 15$ inodes overwritten. What is the recovery probability if action is taken 2 hours after deletion?

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{recovery}} &= e^{-\lambda \cdot t} \\
&= e^{-30 \cdot 2} \\
&= e^{-60} \\
&\approx 8.76 \times 10^{-27}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Conversion logic:* This astronomically small probability means recovery is essentially **impossible** without prior disk imaging. This demonstrates the critical importance of **proactive forensic imaging** in enterprise environments.
