# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define an inode in the Linux file system. List any four metadata fields stored in it.**

**Model Answer:**
An **inode (Index Node)** is a 256-byte data structure (in ext4) used by Linux to store all metadata about a file except its name and actual data. It acts as the unique identifier for a file within the file system.

**Four metadata fields:**
1. **File type and permissions** (`i_mode`)
2. **Owner and group IDs** (`i_uid`, `i_gid`)
3. **File size** (`i_size`)
4. **Timestamps** — `i_atime`, `i_mtime`, `i_ctime`, `i_crtime`, `i_dtime`

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Explain the difference between `mtime`, `atime`, and `ctime` in Linux. What command reveals the deletion time?**

**Model Answer:**
| Timestamp | Meaning | Triggered When |
|---|---|---|
| `mtime` | Modification time | File **content** is written |
| `atime` | Access time | File is **read** |
| `ctime` | Change time | **Metadata** (perm, owner) is altered |

**Deletion time** is stored in the **`i_dtime`** field of the inode. The `istat` command from **The Sleuth Kit** displays it: `istat disk.img <inode_number>`.

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> - **Don't confuse `ctime` with "creation time".** `ctime` is **Change time**, not Creation. Many students lose 2 marks here every semester.
> - When asked for Sleuth Kit commands, **write the full syntax with example** (`fls -r -d / disk.img`), not just the tool name.
> - Always mention the **inode table offset, block size, and inode size** when discussing recovery — examiners reward technical precision.
> - For numerical derivations, **show all intermediate steps**. Skipping arithmetic = lost marks.

---

### Question A (Option 1): `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Sleuth Kit (TSK)** tool suite. Explain with suitable examples how `fls`, `icat`, `istat`, and `blkls` are used in Linux file system forensics.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An investigator acquires a 500 GB ext4 disk image. The block size is 4 KiB and the bytes-per-inode ratio is 16384. **Calculate** the maximum number of inodes and the inode table size for each block group.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Sleuth Kit Suite Explanation [7 marks]

The **Sleuth Kit (TSK)** by Brian Carrier is an open-source collection of CLI tools for analyzing disk images in a forensically sound manner without mounting the file system.

**Key Commands:**

| Command | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `fls` | **Lists file and directory names**, including **deleted entries** (with `-d` flag). | `fls -r -o 2048 disk.img /` |
| `icat` | **Extracts the content of a file** given its inode number. | `icat -o 2048 disk.img 1234 > recovered.txt` |
| `istat` | **Displays inode metadata** — permissions, size, timestamps, block pointers. | `istat -o 2048 disk.img 1234` |
| `blkls` | **Dumps unallocated blocks** (used for file carving and slack space analysis). | `blkls -o 2048 disk.img > unalloc.dd` |

**Practical Workflow:**
1. Run `mmls disk.img` to identify partition table.
2. Run `fsstat -o 2048 disk.img` to determine file system type and block geometry.
3. Run `fls -r -d / disk.img` to list **all** files including deleted ones.
4. Identify suspicious inode via `fls` output.
5. Use `istat` to verify timestamps and ownership.
6. Use `icat` to extract the file.
7. Use `blkls` + `foremost` to carve unknown deleted content from unallocated space.

**[7 marks breakdown: 2 for overview, 4 for command explanations with examples, 1 for workflow]**

---

#### Part (b) — Inode Calculations [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Disk size $D = 500 \text{ GB} = 500 \times 2^{30} \text{ bytes}$
- Block size $bs = 4 \text{ KiB} = 4096 \text{ bytes}$
- Bytes-per-inode $r = 16384 \text{ bytes/inode}$

**Step 1 — Total number of blocks:**
$$
N_{\text{blocks}} = \frac{D}{bs} = \frac{500 \times 2^{30}}{4096}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{blocks}} &= \frac{500 \times 1{,}073{,}741{,}824}{4096} \\
&= \frac{536{,}870{,}912{,}000}{4096} \\
&= 131{,}072{,}000 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum number of inodes:**
$$
N_{\text{inode}} = \frac{D}{r} = \frac{500 \times 2^{30}}{16384}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{inode}} &= \frac{536{,}870{,}912{,}000}{16384} \\
&= 32{,}768{,}000 \text{ inodes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Block groups in ext4:**
Default blocks per group = 32,768 blocks.
$$
N_{\text{groups}} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{\text{blocks}}}{32768} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{131{,}072{,}000}{32768} \right\rceil = 4000 \text{ groups}
$$

**Step 4 — Inodes per block group:**
$$
I_{\text{per\_group}} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{\text{inode}}}{N_{\text{groups}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{32{,}768{,}000}{4000} \right\rceil = 8192 \text{ inodes/group}
$$

**Step 5 — Inode table size per block group:**
$$
T_{\text{inode}} = I_{\text{per\_group}} \times \text{inode\_size} = 8192 \times 256
$$

$$
T_{\text{inode}} = 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ bytes} = 2 \text{ MiB per group}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- **Maximum inodes:** **32,768,000**
- **Inode table size per block group:** **2 MiB**

---

### Question B (Option 2): `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **MACB timestamp model** in Linux forensics. With a neat diagram, describe how an inode is structured in ext4 and how it stores these timestamps.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the forensic significance of the following Linux artifacts:
1. `/etc/shadow`
2. `/var/log/auth.log`
3. `~/.bash_history`
4. `/var/log/wtmp`

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — MACB Model and Inode Diagram [7 marks]

The **MACB model** describes the four primary timestamps stored in an ext4 inode:

| Letter | Field | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **M** | `i_mtime` | Last data **Modification** |
| **A** | `i_atime` | Last data **Access** |
| **C** | `i_ctime` | Last metadata **Change** |
| **B** | `i_crtime` | File **Birth** (creation) |
| **D** | `i_dtime` | **Deletion** time (when inode freed) |

**Inode Structure Diagram (textual):**

```
Offset   Field           Size    Purpose
+0       i_mode          2B      Type + permissions
+2       i_uid           2B      Owner UID
+4       i_size          8B      File size (bytes)
+12      i_atime         4B      Last access (sec since epoch)
+16      i_ctime         4B      Last inode change
+20      i_mtime         4B      Last data modification
+24      i_mtime_ns      4B      mtime nanoseconds
+28      i_dtime         4B      Deletion time
+32      i_gid           2B      Group ID
+34      i_links_count   2B      Hard link count
+36      i_blocks        4B      Disk blocks (512-byte units)
+40      i_flags         4B      ext4 flags
+44      i_osd1          4B      OS-specific 1
+48      i_block[15]     60B     Block pointers
+108     i_generation    4B      NFS generation
+112     i_file_acl      4B      Extended attributes block
+116     i_crtime        4B      Creation timestamp
+120     i_crtime_ns     4B      Creation nanoseconds
+124     i_extra_isize   2B      Extra inode size
```

> **Note:** The ext4 `stat` command output (`stat filename`) shows all these timestamps in human-readable form.

**[7 marks breakdown: 2 for MACB explanation, 3 for inode diagram with offsets, 2 for forensic interpretation]**

---

#### Part (b) — Linux Artifacts Significance [7 marks]

**1. `/etc/shadow` (2 marks):**
- Contains **hashed passwords** and account aging info.
- Forensic value: Detect newly created accounts (insider threat), brute-force attempts (lockout policies), and privilege escalation.
- Format: `username:$6$salt$hash:lastchg:min:max:warn:inactive:expire`

**2. `/var/log/auth.log` (2 marks):**
- Records **PAM authentication**, SSH logins, sudo attempts, and session opens.
- Forensic value: Identify unauthorized remote access, lateral movement, and password-guessing attacks.
- Example entry: `May 12 14:32:11 server sshd[4521]: Accepted publickey for root from 192.168.1.50`

**3. `~/.bash_history` (1.5 marks):**
- Stores **last 500–1000 commands** (configurable via `HISTFILESIZE`).
- Forensic value: Reconstruct attacker activity, identify exfiltration commands (e.g., `base64`, `nc`, `curl`).
- **Caution:** A savvy attacker clears or symlinks this file to `/dev/null`.

**4. `/var/log/wtmp` (1.5 marks):**
- Binary log of **all login/logout events**.
- Read with `last` command.
- Forensic value: Track session durations, identify ghost sessions, correlate with `auth.log`.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Linux File Analysis**
> 
> **Definitions (Must Know):**
> - Inode = 256-byte metadata record (ext4)
> - Superblock = global FS metadata at fixed offset 1024 bytes
> - Block size = default 4 KiB
> - Inode table size per group = 2 MiB (8192 × 256)
> - Bytes-per-inode ratio = 16384
> 
> **Timestamps (Don't Mix Up):**
> - `atime` = Access (read)
> - `mtime` = Modify (write content)
> - `ctime` = Change (metadata — NOT creation!)
> - `crtime` = Birth/Creation (ext4 only)
> - `dtime` = Deletion
> 
> **Sleuth Kit Tools (Write Syntax + Example):**
> - `mmls`, `fsstat`, `fls`, `icat`, `istat`, `ifind`, `ffind`, `blkls`, `ils`, `mac-robber`, `mactime`
> 
> **Critical Forensic Artifacts:**
> - `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow`, `/etc/sudoers`
> - `/var/log/auth.log` (Debian) / `/var/log/secure` (RHEL)
> - `/var/log/wtmp` (`last`), `/var/log/btmp` (`lastb`)
> - `~/.bash_history`, `~/.ssh/authorized_keys`
> 
> **Key Formulas:**
> - Max blocks per inode = $12 + 1024 + 1024^{2} + 1024^{3}$ (with 4 KiB blocks)
> - Slack = (allocated blocks × 4096) − file size
> - Inode count = disk size / bytes-per-inode ratio
> - Inode offset = table_start + ((rel_index − 1) × 256)
> 
> **Examiner Traps:**
> - `ctime` ≠ creation time → use `crtime` or `stat -c %w`
> - `mactime` requires a body file from `mac-robber` or `fls -m`
> - Deleted files persist in unallocated blocks until reuse
> - Journal contains pending operations (volatile — analyze first)
> 
> **Real-World Tools (Beyond TSK):**
> - **Autopsy** — GUI frontend for TSK
> - **Sleuth Kit + Plaso (log2timeline)** — super-timeline analysis
> - **Foremost / Scalpel** — file carving from unallocated space
> - **TestDisk / PhotoRec** — partition and file recovery
> - **The Coroner's Toolkit (TCT)** — historical Unix forensic suite

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Crash and Core Dumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Crash Dump (Windows)?

A **Crash Dump** (also called a **Memory Dump** or **Windows Memory Dump**) is a snapshot of the working memory (RAM) automatically captured by the Windows operating system when the kernel encounters an unrecoverable fault, commonly manifested as a **Blue Screen of Death (BSOD)** or a critical system stop. It preserves the volatile state of the system at the exact instant of failure for post-mortem forensic analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Standard):** A crash dump is a file generated by the Windows OS containing a copy of physical memory contents at the moment a STOP error occurs, written to non-volatile storage to facilitate offline debugging, root-cause analysis, and digital forensic investigation.

## 1.2 What is a Core Dump (Linux)?

A **Core Dump** is a binary file (typically in **ELF format** under Linux) generated when a user-space process terminates abnormally due to a signal such as `SIGSEGV` (segmentation fault), `SIGABRT` (assertion/abort), `SIGFPE` (arithmetic error), or `SIGBUS` (bus error). The file contains the process's virtual address space, registers, threads, and metadata, allowing forensic reconstruction of a failed or compromised program.

> [!NOTE]
> In Linux, the term "core dump" predates Windows terminology and is also used for kernel panics — though modern Linux distinguishes between **kdump** (kernel dumps) and **user-space core dumps** (handled by `systemd-coredump`).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a detective arriving at a bank robbery scene. The robbers have fled, but a CCTV camera in the vault continuously recorded the *entire scene* — every face, every note exchanged, every dropped item — right up to the moment the alarm was triggered. That recording is exactly what a **crash/core dump** is for a digital investigator:

- The **robbery** = the crash, exploit, or malicious behavior.
- The **vault memory** = volatile RAM (registers, stack, heap).
- The **CCTV tape** = the dump file on disk.
- The **detective reviewing tape** = the forensic analyst using **WinDbg** or **GDB**.

Without the dump, the memory (the crime scene) is gone forever the moment power is lost. The dump is the *only* permanent record.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Value / Symbol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Windows Minidump default size | **64 KB** | Minimal triage data |
| Windows Kernel dump | **RAM size + 1 MB** header | Captures kernel space |
| Windows Complete dump | **Total physical RAM size** | Full volatile capture |
| Linux `/proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern` | Configuration file | Controls dump location/naming |
| Default core file size limit | `ulimit -c 0` (often disabled) | Soft limit on dump creation |
| ELF Magic Number | `0x7F 'E' 'L' 'F'` | Identifies Linux core files |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Crash Dump Generation Workflow (Windows)

The Windows kernel handles a fatal exception via a structured pipeline. Understanding each stage is essential for KTU questions on forensic procedure.

**Step 1 — Exception Detection**
The CPU raises a structured exception (e.g., `EXCEPTION_ACCESS_VIOLATION` = `0xC0000005`). Kernel-mode exception dispatchers catch it.

**Step 2 — Bugcheck Initiation**
The kernel calls `KeBugCheckEx()`, raising a STOP code (e.g., `0x0000007E` = `SYSTEM_THREAD_EXCEPTION_NOT_HANDLED`).

**Step 3 — Dump Stack Initialization**
The kernel allocates a contiguous buffer in non-paged pool to assemble the dump.

**Step 4 — Memory Capture**
The HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer) and storage stack driver execute a synchronous write of physical pages — interrupting all other I/O to preserve consistency.

**Step 5 — File System Update**
The `CrashControl` subsystem writes either `MEMORY.DMP` or `Minidump\<hash>.dmp`, then triggers a controlled reboot.

## 2.2 Windows Dump Types — Analytical Breakdown

| Dump Type | File | Size | Captures | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Small Memory Dump** | `Minidump\Mini<date>-<seq>.dmp` | **64 KB** | Stop code, drivers, stack of faulting thread, kernel context | Quick triage |
| **Kernel Memory Dump** | `MEMORY.DMP` (kernel variant) | RAM size + 1 MB | All kernel-mode memory, no user pages | Driver debugging |
| **Complete Memory Dump** | `MEMORY.DMP` | **= Physical RAM** | Entire physical memory including user space | Full forensic acquisition |
| **Automatic Memory Dump** | `MEMORY.DMP` | Configurable | Kernel + critical user pages | Auto-tunes between Kernel and Complete |

> [!NOTE]
> The registry path `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\CrashControl` governs all dump configurations including `CrashDumpEnabled`, `DumpFile`, `MinidumpDir`, and `Overwrite`.

## 2.3 Core Dump Generation Workflow (Linux)

**Step 1 — Signal Delivery**
The kernel delivers a fatal signal (`SIGSEGV=11`, `SIGABRT=6`, `SIGFPE=8`, `SIGILL=4`, `SIGBUS=7`) to the offending process.

**Step 2 — Dump Eligibility Check**
The kernel reads `RLIMIT_CORE` resource limit and `/proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern`. If dumping is disabled or filtered, the process is silently terminated.

**Step 3 — ELF Core File Construction**
The kernel constructs an **ELF Core file** containing:
- **Program headers (PT_NOTE):** registers, signal info, auxv
- **Program headers (PT_LOAD):** memory segments (heap, stack, .text, .data)
- **Notes section:** thread status, file mappings (`/proc/<pid>/maps` equivalent)

**Step 4 — Write-out**
The `coredump` handler (legacy: kernel; modern: `systemd-coredump`) writes the file to disk. `systemd-coredump` stores under `/var/lib/systemd/coredump/` indexed by journal.

**Step 5 — Process Termination**
The process exits with the signal number as its wait status.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Configuration Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Setting | Meaning | Typical Forensic Value |
|---|---|---|
| `core_pattern` | `/proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern` | `\|/usr/lib/systemd/systemd-coredump %P %u %g %s %t %c %h` |
| `core_uses_pid` | Append PID to filename | `1` (recommended) |
| `RLIMIT_CORE` | Per-process core file size limit | Set to `unlimited` for forensics |
| `dump.kexec` | kdump load command | `kdump` |
| `kdump_post` | Post-dump executable | `/path/to/evidence/collector.sh` |
| `Windows\\CrashDumpEnabled` | DWORD value | `1`=Complete, `2`=Kernel, `3`=Small, `7`=Automatic |
| `MinidumpsEnabled` | BOOL | `1` |
| `DumpFile` | REG_EXPAND_SZ | `%SystemRoot%\\MEMORY.DMP` |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Digital Forensics

- **Malware Memory Analysis:** Dumps can be carved with Volatility / MemProcFS to extract credentials, encryption keys, hidden processes.
- **Exploit Reconstruction:** Stack traces reveal ROP gadget chains, return addresses, and shellcode injection points.
- **Insider Threat:** Live process dumps captured via `gcore` or `ProcDump` provide evidence of data exfiltration in memory.
- **Incident Timeline:** Time-stamped dumps establish causality for breach reconstruction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

> Since this topic is procedural/forensic rather than purely algebraic, the "derivations" here are explicit procedural chains showing the **complete forensic workflow** required for KTU exam credit.

## 3.1 Procedure A — Acquiring a Windows Crash Dump for Forensics

**Step 1: Verify configuration**

Open `regedit` and navigate to:
```
HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\CrashControl
```

Confirm:
- `CrashDumpEnabled = 0x1` (Complete Dump) or `0x7` (Automatic)
- `DumpFile = %SystemRoot%\MEMORY.DMP`
- `MinidumpDir = %SystemRoot%\Minidump`

*Rationale:* Without these keys the OS discards the dump after reboot.

**Step 2: Trigger or recover the dump**
Either induce a STOP error using `NotMyFault` (Sysinternals) tool with the CLI command:
```
NotMyFault.exe /crash
```
or recover an existing `MEMORY.DMP` after a real crash.

*Rationale:* `NotMyFault` invokes a deliberate kernel fault to produce a controlled dump — a standard forensic validation technique.

**Step 3: Acquire with integrity protection**

Use `FTK Imager` to acquire `MEMORY.DMP`:
```
Source: C:\MEMORY.DMP
Image Format: E01 (Expert Witness Format)
Hash Algorithm: SHA-256
```

Record SHA-256 hash $H_{\text{source}}$:
$$
H_{\text{source}} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{MEMORY.DMP})
$$

*Rationale:* Chain of custody requires cryptographic hash before and after transfer.

**Step 4: Analyze with WinDbg**

Open WinDbg → `File` → `Open Crash Dump` → select `MEMORY.DMP`.

Execute:
```
!analyze -v
.kv          ; show kernel version
!vm          ; virtual memory summary
lm           ; loaded modules
!thread      ; current thread context
```

*Rationale:* `!analyze -v` produces the canonical triage report including STOP code, faulting module, stack trace, and suggested debugging steps.

**Step 5: Generate forensic report**

Document:
- STOP code (e.g., `0x9F: DRIVER_POWER_STATE_FAILURE`)
- Faulting module base address `0xFFFFF80012345678`
- Stack trace frames
- Loaded driver list
- Time of failure (UTC)

## 3.2 Procedure B — Capturing & Analyzing a Linux Core Dump

**Step 1: Enable core dumps persistently**

Edit `/etc/security/limits.conf`:
```
*  hard  core  unlimited
*  soft  core  unlimited
```

Set kernel pattern in `/etc/sysctl.d/99-coredump.conf`:
```
kernel.core_pattern = /var/lib/systemd/coredump/core.%e.%p.%t
```

Reload:
```
sudo sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/99-coredump.conf
ulimit -c unlimited
```

*Rationale:* Modern distributions default to `0` (disabled); forensic readiness requires explicit enabling.

**Step 2: Trigger the dump**

Run the suspect program until it crashes:
```
./vulnerable_app
Segmentation fault (core dumped)
```

**Step 3: Locate the core file**
```
coredumpctl list
coredumpctl info <PID>
coredumpctl dump <PID> > evidence.core
```

*Rationale:* `coredumpctl` is the systemd-managed entry point — it stores compressed dumps in `/var/lib/systemd/coredump/` indexed by journal.

**Step 4: Compute forensic hash**
$$
H_{\text{core}} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{evidence.core})
$$

**Step 5: Analyze with GDB**
```
gdb ./vulnerable_app evidence.core
(gdb) info registers
(gdb) bt full
(gdb) x/64xw $sp
(gdb) info proc mappings
(gdb) disassemble $pc, +32
```

**Step 6: Triage indicators of compromise (IoCs)**

| Check | Command | Forensic Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Suspicious mapped libraries | `info sharedlibrary` | DLL/so injection evidence |
| Shellcode on stack | `x/200xb $sp` | NOP sled, encoded payloads |
| Stack canary overwritten | `bt` | Buffer overflow confirmed |
| RWX regions | `info proc mappings` | Self-modifying code / packers |

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Feature | Windows Crash Dump | Linux Core Dump |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | `KeBugCheckEx` STOP error | POSIX signal (SIGSEGV, SIGABRT) |
| Format | Proprietary DMP / Minidump | ELF Core file |
| Default location | `%SystemRoot%\MEMORY.DMP` | `/var/lib/systemd/coredump/` or `cwd` |
| Primary analyzer | WinDbg, BlueScreenView | GDB, `crash`, LLDB |
| Size basis | Physical RAM | Virtual address space of process |
| Kernel variant | `MEMORY.DMP` (kernel + selective) | `kdump` via `kexec` |
| Forensic hash integrity | `SHA-256` of `.dmp` | `SHA-256` of `.core` |
| Forensic automation | `ProcDump`, `LiveKd` | `gcore`, `systemd-coredump` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Windows Crash Dump Lifecycle (Mermaid Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application / Driver Fault] --> B[CPU Raises Exception]
    B --> C[Kernel Exception Dispatcher]
    C --> D{Is Fault Recoverable?}
    D -- Yes --> E[SEH / Vectored EH Handler]
    D -- No --> F[KeBugCheckEx STOP Code]
    F --> G[DumpStackInitialize]
    G --> H[Allocate NonPaged Pool Buffer]
    H --> I[Capture Physical Memory Pages]
    I --> J{Dump Type?}
    J -- Small --> K1[Write Minidump 64 KB to Minidump dir]
    J -- Kernel --> K2[Write Kernel Dump to MEMORY.DMP]
    J -- Complete --> K3[Write Full Dump size equal to Physical RAM]
    J -- Automatic --> K4[Adaptive dump based on RAM pressure]
    K1 --> L[Reboot System]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    K4 --> L
    L --> M[Forensic Analyst Acquires MEMORY.DMP]
    M --> N[Hash with SHA-256]
    N --> O[WinDbg analyze v]
    O --> P[Forensic Report]
```

## 4.2 Linux Core Dump Generation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[User Process Executes] --> B1[Hardware Fault e.g. Invalid Memory Access]
    B1 --> C1[Kernel Delivers Signal SIGSEGV or SIGABRT]
    C1 --> D1{Core Dump Allowed?}
    D1 -- No RLIMIT CORE equals 0 --> E1[Process Silently Terminated]
    D1 -- Yes --> F1[Read core pattern from proc sys kernel core pattern]
    F1 --> G1[Construct ELF Core File]
    G1 --> H1[Write PT NOTE Program Headers Registers, Signal Info]
    G1 --> H2[Write PT LOAD Program Headers Memory Segments]
    G1 --> H3[Write NT FILE and NT PRPSINFO Notes]
    H1 --> I1[systemd coredump Compresses with LZ4 or ZSTD]
    H2 --> I1
    H3 --> I1
    I1 --> J1[Store in var lib systemd coredump]
    J1 --> K1[Journal Entry Created]
    K1 --> L1[Analyst Runs coredumpctl list]
    L1 --> M1[Extract Core via coredumpctl dump]
    M1 --> N1[GDB Backtrace Analysis]
    N1 --> O1[Forensic Triage Report]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Forensic Acquisition Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WIN[Windows Forensic Pipeline]
        W1[MEMORY.DMP] --> W2[FTK Imager E01 Image]
        W2 --> W3[SHA-256 Hash Verification]
        W3 --> W4[WinDbg analyze v]
        W4 --> W5[Volatility Plugins imageinfo pslist]
    end
    subgraph LIN[Linux Forensic Pipeline]
        L1[evidence.core] --> L2[coredumpctl dump to disk]
        L2 --> L3[SHA-256 Hash Verification]
        L3 --> L4[GDB bt full info registers]
        L4 --> L5[Volatility Linux Profile]
    end
    WIN --> MERGE[Unified Forensic Report]
    LIN --> MERGE
    MERGE --> CHAIN[Chain of Custody Documentation]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between a Small Memory Dump, Kernel Memory Dump, and Complete Memory Dump in Windows.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Small Memory Dump** (also called a Minidump) is approximately **64 KB** in size and is stored in `%SystemRoot%\Minidump`. It contains only essential triage data: the STOP code, list of loaded drivers, and the context (registers + stack) of the faulting thread. It is the fastest to write and smallest on disk, making it ideal for quick root-cause identification.

A **Kernel Memory Dump** captures all kernel-mode memory but excludes most user-mode pages. Its size is approximately equal to the amount of kernel-mode memory in use plus a small header. It is used when driver-level debugging is required without the storage overhead of a full dump.

A **Complete Memory Dump** records the entire contents of physical RAM (size = total installed physical memory). It is used in forensic investigations where complete volatile evidence — including user-mode processes, network state, encryption keys in memory, and clipboard contents — must be preserved. It requires a paging file of at least the size of physical RAM plus **1 MB**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse the file `MEMORY.DMP` as automatically meaning "Complete Dump." In reality, the same filename is reused for both Kernel and Automatic dumps; the actual type is governed by the `CrashDumpEnabled` registry DWORD, not the filename. Failure to mention this loses **1 mark**.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`

**Explain the role of `/proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern` in Linux core dump management.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The sysctl value `/proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern` is a kernel parameter that defines the **naming convention and destination path** for core dump files produced when a user-space process terminates abnormally. It accepts printf-style specifiers such as `%e` (executable name), `%p` (PID), `%t` (timestamp), `%u` (UID), `%g` (GID), `%s` (signal number), and `%h` (hostname).

When the string begins with the pipe symbol `\vert`, the kernel invokes the specified program (the **coredump helper**) instead of writing the dump itself. Modern distributions configure this to invoke `systemd-coredump`, which compresses the dump, indexes it in the systemd journal, and stores it under `/var/lib/systemd/coredump/`.

From a **forensic readiness** perspective, this file is critical: an incorrectly configured `core_pattern` (e.g., pointing to `/dev/null` or being empty) results in **silent loss of crash evidence**, which is a common anti-forensics mistake investigators must detect.

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**a)** Describe in detail the complete Windows Crash Dump generation process. Explain the four dump types with their sizes and typical use cases. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Generation Process:**

1. **Fault Detection:** A user-mode exception that cannot be handled escalates into kernel mode, or a kernel-mode driver directly violates a constraint (e.g., accessing paged memory at `DISPATCH_LEVEL`).
2. **Bugcheck:** The kernel invokes `KeBugCheckEx()`, raising a STOP code such as `0x1A` (`MEMORY_MANAGEMENT`) or `0x3B` (`SYSTEM_SERVICE_EXCEPTION`).
3. **Dump Stack Initialization:** The kernel switches to a special "crash dump stack" running at high IRQL to prevent re-entry.
4. **Memory Capture:** The crash dump driver (`dump_diskdump.sys` or `dump_atapi.sys`) reads all physical pages using a freeze-aware I/O path, writing them sequentially to the dump file.
5. **File Write:** The dump is written to `MEMORY.DMP` (Complete/Kernel/Automatic) or `Minidump\Mini<MMDDYY>-<NN>.dmp`.
6. **Reboot:** System reboots; dump survives because it is on persistent storage.

**Four Dump Types:**

| Type | Size | Registry Value | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small Memory Dump | **64 KB** | `0x3` | Quick driver triage |
| Kernel Memory Dump | ≈ kernel RAM + **1 MB** | `0x2` | Driver debugging, kernel forensics |
| Complete Memory Dump | = **Total physical RAM** | `0x1` | Full volatile forensics |
| Automatic Memory Dump | Adaptive | `0x7` | Auto-sizes between Kernel and Complete |

Each is configured under `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\CrashControl`.

---

**b)** Explain how a forensic investigator would use **WinDbg** to analyze a captured `MEMORY.DMP`. List the essential commands and their purpose. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Acquisition:** Image `MEMORY.DMP` with FTK Imager in E01 format; record SHA-256 hash.

**Step 2 — Loading:** Open WinDbg → `File` → `Open Crash Dump` → select `MEMORY.DMP`. WinDbg displays the STOP code and image timestamp.

**Step 3 — Set Symbols:** Configure Microsoft symbol server:
```
.sympath srv*C:\symbols*https://mssymbols.blob.core.windows.net/symbols
.symfix
.reload
```

**Step 4 — Triage Commands:**

| Command | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `!analyze -v` | Auto-triage: STOP code, faulting module, stack, suggestions |
| `.bugcheck` | Re-display STOP parameters |
| `!vm` | Virtual memory summary (PFN database, pool usage) |
| `!process 0 0` | List all processes with EPROCESS addresses |
| `lm` | List loaded modules with base addresses and timestamps |
| `!thread` | Inspect the faulting thread's stack and IRQL |
| `!poolused` | Tag-based pool allocation analysis |
| `kb` | Stack trace with three arguments per frame |
| `!devnode 0 1` | Device tree enumeration for driver fault context |

**Step 5 — Correlate:** Cross-reference faulting driver timestamp against known vulnerable versions; cross-reference loaded modules against `lcx`/`lovelydump` malware indicators; extract user-mode strings using `!for_each_module .echo @#ModuleName`.

**Step 6 — Report:** Generate a forensic report including STOP code, faulting module + version, stack frames, recommended remediation, and integrity hash.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2025]`

**a)** Explain the Linux core dump mechanism in detail. Discuss the role of `systemd-coredump`, ELF format, and the `/proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern` directive. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Triggering Mechanism:** When a user-space process receives an uncaught fatal signal — `SIGSEGV (11)`, `SIGABRT (6)`, `SIGFPE (8)`, `SIGILL (4)`, or `SIGBUS (7)` — and the process's `RLIMIT_CORE` resource limit is non-zero, the kernel prepares to dump the process memory.

**`/proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern` Directive:** This is a writable sysctl that controls how core dumps are named and where they are stored. Example format specifiers:
- `%e` → executable filename
- `%p` → PID
- `%t` → UNIX time of dump
- `%u` → UID of dumping process
- `%g` → GID
- `%s` → signal number causing dump
- `%h` → hostname
- `%c` → core file size soft limit

If the value begins with a pipe character `\vert`, the kernel invokes the specified helper program instead of writing the core itself.

**systemd-coredump:** The modern Linux standard. `core_pattern` is set to:
```
|/usr/lib/systemd/systemd-coredump %P %u %g %s %t %c %h
```
The systemd helper:
1. Receives the core via stdin.
2. Compresses it using LZ4 or ZSTD.
3. Stores it under `/var/lib/systemd/coredump/core.<exe>.<pid>.<ts>.lz4`.
4. Indexes the entry in the systemd journal (`journalctl --list-boots` accessible).
5. Retains dumps for `CoredumpRetentionDays` from `/etc/systemd/coredump.conf`.

**ELF Core Format:** The file is an ELF object with:
- `e_type = ET_CORE`
- Program headers of type `PT_NOTE` (auxiliary vectors, registers, signal info, file mappings)
- Program headers of type `PT_LOAD` (one per memory segment: `.text`, `.data`, heap, stack, shared libraries)
- A `PT_NOTE` of type `NT_PRPSINFO` containing process status, and `NT_FILE` listing mapped files.

This standardized structure is why tools like GDB, LLDB, and the `crash` utility can analyze core files generically.

---

**b)** Demonstrate how to analyze a Linux core dump using **GDB** for forensic purposes. Provide the command sequence and explain how each command contributes to the investigation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Setup:**
```
gdb /usr/bin/vulnerable_app /var/lib/systemd/coredump/core.vulnerable_app.1234.1700000000.lz4
```
Decompression is automatic via systemd-coredump.

**Step 2 — Register State Analysis:**
```
(gdb) info registers
```
*Purpose:* Identify the instruction pointer `$pc`, stack pointer `$sp`, and segment registers. A `$pc` pointing to non-executable memory (e.g., heap address space with NX bit) is an **IoC of code injection**.

**Step 3 — Stack Backtrace:**
```
(gdb) bt full
(gdb) bt 20
```
*Purpose:* Reconstruct the call chain leading to the crash. Look for:
- Return addresses outside legitimate module addresses → ROP chain indicator.
- Stack frame containing `0x41414141` or similar patterns → buffer overflow confirmation.
- Frames in `libc` functions like `strcpy`, `memcpy`, `gets` → vulnerable code path identified.

**Step 4 — Memory Mapping:**
```
(gdb) info proc mappings
```
*Purpose:* Reveals loaded libraries with base addresses. Cross-reference with `/etc/ld.so.preload` for evidence of **LD_PRELOAD rootkit** attacks. Any segment marked `rwx` indicates potential self-modifying/packed code.

**Step 5 — Stack Examination:**
```
(gdb) x/200xb $sp
(gdb) x/s $sp
```
*Purpose:* Display raw stack bytes — detect shellcode patterns, suspicious strings (URLs, IP addresses, base64-encoded commands).

**Step 6 — Disassembly around crash:**
```
(gdb) disassemble $pc-32, $pc+32
```
*Purpose:* Examine instructions at crash site for unauthorized syscalls, `execve("/bin/sh", ...)` patterns, or `mprotect` calls making stack executable.

**Step 7 — Library Audit:**
```
(gdb) info sharedlibrary
```
*Purpose:* List all loaded shared objects. Verify each against known-good checksums; investigate any unrecognized library — a strong **malware persistence indicator**.

**Step 8 — Report Generation:** Combine findings into a forensic report with timestamps, hashes (`sha256sum core.*`), and chain-of-custody documentation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting cryptographic hashing** of the dump file (loses **1 mark** under forensic best-practices).
> 2. **Confusing kernel dump with user-space core dump** — Windows `MEMORY.DMP` ≠ Linux core dump. They have different formats and analyzers.
> 3. **Not mentioning chain of custody** — every KTU 14-mark question on memory forensics expects explicit acquisition, hash, and documentation steps.
> 4. **Omitting `ulimit -c unlimited`** — without enabling `RLIMIT_CORE`, no dump is produced; many students forget this prerequisite.
> 5. **Calling the Linux dump a "core crash dump"** — terminology matters; the correct term is **core dump** (Linux) or **crash dump** (Windows).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Windows Crash Dump** is generated by `KeBugCheckEx` on STOP errors; Linux **Core Dump** is generated by fatal signals.
- **Four Windows dump types:** Small (64 KB), Kernel, Complete (size = RAM), Automatic — controlled via `HKLM\...\CrashControl\CrashDumpEnabled`.
- **Default Windows dump file:** `%SystemRoot%\MEMORY.DMP`; **Minidumps:** `%SystemRoot%\Minidump\Mini<MMDDYY>-<NN>.dmp`.
- **Linux core dumps** are ELF files (`ET_CORE`) with `PT_NOTE` and `PT_LOAD` program headers.
- **`/proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern`** governs dump naming/path; leading `\vert` invokes a helper program (commonly `systemd-coredump`).
- **`systemd-coredump`** stores dumps in `/var/lib/systemd/coredump/`, indexed in the systemd journal.
- **Primary analyzers:** Windows → **WinDbg** (`!analyze -v`); Linux → **GDB** (`bt full`, `info registers`, `info proc mappings`).
- **Forensic must-do:** Acquire via E01/raw, compute **SHA-256**, maintain **chain of custody**, document in report.
- **Common signals that trigger Linux core dumps:** `SIGSEGV (11)`, `SIGABRT (6)`, `SIGFPE (8)`, `SIGILL (4)`, `SIGBUS (7)`.
- **Enable Linux core dumps** with `ulimit -c unlimited` and set `kernel.core_pattern` in `/etc/sysctl.conf`.
- **Forensic significance:** Crash/core dumps preserve volatile evidence — credentials, encryption keys, malware in memory, exploit traces — that is lost forever once power is removed.
- **Anti-forensics to detect:** empty `core_pattern`, `core_uses_pid=0`, deleted `MEMORY.DMP`, suspicious timestamps, missing `kdump` config on production servers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Investigating Evidences from Linux Logs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Investigating Evidences from Linux Logs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Log Investigation in Linux Forensics** is the systematic process of collecting, preserving, analyzing, and correlating log records generated by the Linux operating system, its services, applications, and user activities to reconstruct events, detect anomalies, identify intrusions, and produce legally admissible digital evidence.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework for Linux Forensics, log investigation is positioned as a **reactive forensic analysis phase** that follows volatile data acquisition and filesystem analysis. It uses the inherent auditing and event-recording infrastructure of Linux to answer the forensic questions of *who, what, when, where, and how*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 Core Concept):**
> Linux logs are considered **secondary persistent evidence** because they survive system reboots and reside in well-defined directories (mostly under `/var/log/`). They are central to non-volatile forensic timelines.

## 1.2 Key Log Categories in Linux

Linux produces logs through several overlapping subsystems. The KTU syllabus categorizes them as:

| Category | Purpose | Forensics Value |
|----------|---------|-----------------|
| **System Logs** | Kernel, boot, daemon events | Establish baseline behaviour |
| **Authentication Logs** | Login attempts, sudo, SSH | Identify unauthorised access |
| **Application Logs** | Web, mail, DB events | Trace application-layer attacks |
| **Security/Audit Logs** | SELinux, AppArmor, auditd | Detect policy violations |
| **Boot & Hardware Logs** | `dmesg`, `kern.log` | Detect rootkits/bootkits |
| **User Activity Logs** | Shell histories, last, wtmp | Reconstruct attacker actions |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The CCTV Control Room of a Building**
> Imagine a large corporate building. Every door has an entry log, every CCTV camera writes to a recording room, every ATM transaction prints a slip, and every elevator records who pressed which floor. A forensic investigator arriving *after* a robbery would walk into the control room, gather the door logs, CCTV timestamps, ATM slips, and elevator records, then **time-correlate** them to reconstruct the thief's path.
>
> Linux logs work the **same way**: every login, file change, command execution, and network connection leaves a *digital footprint* in a log file. The forensic investigator is the detective in the control room, and the logs are the timestamps that prove (or disprove) what really happened.

## 1.4 Importance in KTU Examinations

Log investigation is a **high-weightage topic** in Module 4 because it directly tests:
- **CO4 (Apply):** Use log analysis tools to detect incidents
- **CO5 (Analyze):** Correlate logs across multiple sources
- **CO6 (Evaluate):** Detect log tampering and assess evidence integrity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners in KTU value two things in log-based answers:
> 1. **Path knowledge** — knowing the exact location of each log file
> 2. **Field interpretation** — explaining what each field in a log line means
>
> Memorising only file paths without field-level analysis will yield only **partial marks (3–4 out of 7)** in sub-questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linux Logging Architecture

Modern Linux systems use a **layered logging architecture**. Understanding each layer is essential for the KTU examination.

### Layer 1: Kernel Ring Buffer
- The Linux kernel writes early boot and hardware messages to an in-memory ring buffer.
- Accessed via the `dmesg` command.
- Contents can be persisted to `/var/log/dmesg` or `/var/log/kern.log`.

### Layer 2: Syslog Protocol (RFC 5424)
- The original standard (RFC 3164) and modern standard (RFC 5424) define a **client–server logging protocol**.
- Uses the **facility.severity** notation to classify messages.
- A typical syslog message has the format:
  `TIMESTAMP HOSTNAME TAG[PID]: MESSAGE`

### Layer 3: Syslog Daemon Implementations
Three major daemons are used to receive and route messages:

| Daemon | Used In | Configuration File |
|--------|---------|-------------------|
| **syslogd** | Older systems | `/etc/syslog.conf` |
| **rsyslogd** | RHEL, CentOS, Ubuntu (default) | `/etc/rsyslog.conf` |
| **syslog-ng** | SUSE, Fedora, advanced distros | `/etc/syslog-ng/syslog-ng.conf` |

### Layer 4: systemd-journald
- Modern Linux distributions using `systemd` log to a **binary journal**.
- Journal files stored in `/var/log/journal/<machine-id>/`.
- Queried using the `journalctl` command.
- Provides structured, indexed, and tamper-evident logging.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two systems often coexist:** `journald` handles early boot and structured logging, while `rsyslog` handles long-term text-based archival. Many distros forward journald output to rsyslog for compatibility with traditional log analysis tools.

## 2.2 Standard Linux Log File Locations

The KTU syllabus expects familiarity with the canonical log paths in `/var/log/`:

| Log File | Source / Facility | Forensic Information |
|----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| `/var/log/syslog` or `/var/log/messages` | General system events | Service starts/stops, errors |
| `/var/log/auth.log` (Debian) or `/var/log/secure` (RHEL) | Authentication, sudo, SSH | Login attempts, privilege escalation |
| `/var/log/kern.log` | Kernel messages | Hardware events, driver loads, rootkits |
| `/var/log/dmesg` | Boot-time kernel ring buffer | Detects bootkits, kernel tampering |
| `/var/log/boot.log` | Boot script output | Detects service manipulation at boot |
| `/var/log/cron` | Cron job execution | Detects scheduled malware/backdoors |
| `/var/log/daemon.log` | Background daemons | Detects abnormal daemon behaviour |
| `/var/log/mail.log` or `/var/log/maillog` | Mail server (Postfix, Sendmail) | Spam relay detection, phishing |
| `/var/log/apache2/` (Debian) or `/var/log/httpd/` (RHEL) | Apache web server | Web attacks, defacements |
| `/var/log/nginx/` | Nginx web server | Reverse proxy logs, web attacks |
| `/var/log/btmp` | **Failed** login attempts | Brute-force detection (binary) |
| `/var/log/wtmp` | **Successful** logins (binary) | User session history |
| `/var/log/utmp` | Currently logged-in users | Live session info |
| `/var/log/lastlog` | Last login of each user | Anomalous user activity |
| `/var/log/faillog` | Failed login count per user | Brute-force tracking |
| `/var/log/audit/audit.log` | `auditd` framework | Detailed syscall & file audit trail |

## 2.3 Syslog Priority Formula

The **facility** and **severity** values follow a standard numeric encoding, which is occasionally tested in KTU numerical problems.

The syslog **Priority (PRI)** value is calculated as:

$$
\text{PRI} = (\text{Facility} \times 8) + \text{Severity}
$$

Where:
- **Facility** = integer 0–23 (e.g., auth = 4, cron = 9, local0 = 16)
- **Severity** = integer 0–7 (0 = emerg, 3 = err, 6 = info, 7 = debug)

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** A message from `auth` facility (4) with `notice` severity (5) gives:
> PRI = (4 × 8) + 5 = 37

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Value / Path | Examiner Tip |
|---------|------------------|--------------|
| Primary log directory | **`/var/log/`** | Always the starting point of an investigation |
| Failed login binary log | `/var/log/btmp` | Read with `lastb` command |
| Successful login binary log | `/var/log/wtmp` | Read with `last` command |
| Last login of every user | `/var/log/lastlog` | Read with `lastlog` command |
| Live user sessions | `/var/log/utmp` | Read with `who`, `w` |
| Auth log (Debian) | `/var/log/auth.log` | SSH + sudo events |
| Auth log (RHEL) | `/var/log/secure` | Same as auth.log |
| systemd binary logs | `/var/log/journal/` | Query with `journalctl` |
| Audit framework | `/var/log/audit/audit.log` | Managed by `auditd` |
| Syslog config (rsyslog) | `/etc/rsyslog.conf` | Filters log by facility/severity |
| Syslog config (syslog-ng) | `/etc/syslog-ng/syslog-ng.conf` | Uses different syntax |
| Facility for auth | **4** | Often tested numerically |
| Severity for info | **6** | Often tested numerically |
| Auth-priv facility | **10** | Secure/private messages |

## 2.5 Why Log Investigation Matters in Real Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Relevance:**
> - **Incident Response (IR):** SOC analysts use Linux logs to detect breaches within minutes.
> - **Compliance:** PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR mandate log retention and audit.
> - **Threat Hunting:** Adversaries often disable logging — detecting this *is* the investigation.
> - **Root Cause Analysis:** Log timeline correlation is the most defensible evidence of an attack chain.
> - **Legal Proceedings:** Properly preserved logs are admissible under Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act (analogous to U.S. Federal Rules of Evidence 902).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: What Each Log Reveals

The following matrix maps a **forensic question** to the **specific log file** and **command** that answers it. This is a high-yield KTU analytical framework.

| Forensic Question | Log File | Command | Typical Evidence |
|-------------------|----------|---------|------------------|
| Who logged in successfully? | `/var/log/wtmp` | `last`, `last -F` | Username, terminal, IP, duration |
| Who tried but failed to log in? | `/var/log/btmp` | `lastb` | Source IP of brute-force |
| When did a user last log in? | `/var/log/lastlog` | `lastlog` | Per-user last login timestamp |
| Who is currently logged in? | `/var/log/utmp` | `who`, `w`, `users` | Live session info |
| What did the user run as root? | `/var/log/auth.log` or `/var/log/secure` | `grep sudo` | sudo command, user, TTY |
| Was a file accessed? | `/var/log/audit/audit.log` | `ausearch` | Syscall name, file path, user |
| Did the kernel load a module? | `/var/log/kern.log` | `grep -i "module"` | `insmod`, `modprobe` traces |
| Did a service restart? | `/var/log/syslog` | `grep -i "systemd"` | Service start/stop events |
| Did a cron job run? | `/var/log/cron` | `grep CRON` | User, command, time |
| Was the log itself tampered? | All logs | Compare mtime, hash, gaps | Missing entries, time jumps |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Reading a Syslog Entry

A typical auth log line looks like this:

`May 14 03:22:17 server1 sshd[2318]: Failed password for invalid user admin from 192.168.1.55 port 50422 ssh2`

### Step-by-Step Field Interpretation

**Step 1 — Timestamp:**
`May 14 03:22:17` → Date and time of event. Used to build the **forensic timeline**.

**Step 2 — Hostname:**
`server1` → The system that produced the event. Useful in multi-host investigations.

**Step 3 — Application Tag and PID:**
`sshd[2318]` → The process that generated the message. PID 2318 helps correlate with `/proc` snapshots taken during volatile acquisition.

**Step 4 — Message Body:**
`Failed password for invalid user admin from 192.168.1.55 port 50422 ssh2` → Contains the *who* (invalid user "admin"), *source* (192.168.1.55), and *protocol* (ssh2).

### Derived Forensic Conclusion

Using the formula below, the **total failed attempts from that IP** in the same hour can be quantified as:

$$
N_{\text{fail}} = \sum_{t \in T_{\text{window}}} \mathbf{1}\{\text{event}(t) = \text{Failed password from } 192.168.1.55\}
$$

Where $T_{\text{window}}$ is the time window of interest (e.g., 1 hour) and $\mathbf{1}\{\cdot\}$ is the indicator function that returns 1 if the condition holds.

This becomes a **brute-force detection signal** when $N_{\text{fail}} > \text{threshold}$ (typically > 20 in 5 minutes).

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Syslog Priority Calculation (Worked Example)

**Problem (KTU-style):** A log message originates from the `auth` facility (numeric 4) with severity `notice` (numeric 5). Calculate the PRI value and identify whether it falls in the "warning" or "informational" range.

### Step 1 — Identify Facility and Severity
Facility $F = 4$ (auth)
Severity $S = 5$ (notice)

### Step 2 — Apply the Priority Formula
The priority calculation in the syslog protocol is:

$$
\text{PRI} = (F \times 8) + S
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\text{PRI} = (4 \times 8) + 5
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Arithmetic

$$
\text{PRI} = 32 + 5 = 37
$$

### Step 4 — Interpret the Result
A PRI of 37 falls in the range $32$–$39$, which corresponds to the **`auth` facility** in the rsyslog standard mapping. This means rsyslog will route it to files such as `/var/log/auth.log` (Debian) or `/var/log/secure` (RHEL) based on the rules in `/etc/rsyslog.conf`.

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation
Any message with facility=4 and severity ≥ 5 is **operationally significant** and should be retained for forensic audit.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Detecting Log Tampering

A classic KTU question is *how to detect that an attacker has cleared a log*.

### Step 1 — Check File Existence and Size
An unusually small or zero-byte `/var/log/wtmp` or `/var/log/auth.log` is the first red flag.

### Step 2 — Compare Timestamps
Use `stat <logfile>` and check `Modify` time. If `mtime` is older than expected, the file may have been rotated or **overwritten**.

### Step 3 — Check for Truncation Holes
Truncate a log with `> /var/log/auth.log` leaves a **gap**. Compare log sequence numbers or use tools like `logwatch` to detect missing events.

### Step 4 — Compare with Remote/Immutable Logs
If logs are forwarded to a **remote syslog server** (`@@remote-server:514`), the remote copy is the **golden source** for comparison.

### Step 5 — Check Audit Framework
The Linux Audit Daemon (`auditd`) can be configured with the **`-e 2`** flag to make logs **immutable** in memory. This is the strongest defence against tampering.

### Step 6 — Cross-Reference with Shell History
Compare `~/.bash_history` entries with `wtmp` sessions. If a session exists in `wtmp` but no history is present, the user may have run `unset HISTFILE` or `history -c` to evade detection.

### Derived Tamper Score

A simple forensic tamper heuristic is:

$$
T_{\text{score}} = w_1 \cdot \mathbf{1}\{\text{mtime\_anomaly}\} + w_2 \cdot \mathbf{1}\{\text{size\_anomaly}\} + w_3 \cdot \mathbf{1}\{\text{gap\_detected}\} + w_4 \cdot \mathbf{1}\{\text{hash\_mismatch}\}
$$

Where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$ are weighted factors. A $T_{\text{score}} > 0.5$ indicates **high probability of tampering** and warrants deeper investigation.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Building a Forensic Timeline from Logs

**Step 1 — Acquire Logs in a Forensically Sound Manner**
Use `dd` or `tar` to copy the `/var/log/` directory and compute SHA-256 hashes for chain of custody.

$$
H_{\text{file}} = \text{SHA256}(\text{file contents})
$$

**Step 2 — Parse All Log Files into a Unified Time Format**
Convert each log entry to **ISO 8601** format: `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ`.

**Step 3 — Merge Sources in Time Order**
Use `log2timeline` (Plaso framework) to merge syslog, auth.log, wtmp, audit.log, and shell histories into a single super-timeline.

**Step 4 — Apply Filters**
Filter by user, IP, file path, or keyword to isolate the attack window.

**Step 5 — Annotate and Export**
Produce a final timeline CSV/HTML for the investigation report.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Linux Logging Architecture — Mermaid Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Application Service] --> A1[libc syslog call]
    A1 --> A2[Unix Domain Socket /dev/log]
    B0[Kernel] --> B1[kernel ring buffer]
    B1 --> B2[klogd / systemd]
    C0[Audit Subsystem] --> C1[auditd daemon]
    A2 --> D1[rsyslogd or syslog-ng]
    B2 --> D1
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2[Filter by facility.severity]
    D2 --> E1[/var/log/syslog/]
    D2 --> E2[/var/log/auth.log/]
    D2 --> E3[/var/log/kern.log/]
    D2 --> E4[/var/log/mail.log/]
    D1 --> F1[systemd-journald]
    F1 --> F2[/var/log/journal/ binary/]
    E1 --> G1[Forensic Investigator]
    E2 --> G1
    E3 --> G1
    E4 --> G1
    F2 --> G2[journalctl query]
    G2 --> G1
    D1 --> H1[Remote Syslog Server over TLS]
    H1 --> G1
```

## 4.2 Log Investigation Workflow — Mermaid Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Acquire logs] --> P2[Step 2: Hash and preserve]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Parse into timeline]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Filter by keyword]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Correlate with auth.log]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Detect anomalies]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Detect tampering]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Document findings]
```

## 4.3 Syslog Routing Rules — Mermaid Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[auth.info] --> R1[/var/log/auth.log/]
    S2[kern.*] --> R2[/var/log/kern.log/]
    S3[mail.crit] --> R3[/var/log/mail.log/]
    S4[cron.*] --> R4[/var/log/cron/]
    S5[*.emerg] --> R5[* All logged-in users /]
    S6[local0.notice] --> R6[/var/log/app/app.log/]
    S7[authpriv.*] --> R7[/var/log/secure/]
```

## 4.4 Attack Detection via Log Correlation — Mermaid Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Attacker
    participant SSH as SSH Daemon
    participant AuthLog as auth.log
    participant SudoLog as secure or auth.log
    participant Audit as auditd
    participant Forensic as Forensic Analyst

    Attacker->>SSH: Brute-force 200 attempts
    SSH->>AuthLog: Failed password entries x200
    Attacker->>SSH: Successful login
    SSH->>AuthLog: Accepted publickey
    Attacker->>SudoLog: sudo su -
    SudoLog->>SudoLog: session opened for root
    Attacker->>Audit: Modify /etc/passwd
    Audit->>Audit: SYSCALL audit record
    Forensic->>AuthLog: lastb | grep attacker IP
    Forensic->>SudoLog: grep sudo auth.log
    Forensic->>Audit: ausearch -f /etc/passwd
    Forensic->>Forensic: Correlate timeline
```

## 4.5 Log Forensics Tool Map — Mermaid Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Linux Log Forensics Tools] --> T1[Built-in Commands]
    T0 --> T2[Log Analysis Suites]
    T0 --> T3[Timeline Generators]
    T0 --> T4[SIEM Platforms]
    T1 --> T1A[last]
    T1 --> T1B[lastb]
    T1 --> T1C[lastlog]
    T1 --> T1D[who and w]
    T1 --> T1E[dmesg]
    T1 --> T1F[journalctl]
    T2 --> T2A[logwatch]
    T2 --> T2B[goaccess]
    T2 --> T2C[awstats]
    T2 --> T2D[graylog]
    T3 --> T3A[Plaso or log2timeline]
    T3 --> T3B[Timesketch]
    T4 --> T4A[Splunk]
    T4 --> T4B[ELK Stack]
    T4 --> T4C[Wazuh]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any six important Linux log files and their forensic significance.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **`/var/log/auth.log` (Debian/Ubuntu) or `/var/log/secure` (RHEL/CentOS):** Records authentication events such as SSH logins, sudo commands, and PAM events. Used to identify unauthorised access attempts and privilege escalation.

2. **`/var/log/syslog` (Debian) or `/var/log/messages` (RHEL):** Captures general system-wide messages from daemons, services, and the kernel. Used for reconstructing system-level events.

3. **`/var/log/kern.log`:** Stores kernel-generated messages including module loading, hardware errors, and driver activity. Useful for detecting rootkits and kernel-level attacks.

4. **`/var/log/wtmp`:** Binary file logging **successful** logins and logouts. Read with the `last` command. Provides the login duration and source terminal.

5. **`/var/log/btmp`:** Binary file recording **failed** login attempts. Read with the `lastb` command. Primary source for brute-force attack detection.

6. **`/var/log/audit/audit.log`:** Output of the `auditd` framework. Records detailed syscall, file access, and command execution events. The most granular source of forensic evidence.

7. **`/var/log/lastlog`:** Binary file storing the last login time of every user account. Read with the `lastlog` command. Useful for spotting accounts that have never been used or show anomalous login times.

*(Any six correctly explained earn full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between `wtmp`, `btmp`, and `utmp` log files in Linux.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `wtmp` | `btmp` | `utmp` |
|---------|--------|--------|--------|
| **Purpose** | Records successful logins and logouts | Records failed login attempts | Records currently logged-in users |
| **Type** | Binary | Binary | Binary |
| **Command to read** | `last` | `lastb` | `who`, `w`, `users` |
| **Information** | Username, terminal, source IP, duration, reboot entries | Username, source IP of failed attempts | Username, terminal, login time |
| **Forensic use** | Reconstruct user session history | Detect brute-force and credential-stuffing | Identify live intruders on the system |
| **Location** | `/var/log/wtmp` | `/var/log/btmp` | `/var/run/utmp` or `/var/log/utmp` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A common mistake is treating all three as text files. They are **binary structures** that *must* be read with dedicated utilities. Trying to `cat` or `grep` them produces garbage output. Examiners deduct 1 mark for missing this fact.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** A 14-mark question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each: one descriptive/conceptual and one analytical/command-based.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A web server running on Ubuntu 22.04 LTS was found to be defaced. You are the forensic investigator. Describe in detail the role of Linux logs in this investigation. As part of your answer:**

**(a) List and explain the logs you will examine, the commands you will use, and the specific evidence you expect to find in each.** [7 marks]

**(b) Demonstrate with a worked example how you would calculate the syslog priority value for an authentication failure message and explain how this is routed by rsyslog.** [7 marks]

---

#### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 Marks

**Logs to examine and their forensic relevance:**

**1. `/var/log/auth.log`** [1 mark]
- Command: `grep -i "fail" /var/log/auth.log`, `grep "Accepted" /var/log/auth.log`
- Evidence: SSH brute-force attempts, successful logins, sudo command history, PAM authentication failures. This is the **most critical** log in a web-defacement case because attackers often gain shell access before defacing.

**2. `/var/log/wtmp` and `/var/log/btmp`** [1 mark]
- Commands: `last`, `lastb`
- Evidence: `wtmp` shows all users who logged in before the defacement; `btmp` reveals any brute-force attempts from external IPs. Cross-reference with auth.log timestamps to identify the attacker's session window.

**3. `/var/log/apache2/access.log` and `error.log`** [2 marks]
- Commands: `grep -E "POST|exec|system|union|select" access.log`, `awk '{print $1}' access.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn`
- Evidence: HTTP requests to the defacement URL, source IP of the attacker, SQL-injection or file-upload patterns, suspicious 200/302/500 status codes. This **directly evidences the defacement** event.

**4. `/var/log/syslog`** [1 mark]
- Commands: `grep -i "apache\|httpd\|systemd" /var/log/syslog`
- Evidence: Apache restart events that may have been triggered by the attacker to reload a planted backdoor or webshell.

**5. `/var/log/audit/audit.log` and `journalctl`** [1 mark]
- Commands: `ausearch -m USER_LOGIN,EXECVE`, `journalctl -u apache2 --since "yesterday"`
- Evidence: Syscall-level record of file modifications in `/var/www/html/`, command executions by the web user `www-data`, and Apache service lifecycle events.

**6. `~/.bash_history` of suspected user accounts** [1 mark]
- Commands: `cat /home/<user>/.bash_history`, `history`
- Evidence: Commands run by the attacker post-login, including `wget` of webshells, `chmod 777`, `mv` operations. Important to check for `unset HISTFILE` indications of evasion.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify the Facility [1 mark]**
The message originates from authentication. The standard syslog facility for authentication is **`auth`** with numeric value **4**.

**Step 2 — Identify the Severity [1 mark]**
An authentication failure is typically logged at severity **`info`** (numeric value **6**) in modern rsyslog configurations. Some legacy systems use `warning` (4) or `notice` (5).

**Step 3 — Apply the Priority Formula [2 marks]**

$$
\text{PRI} = (F \times 8) + S
$$

Substituting $F = 4$ and $S = 6$:

$$
\text{PRI} = (4 \times 8) + 6 = 32 + 6 = 38
$$

**Step 4 — Verify Range [1 mark]**
The PRI value 38 falls within the auth facility range of 32 to 39, confirming the calculation.

**Step 5 — Routing by rsyslog [1 mark]**
The default rsyslog rule for auth facilities in `/etc/rsyslog.conf` is:

`auth,authpriv.*   /var/log/auth.log`

Because our message has facility=4 (auth) and severity=6 (info), it satisfies the wildcard `.*` and is **appended to `/var/log/auth.log`**.

**Step 6 — Engineering Conclusion [1 mark]**
The investigator should filter `/var/log/auth.log` for entries with PRI value 38 to isolate all auth-info messages during the attack window. This is how an automated log-analysis pipeline (e.g., ELK or Wazuh) classifies the event.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: During a routine audit, you suspect that the system administrator has cleared `/var/log/wtmp` and `/var/log/auth.log` to hide unauthorised access. As a forensic investigator:**

**(a) Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure you will follow to detect log tampering and recover the missing evidence. Include the commands, file paths, and the reasoning at each step.** [7 marks]

**(b) Describe the role of the Linux Audit Daemon (`auditd`) in preventing such tampering, with specific rules you would add to `/etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules`.** [7 marks]

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Verify the Suspicion by Checking File Metadata** [1 mark]
Command: `ls -la /var/log/wtmp /var/log/auth.log`
Reasoning: A zero-byte wtmp or auth.log with a recent `mtime` is the first strong indicator. A normally functioning server produces a continuously growing wtmp file.

**Step 2 — Use `stat` for Detailed Metadata** [1 mark]
Command: `stat /var/log/wtmp`
Reasoning: `Birth` time and `Modify` time anomalies (e.g., modified during off-hours) corroborate tampering.

**Step 3 — Check the Size of `btmp` versus Historical Norms** [1 mark]
Command: `wc -c /var/log/btmp` and compare with previous baselines.
Reasoning: If `btmp` is unusually small but login attempts were high, the attacker may have selectively removed only their own failures.

**Step 4 — Cross-Reference with Remote Syslog Server** [1 mark]
Command: `ssh remote-syslog 'grep -i "admin" /var/log/remote/auth.log'`
Reasoning: If the organisation forwards logs to a remote server (e.g., via `@@siem.internal:514`), the remote copy contains the unmodified truth. The local file has been **selectively pruned**.

**Step 5 — Analyse the Journal** [1 mark]
Command: `journalctl --since "3 months ago" | grep -i "sshd"`
Reasoning: systemd-journald may have persisted entries even if the text log was wiped. The binary journal `/var/log/journal/` is often overlooked by attackers.

**Step 6 — Check for `ausearch` and `aureport` Output** [1 mark]
Command: `ausearch -m USER_LOGIN,USER_AUTH -ts today`, `aureport --summary`
Reasoning: auditd logs to a separate file and is rarely tampered with. It retains a complete record of authentication syscalls.

**Step 7 — Examine Shell Histories and Core Dumps** [1 mark]
Commands: `cat /root/.bash_history`, `ls /var/crash/`
Reasoning: Even if logs are wiped, the attacker's commands may remain in the root user's shell history. Core dumps may also contain useful artefacts.

**Conclusion:** A combination of remote log comparison, journald recovery, auditd records, and shell-history analysis allows reconstruction of the attack timeline even when the primary logs are tampered with.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Install and Enable `auditd`** [1 mark]
Commands:
`sudo apt install auditd` and `sudo systemctl enable --now auditd`
Reasoning: The audit framework must be running before any rules are applied.

**Step 2 — Lock the Audit Configuration** [1 mark]
Add the following line at the top of `/etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules`:
`-e 2`
Reasoning: This makes the audit configuration **immutable** until the system is rebooted. The attacker cannot disable auditd even with root access.

**Step 3 — Audit Changes to Authentication Files** [2 marks]
Rules:
`-w /etc/passwd -p wa -k identity`
`-w /etc/shadow -p wa -k identity`
`-w /etc/sudoers -p wa -k sudoers`
`-w /etc/ssh/sshd_config -p wa -k sshd_config`
Reasoning: These rules watch for **write and attribute changes** to critical authentication files. Any modification — even by root — is recorded with a timestamp, user, and syscall.

**Step 4 — Audit Log File Deletions and Modifications** [1 mark]
Rule:
`-w /var/log/ -p wa -k logfile_changes`
Reasoning: This generates an audit event whenever any log file under `/var/log/` is modified, renamed, or deleted. The investigator can detect the tampering attempt *in real time*.

**Step 5 — Audit All sudo and su Executions** [1 mark]
Rule:
`-w /usr/bin/sudo -p x -k privilege_escalation`
Reasoning: Captures every invocation of `sudo`, including the invoking user, target user, and command.

**Step 6 — Configure `audisp-remote` for Off-Host Forwarding** [1 mark]
Configuration in `/etc/audisp/audisp-remote.conf`:
`remote_server = siem.internal`
`transport = tcp`
Reasoning: Even if the attacker disables local audit logging, the remote audit server continues to receive events over a separate channel, providing the **golden copy** for forensic reconstruction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing `wtmp`/`btmp`/`utmp` paths or commands** — Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for stating `cat /var/log/wtmp` instead of `last /var/log/wtmp`.
> 2. **Omitting field-level interpretation** — Writing "I will check auth.log" earns only 1 mark. You must explain *what fields* (timestamp, user, source IP) you will extract.
> 3. **Forgetting the `journalctl` aspect** — In the 2024 scheme, the systemd journal is explicitly mentioned. Skipping it signals outdated knowledge.
> 4. **Confusing rsyslog and syslog-ng** — The 2024 syllabus distinguishes them. Mixing syntax between the two costs marks.
> 5. **Forgetting immutable audit** — The `-e 2` flag is a favourite KTU point. Not mentioning it indicates shallow preparation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Linux Log Investigation (Module 4)**

- **Primary log directory is always `/var/log/`.** Begin every investigation here.
- **Three binary logs** — `wtmp` (success), `btmp` (failure), `utmp` (current). Use `last`, `lastb`, `who` to read them. Never `cat` them.
- **`auth.log` (Debian) vs `secure` (RHEL)** contain the same information; path differs by distro.
- **`kern.log` and `dmesg`** are essential for rootkit and bootkit detection.
- **`auditd` is the most granular evidence source.** Configure with `-e 2` for immutability and forward events to a remote SIEM.
- **`systemd-journald` stores binary logs** under `/var/log/journal/`. Query with `journalctl`. Often overlooked by attackers.
- **Syslog Priority formula:** $\text{PRI} = (F \times 8) + S$. Facility for `auth` is **4**; severity for `info` is **6**.
- **rsyslog config** is in `/etc/rsyslog.conf`. Rules use `facility.severity` notation, e.g., `auth.* /var/log/auth.log`.
- **Log tampering detection** — check `mtime`, `size`, compare with remote copies, look for `unset HISTFILE` traces in shell history.
- **Timeline creation** — use Plaso/`log2timeline` to merge sources into a single super-timeline.
- **Tools to remember:** `last`, `lastb`, `lastlog`, `who`, `w`, `dmesg`, `journalctl`, `ausearch`, `aureport`, `grep`, `awk`, `plaso/log2timeline`.
- **Industry keywords:** SOC, SIEM, ELK, Wazuh, Splunk, PCI-DSS, Section 65B, chain of custody, hash verification.
- **Always preserve with hashes:** `sha256sum /var/log/* > evidence_hashes.txt` before any analysis.
- **Cross-reference multiple sources** — auth.log + wtmp + audit.log + shell history form a triangulated evidence chain.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Configuration Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT866 - WINDOWS AND LINUX FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Network Configuration Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Network Configuration Analysis** in digital forensics is the systematic process of identifying, extracting, preserving, and interpreting the network-related settings, parameters, state information, and connection artifacts from a Windows or Linux computing system to reconstruct its historical and present network behavior. It is a critical sub-discipline of host-based forensics that focuses on volatile (live) and non-volatile (persistent) network evidence such as **IP addressing schemes**, **routing tables**, **DNS resolver configurations**, **ARP caches**, **firewall rule sets**, **active sockets**, **wireless profiles**, and **VPN configurations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT866 - Module 4):**
> *Network Configuration Analysis is the forensic examination of the operating system's networking subsystem — including interface configurations, name resolution data, routing policies, listening ports, and firewall policies — to establish an evidentiary trail of network identity, connectivity, and communication patterns at a given point in time.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Post Office Network" View

Imagine a city post office. To understand where a suspect has been sending letters, the forensic investigator doesn't just look at the letters themselves — they first look at the **post office configuration board**:

- Which **post office boxes** (IP addresses) were assigned to the suspect's building (host)?
- Which **delivery routes** (routing table entries) were active?
- Which **local directory** (DNS resolver / hosts file) was used to look up addresses?
- Which **security guards** (firewall rules) were stationed at which doors?
- Which **outgoing trucks** (active TCP/UDP connections) were loaded and dispatched?

**Network Configuration Analysis is exactly this — reading the post office board to reconstruct who the system was talking to, how it identified destinations, what was permitted/denied, and whether the configuration itself was tampered with.** If a backdoor was planted, the firewall rule allowing it is like finding a guard who was bribed — and the firewall logs are the witness statement.

## 1.3 Why Network Configuration Analysis Matters in Forensics

| Forensic Objective | What Network Configuration Reveals |
|---|---|
| **Attribution** | IP address, MAC address, hostname, DHCP lease identity |
| **C2 Communication** | DNS servers queried, proxy settings, VPN endpoints, static routes to attacker networks |
| **Lateral Movement** | SMB shares mounted, RDP/SSH services exposed, ARP neighbors discovered |
| **Persistence** | Services bound to unusual ports, scheduled tasks with network components, suspicious firewall allow-rules |
| **Anti-Forensics** | Disabled logging, cleared DNS cache, manipulated hosts file, MAC spoofing evidence |
| **Timeline Reconstruction** | DHCP lease times, wireless profile last-connected timestamps, route table changes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Volatile vs. Non-Volatile Network Evidence:**
> - **Volatile** (lost on reboot): ARP cache, routing table in kernel, active socket table, DNS resolver cache, current connection state.
> - **Non-Volatile** (persisted on disk): `/etc/network/interfaces`, Windows Registry (`HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip`), DNS client cache database (`%SystemRoot%\System32\drivers\etc\hosts`), firewall rule stores, wireless profile XML files.
>
> The **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)** mandates capturing volatile network state **first** before any disk-level imaging.

## 1.4 Core Artifacts — At a Glance

The following artifacts are universally recognized as the **"core 12"** of network configuration analysis on Windows and Linux:

1. **IP Address & Subnet Mask** (interface binding)
2. **Default Gateway**
3. **DNS Server Addresses** (resolver configuration)
4. **MAC Address / Hardware Address**
5. **Routing Table** (static and dynamic routes)
6. **ARP / Neighbor Cache**
7. **Active Listening Ports** (TCP/UDP)
8. **Established Connections** (socket table)
9. **Firewall Rules** (iptables, Windows Filtering Platform)
10. **Hosts File** (local name overrides)
11. **Proxy Configuration** (WPAD, PAC files, HTTP_PROXY env)
12. **Wireless / VPN Profiles** (SSID history, tunnel endpoints)

These will be analyzed in exhaustive detail in **SECTION 2**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition — The Network Configuration Stack

A computer's networking subsystem is best understood as a **layered stack** of configuration sources. Forensically, each layer must be queried independently, because an attacker may tamper with one layer while leaving others untouched (defense-in-evasion).

### Layer 1 — Link Layer (Layer 2) Configuration
- **Windows:** `ipconfig /all` reports physical (MAC) addresses per adapter. Registry key `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Class\{4D36E972-E325-11CE-BFC1-08002BE10318}` stores adapter descriptions and driver parameters.
- **Linux:** `ip link show`, `ifconfig -a`, `/sys/class/net/<iface>/address`.
- **Forensic value:** MAC address uniquely identifies the NIC. **MAC spoofing** is detectable by comparing the current value against burned-in address (BIA) stored in firmware/EEPROM.

### Layer 2 — Network Layer (Layer 3) Configuration
- **Windows:** `ipconfig` (IPv4/IPv6), `Get-NetIPAddress` (PowerShell), `netsh interface ip show config`.
- **Linux:** `ip addr show`, `cat /proc/net/fib_trie`, `ifconfig`, `/etc/network/interfaces` or NetworkManager profiles in `/etc/NetworkManager/system-connections/`.
- **Forensic value:** Reveals dual-stack configurations, link-local addresses (169.254.x.x indicating APIPA / DHCP failure), and unusual subnet assignments.

### Layer 3 — Routing Table (Layer 3 Forwarding Decisions)
- **Windows:** `route print`, `Get-NetRoute`.
- **Linux:** `ip route show`, `netstat -rn`, `/proc/net/route`.
- **Forensic value:** A **default route pointing to an attacker-controlled gateway**, or a **specific /32 host route to a C2 server**, is a high-confidence indicator of compromise.

### Layer 4 — Name Resolution (Application-Layer Helper)
- **Windows:** `ipconfig /displaydns` (in-memory cache), `C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts`, registry `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip\Parameters` (search list, domain suffix).
- **Linux:** `/etc/resolv.conf` (DNS servers, search domains), `/etc/nsswitch.conf` (resolution order), `/etc/hosts`, `systemd-resolved` cache.
- **Forensic value:** The **hosts file** is a classic persistence and phishing vector — entries like `127.0.0.1 microsoft.com` redirect legitimate traffic to localhost.

### Layer 5 — Firewall / Packet Filter Rules
- **Windows:** `netsh advfirewall show allprofiles`, Windows Filtering Platform (WFP) via `netsh wfp show state`, `Get-NetFirewallRule`.
- **Linux:** `iptables -L -n -v`, `iptables-save`, `nft list ruleset`, `ufw status verbose`.
- **Forensic value:** A rule allowing inbound traffic on a non-standard port, or a **deny rule that was deliberately added to block anti-virus update servers**, is direct evidence.

### Layer 6 — Active State (Sockets, Connections, ARP)
- **Windows:** `netstat -ano`, `Get-NetTCPConnection`, ARP via `arp -a` or `Get-NetNeighbor`.
- **Linux:** `ss -tunap`, `netstat -tunap`, `ip neigh show`, `/proc/net/tcp` (hex-encoded).
- **Forensic value:** A listening service on a port without a corresponding legitimate executable is a **rootkit indicator**.

### Layer 7 — Proxy / VPN / Wireless Profiles
- **Windows:** `netsh winhttp show proxy`, registry `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Internet Settings`, Wi-Fi profiles at `C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Wlansvc\Profiles\Interfaces\*.xml`.
- **Linux:** Environment variables (`HTTP_PROXY`, `HTTPS_PROXY`), `/etc/apt/apt.conf.d/proxy.conf`, NetworkManager VPN files in `/etc/NetworkManager/system-connections/`.
- **Forensic value:** Reveals out-of-band communication channels and unauthorized wireless associations (evil twin attacks).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-critical cheat sheet**. Every cell is a potential Part A (3-mark) or Part B (14-mark) question stem.

| # | Artifact | Windows Location / Command | Linux Location / Command | Forensic Indicator |
|---|----------|---------------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | IP Address | `ipconfig /all` | `ip addr show` | APIPA (169.254.x.x) = DHCP failure / isolation |
| 2 | MAC Address | `ipconfig /all` → Physical Address | `ip link show` → link/ether | Mismatch with burned-in = spoofing |
| 3 | Default Gateway | `ipconfig` | `ip route show default` | Gateway pointing to attacker subnet |
| 4 | DNS Servers | `ipconfig /all` | `cat /etc/resolv.conf` | 8.8.8.8 in corporate env = covert tunnel DNS |
| 5 | Routing Table | `route print` | `ip route` | Host route to single C2 IP |
| 6 | ARP Cache | `arp -a` | `ip neigh show` | Duplicate/multiple MACs for one IP = ARP spoof |
